title: Title and Extent of Operation of the Code
chapter: Chapter I - Introduction
section_text: This Act shall be called the Indian Penal Code, and shall extend to the whole of India.
meaning: This section gives the official name of the law and states that the Indian Penal Code applies throughout India.
key_points: The law is called the Indian Penal Code (IPC).
The IPC extends to the whole of India.
The previous exception relating to Jammu and Kashmir has been removed.
legal_significance: This section establishes the identity and territorial applicability of the IPC. It is the foundational provision that defines where the Code operates.
example: A criminal offence committed in Kerala, Karnataka, Delhi, or any other part of India is governed by the IPC.
historical_note: Originally, the IPC did not apply to certain territories and later excluded the State of Jammu and Kashmir. The words 'except the State of Jammu and Kashmir' were omitted by the Jammu and Kashmir Reorganisation Act, 2019, making the IPC applicable throughout India from 31 October 2019.

faqs.question: What is the purpose of IPC Section 1?
faqs.answer: It provides the name of the law and specifies its territorial applicability.

faqs.question: Does IPC apply throughout India?
faqs.answer: Yes. After the 2019 amendment, the IPC applies throughout India, including Jammu and Kashmir.

ai_summary: Section 1 names the law as the Indian Penal Code and declares that it applies throughout India.

title: Punishment of Offences Committed Within India
chapter: Chapter I - Introduction
section_text: Every person shall be liable to punishment under this Code and not otherwise for every act or omission contrary to the provisions thereof, of which he shall be guilty within India.
meaning: Any person who commits an offence within India is liable to be punished under the Indian Penal Code. The law applies equally to citizens, foreign nationals, and other persons present within Indian territory.
key_points: Applies to every person within India.
Includes both acts and omissions that constitute offences.
Nationality does not affect liability under the IPC.
Establishes territorial criminal jurisdiction of the IPC.
Offences committed within India are punishable under the IPC.
legal_significance: This section is one of the foundational jurisdictional provisions of the IPC. It establishes that every person who commits an offence within India is subject to Indian criminal law, regardless of citizenship or status.
example: A foreign tourist commits theft while visiting India. The person can be prosecuted and punished under the IPC in the same manner as an Indian citizen.
historical_note: The expression 'the said territories' was replaced by the word 'India' through subsequent amendments. The phrase 'on or after the said first day of May, 1861' was removed by the Indian Penal Code Amendment Act, 1891.

faqs.question: Does IPC Section 2 apply to foreigners?
faqs.answer: Yes. Every person committing an offence within India is liable under the IPC, regardless of nationality.

faqs.question: What does 'act or omission' mean?
faqs.answer: It includes both doing something prohibited by law and failing to do something required by law.

faqs.question: Why is Section 2 important?
faqs.answer: It establishes the territorial jurisdiction of the IPC and ensures that all persons committing offences within India can be prosecuted.

ai_summary: Section 2 makes every person liable under the IPC for offences committed within India, regardless of nationality, and establishes the territorial jurisdiction of Indian criminal law.

title: Punishment of Offences Committed Beyond, but Which by Law May Be Tried Within India
chapter: Chapter I - Introduction
section_text: Any person liable, by any Indian law, to be tried for an offence committed beyond India shall be dealt with according to the provisions of this Code for any act committed beyond India in the same manner as if such act had been committed within India.
meaning: A person who commits an offence outside India can still be prosecuted under Indian law if any Indian law gives Indian courts the authority to try that offence.
key_points: Applies to offences committed outside India.
Requires an Indian law that allows trial within India.
The offender is punished according to the IPC.
The offence is treated as if it had been committed within India.
Creates extra-territorial jurisdiction.
legal_significance: This section enables Indian courts to punish certain offences committed outside India's territory when Indian law permits such prosecution. It prevents offenders from escaping liability merely because the offence occurred abroad.
example: An Indian citizen commits a serious offence in another country and Indian law permits prosecution in India. The offender may be tried and punished under the IPC as if the offence had occurred within India.
historical_note: The phrase 'law passed by the Governor General of India in Council' was replaced with 'Indian law' by the Adaptation of Orders. Similarly, references to 'the said territories' were replaced with 'India' through subsequent amendments.

faqs.question: Can a person be punished under IPC for an offence committed outside India?
faqs.answer: Yes, if Indian law authorises Indian courts to try that offence.

faqs.question: Does Section 3 itself prescribe punishment?
faqs.answer: No. It only provides jurisdiction and states that punishment will be according to the IPC.

faqs.question: Why is Section 3 important?
faqs.answer: It allows India to prosecute certain offences committed outside its territory and ensures offenders cannot avoid liability simply because the crime occurred abroad.

ai_summary: Section 3 allows India to prosecute and punish certain offences committed outside India when Indian law permits such trial, treating the offence as if it were committed within India.

title: Extension of Code to Extra-Territorial Offences
chapter: Chapter I - Introduction
section_text: The provisions of this Code apply also to any offence committed by (1) any citizen of India in any place without and beyond India; (2) any person on any ship or aircraft registered in India wherever it may be; and (3) any person in any place without and beyond India committing an offence targeting a computer resource located in India.
meaning: The IPC can apply to certain offences committed outside India's geographical boundaries. Indian citizens abroad, persons on Indian-registered ships or aircraft, and individuals committing cyber offences against computer resources located in India may be prosecuted under Indian law.
key_points: Applies to Indian citizens anywhere in the world.
Applies to offences committed on Indian-registered ships and aircraft.
Applies to cyber offences targeting computer resources located in India.
Creates extra-territorial criminal jurisdiction.
Allows prosecution in India even when the offence occurs outside India.
legal_significance: This section extends the reach of Indian criminal law beyond the territorial boundaries of India. It ensures that Indian citizens and certain offenders cannot escape criminal liability merely because the offence was committed abroad.
example: An Indian citizen commits murder in Uganda. The person can be tried and punished in India as if the offence had been committed within India.
illustration: A, who is a citizen of India, commits a murder in Uganda. He can be tried and convicted of murder in any place in India in which he may be found.

explanation.offence: Includes every act committed outside India which, if committed in India, would be punishable under the IPC.
explanation.computer_resource: Has the meaning assigned under Section 2(1)(k) of the Information Technology Act, 2000.

historical_note: Section 4 was substituted by Act 4 of 1898. Cyber-crime jurisdiction relating to computer resources located in India was inserted by the Information Technology (Amendment) Act, 2008 and came into force on 27 October 2009.

faqs.question: Can an Indian citizen be punished for a crime committed abroad?
faqs.answer: Yes. Under Section 4, Indian citizens may be tried under IPC for certain offences committed outside India.

faqs.question: Does IPC apply to cyber crimes committed from another country?
faqs.answer: Yes. If the offence targets a computer resource located in India, IPC jurisdiction may apply.

faqs.question: Does Section 4 itself provide punishment?
faqs.answer: No. It extends the jurisdiction of the IPC. Punishment is provided under the relevant offence section.

ai_summary: Section 4 extends the IPC to offences committed outside India by Indian citizens, on Indian-registered ships or aircraft, and to cyber offences targeting computer resources located in India.

title: Certain Laws Not to be Affected by this Act
chapter: Chapter I - Introduction
section_text: Nothing in this Act shall affect the provisions of any Act for punishing mutiny and desertion of officers, soldiers, sailors or airmen in the service of the Government of India or the provisions of any special or local law.
meaning: The Indian Penal Code does not override or interfere with military laws, special laws, or local laws that operate independently. Such laws continue to remain valid and enforceable alongside the IPC.
key_points: Protects military laws relating to mutiny and desertion.
Preserves the operation of special laws.
Preserves the operation of local laws.
IPC does not replace or invalidate other statutory laws.
Acts as a saving clause within the Code.
legal_significance: This section ensures that the IPC coexists with other specialized legal frameworks. Where a special law or local law governs a particular matter, that law continues to operate independently and is not affected by the IPC.
example: An offence committed by a member of the armed forces relating to military discipline may be governed by military law rather than the IPC.
historical_note: Section 5 was substituted to clarify that the IPC does not affect laws dealing with mutiny, desertion, or any special or local law enacted separately.

faqs.question: Does the IPC override special laws?
faqs.answer: No. Section 5 expressly preserves the operation of special and local laws.

faqs.question: Why is Section 5 called a saving clause?
faqs.answer: Because it saves or protects the continued operation of other laws from being affected by the IPC.

faqs.question: Does Section 5 apply to military laws?
faqs.answer: Yes. Laws dealing with mutiny, desertion, and military discipline remain unaffected by the IPC.

ai_summary: Section 5 preserves military laws, special laws, and local laws by ensuring that the Indian Penal Code does not affect their operation.

title: Definitions in the Code to be Understood Subject to Exceptions
chapter: Chapter II - General Explanations
section_text: Throughout this Code every definition of an offence, every penal provision, and every illustration of every such definition or penal provision, shall be understood subject to the exceptions contained in the Chapter entitled 'General Exceptions', though those exceptions are not repeated in such definition, penal provision, or illustration.
meaning: Every offence, punishment provision, and illustration in the IPC must be read together with the General Exceptions contained in Chapter IV. Even if an exception is not specifically mentioned in a section, it still applies wherever relevant.
key_points: General Exceptions apply throughout the IPC.
Exceptions need not be repeated in every section.
Definitions of offences are subject to General Exceptions.
Punishment provisions are subject to General Exceptions.
Illustrations are also interpreted subject to General Exceptions.
legal_significance: Section 6 ensures that criminal liability is not determined solely by reading an offence section in isolation. Courts must consider the General Exceptions, such as infancy, insanity, mistake of fact, accident, necessity, and private defence, before deciding criminal responsibility.

illustrations.label: Illustration (a)
illustrations.text: A child under seven years of age cannot commit an offence even though offence definitions do not expressly mention this exception, because the General Exceptions chapter provides immunity to such children.

illustrations.label: Illustration (b)
illustrations.text: A police officer lawfully apprehending a murderer without a warrant is not guilty of wrongful confinement because he is bound by law to perform that act.

faqs.question: Why is Section 6 important?
faqs.answer: It ensures that every offence under the IPC is interpreted together with the General Exceptions, preventing unfair criminal liability.

faqs.question: Do General Exceptions apply even when not mentioned in a section?
faqs.answer: Yes. Section 6 expressly states that all offence definitions and penal provisions are subject to the General Exceptions.

faqs.question: Can a child under seven years be held criminally liable?
faqs.answer: No. The General Exceptions provide complete immunity to children under seven years of age.

faqs.question: Can a police officer be guilty of wrongful confinement while making a lawful arrest?
faqs.answer: No. If the officer is acting under legal authority, the General Exceptions protect such conduct.

important_concepts: General Exceptions override offence definitions where applicable.
Criminal liability requires consideration of statutory defences.
The IPC must be interpreted as a complete code rather than isolated sections.
ai_summary: Section 6 provides that every offence, punishment provision, and illustration in the IPC must be read subject to the General Exceptions, even when those exceptions are not expressly mentioned.

title: Sense of Expression Once Explained
chapter: Chapter II - General Explanations
section_text: Every expression which is explained in any part of this Code, is used in every part of this Code in conformity with the explanation.
meaning: Whenever a word or expression is defined or explained in one part of the IPC, the same meaning applies throughout the Code unless the context clearly requires otherwise.
key_points: Definitions apply uniformly throughout the IPC.
The same expression carries the same meaning wherever it appears.
Promotes consistency in legal interpretation.
Prevents conflicting interpretations of legal terms.
Ensures uniform application of the law.
legal_significance: Section 7 establishes the principle of consistent interpretation within the IPC. It ensures that courts, lawyers, and law enforcement authorities interpret defined terms uniformly throughout the Code, thereby reducing ambiguity and maintaining legal certainty.
example: If the term 'offence' is explained in one part of the IPC, that explanation applies whenever the term 'offence' appears elsewhere in the Code unless the context indicates a different meaning.

faqs.question: What is the purpose of Section 7?
faqs.answer: It ensures that words and expressions explained in one part of the IPC have the same meaning throughout the Code.

faqs.question: Why is Section 7 important?
faqs.answer: It promotes consistency and prevents different interpretations of the same legal term.

faqs.question: Can the meaning of an expression change in another section?
faqs.answer: Generally no. The explained meaning applies throughout the IPC unless the context clearly requires a different interpretation.

important_concepts: Definitions apply across the entire Code.
ai_summary: Section 7 provides that any expression explained in one part of the IPC carries the same meaning throughout the Code, ensuring consistent legal interpretation.

title: Gender
chapter: Chapter II - General Explanations
section_text: The pronoun 'he' and its derivatives are used of any person, whether male or female.
meaning: Whenever the IPC uses the pronoun 'he' or related words such as 'him' or 'his', those words should be interpreted as referring to both males and females unless the context requires otherwise.
key_points: The word 'he' includes both men and women.
Gender-specific pronouns are interpreted in a gender-neutral manner.
The provision applies throughout the IPC.
Prevents exclusion based on grammatical gender.
Ensures equal application of criminal law.
legal_significance: Section 8 establishes a rule of statutory interpretation that masculine pronouns used in the IPC are not restricted to males. This ensures that criminal liability and legal provisions apply equally to all persons regardless of gender unless a section specifically provides otherwise.
example: If a section states that 'he commits an offence', the provision applies equally to a woman committing the same act unless the offence is specifically gender-based.

faqs.question: Does the word 'he' in the IPC refer only to men?
faqs.answer: No. Section 8 states that 'he' and its derivatives include both males and females.

faqs.question: Why is Section 8 important?
faqs.answer: It ensures that IPC provisions apply equally regardless of gender and prevents confusion in legal interpretation.

faqs.question: Does Section 8 create any offence or punishment?
faqs.answer: No. It is an interpretative provision and does not create any offence or prescribe punishment.

ai_summary: Section 8 provides that the pronoun 'he' and its derivatives used in the IPC include both males and females, ensuring gender-neutral interpretation of the law.

title: Number
chapter: Chapter II - General Explanations
section_text: Unless the contrary appears from the context, words importing the singular number include the plural number, and words importing the plural number include the singular number.
meaning: Words written in the singular form may also refer to multiple persons or things, and words written in the plural form may also refer to a single person or thing, unless the context clearly indicates otherwise.
key_points: Singular words may include plural meanings.
Plural words may include singular meanings.
The context of the provision is important.
Applies throughout the IPC.
Prevents technical loopholes based on grammatical number.
legal_significance: Section 9 ensures flexibility and consistency in interpreting statutory language. It prevents offenders from escaping liability due to technical arguments based solely on whether a word is used in the singular or plural form.
example: If a section refers to 'a person', it may apply to multiple persons acting together if the context permits. Similarly, a provision referring to 'persons' may apply to a single individual where appropriate.

faqs.question: What does Section 9 mean?
faqs.answer: It means that singular words can include plural meanings and plural words can include singular meanings unless the context indicates otherwise.

faqs.question: Why is Section 9 important?
faqs.answer: It ensures that legal provisions are interpreted broadly and prevents disputes based on grammatical technicalities.

faqs.question: Does Section 9 create any offence?
faqs.answer: No. It is an interpretative provision that helps in understanding the language used throughout the IPC.

ai_summary: Section 9 provides that, unless the context requires otherwise, singular words include the plural and plural words include the singular throughout the IPC.

title: Man and Woman
chapter: Chapter II - General Explanations
section_text: The word 'man' denotes a male human being of any age; the word 'woman' denotes a female human being of any age.
meaning: For the purposes of the IPC, a 'man' means any male human being regardless of age, and a 'woman' means any female human being regardless of age.
key_points: Defines the term 'man'.
Defines the term 'woman'.
Age does not affect these definitions.
Applies throughout the IPC unless the context requires otherwise.
Provides clarity for interpreting gender-specific provisions.
legal_significance: Section 10 establishes clear legal definitions of the terms 'man' and 'woman' used throughout the IPC. By specifying that age is irrelevant, the section ensures uniform interpretation of these terms in all applicable provisions.
example: A male child and an adult male are both included within the legal definition of 'man'. Similarly, a female child and an adult female are both included within the legal definition of 'woman'.

faqs.question: Does the term 'man' include boys?
faqs.answer: Yes. Section 10 states that a man is a male human being of any age.

faqs.question: Does the term 'woman' include girls?
faqs.answer: Yes. Section 10 states that a woman is a female human being of any age.

faqs.question: Why is Section 10 important?
faqs.answer: It provides precise legal definitions that help ensure consistent interpretation of gender-related terms throughout the IPC.

important_concepts: Legal definitions apply throughout the Code.
Age is irrelevant for determining whether a person is a man or woman under the IPC.
Promotes uniform interpretation of statutory language.
ai_summary: Section 10 defines 'man' as any male human being and 'woman' as any female human being, regardless of age, for the purposes of interpreting the IPC.

title: Person
chapter: Chapter II - General Explanations
section_text: The word 'person' includes any Company or Association or body of persons, whether incorporated or not.
meaning: The term 'person' under the IPC is not limited to individual human beings. It also includes companies, associations, organizations, firms, societies, and groups of persons, regardless of whether they are legally incorporated.
key_points: A person can be an individual human being.
A company is considered a person under the IPC.
An association of persons is considered a person.
A body of persons may be incorporated or unincorporated.
Organizations can be held legally responsible under certain IPC provisions.
legal_significance: Section 11 broadens the meaning of the term 'person' so that legal entities such as companies, corporations, associations, societies, and other collective bodies can be subject to legal rights, duties, liabilities, and criminal proceedings wherever applicable under the IPC.
example: If a company submits forged documents to a government authority, the company may be treated as a 'person' under the IPC and can be prosecuted where the offence is applicable to corporate entities.

faqs.question: Does the word 'person' only mean a human being?
faqs.answer: No. Under Section 11, it also includes companies, associations, and bodies of persons.

faqs.question: Can a company be treated as a person under the IPC?
faqs.answer: Yes. A company is expressly included within the definition of 'person'.

faqs.question: Does an unregistered association qualify as a person?
faqs.answer: Yes. The section applies whether the body of persons is incorporated or not.

faqs.question: Why is Section 11 important?
faqs.answer: It allows legal entities such as companies and associations to be recognized and held accountable under criminal law where applicable.

ai_summary: Section 11 defines 'person' broadly to include individuals, companies, associations, and bodies of persons, whether incorporated or not.

title: Public
chapter: Chapter II - General Explanations
section_text: The word 'public' includes any class of the public or any community.
meaning: The term 'public' does not necessarily mean the entire population. It can refer to a specific group, section, class, or community of people who share a common characteristic or interest.
key_points: Public includes the general public.
Public may refer to a specific class of people.
Public may refer to a particular community.
The entire population need not be affected for an act to concern the public.
The definition applies throughout the IPC.
legal_significance: Section 12 expands the meaning of 'public' to include not only society as a whole but also particular communities or classes of people. This is important in offences affecting public peace, public health, public safety, public order, and public morality.
example: If an act creates fear among residents of a particular locality or members of a religious community, it may be considered an act affecting the 'public' even though the entire population of India is not affected.

faqs.question: Does 'public' mean all people in India?
faqs.answer: No. It may include any class of the public or a particular community.

faqs.question: Can a small group of people be considered the public?
faqs.answer: Yes. A specific class or community may fall within the meaning of 'public' under Section 12.

faqs.question: Why is Section 12 important?
faqs.answer: It helps courts determine whether an act affects the public, a community, or a particular class of people for various IPC offences.

faqs.question: Does Section 12 create an offence?
faqs.answer: No. It is a definitional provision used for interpreting other sections of the IPC.

important_concepts: Applicability to specific classes of people.
ai_summary: Section 12 defines 'public' broadly to include not only the general public but also any class of the public or any community.

title: Definition of Queen (Omitted)
chapter: Chapter II - General Explanations
section_text: [Omitted]
meaning: Section 13 originally contained the definition of the term 'Queen'. Following India's transition from British rule to a sovereign republic, this provision became obsolete and was removed from the Indian Penal Code.
key_points: Section 13 has been omitted.
The section originally defined the term 'Queen'.
The provision became unnecessary after India became a republic.
The omission was carried out through the Adaptation of Laws Order, 1950.
The section has no legal effect today.
legal_significance: Section 13 is historically significant because it reflects the colonial origins of the IPC. Its omission demonstrates the adaptation of Indian laws following independence and the replacement of references to the British Crown.
example: Modern courts and legal authorities do not apply Section 13 because it has been formally omitted from the IPC.
historical_note: The section titled 'Definition of Queen' was omitted by the Adaptation of Laws Order, 1950, after India became a republic and references to the British Crown were removed from Indian legislation.

faqs.question: Does Section 13 currently have any legal effect?
faqs.answer: No. The section has been omitted and is no longer operative.

faqs.question: What did Section 13 originally contain?
faqs.answer: It contained the definition of the term 'Queen' during the British colonial period.

faqs.question: Why was Section 13 removed?
faqs.answer: It became obsolete after India's independence and the establishment of the Republic of India.

faqs.question: Should omitted sections be included in a legal AI dataset?
faqs.answer: Yes. Including omitted sections preserves historical context and helps with legal research and IPC evolution tracking.

important_concepts: Adaptation of Indian laws after independence.
ai_summary: Section 13, which originally defined the term 'Queen', was omitted by the Adaptation of Laws Order, 1950, and no longer has any legal effect.

title: Servant of Government
chapter: Chapter II - General Explanations
section_text: The words 'servant of Government' denote any officer or servant continued, appointed or employed in India by or under the authority of Government.
meaning: A 'servant of Government' is any person who is appointed, employed, or continues in service under the authority of the Government in India. This includes government officers and employees working in various departments and public institutions.
key_points: Defines the term 'servant of Government'.
Includes both officers and servants employed by the Government.
Applies to persons appointed under governmental authority.
Covers individuals continuing in government service.
Used throughout the IPC wherever government servants are referred to.
legal_significance: Section 14 provides a statutory definition of 'servant of Government', which is important for interpreting offences involving public administration, abuse of authority, corruption-related conduct, and offences committed by or against government employees.
example: A revenue officer appointed by the State Government is considered a 'servant of Government' under this section.
historical_note: Section 14 was substituted by the Adaptation of Laws Order, 1950, to align the terminology with the constitutional framework of independent India.

faqs.question: Who is considered a servant of Government under IPC Section 14?
faqs.answer: Any officer or employee appointed, employed, or continued in service by or under the authority of the Government.

faqs.question: Does Section 14 create any offence?
faqs.answer: No. It only defines the term 'servant of Government' for use throughout the IPC.

faqs.question: Why is Section 14 important?
faqs.answer: It helps determine who qualifies as a government servant for offences and legal provisions involving public administration.

faqs.question: Are all government employees covered by this definition?
faqs.answer: Yes, provided they are appointed, employed, or continued in service under governmental authority.

ai_summary: Section 14 defines a 'servant of Government' as any officer or employee appointed, employed, or continued in service in India under the authority of the Government.

title: Definition of British India (Repealed)
chapter: Chapter II - General Explanations
section_text: [Repealed]
meaning: Section 15 originally contained the definition of 'British India'. The provision was repealed because the term became obsolete following constitutional and political changes before India's independence.
key_points: Section 15 has been repealed.
The section originally defined 'British India'.
The provision is no longer in force.
It has no legal effect in modern Indian law.
The repeal reflects changes in India's constitutional and territorial status.
legal_significance: Section 15 is of historical importance because it reflects the colonial framework under which the IPC was originally enacted. Its repeal removed an outdated territorial definition that was no longer relevant to the legal system.
example: Modern courts do not rely on Section 15 because it was repealed and no longer forms part of the operative provisions of the IPC.
historical_note: The section titled 'Definition of British India' was repealed by the Adaptation of Laws Order, 1937. Following the repeal, references to 'British India' in legislation were gradually replaced with terminology appropriate to India's evolving constitutional status.

faqs.question: Is Section 15 currently in force?
faqs.answer: No. Section 15 was repealed and is no longer part of the operative IPC.

faqs.question: What did Section 15 originally define?
faqs.answer: It provided the definition of the term 'British India'.

faqs.question: Why was Section 15 repealed?
faqs.answer: The term 'British India' became outdated due to constitutional and territorial changes, making the provision unnecessary.

faqs.question: Should repealed sections be included in a legal AI dataset?
faqs.answer: Yes. They provide valuable historical and legislative context for legal research and statutory evolution.

ai_summary: Section 15, which originally defined 'British India', was repealed by the Adaptation of Laws Order, 1937, and no longer has any legal effect.

title: Definition of Government of India (Repealed)
chapter: Chapter II - General Explanations
section_text: [Repealed]
meaning: Section 16 originally contained the definition of 'Government of India'. The provision was repealed because the definition became outdated due to constitutional and administrative changes in India.
key_points: Section 16 has been repealed.
The section originally defined 'Government of India'.
The provision is no longer operative.
Modern legal interpretation relies on current constitutional and statutory provisions.
The repeal reflects the evolution of India's governance structure.
legal_significance: Although no longer in force, Section 16 is historically significant because it demonstrates how the IPC originally defined governmental authority during the colonial era. Its repeal helped modernize the Code and remove obsolete terminology.
example: Courts and legal authorities today do not refer to Section 16 when interpreting the term 'Government of India'; instead, they rely on the Constitution of India and other applicable laws.
historical_note: The section titled 'Definition of Government of India' was repealed by the Adaptation of Laws Order, 1937. The repeal was part of broader legislative reforms aimed at updating colonial-era terminology.

faqs.question: Is Section 16 currently in force?
faqs.answer: No. Section 16 was repealed and no longer forms part of the operative provisions of the IPC.

faqs.question: What did Section 16 originally define?
faqs.answer: It contained the definition of 'Government of India'.

faqs.question: Why was Section 16 repealed?
faqs.answer: The provision became outdated due to constitutional and administrative developments, making the definition unnecessary.

faqs.question: Why include repealed sections in a legal AI dataset?
faqs.answer: Repealed sections provide historical context and help researchers understand the legislative evolution of the IPC.

ai_summary: Section 16, which originally defined 'Government of India', was repealed by the Adaptation of Laws Order, 1937, and no longer has any legal effect.

title: Government
chapter: Chapter II - General Explanations
section_text: The word 'Government' denotes the Central Government or the Government of a State.
meaning: Whenever the term 'Government' is used in the IPC, it refers to either the Central Government of India or the Government of any State, depending on the context.
key_points: Defines the term 'Government'.
Includes the Central Government.
Includes State Governments.
Applies throughout the IPC.
Helps determine the authority referred to in various provisions.
legal_significance: Section 17 provides a uniform definition of 'Government' for interpreting the IPC. It ensures that references to governmental authority include both the Union Government and State Governments, thereby avoiding ambiguity in the application of criminal law.
example: If an IPC provision refers to an act done under the authority of the Government, that authority may be exercised either by the Central Government or the Government of a State, depending on the circumstances.
historical_note: Section 17 was substituted by the Adaptation of Laws Order, 1950. The words 'Part A State' were later omitted by the Adaptation of Laws Act, 1951, reflecting constitutional changes in the classification of States.

faqs.question: What does 'Government' mean under IPC Section 17?
faqs.answer: It means either the Central Government or the Government of a State.

faqs.question: Does Section 17 apply throughout the IPC?
faqs.answer: Yes. The definition applies wherever the term 'Government' appears in the IPC unless the context suggests otherwise.

faqs.question: Does Section 17 create any offence or punishment?
faqs.answer: No. It is purely a definitional provision used for interpretation.

faqs.question: Why is Section 17 important?
faqs.answer: It ensures clarity and consistency when interpreting references to governmental authority in criminal law.

ai_summary: Section 17 defines 'Government' as either the Central Government or the Government of a State for the purposes of interpreting the IPC.

title: India
chapter: Chapter II - General Explanations
section_text: "India" means the territory of India excluding the State of Jammu and Kashmir.
meaning: This section defines the geographical area referred to as 'India' for the purposes of the IPC. At the time this provision was in force, Jammu and Kashmir was excluded from the territorial scope of the IPC.
key_points: Defines the term 'India' for the IPC.
Specifies the territorial extent of the Code.
Historically excluded the State of Jammu and Kashmir.
Used to determine the jurisdiction of IPC provisions.
Applied throughout the Code wherever the word 'India' appeared.
legal_significance: Section 18 provided a statutory definition of India's territorial limits for the purpose of applying the IPC. It played an important role in determining jurisdiction and the territorial applicability of criminal law.
example: If an offence was committed within a territory covered by the IPC definition of India, the offender could be prosecuted under the Code. Historically, separate legal arrangements applied to Jammu and Kashmir.
historical_note: This definition reflected the legal position before the Jammu and Kashmir Reorganisation Act, 2019. The IPC itself was extended to the whole of India from 31 October 2019, and the exclusion of Jammu and Kashmir is no longer legally applicable. Researchers should treat this provision as having historical significance.

faqs.question: What does 'India' mean under IPC Section 18?
faqs.answer: Historically, it meant the territory of India excluding the State of Jammu and Kashmir.

faqs.question: Is this definition still fully applicable today?
faqs.answer: No. After the Jammu and Kashmir Reorganisation Act, 2019, the IPC became applicable throughout India, including Jammu and Kashmir.

faqs.question: Why is Section 18 important?
faqs.answer: It helped determine the territorial jurisdiction and applicability of the IPC.

faqs.question: Does Section 18 create any offence or punishment?
faqs.answer: No. It is a definitional provision used for interpreting the Code.

ai_summary: Section 18 historically defined 'India' for IPC purposes and was used to determine the territorial applicability of the Code, though its exclusion of Jammu and Kashmir is no longer applicable after 2019.

title: Judge
chapter: Chapter II - General Explanations
section_text: The word 'Judge' denotes not only every person who is officially designated as a Judge, but also every person who is empowered by law to give, in any legal proceeding, civil or criminal, a definitive judgment, or a judgment which, if not appealed against, would be definitive, or a judgment which, if confirmed by some other authority, would be definitive, or who is one of a body of persons which body of persons is empowered by law to give such a judgment.
meaning: A Judge is not limited to a person holding the official title of Judge. Any person or member of a legally constituted body who has the authority to deliver binding judicial decisions in civil or criminal proceedings may be considered a Judge under the IPC.
key_points: Includes officially designated Judges.
Includes persons legally empowered to deliver definitive judgments.
Applies to both civil and criminal proceedings.
Includes judgments that become final if not appealed.
Includes judgments requiring confirmation by another authority.
Includes members of legally authorized judicial bodies.
legal_significance: Section 19 provides a broad definition of 'Judge' to ensure that judicial protection, responsibilities, and offences relating to judicial officers apply not only to traditional court judges but also to other legally empowered adjudicating authorities.

illustrations.label: Illustration (a)
illustrations.text: A Collector exercising jurisdiction in a suit under Act X of 1859 is a Judge.

illustrations.label: Illustration (b)
illustrations.text: A Magistrate exercising jurisdiction over a charge for which he can impose a fine or imprisonment is a Judge.

illustrations.label: Illustration (c)
illustrations.text: A member of a Panchayat empowered by law to try and determine suits is a Judge.

illustrations.label: Illustration (d)
illustrations.text: A Magistrate who only has the power to commit a case for trial before another court is not a Judge.

example: A Judicial Magistrate hearing a criminal case and passing a sentence is considered a Judge under Section 19 because the Magistrate has the legal authority to deliver a binding judicial decision.
historical_note: Illustration (c) refers to Regulation VII of 1816 of the Madras Code, which was later repealed by the Madras Civil Courts Act, 1873. The illustration remains part of the IPC for interpretative purposes.

faqs.question: Does Section 19 apply only to court judges?
faqs.answer: No. It also applies to any person legally empowered to deliver definitive judicial decisions.

faqs.question: Is every Magistrate considered a Judge?
faqs.answer: Only those Magistrates who have authority to pass judicial decisions or sentences. A Magistrate who merely commits a case for trial is not considered a Judge under Illustration (d).

faqs.question: Why is the definition of Judge so broad?
faqs.answer: To ensure that legal protections and liabilities relating to judicial functions apply to all persons exercising judicial authority.

faqs.question: Does Section 19 create any offence?
faqs.answer: No. It is a definitional provision used throughout the IPC.

ai_summary: Section 19 defines 'Judge' broadly to include not only officially appointed judges but also any person or body legally empowered to deliver binding judicial decisions in civil or criminal proceedings.

title: Court of Justice
chapter: Chapter II - General Explanations
section_text: The words 'Court of Justice' denote a Judge who is empowered by law to act judicially alone, or a body of Judges which is empowered by law to act judicially as a body, when such Judge or body of Judges is acting judicially.
meaning: A Court of Justice is any legally authorized judicial authority consisting of either a single Judge or a group of Judges who exercise judicial powers and make decisions according to law.
key_points: Includes a single Judge acting judicially.
Includes a body of Judges acting together.
The authority must be legally empowered to act judicially.
The Judge or body must be exercising judicial functions.
Applies to courts and judicial tribunals recognized by law.
legal_significance: Section 20 defines what constitutes a Court of Justice for the purposes of the IPC. This definition is important because many offences under the IPC relate to judicial proceedings, false evidence, contemptuous conduct, obstruction of justice, and offences against public justice.

illustrations.label: Illustration
illustrations.text: A Panchayat acting under Regulation VII of 1816 of the Madras Code and having power to try and determine suits is a Court of Justice.

example: A Sessions Court presided over by a Judge hearing a criminal trial is a Court of Justice because it is legally empowered to exercise judicial authority and deliver binding judgments.
historical_note: The illustration refers to Regulation VII of 1816 of the Madras Code, which was later repealed by the Madras Civil Courts Act, 1873. The illustration remains in the IPC for explanatory purposes.

faqs.question: What is a Court of Justice under IPC Section 20?
faqs.answer: It is a Judge or body of Judges legally empowered to act judicially when exercising judicial functions.

faqs.question: Is every government authority a Court of Justice?
faqs.answer: No. Only authorities empowered by law to perform judicial functions qualify as a Court of Justice.

faqs.question: Can a tribunal be considered a Court of Justice?
faqs.answer: Yes, if it is legally empowered to act judicially and determine disputes or legal matters.

faqs.question: Why is Section 20 important?
faqs.answer: It helps identify the authorities protected by laws relating to judicial proceedings and offences against public justice.

ai_summary: Section 20 defines a 'Court of Justice' as a Judge or body of Judges legally empowered to act judicially while exercising judicial functions.

title: Public Servant
chapter: Chapter II - General Explanations
section_text: The words 'public servant' denote a person falling under any of the descriptions specified in Section 21, including military officers, judges, court officers, jurors, arbitrators, persons empowered to keep individuals in confinement, government officers, officers dealing with government property or revenue, officers involved in local taxation and administration, election officials, and persons serving or paid by the Government, local authorities, statutory corporations, or government companies.
meaning: A public servant is any person who performs public duties, exercises public authority, holds a government-related office, or works for the Government, local authorities, statutory bodies, public corporations, or government companies. The definition is intentionally broad to ensure accountability in public administration.
key_points: Includes commissioned officers of the Armed Forces.
Includes judges and persons performing adjudicatory functions.
Includes officers of Courts of Justice.
Includes jurors, assessors, and panchayat members assisting courts.
Includes arbitrators appointed by courts or competent authorities.
Includes persons empowered to keep individuals in confinement.
Includes officers responsible for preventing offences and protecting public safety.
Includes officers handling government property, revenue, surveys, assessments, and contracts.
Includes officers involved in local administration, taxation, and public records.
Includes election officials and electoral roll authorities.
Includes persons employed by Government, local authorities, statutory corporations, and government companies.
legal_significance: Section 21 is one of the most important definitional provisions in the IPC. Numerous offences such as bribery, corruption, abuse of authority, disobedience of law by public servants, fabrication of records, and misconduct in public office depend upon whether a person qualifies as a 'public servant'.

illustrations.label: Illustration
illustrations.text: A Municipal Commissioner is a public servant.

explanations.title: Explanation 1
explanations.text: Persons falling under any of the specified categories are public servants whether appointed by the Government or not.

explanations.title: Explanation 2
explanations.text: A person is considered a public servant even if there is a legal defect in the appointment, provided the person is actually occupying and performing the duties of the office.

explanations.title: Explanation 3
explanations.text: The term 'election' includes elections for legislative bodies, municipalities, and other public authorities conducted under law.

example: A government school principal, a police officer, a judge, an election officer, and a Municipal Commissioner are all considered public servants under Section 21.
historical_note: Section 21 has been amended multiple times to expand the scope of 'public servant'. Major amendments in 1920 and 1964 broadened the definition to include election officials, adjudicatory authorities, local authorities, statutory corporations, and government companies.

rajasthan.description: Rajasthan added a Thirteenth category to include persons employed or engaged by public bodies in conducting or supervising examinations recognized under law.

faqs.question: Who is a public servant under IPC Section 21?
faqs.answer: Any person falling within the categories specified in Section 21, including government officials, judges, court officers, election officers, and many others performing public duties.

faqs.question: Is a Municipal Commissioner a public servant?
faqs.answer: Yes. The illustration to Section 21 specifically states that a Municipal Commissioner is a public servant.

faqs.question: Can a person be a public servant even if their appointment is defective?
faqs.answer: Yes. Explanation 2 states that actual possession of the office is sufficient even if there is a legal defect in the appointment.

faqs.question: Why is Section 21 important?
faqs.answer: Many IPC offences specifically apply to public servants, making this definition crucial for determining criminal liability and accountability.

ai_summary: Section 21 provides a comprehensive definition of 'public servant', covering government officials, judges, court officers, election authorities, local authority employees, and other persons performing public duties or exercising public authority.

title: Movable Property
chapter: Chapter II - General Explanations
section_text: The words 'movable property' are intended to include corporeal property of every description, except land and things attached to the earth or permanently fastened to anything which is attached to the earth.
meaning: Movable property refers to physical property that can be moved from one place to another. It includes all tangible objects except land, buildings, trees rooted in the ground, and items permanently attached to the earth.
key_points: Applies only to corporeal (physical and tangible) property.
Includes movable physical objects of every description.
Excludes land.
Excludes things attached to the earth.
Excludes things permanently fastened to anything attached to the earth.
Frequently used in offences relating to theft, robbery, extortion, and misappropriation.
legal_significance: Section 22 provides the legal definition of movable property, which is fundamental for determining whether offences such as theft, robbery, criminal misappropriation, and extortion can occur. Many property-related offences under the IPC require the subject matter to be movable property.
example: A car, mobile phone, laptop, bicycle, cash, jewellery, and furniture are movable property because they can be physically moved from one place to another.
non_examples: Land
Buildings permanently attached to land
Trees rooted in the ground
Electric poles fixed to the earth
Permanent fixtures attached to a building
historical_note: The definition has remained substantially unchanged since the enactment of the IPC and continues to be an important concept in property-related criminal offences.

faqs.question: What is movable property under IPC Section 22?
faqs.answer: It is any tangible property that can be moved, excluding land and things permanently attached to the earth.

faqs.question: Is a mobile phone movable property?
faqs.answer: Yes. A mobile phone is tangible and can be moved from one place to another.

faqs.question: Is land movable property?
faqs.answer: No. Land is specifically excluded from the definition of movable property.

faqs.question: Why is Section 22 important?
faqs.answer: Many property-related offences such as theft and robbery apply only to movable property.

ai_summary: Section 22 defines movable property as all tangible property capable of being moved, excluding land and things attached to or permanently fastened to the earth.

title: Wrongful Gain and Wrongful Loss
chapter: Chapter II - General Explanations
section_text:
"Wrongful gain" is gain by unlawful means of property to which the person gaining is not legally entitled.

"Wrongful loss" is the loss by unlawful means of property to which the person losing it is legally entitled.

A person is said to gain wrongfully when such person retains wrongfully, as well as when such person acquires wrongfully. A person is said to lose wrongfully when such person is wrongfully kept out of any property, as well as when such person is wrongfully deprived of property.
meaning: Wrongful gain occurs when a person unlawfully acquires or retains property that legally belongs to someone else. Wrongful loss occurs when a person is unlawfully deprived of property or prevented from enjoying property to which they are legally entitled.
key_points: Wrongful gain involves unlawful benefit from property.
Wrongful loss involves unlawful deprivation of property.
A person can gain wrongfully by acquiring property illegally.
A person can gain wrongfully by retaining property illegally.
A person can suffer wrongful loss by being deprived of property.
A person can suffer wrongful loss by being prevented from accessing property.
legal_significance: Section 23 is a foundational provision used throughout the IPC to determine dishonest intention and criminal liability in property-related offences. Concepts of wrongful gain and wrongful loss are essential elements in offences such as theft, cheating, criminal breach of trust, misappropriation, and dishonestly receiving stolen property.
example: A finds a wallet containing money and knows the owner but decides to keep it for personal use. A gains wrongfully because he retains property to which he is not legally entitled, while the owner suffers wrongful loss.
examples_of_wrongful_gain: Keeping another person's lost property despite knowing the owner.
Selling property that legally belongs to someone else.
Using another person's money without authorization.
examples_of_wrongful_loss: Being unlawfully deprived of personal property.
Being prevented from accessing one's own bank account through fraud.
Losing possession of property due to theft.

faqs.question: What is wrongful gain under IPC Section 23?
faqs.answer: It is the unlawful acquisition or retention of property to which a person is not legally entitled.

faqs.question: What is wrongful loss under IPC Section 23?
faqs.answer: It is the unlawful deprivation of property from a person who is legally entitled to it.

faqs.question: Can retaining property amount to wrongful gain?
faqs.answer: Yes. A person gains wrongfully not only by acquiring property unlawfully but also by retaining it unlawfully.

faqs.question: Why is Section 23 important?
faqs.answer: It provides the legal foundation for determining dishonest conduct in many property-related offences under the IPC.

ai_summary: Section 23 defines wrongful gain as the unlawful acquisition or retention of property and wrongful loss as the unlawful deprivation of property from a person legally entitled to it.

title: Dishonestly
chapter: Chapter II - General Explanations
section_text: Whoever does anything with the intention of causing wrongful gain to one person or wrongful loss to another person, is said to do that thing 'dishonestly'.
meaning: A person acts dishonestly when they intentionally seek to obtain an unlawful benefit for themselves or another person, or intentionally cause an unlawful loss to someone else.
key_points: Dishonesty depends on intention.
Wrongful gain and wrongful loss are essential elements.
Either wrongful gain or wrongful loss is sufficient.
Actual gain or loss is not necessary; intention is enough.
The concept is widely used in property-related offences.
legal_significance: Section 24 is one of the most important definitions in the IPC because the concept of 'dishonesty' forms the basis of several criminal offences, including theft, cheating, criminal misappropriation, criminal breach of trust, and receiving stolen property. Courts often examine the accused's intention to determine whether an act was done dishonestly.
example: A knowingly takes B's mobile phone intending to keep it permanently. A acts dishonestly because he intends to cause wrongful loss to B and wrongful gain to himself.
examples_of_dishonest_conduct: Keeping lost property despite knowing the rightful owner.
Misappropriating entrusted funds for personal use.
non_examples: Taking property under a genuine belief of legal ownership.
An accidental act without intention to cause wrongful gain or loss.
A lawful transaction carried out in good faith.

faqs.question: What does 'dishonestly' mean under IPC Section 24?
faqs.answer: It means acting with the intention of causing wrongful gain to one person or wrongful loss to another.

faqs.question: Is actual wrongful gain necessary to prove dishonesty?
faqs.answer: No. The intention to cause wrongful gain or wrongful loss is sufficient.

faqs.question: Why is Section 24 important?
faqs.answer: Many IPC offences require proof that the accused acted dishonestly.

faqs.question: What is the difference between wrongful gain and dishonesty?
faqs.answer: Wrongful gain is the unlawful benefit itself, while dishonesty is the intention to cause wrongful gain or wrongful loss.

ai_summary: Section 24 defines 'dishonestly' as doing any act with the intention of causing wrongful gain to one person or wrongful loss to another person.

title: Fraudulently
chapter: Chapter II - General Explanations
section_text: A person is said to do a thing fraudulently if he does that thing with intent to defraud but not otherwise.
meaning: A person acts fraudulently when they perform an act with the intention of deceiving another person in order to gain an unfair advantage, cause loss, or induce someone to act against their interests.
key_points: Fraud requires an intention to defraud.
Mere deception alone may not amount to fraud unless there is intent to defraud.
Fraud involves dishonest or deceitful conduct.
Intent is the essential element of fraud.
The term is used in various IPC offences involving deception and false representation.
legal_significance: Section 25 defines the concept of fraud, which is a crucial element in many criminal offences. Courts examine whether the accused acted with an intention to deceive or cheat another person. Without an intent to defraud, an act cannot be considered fraudulent under the IPC.
example: A person knowingly submits forged educational certificates to obtain a government job. The act is fraudulent because it is done with the intention to deceive the employer and obtain an unfair benefit.
examples_of_fraudulent_conduct: Using forged documents to secure employment.
Creating fake identities to obtain money.
Providing false information to obtain a loan.
Selling counterfeit products as genuine goods.
non_examples: A genuine mistake made without any intent to deceive.
An incorrect statement made in good faith.
An act performed honestly without intent to obtain an unfair advantage.

faqs.question: What does 'fraudulently' mean under IPC Section 25?
faqs.answer: It means doing an act with the intention to defraud another person.

faqs.question: Is deception alone enough to prove fraud?
faqs.answer: No. There must be an intention to defraud; otherwise the act is not fraudulent under Section 25.

faqs.question: What is the difference between 'dishonestly' and 'fraudulently'?
faqs.answer: Dishonestly focuses on causing wrongful gain or wrongful loss, whereas fraudulently focuses on the intention to deceive or defraud.

faqs.question: Why is Section 25 important?
faqs.answer: Many offences involving cheating, forgery, and deception depend on proving that the accused acted fraudulently.

ai_summary: Section 25 defines 'fraudulently' as doing an act with the intention to defraud. An act is fraudulent only when such intent exists.

title: Reason to Believe
chapter: Chapter II - General Explanations
section_text: A person is said to have 'reason to believe' a thing, if he has sufficient cause to believe that thing but not otherwise.
meaning: A person has 'reason to believe' when the facts and circumstances are strong enough to make a reasonable person believe that something is true. Mere suspicion, guesswork, or speculation is not enough.
key_points: Requires sufficient cause for belief.
Mere suspicion does not amount to reason to believe.
Based on facts, circumstances, and available evidence.
Stronger than suspicion but may not require absolute certainty.
Frequently used in offences involving knowledge and criminal intent.
legal_significance: Section 26 establishes an important legal standard used throughout the IPC. Courts often examine whether an accused had 'reason to believe' a fact existed when determining criminal liability. The standard is objective and depends on whether a reasonable person would have formed the same belief based on the available circumstances.
example: A person purchases a laptop at an unusually low price from a stranger in suspicious circumstances. If the circumstances clearly suggest that the laptop is stolen, the buyer may be said to have 'reason to believe' that the property was stolen.
examples_of_reason_to_believe: Receiving stolen goods under suspicious circumstances.
Accepting forged documents despite obvious signs of forgery.
Transacting with a person known to be involved in criminal activity when circumstances indicate illegality.
non_examples: A mere rumor without supporting facts.
A vague suspicion unsupported by evidence.
A belief based entirely on speculation.

faqs.question: What does 'reason to believe' mean under IPC Section 26?
faqs.answer: It means having sufficient cause based on facts and circumstances to believe that something is true.

faqs.question: Is suspicion the same as reason to believe?
faqs.answer: No. Suspicion alone is insufficient. There must be adequate grounds to support the belief.

faqs.question: Does 'reason to believe' require absolute certainty?
faqs.answer: No. It requires sufficient cause for belief, not complete proof or certainty.

faqs.question: Why is Section 26 important?
faqs.answer: Many IPC offences depend on proving that a person knew or had reason to believe certain facts.

ai_summary: Section 26 defines 'reason to believe' as having sufficient cause to believe a fact exists. It requires more than mere suspicion and is an important standard for determining criminal liability under the IPC.

title: Property in Possession of Wife, Clerk or Servant
chapter: Chapter II - General Explanations
section_text:
When property is in the possession of a person's wife, clerk or servant, on account of that person, it is in that person's possession within the meaning of this Code.

Explanation.—A person employed temporarily or on a particular occasion in the capacity of a clerk or servant, is a clerk or servant within the meaning of this section.
meaning: If property is held by a person's wife, clerk, or servant on behalf of that person, the law treats the property as still being in the possession of the owner. Even temporary employees acting as clerks or servants are covered by this rule.
key_points: Possession by a wife may legally amount to possession by the husband or owner.
Possession by a clerk is treated as possession of the employer.
Possession by a servant is treated as possession of the employer.
The property must be held on behalf of the person concerned.
Temporary or occasional clerks and servants are also covered.
This provision helps determine possession in property-related offences.
legal_significance: Section 27 clarifies the legal concept of possession under the IPC. It prevents offenders from arguing that property was not in the owner's possession merely because it was physically held by a wife, clerk, or servant. The section is particularly important in offences involving theft, criminal breach of trust, misappropriation, and dishonest possession of property.
example: A shop owner leaves cash with a cashier for business purposes. Although the cashier physically holds the cash, the law considers the cash to remain in the possession of the shop owner.
illustrative_scenarios: A domestic servant holding household jewellery on behalf of the owner.
A company clerk maintaining custody of official documents.
A temporary employee entrusted with company property during an event.
A wife safeguarding family valuables belonging to her spouse.
explanation: A person employed temporarily or on a particular occasion as a clerk or servant is also treated as a clerk or servant under this section.

faqs.question: What is the purpose of IPC Section 27?
faqs.answer: It clarifies that property held by a wife, clerk, or servant on behalf of a person is legally considered to remain in that person's possession.

faqs.question: Does Section 27 apply to temporary employees?
faqs.answer: Yes. The explanation specifically includes persons employed temporarily or on a particular occasion as clerks or servants.

faqs.question: Why is Section 27 important in theft cases?
faqs.answer: It helps determine legal possession of property, which is an essential element in offences such as theft and criminal misappropriation.

faqs.question: Does Section 27 create an offence?
faqs.answer: No. It is a definitional provision used to interpret possession under the IPC.

ai_summary: Section 27 provides that property held by a person's wife, clerk, or servant on that person's behalf is legally considered to remain in that person's possession. The rule also applies to temporary clerks and servants.

title: Counterfeit
chapter: Chapter II - General Explanations
section_text:
A person is said to 'counterfeit' who causes one thing to resemble another thing, intending by means of that resemblance to practise deception, or knowing it to be likely that deception will thereby be practised.

Explanation 1.—It is not essential to counterfeiting that the imitation should be exact.

Explanation 2.—When a person causes one thing to resemble another thing, and the resemblance is such that a person might be deceived thereby, it shall be presumed, until the contrary is proved, that the person so causing the one thing to resemble the other thing intended by means of that resemblance to practise deception or knew it to be likely that deception would thereby be practised.
meaning: A person commits counterfeiting when they make one thing resemble another with the intention of deceiving people or knowing that the resemblance is likely to deceive. The imitation need not be perfect; even a close resemblance capable of misleading a person may amount to counterfeiting.
key_points: Counterfeiting involves creating a deceptive resemblance.
Exact imitation is not necessary.
Intent to deceive or knowledge of likely deception is essential.
A resemblance capable of misleading a reasonable person may be sufficient.
The law presumes intent to deceive when a deceptive resemblance is established, unless proven otherwise.
legal_significance: Section 28 provides the foundational definition of counterfeiting used throughout the IPC. It applies to offences involving counterfeit currency, coins, government stamps, seals, trademarks, documents, and other objects where imitation is used to deceive or defraud others.
example: A person manufactures fake currency notes that closely resemble genuine notes. Even if the notes are not exact copies, the act amounts to counterfeiting because they are intended to deceive people into believing they are genuine.
examples_of_counterfeiting: Producing fake certificates that resemble genuine ones.
Making duplicate branded products designed to appear genuine.
non_examples: A replica clearly marked as a copy and not intended to deceive.
An artistic imitation made solely for educational purposes.
A model object that cannot reasonably mislead anyone.

explanations.title: Explanation 1
explanations.text: Counterfeiting does not require an exact imitation. A substantial resemblance is sufficient.

explanations.title: Explanation 2
explanations.text: If the resemblance is capable of deceiving a person, the law presumes an intention to deceive unless the accused proves otherwise.

faqs.question: What is counterfeiting under IPC Section 28?
faqs.answer: It is causing one thing to resemble another with the intention to deceive or knowing that deception is likely.

faqs.question: Must a counterfeit item be an exact copy?
faqs.answer: No. Explanation 1 specifically states that an exact imitation is not necessary.

faqs.question: How does the law determine intent to deceive?
faqs.answer: If the resemblance is capable of deceiving a person, the law presumes intent to deceive unless the contrary is proved.

faqs.question: Why is Section 28 important?
faqs.answer: It forms the basis for offences involving counterfeit currency, documents, seals, trademarks, and other deceptive imitations.

ai_summary: Section 28 defines counterfeiting as causing one thing to resemble another with the intention to deceive or with knowledge that deception is likely. The imitation need not be exact if it is capable of misleading others.

title: Document
chapter: Chapter II - General Explanations
section_text: The word 'document' denotes any matter expressed or described upon any substance by means of letters, figures or marks, or by more than one of those means, intended to be used, or which may be used, as evidence of that matter.
meaning: A document is any information, statement, record, instruction, or representation expressed on any material through writing, numbers, symbols, signs, or marks that can be used as evidence of a fact or matter.
key_points: A document can be written on any substance.
It may contain letters, figures, marks, or a combination of them.
The document must be intended to be used as evidence or capable of being used as evidence.
It is irrelevant whether the document is used in court.
Commercial symbols and customary notations can also constitute a document.
legal_significance: Section 29 provides the foundational definition of 'document' used throughout the IPC. It is particularly important in offences relating to forgery, falsification, fraud, cheating, counterfeit documents, and evidentiary records. The definition is intentionally broad to cover all forms of recorded information capable of proving a fact.

illustrations.label: Illustration (a)
illustrations.text: A writing expressing the terms of a contract is a document.

illustrations.label: Illustration (b)
illustrations.text: A cheque upon a banker is a document.

illustrations.label: Illustration (c)
illustrations.text: A power-of-attorney is a document.

illustrations.label: Illustration (d)
illustrations.text: A map or plan intended to be used as evidence is a document.

illustrations.label: Illustration (e)
illustrations.text: A writing containing directions or instructions is a document.

illustrations.label: Illustration (Explanation 2)
illustrations.text: An endorsement on a bill of exchange is treated as a document because commercial usage gives legal meaning to the signature.

explanations.title: Explanation 1
explanations.text: It does not matter how the letters, figures, or marks are created, on what material they are created, or whether the document is intended for use in a Court of Justice.

explanations.title: Explanation 2
explanations.text: Expressions understood through commercial, mercantile, or customary usage are considered part of the document even if not expressly written.

example: A signed employment agreement stored on paper contains terms and conditions that may be used as evidence of the employment relationship. Therefore, it is a document under Section 29.

faqs.question: What is a document under IPC Section 29?
faqs.answer: Any matter expressed by letters, figures, or marks on any substance that can be used as evidence of a fact.

faqs.question: Must a document be intended for court use?
faqs.answer: No. A document may be used as evidence even outside a Court of Justice.

faqs.question: Is a cheque considered a document?
faqs.answer: Yes. The IPC specifically recognizes a cheque as a document.

faqs.question: Why is Section 29 important?
faqs.answer: Many offences such as forgery, falsification, and fraud depend on whether an item qualifies as a document.

ai_summary: Section 29 defines a document as any matter expressed through letters, figures, or marks on any substance that is intended to be used, or may be used, as evidence of a fact or matter.

title: Electronic Record
chapter: Chapter II - General Explanations
section_text: The words 'electronic record' shall have the meaning assigned to them in clause (t) of sub-section (1) of section 2 of the Information Technology Act, 2000 (21 of 2000).
meaning: An electronic record is any data, information, image, sound, document, message, or record that is created, stored, received, or transmitted in electronic form as defined under the Information Technology Act, 2000.
key_points: The definition is adopted from the Information Technology Act, 2000.
Includes information stored electronically.
Covers emails, digital documents, electronic files, and databases.
Applies to records created, received, stored, or transmitted electronically.
Recognizes digital information as legally significant evidence.
legal_significance: Section 29A was inserted to align the IPC with modern technology and digital communication. It ensures that electronic records receive legal recognition and can be the subject matter of offences such as forgery, falsification, fraud, cybercrime, and destruction of evidence.
example: An email stored on a computer, a PDF contract, a digital invoice, a database record, or a message stored on a mobile device may constitute an electronic record.
historical_note: Section 29A was inserted by the Information Technology Act, 2000 through Act 21 of 2000 and came into force on 17 October 2000 to provide legal recognition to electronic records within the IPC framework.

linked_legislation.purpose: Provides the statutory definition of 'electronic record'.

faqs.question: What is an electronic record under IPC Section 29A?
faqs.answer: It is any record, data, document, image, sound, or information stored or transmitted electronically as defined under the Information Technology Act, 2000.

faqs.question: Does an email qualify as an electronic record?
faqs.answer: Yes. Emails are electronic records because they are created, transmitted, and stored electronically.

faqs.question: Why was Section 29A introduced?
faqs.answer: It was introduced to recognize digital records and align criminal law with technological developments.

faqs.question: Does Section 29A create an offence?
faqs.answer: No. It only defines the term 'electronic record' for use throughout the IPC.

ai_summary: Section 29A defines 'electronic record' by adopting the definition provided in the Information Technology Act, 2000, thereby giving legal recognition to digitally stored and transmitted information under the IPC.

title: Valuable Security
chapter: Chapter II - General Explanations
section_text: The words 'valuable security' denote a document which is, or purports to be, a document whereby any legal right is created, extended, transferred, restricted, extinguished or released, or whereby any person acknowledges that he lies under legal liability, or has not a certain legal right.
meaning: A valuable security is a document that creates, changes, transfers, limits, ends, or releases a legal right or legal obligation. It also includes documents that acknowledge a legal liability or admit the absence of a legal right.
key_points: A valuable security must be a document.
It may create a legal right.
It may transfer or extend a legal right.
It may restrict, extinguish, or release a legal right.
It may acknowledge a legal liability.
It includes documents having legal and financial significance.
legal_significance: Section 30 is an important provision because many serious offences such as forgery, falsification, fraud, cheating, and criminal breach of trust involve valuable securities. Forgery of a valuable security is treated more severely than ordinary forgery due to its significant legal and financial consequences.

illustrations.label: Illustration
illustrations.text: A writes his name on the back of a bill of exchange. Since the endorsement transfers rights in the bill to another holder, the endorsement is a valuable security.

example: A signed promissory note acknowledging a debt of ₹1,00,000 is a valuable security because it creates and recognizes a legal obligation to pay money.
examples_of_valuable_security: Power of Attorney creating legal authority
non_examples: A personal letter with no legal effect.
A greeting card.
A casual note without legal rights or obligations.
A document containing only general information.

faqs.question: What is a valuable security under IPC Section 30?
faqs.answer: It is a document that creates, transfers, restricts, extinguishes, or acknowledges legal rights or legal liabilities.

faqs.question: Is a cheque a valuable security?
faqs.answer: Yes. A cheque represents a legal right relating to money and is treated as a valuable security.

faqs.question: Why is Section 30 important?
faqs.answer: Many forgery and fraud offences become more serious when they involve valuable securities.

faqs.question: Can a document acknowledging a debt be a valuable security?
faqs.answer: Yes. A document acknowledging legal liability falls within the definition of valuable security.

ai_summary: Section 30 defines a valuable security as a document that creates, transfers, restricts, extinguishes, or acknowledges legal rights or liabilities, making it legally and financially significant.

title: Will
chapter: Chapter II - General Explanations
section_text: The words 'a will' denote any testamentary document.
meaning: A will is a testamentary document through which a person expresses how their property, assets, or rights should be distributed or dealt with after their death.
key_points: A will is a testamentary document.
It expresses the intentions of a person regarding property after death.
It becomes operative upon the death of the maker (testator).
It can be written, modified, or revoked during the lifetime of the testator.
The definition applies throughout the IPC.
legal_significance: Section 31 provides the legal meaning of 'will' for the purposes of the IPC. The definition is important in offences relating to forgery, falsification of documents, fraud, criminal breach of trust, and inheritance-related disputes. A forged or altered will can significantly affect legal rights and property ownership.
example: A person executes a document stating that after his death his house shall pass to his daughter. This document is a will because it contains testamentary instructions regarding property after death.
examples_of_wills: A handwritten will distributing family property.
A registered will executed before witnesses.
A testamentary document appointing an executor.
A codicil modifying an existing will.
non_examples: A sale deed transferring property during lifetime.
A gift deed taking immediate effect.
A lease agreement.
A power of attorney.

faqs.question: What is a will under IPC Section 31?
faqs.answer: A will is any testamentary document expressing a person's wishes regarding property or rights after death.

faqs.question: When does a will take effect?
faqs.answer: A will generally takes effect after the death of the testator.

faqs.question: Why is Section 31 important?
faqs.answer: It provides the legal definition of a will, which is important in offences involving forgery, fraud, and inheritance disputes.

faqs.question: Is a gift deed a will?
faqs.answer: No. A gift deed transfers property immediately, whereas a will operates after the death of the maker.

ai_summary: Section 31 defines a 'will' as any testamentary document that expresses a person's intentions regarding the distribution of property or rights after their death.

title: Words Referring to Acts Include Illegal Omissions
chapter: Chapter II - General Explanations
section_text: In every part of this Code, except where a contrary intention appears from the context, words which refer to acts done extend also to illegal omissions.
meaning: Whenever the IPC refers to an act being done, it generally includes not only positive actions but also illegal failures to act when there is a legal duty to do so. Thus, criminal liability may arise from both actions and unlawful omissions.
key_points: Acts include illegal omissions.
Criminal liability may arise from failing to perform a legal duty.
The provision applies throughout the IPC unless the context indicates otherwise.
Both actions and omissions can constitute offences.
An omission becomes relevant only when there is a legal obligation to act.
