The provision protects prisoners from excessive isolation.
legal_significance: Section 74 serves as a safeguard against the harsh effects of prolonged solitary confinement. It ensures that solitary confinement is imposed only in limited and regulated periods, thereby reducing the risk of physical and psychological harm to prisoners.
essential_elements: A valid sentence of solitary confinement must exist.
The confinement must be executed according to statutory limits.
No single period of solitary confinement may exceed 14 days.
Mandatory intervals must be provided between periods.
Special monthly limits apply when imprisonment exceeds three months.

maximum_limits.interval_requirement: Interval must be at least equal to the duration of solitary confinement served.
maximum_limits.for_imprisonment_exceeding_3_months: Maximum 7 days of solitary confinement in any one month.

illustrative_examples.scenario: Prisoner placed in solitary confinement for 10 days.
illustrative_examples.rule: The next period of solitary confinement cannot begin until at least 10 days have passed.

illustrative_examples.scenario: Prisoner sentenced to 6 months' rigorous imprisonment with solitary confinement.
illustrative_examples.rule: Solitary confinement cannot exceed 7 days during any single month.

illustrative_examples.scenario: Prison authorities attempt to keep a prisoner in solitary confinement for 20 continuous days.
illustrative_examples.result: Illegal because Section 74 limits solitary confinement to 14 days at a time.

relationship_with_section_73.section_73: Authorizes the court to impose solitary confinement and specifies the maximum total period.
relationship_with_section_73.section_74: Regulates how the solitary confinement sentence is executed and imposes operational limits.

judicial_principles: Solitary confinement must be strictly regulated.
Prisoners retain fundamental human rights.
The punishment must not become cruel, inhuman or degrading.
The statutory safeguards must be followed strictly.

constitutional_aspects.article_21: Protection of life and personal liberty requires humane prison conditions.
constitutional_aspects.human_dignity: Excessive solitary confinement may violate constitutional protections.
constitutional_aspects.judicial_review: Courts may intervene if solitary confinement is imposed contrary to law.

practical_application.purpose: To prevent prolonged isolation of prisoners.
practical_application.benefit: Protects mental and physical health of inmates.
practical_application.administration: Guides prison authorities in implementing sentences.

faqs.question: What is the maximum period of solitary confinement at one time?
faqs.answer: Fourteen days.

faqs.question: Can solitary confinement be continuous?
faqs.answer: No. Mandatory intervals must be provided between periods of solitary confinement.

faqs.question: What happens when imprisonment exceeds three months?
faqs.answer: Solitary confinement cannot exceed seven days in any one month.

faqs.question: Why does Section 74 exist?
faqs.answer: To protect prisoners from excessive and harmful periods of isolation.

important_differences.section_73: Determines how much solitary confinement a court may award.
important_differences.section_74: Determines how that solitary confinement must be executed.

ai_summary: Section 74 imposes strict limits on the execution of solitary confinement. No period of solitary confinement may exceed 14 days at a time, mandatory intervals must be provided between such periods, and where imprisonment exceeds three months, solitary confinement cannot exceed seven days in any one month. The provision protects prisoners from prolonged isolation and ensures humane treatment.

title: Enhanced Punishment for Certain Offences Under Chapter XII or Chapter XVII After Previous Conviction
chapter: Chapter III - Of Punishments
section_text: Whoever, having been convicted by a Court in India of an offence punishable under Chapter XII or Chapter XVII of this Code with imprisonment of either description for a term of three years or upwards, and subsequently commits any offence punishable under either of those Chapters with like imprisonment for the like term, shall be subject for every such subsequent offence to imprisonment for life, or to imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years.
meaning: Section 75 provides enhanced punishment for repeat offenders. If a person has previously been convicted of a serious offence under Chapter XII (Offences Relating to Coin and Government Stamps) or Chapter XVII (Offences Against Property) and later commits another similar serious offence under those chapters, the court may impose a much harsher punishment.
key_points: Applies to repeat offenders.
Requires a previous conviction by a court in India.
The previous offence must be punishable with imprisonment of three years or more.
The subsequent offence must also fall under Chapter XII or Chapter XVII.
Enhanced punishment becomes available for the subsequent offence.
The provision aims to deter habitual criminals.
legal_significance: Section 75 recognizes that repeated commission of serious property-related offences demonstrates a greater degree of criminality. Therefore, the law authorizes courts to impose stricter punishment on habitual offenders than would ordinarily be imposed on first-time offenders.
essential_elements: There must be a previous conviction.
The conviction must have been by a court in India.
The previous offence must fall under Chapter XII or Chapter XVII.
The previous offence must be punishable with imprisonment of three years or more.
The accused must commit a subsequent offence under the same chapters.
The subsequent offence must also be punishable with similar imprisonment.

chapter_xii.title: Offences Relating to Coin and Government Stamps

enhanced_punishment.alternative_punishment: Imprisonment of either description up to 10 Years
enhanced_punishment.fine: May also be imposed where provided by the substantive offence

example_1.facts: A is convicted of a serious theft offence punishable with imprisonment exceeding three years. After release, A again commits a similar serious property offence.
example_1.result: Section 75 may be invoked and A may receive enhanced punishment.

example_2.facts: A previously convicted of robbery commits another robbery.
example_2.result: The court may impose imprisonment for life or imprisonment up to ten years under Section 75.

judicial_principles: Previous conviction must be properly proved.
The prosecution bears the burden of proving the prior conviction.
Enhanced punishment is justified only when statutory conditions are satisfied.
Courts must consider the nature of both offences before imposing enhanced punishment.

difference_between_first_and_repeat_offe.first_offender: Punished according to the substantive offence provision.
difference_between_first_and_repeat_offe.repeat_offender: May receive enhanced punishment under Section 75 if statutory conditions are met.

punishment.minimum: As determined by the court according to the offence

faqs.question: What is the purpose of IPC Section 75?
faqs.answer: It provides enhanced punishment for persons who repeatedly commit serious offences under Chapter XII or Chapter XVII.

faqs.question: Does every previous conviction attract Section 75?
faqs.answer: No. The previous conviction must relate to specified offences punishable with imprisonment of three years or more.

faqs.question: What is the maximum punishment under Section 75?
faqs.answer: Imprisonment for life.

faqs.question: Why does the law provide enhanced punishment?
faqs.answer: To deter habitual offenders and protect society from repeated criminal conduct.

ai_summary: Section 75 provides enhanced punishment for persons who, after being convicted of certain serious offences under Chapter XII or Chapter XVII of the IPC, commit similar offences again. Such repeat offenders may be punished with imprisonment for life or imprisonment of either description for a term extending up to ten years.

title: Act Done by a Person Bound, or by Mistake of Fact Believing Himself Bound, by Law
chapter: Chapter IV - General Exceptions
section_text: Nothing is an offence which is done by a person who is, or who by reason of a mistake of fact and not by reason of a mistake of law in good faith believes himself to be, bound by law to do it.
meaning: A person is not criminally liable for an act if he is legally required to perform that act. Similarly, if due to a genuine mistake of fact (and not a mistake of law), he honestly and in good faith believes that the law requires him to perform the act, he is also protected from criminal liability.
key_points: Section 76 is a General Exception.
Acts done under a legal duty are not offences.
Protection extends to a person who mistakenly believes he is legally bound to act.
The mistake must be a mistake of fact, not a mistake of law.
The belief must be held in good faith.
The burden of proving the exception generally lies on the accused.
legal_significance: Section 76 protects individuals who act under lawful authority or under a bona fide belief arising from a mistake of fact that they are legally obligated to act. The law recognizes that criminal liability should not arise where a person is merely carrying out what he honestly believes to be a legal duty.
essential_elements: The act must have been done by the accused.
The accused must either be legally bound to do the act, or
The accused must honestly believe he is legally bound to do the act.
Such belief must arise from a mistake of fact.
The belief must be held in good faith.
The belief must not arise from a mistake of law.

ingredients_of_section_76.legal_obligation: The person is actually required by law to perform the act.
ingredients_of_section_76.mistake_of_fact: The person mistakenly believes certain facts exist that make the act legally required.
ingredients_of_section_76.good_faith: The belief must be formed with due care and attention as defined under Section 52 IPC.
ingredients_of_section_76.not_mistake_of_law: Ignorance or misunderstanding of the law does not provide protection.

mistake_of_fact.example: An officer mistakenly believes a person is the individual named in an arrest warrant.
mistake_of_fact.protection: Protected under Section 76 if acting in good faith.

mistake_of_law.example: A person wrongly believes the law permits him to assault another person.

illustration_a.facts: A, a soldier, fires on a mob by order of his superior officer in conformity with law.
illustration_a.result: A commits no offence because he is bound by law to obey lawful military commands.

illustration_b.facts: A court officer is ordered to arrest Y. After due enquiry, he mistakenly believes Z is Y and arrests Z.
illustration_b.result: A commits no offence because he acted under a mistake of fact in good faith.

additional_examples.scenario: A police officer arrests a person whom he honestly and reasonably believes to be the wanted accused.
additional_examples.result: Protected under Section 76 if the belief arose from a mistake of fact.

additional_examples.scenario: A prison officer detains a prisoner pursuant to a valid court order.
additional_examples.result: No offence because the officer is legally bound to act.

additional_examples.scenario: A citizen wrongly believes the law authorizes him to seize another person's property.
additional_examples.result: Not protected because this is a mistake of law.

judicial_principles: Mistake of fact may excuse criminal liability.
Mistake of law is generally no excuse.
Good faith requires due care and attention.
Acts done under lawful authority are protected.
The exception must be interpreted according to the facts of each case.

burden_of_proof.rule: The accused must establish circumstances bringing the case within the exception.
burden_of_proof.standard: Preponderance of probabilities rather than proof beyond reasonable doubt.

relationship_with_other_sections.section_76: Act done under legal obligation or mistake of fact.
relationship_with_other_sections.section_79: Act justified by law or believed to be justified by law.
relationship_with_other_sections.section_78: Act done pursuant to a court judgment or order.

faqs.question: What is the main purpose of Section 76 IPC?
faqs.answer: To protect persons who act under a legal duty or who, due to a mistake of fact, honestly believe they are legally bound to act.

faqs.question: Does Section 76 apply to mistakes of law?
faqs.answer: No. The protection applies only to mistakes of fact, not mistakes of law.

faqs.question: What does 'good faith' mean in Section 76?
faqs.answer: The belief must be formed with due care and attention as required by Section 52 IPC.

faqs.question: Can a police officer claim protection under Section 76?
faqs.answer: Yes, if he acts under a legal duty or under a bona fide mistake of fact.

max_punishment: Not Applicable
ai_summary: Section 76 provides a complete defence where a person acts because he is legally bound to do so, or where, due to a mistake of fact and acting in good faith, he honestly believes himself to be legally bound to act. The protection does not extend to mistakes of law.

title: Act of Judge When Acting Judicially
chapter: Chapter IV - General Exceptions
section_text: Nothing is an offence which is done by a Judge when acting judicially in the exercise of any power which is, or which in good faith he believes to be, given to him by law.
meaning: A Judge is not criminally liable for acts performed while exercising judicial functions, provided the act is done under a power actually conferred by law or under a good-faith belief that such power exists.
key_points: Section 77 is a General Exception.
Applies only to Judges acting in a judicial capacity.
Protects judicial acts performed under lawful authority.
Protection extends to acts done under a good-faith belief of lawful authority.
The Judge must be acting judicially.
Acts outside judicial functions are not protected.
legal_significance: Section 77 protects judicial independence by ensuring that Judges can perform their duties without fear of criminal prosecution for acts done in the exercise of judicial powers. It enables the judiciary to function freely and impartially while administering justice.
essential_elements: The person must be a Judge.
The act must be performed while acting judicially.
The act must be within a power conferred by law, or
The Judge must in good faith believe that such power is conferred by law.
The act must be connected with judicial duties.

ingredients_of_section_77.judge: The person must fall within the definition of 'Judge' under Section 19 IPC.
ingredients_of_section_77.judicial_act: The act must be performed while deciding, hearing, adjudicating, or exercising judicial authority.
ingredients_of_section_77.lawful_power: The act must be authorized by law or believed in good faith to be authorized.
ingredients_of_section_77.good_faith: The belief must be formed with due care and attention as required by Section 52 IPC.

unprotected_acts: Acts done with mala fide intentions.

example_1.facts: A Magistrate issues an arrest warrant while exercising lawful judicial powers.

example_2.facts: A Judge sentences an accused person after trial.
example_2.result: The judicial act is protected under Section 77.

example_3.facts: A Judge mistakenly exercises jurisdiction believing in good faith that he possesses such authority.
example_3.result: Protected under Section 77 because of the good-faith belief.

judicial_principles: Judicial independence must be preserved.
Judges should not fear criminal liability for judicial decisions.
Good-faith exercise of judicial power is protected.
The protection applies even if the decision later proves incorrect.

good_faith.meaning: Honest belief formed with due care and attention.

relationship_with_other_sections.section_78: Protects acts done pursuant to judgments or orders of courts.

faqs.question: What is the purpose of Section 77 IPC?
faqs.answer: To protect Judges from criminal liability for acts performed while exercising judicial powers.

faqs.question: Does Section 77 protect every act of a Judge?
faqs.answer: No. It protects only acts performed judicially and under lawful authority or a good-faith belief of such authority.

faqs.question: What if the Judge makes a wrong decision?
faqs.answer: A wrong decision made while acting judicially and in good faith remains protected under Section 77.

faqs.question: Why is Section 77 important?
faqs.answer: It preserves judicial independence and allows Judges to discharge their duties without fear of criminal prosecution.

max_punishment: Not Applicable
ai_summary: Section 77 provides that no offence is committed by a Judge when acting judicially in the exercise of powers conferred by law, or which the Judge in good faith believes to be conferred by law. The provision protects judicial independence and ensures that Judges can perform their functions without fear of criminal liability.

title: Act Done Pursuant to the Judgment or Order of Court
chapter: Chapter IV - General Exceptions
section_text: Nothing which is done in pursuance of, or which is warranted by the judgment or order of, a Court of Justice, if done whilst such judgment or order remains in force, is an offence, notwithstanding the Court may have had no jurisdiction to pass such judgment or order, provided the person doing the act in good faith believes that the Court had such jurisdiction.
meaning: A person who acts according to a judgment or order of a Court of Justice is protected from criminal liability, provided the order is still in force and the person honestly and in good faith believes that the Court had the authority to issue it. This protection applies even if the Court is later found to have lacked jurisdiction.
key_points: Section 78 is a General Exception.
Protects persons acting under a court's judgment or order.
The judgment or order must be in force at the time of the act.
Protection applies even if the court lacked jurisdiction.
The person must act in good faith.
The person must honestly believe that the court had jurisdiction.
legal_significance: Section 78 protects individuals who carry out court orders from criminal liability. It ensures that citizens, police officers, court officials, and other persons can obey judicial orders without fear of prosecution if they act honestly and in good faith.
essential_elements: There must be a judgment or order of a Court of Justice.
The act must be done in pursuance of or under that judgment or order.
The judgment or order must still be operative.
The person must act in good faith.
The person must believe that the Court had jurisdiction.
The belief must be honest and reasonable.

ingredients_of_section_78.court_order: A validly existing judgment, decree, warrant, or order must exist.
ingredients_of_section_78.execution_of_order: The act must be directly connected with carrying out the order.
ingredients_of_section_78.good_faith: The person must act with due care and attention.
ingredients_of_section_78.belief_in_jurisdiction: The person must genuinely believe that the court was competent to issue the order.

protected_persons: Private individuals acting under court directions
protected_acts: Seizure of property ordered by a court

example_1.facts: A police officer arrests a person pursuant to a warrant issued by a court.
example_1.result: The officer is protected under Section 78 if he acts in good faith.

example_2.facts: A court official attaches property under a judicial order that is later found to be without jurisdiction.
example_2.result: The official is protected if he honestly believed the court had jurisdiction.

example_3.facts: A prison superintendent detains a person under a court's commitment order.
example_3.result: No offence is committed while acting under the order in good faith.

judicial_principles: Court orders must be capable of execution without fear of prosecution.
Good-faith obedience to judicial orders is protected.
The focus is on the actor's honest belief regarding jurisdiction.
Protection promotes the effective administration of justice.

difference_between_sections_76_77_and_78.section_76: Protects acts done by a person bound by law or acting under a mistake of fact.
difference_between_sections_76_77_and_78.section_78: Protects persons acting under a court's judgment or order.

good_faith_requirement.definition: As per Section 52 IPC, nothing is done in good faith without due care and attention.
good_faith_requirement.importance: The protection of Section 78 depends on the existence of good faith.

relationship_with_other_sections.section_76: Act done by a person bound by law.
relationship_with_other_sections.section_77: Act of Judge when acting judicially.
relationship_with_other_sections.section_78: Act done pursuant to judgment or order of court.

faqs.question: What is the purpose of Section 78 IPC?
faqs.answer: To protect persons who act in obedience to a court's judgment or order.

faqs.question: Does protection exist if the court lacked jurisdiction?
faqs.answer: Yes, provided the person acted in good faith and believed that the court had jurisdiction.

faqs.question: Who can claim protection under Section 78?
faqs.answer: Any person carrying out a court order, including police officers, court officials, and others acting under judicial authority.

faqs.question: What is the most important requirement under Section 78?
faqs.answer: Good-faith belief that the court had jurisdiction to issue the order.

max_punishment: Not Applicable
ai_summary: Section 78 provides that a person who acts in pursuance of a court's judgment or order commits no offence if the order is in force and he acts in good faith believing that the court had jurisdiction. The protection applies even if the court is later found to have lacked jurisdiction.

title: Act Done by a Person Justified, or by Mistake of Fact Believing Himself Justified, by Law
chapter: Chapter IV - General Exceptions
section_text: Nothing is an offence which is done by any person who is justified by law, or who by reason of a mistake of fact and not by reason of a mistake of law in good faith, believes himself to be justified by law, in doing it.
meaning: A person does not commit an offence when performing an act that is legally justified. The protection also extends to a person who, because of a genuine mistake of fact (and not a mistake of law), honestly and in good faith believes that the law authorizes or justifies the act.
key_points: Section 79 is a General Exception.
Protects acts that are legally justified.
Also protects acts done under a good-faith mistake of fact.
Mistake of law is not protected.
The belief must be honest and in good faith.
The protection operates as a complete defence to criminal liability.
legal_significance: Section 79 recognizes that criminal liability should not be imposed upon persons who act under lawful authority or who honestly believe, due to a mistake of fact, that they are acting within the authority of law. The provision promotes fairness and protects bona fide actions taken under perceived legal justification.
essential_elements: The act must be done by the accused.
The act must either be justified by law, or
The accused must believe himself justified by law.
The belief must arise from a mistake of fact.
The belief must be held in good faith.
The belief must not arise from a mistake of law.

ingredients_of_section_79.justified_by_law: The law actually authorizes the act.
ingredients_of_section_79.mistake_of_fact: The accused misunderstands facts, not the law.
ingredients_of_section_79.good_faith: The belief must be formed with due care and attention.
ingredients_of_section_79.honest_belief: The belief must be genuine and reasonable.

section_76.basis: Person is bound by law to do the act.
section_76.example: A police officer arrests a person under a legal duty.

section_79.basis: Person is justified by law in doing the act.
section_79.example: A private citizen arrests a person whom he reasonably believes to be a murderer.

illustration_analysis.facts: A sees Z commit what appears to be a murder. Believing Z to be a murderer, A arrests Z and takes him to the authorities.
illustration_analysis.actual_position: Z was later found to have acted in self-defence.
illustration_analysis.result: A is protected under Section 79 because he acted under a mistake of fact and honestly believed he was justified by law.

additional_examples.scenario: A citizen catches a person apparently committing robbery and restrains him until police arrive.
additional_examples.result: Protected if done in good faith.

additional_examples.scenario: A police officer seizes property believing it to be stolen property based on reasonable facts.
additional_examples.result: Protected if the belief was in good faith.

additional_examples.scenario: A person assaults another because he mistakenly believes the law allows him to do so.
additional_examples.result: Not protected because it is a mistake of law.

mistake_of_fact.example: Believing a person committed murder when he actually acted in self-defence.

mistake_of_law.example: Believing that the law permits private punishment of offenders.

judicial_principles: Good-faith actions deserve protection.
Mistake of fact may excuse criminal liability.
Mistake of law is generally not a defence.
The belief must be reasonable and honest.
Criminal intent is absent where lawful justification is honestly believed.

burden_of_proof.rule: The accused must establish facts bringing the case within the exception.

relationship_with_other_sections.section_76: Act done by a person bound by law.
relationship_with_other_sections.section_79: Act done by a person justified by law.
relationship_with_other_sections.section_80: Accident in doing a lawful act.
relationship_with_other_sections.section_81: Act likely to cause harm but done without criminal intent to prevent greater harm.

legal_application: Acts performed under perceived legal authority.

faqs.question: What is the purpose of Section 79 IPC?
faqs.answer: To protect persons who act under lawful justification or under a good-faith mistake of fact believing themselves justified by law.

faqs.question: Does Section 79 apply to mistakes of law?
faqs.answer: No. It applies only to mistakes of fact.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Sections 76 and 79?
faqs.answer: Section 76 applies when a person is bound by law, while Section 79 applies when a person is justified by law.

faqs.question: Why was A protected in the illustration?
faqs.answer: Because A honestly believed, due to a mistake of fact, that Z had committed murder and that he was legally justified in arresting him.

max_punishment: Not Applicable
ai_summary: Section 79 provides that an act is not an offence when it is justified by law or when the person, because of a mistake of fact and acting in good faith, honestly believes himself justified by law in doing it. The protection does not apply to mistakes of law.

title: Accident in Doing a Lawful Act
chapter: Chapter IV - General Exceptions
section_text: Nothing is an offence which is done by accident or misfortune, and without any criminal intention or knowledge in the doing of a lawful act in a lawful manner by lawful means and with proper care and caution.
meaning: A person is not criminally liable for harm caused accidentally while performing a lawful act, provided the act was done lawfully, without criminal intention or knowledge, and with proper care and caution.
key_points: Section 80 is a General Exception.
Protects genuine accidents and misfortunes.
There must be no criminal intention.
There must be no criminal knowledge.
The act must be lawful.
The act must be performed in a lawful manner.
The act must be performed by lawful means.
Proper care and caution must be exercised.
legal_significance: Section 80 embodies the principle that criminal law punishes intentional or negligent wrongdoing, not pure accidents. When a person acts lawfully and carefully, an unforeseen accident resulting in harm does not constitute an offence.
essential_elements: The act must be accidental or due to misfortune.
There must be no criminal intention.
There must be no criminal knowledge.
The act must be lawful.
The act must be performed in a lawful manner.
The means employed must be lawful.
The person must exercise proper care and caution.

ingredients_of_section_80.accident_or_misfortune: The harmful consequence must occur unexpectedly and unintentionally.
ingredients_of_section_80.absence_of_intention: The accused must not intend the harmful result.
ingredients_of_section_80.absence_of_knowledge: The accused must not know that the act is likely to cause the harmful result.
ingredients_of_section_80.lawful_act: The act itself must be legally permissible.
ingredients_of_section_80.lawful_manner: The act must be carried out according to legal requirements.
ingredients_of_section_80.lawful_means: The method used must not be unlawful.
ingredients_of_section_80.proper_care_and_caution: Reasonable care must be exercised to prevent harm.

illustration_analysis.facts: A is working with a hatchet. The head of the hatchet unexpectedly flies off and kills a nearby person.
illustration_analysis.condition: A had exercised proper care and caution.
illustration_analysis.result: A commits no offence because the death was caused by accident during a lawful act.

additional_examples.scenario: A driver carefully follows all traffic rules, but a mechanical failure causes an accident.
additional_examples.result: Protected under Section 80 if proper care was exercised.

additional_examples.scenario: A hunter lawfully fires at a target after ensuring the area is clear, but an unseen person is accidentally injured.
additional_examples.result: May be protected if all precautions were taken.

additional_examples.scenario: A surgeon performs an operation with due care and an unforeseen complication causes death.
additional_examples.result: May be protected under Section 80 if there was no negligence.

when_section_80_does_not_apply.reason: Proper care and caution were not exercised.
The protection applies only to lawful acts.

when_section_80_does_not_apply.situation: The accused intended the harmful result.
The accused knew harm was likely.

judicial_principles: Accident is a valid defence when all statutory conditions are satisfied.
The burden is on the accused to establish the exception.
Reasonable care and caution are essential.
Negligence destroys the protection of Section 80.
The section applies only to genuine accidents.

difference_between_related_sections.section_76: Act done because a person is bound by law.
difference_between_related_sections.section_79: Act done because a person is justified by law.
difference_between_related_sections.section_80: Accident occurring during a lawful act.
difference_between_related_sections.section_81: Act likely to cause harm but done to prevent greater harm.

burden_of_proof.rule: The accused must show circumstances bringing the case within the exception.

faqs.question: What is the purpose of Section 80 IPC?
faqs.answer: To exempt genuine accidents occurring during lawful acts performed with proper care and caution.

faqs.question: Can negligence be protected under Section 80?
faqs.answer: No. Negligence prevents the application of Section 80.

faqs.question: Is every accident protected?
faqs.answer: No. Only accidents occurring during lawful acts done with proper care and caution are protected.

faqs.question: Why was A not liable in the illustration?
faqs.answer: Because the death occurred accidentally while A was performing a lawful act with proper care and caution.

max_punishment: Not Applicable
ai_summary: Section 80 provides that an act causing harm is not an offence when it occurs by accident or misfortune during the performance of a lawful act, carried out in a lawful manner, by lawful means, without criminal intention or knowledge, and with proper care and caution.

title: Act Likely to Cause Harm, but Done Without Criminal Intent, and to Prevent Other Harm
chapter: Chapter IV - General Exceptions
section_text: Nothing is an offence merely by reason of its being done with the knowledge that it is likely to cause harm, if it be done without any criminal intention to cause harm, and in good faith for the purpose of preventing or avoiding other harm to person or property.
explanation: It is a question of fact whether the harm sought to be prevented was of such a nature and so imminent as to justify or excuse the risk of causing the harm.
meaning: A person is not criminally liable for an act done in good faith to prevent a greater harm, even if he knows that his act may cause some harm. The act must be done without criminal intent and for the purpose of avoiding a more serious danger to persons or property.
key_points: Section 81 is a General Exception.
The act may be likely to cause harm.
There must be no criminal intention to cause harm.
The act must be done in good faith.
The purpose must be to prevent or avoid greater harm.
The greater harm may relate to persons or property.
The danger sought to be avoided must be real and imminent.
The justification depends on the facts of each case.
legal_significance: Section 81 is based on the doctrine of necessity. The law recognizes that in emergency situations a person may be compelled to choose between two harmful alternatives. If the act is done honestly and in good faith to avoid a greater danger, criminal liability is excluded.
essential_elements: The act is likely to cause harm.
The accused knows that harm may result.
There is no criminal intention.
The act is performed in good faith.
The purpose is to prevent greater harm.
The threatened harm is imminent.
The risk taken is reasonable in the circumstances.

ingredients_of_section_81.knowledge_of_harm: The person may know that some harm is likely to occur.
ingredients_of_section_81.absence_of_criminal_intent: There must be no intention to cause the resulting harm.
ingredients_of_section_81.good_faith: The act must be done honestly with due care and attention.
ingredients_of_section_81.greater_good: The objective must be to prevent a more serious danger.
ingredients_of_section_81.necessity: The circumstances must justify the risk taken.

doctrine_of_necessity.principle: The law may excuse a lesser harm committed to prevent a greater harm.
doctrine_of_necessity.objective: Protection of life, safety, or property.
doctrine_of_necessity.limitation: The action must be reasonable and proportionate to the danger.

illustration_a_analysis.facts: A captain must either continue his course and certainly collide with a boat carrying many passengers or change course and risk colliding with another boat carrying only two passengers.
illustration_a_analysis.action: A changes course to avoid the greater danger.
illustration_a_analysis.result: A is protected under Section 81 if he acted in good faith and the danger justified the risk.

illustration_b_analysis.facts: A pulls down houses during a major fire to stop the spread of flames.
illustration_b_analysis.result: A is not guilty of an offence if the danger was sufficiently imminent and the action was taken in good faith.

additional_examples.scenario: A doctor performs an emergency procedure that causes minor injury to save a patient's life.
additional_examples.result: Protected under Section 81 if done in good faith.

additional_examples.scenario: A breaks a locked door to rescue a child trapped inside a burning house.
additional_examples.result: Protected because the property damage was caused to prevent greater harm.

additional_examples.scenario: A driver swerves into a fence to avoid hitting a group of pedestrians.

when_section_81_does_not_apply.situation: The act is done with criminal intention.
No real or imminent danger exists.
when_section_81_does_not_apply.reason: The doctrine of necessity cannot be invoked.

when_section_81_does_not_apply.situation: The act is not done in good faith.
when_section_81_does_not_apply.reason: Good faith is an essential requirement.

when_section_81_does_not_apply.situation: The harm caused is disproportionate to the danger avoided.
when_section_81_does_not_apply.reason: The risk must be reasonable and justified.

judicial_principles: Necessity may excuse conduct that would otherwise be criminal.
Good faith is essential.
The danger must be immediate and substantial.
The harm prevented should outweigh the harm caused.
Each case must be decided on its own facts.

difference_between_related_sections.section_80: Protects accidental acts done without intention or knowledge.
difference_between_related_sections.section_81: Protects intentional acts done without criminal intent to prevent greater harm.
difference_between_related_sections.section_88: Protects acts done in good faith for a person's benefit with consent.
difference_between_related_sections.section_92: Protects acts done in good faith for a person's benefit without consent in emergencies.

burden_of_proof.rule: The accused must establish facts bringing the case within the exception.

relationship_with_other_sections.section_80: Accident in doing a lawful act.
relationship_with_other_sections.section_81: Act done to prevent greater harm.
relationship_with_other_sections.section_88: Act done with consent for benefit.
relationship_with_other_sections.section_92: Act done without consent for benefit.

faqs.question: What is the purpose of Section 81 IPC?
faqs.answer: To protect acts done in good faith to prevent a greater harm, even when some harm is likely to result.

faqs.question: Can a person knowingly cause harm and still be protected?
faqs.answer: Yes, if the act is done without criminal intent and to prevent a more serious harm.

faqs.question: What is the doctrine behind Section 81?
faqs.answer: The doctrine of necessity.

faqs.question: Is good faith necessary under Section 81?
faqs.answer: Yes. Good faith is one of the essential requirements.

max_punishment: Not Applicable
ai_summary: Section 81 provides that an act likely to cause harm is not an offence when it is done without criminal intent, in good faith, and for the purpose of preventing or avoiding a greater harm to persons or property. The justification depends on whether the danger was sufficiently imminent and serious to warrant the risk taken.

title: Act of a Child Under Seven Years of Age
chapter: Chapter IV - General Exceptions
section_text: Nothing is an offence which is done by a child under seven years of age.
meaning: A child who is below seven years of age cannot be held criminally liable for any act. The law conclusively presumes that a child under seven years lacks the mental capacity to understand the nature and consequences of his actions.
key_points: Section 82 is a General Exception.
Applies only to children below seven years of age.
Provides absolute immunity from criminal liability.
No offence can be committed by a child under seven years.
The child's intention, knowledge, or understanding is irrelevant.
The protection is complete and unconditional.
legal_significance: Section 82 is based on the principle of 'Doli Incapax' (incapable of committing a crime). The law recognizes that very young children do not possess sufficient maturity or understanding to form criminal intent and therefore cannot be punished for their acts.
essential_elements: The accused must be a child.
The child must be under seven years of age at the time of the act.
The act complained of would otherwise constitute an offence.
The age requirement alone is sufficient to attract the protection.

principle_of_doli_incapax.basis: Children below a certain age are presumed incapable of understanding criminal conduct.
principle_of_doli_incapax.nature: The presumption under Section 82 is absolute and cannot be rebutted.

age_requirement.presumption: Absolute incapacity to commit an offence

examples.scenario: A six-year-old child takes another person's property.
examples.result: The child cannot be prosecuted for theft under Section 82.

examples.scenario: A child aged five accidentally causes injury to another person.
examples.result: No criminal liability arises.

examples.scenario: A child aged six intentionally damages property.
examples.result: The child is protected under Section 82 regardless of intention.

judicial_principles: Children under seven years are absolutely exempt from criminal liability.
Criminal intention is legally irrelevant for such children.
The prosecution cannot rebut the presumption of incapacity.
Age is the determining factor.
legal_application: Determination of criminal liability of children.
Cases involving minors below seven years.

faqs.question: What is the purpose of Section 82 IPC?
faqs.answer: To provide complete immunity from criminal liability to children below seven years of age.

faqs.question: Can a child under seven be punished for a crime?
faqs.answer: No. A child under seven years cannot be held criminally liable.

faqs.question: What does 'Doli Incapax' mean?
faqs.answer: It means incapable of committing a crime due to lack of sufficient understanding.

faqs.question: Can the prosecution prove that a child under seven understood the act?
faqs.answer: No. The presumption under Section 82 is absolute and cannot be rebutted.

max_punishment: Not Applicable
ai_summary: Section 82 provides that no act done by a child under seven years of age amounts to an offence. The law conclusively presumes that such a child lacks the mental capacity required for criminal responsibility, thereby granting complete immunity from criminal prosecution.

title: Act of a Child Above Seven and Under Twelve of Immature Understanding
chapter: Chapter IV - General Exceptions
section_text: Nothing is an offence which is done by a child above seven years of age and under twelve, who has not attained sufficient maturity of understanding to judge of the nature and consequences of his conduct on that occasion.
meaning: A child between seven and twelve years of age is not criminally liable if, at the time of committing the act, he or she had not attained sufficient maturity of understanding to know the nature and consequences of the conduct. Unlike Section 82, this protection is conditional and depends on the child's mental maturity.
key_points: Section 83 is a General Exception.
Applies to children above 7 years and below 12 years.
Provides conditional immunity from criminal liability.
The child's maturity of understanding is the deciding factor.
The court examines the child's capacity on the specific occasion.
Protection is available only when sufficient maturity is absent.
legal_significance: Section 83 is based on the doctrine of 'Doli Incapax'. It recognizes that some children between seven and twelve may not possess sufficient understanding to appreciate the nature and consequences of their actions. Criminal liability depends upon the child's level of maturity rather than age alone.
essential_elements: The accused must be above seven years of age.
The accused must be below twelve years of age.
The act must otherwise amount to an offence.
The child must lack sufficient maturity of understanding.
The child must be unable to judge the nature and consequences of the conduct.

doctrine_of_doli_incapax.meaning: Incapable of committing a crime due to insufficient understanding.
doctrine_of_doli_incapax.application_under_section_83: The court determines whether the child possessed sufficient maturity at the time of the act.
doctrine_of_doli_incapax.presumption: Children between 7 and 12 are presumed incapable unless sufficient maturity is proved.

factors_considered: Behavior before and after the act

examples.scenario: An 8-year-old child takes another person's property without understanding that it is wrong.
examples.result: Protected under Section 83 if lack of maturity is proved.

examples.scenario: An 11-year-old child carefully plans and executes a theft while attempting to conceal evidence.
examples.result: May not be protected because the conduct indicates sufficient maturity.

examples.scenario: A 9-year-old child causes damage while playing without understanding the consequences.
examples.result: Likely protected under Section 83.

judicial_principles: Age alone is not decisive.
Mental maturity is the key consideration.
Each case depends on its own facts.
The prosecution may prove sufficient understanding.
The defence may establish lack of maturity.

burden_of_proof.rule: The court must determine whether sufficient maturity existed.
burden_of_proof.consideration: Evidence regarding intelligence, conduct, and understanding may be examined.

legal_application: Cases involving children aged 7 to 12 years.

faqs.question: What is the purpose of Section 83 IPC?
faqs.answer: To protect children between seven and twelve years of age who lack sufficient maturity to understand the nature and consequences of their actions.

faqs.question: Does every child under twelve get protection under Section 83?
faqs.answer: No. The child must lack sufficient maturity of understanding.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Sections 82 and 83?
faqs.answer: Section 82 provides absolute immunity to children below seven years, while Section 83 provides conditional immunity to children between seven and twelve years.

faqs.question: How does a court determine maturity?
faqs.answer: By examining the child's intelligence, behavior, understanding, and the circumstances of the case.

max_punishment: Not Applicable
ai_summary: Section 83 provides that a child above seven years and below twelve years is not criminally liable if he or she has not attained sufficient maturity of understanding to judge the nature and consequences of the conduct. Criminal responsibility depends on the child's mental maturity rather than age alone.

title: Act of a Person of Unsound Mind
chapter: Chapter IV - General Exceptions
section_text: Nothing is an offence which is done by a person who, at the time of doing it, by reason of unsoundness of mind, is incapable of knowing the nature of the act, or that he is doing what is either wrong or contrary to law.
meaning: A person is not criminally liable for an act committed while suffering from unsoundness of mind if, at the time of the act, he was incapable of understanding the nature of the act or incapable of knowing that the act was wrong or against the law.
key_points: Section 84 is a General Exception.
Provides the defence of insanity.
The unsoundness of mind must exist at the time of the act.
Medical insanity alone is not sufficient.
Legal insanity must be established.
The accused must be incapable of understanding the nature of the act or its wrongfulness.
The burden of proving the exception lies on the accused.
legal_significance: Section 84 embodies the principle that criminal liability requires a guilty mind (mens rea). A person who is incapable of understanding the nature or wrongfulness of his conduct due to unsoundness of mind lacks the mental capacity necessary for criminal responsibility.
essential_elements: The accused must suffer from unsoundness of mind.
The unsoundness must exist at the time of the act.
The accused must be incapable of knowing the nature of the act, or
The accused must be incapable of knowing that the act is wrong, or
The accused must be incapable of knowing that the act is contrary to law.

ingredients_of_section_84.unsoundness_of_mind: A mental condition affecting the person's ability to reason.
ingredients_of_section_84.time_of_act: The condition must exist when the offence is committed.
ingredients_of_section_84.incapacity_to_know_nature: The accused does not understand what he is doing.
ingredients_of_section_84.incapacity_to_know_wrongfulness: The accused does not understand that the act is morally wrong.
ingredients_of_section_84.incapacity_to_know_illegality: The accused does not understand that the act is against the law.

medical_insanity.meaning: Any mental illness diagnosed by medical experts.
medical_insanity.effect: Not automatically a defence under Section 84.

legal_insanity.meaning: A mental condition making the person incapable of understanding the nature or wrongfulness of the act.
legal_insanity.effect: May provide complete protection under Section 84.

mcnaughten_rule.principle: A person is not criminally responsible if, due to mental disease, he did not know the nature and quality of the act or did not know that it was wrong.
mcnaughten_rule.relevance: Section 84 IPC is largely based on the McNaughten Rules.

examples.scenario: A mentally ill person kills another person believing he is striking a dangerous animal.
examples.result: Protected if incapable of understanding the nature of the act.

examples.scenario: A person suffering from severe psychosis commits an act without understanding that it is wrong.
examples.result: May be protected under Section 84.

examples.scenario: A person with a mental illness carefully plans a crime and conceals evidence.
examples.result: Protection may not apply because the conduct suggests awareness of wrongdoing.

judicial_principles: Every mentally ill person is not exempt from criminal liability.
The crucial test is legal insanity, not medical insanity.
The mental condition must exist at the time of the offence.
Past or future insanity alone is insufficient.
The court examines conduct before, during, and after the act.

burden_of_proof.rule: The accused must establish circumstances bringing the case within Section 84.
burden_of_proof.standard: Preponderance of probabilities, not proof beyond reasonable doubt.

difference_between_sections.section_82: Absolute immunity for children below seven years.
difference_between_sections.section_83: Conditional immunity for children between seven and twelve years.
difference_between_sections.section_84: Immunity based on unsoundness of mind.

faqs.question: What is the purpose of Section 84 IPC?
faqs.answer: To exempt persons from criminal liability when they are incapable of understanding their actions due to unsoundness of mind.

faqs.question: Is every mentally ill person protected under Section 84?
faqs.answer: No. Only those suffering from legal insanity at the time of the act are protected.

faqs.question: What is the difference between legal insanity and medical insanity?
faqs.answer: Medical insanity refers to mental illness generally, while legal insanity refers to incapacity to understand the nature or wrongfulness of the act.

faqs.question: Who must prove the defence under Section 84?
faqs.answer: The accused must establish circumstances bringing the case within the exception.

max_punishment: Not Applicable
ai_summary: Section 84 provides a complete defence when a person, due to unsoundness of mind at the time of the act, is incapable of understanding the nature of the act or incapable of knowing that the act is wrong or contrary to law. The section is based on the principle that criminal liability requires mental capacity and a guilty mind.

title: Act of a Person Incapable of Judgment by Reason of Intoxication Caused Against His Will
chapter: Chapter IV - General Exceptions
section_text: Nothing is an offence which is done by a person who, at the time of doing it, is, by reason of intoxication, incapable of knowing the nature of the act, or that he is doing what is either wrong, or contrary to law: provided that the thing which intoxicated him was administered to him without his knowledge or against his will.
meaning: A person is not criminally liable for an act committed while intoxicated if the intoxication was caused without his knowledge or against his will, and because of that intoxication he was incapable of understanding the nature of his act or that it was wrong or contrary to law.
key_points: Section 85 is a General Exception.
Applies only to involuntary intoxication.
The intoxication must be caused without knowledge or against the person's will.
The accused must be incapable of understanding the nature of the act.
The accused must be incapable of knowing that the act was wrong or illegal.
Voluntary intoxication is not protected under this section.
legal_significance: Section 85 recognizes that a person should not be held criminally responsible when his mental faculties are impaired due to intoxication forced upon him or administered without his knowledge. Since the intoxication is involuntary, the law treats the situation similarly to legal insanity under Section 84.
essential_elements: The accused was intoxicated.
The intoxication was caused without his knowledge or against his will.
At the time of the act, the accused was incapable of knowing the nature of the act, or
The accused was incapable of knowing that the act was wrong, or
The accused was incapable of knowing that the act was contrary to law.

ingredients_of_section_85.intoxication: The accused must be under the influence of an intoxicating substance.
ingredients_of_section_85.involuntary_intoxication: The substance must have been administered without knowledge or against the will of the accused.
ingredients_of_section_85.mental_incapacity: The intoxication must be severe enough to destroy the capacity to understand the act.
ingredients_of_section_85.time_of_act: The incapacity must exist at the time the act is committed.

involuntary_intoxication.meaning: Intoxication caused without knowledge or against the will of the person.

voluntary_intoxication.meaning: Intoxication caused by willingly consuming alcohol or drugs.

examples.scenario: A person's drink is secretly mixed with an intoxicating substance, causing him to lose understanding and commit an act.
examples.result: Protected under Section 85 if the statutory conditions are satisfied.

examples.scenario: A person is forcibly administered a drug and commits an act while incapable of understanding its nature.
examples.result: Protected under Section 85.

examples.scenario: A voluntarily consumes alcohol and later commits an offence.
examples.result: Not protected under Section 85.

section_84.requirement: Incapacity to know nature or wrongfulness of the act.

section_85.cause: Intoxicating substance administered without knowledge or against will.
section_85.requirement: Incapacity to know nature or wrongfulness of the act.

comparison_with_section_86.section_85: Deals with involuntary intoxication and provides a complete defence.
comparison_with_section_86.section_86: Deals with voluntary intoxication and limits the defence.

judicial_principles: The law distinguishes between voluntary and involuntary intoxication.
A person should not suffer criminal liability for intoxication forced upon him.
The burden is on the accused to establish involuntary intoxication.
Mere intoxication is insufficient; incapacity must be proved.

burden_of_proof.rule: The accused must establish facts bringing the case within Section 85.

faqs.question: What is the purpose of Section 85 IPC?
faqs.answer: To protect persons who commit acts while incapable of judgment due to involuntary intoxication.

faqs.question: Does Section 85 apply to voluntary drinking?
faqs.answer: No. It applies only when intoxication is caused without knowledge or against the person's will.

faqs.question: Is mere intoxication enough for protection?
faqs.answer: No. The intoxication must render the person incapable of understanding the nature or wrongfulness of the act.

faqs.question: What is an example of involuntary intoxication?
faqs.answer: A person unknowingly consuming a drug mixed into his drink.

max_punishment: Not Applicable
ai_summary: Section 85 provides a complete defence where a person, due to intoxication caused without his knowledge or against his will, becomes incapable of understanding the nature of his act or that it is wrong or contrary to law. The section applies only to involuntary intoxication and not to voluntary consumption of intoxicants.

title: Offence Requiring a Particular Intent or Knowledge Committed by One Who is Intoxicated
chapter: Chapter IV - General Exceptions
section_text: In cases where an act done is not an offence unless done with a particular knowledge or intent, a person who does the act in a state of intoxication shall be liable to be dealt with as if he had the same knowledge as he would have had if he had not been intoxicated, unless the thing which intoxicated him was administered to him without his knowledge or against his will.
meaning: A person cannot escape criminal liability merely because he was intoxicated when committing an offence. If an offence requires a particular knowledge or intent, the law generally assumes that a voluntarily intoxicated person possessed the same knowledge that he would have had if sober. The exception applies only when the intoxication was involuntary.
key_points: Section 86 deals with offences requiring specific intent or knowledge.
Voluntary intoxication is generally not a defence.
The law imputes knowledge to an intoxicated person.
A voluntarily intoxicated person is treated as if he possessed normal knowledge.
Involuntary intoxication remains protected under Section 85.
The section limits the defence of intoxication.
legal_significance: Section 86 prevents offenders from avoiding criminal responsibility by claiming that intoxication prevented them from understanding their actions. The law treats voluntary intoxication as a self-induced condition and therefore does not allow it to excuse criminal conduct requiring knowledge.
essential_elements: The accused committed an act while intoxicated.
The offence requires a particular knowledge or intent.
The intoxication was voluntary.
The law presumes the accused possessed the same knowledge as a sober person.
The exception does not apply if intoxication was involuntary.

ingredients_of_section_86.intoxication: The accused must be under the influence of an intoxicating substance.
ingredients_of_section_86.specific_mental_element: The offence must require particular knowledge or intent.
ingredients_of_section_86.presumption_of_knowledge: The law assumes the accused had the same knowledge as if sober.
ingredients_of_section_86.exception: No such presumption applies when intoxication was caused without knowledge or against the will.

principle_underlying_section.voluntary_intoxication: A person who voluntarily becomes intoxicated cannot use that condition as a shield against criminal liability.
principle_underlying_section.public_policy: The law discourages irresponsible conduct caused by self-induced intoxication.

section_85.protection: Complete defence if incapacity is proved

examples.scenario: A voluntarily consumes alcohol and assaults another person.
examples.result: A cannot avoid liability by claiming intoxication.

examples.scenario: A drinks heavily and commits theft, claiming he did not realize what he was doing.
examples.result: The law treats A as possessing the knowledge of a sober person.

examples.scenario: A's drink is secretly drugged and he commits an act while incapable of understanding it.
examples.result: Section 85 may apply instead of Section 86.

judicial_principles: Voluntary intoxication is not generally a defence.
Knowledge is presumed despite intoxication.
The burden of proving involuntary intoxication lies on the accused.
Courts distinguish between intention and knowledge depending on the offence.
Each case is decided according to its facts and mental element requirements.

important_case_law_principle.rule: A drunken offender cannot ordinarily escape liability for crimes requiring knowledge merely because intoxication impaired his judgment.
important_case_law_principle.exception: Protection exists where intoxication was involuntary.

faqs.question: Can voluntary intoxication be used as a defence under IPC?
faqs.answer: Generally no. Section 86 treats the person as having the same knowledge as if sober.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Sections 85 and 86?
faqs.answer: Section 85 protects involuntary intoxication, whereas Section 86 deals with voluntary intoxication and usually denies the defence.

faqs.question: Why does the law presume knowledge despite intoxication?
faqs.answer: Because the intoxication was self-induced and public policy does not allow a person to avoid responsibility for that reason.

faqs.question: Does Section 86 completely ignore intoxication?
faqs.answer: No. Courts may consider intoxication in determining specific intent in certain cases, but knowledge is generally attributed to the accused.

max_punishment: Not Applicable
ai_summary: Section 86 provides that a person who commits an offence while voluntarily intoxicated is generally treated as having the same knowledge he would have possessed if sober. Therefore, voluntary intoxication is not usually a defence to criminal liability. The exception applies only where intoxication was caused without the person's knowledge or against his will.

title: Act Not Intended and Not Known to Be Likely to Cause Death or Grievous Hurt, Done by Consent
chapter: Chapter IV - General Exceptions
section_text: Nothing which is not intended to cause death, or grievous hurt, and which is not known by the doer to be likely to cause death or grievous hurt, is an offence by reason of any harm which it may cause, or be intended by the doer to cause, to any person, above eighteen years of age, who has given consent, whether express or implied, to suffer that harm; or by reason of any harm which it may be known by the doer to be likely to cause to any such person who has consented to take the risk of that harm.
meaning: A person does not commit an offence when causing harm to another adult who has voluntarily consented to suffer that harm or to take the risk of that harm, provided the act is not intended and is not known to be likely to cause death or grievous hurt.
key_points: Section 87 is a General Exception.
Valid consent is the foundation of this defence.
The person giving consent must be above 18 years of age.
Consent may be express or implied.
The act must not be intended to cause death.
The act must not be intended to cause grievous hurt.
The doer must not know that death or grievous hurt is likely.
The consented harm must be within lawful limits.
legal_significance: Section 87 recognizes the principle that an adult may voluntarily consent to ordinary risks of harm in lawful activities such as sports, games, and recreational activities. Criminal liability is excluded when the harm occurs within the scope of that consent and without intention or knowledge of serious injury.
essential_elements: The victim must be above eighteen years of age.
The victim must give valid consent.
Consent may be express or implied.
The act must not be intended to cause death.
The act must not be intended to cause grievous hurt.
The accused must not know that death or grievous hurt is likely.
The harm must arise within the scope of the consent given.

ingredients_of_section_87.consent: The injured person must voluntarily agree to suffer the harm or risk.
ingredients_of_section_87.age_requirement: The consenting person must be above eighteen years.
ingredients_of_section_87.absence_of_intention: No intention to cause death or grievous hurt.
ingredients_of_section_87.absence_of_knowledge: No knowledge that death or grievous hurt is likely.
ingredients_of_section_87.lawful_activity: The act generally arises in socially accepted activities.

express_consent.meaning: Consent clearly communicated through words or writing.
express_consent.example: Agreeing to participate in a boxing match.

implied_consent.meaning: Consent inferred from conduct or circumstances.
implied_consent.example: Participating in a football game where physical contact is expected.

illustration_analysis.facts: A and Z agree to fence with each other for amusement.
illustration_analysis.consent: Each person impliedly consents to ordinary risks of fencing.
illustration_analysis.result: If A fairly injures Z during the game, A commits no offence.

additional_examples.scenario: Two adults participate in a wrestling match and one suffers a minor injury.
additional_examples.result: Protected under Section 87 if the injury occurs within the accepted rules.

additional_examples.scenario: A player accidentally injures another player during a lawful football match.
A deliberately attacks an opponent with intent to cause grievous hurt during a sports event.

when_section_87_does_not_apply.situation: Victim is below 18 years of age.
The act is intended to cause death.
The act is intended to cause grievous hurt.
The accused knows death or grievous hurt is likely.

judicial_principles: Consent can excuse certain harms.
The consent must be voluntary and informed.
The defence does not extend to serious bodily harm intended by the accused.
Sports and recreational activities commonly involve implied consent.
The scope of consent is determined by the circumstances.

difference_between_related_sections.section_87: Consent by a person above 18 years regarding harm not intended to cause death or grievous hurt.
difference_between_related_sections.section_88: Act done in good faith for a person's benefit with consent.
difference_between_related_sections.section_89: Act done in good faith for the benefit of a child or person of unsound mind.

burden_of_proof.rule: The accused must establish circumstances bringing the case within the exception.

faqs.question: What is the purpose of Section 87 IPC?
faqs.answer: To protect acts causing harm where an adult has voluntarily consented to the risk and there is no intention or knowledge of causing death or grievous hurt.

faqs.question: Can a minor give valid consent under Section 87?
faqs.answer: No. The person giving consent must be above eighteen years of age.

faqs.question: Does Section 87 protect acts intended to cause grievous hurt?
faqs.answer: No. The section specifically excludes such acts.

faqs.question: Why was A not liable in the fencing illustration?
faqs.answer: Because both participants impliedly consented to the ordinary risks of fencing and A acted fairly without intending serious injury.

max_punishment: Not Applicable
ai_summary: Section 87 provides that an act causing harm to a person above eighteen years of age is not an offence when that person has consented to suffer the harm or its risk, provided the act is not intended and is not known to be likely to cause death or grievous hurt. The section commonly applies to lawful sports and recreational activities involving accepted risks.

title: Act Not Intended to Cause Death, Done by Consent in Good Faith for Person's Benefit
chapter: Chapter IV - General Exceptions
section_text: Nothing, which is not intended to cause death, is an offence by reason of any harm which it may cause, or be intended by the doer to cause, or be known by the doer to be likely to cause, to any person for whose benefit it is done in good faith, and who has given a consent, whether express or implied, to suffer that harm, or to take the risk of that harm.
meaning: A person is not criminally liable for causing harm to another if the act is done in good faith for that person's benefit, with the person's consent, and without any intention to cause death, even though the act may involve risk or may be likely to cause harm.
key_points: Section 88 is a General Exception.
The act must be done in good faith.
The act must be for the benefit of the person affected.
Valid consent is required.
Consent may be express or implied.
There must be no intention to cause death.
The act may involve risk of harm or even risk of death.
The exception commonly applies to medical treatment.
legal_significance: Section 88 protects persons who act for another's benefit with consent, especially doctors and surgeons performing medical procedures. The law recognizes that certain beneficial acts may involve risks, but such acts should not attract criminal liability when done honestly, carefully, and without intent to cause death.
essential_elements: The act must be done for the benefit of another person.
The act must be performed in good faith.
The affected person must consent.
Consent may be express or implied.
There must be no intention to cause death.
The act may cause harm or involve known risks.

ingredients_of_section_88.good_faith: The act must be performed honestly with due care and attention as required by Section 52 IPC.
ingredients_of_section_88.benefit_of_person: The primary purpose must be the welfare, health, or advantage of the person concerned.
ingredients_of_section_88.consent: The person must voluntarily agree to the act or risk.
ingredients_of_section_88.absence_of_intention_to_cause_death: Death must not be the intended result.

illustration_analysis.facts: A surgeon performs a dangerous operation on Z, knowing there is a risk of death.
illustration_analysis.consent: Z has consented to the operation.
illustration_analysis.result: A commits no offence because the operation was done in good faith for Z's benefit and without intention to cause death.

common_applications.example: A surgeon performs a risky heart operation with the patient's consent.
A doctor administers a treatment involving serious side effects to save a patient's life.
A physician recommends and performs a procedure that causes temporary pain for long-term benefit.

examples.scenario: A doctor amputates a patient's infected leg with consent to save the patient's life.
examples.result: Protected under Section 88.

examples.scenario: A dentist extracts a painful tooth after obtaining consent.
examples.result: Protected under Section 88.

examples.scenario: A surgeon performs a life-saving operation that unexpectedly results in complications.
examples.result: Protected if done in good faith and without intent to cause death.

when_section_88_does_not_apply.situation: The act is done with intention to cause death.
when_section_88_does_not_apply.reason: The section expressly excludes such acts.

when_section_88_does_not_apply.situation: The act is not for the person's benefit.
The act is not done in good faith.
when_section_88_does_not_apply.reason: Good faith is necessary for protection.

judicial_principles: Consent can justify harm when given for beneficial purposes.
Medical professionals are protected when acting in good faith.
The intention behind the act is crucial.
Good faith requires due care and attention.
The benefit to the person must be genuine.

section_87.focus: Consent to ordinary harm or risk.

section_88.focus: Benefit of the person with consent.
section_88.purpose: Protection of beneficial acts such as medical treatment.

section_89.focus: Benefit of children or persons of unsound mind.
section_89.consent: Given by guardian or lawful custodian.

burden_of_proof.rule: The accused must show circumstances bringing the case within the exception.

faqs.question: What is the purpose of Section 88 IPC?
faqs.answer: To protect acts done in good faith for a person's benefit with that person's consent.

faqs.question: Why are doctors often protected under Section 88?
faqs.answer: Because medical treatments may involve risks but are performed in good faith for the patient's benefit.

faqs.question: Can Section 88 apply if death is intended?
faqs.answer: No. The section applies only when there is no intention to cause death.

faqs.question: Is consent necessary under Section 88?
faqs.answer: Yes. Express or implied consent is an essential requirement.

max_punishment: Not Applicable
ai_summary: Section 88 provides that an act done in good faith for a person's benefit, with that person's consent and without intention to cause death, is not an offence even if it causes harm or carries a known risk of harm. The section is particularly important in protecting doctors and others performing beneficial acts involving reasonable risks.

title: Act Done in Good Faith for Benefit of Child or Person of Unsound Mind, by or by Consent of Guardian
chapter: Chapter IV - General Exceptions
section_text: Nothing which is done in good faith for the benefit of a person under twelve years of age, or of unsound mind, by or by consent, either express or implied, of the guardian or other person having lawful charge of that person, is an offence by reason of any harm which it may cause, or be intended by the doer to cause or be known by the doer to be likely to cause to that person.
meaning: A person is not criminally liable for causing harm to a child below twelve years of age or a person of unsound mind when the act is done in good faith for that person's benefit and with the consent of the guardian or lawful caretaker. However, this protection is subject to important limitations.
key_points: Section 89 is a General Exception.
Applies to children below 12 years of age.
Applies to persons of unsound mind.
The act must be done in good faith.
The act must be for the benefit of the person concerned.
Consent of the guardian or lawful caretaker is required.
The section contains four important provisos limiting its application.
Commonly applies to medical treatment and welfare measures.
legal_significance: Section 89 protects actions taken for the welfare of children and persons of unsound mind who may be incapable of giving valid consent themselves. The law permits guardians and caretakers to authorize beneficial acts, especially medical treatment, while imposing safeguards against abuse.
essential_elements: The person must be under twelve years of age or of unsound mind.
The act must be done in good faith.
The act must be for the person's benefit.
The guardian or lawful caretaker must consent.
The act must not fall within any of the statutory exceptions.

persons_covered.child: Any person below twelve years of age.
persons_covered.person_of_unsound_mind: A person incapable of making rational decisions due to mental incapacity.

consent_requirement.express_consent: Direct permission given by the guardian or lawful caretaker.
consent_requirement.guardian: Parent, legal guardian, or person having lawful charge of the individual.

first_proviso.rule: The exception does not permit intentional causing of death or an attempt to cause death.

second_proviso.rule: The exception does not apply where the act is known to be likely to cause death unless done to prevent death, grievous hurt, or cure a grievous disease or infirmity.

third_proviso.rule: The exception does not permit voluntary causing of grievous hurt unless necessary to prevent death, grievous hurt, or cure a grievous disease or infirmity.

fourth_proviso.rule: The exception does not extend to abetment of offences that are not themselves protected under this section.

illustration_analysis.facts: A father allows a surgeon to operate on his child suffering from stone disease.
illustration_analysis.result: The father and surgeon are protected because the act is done in good faith for the child's benefit.

common_applications.example: A guardian consents to surgery for a child.
A guardian authorizes treatment for a person of unsound mind.

examples.scenario: A parent consents to a risky surgery necessary to save a child's life.
examples.result: Protected under Section 89.

examples.scenario: A guardian authorizes psychiatric treatment for a person of unsound mind.
examples.result: Protected if done in good faith.

examples.scenario: A person intentionally causes grievous hurt to a child without medical necessity.
examples.result: Section 89 does not apply.

when_section_89_does_not_apply.situation: Voluntary causing of grievous hurt without necessity.
Act not done in good faith.
Act not done for the person's benefit.

judicial_principles: Welfare of the child or person of unsound mind is paramount.
Good faith is judged by due care and attention.
Guardian's consent does not authorize harmful acts beyond statutory limits.
The exception is interpreted strictly because vulnerable persons are involved.

section_89.person_concerned: Child below 12 or person of unsound mind

burden_of_proof.rule: The accused must establish facts bringing the case within the exception.

faqs.question: What is the purpose of Section 89 IPC?
faqs.answer: To protect acts done in good faith for the benefit of children under twelve years and persons of unsound mind with guardian consent.

faqs.question: Can a guardian consent to intentional killing under Section 89?
faqs.answer: No. Intentional causing of death is expressly prohibited.

faqs.question: Why is guardian consent required?
faqs.answer: Because children and persons of unsound mind may be incapable of giving valid legal consent.

faqs.question: Does Section 89 protect medical operations involving risk?
faqs.answer: Yes, when performed in good faith for the person's benefit and within the limits of the provisos.

max_punishment: Not Applicable
ai_summary: Section 89 provides protection for acts done in good faith for the benefit of children under twelve years of age or persons of unsound mind, with the consent of their guardian or lawful caretaker. The section is commonly applied to medical treatment and welfare measures but does not permit intentional causing of death, unnecessary grievous hurt, or other acts prohibited by its provisos.

title: Consent Known to be Given Under Fear or Misconception
chapter: Chapter IV - General Exceptions
section_text: A consent is not such a consent as is intended by any section of this Code, if the consent is given by a person under fear of injury, or under a misconception of fact, and if the person doing the act knows, or has reason to believe, that the consent was given in consequence of such fear or misconception; or if the consent is given by a person who, from unsoundness of mind, or intoxication, is unable to understand the nature and consequence of that to which he gives his consent; or unless the contrary appears from the context, if the consent is given by a person who is under twelve years of age.
meaning: Section 90 explains situations where consent is not legally valid. Consent obtained through fear, misconception of fact, unsoundness of mind, intoxication, or from a child under twelve years of age is not considered valid consent under the IPC.
key_points: Section 90 does not define consent; it defines invalid consent.
Consent obtained through fear of injury is not valid.
Consent given under a misconception of fact is not valid.
The accused must know or have reason to believe that such fear or misconception exists.
Consent given by a person of unsound mind may be invalid.
Consent given by an intoxicated person may be invalid.
Consent of a child below twelve years is generally invalid.
Valid consent must be free, informed and voluntary.
legal_significance: Section 90 plays a crucial role throughout criminal law because many offences depend upon consent. It establishes that consent must be genuine, voluntary, and informed. If consent is obtained improperly or from a person incapable of understanding its consequences, the law treats it as no consent at all.
essential_elements: Consent obtained under fear of injury is invalid.
Consent obtained under misconception of fact is invalid.
The accused must know or reasonably believe that such fear or misconception exists.
Consent by an intoxicated person may be invalid if understanding is absent.
Consent by a person of unsound mind may be invalid if understanding is absent.
Consent by a child below twelve years is generally invalid.

fear_of_injury.meaning: Consent obtained by threat, coercion, or fear.
fear_of_injury.example: A person agrees to an act because he is threatened with violence.
