However, the defender may lawfully cause any necessary harm short of death, subject to the restrictions contained in Section 99.

title: Commencement and Continuance of the Right of Private Defence of Property
chapter: Chapter IV - General Exceptions
section_type: Right of Private Defence of Property
section_text: The right of private defence of property commences when a reasonable apprehension of danger to the property commences. The right of private defence of property against theft continues till the offender has effected his retreat with the property or either the assistance of the public authorities is obtained, or the property has been recovered. The right of private defence of property against robbery continues as long as the offender causes or attempts to cause death, hurt, wrongful restraint, or as long as the fear of instant death, instant hurt, or instant personal restraint continues. The right of private defence of property against criminal trespass or mischief continues as long as the offender continues in the commission of criminal trespass or mischief. The right of private defence of property against house-breaking by night continues as long as the house-trespass begun by such house-breaking continues.
meaning: Section 105 explains when the right of private defence of property begins and when it ends. The duration of the right depends upon the nature of the offence committed against the property.
key_points: Section 105 deals with commencement and continuance of defence of property.
The right begins when there is a reasonable apprehension of danger to property.
The duration of the right varies according to the offence.
Different rules apply for theft, robbery, mischief, criminal trespass, and house-breaking by night.
The right exists only while the danger continues.
The right ends when the legal conditions for continuation cease.
The section prevents misuse of private defence after the danger has ended.
legal_significance: Section 105 determines the exact period during which a person may lawfully exercise the right of private defence of property. It ensures that defensive force is used only while protection is genuinely required and not as retaliation after the offence has ended.
essential_elements: There must be danger or apprehension of danger to property.
The apprehension must be reasonable.
The offence must affect property.
The right continues only for the period specified by law.
The right ends when the relevant danger ceases.

commencement_of_right.rule: The right begins when a reasonable apprehension of danger to property arises.
commencement_of_right.basis: Reasonable fear of unlawful interference with property.

continues_until: The offender has escaped with the property.
Assistance of public authorities is obtained.
The offender stops causing or attempting to cause death, hurt, or wrongful restraint.
The fear of instant death, hurt, or restraint ends.
The house-trespass resulting from the house-breaking continues.

theft.duration: Until escape, recovery of property, or police assistance.

criminal_trespass.duration: As long as the trespass continues.

mischief.duration: As long as the damage-causing act continues.

house_breaking_by_night.nature: House-trespass committed by night through house-breaking.

examples.scenario: A steals B's motorcycle and attempts to escape.
examples.result: B's right of private defence continues until the property is recovered, the thief escapes, or police assistance is obtained.

examples.scenario: A commits robbery while threatening B with a knife.
examples.result: The right continues while the threat of violence remains.

examples.scenario: A remains unlawfully on B's land.
examples.result: The right continues as long as the trespass continues.

examples.scenario: A is breaking into B's house at night.
examples.result: The right continues as long as the resulting house-trespass continues.

when_right_ends.situation: The offender escapes with stolen property.

judicial_principles: Private defence is preventive, not punitive.
The right begins before actual loss occurs if danger is reasonably apprehended.
The right exists only while the threat continues.
Once the danger ends, the right ends.
Any force used after the danger ceases may become unlawful.

relationship_with_other_sections.section_97: Right of defence of body and property.
relationship_with_other_sections.section_103: Cases where defence of property extends to causing death.
relationship_with_other_sections.section_104: Cases where defence extends only to non-fatal harm.
relationship_with_other_sections.section_105: Commencement and continuance of defence of property.

section_102.subject: Commencement and continuance of private defence of the body.

section_105.subject: Commencement and continuance of private defence of property.

offence_category: Right of Private Defence of Property

faqs.question: When does the right of private defence of property begin?
faqs.answer: It begins as soon as a reasonable apprehension of danger to property arises.

faqs.question: How long does the right continue against theft?
faqs.answer: Until the thief escapes with the property, police assistance is obtained, or the property is recovered.

faqs.question: How long does the right continue against criminal trespass?
faqs.answer: As long as the trespass continues.

faqs.question: Can the right be exercised after the danger has ended?
faqs.answer: No. The right ends when the legally recognized danger ends.

max_punishment: Not Applicable
ai_summary: Section 105 IPC explains when the right of private defence of property begins and how long it continues. The right starts when there is a reasonable apprehension of danger to property and continues for different periods depending on whether the offence is theft, robbery, mischief, criminal trespass, or house-breaking by night. The right ends when the relevant danger or offence comes to an end.

title: Right of Private Defence Against Deadly Assault When There is Risk of Harm to Innocent Person
chapter: Chapter IV - General Exceptions
section_text: If in the exercise of the right of private defence against an assault which reasonably causes the apprehension of death, the defender is so situated that he cannot effectually exercise that right without risk of harm to an innocent person, his right of private defence extends to the running of that risk.
meaning: Section 106 protects a person who is facing a deadly assault and cannot save himself without risking harm to an innocent person. In such exceptional circumstances, the law allows the defender to exercise private defence even though an innocent person may unintentionally be harmed.
key_points: Section 106 applies only in cases of deadly assault.
There must be a reasonable apprehension of death.
The defender must have no safe alternative.
The risk to an innocent person must be unavoidable.
The harm to the innocent person must be incidental and unintended.
The right exists to preserve the defender's life.
The section applies only in exceptional situations.
legal_significance: Section 106 recognizes that in extreme situations a person threatened with death cannot be expected to sacrifice his own life merely because an innocent person may accidentally be harmed while defending him. The law permits the defender to take necessary action when no safer option exists.
essential_elements: There must be an assault.
The assault must create a reasonable apprehension of death.
The defender must possess a valid right of private defence.
The defender cannot effectively exercise the right without risking harm to an innocent person.
The risk must be unavoidable.
The defender acts solely for self-protection.

reasonable_apprehension.requirement: A reasonable person would fear death in the circumstances.

absence_of_alternative.requirement: No safer method of defence is available.

incidental_risk.requirement: The harm to the innocent person is accidental and not intended.

illustration_analysis.facts: A is attacked by a mob attempting to murder him. Children are mixed with the mob.
illustration_analysis.problem: A cannot defend himself by firing without risking injury to the children.
illustration_analysis.result: A commits no offence if a child is accidentally harmed.
illustration_analysis.reason: The risk was unavoidable while exercising a lawful right of private defence.

principle_of_section_106.core_principle: The law does not require a person facing death to surrender his life merely because innocent persons may accidentally be endangered.
principle_of_section_106.objective: Protection of life in unavoidable emergency situations.

examples.scenario: A terrorist uses innocent civilians as human shields while attacking B.
examples.result: B may exercise private defence if no other effective option exists.

examples.scenario: A mob surrounds C and attempts to kill him while innocent persons are among the crowd.
examples.result: C may defend himself even if accidental harm to innocent persons cannot be avoided.

examples.scenario: An attacker hides behind a hostage while attempting to kill D.
examples.result: D may still exercise private defence if no safer alternative exists.

when_section_106_does_not_apply.situation: The assault does not create apprehension of death.
when_section_106_does_not_apply.reason: The section applies only to deadly assaults.
The risk to innocent persons is not unavoidable.

when_section_106_does_not_apply.situation: The defender intentionally targets the innocent person.
when_section_106_does_not_apply.reason: The protection applies only to incidental harm.

when_section_106_does_not_apply.situation: The right of private defence itself does not exist.
when_section_106_does_not_apply.reason: Section 106 depends upon a valid right of private defence.

judicial_principles: Necessity is the foundation of the defence.
The danger must be immediate and deadly.
The harm to innocent persons must be unavoidable.
The defender's action must be proportionate to the threat.
The law values the right of self-preservation.

relationship_with_other_sections.section_100: Cases where defence of body extends to causing death.
relationship_with_other_sections.section_102: Commencement and continuance of defence of body.
relationship_with_other_sections.section_106: Risk to innocent persons during exercise of private defence.

section_100.focus: When death may be caused to the assailant.

section_106.focus: When accidental risk to innocent persons is unavoidable during lawful self-defence.

faqs.question: What is the purpose of Section 106 IPC?
faqs.answer: To protect a person who must risk accidental harm to an innocent person while defending himself against a deadly assault.

faqs.question: Does Section 106 allow intentional harm to innocent persons?
faqs.answer: No. The harm must be incidental and unavoidable.

faqs.question: Is the section applicable to every assault?
faqs.answer: No. It applies only when there is a reasonable apprehension of death.

faqs.question: Why does the law provide this exception?
faqs.answer: Because a person facing death cannot always defend himself without creating unavoidable risks to others.

max_punishment: Not Applicable
ai_summary: Section 106 IPC provides that when a person is facing a deadly assault and cannot effectively exercise the right of private defence without risking harm to an innocent person, the law permits him to take that risk. The section applies only where the danger is serious, the risk is unavoidable, and the harm to the innocent person is incidental rather than intentional.

title: Abetment of a Thing
chapter: Chapter V - Of Abetment
section_text: A person abets the doing of a thing, who— First: Instigates any person to do that thing; or Secondly: Engages with one or more other persons in any conspiracy for the doing of that thing, if an act or illegal omission takes place in pursuance of that conspiracy and in order to the doing of that thing; or Thirdly: Intentionally aids, by any act or illegal omission, the doing of that thing.
meaning: Section 107 defines 'abetment'. A person commits abetment when he encourages, conspires with others, or intentionally assists another person in committing an act or offence.
key_points: Section 107 is the foundation of the law relating to abetment.
Abetment can occur even if the abettor does not personally commit the act.
There are three modes of abetment.
Instigation is one form of abetment.
Conspiracy coupled with an act or omission is another form.
Intentional aid is the third form.
Mens rea (guilty intention) is essential.
legal_significance: Section 107 extends criminal liability to persons who encourage, support, facilitate, or assist the commission of offences. It ensures that not only the principal offender but also those who contribute to the offence are held responsible.

first_instigation.definition: Provoking, urging, encouraging, inciting, or stimulating another person to commit an act.
essential_requirements: There must be encouragement or provocation.
The instigation may be express or implied.
The instigator must intend the consequence.

second_conspiracy.definition: Agreement between two or more persons to commit an act, followed by an act or illegal omission in furtherance of that agreement.
essential_requirements: There must be an act or illegal omission.
The act or omission must be in pursuance of the conspiracy.

third_intentional_aid.definition: Knowingly helping or facilitating the commission of an act.
essential_requirements: The aid may be by act or illegal omission.

explanation_1.rule: A person who intentionally causes or procures an act through wilful misrepresentation or concealment of a material fact is deemed to instigate the act.

illustration_analysis.facts: A police officer is authorized to arrest Z. B knows that C is not Z but falsely tells the officer that C is Z.
illustration_analysis.legal_effect: B has abetted the arrest of C by instigation.
illustration_analysis.reason: B intentionally misrepresented a material fact.

explanation_2.rule: A person who does anything before or during the commission of an act to facilitate it is said to aid the doing of that act.
explanation_2.importance: Facilitation itself may amount to abetment.

essential_elements_of_abetment: Existence of an act to be done.
Knowledge or intention of the abettor.
Connection between the abettor's conduct and the act.

examples.scenario: A encourages B to commit theft.
examples.result: A abets the theft.

examples.scenario: A and B plan a robbery and purchase weapons for the plan.
examples.result: Abetment by conspiracy.

examples.scenario: A knowingly supplies tools to B for house-breaking.
examples.result: A abets the offence.

examples.scenario: A deliberately hides information that would prevent an offence.
examples.result: May amount to abetment.

judicial_principles: Abetment requires guilty intention.
Instigation may be direct or indirect.
Active participation is not always necessary.
Intentional facilitation amounts to aid.
The role of the accused must be examined from the surrounding circumstances.

relationship_with_other_sections.section_109: Punishment of abetment when no separate punishment is provided.
relationship_with_other_sections.section_110: Liability where the abetted person acts with different intention.
relationship_with_other_sections.section_111: Liability for acts different from the act abetted.

section_107.requirement: Conspiracy plus an act or illegal omission in pursuance of it.

criminal_conspiracy_section_120A.requirement: Agreement itself may be sufficient in many cases.

faqs.question: What are the three forms of abetment under Section 107 IPC?
faqs.answer: Instigation, conspiracy, and intentional aid.

faqs.question: Can a person be guilty of abetment without committing the offence himself?
faqs.answer: Yes. Abetment creates independent criminal liability.

faqs.question: What is instigation?
faqs.answer: Encouraging, provoking, or inciting another person to commit an act.

faqs.question: What is intentional aid?
faqs.answer: Knowingly helping or facilitating the commission of an act.

max_punishment: Not Applicable (Definition Section)
ai_summary: Section 107 IPC defines abetment. A person abets an act if he instigates another person to do it, engages in a conspiracy and an act or illegal omission occurs in furtherance of that conspiracy, or intentionally aids the commission of the act. The section forms the foundation of criminal liability for persons who encourage, facilitate, or assist offences.

title: Abettor
chapter: Chapter V - Of Abetment
section_text: A person abets an offence who abets either the commission of an offence, or the commission of an act which would be an offence if committed by a person capable by law of committing an offence with the same intention or knowledge as that of the abettor.
meaning: Section 108 defines an 'abettor'. An abettor is a person who instigates, conspires, or intentionally aids another person in committing an offence or an act that would amount to an offence if committed by a legally capable person.
key_points: Section 108 defines who is an abettor.
Abetment may exist even if the offence is not completed.
The person abetted need not be legally capable of committing the offence.
A child, lunatic, or person acting under mistake can be used as an instrument of crime.
Abetment of abetment is also an offence.
Conspiracy-based abetment does not require direct contact between all conspirators.
Criminal intention of the abettor is the important factor.
legal_significance: Section 108 expands criminal liability beyond the principal offender and punishes persons who encourage, facilitate, or influence the commission of offences. It ensures that masterminds and indirect participants cannot escape punishment merely because they did not personally commit the act.
essential_elements: There must be abetment as defined in Section 107.
There must be an offence or an act which would be an offence if committed by a legally capable person.
The abettor must possess the required intention or knowledge.
The offence need not actually be completed.

explanation_1.rule: Abetment of an illegal omission may amount to an offence even if the abettor himself had no legal duty to perform the omitted act.
explanation_1.importance: A person may abet another's unlawful failure to act.

explanation_2.rule: The offence of abetment is complete even if the act abetted is not committed.
explanation_2.importance: Actual commission of the offence is not necessary.

illustration_a.facts: A instigates B to murder C. B refuses.
illustration_a.result: A is still guilty of abetment of murder.

illustration_b.facts: A instigates B to murder D. B stabs D, but D survives.
illustration_b.result: A is guilty of abetting murder despite the failure of the intended result.

explanation_3.rule: The person abetted need not be legally capable of committing an offence.
explanation_3.importance: The abettor's guilt does not depend on the legal capacity of the person used.

persons_who_may_be_used_as_instruments: Children below the age of criminal responsibility.
Persons acting under mistake of fact.

illustration_c.facts: A instigates B, a person of unsound mind, to set fire to a house.
illustration_c.result: B commits no offence, but A is guilty of abetting arson.

illustration_d.facts: A deceives B into taking property belonging to Z, making B believe it belongs to A.
illustration_d.result: B commits no theft due to lack of dishonest intention, but A is guilty of abetting theft.

explanation_4.rule: Abetment of an offence is itself an offence; therefore, abetment of abetment is also punishable.
explanation_4.importance: Liability extends through multiple levels of instigation.

illustration_of_explanation_4.facts: A instigates B to instigate C to murder Z. C commits murder.
illustration_of_explanation_4.result: Both A and B may be punished for abetment leading to murder.

explanation_5.rule: For abetment by conspiracy, direct communication with the principal offender is unnecessary.
explanation_5.importance: Participation in the conspiracy is sufficient.

illustration_of_explanation_5.facts: A and B plan to poison Z. C agrees to procure poison without knowing A personally. The poison is supplied and Z dies.
illustration_of_explanation_5.result: C is liable as an abettor because he participated in the conspiracy.

important_principles: Abetment may exist even without completion of the offence.
The person abetted need not possess criminal intent.
Abetment can be direct or indirect.
Participation in a criminal plan attracts liability.
what_is_not_required: Legal capacity of the person abetted.

examples.scenario: A persuades a child to steal jewellery.
examples.result: A is guilty of abetment even though the child may not be criminally liable.

examples.scenario: A encourages B to commit murder, but B refuses.
examples.result: A is guilty of abetment.

examples.scenario: A supplies tools and instructions for a burglary.
examples.result: A may be liable as an abettor.

examples.scenario: A participates in a conspiracy but never meets the final offender.
examples.result: A may still be liable for abetment.

judicial_principles: Mens rea of the abettor is the foundation of liability.
Abetment is a substantive offence.
Indirect participation may attract criminal responsibility.
Criminal liability extends to masterminds behind offences.
The law punishes encouragement of crime as well as commission of crime.

relationship_with_other_sections.section_109: Punishment for abetment where no express punishment exists.
relationship_with_other_sections.section_110: Liability where person abetted acts with different intention.
relationship_with_other_sections.section_111: Liability for different acts done than those abetted.

faqs.question: Who is an abettor under Section 108 IPC?
faqs.answer: A person who abets the commission of an offence or an act that would be an offence if committed by a legally capable person.

faqs.question: Is completion of the offence necessary for abetment?
faqs.answer: No. Abetment may be complete even if the offence is never committed.

faqs.question: Can a person abet a child or insane person?
faqs.answer: Yes. The abettor remains liable even if the person used cannot commit an offence in law.

faqs.question: Is abetment of abetment punishable?
faqs.answer: Yes. Explanation 4 expressly makes it an offence.

max_punishment: Depends on the offence abetted
ai_summary: Section 108 IPC defines an abettor as a person who abets the commission of an offence or an act that would amount to an offence if committed by a legally capable person. The section clarifies that the offence need not actually be committed, the person abetted need not be criminally liable, and even abetment of abetment is punishable. The focus is on the guilty intention and participation of the abettor.

title: Abetment in India of Offences Outside India
chapter: Chapter V - Of Abetment
section_text: A person abets an offence within the meaning of this Code who, in India, abets the commission of any act outside and beyond India which would constitute an offence if committed in India.
meaning: Section 108A provides that a person who is present in India and abets the commission of an act outside India can be held liable for abetment if that act would be an offence had it been committed in India.
key_points: Section 108A deals with cross-border abetment.
The abetment must take place in India.
The actual offence may occur outside India.
The act committed abroad must be one that would be an offence in India.
The abettor can be punished under Indian law.
Physical presence of the abettor in India is essential.
The provision extends the reach of Indian criminal law beyond territorial boundaries.
legal_significance: Section 108A prevents offenders from escaping liability by planning, encouraging, or assisting crimes from within India while ensuring that the actual offence is committed abroad. It strengthens India's jurisdiction over transnational criminal activities.
essential_elements: The abetment must occur in India.
The act abetted must occur outside India.
The act must amount to an offence if committed in India.
There must be abetment as defined under Section 107.
The abettor must possess the necessary criminal intention or knowledge.

abetment_in_india.requirement: The instigation, conspiracy, or intentional aid must occur within India.

offence_outside_india.requirement: The principal act is committed beyond Indian territory.

equivalent_offence_test.requirement: The act committed abroad must be punishable if committed in India.

illustration_analysis.facts: A, in India, instigates B, a foreigner in Goa (outside British India at the time), to commit murder there.
illustration_analysis.result: A is guilty of abetting murder.
illustration_analysis.reason: The abetment occurred in India and murder is an offence under Indian law.

scope_of_section: Terrorist financing or assistance from India.
Human trafficking arrangements made in India.

examples.scenario: A, sitting in India, instructs B in another country to murder C.
examples.result: A is liable for abetment under Section 108A.

examples.scenario: A in India helps plan a robbery to be committed abroad.
examples.result: A may be liable if the act would be an offence in India.

examples.scenario: A in India provides funds and directions for a criminal act in another country.
examples.result: Section 108A may apply.

examples.scenario: A sends instructions from India to commit arson in a foreign country.
examples.result: A can be prosecuted for abetment.

when_section_108A_applies.result: Jurisdiction may arise under Indian law.

when_section_108A_applies.condition: The foreign act would be an offence in India.

when_section_108A_does_not_apply.reason: The section specifically requires abetment in India.

when_section_108A_does_not_apply.situation: The act would not be an offence under Indian law.
when_section_108A_does_not_apply.reason: The equivalent offence requirement is not satisfied.

judicial_principles: Criminal liability can extend beyond territorial boundaries.
The place of abetment is crucial.
Indian law focuses on the conduct of the abettor within India.
The offence abroad is assessed by Indian legal standards.
The provision helps combat transnational crime.

relationship_with_other_sections.section_108A: Extends abetment liability to offences committed outside India.
relationship_with_other_sections.section_109: Provides punishment for abetment where no separate punishment is prescribed.

section_108A.scope: Special provision dealing with offences committed outside India.

faqs.question: What is the purpose of Section 108A IPC?
faqs.answer: To punish persons in India who abet offences committed outside India.

faqs.question: Must the actual offence occur in India?
faqs.answer: No. The offence may occur outside India.

faqs.question: What is the key requirement for Section 108A?
faqs.answer: The abetment must take place in India and the foreign act must be an offence under Indian law.

faqs.question: Does Section 108A apply to international criminal conspiracies?
faqs.answer: Yes, if the requirements of abetment are satisfied.

max_punishment: Depends on the offence abetted
ai_summary: Section 108A IPC extends the law of abetment to situations where a person in India instigates, conspires, or intentionally aids the commission of an act outside India. If the act would constitute an offence had it been committed in India, the person who abetted it from India is liable under Indian law, even though the actual offence occurred abroad.

title: Punishment of Abetment if the Act Abetted is Committed in Consequence and When No Express Provision is Made for Its Punishment
chapter: Chapter V - Of Abetment
section_text: Whoever abets any offence shall, if the act abetted is committed in consequence of the abetment, and no express provision is made by this Code for the punishment of such abetment, be punished with the punishment provided for the offence.
meaning: Section 109 provides the general punishment for abetment. If a person abets an offence and the offence is actually committed because of that abetment, and the IPC does not prescribe a separate punishment for such abetment, the abettor receives the same punishment as the principal offender.
key_points: Section 109 is a general punishment provision for abetment.
It applies only when no specific punishment for abetment is provided elsewhere in the IPC.
The offence abetted must actually be committed.
The offence must be committed because of the abetment.
The abettor receives the same punishment as the principal offender.
The section covers instigation, conspiracy, and intentional aid.
A causal connection between the abetment and the offence is required.
legal_significance: Section 109 ensures that persons who encourage, assist, or conspire in the commission of offences cannot escape liability merely because they did not personally commit the offence. It places the abettor on the same footing as the principal offender where no separate punishment exists.
essential_elements: There must be abetment as defined in Section 107.
The offence abetted must be committed.
The offence must be committed in consequence of the abetment.
No specific provision for punishment of that abetment should exist in the IPC.
There must be a direct connection between the abetment and the offence.

commission_of_offence.requirement: The offence must actually be committed.

causal_connection.requirement: The offence must be committed because of the abetment.

absence_of_specific_provision.requirement: No separate punishment should be prescribed elsewhere in the IPC.

explanation.rule: An act or offence is committed in consequence of abetment when it occurs because of the instigation, conspiracy, or aid that constitutes the abetment.
explanation.importance: This establishes the necessary link between the abettor's conduct and the offence.

illustration_a_analysis.facts: A offers a bribe to B, a public servant, for official favour. B accepts the bribe.
illustration_a_analysis.result: A abets the offence committed by B.

illustration_b_analysis.facts: A instigates B to give false evidence. B gives false evidence.
illustration_b_analysis.liability: A receives the same punishment as B.

illustration_c_analysis.facts: A and B conspire to poison Z. A procures poison and B administers it, causing Z's death.
illustration_c_analysis.liability: A is guilty of abetment by conspiracy and is liable for punishment equivalent to murder.

scope_of_section: Cases where no separate punishment is prescribed.

examples.scenario: A persuades B to commit theft and B commits theft.
examples.result: A is punishable under Section 109.

examples.scenario: A provides a weapon to B for committing murder and B commits murder.
examples.result: A may be punished under Section 109.

examples.scenario: A and B conspire to commit robbery and the robbery occurs.
examples.result: A may be liable under Section 109.

when_section_109_applies.condition: The offence is committed due to the abetment.
No specific punishment for the abetment exists.

when_section_109_does_not_apply.situation: The IPC provides a specific punishment for the abetment.
The offence is not committed in consequence of the abetment.
when_section_109_does_not_apply.reason: The required causal link is missing.

punishment.rule: Same punishment as provided for the offence committed.
punishment.nature: Derivative punishment based on the principal offence.

judicial_principles: Abetment is a substantive offence.
The offence must be connected to the abetment.
The abettor's liability may equal that of the principal offender.
Mens rea is an important component.
Participation may be direct or indirect.

relationship_with_other_sections.section_110: Abettor liable when person abetted acts with different intention.
relationship_with_other_sections.section_111: Liability for acts different from those abetted.

difference_between_sections_107_and_109.section_109: Prescribes punishment for abetment when the offence is committed.

faqs.question: What is the purpose of Section 109 IPC?
faqs.answer: To punish an abettor when the offence is committed as a result of the abetment and no separate punishment is prescribed.

faqs.question: Must the offence actually be committed for Section 109 to apply?
faqs.answer: Yes. The act abetted must be committed in consequence of the abetment.

faqs.question: What punishment does the abettor receive?
faqs.answer: Generally, the same punishment as provided for the principal offence.

faqs.question: Does Section 109 apply when the IPC provides a specific punishment for abetment?
faqs.answer: No. It applies only when no express punishment is separately provided.

max_punishment: Same as the punishment prescribed for the offence committed
ai_summary: Section 109 IPC provides that when an offence is committed as a consequence of abetment and the IPC contains no separate punishment for that abetment, the abettor shall be punished with the same punishment prescribed for the offence itself. The section applies to abetment by instigation, conspiracy, or intentional aid and requires a direct connection between the abetment and the commission of the offence.

title: Punishment of Abetment if Person Abetted Does Act with Different Intention from that of Abettor
chapter: Chapter V - Of Abetment
section_text: Whoever abets the commission of an offence shall, if the person abetted does the act with a different intention or knowledge from that of the abettor, be punished with the punishment provided for the offence which would have been committed if the act had been done with the intention or knowledge of the abettor and with no other.
meaning: Section 110 provides that when an abettor encourages another person to commit an act, but the person committing the act acts with a different intention or knowledge, the abettor is punished according to his own intention or knowledge and not according to the intention of the person who actually committed the act.
key_points: Section 110 deals with differing intentions between the abettor and the principal offender.
The liability of the abettor depends upon his own intention or knowledge.
The abettor is not automatically liable for the actual intention of the person committing the act.
The punishment is determined by the offence intended by the abettor.
The section ensures fair attribution of criminal responsibility.
Mens rea (guilty intention) remains the basis of liability.
The actual offender and the abettor may receive different punishments.
legal_significance: Section 110 ensures that criminal liability corresponds to the mental state of the abettor. It prevents an abettor from being punished for consequences that arise solely because the principal offender acted with a different intention or knowledge.
essential_elements: There must be abetment of an offence.
The act abetted must be committed.
The person committing the act must act with a different intention or knowledge.
The abettor's intention or knowledge must be identifiable.
Punishment is based on the offence intended by the abettor.

principle_of_section.rule: The abettor is punished according to his own guilty mind (mens rea).
principle_of_section.objective: To ensure that punishment reflects the abettor's actual intention or knowledge.

how_liability_is_determined.step_1: Identify the intention or knowledge of the abettor.
how_liability_is_determined.step_2: Assume the act was committed with that intention or knowledge alone.
how_liability_is_determined.step_3: Determine what offence would have resulted.

illustrative_example_1.facts: A abets B to cause simple hurt to C.
illustrative_example_1.actual_event: B acts with the intention to cause grievous hurt and causes grievous hurt.
illustrative_example_1.result: A is punished as if the offence intended by him (simple hurt) had been committed.
illustrative_example_1.reason: B acted with a different intention.

illustrative_example_2.facts: A encourages B to damage property.
illustrative_example_2.actual_event: B intentionally burns the property causing far greater damage.
illustrative_example_2.result: A's punishment is determined by his own intention, not B's greater criminal intention.

examples.scenario: A instigates B to commit simple assault.
examples.liability_of_A: Based on the offence intended by A.

examples.scenario: A aids B in causing minor property damage.
A encourages B to intimidate C.
examples.liability_of_A: Depends upon A's own intention and knowledge.

when_section_110_applies.condition: The principal offender acts with a different intention.
The principal offender acts with different knowledge.

when_section_110_does_not_apply.situation: The abettor and offender share the same intention.

judicial_principles: Mens rea is the foundation of criminal liability.
An abettor is generally liable according to his own intention.
Punishment should be proportionate to individual culpability.
Different mental states may justify different punishments.
The section protects against excessive attribution of criminal responsibility.

relationship_with_other_sections.section_109: Punishment when offence is committed in consequence of abetment.
relationship_with_other_sections.section_110: Punishment when offender acts with different intention or knowledge.
relationship_with_other_sections.section_111: Liability when a different act is done than the act abetted.

section_109.rule: Abettor generally receives punishment for the offence committed.

section_110.rule: Abettor is punished according to his own intention when it differs from that of the offender.

legal_application: Cases involving different levels of criminal intention.

punishment.rule: Punishment for the offence that would have been committed if the act had been done with the intention or knowledge of the abettor alone.

faqs.question: What is the purpose of Section 110 IPC?
faqs.answer: To determine punishment when the person abetted acts with a different intention or knowledge from the abettor.

faqs.question: Whose intention is important under Section 110?
faqs.answer: The intention or knowledge of the abettor.

faqs.question: Can the abettor and offender receive different punishments?
faqs.answer: Yes. Their punishments may differ because their intentions may differ.

faqs.question: Why does Section 110 exist?
faqs.answer: To ensure that punishment reflects the actual mental state and culpability of the abettor.

max_punishment: Depends on the offence that would have been committed according to the abettor's intention or knowledge
ai_summary: Section 110 IPC states that when a person abets an offence but the person committing the act acts with a different intention or knowledge, the abettor is punished according to the offence that would have resulted from the abettor's own intention or knowledge. The section ensures that criminal liability is based on the abettor's personal mens rea rather than the mental state of the principal offender.

title: Liability of Abettor When One Act Abetted and Different Act Done
chapter: Chapter V - Of Abetment
section_text: When an act is abetted and a different act is done, the abettor is liable for the act done, in the same manner and to the same extent as if he had directly abetted it, provided the act done was a probable consequence of the abetment and was committed under the influence of the instigation, or with the aid or in pursuance of the conspiracy which constituted the abetment.
meaning: Section 111 provides that an abettor may be held responsible not only for the exact act he intended to encourage but also for a different act actually committed, if that different act was a probable consequence of the abetment and resulted from the instigation, aid, or conspiracy.
key_points: The act actually committed may be different from the act originally abetted.
The abettor can still be held liable for the different act.
The different act must be a probable consequence of the abetment.
There must be a connection between the abetment and the act done.
The liability is the same as if the abettor had directly abetted the act done.
The section extends criminal responsibility to foreseeable consequences.
A completely independent act does not make the abettor liable.
legal_significance: Section 111 prevents an abettor from escaping liability merely because the final offence differs from the one originally contemplated. If the actual offence is a natural and probable consequence of the abetment, the abettor remains criminally responsible.
essential_elements: There must be abetment of an act.
A different act must actually be committed.
The different act must be a probable consequence of the abetment.
The act must be committed under the influence of the instigation, aid, or conspiracy.
There must be a causal connection between the abetment and the act done.

principle_of_section.rule: An abettor is responsible for reasonably foreseeable consequences of his abetment.
principle_of_section.objective: To extend liability to probable outcomes arising from criminal encouragement or assistance.

meaning_of_probable_consequence.definition: A consequence that a reasonable person could foresee as likely to result from the abetted act.
meaning_of_probable_consequence.importance: Liability depends upon foreseeability and natural consequences.

illustration_a_analysis.facts: A instigates a child to poison Z. By mistake, the child poisons Y.
illustration_a_analysis.result: A is liable as if he had instigated poisoning of Y.
illustration_a_analysis.reason: The poisoning of the wrong person was a probable consequence of the abetment.

illustration_b_analysis.facts: A instigates B to burn Z's house. While doing so, B also commits theft.
illustration_b_analysis.result: A is liable for abetting arson but not theft.
illustration_b_analysis.reason: The theft was a separate and independent act, not a probable consequence of burning the house.

illustration_c_analysis.facts: A instigates B and C to commit armed robbery in an inhabited house at midnight. During resistance, Z is murdered.
illustration_c_analysis.result: A may be liable for murder.
illustration_c_analysis.reason: Murder during an armed robbery may be a probable consequence of the abetment.

scope_of_section: Cases involving unintended but foreseeable consequences.
Criminal enterprises leading to additional offences.

when_abettor_is_liable.condition: The different act is a natural consequence of the abetment.
The act is committed under the influence of the abetment.

when_abettor_is_not_liable.condition: The act is completely independent of the abetment.
The act is not a probable consequence.
The act has no causal connection with the abetment.

examples.scenario: A encourages B to commit armed robbery. During the robbery, B seriously injures a victim.
examples.result: A may be liable if the injury was a probable consequence.

examples.scenario: A instigates B to vandalize property. B accidentally destroys nearby property.
examples.result: A may be liable if such damage was foreseeable.

examples.scenario: A encourages B to trespass. B later commits an unrelated fraud.
examples.result: A is not liable for the fraud.

judicial_principles: Foreseeability is a key factor.
The actual act need not be identical to the abetted act.
Criminal responsibility extends to probable consequences.
Independent and unrelated acts break the chain of liability.
The existence of causal connection is essential.

relationship_with_other_sections.section_110: Liability when the offender acts with different intention.
relationship_with_other_sections.section_111: Liability when a different act is committed.

section_110.focus: Different intention or knowledge of the person abetted.

section_111.focus: Different act committed as a probable consequence of the abetment.

punishment.rule: Same manner and extent as if the abettor had directly abetted the act actually committed.

faqs.question: What is the purpose of Section 111 IPC?
faqs.answer: To make an abettor liable for a different act that is a probable consequence of the abetment.

faqs.question: Must the act committed be exactly the same as the act abetted?
faqs.answer: No. A different act may still create liability if it was a probable consequence.

faqs.question: What is a probable consequence?
faqs.answer: A consequence that could reasonably be expected to result from the abetment.

faqs.question: Is an abettor liable for every act committed by the offender?
faqs.answer: No. Only for acts that are probable consequences of the abetment.

max_punishment: Same as the punishment for the act actually committed if it was a probable consequence of the abetment
ai_summary: Section 111 IPC provides that when a person abets one act but a different act is committed, the abettor may still be held liable for the act actually committed if it was a probable consequence of the abetment and occurred under the influence of the instigation, aid, or conspiracy. The abettor is punished as though he had directly abetted that act.

title: Abettor When Liable to Cumulative Punishment for Act Abetted and for Act Done
chapter: Chapter V - Of Abetment
section_text: If the act for which the abettor is liable under the last preceding section is committed in addition to the act abetted, and constitutes a distinct offence, the abettor is liable to punishment for each of the offences.
meaning: Section 112 provides that when an abettor becomes liable for a different act under Section 111, and that different act is committed in addition to the original act abetted and constitutes a separate offence, the abettor may be punished for both offences.
key_points: Section 112 is based on Section 111.
The original act abetted must be committed.
An additional distinct offence must also be committed.
The abettor may be punished for both offences.
The additional offence must be a probable consequence of the abetment.
The section creates cumulative criminal liability.
Knowledge of the likely consequence is important.
legal_significance: Section 112 ensures that an abettor cannot escape liability when the offence he abetted results not only in the intended offence but also in an additional and distinct offence that was a probable consequence of the abetment.
essential_elements: There must be abetment of an act.
The act abetted must be committed.
A separate and distinct offence must also be committed.
The additional offence must be connected to the abetment.
The abettor must be liable under Section 111 for the additional act.
The offences must be distinct from each other.

principle_of_section.rule: Where both the intended offence and an additional distinct offence occur, the abettor may be punished for both.
principle_of_section.objective: To impose full liability for all foreseeable criminal consequences.

relationship_with_section_111.section_111: Makes the abettor liable for a different act that is a probable consequence of the abetment.
relationship_with_section_111.section_112: Adds liability when that different act is committed in addition to the original act abetted.

illustration_analysis.facts: A instigates B to resist by force a distress made by a public servant.
illustration_analysis.additional_offence: B voluntarily causes grievous hurt to the public servant.
illustration_analysis.result_for_B: B is liable for both offences.
illustration_analysis.result_for_A: If A knew that grievous hurt was likely to be caused, A is liable for both offences.
illustration_analysis.reason: The grievous hurt is an additional distinct offence arising from the abetment.

when_abettor_is_liable_for_both_offences.condition: A separate offence is also committed.
The additional offence is a probable consequence.

when_section_112_does_not_apply.situation: Only the original offence is committed.
The additional offence is completely unrelated.
when_section_112_does_not_apply.reason: No causal connection with the abetment.

when_section_112_does_not_apply.situation: The additional offence was not a probable consequence.

examples.scenario: A instigates B to commit criminal trespass. During the trespass, B also causes grievous hurt which A knew was likely.
examples.result: A may be liable for both criminal trespass and grievous hurt.

examples.scenario: A encourages B to participate in a violent unlawful assembly. B commits rioting and also causes serious injury.
examples.result: A may be punished for both offences if the injury was a foreseeable consequence.

examples.scenario: A abets property damage. B additionally commits a separate violent offence that was reasonably foreseeable.
examples.result: A may incur cumulative liability.

judicial_principles: Criminal liability extends to probable consequences.
Distinct offences may attract separate punishments.
Knowledge and foreseeability are important factors.
The chain of causation must remain intact.
An abettor may share responsibility for multiple offences.

section_111.focus: Liability for a different act committed instead of or as a consequence of the act abetted.

section_112.focus: Liability when the original act and an additional distinct offence are both committed.

relationship_with_other_sections.section_113: Liability for effects caused by the act abetted.

punishment.rule: Punishment may be imposed separately for each offence.

faqs.question: What is the purpose of Section 112 IPC?
faqs.answer: To impose liability when both the original offence and an additional distinct offence are committed as a result of the abetment.

faqs.question: Can an abettor be punished for more than one offence?
faqs.answer: Yes. If the conditions of Section 112 are satisfied, the abettor may be punished for each offence.

faqs.question: Is foreseeability important under Section 112?
faqs.answer: Yes. The additional offence must be a probable consequence of the abetment.

faqs.question: How is Section 112 related to Section 111?
faqs.answer: Section 112 builds upon Section 111 and applies when the additional act is committed along with the original act abetted.

max_punishment: Punishment for each offence may be imposed separately according to law
ai_summary: Section 112 IPC provides that when an abettor is liable under Section 111 for a different act that is a probable consequence of the abetment, and that act is committed in addition to the original act abetted and constitutes a separate offence, the abettor may be punished for both offences. The section recognizes cumulative liability for multiple criminal consequences arising from the same abetment.

title: Liability of Abettor for an Effect Caused by the Act Abetted Different from that Intended by the Abettor
chapter: Chapter V - Of Abetment
section_text: When an act is abetted with the intention on the part of the abettor of causing a particular effect, and an act for which the abettor is liable in consequence of the abetment causes a different effect from that intended by the abettor, the abettor is liable for the effect caused, in the same manner and to the same extent as if he had abetted the act with the intention of causing that effect, provided he knew that the act abetted was likely to cause that effect.
meaning: Section 113 provides that when an abettor intends one result but the act actually causes a different result, the abettor can still be held liable for the actual result if he knew that the act he abetted was likely to produce that result.
key_points: The abettor intends a particular consequence.
A different consequence actually occurs.
The different consequence must arise from the act abetted.
The abettor must have known that such consequence was likely.
Liability extends to the actual consequence caused.
Knowledge of likely consequences is the foundation of liability.
The abettor may receive punishment for the more serious result.
legal_significance: Section 113 prevents an abettor from avoiding liability merely because the exact consequence intended by him did not occur. If he knew that a more serious consequence was likely, he can be punished for that consequence when it actually occurs.
essential_elements: There must be abetment of an act.
The abettor must intend a particular effect.
A different effect must actually result.
The different effect must arise from the act abetted.
The abettor must have known that the effect caused was likely to occur.

principle_of_section.rule: Knowledge of likely consequences creates liability for the consequence actually caused.
principle_of_section.objective: To hold abettors responsible for foreseeable harmful outcomes.

intended_effect.requirement: The abettor intended a specific consequence.

knowledge.requirement: The abettor knew the act was likely to cause the actual consequence.

illustration_analysis.facts: A instigates B to cause grievous hurt to Z.
illustration_analysis.act_done: B causes grievous hurt to Z.
illustration_analysis.actual_result: Z dies because of the injury.
illustration_analysis.knowledge_test: A knew that the grievous hurt was likely to cause death.
illustration_analysis.result: A is liable as if he had intended the death.

when_section_113_applies.condition: The consequence caused differs from the consequence intended.
The abettor knew the actual consequence was likely.
when_section_113_applies.result: Liability extends to the actual consequence.

when_section_113_does_not_apply.situation: The consequence caused was completely unforeseeable.
The consequence has no connection with the abetted act.
The abettor had no reason to know the consequence was likely.

examples.scenario: A instigates B to seriously injure C.
examples.liability: A may be liable for the death if he knew death was a likely consequence.

examples.scenario: A encourages B to set fire to a small shed.
examples.actual_result: The fire spreads and destroys a dwelling house.
examples.liability: A may be liable if he knew such spread was likely.

examples.scenario: A instigates B to assault D.
examples.liability: A may be punished for the fatal consequence if foreseeable.

section_111.focus: Different act committed from the act abetted.

section_113.focus: Different effect or consequence caused by the act abetted.

section_112.focus: Liability for multiple offences arising from abetment.

section_113.focus: Liability for a more serious consequence actually caused.

judicial_principles: Foreseeability is essential.
Knowledge is more important than actual intention regarding the final result.
The consequence must flow from the act abetted.
Criminal liability extends to likely outcomes.
An abettor cannot avoid responsibility for foreseeable consequences.

relationship_with_other_sections.section_111: Liability for a different act done.
relationship_with_other_sections.section_113: Liability for a different effect caused.
relationship_with_other_sections.section_114: Abettor present when offence is committed.

legal_application: Murder resulting from intended grievous hurt.

punishment.rule: Same punishment as if the abettor had intended the actual effect caused.

faqs.question: What is the purpose of Section 113 IPC?
faqs.answer: To make an abettor liable for a consequence different from the one intended if that consequence was known to be likely.

faqs.question: Is intention to cause the actual result necessary?
faqs.answer: No. Knowledge that the result was likely is sufficient.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Sections 111 and 113?
faqs.answer: Section 111 deals with a different act, while Section 113 deals with a different consequence or effect.

faqs.question: Can an abettor be punished for murder under Section 113?
faqs.answer: Yes, if death occurs and the abettor knew that death was a likely consequence of the act abetted.

max_punishment: Depends on the effect actually caused (may extend to punishment for murder if death results)
cognizable: Depends on the offence resulting from the effect caused
bailable: Depends on the offence resulting from the effect caused
compoundable: Depends on the offence resulting from the effect caused
triable_by: Depends on the offence resulting from the effect caused
ai_summary: Section 113 IPC states that when an abettor intends a particular consequence but a different consequence actually results from the act abetted, the abettor is liable for the actual consequence if he knew that such consequence was likely to occur. The section extends criminal responsibility to foreseeable results arising from the abetted act.

title: Abettor Present When Offence is Committed
chapter: Chapter V - Of Abetment
section_text: Whenever any person, who if absent would be liable to be punished as an abettor, is present when the act or offence for which he would be punishable in consequence of the abetment is committed, he shall be deemed to have committed such act or offence.
meaning: Section 114 provides that when a person who has already abetted an offence is present at the scene when the offence is committed, the law treats him as if he himself committed the offence.
key_points: Section 114 applies only to an abettor.
The person must have already committed abetment.
The abettor must be present when the offence is committed.
Presence converts the abettor's liability into that of a principal offender.
Actual participation at the moment of offence is not necessary.
Physical presence at the scene is sufficient.
The abettor is deemed to have committed the offence.
legal_significance: Section 114 strengthens criminal liability by treating a present abettor as a principal offender. The law assumes that the presence of the abettor gives encouragement, support, or confidence to the person committing the offence.
essential_elements: There must be abetment under Section 107.
The accused must be liable as an abettor if absent.
The offence must actually be committed.
The accused must be present when the offence is committed.
The presence must relate to the commission of the offence.

principle_of_section.rule: Presence of an abettor at the scene of the crime elevates his liability to that of the actual offender.
principle_of_section.objective: To prevent abettors from escaping responsibility when they supervise or support the crime by their presence.

abetment.requirement: Prior instigation, conspiracy, or intentional aid.

presence.requirement: The abettor must be physically present at the commission of the offence.

commission_of_offence.requirement: The offence must actually take place.

effect_of_section_114.legal_consequence: The abettor is deemed to have committed the offence.
effect_of_section_114.punishment: Same punishment as the principal offender.

examples.scenario: A instigates B to assault C and remains present when B commits the assault.
examples.result: A is deemed to have committed the assault.

examples.scenario: A encourages B to commit theft and accompanies B during the theft.
examples.result: A is treated as if he committed the theft.

examples.scenario: A plans a robbery and remains present while the robbery is carried out.
examples.result: A is deemed to have committed the robbery.

when_section_114_applies.condition: A person has already abetted an offence.
The person is present during commission of the offence.

when_section_114_does_not_apply.situation: The abettor is absent when the offence is committed.

when_section_114_does_not_apply.reason: The section requires commission of the offence.

judicial_principles: Presence indicates support and encouragement.
Abetment plus presence equals principal liability.
Actual physical participation is not mandatory.
The section creates a legal fiction.
The prosecution must prove both abetment and presence.

section_109.scope: Punishes an abettor when the offence is committed in consequence of abetment.

section_114.scope: Treats a present abettor as if he himself committed the offence.
Provides enhanced liability when the abettor is present.

relationship_with_other_sections.section_114: Abettor present at commission of offence.

punishment.rule: Same punishment as if the abettor himself committed the offence.

faqs.question: What is the purpose of Section 114 IPC?
faqs.answer: To treat an abettor who is present during the commission of the offence as if he committed the offence himself.

faqs.question: Is prior abetment necessary for Section 114?
faqs.answer: Yes. The person must already be liable as an abettor.

faqs.question: Is actual participation required?
faqs.answer: No. Presence at the scene after abetment is sufficient.

faqs.question: What punishment does the abettor receive under Section 114?
faqs.answer: The same punishment as the principal offender.

max_punishment: Same as the punishment prescribed for the offence committed
ai_summary: Section 114 IPC states that if a person who has abetted an offence is present when that offence is committed, he is deemed to have committed the offence himself. The section creates enhanced liability by treating the present abettor as a principal offender and subjects him to the same punishment as the person who actually carried out the crime.

title: Abetment of Offence Punishable with Death or Imprisonment for Life, if Offence Not Committed
chapter: Chapter V - Of Abetment
section_text: Whoever abets the commission of an offence punishable with death or imprisonment for life shall, if that offence is not committed in consequence of the abetment, and no express provision is made by this Code for the punishment of such abetment, be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine. If any act for which the abettor is liable in consequence of the abetment, and which causes hurt to any person, is done, the abettor shall be liable to imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to fourteen years, and shall also be liable to fine.
meaning: Section 115 punishes a person who abets the commission of an offence punishable with death or life imprisonment, even when the intended offence is not actually committed. The punishment becomes more severe if hurt is caused as a consequence of the abetment.
key_points: Applies to offences punishable with death or life imprisonment.
The intended offence is not actually committed.
No separate punishment for such abetment should exist in the IPC.
Abetment itself is punishable.
Maximum punishment is 7 years imprisonment and fine.
If hurt is caused as a result of the abetment, punishment may extend to 14 years and fine.
The section addresses serious offences even when they fail.
legal_significance: Section 115 ensures that persons who encourage or assist the commission of the gravest offences do not escape punishment merely because the offence was not ultimately completed. The law recognizes the danger posed by such abetment and punishes it independently.
essential_elements: There must be abetment under Section 107.
The offence abetted must be punishable with death or life imprisonment.
The offence must not actually be committed.
No specific punishment for the abetment should be provided elsewhere in the IPC.
The abettor must possess the necessary criminal intention.

serious_offence.requirement: The offence abetted must be punishable with death or life imprisonment.

non_commission.requirement: The intended offence is not committed.

absence_of_specific_provision.requirement: No express punishment exists elsewhere in the IPC.

illustration_analysis.facts: A instigates B to murder Z.
illustration_analysis.event: B does not commit the murder.
illustration_analysis.punishment: Up to 7 years imprisonment and fine.
illustration_analysis.additional_case: If hurt is caused to Z because of the abetment.
illustration_analysis.enhanced_punishment: Up to 14 years imprisonment and fine.

when_section_115_applies.condition: Abetment of murder, but murder is not committed.
Abetment of an offence punishable with life imprisonment, but offence is not committed.

when_section_115_does_not_apply.reason: Other provisions such as Section 109 may apply.

when_section_115_does_not_apply.situation: The offence is not punishable with death or life imprisonment.
when_section_115_does_not_apply.reason: Section 115 is restricted to the gravest offences.

when_section_115_does_not_apply.situation: The IPC already provides a specific punishment for that abetment.

examples.scenario: A persuades B to commit murder, but B changes his mind.
examples.result: A is punishable under Section 115.

examples.scenario: A instigates B to commit a capital offence, and B only injures the victim.
examples.result: A may face punishment up to 14 years.

examples.scenario: A conspires to commit an offence punishable with life imprisonment, but the plan fails.
examples.result: Section 115 may apply.

judicial_principles: Abetment of grave offences is punishable even when unsuccessful.
The seriousness of the intended offence justifies punishment.
Actual commission of the offence is not necessary.
Causing hurt increases criminal liability.
Mens rea remains an essential ingredient.

relationship_with_other_sections.section_114: Abettor present during commission of offence.
relationship_with_other_sections.section_115: Abetment of offences punishable with death or life imprisonment when offence is not committed.
relationship_with_other_sections.section_116: Abetment of offences punishable with imprisonment when offence is not committed.

section_109.scope: Applies when the offence is committed.

section_115.scope: Applies when the offence is not committed.

section_115.offence_type: Offences punishable with death or life imprisonment.

legal_application: Abetment of offences punishable with life imprisonment.

punishment.basic: Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine
punishment.if_hurt_caused: Imprisonment up to 14 years and fine

faqs.question: What is the purpose of Section 115 IPC?
faqs.answer: To punish abetment of offences punishable with death or life imprisonment even when the offence is not committed.

faqs.question: Is actual commission of the offence necessary?
faqs.answer: No. Section 115 applies when the offence is not committed.

faqs.question: What is the maximum punishment under Section 115?
faqs.answer: Up to 7 years imprisonment and fine, or up to 14 years and fine if hurt is caused.

faqs.question: Does Section 115 apply to all offences?
faqs.answer: No. It applies only to offences punishable with death or life imprisonment.

max_punishment: 14 years imprisonment and fine (if hurt is caused)
cognizable: Depends on the nature of the offence abetted
triable_by: Court having jurisdiction over the offence
ai_summary: Section 115 IPC punishes a person who abets an offence punishable with death or life imprisonment when the offence is not actually committed. The punishment may extend to 7 years and fine. If hurt is caused as a consequence of the abetment, the punishment may extend to 14 years and fine. The section ensures accountability for encouraging the commission of extremely serious crimes even when they fail.

title: Abetment of Offence Punishable with Imprisonment if Offence is Not Committed
chapter: Chapter V - Of Abetment
section_text: Whoever abets an offence punishable with imprisonment shall, if that offence is not committed in consequence of the abetment, and no express provision is made by this Code for the punishment of such abetment, be punished with imprisonment of any description provided for that offence for a term which may extend to one-fourth of the longest term provided for that offence, or with such fine as is provided for that offence, or with both. If the abettor or the person abetted is a public servant whose duty it is to prevent the commission of such offence, the abettor shall be punished with imprisonment of any description provided for that offence for a term which may extend to one-half of the longest term provided for that offence, or with such fine as is provided for the offence, or with both.
meaning: Section 116 punishes a person who abets an offence punishable with imprisonment when the offence is not actually committed. The punishment becomes more severe when the abettor or the person abetted is a public servant whose duty is to prevent that offence.
key_points: Applies when an offence punishable with imprisonment is abetted.
The offence is not actually committed.
No specific punishment for such abetment is provided elsewhere in the IPC.
Maximum punishment is one-fourth of the longest imprisonment prescribed for the offence.
Fine may also be imposed.
Enhanced punishment applies when a public servant is involved.
The section punishes unsuccessful abetment.
legal_significance: Section 116 ensures that persons who encourage or assist criminal acts remain punishable even when the intended offence is never committed. The law treats public servants more strictly because they have a special duty to prevent crime.
essential_elements: There must be abetment under Section 107.
The offence abetted must be punishable with imprisonment.
The offence must not be committed.
No express punishment for the abetment should exist elsewhere in the IPC.
The abettor must possess the required criminal intention.

faqs.question: What is the purpose of Section 116 IPC?
faqs.answer: To punish abetment of offences punishable with imprisonment when the offence is not actually committed.

faqs.question: Is actual commission of the offence necessary?
faqs.answer: No. Section 116 applies when the offence is not committed.

faqs.question: What is the maximum punishment in an ordinary case?
faqs.answer: Up to one-fourth of the maximum imprisonment prescribed for the offence.

faqs.question: Why is punishment enhanced for public servants?
faqs.answer: Because public servants have a legal duty to prevent offences and are expected to uphold the law.

max_punishment: Up to one-half of the maximum imprisonment prescribed for the offence (in public servant cases)
ai_summary: Section 116 IPC punishes a person who abets an offence punishable with imprisonment when the offence is not actually committed. The normal punishment may extend to one-fourth of the maximum imprisonment prescribed for the offence, along with fine. If the abettor or the person abetted is a public servant whose duty is to prevent the offence, the punishment may extend to one-half of the maximum imprisonment prescribed, reflecting the higher responsibility imposed on public officials.

title: Abetting Commission of Offence by the Public or by More Than Ten Persons
chapter: Chapter V - Of Abetment
section_text: Whoever abets the commission of an offence by the public generally or by any number or class of persons exceeding ten, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both.
meaning: Section 117 punishes a person who encourages, instigates, or aids the commission of an offence by the public at large or by a group consisting of more than ten persons. The offence focuses on the danger created by inciting large groups to commit unlawful acts.
key_points: Section 117 deals with abetment involving large groups of people.
The abetment may be directed at the public generally.
It also applies to a specific group exceeding ten persons.
Actual commission of the offence is not necessary for liability.
The emphasis is on mass instigation or encouragement.
The section aims to prevent public disorder and group criminality.
The punishment may include imprisonment, fine, or both.
legal_significance: Section 117 is designed to prevent the spread of criminal activity among large groups. It punishes persons who incite crowds, communities, organizations, or sections of the public to commit offences, thereby protecting public order and security.
essential_elements: There must be abetment under Section 107.
The abetment must relate to an offence.
The offence must be intended to be committed by the public generally or by more than ten persons.
The accused must possess the necessary criminal intention or knowledge.
The group involved must exceed ten persons if it is not the public generally.

large_group.requirement: Public generally or a group/class of more than ten persons.

criminal_object.requirement: The abetment must relate to the commission of an offence.

punishment.both: Court may impose both imprisonment and fine

faqs.question: What is the purpose of Section 117 IPC?
faqs.answer: To punish persons who incite or encourage offences by the public or by groups exceeding ten persons.

ai_summary: Section 117 IPC punishes any person who abets the commission of an offence by the public generally or by a group consisting of more than ten persons. The section targets mass instigation and collective criminal activity, recognizing the greater danger posed by encouraging large groups to commit offences. The punishment may extend to three years' imprisonment, fine, or both.

title: Concealing Design to Commit Offence Punishable with Death or Imprisonment for Life
chapter: Chapter V - Of Abetment
section_text: Whoever intending to facilitate or knowing it to be likely that he will thereby facilitate the commission of an offence punishable with death or imprisonment for life, voluntarily conceals by any act or illegal omission, or by the use of encryption or any other information hiding tool, the existence of a design to commit such offence or makes any representation which he knows to be false respecting such design, shall, if that offence be committed, be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years; or, if the offence be not committed, with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years; and in either case shall also be liable to fine.
meaning: Section 118 punishes a person who intentionally hides information about a planned offence punishable with death or life imprisonment, or knowingly gives false information about such a plan, with the intention of helping or facilitating the offence.
key_points: Applies to offences punishable with death or life imprisonment.
Concealment may be by act, omission, encryption, or information-hiding tools.
False information regarding the criminal plan is also punishable.
The accused must intend to facilitate the offence or know that concealment is likely to facilitate it.
Punishment differs depending on whether the offence is ultimately committed.
Fine may be imposed in addition to imprisonment.
The section aims to prevent assistance to serious crimes.
legal_significance: Section 118 punishes persons who knowingly hide or misrepresent plans relating to the most serious offences. It seeks to prevent people from assisting dangerous criminals by concealing information from authorities.
essential_elements: There must be a design or plan to commit an offence.
The offence planned must be punishable with death or life imprisonment.
The accused must conceal the design or provide false information.
The concealment must be voluntary.
The accused must intend to facilitate the offence or know that facilitation is likely.

concealment_by_act.example: Destroying evidence of a criminal plan.

concealment_by_illegal_omission.example: Failing to report known information when legally required.

concealment_by_encryption.example: Using encrypted communication to hide a murder conspiracy.

information_hiding_tools.example: Using software or technology to conceal criminal plans.

false_representation.example: Providing deliberately false information about the planned offence.

illustration_analysis.conduct: A falsely informs the Magistrate that the dacoity will occur at place C.
illustration_analysis.purpose: To mislead authorities and facilitate the offence.
illustration_analysis.result: The dacoity is committed at B.

There was no intention or likelihood of facilitating the offence.

faqs.question: What is the purpose of Section 118 IPC?
faqs.answer: To punish persons who conceal or misrepresent plans relating to offences punishable with death or life imprisonment.

faqs.answer: Yes. Knowingly making false representations about the criminal design is punishable.

faqs.answer: Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.

faqs.answer: Yes. The section expressly includes encryption and information-hiding tools.

ai_summary: Section 118 IPC punishes a person who intentionally conceals, suppresses, encrypts, or falsely represents information about a plan to commit an offence punishable with death or life imprisonment. If the offence is ultimately committed, punishment may extend to 7 years and fine; if it is not committed, punishment may extend to 3 years and fine. The section aims to prevent assistance to serious criminal activities through concealment or deception.

title: Public Servant Concealing Design to Commit Offence Which It Is His Duty to Prevent
chapter: Chapter V - Of Abetment
section_type: Concealment of Criminal Design by Public Servant
section_text: Whoever, being a public servant, intending to facilitate or knowing it to be likely that he will thereby facilitate the commission of an offence which it is his duty as such public servant to prevent, voluntarily conceals, by any act or illegal omission or by the use of encryption or any other information hiding tool, the existence of a design to commit such offence, or makes any representation which he knows to be false respecting such design, shall be punished according to the provisions of this section.
meaning: Section 119 punishes a public servant who knowingly conceals information about a planned offence that it is his official duty to prevent. The concealment may be by action, omission, encryption, information-hiding tools, or false statements.
key_points: Applies only to public servants.
The offence concealed must be one that the public servant is legally bound to prevent.
Concealment may be by act, omission, encryption, or false information.
The public servant must intend to facilitate the offence or know that facilitation is likely.
Punishment varies depending on whether the offence is committed.
More severe punishment applies when the concealed offence is punishable with death or life imprisonment.
The section protects public trust and official responsibility.
legal_significance: Section 119 imposes a higher duty on public servants. Since public officials are entrusted with preventing crime, any deliberate concealment of criminal plans by them is treated as a serious offence attracting enhanced punishment.
essential_elements: The accused must be a public servant.
There must be a design to commit an offence.
Preventing that offence must be part of the public servant's duty.
The public servant must conceal the design or provide false information.
The concealment must be intentional or done with knowledge that it will facilitate the offence.

modes_of_concealment.act: Active steps taken to hide the criminal plan.
modes_of_concealment.illegal_omission: Failure to disclose information despite legal duty.
modes_of_concealment.encryption: Using encrypted methods to conceal information.
modes_of_concealment.information_hiding_tool: Any technology used to hide criminal plans.
modes_of_concealment.false_representation: Providing information known to be false.

if_offence_not_committed.imprisonment: Up to one-fourth of the maximum imprisonment provided for that offence, or fine, or both.

illustration_analysis.conduct: A intentionally fails to report the plan.
