title: Abetment of Assault by Soldier, Sailor or Airman on His Superior Officer, When in Execution of His Office
chapter: Chapter VII - Of Offences Relating to the Army, Navy and Air Force
section_text: Whoever abets an assault by an officer, soldier, sailor or airman in the Army, Navy or Air Force of the Government of India on any superior officer being in the execution of his office, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, and shall also be liable to fine.
meaning: Section 133 punishes any person who encourages, instigates, assists, or facilitates an assault by military personnel on a superior officer while that superior officer is performing official duties.
key_points: Applies to abetment of assault on a superior officer.
The offender may be any person who abets the assault.
The assault must be committed by an officer, soldier, sailor, or airman.
The victim must be a superior officer.
The superior officer must be acting in the execution of official duties.
Military discipline is the primary interest protected.
Punishment may extend to three years imprisonment and fine.
legal_significance: Section 133 protects the chain of command within the Armed Forces. Any attempt to encourage or assist attacks on superior officers can undermine military discipline and operational effectiveness.
essential_elements: There must be an assault.
The assault must be committed by military personnel.
The victim must be a superior officer.
The superior officer must be acting in the execution of official duties.
The accused must have abetted the assault.

abetment.meaning: Instigation, conspiracy, or intentional aid in committing the assault.

assault.meaning: Use or threat of force creating apprehension of immediate harm.

military_personnel.meaning: Officer, soldier, sailor, or airman of the Armed Forces.

superior_officer.meaning: An officer who holds authority or command over the offender.

official_duty.meaning: The superior officer must be performing lawful official functions.

when_section_133_applies.condition: A person encourages a soldier to attack his commanding officer while on duty.

A person conspires with an airman to assault a superior officer during official work.

examples.scenario: A civilian persuades a soldier to physically attack his commanding officer during a military inspection.
examples.result: Section 133 applies.

examples.scenario: A fellow serviceman supplies information and encouragement for an assault on a superior officer performing duty.
examples.result: Section 133 applies.

examples.scenario: A soldier argues with a superior officer without any assault or abetment.
examples.result: Section 133 does not apply.

judicial_principles: Military discipline requires protection of superior officers.
Abetment is punishable even if done by a civilian.
The superior officer must be acting officially.
The prosecution must establish the elements of abetment.
The offence is aimed at preserving the chain of command.

section_131.focus: Abetment of mutiny or seduction from military duty.

section_133.focus: Abetment of assault on a superior officer.

title: Abetment of Such Assault, If the Assault Is Committed
chapter: Chapter VII - Of Offences Relating to the Army, Navy and Air Force
section_text: Whoever abets an assault by an officer, soldier, sailor or airman in the Army, Navy or Air Force of the Government of India on any superior officer being in the execution of his office shall, if such assault is committed in consequence of that abetment, be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.
meaning: Section 134 punishes a person who abets an assault by military personnel on a superior officer when the assault is actually committed as a result of that abetment.
key_points: Applies when an assault on a superior officer is actually committed.
There must be abetment and a resulting assault.
The offender may be any person who abets the assault.
The assault must be committed by an officer, soldier, sailor, or airman.
The victim must be a superior officer performing official duties.
A direct connection between abetment and assault is required.
Punishment may extend to seven years imprisonment and fine.
legal_significance: Section 134 strengthens military discipline by imposing a higher punishment when abetment successfully results in an assault on a superior officer. It protects the chain of command and authority within the Armed Forces.
essential_elements: There must be an act of abetment.
The assault must be committed by military personnel.
The victim must be a superior officer.
The superior officer must be acting in the execution of official duties.
The assault must occur as a consequence of the abetment.

abetment.meaning: Instigation, conspiracy, or intentional aid in committing the assault.

assault.meaning: Use or threat of force creating apprehension of immediate harm.

superior_officer.meaning: An officer having authority over the military personnel committing the assault.

causal_connection.meaning: The assault must have occurred because of the abetment.

judicial_principles: Actual commission of the assault is essential.
A causal link between abetment and assault must be established.
Protection of military discipline is the primary objective.
The superior officer must be acting in an official capacity.
Higher punishment applies because the abetment has succeeded.

title: Abetment of Desertion of Soldier, Sailor or Airman
chapter: Chapter VII - Of Offences Relating to the Army, Navy and Air Force
section_text: Whoever abets the desertion of any officer, soldier, sailor or airman in the Army, Navy or Air Force of the Government of India shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.
meaning: Section 135 punishes any person who encourages, assists, instigates, or facilitates the desertion of an officer, soldier, sailor, or airman from the Armed Forces of India.
key_points: Applies to abetment of desertion from the Armed Forces.
Covers officers, soldiers, sailors, and airmen.
Actual desertion is not always necessary for liability as abetment itself is punishable.
The offence affects military discipline and readiness.
Abetment may occur through instigation, conspiracy, or intentional aid.
Punishable with imprisonment, fine, or both.
Protects the operational strength of the Armed Forces.
legal_significance: Section 135 safeguards military discipline and national security by punishing those who encourage or assist military personnel to abandon their service unlawfully.
essential_elements: There must be abetment.
The abetment must relate to desertion.
The person being influenced must be an officer, soldier, sailor, or airman.
The individual must belong to the Armed Forces of India.
The accused must intentionally aid, instigate, or encourage the desertion.

desertion.meaning: Unauthorized abandonment of military service with the intention of permanently avoiding duty.

armed_forces_personnel.meaning: Officer, soldier, sailor, or airman serving in the Armed Forces.

meaning_of_desertion.definition: Leaving military service or duty without lawful authority and with the intention of not returning.
meaning_of_desertion.importance: Desertion weakens military effectiveness and discipline.

when_section_135_applies.condition: A person persuades a soldier to abandon military service permanently.

examples.result: Section 135 does not apply.

judicial_principles: Military discipline is essential to national security.
Intentional encouragement or assistance is required.
Abetment can occur through words, actions, or conspiracy.
The offence protects the operational effectiveness of the Armed Forces.
The prosecution must prove the element of abetment.

faqs.answer: Abetting the desertion of an officer, soldier, sailor, or airman.

title: Harbouring Deserter
chapter: Chapter VII - Of Offences Relating to the Army, Navy and Air Force
section_text: Whoever, except as hereinafter excepted, knowing or having reason to believe that an officer, soldier, sailor or airman in the Army, Navy or Air Force of the Government of India has deserted, harbours such officer, soldier, sailor or airman, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.

exception.text: This provision does not extend to the case in which the harbour is given by a wife to her husband.

meaning: Section 136 punishes any person who knowingly gives shelter, protection, or refuge to a deserter from the Indian Armed Forces. The offence applies when the person knows or has reason to believe that the military personnel has deserted service.
key_points: Applies to harbouring military deserters.
Knowledge or reasonable belief of desertion is required.
Covers officers, soldiers, sailors, and airmen.
Protects military discipline and operational readiness.
Harbouring means giving shelter, protection, or concealment.
A special exception exists for a wife harbouring her husband.
Punishable with imprisonment, fine, or both.
legal_significance: Section 136 discourages civilians and others from assisting military deserters by providing them refuge or concealment. The provision helps maintain discipline within the Armed Forces and supports enforcement of military obligations.
essential_elements: The person harboured must be an officer, soldier, sailor, or airman.
The person must have deserted military service.
The accused must harbour the deserter.
The accused must know or have reason to believe that the person is a deserter.
The case must not fall within the statutory exception.

deserter.meaning: A member of the Armed Forces who has unlawfully abandoned military service.

harbouring.meaning: Providing shelter, concealment, protection, food, accommodation, or assistance to avoid detection.

knowledge.meaning: Actual knowledge or reasonable belief that the person has deserted.

exception_explained.rule: A wife who gives shelter to her deserter husband is not punishable under this section.
exception_explained.purpose: The law recognizes the special marital relationship and exempts such conduct from criminal liability.

judicial_principles: Knowledge or reasonable belief is essential.
Harbouring includes any form of protection or concealment.
Military discipline is the object of protection.
The statutory exception must be strictly applied.
The prosecution must prove awareness of the desertion.

The offence requires knowledge or reasonable belief that the individual has deserted military service. A special exception protects a wife who shelters her deserter husband. The punishment may extend to two years' imprisonment, fine, or both.

title: Deserter Concealed on Board Merchant Vessel Through Negligence of Master
chapter: Chapter VII - Of Offences Relating to the Army, Navy and Air Force
section_text: The master or person in charge of a merchant vessel, on board of which any deserter from the Army, Navy or Air Force of the Government of India is concealed, shall, though ignorant of such concealment, be liable to a penalty not exceeding five hundred rupees, if he might have known of such concealment but for some neglect of his duty as such master or person in charge, or but for some want of discipline on board of the vessel.
meaning: Section 137 imposes liability on the master or person in charge of a merchant vessel when a military deserter is concealed on the vessel due to the master's negligence or failure to maintain proper discipline, even if the master was unaware of the concealment.
key_points: Applies to masters or persons in charge of merchant vessels.
Covers deserters from the Army, Navy, or Air Force.
Actual knowledge of concealment is not required.
Negligence or lack of discipline must be proved.
The deserter must be concealed on the vessel.
Liability arises from failure to perform duties properly.
Punishable with a monetary penalty.
legal_significance: Section 137 ensures that ship masters maintain proper supervision and discipline on merchant vessels so that military deserters cannot use such vessels as a means of escape.
essential_elements: There must be a merchant vessel.
A deserter must be concealed on the vessel.
The accused must be the master or person in charge.
The concealment must have remained undetected because of negligence or lack of discipline.
The master could have discovered the concealment if proper care had been exercised.

title: Abetment of Act of Insubordination by Soldier, Sailor or Airman
chapter: Chapter VII - Of Offences Relating to the Army, Navy and Air Force
section_text: Whoever abets what he knows to be an act of insubordination by an officer, soldier, sailor or airman in the Army, Navy or Air Force of the Government of India shall, if such act of insubordination is committed in consequence of that abetment, be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine, or with both.
meaning: Section 138 punishes a person who knowingly encourages, assists, or instigates an act of insubordination by military personnel, where such insubordination is actually committed as a result of the abetment.

The abettor must know that the act amounts to insubordination.

Protects military discipline and obedience.
Requires a connection between the abetment and the act.
Punishable with imprisonment, fine, or both.
legal_significance: Section 138 safeguards discipline within the Armed Forces by punishing those who knowingly encourage military personnel to disobey lawful authority or engage in acts of insubordination.

Knowledge of the insubordinate nature of the act is required.
Actual commission of insubordination is necessary.
A causal connection between abetment and the act must exist.
The section protects the chain of command.

section_text: Section 138A has been repealed by the Amending Act, 1934 (35 of 1934), Section 2 and Schedule.

title: Persons Subject to Certain Acts
chapter: Chapter VII - Of Offences Relating to the Army, Navy and Air Force
section_text: No person subject to the Army Act, 1950, the Naval Discipline Act / Navy Act, or the Air Force Act, 1950, is subject to punishment under this Code for any of the offences defined in this Chapter.
meaning: Section 139 provides that persons who are governed by military laws such as the Army Act, Navy Act, or Air Force Act cannot be punished under the IPC for offences contained in Chapter VII. Such offences are dealt with under the respective military laws.
key_points: Applies to persons subject to military law.
Prevents double punishment under IPC and military laws.
Chapter VII offences are dealt with under service laws.
Covers Army, Navy, and Air Force personnel.
Ensures military discipline is governed by specialized legislation.
Acts as an exclusionary provision.
Protects against overlapping jurisdiction.
legal_significance: Section 139 establishes that military personnel governed by special service laws are not punishable under IPC Chapter VII offences. Instead, disciplinary and criminal proceedings are conducted under the relevant military legislation.

purpose.objective: To avoid duplication of punishment and jurisdiction.
purpose.reason: Military personnel are governed by specialized disciplinary codes designed for the Armed Forces.

essential_elements: The accused must be subject to military law.
The alleged offence must fall within Chapter VII IPC.
The person must be governed by the Army Act, Navy Act, or Air Force Act.
The offence must be one covered by military jurisdiction.
persons_covered: Army personnel governed by the Army Act, 1950
Naval personnel governed by the Navy Act
Air Force personnel governed by the Air Force Act, 1950
Other persons legally subject to military service laws

effect_of_section_139.purpose: Exclusive military jurisdiction over service-related offences.

offences_affected: Other Chapter VII offences relating to Armed Forces

when_section_139_applies.result: Proceedings are governed by military law, not IPC Chapter VII.

when_section_139_does_not_apply.reason: The civilian is not subject to military law.

when_section_139_does_not_apply.situation: The offence is outside Chapter VII and military law does not provide exclusive jurisdiction.
when_section_139_does_not_apply.reason: Section 139 only relates to Chapter VII offences.

when_section_139_does_not_apply.situation: The person is not governed by the Army Act, Navy Act, or Air Force Act.

examples.scenario: A soldier participates in mutiny.
examples.result: The soldier is tried under military law rather than IPC Chapter VII.

examples.scenario: An airman refuses a lawful command.
examples.result: Disciplinary action is taken under the Air Force Act.

examples.scenario: A civilian abets mutiny.
examples.result: IPC provisions may apply because the civilian is not covered by Section 139.

judicial_principles: Special law prevails over general law.
Military discipline requires specialized legal mechanisms.
Double punishment for the same military offence is avoided.
Exclusive jurisdiction may vest in military authorities.
Chapter VII offences are primarily military-service offences.

relationship_with_other_sections.section_132: Abetment of mutiny if mutiny is committed.
relationship_with_other_sections.section_133: Abetment of assault on superior officer.
relationship_with_other_sections.section_134: Abetment of such assault if committed.
relationship_with_other_sections.section_139: Excludes punishment under IPC for persons subject to military laws.

legal_application: Jurisdictional determinations involving Armed Forces personnel.

faqs.question: Does Section 139 create an offence?
faqs.answer: No. It is a jurisdictional and exclusionary provision.

faqs.question: Why are military personnel excluded from IPC Chapter VII punishment?
faqs.answer: Because they are governed by specialized military laws.

faqs.question: Can a civilian claim protection under Section 139?
faqs.answer: No. The section applies only to persons subject to military law.

faqs.question: What is the main purpose of Section 139?
faqs.answer: To ensure that service-related military offences are handled under military legislation rather than IPC Chapter VII.

max_punishment: Not Applicable
ai_summary: Section 139 IPC is not a penal provision. It states that persons who are subject to the Army Act, Navy Act, or Air Force Act cannot be punished under IPC Chapter VII for offences relating to the Armed Forces. Such matters are instead governed by the respective military laws and court-martial procedures.

title: Wearing Garb or Carrying Token Used by Soldier, Sailor or Airman
chapter: Chapter VII - Of Offences Relating to the Army, Navy and Air Force
section_text: Whoever, not being a soldier, sailor or airman in the Military, Naval or Air service of the Government of India, wears any garb or carries any token resembling any garb or token used by such a soldier, sailor or airman with the intention that it may be believed that he is such a soldier, sailor or airman, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three months, or with fine which may extend to five hundred rupees, or with both.
meaning: Section 140 punishes a civilian or unauthorized person who wears military, naval, or air force uniforms, badges, insignia, medals, or similar tokens with the intention of making others believe that he is a member of the Armed Forces.
key_points: Applies to persons who are not members of the Armed Forces.
Covers military uniforms, insignia, badges, medals, and similar tokens.
Intention to deceive is essential.
Mere possession is not enough.
The offence protects the dignity and identity of the Armed Forces.
False representation as military personnel is punishable.
Punishment includes imprisonment, fine, or both.
legal_significance: Section 140 protects the reputation, authority, and public trust associated with the Armed Forces by preventing unauthorized persons from impersonating military personnel.
essential_elements: The accused must not be a soldier, sailor, or airman.
The accused must wear military garb or carry a military token.
The garb or token must resemble that used by the Armed Forces.
The accused must intend that others believe he is military personnel.
The impersonation must be deliberate.

unauthorized_person.meaning: A person who is not a member of the Armed Forces.

garb.meaning: Uniform, dress, clothing, or attire associated with military service.

token.meaning: Badge, insignia, medal, rank symbol, identification mark, or similar item.

intention_to_deceive.meaning: The accused intends others to believe that he belongs to the Armed Forces.

when_section_140_applies.condition: A civilian wears an Army uniform and pretends to be an Army officer.
A person wears military insignia to gain public trust or authority.
A person carries fake military identification intending to appear as Armed Forces personnel.

when_section_140_does_not_apply.situation: A serving soldier wears his official uniform.
when_section_140_does_not_apply.reason: He is authorized to wear it.

when_section_140_does_not_apply.situation: A costume is worn in a drama, movie, or cultural event without any intention to deceive.
A collector possesses military memorabilia without impersonation.
when_section_140_does_not_apply.reason: No intention to misrepresent military status.

examples.scenario: A civilian wears a military uniform and introduces himself as an Army officer to obtain benefits.
examples.result: Section 140 applies.

examples.scenario: An actor wears a military costume during a film shoot with no intention to deceive the public.
examples.result: Section 140 does not apply.

examples.scenario: A person displays military medals at home as collectibles.
examples.result: Section 140 does not apply.

examples.scenario: A person wears Air Force rank insignia to gain access to a restricted area.
examples.result: Section 140 applies.

judicial_principles: Intention to deceive is the core element.
Mere wearing of similar clothing is not sufficient.
The prosecution must prove impersonation intent.
The provision protects public confidence in the Armed Forces.
Unauthorized assumption of military identity is punishable.

relationship_with_other_sections.section_139: Exclusion of punishment for persons subject to military laws.
relationship_with_other_sections.section_140: Unauthorized wearing of military garb or tokens.

legal_application: Misuse of military insignia and decorations.

faqs.question: What is punished under Section 140 IPC?
faqs.answer: Wearing military uniforms or carrying military tokens with the intention of making others believe that one is a member of the Armed Forces.

faqs.question: Is intention necessary?
faqs.answer: Yes. Intention to deceive is an essential ingredient.

faqs.question: Can an actor wearing a military uniform be punished?
faqs.answer: Generally no, if there is no intention to deceive the public.

faqs.question: What is the maximum punishment?
faqs.answer: Three months imprisonment, or fine up to 500 rupees, or both.

max_punishment: 3 months imprisonment, or fine up to 500 rupees, or both
ai_summary: Section 140 IPC punishes unauthorized persons who wear military uniforms, badges, insignia, or similar tokens with the intention of making others believe that they are members of the Indian Armed Forces. The offence is aimed at preventing military impersonation and protecting the dignity and authority of the Armed Forces. The maximum punishment is three months' imprisonment, fine up to 500 rupees, or both.

title: Unlawful Assembly
chapter: Chapter VIII - Of Offences Against the Public Tranquillity
section_text: An assembly of five or more persons is designated an 'unlawful assembly' if the common object of the persons composing that assembly falls within any of the five objects specified in Section 141 IPC.
meaning: Section 141 defines what constitutes an unlawful assembly. When five or more persons gather with a common unlawful object such as committing an offence, resisting the law, forcibly taking property, or intimidating authorities, the assembly becomes unlawful.
key_points: Minimum five persons are required.
A common object must exist.
The common object must fall under one of the five categories mentioned in the section.
An assembly lawful at first may later become unlawful.
Actual violence is not always necessary.
Membership in an unlawful assembly can attract criminal liability.
The section aims to preserve public peace and order.
legal_significance: Section 141 forms the foundation for offences relating to unlawful assembly, rioting, and public disorder. It enables the law to prevent collective unlawful conduct that threatens public tranquillity.
essential_elements: There must be an assembly.
The assembly must consist of five or more persons.
The members must share a common object.
The common object must be one mentioned in Section 141.
The person accused must be a member of the assembly.

minimum_requirement.rule: An assembly of fewer than five persons cannot be an unlawful assembly under Section 141.

first.object: To overawe the Government or a public servant by criminal force or show of criminal force.
first.example: A group gathers with weapons to intimidate a public official into changing an official decision.

second.object: To resist the execution of any law or legal process.
second.example: A crowd prevents police officers from executing a lawful arrest warrant.

third.object: To commit mischief, criminal trespass, or any other offence.
third.example: A group gathers to vandalize public property.

fourth.object: To obtain possession of property or enforce a right by criminal force.
fourth.example: A group forcibly occupies disputed land.

fifth.object: To compel a person to do something he is not legally bound to do or prevent him from doing something he is legally entitled to do.
fifth.example: A group threatens a shopkeeper to close his business.

explanation.rule: An assembly that was originally lawful can later become unlawful if its common object changes to one covered by Section 141.

common_object.meaning: A shared purpose or goal among members of the assembly.
common_object.importance: The common object distinguishes an unlawful assembly from a mere gathering of people.

when_section_141_applies.condition: Five or more persons gather to forcibly take possession of land.
A crowd gathers to prevent police from executing a court order.
Five or more persons assemble to commit vandalism.

when_section_141_does_not_apply.situation: A peaceful gathering without an unlawful common object.

when_section_141_does_not_apply.reason: Minimum number requirement is not satisfied.

when_section_141_does_not_apply.situation: People assemble for a lawful protest without criminal force or unlawful purpose.

examples.scenario: Six persons gather to attack and damage a rival's shop.
examples.result: Unlawful assembly under the third object.

examples.scenario: Ten people peacefully protest government policy without violence or unlawful purpose.
examples.result: Not an unlawful assembly.

examples.scenario: A lawful protest turns violent and participants start attacking police officers.
examples.result: The assembly may subsequently become unlawful.

judicial_principles: Common object need not be the result of prior planning.
Common object can develop suddenly.
Actual commission of the intended offence is not necessary.
Membership with knowledge of the unlawful object may attract liability.
The object of the assembly is more important than individual acts.

relationship_with_other_sections.section_142: Being a member of an unlawful assembly.
relationship_with_other_sections.section_149: Every member of unlawful assembly guilty of offence committed in prosecution of common object.

faqs.question: How many persons are required for an unlawful assembly?
faqs.answer: At least five persons.

faqs.question: What is a common object?
faqs.answer: A shared unlawful purpose among members of the assembly.

faqs.question: Can a lawful assembly become unlawful later?
faqs.answer: Yes. If its common object changes to one covered by Section 141.

faqs.question: Is violence always necessary?
faqs.answer: No. The existence of a prohibited common object is sufficient.

max_punishment: Not Applicable (Definition Section)
ai_summary: Section 141 IPC defines an unlawful assembly as a gathering of five or more persons sharing a common object such as resisting the law, committing offences, forcibly taking property, intimidating authorities, or compelling someone through criminal force. It is the foundational provision for offences relating to unlawful assembly, rioting, and public disorder.

title: Being Member of Unlawful Assembly
chapter: Chapter VIII - Of Offences Against the Public Tranquillity
section_text: Whoever, being aware of facts which render any assembly an unlawful assembly, intentionally joins that assembly, or continues in it, is said to be a member of an unlawful assembly.
meaning: Section 142 defines who is considered a member of an unlawful assembly. A person becomes a member when he knowingly and intentionally joins or continues in an assembly that is unlawful.
key_points: Knowledge of the unlawful nature of the assembly is essential.
Intentional joining is required.
Continuing in the assembly after gaining knowledge is sufficient.
Actual participation in violence is not necessary.
The section defines membership of an unlawful assembly.
It works together with Section 141 IPC.
Membership may lead to criminal liability under subsequent sections.
legal_significance: Section 142 identifies the persons who can be held legally responsible as members of an unlawful assembly. It forms the basis for imposing liability under Sections 143 to 149 IPC.
essential_elements: There must be an unlawful assembly as defined under Section 141.
The accused must know the facts that make the assembly unlawful.
The accused must intentionally join the assembly or continue to remain in it.
Knowledge and intention must coexist.

unlawful_assembly.meaning: An assembly of five or more persons having a common object mentioned in Section 141 IPC.

knowledge.meaning: Awareness of the facts that make the assembly unlawful.

intentional_joining.meaning: Voluntarily becoming part of the assembly.

continuance.meaning: Remaining in the assembly after knowing that it is unlawful.

knowledge_requirement.rule: A person cannot be treated as a member merely because he is present at the scene.
knowledge_requirement.importance: The prosecution must prove awareness of the unlawful nature of the assembly.

when_section_142_applies.condition: A person knowingly joins a group intending to forcibly occupy land.
when_section_142_applies.result: He becomes a member of an unlawful assembly.

when_section_142_applies.condition: A person initially joins a lawful gathering but remains after it becomes unlawful.
when_section_142_applies.result: He becomes a member if he knowingly continues.

when_section_142_applies.condition: A person stays in a violent mob despite understanding its unlawful object.

when_section_142_does_not_apply.situation: A person accidentally passes through a crowd.
A person is unaware that the assembly has an unlawful object.
A person leaves immediately after learning of the unlawful object.
when_section_142_does_not_apply.reason: He does not continue in the assembly.

examples.scenario: A joins a group of six people planning to vandalize public property and is aware of the plan.
examples.result: A is a member of an unlawful assembly.

examples.scenario: A joins a peaceful protest. Later, the crowd begins attacking police officers. A remains with the crowd despite knowing this.
examples.result: A becomes a member of an unlawful assembly.

examples.scenario: A attends a gathering without knowing its unlawful purpose and leaves when he learns of it.
examples.result: Section 142 does not apply.

judicial_principles: Mere presence is not enough.
Knowledge of unlawful facts is essential.
Membership may arise by joining or by continuing.
Common object can develop after the assembly begins.
Liability depends on awareness and intention.

section_141.purpose: Defines what an unlawful assembly is.

section_142.purpose: Defines who becomes a member of an unlawful assembly.

relationship_with_other_sections.section_142: Definition of membership of unlawful assembly.
relationship_with_other_sections.section_143: Punishment for being a member of an unlawful assembly.
relationship_with_other_sections.section_149: Liability of every member for offences committed in prosecution of common object.

faqs.question: Is mere presence in a crowd enough to make a person a member of an unlawful assembly?
faqs.answer: No. The person must know the unlawful nature of the assembly and intentionally join or continue in it.

faqs.question: Can a lawful participant become a member of an unlawful assembly later?
faqs.answer: Yes. If he knowingly continues after the assembly becomes unlawful.

faqs.question: Does Section 142 itself provide punishment?
faqs.answer: No. Punishment is provided under Section 143 IPC.

faqs.question: Why is knowledge important?
faqs.answer: Because criminal liability requires awareness of the unlawful nature of the assembly.

max_punishment: Not Applicable (Definition Section)
ai_summary: Section 142 IPC defines who is considered a member of an unlawful assembly. A person becomes a member when, knowing the facts that make the assembly unlawful, he intentionally joins it or continues to remain in it. The section is important because membership forms the basis for punishment and collective liability under later provisions such as Sections 143 and 149 IPC.

title: Punishment for Being a Member of an Unlawful Assembly
chapter: Chapter VIII - Of Offences Against the Public Tranquillity
section_text: Whoever is a member of an unlawful assembly shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine, or with both.
meaning: Section 143 prescribes punishment for any person who is a member of an unlawful assembly as defined under Sections 141 and 142 IPC.
key_points: Applies to members of an unlawful assembly.
Relies on the definitions in Sections 141 and 142.
Actual violence is not necessary.
Mere membership with knowledge is sufficient.
Punishment may be imprisonment, fine, or both.
Protects public peace and order.
Forms the basic offence relating to unlawful assembly.
legal_significance: Section 143 is the primary penal provision for unlawful assembly. Once a person is proved to be a member of an unlawful assembly, punishment can be imposed even if no further offence is committed.
essential_elements: There must be an unlawful assembly under Section 141.
The accused must be a member under Section 142.
The accused must knowingly and intentionally join or continue in the assembly.
Membership must be established by evidence.

unlawful_assembly.meaning: An assembly of five or more persons having a prohibited common object.

membership.meaning: Knowingly joining or continuing in the unlawful assembly.

knowledge.meaning: Awareness of the facts making the assembly unlawful.

when_section_143_applies.condition: A person knowingly joins a group of five or more persons intending to commit criminal trespass.
A person remains in a gathering after learning that its common object is unlawful.
A person becomes a member of a mob intending to resist lawful arrest by force.

when_section_143_does_not_apply.situation: The assembly contains fewer than five persons.
The person has no knowledge of the unlawful object.
The gathering is lawful and peaceful.

examples.scenario: Six persons gather to forcibly occupy another person's land, and A knowingly joins them.
examples.result: A is punishable under Section 143.

examples.scenario: A joins a peaceful protest, which later becomes violent. A stays after learning about the unlawful object.
examples.result: A may be liable under Section 143.

examples.scenario: A unknowingly walks into a crowd and immediately leaves after discovering its unlawful purpose.
examples.result: Section 143 does not apply.

judicial_principles: Mere presence is not enough; knowledge is required.
Actual commission of the intended offence is not necessary.
Membership itself constitutes the offence.
Common object distinguishes unlawful assembly from lawful gatherings.
Criminal liability arises from conscious participation.

relationship_with_other_sections.section_142: Being a member of unlawful assembly.
relationship_with_other_sections.section_144: Joining unlawful assembly armed with deadly weapon.
relationship_with_other_sections.section_145: Joining or continuing in unlawful assembly after command to disperse.
relationship_with_other_sections.section_149: Liability of every member for offences committed in prosecution of common object.

faqs.question: What is punished under Section 143 IPC?
faqs.answer: Being a member of an unlawful assembly.

faqs.question: Is violence necessary for punishment under Section 143?
faqs.answer: No. Membership itself is sufficient.

faqs.question: What is the maximum punishment?
faqs.answer: Imprisonment up to six months, or fine, or both.

faqs.question: How is membership determined?
faqs.answer: By proving knowledge of the unlawful object and intentional participation.

max_punishment: 6 months imprisonment, or fine, or both
ai_summary: Section 143 IPC provides punishment for any person who is a member of an unlawful assembly. Once it is established that the accused knowingly joined or continued in an unlawful assembly as defined under Sections 141 and 142, he may be punished with imprisonment up to six months, fine, or both, even if no further offence is committed.

title: Joining Unlawful Assembly Armed with Deadly Weapon
chapter: Chapter VIII - Of Offences Against the Public Tranquillity
section_text: Whoever, being armed with any deadly weapon, or with anything which, used as a weapon of offence, is likely to cause death, is a member of an unlawful assembly, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.
meaning: Section 144 IPC punishes a person who joins or remains a member of an unlawful assembly while carrying a deadly weapon or any object capable of causing death if used as a weapon.
key_points: The person must be a member of an unlawful assembly.
The person must be armed with a deadly weapon or dangerous object.
Actual use of the weapon is not required.
Possession of the weapon during membership is sufficient.
The punishment is more severe than Section 143.
The section aims to prevent violent public disturbances.
It protects public safety by discouraging armed unlawful gatherings.
legal_significance: Section 144 treats armed participation in an unlawful assembly as a more serious offence because the presence of deadly weapons increases the likelihood of violence, injury, and public disorder.
essential_elements: There must be an unlawful assembly under Section 141.
The accused must be a member of that assembly.
The accused must be armed.
The weapon must be deadly or capable of causing death if used offensively.
The weapon must be possessed during membership of the unlawful assembly.

unlawful_assembly.meaning: An assembly of five or more persons having a common unlawful object under Section 141 IPC.

membership.meaning: Knowingly joining or continuing in the unlawful assembly.

deadly_weapon.meaning: A weapon inherently capable of causing death, such as firearms, swords, daggers, or similar weapons.

dangerous_object.meaning: Any object which, when used as a weapon of offence, is likely to cause death.

examples_of_dangerous_objects: Iron rod used as a weapon
Large stone intended for lethal attack

when_section_144_applies.condition: A person joins an unlawful assembly while carrying a sword.
A member of an unlawful assembly carries a loaded firearm.
A person in an unlawful assembly carries an iron rod intended for violent use.

when_section_144_does_not_apply.reason: Unlawful assembly is an essential requirement.

when_section_144_does_not_apply.situation: The accused is not armed with a deadly weapon or dangerous object.
The accused is unaware of the unlawful nature of the assembly.

examples.scenario: A joins a mob of six persons intending to forcibly occupy land while carrying a sword.
examples.result: A is punishable under Section 144.

examples.scenario: A participates in an unlawful assembly carrying a loaded revolver.
examples.result: Section 144 applies.

examples.scenario: A attends a lawful public meeting while carrying a licensed item without any unlawful assembly.
examples.result: Section 144 does not apply.

judicial_principles: Actual use of the weapon is unnecessary.
Possession of the weapon during unlawful assembly is sufficient.
The likelihood of causing death determines whether an object is dangerous.
The offence is aggravated due to increased risk of violence.
The prosecution must prove both membership and possession of the weapon.

section_144.offence: Membership of unlawful assembly while armed with a deadly weapon.

relationship_with_other_sections.section_145: Joining unlawful assembly after command to disperse.
relationship_with_other_sections.section_149: Liability of members for offences committed in prosecution of common object.

legal_application: Political or communal violence involving weapons.

faqs.question: What is punished under Section 144 IPC?
faqs.answer: Being a member of an unlawful assembly while armed with a deadly weapon or dangerous object.

faqs.question: Is actual use of the weapon necessary?
faqs.answer: No. Mere possession while being a member of the unlawful assembly is sufficient.

faqs.question: What is the maximum punishment?
faqs.answer: Imprisonment up to two years, or fine, or both.

faqs.question: How is Section 144 different from Section 143?
faqs.answer: Section 144 applies when the member of the unlawful assembly is armed with a deadly weapon, making it a more serious offence.

max_punishment: 2 years imprisonment, or fine, or both
ai_summary: Section 144 IPC punishes any person who is a member of an unlawful assembly while carrying a deadly weapon or any object capable of causing death if used offensively. The offence is treated more seriously than ordinary unlawful assembly because the presence of weapons increases the risk of violence and public disorder. The maximum punishment is two years' imprisonment, fine, or both.

title: Joining or Continuing in Unlawful Assembly, Knowing It Has Been Commanded to Disperse
chapter: Chapter VIII - Of Offences Against the Public Tranquillity
section_text: Whoever joins or continues in an unlawful assembly, knowing that such unlawful assembly has been commanded in the manner prescribed by law to disperse, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.
meaning: Section 145 IPC punishes a person who joins or remains in an unlawful assembly after it has been lawfully ordered to disperse and the person knows about that order.
key_points: The assembly must be unlawful.
A lawful command to disperse must have been given.
The accused must know about the command.
The accused either joins or continues in the assembly after the order.
Actual violence is not required.
The offence protects public order and lawful authority.
Punishment may include imprisonment, fine, or both.
legal_significance: Section 145 strengthens the authority of law enforcement and public officials by penalizing persons who deliberately ignore lawful orders to disperse an unlawful assembly.
essential_elements: There must be an unlawful assembly under Section 141 IPC.
A lawful command to disperse must be issued.
The accused must know of the command.
The accused must either join or continue in the assembly after the command.
The conduct must be intentional.

unlawful_assembly.meaning: An assembly of five or more persons having a common object prohibited under Section 141 IPC.

lawful_command.meaning: A direction issued by a competent authority in the manner prescribed by law requiring the assembly to disperse.

knowledge.meaning: Awareness that the order to disperse has been given.

continuance_or_joining.meaning: Remaining in or entering the unlawful assembly after the order.

when_section_145_applies.condition: Police lawfully order an unlawful assembly to disperse, but members remain.
A person joins an unlawful assembly after hearing a lawful dispersal order.
Members knowingly ignore a magistrate's order to disperse.

when_section_145_does_not_apply.situation: No lawful order to disperse was issued.
The person was unaware of the dispersal order.
The person immediately leaves after the order is given.

examples.scenario: A magistrate orders a violent crowd to disperse. A remains with the crowd despite hearing the order.
examples.result: A is punishable under Section 145.

examples.scenario: After police announce a dispersal order, B joins the crowd to support its activities.
examples.result: Section 145 applies.

examples.scenario: C leaves immediately after hearing the dispersal order.
examples.result: Section 145 does not apply.

judicial_principles: Knowledge of the dispersal order is essential.
Lawful authority must issue the order.
Continuing participation after the order increases criminal liability.
The section aims to maintain public peace and respect for lawful commands.
Actual violence is not necessary for conviction.

section_143.offence: Being a member of an unlawful assembly.

section_145.offence: Joining or continuing in an unlawful assembly after a lawful command to disperse.

relationship_with_other_sections.section_144: Joining unlawful assembly armed with deadly weapon.
relationship_with_other_sections.section_145: Joining or continuing after command to disperse.
relationship_with_other_sections.section_149: Liability of members for offences committed in prosecution of common object.

legal_application: Failure to obey lawful police or magistrate orders.

faqs.question: What is punished under Section 145 IPC?
faqs.answer: Joining or continuing in an unlawful assembly after knowing that it has been lawfully ordered to disperse.

faqs.question: Is knowledge of the dispersal order necessary?
faqs.answer: Yes. The accused must know that the order has been given.

faqs.question: What is the maximum punishment?
faqs.answer: Imprisonment up to two years, or fine, or both.

faqs.question: Does the assembly need to be unlawful?
faqs.answer: Yes. The section applies only to unlawful assemblies.

max_punishment: 2 years imprisonment, or fine, or both
ai_summary: Section 145 IPC punishes any person who knowingly joins or remains in an unlawful assembly after it has been lawfully ordered to disperse. The provision reinforces the authority of police and magistrates to maintain public order. The maximum punishment is two years' imprisonment, fine, or both.

title: Rioting
chapter: Chapter VIII - Of Offences Against the Public Tranquillity
section_text: Whenever force or violence is used by an unlawful assembly, or by any member thereof, in prosecution of the common object of such assembly, every member of such assembly is guilty of the offence of rioting.
meaning: Section 146 defines the offence of rioting. When an unlawful assembly or any of its members uses force or violence to achieve the common object of the assembly, every member of that assembly becomes guilty of rioting.
key_points: Rioting can occur only when there is an unlawful assembly.
Force or violence must be used.
The force or violence must be connected to the common object.
Even if only one member uses force, all members may be liable.
Actual injury is not necessary.
Collective responsibility applies.
The section defines the offence; punishment is provided under Section 147.
legal_significance: Section 146 converts an unlawful assembly into a rioting assembly when force or violence is used. It imposes collective criminal liability on all members participating in the unlawful assembly.
essential_elements: There must be an unlawful assembly under Section 141 IPC.
Force or violence must be used.
The force or violence must be used in prosecution of the common object.
The accused must be a member of the unlawful assembly.

unlawful_assembly.meaning: An assembly of five or more persons having a common object specified under Section 141 IPC.

force.meaning: Application of physical power against a person or property.

violence.meaning: Use of force causing injury, damage, intimidation, or disturbance.

common_object.meaning: The shared unlawful purpose of the assembly.

important_rule.principle: If even one member uses force or violence in pursuit of the common object, every member of the unlawful assembly may be guilty of rioting.

when_section_146_applies.condition: An unlawful assembly attacks a person to enforce its objective.
A member of an unlawful assembly damages property while pursuing the common object.
when_section_146_applies.result: Every member may be guilty of rioting.

when_section_146_applies.condition: A mob uses force to prevent the execution of a lawful order.

when_section_146_does_not_apply.situation: No force or violence is used.
when_section_146_does_not_apply.reason: Force or violence is an essential ingredient.

when_section_146_does_not_apply.situation: The force used is unrelated to the common object.
when_section_146_does_not_apply.reason: The force must be used in prosecution of the common object.

examples.scenario: Six persons gather to forcibly occupy land. One member assaults the owner while the others support the objective.
examples.result: All members are guilty of rioting.

examples.scenario: A mob damages public property while protesting unlawfully.
examples.result: The offence of rioting is committed.

examples.scenario: Five persons gather unlawfully but disperse without using force or violence.
examples.result: Rioting is not committed, though unlawful assembly may exist.

judicial_principles: Actual participation in violence by every member is unnecessary.
Use of force by one member can make all members liable.
Common object is the foundation of collective liability.
Rioting begins when force or violence is employed.
The prosecution must establish a connection between the violence and the common object.

rioting.requirement: Unlawful assembly plus use of force or violence.

relationship_with_other_sections.section_144: Joining unlawful assembly armed with deadly weapon.
relationship_with_other_sections.section_145: Joining or continuing after command to disperse.
relationship_with_other_sections.section_149: Liability of every member of unlawful assembly.

faqs.question: What is rioting under Section 146 IPC?
faqs.answer: Rioting occurs when force or violence is used by an unlawful assembly or any of its members in pursuit of the assembly's common object.

faqs.question: Must every member use force?
faqs.answer: No. Force used by even one member can make all members liable.

faqs.question: Does Section 146 prescribe punishment?
faqs.answer: No. Punishment is provided under Section 147 IPC.

faqs.question: Can there be rioting without an unlawful assembly?
faqs.answer: No. An unlawful assembly is a mandatory requirement.

max_punishment: Not Applicable (Definition Section)
ai_summary: Section 146 IPC defines rioting as the use of force or violence by an unlawful assembly or any of its members in pursuit of the assembly's common object. Once such force or violence is used, every member of the unlawful assembly becomes guilty of rioting, even if only one member actually committed the violent act.

title: Punishment for Rioting
chapter: Chapter VIII - Of Offences Against the Public Tranquillity
section_text: Whoever is guilty of rioting shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.
meaning: Section 147 prescribes the punishment for the offence of rioting as defined under Section 146 IPC. Any person who is a member of an unlawful assembly that uses force or violence in pursuit of its common object is liable under this section.
key_points: Applies to persons guilty of rioting.
Rioting is defined under Section 146 IPC.
Force or violence must be used by the unlawful assembly or its members.
Every member of the unlawful assembly may be held liable.
Actual participation in violence by every member is not necessary.
Punishment may include imprisonment, fine, or both.
The provision aims to maintain public peace and order.
legal_significance: Section 147 provides the punishment for rioting and acts as a deterrent against mob violence, public disorder, and collective unlawful conduct involving force or violence.
essential_elements: There must be an unlawful assembly.
The unlawful assembly must use force or violence.
The force or violence must be used in pursuit of the common object.
The accused must be a member of the assembly.

unlawful_assembly.meaning: An assembly of five or more persons with a common unlawful object under Section 141 IPC.

force_or_violence.meaning: Physical force or violent conduct used in furtherance of the common object.

membership.meaning: Being a member of the unlawful assembly at the time of rioting.

when_section_147_applies.condition: An unlawful assembly uses violence to achieve its objective.
when_section_147_applies.result: All members may be punished under Section 147.

when_section_147_applies.condition: A mob damages property while pursuing its common object.
Members of an unlawful assembly assault a person to enforce their demands.

when_section_147_does_not_apply.situation: No force or violence is used.

when_section_147_does_not_apply.reason: Rioting can arise only from an unlawful assembly.

when_section_147_does_not_apply.situation: The accused is not a member of the unlawful assembly.

examples.scenario: Six persons gather unlawfully and attack a landowner to seize property.
examples.result: All members are guilty of rioting and punishable under Section 147.

examples.scenario: A mob damages buses and public property during a violent protest.
examples.result: Section 147 applies.

examples.scenario: An unlawful assembly disperses without using force or violence.
examples.result: Section 147 does not apply, though Section 143 may apply.

judicial_principles: Rioting requires force or violence.
Collective liability applies to all members.
Actual violence by every member is unnecessary.
The common object links members to the offence.
Public peace and order are the interests protected.

relationship_with_other_sections.section_144: Joining unlawful assembly armed with deadly weapon.
relationship_with_other_sections.section_145: Joining unlawful assembly after command to disperse.
relationship_with_other_sections.section_149: Liability of members for offences committed in prosecution of common object.

faqs.question: What is punished under Section 147 IPC?
faqs.answer: The offence of rioting.

faqs.question: What is the maximum punishment?
faqs.answer: Imprisonment up to two years, or fine, or both.

faqs.question: Is actual violence by every member necessary?
faqs.answer: No. Every member may be liable if force or violence is used in pursuit of the common object.

faqs.question: Which section defines rioting?
faqs.answer: Section 146 IPC.

max_punishment: 2 years imprisonment, or fine, or both
ai_summary: Section 147 IPC provides punishment for the offence of rioting. When an unlawful assembly uses force or violence in pursuit of its common object, every member of that assembly may be held guilty of rioting and punished with imprisonment up to two years, fine, or both.

title: Rioting, Armed with Deadly Weapon
chapter: Chapter VIII - Of Offences Against the Public Tranquillity
section_text: Whoever is guilty of rioting, being armed with a deadly weapon or with anything which, used as a weapon of offence, is likely to cause death, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both.
meaning: Section 148 IPC punishes a person who commits rioting while carrying a deadly weapon or any object capable of causing death when used as a weapon. It is an aggravated form of the offence of rioting under Section 147 IPC.
key_points: The accused must be guilty of rioting.
The accused must be armed with a deadly weapon or dangerous object.
Actual use of the weapon is not necessary.
Possession of the weapon during rioting is sufficient.
It is a more serious offence than ordinary rioting.
The law recognizes the increased danger posed by armed mobs.
Punishment is higher than that prescribed under Section 147.
legal_significance: Section 148 aims to deter violent assemblies carrying weapons. Armed rioters pose a greater threat to public safety, and therefore the law prescribes a stricter punishment.
essential_elements: There must be rioting as defined under Section 146 IPC.
The accused must be a member of the rioting assembly.
The accused must be armed with a deadly weapon or dangerous object.
The weapon must be capable of causing death if used offensively.

rioting.meaning: Use of force or violence by an unlawful assembly in pursuit of its common object.

deadly_weapon.meaning: A weapon inherently capable of causing death, such as firearms, swords, daggers, or spears.

dangerous_object.meaning: Any object which, when used offensively, is likely to cause death.

armed.meaning: Carrying or possessing the weapon during the commission of rioting.

when_section_148_applies.condition: A rioter carries a sword during a violent mob attack.
A member of a rioting crowd possesses a firearm.
A violent mob member carries an iron rod capable of causing death.

when_section_148_does_not_apply.situation: The object carried is not capable of causing death when used as a weapon.
when_section_148_does_not_apply.reason: It does not qualify as a deadly weapon or dangerous object.

examples.scenario: A group of rioters attacks a market, and A carries a sword while participating in the violence.
examples.result: A is punishable under Section 148.

examples.scenario: A member of a rioting mob possesses a loaded pistol, even though he does not fire it.
examples.result: Section 148 applies.

examples.scenario: A person participates in rioting without carrying any weapon.
examples.result: He may be liable under Section 147, not Section 148.

judicial_principles: Actual use of the weapon is unnecessary.
Possession of a deadly weapon during rioting is sufficient.
The offence is aggravated because of increased danger to life.
The prosecution must prove both rioting and possession of the weapon.
Public safety is the primary interest protected.

section_148.offence: Rioting while armed with a deadly weapon.

section_144.offence: Membership of unlawful assembly while armed.

relationship_with_other_sections.section_144: Joining unlawful assembly armed with deadly weapon.
relationship_with_other_sections.section_149: Liability of every member of unlawful assembly.

faqs.question: What is punished under Section 148 IPC?
faqs.answer: Rioting while armed with a deadly weapon or dangerous object capable of causing death.

faqs.question: Is actual use of the weapon necessary?
faqs.answer: No. Carrying the weapon during rioting is sufficient.

faqs.question: What is the maximum punishment?
faqs.answer: Imprisonment up to three years, or fine, or both.

faqs.question: How is Section 148 different from Section 147?
faqs.answer: Section 148 applies when the rioter is armed with a deadly weapon, making it a more serious offence.

max_punishment: 3 years imprisonment, or fine, or both
ai_summary: Section 148 IPC deals with the aggravated offence of rioting while armed with a deadly weapon or any object capable of causing death if used offensively. The prosecution must prove both rioting and possession of the weapon. Because armed rioting poses a greater threat to public safety, the punishment is enhanced to imprisonment up to three years, fine, or both.

title: Every Member of Unlawful Assembly Guilty of Offence Committed in Prosecution of Common Object
chapter: Chapter VIII - Of Offences Against the Public Tranquillity
section_text: If an offence is committed by any member of an unlawful assembly in prosecution of the common object of that assembly, or such as the members of that assembly knew to be likely to be committed in prosecution of that object, every person who, at the time of the committing of that offence, is a member of the same assembly, is guilty of that offence.
meaning: Section 149 IPC creates the principle of collective or constructive criminal liability. If a member of an unlawful assembly commits an offence in furtherance of the assembly's common object, or an offence that the members knew was likely to be committed, every member of that unlawful assembly becomes guilty of that offence.
key_points: Applies only when there is an unlawful assembly.
At least five persons are required.
An offence must be committed by a member of the assembly.
The offence must be connected with the common object.
All members become liable even if they did not personally commit the act.
Actual participation is not always necessary.
The section creates vicarious or constructive criminal liability.
legal_significance: Section 149 is one of the most important provisions relating to group crimes. It prevents members of unlawful assemblies from escaping liability by claiming that only one person actually committed the offence.
essential_elements: There must be an unlawful assembly under Section 141 IPC.
The accused must be a member of the assembly.
An offence must be committed by one or more members.
The offence must be committed in prosecution of the common object or be one that members knew was likely to occur.
The accused must be a member at the time of the offence.

unlawful_assembly.meaning: An assembly of five or more persons having a common object specified under Section 141 IPC.

common_object.meaning: The shared purpose that unites the members of the unlawful assembly.

membership.meaning: Being a member of the assembly when the offence is committed.

offence_committed.meaning: Any criminal act committed by a member in furtherance of the common object.

knowledge.meaning: Members knew that such an offence was likely to be committed.

first.rule: The offence is committed directly in prosecution of the common object.
first.example: A mob gathers to assault a person and one member causes grievous hurt.

second.rule: The offence was likely to be committed in pursuit of the common object and members knew of that likelihood.
second.example: A violent armed mob attacks a house and one member commits murder.

punishment.rule: There is no separate punishment under Section 149.
punishment.effect: Every member receives the same punishment prescribed for the offence committed.

when_section_149_applies.condition: Five persons form an unlawful assembly to attack a rival group and one member commits murder.
when_section_149_applies.result: All members may be guilty of murder under Section 149.

when_section_149_applies.condition: A mob unlawfully gathers to destroy property and members commit mischief.
Members know that serious violence is likely during execution of the common object.

when_section_149_does_not_apply.situation: The assembly consists of fewer than five persons.
The offence has no connection with the common object.
The accused was not a member when the offence occurred.
when_section_149_does_not_apply.reason: Membership at the relevant time is essential.

when_section_149_does_not_apply.situation: The offence was completely outside the knowledge and object of the assembly.

examples.scenario: Ten persons gather to beat Z. During the assault, one member kills Z.
examples.result: All members may be liable for murder if the killing was in prosecution of the common object or was likely to occur.

examples.scenario: A mob gathers to forcibly occupy land and damages buildings during the process.
examples.result: Every member is liable for the offence committed.

examples.scenario: A member suddenly commits a completely unrelated personal crime during the gathering.
examples.result: Other members may not be liable if the act was outside the common object.

judicial_principles: Section 149 creates constructive liability.
Active participation in the offence is not necessary.
Presence as a member of the unlawful assembly may be sufficient.
Common object is different from common intention under Section 34 IPC.
The prosecution must prove membership and existence of the common object.

section_34.nature: Prior meeting of minds is important.

section_149.nature: Membership of unlawful assembly is sufficient.

section_149.purpose: Fixes liability on every member for offences committed by any member.

faqs.question: What is the purpose of Section 149 IPC?
faqs.answer: To make every member of an unlawful assembly liable for offences committed in pursuit of the common object.

faqs.question: Must every member personally commit the offence?
faqs.answer: No. Membership itself may create liability.

faqs.question: How many persons are required?
faqs.answer: At least five persons.

faqs.question: Does Section 149 provide separate punishment?
faqs.answer: No. Members receive the punishment prescribed for the actual offence committed.

max_punishment: Depends on the offence committed
ai_summary: Section 149 IPC establishes the rule of constructive criminal liability. If a member of an unlawful assembly commits an offence in furtherance of the assembly's common object, or an offence that members knew was likely to occur, every member of that assembly becomes guilty of that offence. This provision is widely used in cases involving mob violence, rioting, group assaults, and communal disturbances.

title: Hiring, or Conniving at Hiring, of Persons to Join Unlawful Assembly
chapter: Chapter VIII - Of Offences Against the Public Tranquillity
section_text: Whoever hires or engages, or employs, or promotes, or connives at the hiring, engagement or employment of any person to join or become a member of any unlawful assembly, shall be punishable as a member of such unlawful assembly, and for any offence which may be committed by any such person as a member of such unlawful assembly in pursuance of such hiring, engagement or employment, in the same manner as if he had been a member of such unlawful assembly, or himself had committed such offence.
meaning: Section 150 IPC punishes any person who recruits, hires, employs, encourages, or knowingly allows others to be hired for joining an unlawful assembly. Such a person is treated as if he were himself a member of that unlawful assembly and may also be held liable for offences committed by the hired persons in furtherance of that unlawful assembly.
key_points: Applies to hiring or recruiting persons for an unlawful assembly.
Actual physical presence in the assembly is not necessary.
The hirer is treated as a member of the unlawful assembly.
Liability extends to offences committed by the hired persons.
Connivance in hiring is also punishable.
The section prevents organized mob violence through hired participants.
Constructive criminal liability is imposed.
legal_significance: Section 150 targets the organizers, financiers, recruiters, and instigators behind unlawful assemblies. It prevents persons from escaping liability by acting through hired individuals instead of personally participating.
essential_elements: There must be an unlawful assembly.
The accused must hire, engage, employ, promote, or connive at the hiring of persons.
The hiring must be for joining or becoming members of the unlawful assembly.
The hired persons must participate pursuant to such hiring or engagement.

hiring.meaning: Paying or promising benefits to induce a person to join an unlawful assembly.

engagement_or_employment.meaning: Recruiting or arranging participation in an unlawful assembly.

connivance.meaning: Knowingly permitting or secretly assisting the hiring process.

unlawful_assembly.meaning: An assembly of five or more persons having a common object described in Section 141 IPC.

scope_of_liability.first_level: The accused is punishable as a member of the unlawful assembly.
scope_of_liability.second_level: The accused is also liable for offences committed by the hired persons in furtherance of the unlawful assembly.
scope_of_liability.principle: The law treats the hirer as if he personally participated.

punishment.rule: The punishment is the same as that applicable to a member of the unlawful assembly and for offences committed by the hired persons.
punishment.depends_on: Nature of the unlawful assembly and offences actually committed.

when_section_150_applies.condition: A person pays individuals to join a mob intended to attack another group.
A political organizer secretly recruits people for a violent unlawful assembly.
A person knowingly facilitates the hiring of persons to participate in a violent mob.

when_section_150_does_not_apply.situation: The accused had no involvement in hiring or recruitment.
The accused unknowingly assists persons without knowledge of the unlawful purpose.
when_section_150_does_not_apply.reason: Connivance or intentional involvement is not proved.

examples.scenario: A pays twenty persons to join a mob that plans to forcibly occupy land.
examples.result: A is punishable as a member of the unlawful assembly.

examples.scenario: The hired mob assaults the landowner during the occupation.
examples.result: A may also be liable for the assault as if he committed it himself.

examples.scenario: A knowingly arranges transport and payment for persons recruited into a violent unlawful assembly.
examples.result: Section 150 applies.

judicial_principles: Actual presence at the scene is unnecessary.
Organizers and financiers can be held liable.
Constructive liability extends to offences committed by hired members.
The law targets the masterminds behind unlawful assemblies.
Liability is based on recruitment and facilitation of unlawful participation.

section_149.focus: Liability of actual members of an unlawful assembly.

section_150.focus: Liability of persons who hire or recruit others to join an unlawful assembly.

relationship_with_other_sections.section_149: Liability of members for offences committed in prosecution of common object.
relationship_with_other_sections.section_150: Liability of persons hiring or recruiting members for unlawful assemblies.

faqs.question: Who can be punished under Section 150 IPC?
faqs.answer: Anyone who hires, recruits, employs, promotes, or connives at hiring persons to join an unlawful assembly.

faqs.question: Must the accused be physically present in the unlawful assembly?
faqs.answer: No. Physical presence is not necessary.

faqs.question: Can the accused be liable for offences committed by the hired persons?
faqs.answer: Yes. The section treats the accused as if he were a member of the unlawful assembly.

faqs.question: What is the purpose of Section 150?
faqs.answer: To punish organizers and recruiters who create or strengthen unlawful assemblies through hired participants.

max_punishment: Depends on the offence committed by the unlawful assembly
ai_summary: Section 150 IPC imposes liability on persons who hire, recruit, employ, promote, or knowingly facilitate the hiring of individuals to join an unlawful assembly. Such persons are treated as members of the unlawful assembly and may also be punished for offences committed by the hired participants in furtherance of the assembly's common object, even if they were not physically present.

title: Knowingly Joining or Continuing in Assembly of Five or More Persons After It Has Been Commanded to Disperse
chapter: Chapter VIII - Of Offences Against the Public Tranquillity
section_text: Whoever knowingly joins or continues in any assembly of five or more persons likely to cause a disturbance of the public peace, after such assembly has been lawfully commanded to disperse, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine, or with both. Explanation.—If the assembly is an unlawful assembly within the meaning of section 141, the offender will be punishable under section 145.
meaning: Section 151 IPC punishes a person who knowingly joins or remains in an assembly of five or more persons that is likely to disturb public peace after it has been lawfully ordered to disperse.
key_points: The assembly must consist of five or more persons.
The assembly must be likely to disturb public peace.
A lawful order to disperse must have been given.
The accused must knowingly join or continue in the assembly.
Actual violence is not necessary.
The offence protects public order and tranquility.
If the assembly is unlawful under Section 141, Section 145 applies instead.
legal_significance: Section 151 empowers authorities to maintain public peace by penalizing persons who deliberately ignore lawful dispersal orders, even when the assembly has not yet become an unlawful assembly under Section 141 IPC.
essential_elements: There must be an assembly of five or more persons.
The assembly must be likely to disturb public peace.
A lawful command to disperse must be issued.
The accused must know of the command.
The accused must join or continue in the assembly after the command.

assembly.meaning: A gathering of five or more persons.

disturbance_of_public_peace.meaning: A likelihood of causing disorder, unrest, or breach of peace.

lawful_command.meaning: An order issued by a competent authority requiring the assembly to disperse.

knowledge.meaning: Awareness that the dispersal order has been issued.

continuance_or_joining.meaning: Remaining in or joining the assembly after the order.

explanation.rule: If the assembly qualifies as an unlawful assembly under Section 141 IPC, punishment will be under Section 145 IPC instead of Section 151.

when_section_151_applies.condition: A crowd of five or more persons likely to disturb public peace refuses to disperse after a lawful police order.
A person knowingly joins such an assembly after the dispersal order has been announced.
Members remain in the assembly despite being aware of the lawful order.

when_section_151_does_not_apply.situation: No lawful order to disperse was issued.
The accused was unaware of the dispersal order.
The gathering consists of fewer than five persons.
when_section_151_does_not_apply.reason: The minimum number requirement is not met.

when_section_151_does_not_apply.situation: The accused immediately leaves after the order.

examples.scenario: Police order a gathering of six persons likely to cause unrest to disperse. A remains in the crowd despite hearing the order.
examples.result: A is punishable under Section 151.

examples.scenario: B joins a crowd after a lawful dispersal order has already been announced.
examples.result: Section 151 applies.

examples.scenario: C leaves the assembly immediately after hearing the order.
examples.result: Section 151 does not apply.

judicial_principles: Knowledge of the dispersal order is essential.
Actual violence is not required.
The likelihood of disturbing public peace is sufficient.
The section operates even when the assembly is not technically unlawful under Section 141.
Public order and obedience to lawful authority are the interests protected.

section_151.applies_to: Assembly likely to disturb public peace but not necessarily an unlawful assembly.

relationship_with_other_sections.section_145: Joining or continuing in unlawful assembly after command to disperse.
relationship_with_other_sections.section_151: Joining or continuing in certain assemblies after command to disperse.
relationship_with_other_sections.section_152: Assaulting or obstructing public servant suppressing riot.

faqs.question: What is punished under Section 151 IPC?
faqs.answer: Knowingly joining or remaining in an assembly of five or more persons likely to disturb public peace after it has been lawfully ordered to disperse.

faqs.question: Is actual violence necessary?
faqs.answer: No. The likelihood of disturbing public peace is sufficient.

faqs.question: What is the maximum punishment?
faqs.answer: Imprisonment up to six months, or fine, or both.

faqs.question: When does Section 145 apply instead of Section 151?
faqs.answer: When the assembly is an unlawful assembly under Section 141 IPC.

important_concepts: Assembly of five or more persons.
max_punishment: 6 months imprisonment, or fine, or both
ai_summary: Section 151 IPC punishes any person who knowingly joins or continues in an assembly of five or more persons likely to disturb public peace after a lawful order to disperse has been issued. The offence is designed to prevent breaches of public order and carries a maximum punishment of six months' imprisonment, fine, or both. If the assembly is an unlawful assembly under Section 141 IPC, Section 145 applies instead.

title: Assaulting or Obstructing Public Servant When Suppressing Riot, etc.
chapter: Chapter VIII - Of Offences Against the Public Tranquillity
section_text: Whoever assaults or threatens to assault, or obstructs or attempts to obstruct, any public servant in the discharge of his duty as such public servant, in endeavouring to disperse an unlawful assembly, or to suppress a riot or affray, or uses, or threatens, or attempts to use criminal force to such public servant, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both.
meaning: Section 152 IPC punishes any person who assaults, threatens, obstructs, or uses criminal force against a public servant who is performing official duties related to dispersing an unlawful assembly or suppressing a riot or affray.
key_points: The victim must be a public servant.
The public servant must be acting in the discharge of official duty.
The duty must relate to dispersing an unlawful assembly or suppressing a riot or affray.
Assault, threat, obstruction, or criminal force is sufficient.
Actual injury is not necessary.
Attempted obstruction is also punishable.
The section protects law enforcement and public order.
legal_significance: Section 152 protects public servants engaged in maintaining law and order. It ensures that officials can perform their duties without unlawful interference, intimidation, or violence.
essential_elements: The person affected must be a public servant.
The public servant must be acting in the discharge of official duties.
The duty must involve dispersing an unlawful assembly or suppressing a riot or affray.
The accused must assault, threaten, obstruct, attempt to obstruct, or use criminal force.

public_servant.meaning: A person falling within the legal definition of a public servant under law.

official_duty.meaning: Lawful duty performed as part of official responsibilities.

assault.meaning: An act creating apprehension of immediate use of criminal force.

obstruction.meaning: Preventing or hindering the public servant from performing official duties.

criminal_force.meaning: Intentional use of force without consent to commit an offence or cause injury, fear, or annoyance.

riot_or_affray.meaning: Public disorder involving violence or fighting.
