faqs.question: What is the maximum punishment under Section 171H?
faqs.answer: Fine up to ₹500.

max_punishment: Fine up to ₹500
ai_summary: Section 171H IPC punishes unauthorized election expenditure incurred for promoting a candidate without the candidate's written approval. The provision seeks to maintain transparency in campaign financing and prevent hidden election spending. The punishment is a fine that may extend to ₹500.

title: Failure to Keep Election Accounts
chapter: Chapter IXA - Of Offences Relating to Elections
section_text: Whoever being required by any law for the time being in force or any rule having the force of law to keep accounts of expenses incurred at or in connection with an election fails to keep such accounts shall be punished with fine which may extend to five hundred rupees.
meaning: Section 171I IPC punishes a person who is legally required to maintain election expense accounts but fails to keep such accounts.
legal_significance: The section promotes transparency and accountability in election financing. It ensures that candidates and other persons required by law maintain proper records of election expenditure so that election authorities can verify compliance with election laws.
essential_elements: The person must be legally required to maintain election expense accounts.
The requirement must arise from a law or rule having the force of law.
The accounts must relate to expenses incurred at or in connection with an election.
The person fails to keep the required accounts.

legal_requirement.meaning: A statutory provision, election law, or valid rule requires maintenance of accounts.

election_expenses.meaning: Expenses incurred for campaigning, meetings, advertisements, publicity, travel, or other election-related activities.

accounts.meaning: Proper records, statements, books, vouchers, or documents showing election expenditure.

failure_to_keep.meaning: Not maintaining, preserving, or recording the required expenditure details.

purpose_of_the_section: Assist election authorities in auditing expenses.
Prevent misuse of money power in elections.

when_section_171I_applies.condition: A candidate is legally required to maintain election expenditure records but keeps no accounts.
Required election accounts are deliberately not maintained.
A person subject to election accounting rules fails to preserve expenditure records.

when_section_171I_does_not_apply.situation: The person is not legally required to maintain election accounts.
Proper election expenditure records are maintained.
The expenses are unrelated to an election.
when_section_171I_does_not_apply.reason: The section applies only to election-related expenses.

examples.scenario: A candidate spends money on election rallies and advertisements but keeps no expenditure records.
examples.result: A commits an offence under Section 171I.

examples.scenario: A person required by election rules maintains incomplete or no accounts of campaign expenses.
examples.result: Section 171I may apply.

examples.scenario: A candidate properly records all election expenses and preserves supporting documents.
examples.result: No offence under Section 171I.

judicial_principles: Election finance transparency is essential for democratic governance.
Proper accounting helps detect illegal election expenditure.
Compliance with election accounting rules is mandatory where prescribed by law.
The offence focuses on failure to maintain records rather than the amount spent.

relationship_with_other_sections.section_171I: Punishes failure to maintain election expenditure accounts.
relationship_with_other_sections.representation_of_the_people_act_1951: Contains detailed provisions regarding election expenses and maintenance of accounts.

section_171H.focus: Unauthorized election spending without written authority of the candidate.

section_171I.focus: Failure to maintain legally required election expenditure records.

faqs.question: What is punished under Section 171I IPC?
faqs.answer: Failure to maintain election expense accounts when required by law.

faqs.question: Is imprisonment prescribed under Section 171I?
faqs.answer: No. The punishment is fine only.

faqs.question: Why are election accounts important?
faqs.answer: They ensure transparency and help monitor election expenditure.

faqs.question: What is the maximum punishment under Section 171I IPC?
faqs.answer: Fine up to ₹500.

max_punishment: Fine up to ₹500
ai_summary: Section 171I IPC punishes a person who is legally required to maintain accounts of election expenses but fails to do so. The provision promotes transparency and accountability in election financing by ensuring proper maintenance of expenditure records. The punishment prescribed is a fine that may extend to ₹500.

title: Absconding to Avoid Service of Summons or Other Proceeding
chapter: Chapter X - Of Contempts of the Lawful Authority of Public Servants
section_text: Whoever absconds in order to avoid being served with a summons, notice or order proceeding from any public servant legally competent to issue such summons, notice or order, shall be punished with simple imprisonment for a term which may extend to one month, or with fine which may extend to five hundred rupees, or with both. If the summons, notice or order requires attendance in person or by agent, or requires production of a document or electronic record in a Court of Justice, the punishment may extend to six months imprisonment, or fine up to one thousand rupees, or both.
meaning: Section 172 IPC punishes a person who deliberately hides, absconds, or keeps out of the way to avoid receiving a legally issued summons, notice, or order from a competent public servant.
legal_significance: The section ensures that legal proceedings are not obstructed by persons intentionally avoiding service of official notices, court summons, or lawful orders.
essential_elements: The accused absconds or conceals himself.
The purpose of absconding is to avoid service of a summons, notice, or order.
The summons, notice, or order is issued by a legally competent public servant.
There is intentional avoidance of lawful service.

absconding.meaning: Hiding, disappearing, changing location, or intentionally remaining unavailable to avoid service.

summons_notice_or_order.meaning: A lawful communication requiring appearance, compliance, production of records, or other legal action.

competent_public_servant.meaning: A public servant legally authorized to issue such summons, notice, or order.

intent.meaning: The absconding must be done specifically to avoid service.

category_1.offence: Avoiding ordinary summons, notice, or order

category_2.offence: Avoiding summons requiring attendance or production of document/electronic record in court

when_section_172_applies.condition: A person intentionally leaves his residence to avoid receiving a court summons.
A witness hides to avoid a summons requiring court attendance.
A person deliberately avoids receiving an order requiring production of documents before a court.

when_section_172_does_not_apply.situation: The person had no knowledge of the attempted service.
The absence was due to genuine reasons such as illness or travel.
The summons or notice was not issued by a legally competent authority.

examples.scenario: A learns that a court summons will be served on him and deliberately goes into hiding.
examples.result: A commits an offence under Section 172 IPC.

examples.scenario: B changes his address and conceals his whereabouts to avoid producing records before a court.
examples.result: Section 172 applies.

examples.scenario: C misses service of a summons because he was hospitalized and unaware of the notice.
examples.result: Section 172 generally does not apply.

judicial_principles: Intentional avoidance is the essence of the offence.
Mere absence is not sufficient.
The prosecution must prove the purpose of absconding.
Lawful judicial and administrative processes must not be obstructed.
Enhanced punishment applies where court attendance or document production is involved.

relationship_with_other_sections.section_172: Absconding to avoid service of summons or orders.
relationship_with_other_sections.section_173: Preventing service of summons or other proceedings.
relationship_with_other_sections.section_174: Non-attendance in obedience to an order from a public servant.
relationship_with_other_sections.section_175: Omission to produce documents or electronic records.

faqs.question: What is punished under Section 172 IPC?
faqs.answer: Intentionally absconding to avoid service of a lawful summons, notice, or order.

faqs.question: Is merely being absent enough to commit the offence?
faqs.answer: No. There must be intentional avoidance of service.

faqs.question: Why is the punishment higher for court-related summons?
faqs.answer: Because court attendance and document production are essential for the administration of justice.

faqs.question: Does the section apply to electronic records?
faqs.answer: Yes. The provision covers orders requiring production of documents or electronic records.

max_punishment: 6 months simple imprisonment, or fine up to ₹1000, or both

information_technology_act_2000.change: Added the words 'electronic record' to the provision.

ai_summary: Section 172 IPC punishes a person who intentionally absconds or conceals himself to avoid receiving a lawful summons, notice, or order issued by a competent public servant. The punishment is enhanced when the summons requires court attendance or production of a document or electronic record. The maximum punishment is six months' simple imprisonment, a fine up to ₹1000, or both.

title: Preventing Service of Summons or Other Proceeding, or Preventing Publication Thereof
chapter: Chapter X - Of Contempts of the Lawful Authority of Public Servants
section_text: Whoever intentionally prevents the service of a summons, notice or order issued by a legally competent public servant, prevents its lawful affixing, removes it after lawful affixing, or prevents the lawful making of a proclamation, shall be punished. Enhanced punishment applies when the summons, notice, order or proclamation requires attendance before a court or production of a document or electronic record.
meaning: Section 173 IPC punishes any person who deliberately obstructs the service, publication, affixing, or execution of lawful summonses, notices, orders, or proclamations issued by competent public authorities.
legal_significance: The section protects the administration of justice and public administration by ensuring that official communications and legal processes reach the persons concerned without unlawful interference.
essential_elements: There must be a lawful summons, notice, order, or proclamation.
It must be issued by a legally competent public servant.
The accused intentionally interferes with its service, affixing, publication, or execution.
The interference must be deliberate and knowing.

acts_punishable_under_section_173.description: Intentionally preventing a summons, notice, or order from being served on oneself or another person.
Stopping officials from lawfully affixing a summons, notice, or order at a prescribed location.
Removing a summons, notice, or order that has been lawfully affixed.
Obstructing the lawful publication of a proclamation ordered by a competent public servant.

intentional_act.meaning: The obstruction must be deliberate and not accidental.

lawful_process.meaning: The summons, notice, order, or proclamation must be legally issued.

competent_authority.meaning: The issuing public servant must have legal authority to issue the process.

court_related_case.condition: When attendance before a court or production of a document/electronic record is required.

when_section_173_applies.condition: A person hides a court summons to prevent its delivery to the intended recipient.
A person removes a notice lawfully pasted by a public authority.
A person prevents a public proclamation ordered by a magistrate.
A person obstructs service of a court order requiring document production.

when_section_173_does_not_apply.situation: The removal or obstruction was accidental.
The notice was not lawfully issued.
when_section_173_does_not_apply.reason: A valid legal process is necessary.

when_section_173_does_not_apply.situation: The person had no knowledge of the affixed notice.

examples.scenario: A removes a summons pasted outside B's house to prevent B from learning about court proceedings.
examples.result: A commits an offence under Section 173.

examples.scenario: A blocks officials from publicly announcing a lawful proclamation.
examples.result: Section 173 applies.

examples.scenario: A tears down a court notice requiring production of records.
examples.result: Enhanced punishment under Section 173 may apply.

judicial_principles: Intentional obstruction is the core element of the offence.
Legal processes must be allowed to operate effectively.
The offence protects both judicial and administrative proceedings.
The prosecution must prove deliberate interference.

section_172.focus: Person absconds to avoid being served.

section_173.focus: Person actively prevents service or publication of legal process.

relationship_with_other_sections.section_172: Absconding to avoid service of summons.
relationship_with_other_sections.section_173: Preventing service or publication of summons and notices.
relationship_with_other_sections.section_174: Non-attendance in obedience to an order.
relationship_with_other_sections.section_175: Omission to produce documents or electronic records.

faqs.question: What is punished under Section 173 IPC?
faqs.answer: Intentionally preventing service, affixing, publication, or execution of lawful summonses, notices, orders, or proclamations.

faqs.question: Is removing a lawfully affixed notice an offence?
faqs.answer: Yes. Intentional removal of a lawfully affixed notice is punishable.

faqs.question: What is the maximum punishment under Section 173 IPC?
faqs.answer: Up to 6 months' simple imprisonment, or fine up to ₹1000, or both in court-related cases.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Sections 172 and 173 IPC?
faqs.answer: Section 172 deals with absconding to avoid service, while Section 173 deals with actively preventing service or publication.

max_punishment: 6 months simple imprisonment, or fine up to ₹1000, or both

information_technology_act_2000.change: Added the words 'electronic record' to court-related document production requirements.

ai_summary: Section 173 IPC punishes any person who intentionally prevents the service, affixing, publication, or execution of lawful summonses, notices, orders, or proclamations issued by competent public authorities. The offence protects judicial and administrative processes from obstruction. The maximum punishment is six months' simple imprisonment, a fine up to ₹1000, or both when the matter relates to court attendance or production of documents or electronic records.

title: Non-Attendance in Obedience to an Order from Public Servant
chapter: Chapter X - Of Contempts of the Lawful Authority of Public Servants
section_text: Whoever, being legally bound to attend in person or by an agent at a certain place and time in obedience to a summons, notice, order, or proclamation issued by a legally competent public servant, intentionally omits to attend or leaves before being lawfully permitted to do so, commits an offence under this section.
meaning: Section 174 IPC punishes a person who is legally required to appear before a public authority or court but intentionally fails to attend or leaves before the proceedings are completed.
legal_significance: The section ensures obedience to lawful directions issued by public servants and courts. It helps public authorities and courts function effectively by compelling the attendance of persons whose presence is legally required.
essential_elements: A summons, notice, order, or proclamation must be lawfully issued.
The issuing authority must be a legally competent public servant.
The person must be legally bound to attend.
The person intentionally fails to attend or leaves before being lawfully permitted.
The omission must be deliberate and not accidental.

legal_obligation.meaning: A lawful duty to attend at a specified place and time.

competent_public_servant.meaning: A public servant legally authorized to issue the summons, notice, order, or proclamation.

intentional_omission.meaning: A deliberate failure to attend despite knowledge of the obligation.

premature_departure.meaning: Leaving the place of attendance before lawfully permitted.

court_related_case.condition: When attendance is required before a Court of Justice.

when_section_174_applies.condition: A witness intentionally ignores a court summons.
A person required to appear before a public authority deliberately fails to attend.
A witness appears in court but leaves before being permitted to do so.
A person ignores a lawful proclamation requiring attendance.

when_section_174_does_not_apply.situation: A person could not attend due to serious illness or unavoidable circumstances.
The summons was not legally served or issued.
The person had no knowledge of the summons.

illustrations_from_statute.illustration: A is legally bound to appear before the High Court in obedience to a subpoena but intentionally fails to appear.
illustrations_from_statute.result: A commits an offence under Section 174 IPC.

illustrations_from_statute.illustration: A is summoned as a witness before a District Judge and intentionally fails to attend.
illustrations_from_statute.result: A commits an offence under Section 174 IPC.

examples.scenario: A witness receives a valid court summons but deliberately chooses not to attend.
examples.result: Section 174 applies.

examples.scenario: A person required to appear before a revenue officer ignores the notice without justification.
examples.result: Section 174 applies.

examples.scenario: A fails to attend court because he is hospitalized and provides proof.
examples.result: Section 174 generally does not apply.

judicial_principles: Intentional non-compliance is the essence of the offence.
Mere absence is insufficient unless deliberate.
Lawful service and lawful authority must be established.
The section protects the effective functioning of courts and public authorities.

section_172.focus: Absconding to avoid service of summons or notice.

section_173.focus: Preventing service or publication of summons, notice, or proclamation.

section_174.focus: Failure to attend after lawful service of summons, notice, order, or proclamation.

relationship_with_other_sections.section_172: Absconding to avoid service of summons.
relationship_with_other_sections.section_173: Preventing service or publication of summons.
relationship_with_other_sections.section_175: Omission to produce documents or electronic records.

legal_application: Failure to appear as a witness.

faqs.question: What is punished under Section 174 IPC?
faqs.answer: Intentionally failing to attend after being lawfully summoned, notified, ordered, or proclaimed to appear.

faqs.question: Is leaving early from proceedings covered?
faqs.answer: Yes. Leaving before being lawfully permitted is also punishable.

faqs.question: What is the maximum punishment?
faqs.answer: Up to 6 months' simple imprisonment, or fine up to ₹1000, or both for court-related cases.

faqs.question: Does genuine inability to attend amount to an offence?
faqs.answer: Generally no, because intentional omission is a necessary ingredient.

max_punishment: 6 months simple imprisonment, or fine up to ₹1000, or both
ai_summary: Section 174 IPC punishes a person who is legally required to attend before a public servant or court but intentionally fails to do so or leaves before being lawfully permitted. The offence safeguards the functioning of courts and public authorities. The maximum punishment is six months' simple imprisonment, a fine up to ₹1000, or both when the attendance relates to a Court of Justice.

title: Non-Appearance in Response to a Proclamation under Section 82 CrPC
chapter: Chapter X - Of Contempts of the Lawful Authority of Public Servants
section_text: Whoever fails to appear at the specified place and time as required by a proclamation published under Section 82(1) of the Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973, shall be punished with imprisonment which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both. Where a declaration has been made under Section 82(4) of the Code of Criminal Procedure declaring him a proclaimed offender, he shall be punished with imprisonment which may extend to seven years and shall also be liable to fine.
meaning: Section 174A IPC punishes a person who fails to appear before a court after a proclamation has been lawfully issued under Section 82 of the Code of Criminal Procedure (CrPC).
legal_significance: The provision strengthens the criminal justice system by penalizing accused persons who evade court proceedings and ignore proclamations issued by courts.
essential_elements: A proclamation must be validly issued under Section 82 CrPC.
The proclamation must specify a place and time for appearance.
The accused must fail to appear as directed.
The failure must occur after lawful publication of the proclamation.

proclamation.meaning: A public declaration issued by a court requiring an absconding person to appear.

section_82_crpc.meaning: Provision allowing a court to issue a proclamation against a person who is absconding or concealing himself to avoid arrest.

non_appearance.meaning: Failure to appear at the specified place and time mentioned in the proclamation.

proclaimed_offender.meaning: A person formally declared a proclaimed offender under Section 82(4) CrPC for certain serious offences.

failure_to_appear_after_proclamation.description: Failure to appear in response to a proclamation under Section 82(1) CrPC.

proclaimed_offender_case.description: Failure to appear after being declared a proclaimed offender under Section 82(4) CrPC.

ordinary_case.offence: Failure to appear after proclamation under Section 82(1) CrPC

proclaimed_offender_case.offence: Person declared a proclaimed offender under Section 82(4) CrPC

when_section_174A_applies.condition: A court issues a proclamation under Section 82 CrPC and the person does not appear.
A proclaimed offender continues to evade court proceedings.
An absconding accused ignores the date and place specified in a valid proclamation.

when_section_174A_does_not_apply.situation: No valid proclamation under Section 82 CrPC was issued.
The proclamation was not properly published according to law.
when_section_174A_does_not_apply.reason: Legal requirements for proclamation must be satisfied.

when_section_174A_does_not_apply.situation: The person appears before the court within the prescribed time.

examples.scenario: A is an absconding accused. The court issues a proclamation under Section 82 CrPC requiring him to appear within the specified period, but he fails to do so.
examples.result: A commits an offence under Section 174A IPC.

examples.scenario: B is declared a proclaimed offender for a serious offence and still avoids appearing before the court.
examples.result: B is liable for enhanced punishment under Section 174A.

examples.scenario: C appears before the court as directed in the proclamation.
examples.result: No offence under Section 174A.

judicial_principles: The offence is independent of the original offence for which the accused is wanted.
Valid issuance and publication of the proclamation are essential.
The section aims to deter absconding accused persons.
Failure to appear after a proclamation is itself a punishable offence.

section_174.focus: Failure to attend in response to a summons, notice, order, or proclamation.

section_174A.focus: Failure to appear after a proclamation issued specifically under Section 82 CrPC.

relationship_with_other_sections.section_172: Absconding to avoid service of summons.
relationship_with_other_sections.section_173: Preventing service of summons or proclamation.
relationship_with_other_sections.section_174: Non-attendance in obedience to an order.
relationship_with_other_sections.section_174A: Non-appearance after proclamation under Section 82 CrPC.

faqs.question: What is punished under Section 174A IPC?
faqs.answer: Failure to appear after a proclamation has been issued under Section 82 CrPC.

faqs.question: What is a proclaimed offender?
faqs.answer: A person formally declared by a court under Section 82(4) CrPC for certain serious offences after failing to appear.

faqs.question: Can a person be punished separately under Section 174A even if the original case is pending?
faqs.answer: Yes. Section 174A creates an independent offence.

faqs.question: What is the maximum punishment under Section 174A?
faqs.answer: Up to 7 years' imprisonment and fine if the person is declared a proclaimed offender.

max_punishment: 7 years imprisonment and fine
ai_summary: Section 174A IPC punishes a person who fails to appear in response to a proclamation issued under Section 82 CrPC. If the person merely ignores the proclamation, the punishment may extend to 3 years, fine, or both. If the person is declared a proclaimed offender under Section 82(4) CrPC, the punishment may extend to 7 years and fine. The offence is separate from the original criminal case and aims to prevent accused persons from evading justice.

title: Omission to Produce Document or Electronic Record to Public Servant by Person Legally Bound to Produce It
chapter: Chapter X - Of Contempts of the Lawful Authority of Public Servants
section_text: Whoever, being legally bound to produce or deliver up any document or electronic record to any public servant, intentionally omits to produce or deliver up the same, shall be punished. Enhanced punishment applies where the document or electronic record is required to be produced before a Court of Justice.
meaning: Section 175 IPC punishes a person who is under a legal obligation to produce or hand over a document or electronic record to a public servant or court but intentionally fails to do so.
legal_significance: The section ensures cooperation with lawful investigations, inquiries, and judicial proceedings by requiring persons to produce documents and electronic records when legally obligated.
essential_elements: The accused is legally bound to produce or deliver a document or electronic record.
The document or electronic record is required by a public servant or court.
The accused intentionally omits to produce or deliver it.
The omission is deliberate and not accidental.

legal_obligation.meaning: A duty imposed by law, court order, summons, notice, or statutory provision.

document.meaning: Any written, printed, recorded, or official paper containing information.

electronic_record.meaning: Data, record, image, email, database, computer file, or other information stored electronically.

intentional_omission.meaning: A deliberate failure to produce or deliver the required material.

public_servant.meaning: A person falling within the definition of public servant under law.

ordinary_case.condition: Document or electronic record required by a public servant.

court_case.condition: Document or electronic record required by a Court of Justice.

when_section_175_applies.condition: A person deliberately refuses to produce documents demanded by a lawful authority.
A witness intentionally withholds records ordered by a court.
A company official refuses to submit electronic records required during an investigation.

when_section_175_does_not_apply.situation: The person is not legally bound to produce the document.
The document has been genuinely lost or destroyed without fault.
The authority demanding the document has no legal power to require it.

illustration_from_statute.scenario: A, being legally bound to produce a document before a District Court, intentionally omits to produce it.
illustration_from_statute.result: A commits an offence under Section 175 IPC.

examples.scenario: A taxpayer intentionally refuses to submit records demanded by an authorized officer.
examples.result: Section 175 may apply.

examples.scenario: A witness destroys or withholds an email record that a court ordered him to produce.
examples.result: Enhanced punishment under Section 175 may apply.

examples.scenario: A person cannot produce a document because it was lost in a fire and promptly informs the authority.
examples.result: Section 175 generally does not apply.

judicial_principles: Intentional refusal is the core ingredient of the offence.
The prosecution must prove a legal duty to produce the document.
Electronic records are treated similarly to physical documents.
The provision supports effective administration of justice and public administration.

section_174.focus: Failure to attend when legally required.

section_175.focus: Failure to produce documents or electronic records when legally required.

relationship_with_other_sections.section_172: Absconding to avoid service of summons.
relationship_with_other_sections.section_173: Preventing service of summons or proclamation.
relationship_with_other_sections.section_174: Non-attendance in obedience to an order.
relationship_with_other_sections.section_175: Omission to produce documents or electronic records.
relationship_with_other_sections.section_176: Omission to give notice or information to a public servant.

faqs.question: What is punished under Section 175 IPC?
faqs.answer: Intentionally failing to produce or deliver a document or electronic record when legally required.

faqs.question: Does the section apply to digital records?
faqs.answer: Yes. Electronic records are specifically included.

faqs.question: What is the maximum punishment?
faqs.answer: Up to 6 months' simple imprisonment, or fine up to ₹1000, or both when the document is required by a court.

faqs.question: Is accidental inability to produce a document punishable?
faqs.answer: No. The omission must be intentional.

max_punishment: 6 months simple imprisonment, or fine up to ₹1000, or both

information_technology_act_2000.change: Expanded the provision to include electronic records.

ai_summary: Section 175 IPC punishes a person who is legally required to produce a document or electronic record before a public servant or court but intentionally fails to do so. The offence safeguards investigations, administrative proceedings, and judicial processes. The maximum punishment is six months' simple imprisonment, a fine up to ₹1000, or both when the document or electronic record is required by a Court of Justice.

title: Omission to Give Notice or Information to Public Servant by Person Legally Bound to Give It
chapter: Chapter X - Of Contempts of the Lawful Authority of Public Servants
section_text: Whoever, being legally bound to give any notice or furnish information on any subject to a public servant, intentionally omits to give such notice or information in the manner and at the time required by law, commits an offence under this section.
meaning: Section 176 IPC punishes a person who is under a legal duty to provide notice or information to a public servant but intentionally fails to do so.
legal_significance: The section ensures that public servants receive information necessary for administration, law enforcement, crime prevention, and public safety. It penalizes deliberate concealment or non-reporting where the law imposes a duty to inform.
essential_elements: The accused must be legally bound to provide notice or information.
The notice or information must be required by law.
The information must be intended for a public servant.
The accused intentionally omits to provide the information.
The omission must occur in violation of the prescribed manner or time.

legal_duty.meaning: A duty imposed by statute, rule, regulation, or lawful order.

information.meaning: Facts, details, reports, or intelligence required by a public authority.

intentional_omission.meaning: Deliberate failure to provide information despite being legally obligated.

public_servant.meaning: A person legally recognized as a public servant under law.

categories_of_offence.description: Failure to provide legally required notice or information.
Failure to provide information regarding the commission, prevention, or investigation of an offence.

categories_of_offence.category: Information Required Under Court/Criminal Procedure Orders
categories_of_offence.description: Failure to provide information required by specified orders under criminal procedure law.

offence_related_case.condition: Information relates to commission, prevention, or detection of an offence.

special_order_case.condition: Information required under specified criminal procedure orders.

when_section_176_applies.condition: A person legally required to report a birth or death intentionally fails to do so.
A person legally bound to report knowledge of an offence deliberately withholds the information.
A person intentionally fails to provide information required by a lawful government order.

when_section_176_does_not_apply.situation: The person was not legally required to provide the information.
The omission was accidental or due to lack of knowledge.
The information was provided within the prescribed time and manner.

examples.scenario: A village officer is legally required to report a serious offence to the police but intentionally conceals it.
examples.result: Section 176 IPC applies.

examples.scenario: A person required by law to furnish information regarding a criminal suspect deliberately withholds the information.
examples.result: Enhanced punishment may apply.

examples.scenario: A citizen fails to submit a required notice because of a genuine mistake and promptly corrects it.
examples.result: Section 176 may not apply if intentional omission is absent.

judicial_principles: The existence of a legal duty is fundamental.
Intentional omission must be proved.
The offence focuses on failure to communicate required information.
Crime-related non-reporting is treated more seriously because it affects law enforcement.

section_175.focus: Failure to produce documents or electronic records.

section_176.focus: Failure to provide legally required notice or information.

relationship_with_other_sections.section_175: Omission to produce document or electronic record.
relationship_with_other_sections.section_176: Omission to give notice or information.
relationship_with_other_sections.section_177: Furnishing false information to a public servant.
relationship_with_other_sections.section_202: Intentional omission to give information of an offence.

faqs.question: What is punished under Section 176 IPC?
faqs.answer: Intentionally failing to provide notice or information that a person is legally required to give to a public servant.

faqs.question: Is crime-related information treated more seriously?
faqs.answer: Yes. Failure to provide information regarding offences can attract higher punishment.

faqs.question: Is a legal duty necessary?
faqs.answer: Yes. The section applies only when the law requires the person to provide the information.

faqs.question: What is the maximum punishment under Section 176 IPC?
faqs.answer: Up to 6 months' imprisonment and fine up to ₹1000 in serious cases.

max_punishment: 6 months imprisonment and fine up to ₹1000

act_22_of_1939.change: Added a special category relating to information required under criminal procedure orders.

ai_summary: Section 176 IPC punishes a person who intentionally fails to provide notice or information to a public servant when legally required to do so. The offence is treated more seriously when the information relates to the commission, prevention, or investigation of a crime. The maximum punishment is six months' imprisonment and a fine up to ₹1000.

title: Furnishing False Information
chapter: Chapter X - Of Contempts of the Lawful Authority of Public Servants
section_text: Whoever, being legally bound to furnish information on any subject to any public servant, furnishes as true information which he knows or has reason to believe to be false, commits an offence under this section.
meaning: Section 177 IPC punishes a person who is legally required to provide information to a public servant but knowingly supplies false information or information that he has reason to believe is false.
legal_significance: The section protects the integrity of public administration, investigations, and law enforcement by ensuring that public servants receive truthful information while performing official duties.
essential_elements: The accused is legally bound to furnish information.
The information is furnished to a public servant.
The information furnished is false.
The accused knows or has reason to believe that the information is false.
The information is presented as true.

legal_obligation.meaning: A duty imposed by law requiring the person to provide information.

public_servant.meaning: A person legally recognized as a public servant under law.

false_information.meaning: Information that is untrue, misleading, or deliberately inaccurate.

knowledge_of_falsity.meaning: The accused knows or reasonably believes that the information is false.

representation_as_true.meaning: The false information is intentionally presented as genuine and correct.

ordinary_case.condition: General false information furnished to a public servant.

offence_related_case.condition: False information relating to the commission, prevention, or investigation of an offence.

aggravated_form.description: Where the false information concerns the commission of an offence, prevention of an offence, or apprehension of an offender.
aggravated_form.maximum_punishment: 2 years imprisonment, or fine, or both.

when_section_177_applies.condition: A person knowingly gives false information to a government officer.
A witness intentionally provides false facts to a public authority.
A person falsely reports the cause of a crime to mislead authorities.

when_section_177_does_not_apply.situation: The person honestly believed the information to be true.
The person was not legally bound to provide the information.

statutory_illustrations.illustration: A landholder knows a murder occurred but falsely informs the Magistrate that the death was caused by a snake bite.
statutory_illustrations.result: A commits an offence under Section 177 IPC.

statutory_illustrations.illustration: A village watchman knowingly gives incorrect information regarding persons planning a dacoity and misdirects the police investigation.
statutory_illustrations.result: A commits the aggravated form of the offence.

examples.scenario: A person knowingly files a false report with a public authority regarding an incident.
examples.result: Section 177 applies.

examples.scenario: A witness intentionally gives false information to the police to protect an offender.
examples.result: Enhanced punishment applies.

examples.scenario: A person provides inaccurate information due to a genuine mistake and later corrects it.
examples.result: Section 177 generally does not apply.

explanation_added_by_act_3_of_1894.purpose: Expands the meaning of 'offence' and 'offender' in Sections 176 and 177.
explanation_added_by_act_3_of_1894.effect: Certain serious acts committed outside India are treated as offences if they would have been punishable in India.

judicial_principles: Truthfulness in information supplied to public authorities is essential.
The prosecution must prove knowledge of falsity.
A mere mistake is not sufficient.
False information affecting criminal investigations is treated more seriously.

relationship_with_other_sections.section_176: Omission to give notice or information.
relationship_with_other_sections.section_182: False information given to a public servant causing misuse of lawful power.
relationship_with_other_sections.section_203: Giving false information regarding an offence.

faqs.question: What is punished under Section 177 IPC?
faqs.answer: Knowingly furnishing false information to a public servant when legally required to provide information.

faqs.question: What is the maximum punishment under Section 177 IPC?
faqs.answer: Up to 2 years imprisonment, or fine, or both in offence-related cases.

faqs.question: Is a genuine mistake punishable?
faqs.answer: No. The person must know or have reason to believe that the information is false.

faqs.question: How is Section 177 different from Section 176?
faqs.answer: Section 176 deals with failure to provide information, whereas Section 177 deals with providing false information.

max_punishment: 2 years imprisonment, or fine, or both

act_3_of_1894.change: Added explanation extending the meaning of 'offence' and 'offender' for Sections 176 and 177.

ai_summary: Section 177 IPC punishes a person who is legally bound to provide information to a public servant but knowingly furnishes false information as true. The offence carries a maximum punishment of 6 months' imprisonment in ordinary cases and up to 2 years' imprisonment when the false information relates to the commission, prevention, or investigation of an offence.

title: Refusing Oath or Affirmation When Duly Required by Public Servant to Make It
chapter: Chapter X - Of Contempts of the Lawful Authority of Public Servants
section_text: Whoever refuses to bind himself by an oath or affirmation to state the truth, when required so to bind himself by a public servant legally competent to require that he shall so bind himself, shall be punished with simple imprisonment for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine which may extend to one thousand rupees, or with both.
meaning: Section 178 IPC punishes a person who refuses to take an oath or affirmation to tell the truth when a legally authorized public servant requires him to do so.
legal_significance: The section helps ensure the proper administration of justice and public inquiries by requiring persons to formally undertake to speak the truth when lawfully required.
essential_elements: A public servant legally competent to administer an oath or affirmation exists.
The person is lawfully required to take the oath or affirmation.
The oath or affirmation is for stating the truth.
The person refuses to take the oath or affirmation.

oath.meaning: A solemn declaration made before a lawful authority, often invoking religious belief, to speak the truth.

affirmation.meaning: A solemn non-religious declaration to state the truth.

public_servant.meaning: A person legally empowered to administer an oath or affirmation.

refusal.meaning: A deliberate refusal to take the required oath or affirmation.

legal_competence.meaning: The public servant must have legal authority to require the oath or affirmation.

when_section_178_applies.condition: A witness refuses to take an oath before giving evidence when legally required.
A person appearing before a lawful inquiry refuses to make an affirmation to speak the truth.
A witness declines both oath and affirmation despite lawful directions.

when_section_178_does_not_apply.situation: The public servant has no legal authority to administer the oath.
The person takes an affirmation instead of an oath where permitted by law.
when_section_178_does_not_apply.reason: Affirmation is legally equivalent to an oath.
The section applies only to lawful requirements.

examples.scenario: A witness appears before a court but refuses to take either an oath or affirmation before testifying.
examples.result: A commits an offence under Section 178 IPC.

examples.scenario: A person objects to a religious oath but agrees to make an affirmation.
examples.result: No offence is committed.

examples.scenario: A person refuses an oath demanded by an officer who lacks legal authority to administer it.
examples.result: Section 178 does not apply.

judicial_principles: An affirmation has the same legal effect as an oath.
The public servant must be legally authorized to require the oath or affirmation.
A deliberate refusal is necessary for the offence.
The provision protects the integrity of judicial and administrative proceedings.

section_177.focus: Giving false information to a public servant.

section_178.focus: Refusing to take an oath or affirmation when lawfully required.

faqs.question: What is punished under Section 178 IPC?
faqs.answer: Refusing to take an oath or affirmation to state the truth when lawfully required by a competent public servant.

faqs.question: Can a person choose affirmation instead of oath?
faqs.answer: Yes. An affirmation is legally recognized as an alternative to an oath.

faqs.question: What is the maximum punishment under Section 178 IPC?
faqs.answer: Simple imprisonment up to 6 months, or fine up to ₹1000, or both.

faqs.question: Is legal authority important?
faqs.answer: Yes. The public servant must be legally competent to require the oath or affirmation.

max_punishment: 6 months simple imprisonment, or fine up to ₹1000, or both

act_10_of_1873.change: Inserted the words 'or affirmation' after oath.

ai_summary: Section 178 IPC punishes a person who refuses to take an oath or affirmation to tell the truth when lawfully required by a competent public servant. The provision ensures the integrity of judicial and administrative proceedings by requiring witnesses and other persons to formally undertake to speak truthfully. The maximum punishment is six months' simple imprisonment, a fine up to ₹1000, or both.

title: Refusing to Answer Public Servant Authorized to Question
chapter: Chapter X - Of Contempts of the Lawful Authority of Public Servants
section_text: Whoever, being legally bound to state the truth on any subject to any public servant, refuses to answer any question demanded of him touching that subject by such public servant in the exercise of the legal powers of such public servant, shall be punished with simple imprisonment for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine which may extend to one thousand rupees, or with both.
meaning: Section 179 IPC punishes a person who is legally required to answer truthfully but refuses to answer questions lawfully asked by a competent public servant.
legal_significance: The section ensures cooperation with lawful investigations, inquiries, and administrative proceedings by requiring individuals to answer questions when the law imposes a duty to do so.
essential_elements: The accused must be legally bound to state the truth.
The question must be asked by a public servant.
The public servant must be acting within his legal authority.
The question must relate to the subject on which the person is bound to state the truth.
The accused refuses to answer the question.

legal_duty_to_answer.meaning: The law imposes an obligation to answer truthfully on a particular subject.

public_servant.meaning: A person legally recognized as a public servant and empowered to ask the question.

lawful_question.meaning: A question asked within the legal powers and jurisdiction of the public servant.

refusal.meaning: A deliberate refusal to answer the question.

subject_matter.meaning: The question must relate to the matter regarding which the person is legally bound to tell the truth.

when_section_179_applies.condition: A witness legally bound to answer questions refuses to answer during an official inquiry.
A person refuses to answer a lawful question asked by a Magistrate during proceedings.
A witness deliberately remains silent when legally required to provide information.

when_section_179_does_not_apply.situation: The person is not legally bound to answer the question.
when_section_179_does_not_apply.reason: A legal duty to answer is essential.

when_section_179_does_not_apply.situation: The public servant lacks authority to ask the question.
when_section_179_does_not_apply.reason: The question must be within lawful powers.

when_section_179_does_not_apply.situation: The question is unrelated to the subject matter on which the person must state the truth.
when_section_179_does_not_apply.reason: The refusal must concern a legally relevant question.

examples.scenario: A witness before a Magistrate refuses to answer a relevant question despite being legally required to do so.
examples.result: A commits an offence under Section 179 IPC.

examples.scenario: A person refuses to answer a lawful question during an official inquiry conducted under statutory authority.
examples.result: Section 179 applies.

examples.scenario: A person declines to answer a question asked by an officer who has no legal authority to demand the information.
examples.result: Section 179 does not apply.

judicial_principles: The public servant must possess legal authority to ask the question.
The person must be under a legal obligation to answer truthfully.
A deliberate refusal is necessary.
The provision supports effective administration of justice and public administration.

section_178.focus: Refusal to take an oath or affirmation.

section_179.focus: Refusal to answer questions after being legally required to do so.

relationship_with_other_sections.section_181: Making a false statement on oath or affirmation.

faqs.question: What is punished under Section 179 IPC?
faqs.answer: Refusing to answer a lawful question asked by a public servant when legally bound to state the truth.

faqs.question: Must the public servant have legal authority?
faqs.answer: Yes. The question must be asked within the lawful powers of the public servant.

faqs.question: What is the maximum punishment under Section 179 IPC?
faqs.answer: Simple imprisonment up to 6 months, or fine up to ₹1000, or both.

faqs.question: Is every refusal to answer punishable?
faqs.answer: No. The person must be legally bound to answer and the question must be lawfully asked.

max_punishment: 6 months simple imprisonment, or fine up to ₹1000, or both
ai_summary: Section 179 IPC punishes a person who is legally required to state the truth but refuses to answer a lawful question asked by a competent public servant. The provision ensures cooperation in judicial, administrative, and investigative proceedings. The maximum punishment is six months' simple imprisonment, a fine up to ₹1000, or both.

title: Refusing to Sign Statement
chapter: Chapter X - Of Contempts of the Lawful Authority of Public Servants
section_text: Whoever refuses to sign any statement made by him, when required to sign that statement by a public servant legally competent to require that he shall sign that statement, shall be punished with simple imprisonment for a term which may extend to three months, or with fine which may extend to five hundred rupees, or with both.
meaning: Section 180 IPC punishes a person who refuses to sign a statement made by him when a legally authorized public servant requires him to sign it.
legal_significance: The section ensures authenticity and reliability of statements recorded during official proceedings, investigations, and inquiries. A person's signature serves as confirmation that the statement was made by him.
essential_elements: A statement must have been made by the accused.
A public servant must require the accused to sign the statement.
The public servant must be legally competent to demand the signature.
The accused refuses to sign the statement.

statement.meaning: Any declaration, explanation, testimony, or information recorded by a public servant.

signature.meaning: The act of signing the statement to acknowledge that it was made by the person.

public_servant.meaning: A person legally empowered to record statements and require signatures.

legal_competence.meaning: The public servant must possess lawful authority to demand the signature.

refusal.meaning: A deliberate refusal to sign the statement.

when_section_180_applies.condition: A person gives a statement before a competent authority and later refuses to sign it.
A witness refuses to sign a statement lawfully recorded by an authorized public servant.
A person acknowledges making a statement but deliberately refuses to sign it when lawfully required.

when_section_180_does_not_apply.situation: The public servant has no legal authority to require the signature.
when_section_180_does_not_apply.reason: Legal competence is an essential requirement.

when_section_180_does_not_apply.situation: The statement was not actually made by the person.
when_section_180_does_not_apply.reason: The section applies only to statements made by the accused.

when_section_180_does_not_apply.situation: The refusal occurs because the recorded statement is materially different from what was actually stated.
when_section_180_does_not_apply.reason: The person may have a valid legal justification.

examples.scenario: A gives a statement before a Magistrate and then refuses to sign it despite being lawfully directed to do so.
examples.result: A commits an offence under Section 180 IPC.

examples.scenario: A witness refuses to sign his recorded statement before an authorized inquiry officer.
examples.result: Section 180 applies.

examples.scenario: A person refuses to sign because the statement contains facts he never stated.
examples.result: Section 180 may not apply if the refusal is justified.

judicial_principles: The statement must have been made by the accused.
The demand for signature must be lawful.
The public servant must be legally competent.
The refusal must be deliberate.
The provision protects the authenticity of official records.

section_180.focus: Refusal to sign a statement already made.

relationship_with_other_sections.section_181: False statement on oath or affirmation.

faqs.question: What is punished under Section 180 IPC?
faqs.answer: Refusing to sign a statement made by oneself when lawfully required by a competent public servant.

faqs.question: Must the statement be made by the accused?
faqs.answer: Yes. The section applies only to statements made by the person refusing to sign.

faqs.question: What is the maximum punishment under Section 180 IPC?
faqs.answer: Simple imprisonment up to 3 months, or fine up to ₹500, or both.

faqs.question: Can a person refuse to sign an incorrect statement?
faqs.answer: A justified refusal may not attract liability if the recorded statement does not accurately reflect what was actually stated.

max_punishment: 3 months simple imprisonment, or fine up to ₹500, or both
ai_summary: Section 180 IPC punishes a person who refuses to sign a statement made by him when a legally competent public servant lawfully requires such signature. The provision helps maintain the authenticity and reliability of official records and proceedings. The maximum punishment is three months' simple imprisonment, a fine up to ₹500, or both.

title: False Statement on Oath or Affirmation to Public Servant or Person Authorized to Administer an Oath or Affirmation
chapter: Chapter X - Of Contempts of the Lawful Authority of Public Servants
section_text: Whoever, being legally bound by an oath or affirmation to state the truth on any subject to any public servant or other person authorized by law to administer such oath or affirmation, makes any statement which is false, and which he knows or believes to be false or does not believe to be true, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, and shall also be liable to fine.
meaning: Section 181 IPC punishes a person who, after taking a lawful oath or affirmation to tell the truth, knowingly makes a false statement to a public servant or another person legally authorized to administer the oath or affirmation.
legal_significance: The section protects the integrity of official proceedings, inquiries, investigations, and public administration by ensuring that statements made under oath or affirmation are truthful.
essential_elements: The person must be legally bound by an oath or affirmation.
The oath or affirmation must require the person to state the truth.
The statement must be made to a public servant or a person legally authorized to administer the oath or affirmation.
The statement must be false.
The person must know it is false, believe it to be false, or not believe it to be true.

oath_or_affirmation.meaning: A solemn undertaking to speak the truth, whether religious (oath) or non-religious (affirmation).

legal_obligation.meaning: The person must be legally required to tell the truth under the oath or affirmation.

false_statement.meaning: A statement that is factually incorrect or misleading.

knowledge_of_falsity.meaning: The person knows the statement is false, believes it is false, or does not believe it to be true.

authorized_person.meaning: A public servant or any person empowered by law to administer an oath or affirmation.

when_section_181_applies.condition: A person knowingly gives false information after taking an oath before a public authority.
A witness makes a false statement after affirming to speak the truth.
A person intentionally lies during an official inquiry conducted under oath.

when_section_181_does_not_apply.situation: The person honestly believes the statement to be true.
No lawful oath or affirmation was administered.
when_section_181_does_not_apply.reason: The section requires a legal oath or affirmation.

examples.scenario: A takes an oath before a revenue officer and knowingly gives false information regarding ownership of land.
examples.result: A commits an offence under Section 181 IPC.

examples.scenario: A witness makes a statement under affirmation and deliberately conceals the truth by giving false facts.
examples.result: Section 181 applies.

examples.scenario: A person makes an incorrect statement because of an honest mistake and genuinely believes it to be true.
examples.result: Section 181 generally does not apply.

judicial_principles: The falsity must be intentional.
Knowledge or belief regarding falsity is an essential ingredient.
An affirmation carries the same legal value as an oath.
The section is intended to preserve the reliability of statements made under oath.

section_177.focus: Knowingly furnishing false information to a public servant when legally bound to provide information.

section_181.focus: Making a false statement while under a legal oath or affirmation.

difference_between_sections_178_179_180_.section_178: Refusal to take oath or affirmation.
difference_between_sections_178_179_180_.section_181: Making a false statement after taking oath or affirmation.

relationship_with_other_sections.section_181: False statement on oath or affirmation.

faqs.question: What is punished under Section 181 IPC?
faqs.answer: Knowingly making a false statement after taking a lawful oath or affirmation.

faqs.question: Is an affirmation treated the same as an oath?
faqs.answer: Yes. Both have the same legal effect under this section.

faqs.question: What is the maximum punishment under Section 181 IPC?
faqs.answer: Imprisonment up to 3 years, and fine.

faqs.question: Is an honest mistake punishable?
faqs.answer: No. The person must know, believe, or have reason to believe that the statement is false.

max_punishment: 3 years imprisonment and fine
ai_summary: Section 181 IPC punishes a person who knowingly makes a false statement after being legally bound by an oath or affirmation to tell the truth. The offence applies when the statement is made to a public servant or another person authorized to administer the oath or affirmation. The maximum punishment is imprisonment up to 3 years and fine.

title: False Information, with Intent to Cause Public Servant to Use His Lawful Power to the Injury of Another Person
chapter: Chapter X - Of Contempts of the Lawful Authority of Public Servants
section_text: Whoever gives to any public servant any information which he knows or believes to be false, intending thereby to cause, or knowing it to be likely that he will thereby cause, such public servant to act or omit to act improperly, or to use his lawful power to the injury or annoyance of another person, commits an offence under this section.
meaning: Section 182 IPC punishes a person who knowingly gives false information to a public servant with the intention of causing that public servant to misuse his lawful authority against another person.
legal_significance: The section protects innocent persons from harassment, injury, wrongful investigation, search, arrest, or administrative action caused by false complaints or false information given to public authorities.
essential_elements: Information must be given to a public servant.
The information must be false.
The accused must know or believe the information to be false.
The accused must intend or know that the information is likely to cause the public servant to act improperly.
Such action must cause injury, annoyance, or prejudice to another person.

false_information.meaning: Information that is knowingly untrue or misleading.

public_servant.meaning: A person legally recognized as a public servant under law.

knowledge_of_falsity.meaning: The accused knows or believes that the information is false.

intent.meaning: The information is given with the intention of causing misuse of lawful authority.

injury_or_annoyance.meaning: Any wrongful harm, inconvenience, investigation, search, prosecution, or adverse action suffered by another person.

clause_a.description: Causing a public servant to do or omit something which he would not have done if the true facts were known.

clause_b.description: Causing a public servant to use his lawful power to injure or annoy another person.

when_section_182_applies.condition: A person knowingly files a false complaint against another person to trigger police action.
A person falsely informs authorities about illegal activities at another person's property to cause a search.
A false allegation is made intending disciplinary action against a government employee.

when_section_182_does_not_apply.situation: The person genuinely believed the information to be true.
when_section_182_does_not_apply.reason: Knowledge or belief of falsity is essential.

when_section_182_does_not_apply.situation: No intention exists to cause misuse of official authority.

statutory_illustrations.illustration: A falsely informs a Magistrate that police officer Z committed misconduct, knowing the allegation is false and likely to cause Z's dismissal.
statutory_illustrations.result: A commits an offence under Section 182 IPC.

statutory_illustrations.illustration: A falsely informs a public servant that Z possesses contraband goods, intending to cause a search of Z's premises.
statutory_illustrations.result: A commits an offence under Section 182 IPC.

statutory_illustrations.illustration: A falsely reports a robbery near a village, knowing police investigations will harass villagers.
statutory_illustrations.result: A commits an offence under Section 182 IPC.

examples.scenario: A knowingly files a false complaint accusing B of theft to get B investigated by police.
examples.result: Section 182 IPC applies.

examples.scenario: A gives false information to municipal authorities to have B's shop sealed.
examples.result: Section 182 IPC applies.

examples.scenario: A honestly reports information that later turns out to be incorrect.
examples.result: Section 182 IPC generally does not apply.

judicial_principles: Mere falsity is not enough; knowledge of falsity must be proved.
The prosecution must establish intention or likelihood of misuse of official power.
The offence protects both public administration and private individuals.
A bona fide mistake is not punishable under this section.

section_177.focus: Providing false information when legally bound to furnish information.

section_182.focus: Providing false information intending to cause wrongful use of official power against another person.

relationship_with_other_sections.section_182: False information causing misuse of lawful authority.
relationship_with_other_sections.section_211: False charge of offence made with intent to injure.

faqs.question: What is punished under Section 182 IPC?
faqs.answer: Knowingly giving false information to a public servant to cause misuse of official authority against another person.

faqs.question: Is every false statement covered under Section 182?
faqs.answer: No. There must also be an intention or likelihood of causing wrongful official action.

faqs.question: What is the maximum punishment under Section 182 IPC?
faqs.answer: Imprisonment up to 6 months, or fine up to ₹1000, or both.

faqs.question: Does an honest mistake amount to an offence?
faqs.answer: No. The person must know or believe the information to be false.

max_punishment: 6 months imprisonment, or fine up to ₹1000, or both

act_3_of_1895.change: Substituted and expanded the scope of Section 182 IPC.

ai_summary: Section 182 IPC punishes a person who knowingly gives false information to a public servant with the intention of causing the public servant to misuse his lawful powers or take action that harms, annoys, or injures another person. Common examples include false police complaints and malicious reports to government authorities. The maximum punishment is imprisonment up to 6 months, fine up to ₹1000, or both.

title: Resistance to the Taking of Property by the Lawful Authority of a Public Servant
chapter: Chapter X - Of Contempts of the Lawful Authority of Public Servants
section_text: Whoever offers any resistance to the taking of any property by the lawful authority of any public servant, knowing or having reason to believe that he is such public servant, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine which may extend to one thousand rupees, or with both.
meaning: Section 183 IPC punishes a person who resists or obstructs a public servant in lawfully taking possession of property when the person knows or has reason to believe that the authority is acting as a public servant.
legal_significance: The section protects the lawful execution of official duties by public servants, particularly in matters involving seizure, attachment, confiscation, recovery, or taking possession of property under legal authority.
essential_elements: Property is being taken by a public servant.
The taking of property is lawful.
The accused offers resistance or obstruction.
The accused knows or has reason to believe that the person is a public servant.

resistance.meaning: Any physical, verbal, or other obstruction preventing the lawful taking of property.

property.meaning: Movable or immovable property lawfully seized, attached, confiscated, or taken under authority of law.

public_servant.meaning: A person legally recognized as a public servant and acting in an official capacity.

lawful_authority.meaning: The action of taking property must be authorized by law, court order, warrant, or statutory power.

knowledge.meaning: The accused knows or has reason to believe that the person taking the property is a public servant.

when_section_183_applies.condition: A person physically prevents a revenue officer from attaching property under a lawful order.
A person obstructs police officers lawfully seizing property during an investigation.
A debtor resists court officials executing a lawful attachment order.

when_section_183_does_not_apply.situation: The public servant has no legal authority to take the property.
The accused does not know and has no reason to believe that the person is a public servant.
A person lawfully challenges the action through legal remedies without resistance.
when_section_183_does_not_apply.reason: Legal challenge is different from unlawful resistance.

examples.scenario: A court bailiff arrives with a valid attachment order and A blocks the removal of attached property.
examples.result: A commits an offence under Section 183 IPC.

examples.scenario: Police lawfully seize stolen goods and the accused forcibly prevents the seizure.
examples.result: Section 183 applies.

examples.scenario: A person files an appeal against a seizure order without obstructing officials.
examples.result: Section 183 does not apply.

judicial_principles: The authority exercised by the public servant must be lawful.
Resistance may be physical or otherwise intentional obstruction.
Knowledge of the public servant's status is necessary.
The section protects the execution of lawful governmental and judicial functions.

section_182.focus: Giving false information to cause misuse of official power.

section_183.focus: Resisting the lawful taking of property by a public servant.

relationship_with_other_sections.section_182: False information causing misuse of authority.
relationship_with_other_sections.section_183: Resistance to lawful taking of property.
relationship_with_other_sections.section_184: Obstructing sale of property offered for sale by authority of a public servant.
relationship_with_other_sections.section_186: Obstructing a public servant in discharge of public functions.

legal_application: Execution of warrants relating to property.

faqs.question: What is punished under Section 183 IPC?
faqs.answer: Resisting a public servant who is lawfully taking property under legal authority.

faqs.question: Must the taking of property be lawful?
faqs.answer: Yes. The section applies only when the public servant is acting under lawful authority.

faqs.question: What is the maximum punishment under Section 183 IPC?
faqs.answer: Imprisonment up to 6 months, or fine up to ₹1000, or both.

faqs.question: Can a person legally challenge the seizure?
faqs.answer: Yes. Legal remedies are available, but unlawful resistance is punishable.

max_punishment: 6 months imprisonment, or fine up to ₹1000, or both
ai_summary: Section 183 IPC punishes any person who resists or obstructs a public servant in the lawful taking of property. The offence applies when the public servant acts under legal authority and the accused knows or has reason to believe that the person is a public servant. The maximum punishment is imprisonment up to 6 months, fine up to ₹1000, or both.

title: Obstructing Sale of Property Offered for Sale by Authority of Public Servant
chapter: Chapter X - Of Contempts of the Lawful Authority of Public Servants
section_text: Whoever intentionally obstructs any sale of property offered for sale by the lawful authority of any public servant, as such, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to one month, or with fine which may extend to five hundred rupees, or with both.
meaning: Section 184 IPC punishes a person who deliberately interferes with or obstructs the lawful sale of property conducted by a public servant acting under legal authority.
legal_significance: The section protects public auctions, judicial sales, revenue sales, confiscation sales, and other legally authorized disposals of property from unlawful interference.
essential_elements: A sale of property must be taking place.
The sale must be conducted under the lawful authority of a public servant.
The accused intentionally obstructs or interferes with the sale.
The obstruction must be deliberate and not accidental.

sale_of_property.meaning: A public auction, judicial sale, revenue sale, confiscation sale, or any sale authorized by law.

public_servant.meaning: A person legally recognized as a public servant conducting or supervising the sale.

lawful_authority.meaning: The sale must be authorized by law, court order, government order, or statutory power.

intentional_obstruction.meaning: A deliberate act that interferes with, delays, disrupts, or prevents the sale.

when_section_184_applies.condition: A person intentionally disrupts a government auction to prevent the sale of property.
A debtor interferes with a court-authorized auction of attached property.
A person threatens bidders to stop participation in a lawful auction.

when_section_184_does_not_apply.situation: The sale is not authorized by law.
when_section_184_does_not_apply.reason: The section applies only to lawful sales.

when_section_184_does_not_apply.situation: A person legally challenges the sale through court proceedings.
when_section_184_does_not_apply.reason: Using lawful remedies is not obstruction.

when_section_184_does_not_apply.situation: The interference is accidental and lacks intention.

examples.scenario: A revenue officer conducts an auction of seized property. A intentionally creates disturbances to stop the auction.
examples.result: A commits an offence under Section 184 IPC.

examples.scenario: A threatens prospective bidders so that no one participates in a court-ordered sale.
examples.result: Section 184 applies.

examples.scenario: A files a lawful appeal against the auction order without disrupting the auction process.
examples.result: Section 184 does not apply.

judicial_principles: The sale must be legally authorized.
The obstruction must be intentional.
Lawful legal remedies do not amount to obstruction.
The section protects the effective execution of public and judicial sales.

section_183.focus: Resistance to the lawful taking or seizure of property.

section_184.focus: Obstruction of the lawful sale of property.

relationship_with_other_sections.section_183: Resistance to taking of property by public servant.
relationship_with_other_sections.section_184: Obstructing sale of property by public servant.
relationship_with_other_sections.section_185: Illegal purchase or bidding at such sale.
relationship_with_other_sections.section_186: Obstructing public servant in discharge of public functions.

faqs.question: What is punished under Section 184 IPC?
faqs.answer: Intentionally obstructing a lawful sale of property conducted by a public servant.

faqs.question: Does the sale need to be lawful?
faqs.answer: Yes. The sale must be conducted under lawful authority.

faqs.question: What is the maximum punishment under Section 184 IPC?
faqs.answer: Imprisonment up to 1 month, or fine up to ₹500, or both.

faqs.question: Can a person legally challenge the sale?
faqs.answer: Yes. Lawful legal challenges do not amount to obstruction.

max_punishment: 1 month imprisonment, or fine up to ₹500, or both
ai_summary: Section 184 IPC punishes any person who intentionally obstructs a lawful sale of property conducted by a public servant. The provision protects public auctions, judicial sales, and other legally authorized disposals of property from unlawful interference. The maximum punishment is imprisonment up to 1 month, fine up to ₹500, or both.

title: Illegal Purchase or Bid for Property Offered for Sale by Authority of Public Servant
chapter: Chapter X - Of Contempts of the Lawful Authority of Public Servants
section_text: Whoever, at any sale of property held by the lawful authority of a public servant, as such, purchases or bids for any property on account of any person, whether himself or any other, whom he knows to be under a legal incapacity to purchase that property at that sale, or bids for such property not intending to perform the obligations under which he lays himself by such bidding, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to one month, or with fine which may extend to two hundred rupees, or with both.
meaning: Section 185 IPC punishes persons who improperly participate in public auctions or sales conducted by public servants. The offence occurs when a person bids or purchases property on behalf of someone who is legally disqualified from buying it, or when a person makes a bid without intending to fulfill the obligations arising from that bid.
legal_significance: The section safeguards the fairness, transparency, and integrity of auctions and sales conducted under lawful authority. It prevents fraud, collusion, sham bidding, and circumvention of legal restrictions imposed on certain persons.
objective: To ensure that public auctions and sales conducted by lawful authorities are genuine, transparent, and free from manipulation or fraudulent practices.
essential_elements: A sale of property must be conducted by the lawful authority of a public servant.
The accused purchases or bids for property.
The purchase or bid is made for a person known to be legally incapable of purchasing the property, or
The accused bids without intending to fulfill the obligations arising from the bid.
The act is done knowingly and intentionally.

sale_by_public_servant.meaning: A sale conducted under legal authority by a public servant such as a court officer, revenue officer, auction officer, or government authority.

property.meaning: Any movable or immovable property offered for sale under lawful authority.

legal_incapacity.meaning: A legal disability or prohibition preventing a person from purchasing the property at that particular sale.

purchase.meaning: Acquiring ownership of the property through the sale.

bid.meaning: Offering a price for the property during an auction or sale.

knowledge.meaning: The bidder knows that the person for whom he acts is legally disqualified from purchasing.

fraudulent_bidding.meaning: Submitting bids without any genuine intention to comply with payment or other obligations arising from the bid.

type_1.name: Purchase or Bid for a Legally Disqualified Person
type_1.description: A person purchases or bids for property on behalf of another person whom he knows is legally incapable of purchasing that property.

type_2.name: Sham or Fraudulent Bidding
type_2.description: A person bids for property without intending to perform the obligations created by the bid.

persons_commonly_under_legal_incapacity: Public servants prohibited by law from purchasing certain property.
Court officials prohibited from purchasing property sold through judicial proceedings.
Trustees or fiduciaries prohibited from purchasing trust property.
Persons disqualified by statute or court order.

mens_rea.description: Knowledge of legal incapacity or intention not to fulfill bidding obligations is necessary.

actus_reus.description: Making the purchase or bid during a lawful public sale.

when_section_185_applies.condition: A person bids on behalf of a court officer who is legally prohibited from purchasing auctioned property.
A person participates in an auction merely to inflate prices and has no intention of paying.
A bidder acts as a proxy for a legally disqualified purchaser.
A bidder deliberately places bids without intending to complete the transaction.

when_section_185_does_not_apply.situation: The bidder genuinely intends to fulfill all obligations arising from the bid.
The person for whom the bid is made is legally eligible to purchase the property.
The bidder later becomes unable to pay due to unforeseen circumstances.
when_section_185_does_not_apply.reason: Absence of fraudulent intention at the time of bidding.

examples.scenario: A court clerk is prohibited by law from purchasing property sold through court auction. He asks his friend B to bid and purchase the property on his behalf.
examples.result: B commits an offence under Section 185 IPC.

examples.scenario: A participates in a government auction and repeatedly places high bids only to disrupt the auction process, never intending to pay.
examples.result: A commits an offence under Section 185 IPC.

examples.scenario: A places a genuine bid and intends to purchase the property, but later faces financial difficulties.
examples.result: Section 185 generally does not apply.

examples.scenario: A bids for property on behalf of a person who is legally entitled to purchase it.
examples.result: No offence under Section 185 IPC.

judicial_principles: Knowledge of the purchaser's legal incapacity is essential.
