Fraudulent intention at the time of bidding must be established.
Mere inability to complete payment later is insufficient.
The section aims to prevent manipulation of lawful sales and auctions.
Public confidence in official sales must be protected.

section_183.focus: Resistance to the lawful taking of property.

section_184.focus: Obstructing the lawful sale of property.

section_185.focus: Illegal purchase or fraudulent bidding during the lawful sale.

relationship_with_other_sections.section_183: Resistance to taking of property by public servant.
relationship_with_other_sections.section_184: Obstructing sale of property by public servant.
relationship_with_other_sections.section_185: Illegal purchase or bid at such sale.
relationship_with_other_sections.section_186: Obstructing public servant in discharge of public functions.
relationship_with_other_sections.section_169: Public servant unlawfully buying or bidding for property.

faqs.question: What is punished under Section 185 IPC?
faqs.answer: Purchasing or bidding for property on behalf of a legally disqualified person, or bidding without intending to fulfill the obligations of the bid.

faqs.question: What is a legally incapacitated purchaser?
faqs.answer: A person who is prohibited by law from purchasing property at a particular sale.

faqs.question: Is every failed bid an offence?
faqs.answer: No. The offence requires lack of intention to perform the obligations at the time of bidding.

faqs.question: What is the maximum punishment under Section 185 IPC?
faqs.answer: Imprisonment up to 1 month, fine up to ₹200, or both.

faqs.question: Can bidding through another person become an offence?
faqs.answer: Yes, if it is done for a person who is legally prohibited from purchasing the property.

max_punishment: 1 month imprisonment, or fine up to ₹200, or both

historical_background.purpose: Introduced to prevent abuse and manipulation of public auctions conducted under legal authority.

ai_summary: Section 185 IPC punishes two kinds of misconduct during public auctions and sales conducted by public servants: (1) bidding or purchasing property on behalf of a person who is legally prohibited from purchasing it, and (2) making bids without intending to fulfill the obligations arising from the bid. The provision protects the integrity, fairness, and transparency of public sales. The maximum punishment is imprisonment up to one month, a fine up to ₹200, or both.

title: Obstructing Public Servant in Discharge of Public Functions
chapter: Chapter X - Of Contempts of the Lawful Authority of Public Servants
section_text: Whoever voluntarily obstructs any public servant in the discharge of his public functions, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three months, or with fine which may extend to five hundred rupees, or with both.
meaning: Section 186 IPC punishes any person who voluntarily obstructs a public servant while the public servant is lawfully performing his official duties.
legal_significance: The section protects public administration and ensures that public servants can perform their official functions without unlawful interference, resistance, or obstruction from individuals.
objective: To maintain the smooth functioning of government administration and public services by preventing interference with public servants performing lawful duties.
essential_elements: There must be a public servant.
The public servant must be discharging a public function or official duty.
The accused must voluntarily obstruct the public servant.
The obstruction must occur during the performance of the public function.

public_servant.meaning: A person falling within the definition of a public servant under Section 21 IPC.

public_function.meaning: Any duty, responsibility, or function that a public servant is legally authorized or required to perform.

obstruction.meaning: Any act that hinders, delays, interferes with, prevents, or makes difficult the performance of official duties.

voluntarily.meaning: The obstruction must be intentional and conscious, not accidental.

physical_obstruction.description: Blocking movement, preventing access, physically restraining officials, or interfering with official operations.

verbal_obstruction.description: Using threats, intimidation, or deliberate interference that prevents performance of duties.

indirect_obstruction.description: Any deliberate act that hinders the public servant's ability to perform official functions.

when_section_186_applies.condition: A person prevents a police officer from conducting a lawful investigation.
A person blocks a revenue officer from carrying out an official inspection.
A crowd intentionally prevents government officials from performing official duties.
A person interferes with election officials while they are performing election duties.

when_section_186_does_not_apply.situation: The public servant is acting outside the scope of lawful authority.
when_section_186_does_not_apply.reason: The duty being performed must be a lawful public function.

when_section_186_does_not_apply.situation: The interference is accidental or unintentional.
A person lawfully challenges the action through legal remedies without obstructing the official.

examples.scenario: A police officer arrives to execute a lawful warrant. A deliberately blocks the officer's entry and prevents execution of the warrant.
examples.result: A commits an offence under Section 186 IPC.

examples.scenario: A government survey officer is conducting an official land survey. A intentionally prevents the survey from taking place.
examples.result: Section 186 IPC applies.

examples.scenario: A citizen files a legal objection before a competent authority against an administrative action without obstructing officials.
examples.result: Section 186 IPC does not apply.

judicial_principles: Actual use of force is not necessary; obstruction alone is sufficient.
The obstruction must be voluntary and intentional.
The public servant must be engaged in lawful public duties.
Physical resistance is not required; indirect interference may also amount to obstruction.
The section protects the functioning of public administration.

section_183.focus: Resistance to lawful taking of property by a public servant.

section_184.focus: Obstruction of a lawful sale of property.

section_185.focus: Illegal purchase or fraudulent bidding at such sale.

section_186.focus: General obstruction of a public servant performing official duties.

relationship_with_other_sections.section_183: Resistance to taking of property by public servant.
relationship_with_other_sections.section_185: Illegal purchase or bid at sale.
relationship_with_other_sections.section_186: Obstructing public servant in discharge of public functions.
relationship_with_other_sections.section_353: Assault or criminal force to deter public servant from duty.
relationship_with_other_sections.section_188: Disobedience to order duly promulgated by public servant.

faqs.question: What is punished under Section 186 IPC?
faqs.answer: Voluntarily obstructing a public servant while performing official duties.

faqs.question: Is physical force necessary for the offence?
faqs.answer: No. Any intentional obstruction is sufficient.

faqs.question: What is the maximum punishment under Section 186 IPC?
faqs.answer: Imprisonment up to 3 months, fine up to ₹500, or both.

faqs.question: Can lawful criticism of a public servant amount to obstruction?
faqs.answer: No. Mere criticism without interference in official duties is not an offence.

faqs.question: Must the public servant be acting lawfully?
faqs.answer: Yes. The public servant must be discharging lawful public functions.

max_punishment: 3 months imprisonment, or fine up to ₹500, or both

historical_background.purpose: Enacted to protect public servants from interference while carrying out official responsibilities and to ensure effective administration of law.

ai_summary: Section 186 IPC punishes any person who intentionally obstructs a public servant while he is performing lawful official duties. The obstruction may be physical, verbal, or indirect, provided it interferes with the performance of public functions. The section safeguards public administration and ensures that government officials can discharge their duties without unlawful interference. The maximum punishment is imprisonment up to three months, fine up to ₹500, or both.

title: Omission to Assist Public Servant When Bound by Law to Give Assistance
chapter: Chapter X - Of Contempts of the Lawful Authority of Public Servants
section_text: Whoever, being bound by law to render or furnish assistance to any public servant in the execution of his public duty, intentionally omits to give such assistance, shall be punished. Enhanced punishment applies where assistance is demanded for executing court process, preventing offences, suppressing riots or affrays, or apprehending offenders.
meaning: Section 187 IPC punishes a person who is legally required to assist a public servant in performing official duties but intentionally refuses or fails to provide such assistance.
legal_significance: The section ensures cooperation between citizens and public authorities when the law specifically requires assistance. It helps maintain public order, enforce court orders, prevent crimes, suppress disturbances, and apprehend offenders.
objective: To compel lawful public cooperation in situations where public servants require assistance for effective administration of justice and maintenance of public peace.
essential_elements: The accused must be legally bound to assist a public servant.
The public servant must be engaged in the execution of a public duty.
Assistance must be lawfully required or demanded.
The accused intentionally omits or refuses to provide such assistance.

legal_duty.meaning: An obligation imposed by law requiring a person to assist a public servant.

public_servant.meaning: A person recognized as a public servant under law and acting in an official capacity.

assistance.meaning: Any lawful help, support, information, manpower, or cooperation required by the public servant.

intentional_omission.meaning: A deliberate refusal or failure to provide the required assistance.

execution_of_public_duty.meaning: Performance of official functions authorized by law.

ordinary_case.description: Failure to provide assistance when legally bound to do so.

aggravated_case.description: Failure to provide assistance when demanded for court processes, crime prevention, riot suppression, or apprehension of offenders.

aggravated_case.condition: Assistance demanded for court process, preventing offences, suppressing riot or affray, or apprehending offenders.

situations_covered.situation: Executing a lawful court warrant or process.
Preventing the commission of an offence.
Apprehending a person accused or convicted of an offence.
Capturing an escaped prisoner or detainee.

when_section_187_applies.condition: A police officer lawfully requests assistance to arrest an offender and a person deliberately refuses.
Citizens are legally required to help suppress a riot but intentionally refuse.
A person refuses assistance in executing a lawful court warrant despite being legally bound.
A person intentionally refuses to help apprehend an escaped prisoner when lawfully required.

when_section_187_does_not_apply.situation: The person is not legally bound to provide assistance.
The demand for assistance is unlawful.
when_section_187_does_not_apply.reason: The request must be legally authorized.

when_section_187_does_not_apply.situation: The person is physically incapable of providing assistance.
The public servant acts outside his lawful authority.
when_section_187_does_not_apply.reason: The duty must be connected with lawful public functions.

examples.scenario: A police officer requests A's help to arrest a fleeing accused person. A deliberately refuses despite being legally bound to assist.
examples.result: A commits an offence under Section 187 IPC.

examples.scenario: A Magistrate orders citizens to assist in controlling a riot. A intentionally refuses to cooperate.
examples.result: Section 187 IPC applies with enhanced punishment.

examples.scenario: A person is unable to assist because of a serious physical disability.
examples.result: Section 187 IPC generally does not apply.

examples.scenario: A public servant makes an unlawful demand unrelated to official duties.
examples.result: Section 187 IPC does not apply.

judicial_principles: The duty to assist must arise from law.
The omission must be intentional.
The public servant must be performing lawful duties.
Enhanced punishment applies where public safety and law enforcement are directly affected.
Citizens have a legal obligation to assist authorities in specific circumstances prescribed by law.

section_186.focus: Voluntarily obstructing a public servant in the discharge of public functions.

section_187.focus: Failing to assist a public servant when legally bound to provide assistance.

relationship_with_other_sections.section_187: Omission to assist public servant when legally bound.

relationship_with_other_sections.section_353: Assault or criminal force to deter public servant from discharge of duty.

faqs.question: What is punished under Section 187 IPC?
faqs.answer: Intentionally failing to assist a public servant when legally bound to provide assistance.

faqs.question: When does enhanced punishment apply?
faqs.answer: When assistance is required for court processes, crime prevention, riot suppression, or apprehending offenders.

faqs.question: What is the maximum punishment under Section 187 IPC?
faqs.answer: Simple imprisonment up to 6 months, or fine up to ₹500, or both.

faqs.question: Can a person be punished if he is unable to help?
faqs.answer: Generally no, if the inability is genuine and the omission is not intentional.

faqs.question: Is every request from a public servant covered?
faqs.answer: No. The person must be legally bound to assist and the request must be lawful.

max_punishment: 6 months simple imprisonment, or fine up to ₹500, or both

historical_background.purpose: Enacted to ensure public cooperation with lawful authorities in maintaining law and order and enforcing judicial processes.

ai_summary: Section 187 IPC punishes a person who intentionally fails to assist a public servant when legally required to do so. The offence becomes more serious when assistance is demanded for executing court orders, preventing offences, suppressing riots or affrays, apprehending offenders, or capturing escaped prisoners. The maximum punishment is six months' simple imprisonment, a fine up to ₹500, or both.

title: Disobedience to Order Duly Promulgated by Public Servant
chapter: Chapter X - Of Contempts of the Lawful Authority of Public Servants
section_text: Whoever, knowing that an order has been duly promulgated by a public servant lawfully empowered to issue such order, and being directed thereby to abstain from a certain act or to take certain action with respect to property in his possession or management, disobeys such order, commits an offence under this section.
meaning: Section 188 IPC punishes a person who knowingly disobeys a lawful order issued by a competent public servant, where such disobedience causes or is likely to cause obstruction, annoyance, injury, danger to human life, health, safety, or public disorder.
legal_significance: The section helps maintain public order, public safety, public health, and effective administration by ensuring compliance with lawful orders issued by competent authorities.
objective: To enforce obedience to lawful public orders and prevent harm arising from their violation.
essential_elements: An order must be promulgated by a public servant.
The public servant must be lawfully empowered to issue the order.
The accused must have knowledge of the order.
The accused must disobey the order.
The disobedience must cause or tend to cause the consequences specified in the section.

promulgated_order.meaning: An order officially announced, published, communicated, or made known to the public or affected persons.

public_servant.meaning: A person legally empowered to issue the order in an official capacity.

knowledge.meaning: The accused knew about the existence of the order.

disobedience.meaning: Failure or refusal to comply with the lawful directions contained in the order.

harm_or_likelihood_of_harm.meaning: Actual harm is not necessary; likelihood of harm is sufficient.

simple_disobedience.description: Disobedience causing or likely to cause obstruction, annoyance, injury, or risk thereof.

aggravated_disobedience.description: Disobedience causing or likely to cause danger to human life, health, safety, riot, or affray.

simple_disobedience.condition: Causes or tends to cause obstruction, annoyance, injury, or risk thereof.

aggravated_disobedience.condition: Causes or tends to cause danger to human life, health, safety, riot, or affray.

explanation.text: It is not necessary that the offender intended to produce harm or foresaw the harm. It is sufficient that he knew of the order and that his disobedience caused or was likely to cause harm.
explanation.importance: Mens rea regarding the harmful consequence is not required; knowledge of the order is sufficient.

statutory_illustration.scenario: A lawful order prohibits a religious procession from passing through a particular street. A knowingly disobeys the order, creating a danger of riot.
statutory_illustration.result: A commits an offence under Section 188 IPC.

when_section_188_applies.condition: A person violates a prohibitory order issued under law.
A person knowingly violates restrictions imposed to prevent public disorder.
A person ignores quarantine, health, or safety orders issued by competent authorities.
A person violates a lawful order and thereby creates danger to public safety.

when_section_188_does_not_apply.situation: The accused had no knowledge of the order.
when_section_188_does_not_apply.reason: Knowledge of the order is essential.

when_section_188_does_not_apply.situation: The order was issued by a person lacking legal authority.
when_section_188_does_not_apply.reason: The order must be lawfully promulgated.

when_section_188_does_not_apply.situation: The order itself is invalid or unlawful.
There is no disobedience of the order.

examples.scenario: A district authority prohibits entry into a dangerous area. A knowingly enters the area despite the order.
examples.result: Section 188 IPC applies.

examples.scenario: A person violates a lawful curfew order causing public disturbance.
examples.result: Section 188 IPC applies.

examples.scenario: A person knowingly violates a public health order and creates risk to public health.
examples.result: Aggravated punishment may apply.

examples.scenario: A person genuinely had no knowledge of the order.
examples.result: Section 188 IPC generally does not apply.

judicial_principles: Knowledge of the order is mandatory.
Intention to cause harm is not required.
Likelihood of harm is sufficient; actual harm need not occur.
The order must be issued by a legally competent authority.
The section protects public order, safety, and administration.

section_186.focus: Obstructing a public servant in performing official duties.

section_188.focus: Disobeying a lawful order issued by a public servant.

relationship_with_other_sections.section_186: Obstructing public servant in discharge of public functions.
relationship_with_other_sections.section_187: Failure to assist public servant when legally bound.
relationship_with_other_sections.section_188: Disobedience to lawful orders of public servants.
relationship_with_other_sections.section_353: Assault or criminal force against public servant.

faqs.question: What is punished under Section 188 IPC?
faqs.answer: Knowingly disobeying a lawful order issued by a competent public servant.

faqs.question: Is intention to cause harm necessary?
faqs.answer: No. Knowledge of the order and resulting or likely harm are sufficient.

faqs.question: What is the maximum punishment under Section 188 IPC?
faqs.answer: Up to 6 months imprisonment, fine up to ₹1000, or both in aggravated cases.

faqs.question: Does actual harm need to occur?
faqs.answer: No. A likelihood of harm is enough.

faqs.question: Can violation of a Section 144 order attract Section 188 IPC?
faqs.answer: Yes, if the order is lawful and the person knowingly disobeys it.

max_punishment: 6 months imprisonment, or fine up to ₹1000, or both

historical_background.purpose: Enacted to ensure compliance with lawful administrative and public safety orders and to prevent disorder arising from disobedience.

ai_summary: Section 188 IPC punishes a person who knowingly disobeys a lawful order issued by a competent public servant. The offence applies even if the person did not intend to cause harm, provided the disobedience causes or is likely to cause obstruction, injury, danger to human life, health, safety, or public disorder. The maximum punishment is six months' imprisonment, a fine up to ₹1000, or both.

title: Threat of Injury to Public Servant
chapter: Chapter X - Of Contempts of the Lawful Authority of Public Servants
section_text: Whoever holds out any threat of injury to any public servant, or to any person in whom he believes that public servant to be interested, for the purpose of inducing that public servant to do any act, or to forbear or delay to do any act, connected with the exercise of the public functions of such public servant, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.
meaning: Section 189 IPC punishes a person who threatens a public servant, or someone connected to that public servant, with injury in order to influence the performance of the public servant's official duties.
legal_significance: The section protects public servants from intimidation, coercion, and undue pressure while performing official functions. It ensures that government officials can discharge their duties independently and without fear.
objective: To safeguard the integrity and independence of public administration by preventing threats intended to influence official actions.
essential_elements: There must be a threat of injury.
The threat must be directed at a public servant or a person in whom the public servant is interested.
The threat must be made with the intention of influencing the public servant's official conduct.
The act sought to be influenced must relate to the public servant's official functions.

threat.meaning: Any expression, conduct, or communication indicating an intention to cause harm.

injury.meaning: Any harm illegally caused to body, mind, reputation, property, or rights as defined under IPC.

public_servant.meaning: A person falling within the definition of public servant under Section 21 IPC.

person_of_interest.meaning: Any person whom the accused believes the public servant cares about or is interested in, such as a family member, friend, or associate.

inducement.meaning: An attempt to influence the public servant to act, refrain from acting, or delay acting.

public_function.meaning: Any official duty or function performed by the public servant.

forcing_action.description: Threatening a public servant to compel him to perform a particular official act.

preventing_action.description: Threatening a public servant to stop him from performing an official duty.

delaying_action.description: Threatening a public servant to delay official action.

when_section_189_applies.condition: A person threatens a tax officer to stop an investigation.
A person threatens harm to a police officer's family member to prevent an arrest.
A person intimidates a government official to delay issuing an adverse order.
A person threatens a public servant to obtain a favorable official decision.

when_section_189_does_not_apply.situation: No threat of injury is made.
The threat is unrelated to the public servant's official duties.
when_section_189_does_not_apply.reason: The threat must be connected with public functions.

when_section_189_does_not_apply.situation: A person merely criticizes or complains against a public servant through lawful means.
when_section_189_does_not_apply.reason: Lawful criticism is not a threat.

when_section_189_does_not_apply.situation: The accused has no intention of influencing official conduct.

examples.scenario: A threatens a police officer that he will harm the officer's son unless the officer drops an investigation.
examples.result: A commits an offence under Section 189 IPC.

examples.scenario: A threatens a revenue officer with damage to his property if he proceeds with a lawful inspection.
examples.result: Section 189 IPC applies.

examples.scenario: A files a lawful complaint against a public servant's misconduct without making any threats.
examples.result: Section 189 IPC does not apply.

examples.scenario: A threatens a public servant to delay issuing a demolition order.
examples.result: Section 189 IPC applies.

judicial_principles: Actual injury need not occur; the threat itself is sufficient.
The threat must be connected to the exercise of public functions.
The intention to influence official conduct is essential.
Threats against persons connected to the public servant are also covered.
The section protects the independence of public administration.

section_186.focus: Obstructing a public servant in discharge of public functions.

section_188.focus: Disobedience of a lawful order promulgated by a public servant.

section_189.focus: Threatening a public servant to influence official action.

relationship_with_other_sections.section_186: Obstructing public servant in discharge of public functions.
relationship_with_other_sections.section_188: Disobedience to order promulgated by public servant.
relationship_with_other_sections.section_189: Threat of injury to public servant.
relationship_with_other_sections.section_353: Assault or criminal force to deter public servant from duty.

faqs.question: What is punished under Section 189 IPC?
faqs.answer: Threatening a public servant or a person connected to him in order to influence official actions.

faqs.question: Must actual injury occur?
faqs.answer: No. The threat itself is sufficient.

faqs.question: Can threats against family members of public servants be covered?
faqs.answer: Yes. Threats against persons in whom the public servant is interested are covered.

faqs.question: What is the maximum punishment under Section 189 IPC?
faqs.answer: Imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both.

faqs.question: Does lawful criticism of a public servant amount to an offence?
faqs.answer: No. Lawful criticism or complaints are not offences unless accompanied by threats.

max_punishment: 2 years imprisonment, or fine, or both

historical_background.purpose: Enacted to protect public servants from intimidation and coercion while performing official duties.

ai_summary: Section 189 IPC punishes any person who threatens a public servant, or someone connected to that public servant, in order to influence, prevent, or delay the performance of official duties. The offence is complete once the threat is made with the required intention, even if no actual injury occurs. The maximum punishment is imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both.

title: Threat of Injury to Induce Person to Refrain from Applying for Protection to Public Servant
chapter: Chapter X - Of Contempts of the Lawful Authority of Public Servants
section_text: Whoever holds out any threat of injury to any person for the purpose of inducing that person to refrain or desist from making a legal application for protection against any injury to any public servant legally empowered as such to give such protection, or to cause such protection to be given, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to one year, or with fine, or with both.
meaning: Section 190 IPC punishes a person who threatens another person with injury in order to prevent that person from seeking lawful protection from a competent public servant or authority.
legal_significance: The section safeguards an individual's right to seek legal protection from public authorities. It prevents intimidation, coercion, and threats that discourage victims from approaching law enforcement or other competent public officials.
objective: To ensure that every person can freely seek legal protection from public authorities without fear, intimidation, or coercion.
essential_elements: There must be a threat of injury.
The threat must be directed against a person.
The threat must be intended to prevent or discourage that person from making a legal application.
The application must be for protection against an injury.
The protection must be sought from a public servant legally empowered to provide or arrange such protection.

threat.meaning: Any communication, conduct, or act indicating an intention to cause harm.

injury.meaning: Any harm illegally caused to body, mind, reputation, property, or legal rights.

legal_application.meaning: A lawful request, complaint, petition, report, or application made to a competent authority.

public_servant.meaning: A public servant legally authorized to provide protection or cause protection to be provided.

protection.meaning: Legal assistance, security, intervention, or relief against threatened or actual injury.

intent.meaning: The threat must be made with the purpose of preventing or discouraging the person from seeking protection.

covered_actions: Threatening a victim to stop them from filing a police complaint.
Threatening a witness to prevent reporting an offence.
Threatening a person from seeking protection from authorities.
Coercing a person to withdraw a request for police protection.
scope_of_section.protected_right: The legal right to seek assistance and protection from public authorities.

when_section_190_applies.condition: A threatens B with violence if B files a police complaint.
A threatens a victim to stop the victim from approaching authorities for protection.
A intimidates a witness into withdrawing a request for police protection.
A threatens a person to prevent reporting harassment to a competent authority.

when_section_190_does_not_apply.situation: No threat of injury is made.
The threat is unrelated to any application for legal protection.
when_section_190_does_not_apply.reason: The purpose required by the section is absent.

when_section_190_does_not_apply.situation: The person is not being discouraged from approaching a public servant.
when_section_190_does_not_apply.reason: The offence specifically concerns interference with legal protection.

when_section_190_does_not_apply.situation: The conduct amounts only to persuasion without intimidation or threat.
when_section_190_does_not_apply.reason: A threat of injury is necessary.

examples.scenario: A threatens B that he will damage B's property if B reports an assault to the police.
examples.result: A commits an offence under Section 190 IPC.

examples.scenario: A threatens a witness to stop him from seeking police protection after receiving threats.
examples.result: Section 190 IPC applies.

examples.scenario: A advises B not to file a complaint but does not threaten or intimidate B.
examples.result: Section 190 IPC generally does not apply.

examples.scenario: A threatens B with bodily harm if B approaches a Magistrate for protection.
examples.result: Section 190 IPC applies.

judicial_principles: Actual injury need not occur; the threat itself is sufficient.
The intention to prevent a legal application for protection is essential.
The public servant must be legally empowered to provide protection.
The section protects access to lawful remedies and public protection.
The offence is complete once the threat is made with the required purpose.

section_189.focus: Threatening a public servant to influence official actions.

section_190.focus: Threatening a person to stop him from seeking protection from a public servant.

relationship_with_other_sections.section_189: Threat of injury to public servant.
relationship_with_other_sections.section_190: Threat of injury to prevent application for protection.
relationship_with_other_sections.section_195A: Threatening a person to give false evidence.

legal_application: Obstruction of access to lawful protection.

faqs.question: What is punished under Section 190 IPC?
faqs.answer: Threatening a person to prevent him from seeking legal protection from a competent public servant.

faqs.question: Must the victim actually refrain from filing the complaint?
faqs.answer: No. The offence is complete when the threat is made with the required intention.

faqs.question: What is the maximum punishment under Section 190 IPC?
faqs.answer: Imprisonment up to 1 year, or fine, or both.

faqs.question: Does actual injury need to occur?
faqs.answer: No. The threat itself is sufficient.

faqs.question: How is Section 190 different from Section 189?
faqs.answer: Section 189 protects public servants from threats, while Section 190 protects individuals seeking assistance from public servants.

max_punishment: 1 year imprisonment, or fine, or both

historical_background.purpose: Enacted to protect individuals from intimidation that prevents them from seeking lawful protection and assistance from public authorities.

ai_summary: Section 190 IPC punishes any person who threatens another with injury in order to stop that person from approaching a competent public servant for legal protection. The section safeguards access to justice, victim protection, and the ability of citizens to seek assistance from public authorities without fear or intimidation. The maximum punishment is imprisonment up to one year, or fine, or both.

title: Giving False Evidence
chapter: Chapter XI - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
bare_act_text:
Whoever, being legally bound by an oath or by an express provision of law to state the truth, or being bound by law to make a declaration upon any subject, makes any statement which is false, and which he either knows or believes to be false or does not believe to be true, is said to give false evidence.

Explanation 1.—A statement is within the meaning of this section, whether it is made verbally or otherwise.

Explanation 2.—A false statement as to the belief of the person attesting is within the meaning of this section, and a person may be guilty of giving false evidence by stating that he believes a thing which he does not believe, as well as by stating that he knows a thing which he does not know.
meaning: Section 191 IPC defines the offence of giving false evidence (commonly called perjury). A person gives false evidence when he is legally required to tell the truth and knowingly makes a false statement or makes a statement that he does not believe to be true.
simple_explanation: If a person takes an oath, affirmation, or is legally required to tell the truth and deliberately lies, he commits the offence of giving false evidence. The lie may be spoken, written, signed, declared, or certified in any form.
objective: To preserve the integrity of judicial proceedings, investigations, affidavits, declarations, and other legal processes by ensuring that persons under a legal duty speak the truth.
essential_elements: The person must be legally bound to state the truth.
The legal obligation may arise from an oath, affirmation, declaration, or express provision of law.
A statement must be made.
The statement must be false.
The person must know or believe that the statement is false, or must not believe it to be true.

legal_duty_to_speak_truth.description: The accused must be under a legal obligation to state the truth.

making_of_statement.description: A statement must actually be made verbally, in writing, electronically, or otherwise.

knowledge_or_belief.description: The accused must know the statement is false, believe it is false, or not believe it to be true.

explanation_1.text: A statement is within the meaning of this section whether it is made verbally or otherwise.
explanation_1.legal_effect: False evidence can be oral, written, electronic, certified, signed, or communicated in any form.

explanation_2.text: A false statement regarding one's belief is also false evidence.
explanation_2.legal_effect: A person commits the offence not only by falsely claiming knowledge but also by falsely claiming belief.

types_of_false_evidence.description: Knowingly stating a fact that is untrue.
Claiming knowledge of something that the person does not actually know.
Claiming belief in something which the person does not genuinely believe.
Knowingly giving a false translation or interpretation while legally bound to give a true one.

illustrations_from_section.illustration: A falsely swears in court that he heard Z admit owing ₹1000 to B.
A states under oath that he believes a signature belongs to Z although he does not actually believe so.
A genuinely believes a signature belongs to Z and states so under oath.
illustrations_from_section.result: No false evidence, even if the belief later proves incorrect.

illustrations_from_section.illustration: A states that he knows Z was at a particular place although he has no knowledge of the matter.
A translator knowingly provides an incorrect translation while under oath.

when_section_191_applies.condition: A witness knowingly lies before a court.
A person files a false affidavit knowing its contents are false.
A person makes a false declaration required by law.
A translator knowingly gives an incorrect translation under legal duty.

when_section_191_does_not_apply.reason: Truthful statements cannot amount to false evidence.

when_section_191_does_not_apply.situation: The person honestly believes the statement to be true.
The person is not legally bound to tell the truth.
The statement is made due to genuine mistake or faulty memory.

examples.scenario: A witness deliberately gives false testimony during a criminal trial.
examples.result: Giving false evidence under Section 191 IPC.

examples.scenario: A person files a false affidavit regarding ownership of land.
examples.result: Giving false evidence under Section 191 IPC.

examples.scenario: A person genuinely misremembers a date and gives incorrect testimony.
examples.result: Generally not false evidence.

examples.scenario: A falsely certifies a document as genuine despite knowing it is forged.
examples.result: Giving false evidence.

mens_rea.description: The accused must knowingly make a false statement or must not believe the statement to be true.

actus_reus.description: Making a false statement while under a legal obligation to speak the truth.

judicial_principles: A deliberate falsehood is essential.
Knowledge or belief regarding falsity must be established.
False claims of belief are punishable.
Actual success of the false statement is irrelevant.
The offence protects the administration of justice.

punishment.note: Section 191 only defines the offence. Punishment is prescribed under Section 193 IPC.

section_181.title: False Statement on Oath
section_181.difference: Applies specifically to false statements made on oath to a public servant or authorized person.

section_191.title: Giving False Evidence
section_191.difference: General definition of false evidence under legal obligation.

section_192.title: Fabricating False Evidence
section_192.difference: Creating false circumstances, records, or documents to mislead legal proceedings.

section_193.title: Punishment for False Evidence
section_193.difference: Provides punishment for offences defined under Sections 191 and 192.

relationship_with_other_sections.196: Using evidence known to be false.

faqs.question: What is giving false evidence?
faqs.answer: Knowingly making a false statement while legally bound to tell the truth.

faqs.question: Can a false belief amount to false evidence?
faqs.answer: Yes. Claiming to believe something which one does not actually believe is false evidence.

faqs.question: Does Section 191 itself prescribe punishment?
faqs.answer: No. Punishment is prescribed under Section 193 IPC.

faqs.question: Can an honest mistake amount to false evidence?
faqs.answer: No. There must be knowledge of falsity or absence of belief in truth.

faqs.question: Is written false evidence covered?
faqs.answer: Yes. Statements may be oral, written, electronic, signed, certified, or otherwise communicated.

max_punishment: Punishment prescribed under Section 193 IPC

historical_background.purpose: To ensure truthfulness in legal proceedings and protect courts and authorities from deception.

ai_summary: Section 191 IPC defines the offence of giving false evidence (perjury). A person commits this offence when, while under a legal duty to speak the truth, he knowingly makes a false statement or a statement that he does not believe to be true. The section covers oral evidence, affidavits, declarations, translations, certifications, and statements regarding belief. Punishment for the offence is provided under Section 193 IPC.

title: Fabricating False Evidence
chapter: Chapter XI - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
bare_act_text: Whoever causes any circumstance to exist or makes any false entry in any book or record, or electronic record or makes any document or electronic record containing a false statement, intending that such circumstance, false entry or false statement may appear in evidence in a judicial proceeding, or in a proceeding taken by law before a public servant as such, or before an arbitrator, and that such circumstance, false entry or false statement, so appearing in evidence, may cause any person who in such proceeding is to form an opinion upon the evidence, to entertain an erroneous opinion touching any point material to the result of such proceeding is said to fabricate false evidence.
meaning: Section 192 IPC defines the offence of fabricating false evidence. A person commits this offence when he creates false circumstances, false records, false documents, false electronic records, or false statements intending that they be used as evidence in a legal proceeding and thereby mislead the authority deciding the case.
simple_explanation: If a person creates fake evidence, plants objects, makes false records, forges documents, manipulates electronic records, or creates false circumstances to mislead a court, public servant, or arbitrator, he commits the offence of fabricating false evidence.
objective: To protect judicial and quasi-judicial proceedings from deception and to ensure that decisions are based on genuine evidence rather than fabricated material.
legal_significance: Section 192 IPC is one of the most important provisions protecting the administration of justice. It covers the creation of false evidence even before such evidence is actually produced in court.
essential_elements: The accused must create a circumstance, record, document, electronic record, or statement.
The circumstance, record, document, or statement must be false.
The accused must intend that it should appear in evidence in a legal proceeding.
The proceeding may be before a court, public servant, or arbitrator.
The accused must intend that the false evidence should cause an erroneous opinion on a material point.

creation_of_false_circumstance.description: Creating an artificial situation that did not genuinely exist.

false_entry.description: Making a false entry in books, registers, records, or electronic records.

false_document.description: Preparing a document containing false information.

false_electronic_record.description: Creating or altering electronic records to contain false information.

intention_to_use_as_evidence.description: The fabricated material must be intended to appear as evidence.

intention_to_mislead.description: The accused must intend that the authority should form an incorrect opinion regarding a material fact.

three_major_forms_of_fabrication.description: Artificially creating facts or situations that never existed.
Creating false entries in books, registers, accounts, databases, or records.
Creating documents or electronic records containing false statements.

persons_before_whom_evidence_may_be_used: Public Servant acting in a legal proceeding

material_point_requirement.meaning: The fabricated evidence must relate to an important fact capable of affecting the outcome of the proceeding.

mens_rea.description: There must be a deliberate intention to use the fabricated material as evidence and mislead the authority.

actus_reus.description: Creating false circumstances, records, documents, statements, or electronic records.

statutory_illustrations.illustration: A secretly places jewels in Z's box intending that they be found there and cause Z to be convicted of theft.
A makes a false entry in his shop-book intending to use it as corroborative evidence in court.
A writes a fake letter imitating Z's handwriting and places it where police are likely to find it, intending to implicate Z in a conspiracy.

when_section_192_applies.condition: A person plants stolen property in another person's house to implicate him.
A person creates fake accounting records to support a legal claim.
A person manipulates electronic records to mislead an investigation.
A person prepares forged documents intending them to be used in court.

when_section_192_does_not_apply.situation: The false material was not intended to be used in a legal proceeding.
An honest clerical or accounting mistake occurs.
The false statement has no connection with any legal proceeding.
when_section_192_does_not_apply.reason: The section requires intended use in evidence.

examples.scenario: A edits CCTV footage to falsely show B committing theft and submits it during investigation.
examples.result: Fabrication of false evidence.

examples.scenario: A inserts fake transaction records into a company database intending to use them in court.
examples.result: Fabrication of false evidence.

examples.scenario: A creates a fake email chain and presents it during arbitration proceedings.
examples.result: Fabrication of false evidence.

examples.scenario: A accidentally records incorrect information due to a software error.
examples.result: Generally not fabrication of false evidence.

judicial_principles: Actual production of the fabricated evidence is not always necessary.
The intention to use the fabricated material as evidence is essential.
The false evidence must relate to a material fact.
Fabrication may occur through physical, documentary, or electronic means.
The offence is complete once fabrication is done with the required intention.

punishment.note: Section 192 defines the offence. Punishment is prescribed under Section 193 IPC.

section_191.title: Giving False Evidence
section_191.description: Making a false statement while under a legal obligation to tell the truth.

section_192.title: Fabricating False Evidence
section_192.description: Creating false circumstances, documents, records, or evidence intended to mislead legal proceedings.

difference_between_false_evidence_and_fa.giving_false_evidence: A person personally tells a lie under oath or legal obligation.
difference_between_false_evidence_and_fa.fabricating_false_evidence: A person creates fake evidence to be relied upon by others.

relationship_with_other_sections.193: Punishment for false evidence and fabricated evidence.
relationship_with_other_sections.196: Using evidence known to be false.
relationship_with_other_sections.200: Using such false declaration as true.

faqs.question: What is fabricating false evidence?
faqs.answer: Creating fake circumstances, records, documents, or electronic records intended to mislead legal proceedings.

faqs.question: Is planting evidence a crime under Section 192 IPC?
faqs.answer: Yes. Planting evidence to implicate someone is a classic example of fabrication.

faqs.question: Does Section 192 itself provide punishment?
faqs.answer: No. Punishment is provided under Section 193 IPC.

faqs.question: Can electronic records be fabricated evidence?
faqs.answer: Yes. The section specifically covers electronic records.

faqs.question: Is actual use in court necessary?
faqs.answer: No. The offence may be complete once the fabrication is done with the required intention.

max_punishment: Punishment prescribed under Section 193 IPC

historical_background.purpose: Introduced to punish those who create false evidence intended to mislead courts and legal authorities.

ai_summary: Section 192 IPC defines the offence of fabricating false evidence. A person commits this offence when he creates false circumstances, false entries, false documents, false electronic records, or false statements intending that they be used as evidence in a judicial or legal proceeding and cause the authority deciding the matter to form an incorrect opinion on a material issue. Punishment for this offence is provided under Section 193 IPC.

title: Punishment for False Evidence
chapter: Chapter XI - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
bare_act_text: Whoever intentionally gives false evidence in any stage of a judicial proceeding, or fabricates false evidence for the purpose of being used in any stage of a judicial proceeding, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine; and whoever intentionally gives or fabricates false evidence in any other case, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, and shall also be liable to fine.
meaning: Section 193 IPC prescribes punishment for intentionally giving false evidence or fabricating false evidence. The punishment is more severe when the offence is committed in relation to a judicial proceeding.
simple_explanation: If a person knowingly lies under oath or creates fake evidence to mislead a court or legal authority, he can be punished under Section 193 IPC. False evidence in court cases attracts a higher punishment than false evidence in other proceedings.
objective: To preserve the integrity of judicial proceedings and ensure that courts and legal authorities rely only on truthful and genuine evidence.
legal_significance: This section serves as the primary punishment provision for offences defined under Sections 191 (Giving False Evidence) and 192 (Fabricating False Evidence).
essential_elements: The accused must intentionally give false evidence or fabricate false evidence.
The false evidence must fall under Sections 191 or 192 IPC.
The act must be done knowingly and deliberately.
The evidence may relate to a judicial proceeding or another legal proceeding.

false_evidence.description: Knowingly making a false statement while under a legal obligation to tell the truth.

fabricated_evidence.description: Creating false circumstances, records, documents, or electronic records intended to mislead legal proceedings.

intentional_act.description: The falsehood must be deliberate and conscious.

judicial_proceeding.description: Includes courts, court-martial proceedings, and legally recognized investigations connected with courts.

explanation_1.text: A trial before a Court-martial is a judicial proceeding.
explanation_1.importance: False evidence before a Court-martial attracts the enhanced punishment.

explanation_2.text: An investigation directed by law preliminary to a proceeding before a Court of Justice is a stage of a judicial proceeding.
explanation_2.importance: False evidence during such investigations is treated as false evidence in a judicial proceeding.

explanation_3.text: An investigation directed and conducted under the authority of a Court of Justice is a stage of a judicial proceeding.
explanation_3.importance: Even investigations outside a courtroom may qualify as judicial proceedings.

illustrations.scenario: A gives false evidence on oath before a Magistrate during an inquiry to determine whether Z should be committed for trial.
illustrations.result: Since the inquiry is a stage of a judicial proceeding, A is punishable under Section 193 IPC.

illustrations.scenario: A knowingly gives false evidence during a court-directed land boundary investigation.
illustrations.result: The investigation is a stage of a judicial proceeding; Section 193 IPC applies.

judicial_proceeding.offence: Giving or fabricating false evidence in a judicial proceeding.

other_cases.offence: Giving or fabricating false evidence in any other case.

when_section_applies.condition: A witness knowingly lies during a criminal trial.
A person files a false affidavit before a court.
A person creates fake records for use in litigation.
A person fabricates evidence during a court-directed investigation.

when_section_does_not_apply.reason: Truthful evidence cannot constitute the offence.

when_section_does_not_apply.situation: The person genuinely believes the statement to be true.
A clerical or accidental error occurs.

mens_rea.description: The giving or fabrication of false evidence must be intentional.

actus_reus.description: Giving false evidence or fabricating false evidence.

judicial_principles: Intentional falsehood is essential.
The section punishes both false testimony and fabricated evidence.
Judicial proceedings attract enhanced punishment.
Court-directed investigations are treated as judicial proceedings.
The provision protects the administration of justice.

relationship_with_other_sections.193: Provides punishment for offences under Sections 191 and 192.
relationship_with_other_sections.194: False evidence intended to secure conviction for a capital offence.
relationship_with_other_sections.195: False evidence intended to secure conviction for serious offences.

nature_of_offence.bailable: Depends upon the circumstances and procedural law

faqs.question: What does Section 193 IPC punish?
faqs.answer: Giving false evidence and fabricating false evidence.

faqs.question: What is the maximum punishment in a judicial proceeding?
faqs.answer: Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.

faqs.question: What is the punishment in non-judicial cases?
faqs.answer: Imprisonment up to 3 years and fine.

faqs.question: Does it apply to fabricated electronic records?
faqs.answer: Yes, if they constitute false evidence under Section 192 IPC.

faqs.question: Is Court-martial considered a judicial proceeding?
faqs.answer: Yes, under Explanation 1.

max_punishment: 7 years imprisonment and fine

historical_background.purpose: To punish deliberate falsehoods and fabricated evidence that interfere with the administration of justice.

ai_summary: Section 193 IPC punishes any person who intentionally gives false evidence or fabricates false evidence. Where the offence is committed in a judicial proceeding, the punishment may extend to 7 years' imprisonment and fine. In other cases, the punishment may extend to 3 years' imprisonment and fine. The section protects the integrity of courts, investigations, and legal proceedings.

title: Giving or Fabricating False Evidence with Intent to Procure Conviction of Capital Offence
chapter: Chapter XI - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
bare_act_text: Whoever gives or fabricates false evidence, intending thereby to cause, or knowing it to be likely that he will thereby cause, any person to be convicted of an offence punishable with death, shall be punished with imprisonment for life, or with rigorous imprisonment for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine. If an innocent person is convicted and executed in consequence of such false evidence, the offender may be punished with death or imprisonment for life.
meaning: Section 194 IPC punishes a person who intentionally gives or fabricates false evidence with the objective of causing another person to be convicted of a capital offence.
simple_explanation: If someone lies in court or creates fake evidence to get another person convicted of an offence punishable by death, the person creating the false evidence commits a serious offence under Section 194 IPC.
objective: To protect innocent persons from wrongful conviction in cases involving capital punishment.
legal_significance: This is one of the gravest offences against public justice because false evidence may result in the execution of an innocent person.
essential_elements: Giving false evidence or fabricating false evidence.
Intention or knowledge that such evidence may cause conviction.
The conviction sought must be for a capital offence.
The false evidence must be capable of influencing the outcome.

mens_rea.description: Intent to cause conviction or knowledge that conviction is likely.

normal_case.imprisonment: Life imprisonment or rigorous imprisonment up to 10 years

examples.scenario: A fabricates evidence to falsely implicate B in a murder case.
examples.result: Section 194 IPC applies.

max_punishment: Death Penalty
ai_summary: Section 194 IPC punishes persons who intentionally give or fabricate false evidence to secure conviction for a capital offence. Where an innocent person is executed because of such false evidence, the offender may be sentenced to death.

title: Giving or Fabricating False Evidence with Intent to Procure Conviction of Offence Punishable with Imprisonment for Life or Imprisonment
chapter: Chapter XI - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
bare_act_text: Whoever gives or fabricates false evidence intending thereby to cause, or knowing it to be likely that he will thereby cause, any person to be convicted of an offence which by the law for the time being in force in India is not capital, but punishable with imprisonment for life, or imprisonment for a term of seven years or upwards, shall be punished as a person convicted of that offence would be liable to be punished.
meaning: Section 195 IPC punishes a person who gives or fabricates false evidence to cause another person to be convicted of a non-capital offence punishable with imprisonment for life or seven or more years.
simple_explanation: If someone gives false evidence in court to get another person convicted of a serious (but non-death penalty) offence like dacoity, that person will receive the same punishment as the person they falsely implicated.
objective: To deter fabrication of false evidence in serious criminal cases by imposing equivalent punishment on the fabricator.
legal_significance: The offender is punished identically to the person they attempted to wrongfully convict, making the punishment proportional to the gravity of the false accusation.
essential_elements: Giving or fabricating false evidence.
Intention or knowledge that conviction is likely to result.
The offence targeted must be non-capital but punishable with imprisonment for life or seven or more years.
The false evidence must be capable of influencing the outcome.

mens_rea.description: Intent to cause conviction or knowledge that such conviction is likely.

illustration: A gives false evidence before a Court of Justice intending to cause Z to be convicted of dacoity. The punishment of dacoity is imprisonment for life or rigorous imprisonment up to ten years, with or without fine. A is therefore liable to imprisonment for life or imprisonment, with or without fine.

normal_case.imprisonment: Same as the offence the accused was falsely implicated for

examples.scenario: A gives false evidence to get Z convicted of dacoity punishable with life imprisonment.
examples.result: A is liable for the same punishment as dacoity under Section 195 IPC.

max_punishment: Imprisonment for Life (equivalent to offence fabricated for)
ai_summary: Section 195 IPC imposes mirror punishment — the person who fabricates false evidence to secure conviction for a serious non-capital offence receives the exact same punishment as the person they attempted to implicate.

title: Threatening Any Person to Give False Evidence
chapter: Chapter XI - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
bare_act_text: Whoever threatens another with any injury to his person, reputation or property or to the person or reputation of any one in whom that person is interested, with intent to cause that person to give false evidence shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, or with fine, or with both; and if innocent person is convicted and sentenced in consequence of such false evidence, with death or imprisonment for more than seven years, the person who threatens shall be punished with the same punishment and sentence in the same manner and to the same extent such innocent person is punished and sentenced.
meaning: Section 195A IPC punishes anyone who coerces or threatens another person to give false evidence, with enhanced punishment if an innocent person is wrongfully convicted as a result.
simple_explanation: If someone threatens another person to make them lie in court — by threatening harm to their body, reputation, or property — they can be jailed up to 7 years. If an innocent person is hanged or jailed for more than 7 years due to that forced false testimony, the threatening person gets the same punishment.
objective: To protect witnesses from coercion and ensure the integrity of evidence presented before courts.
legal_significance: Inserted in 2006, this section addresses witness intimidation. The mirror-punishment clause in aggravated cases is a strong deterrent.
essential_elements: Threatening another person with injury to person, reputation, or property.
The threat is directed at the witness or someone they care about.
Intent to cause that person to give false evidence.
In aggravated cases: an innocent person is convicted and sentenced to death or imprisonment exceeding seven years as a result.

mens_rea.description: Intent to cause the threatened person to give false evidence.

normal_case.imprisonment: Imprisonment of either description up to 7 years

aggravated_case.condition: Innocent person convicted and sentenced to death or imprisonment for more than 7 years
aggravated_case.punishment: Same punishment and sentence as imposed on the innocent person

examples.scenario: X threatens Y that he will harm Y's family unless Y testifies falsely against Z in a murder trial.
examples.result: X is liable under Section 195A IPC. If Z is executed as a result, X faces the death penalty.

max_punishment: Death Penalty (in aggravated case)
ai_summary: Section 195A IPC, inserted in 2006, criminalises witness intimidation. In the worst case where an innocent person is executed due to such forced testimony, the threatening person faces the death penalty.

title: Using Evidence Known to Be False
chapter: Chapter XI - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
bare_act_text: Whoever corruptly uses or attempts to use as true or genuine evidence any evidence which he knows to be false or fabricated, shall be punished in the same manner as if he gave or fabricated false evidence.
meaning: Section 196 IPC punishes a person who knowingly and corruptly uses false or fabricated evidence as if it were true.
simple_explanation: If a person knows that certain evidence is fake but still presents it in court as genuine, they will be punished the same way as someone who actually created or gave the false evidence.
objective: To prevent the corrupt use of false evidence in legal proceedings, even by someone who did not originally fabricate it.
legal_significance: Extends liability beyond the fabricator to anyone who knowingly exploits false evidence, closing a loophole where intermediaries could escape punishment.
essential_elements: Using or attempting to use evidence in legal proceedings.
The evidence is false or fabricated.
The person knows the evidence to be false or fabricated.
The use is corrupt — with dishonest intent.

mens_rea.description: Knowledge that the evidence is false or fabricated, combined with corrupt intent to use it as true.

normal_case.imprisonment: Same as giving or fabricating false evidence — up to 7 years in judicial proceedings, up to 3 years otherwise

examples.scenario: A obtains a forged document from B and presents it in court knowing it is fabricated.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 196 IPC for using false evidence.

max_punishment: Rigorous Imprisonment up to 7 years
ai_summary: Section 196 IPC punishes the corrupt use of known false evidence with the same severity as giving or fabricating it. Knowledge of falsity combined with corrupt intent is the core requirement.

title: Issuing or Signing False Certificate
chapter: Chapter XI - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
bare_act_text: Whoever issues or signs any certificate required by law to be given or signed, or relating to any fact of which such certificate is by law admissible in evidence, knowing or believing that such certificate is false in any material point, shall be punished in the same manner as if he gave false evidence.
meaning: Section 197 IPC punishes a person who issues or signs a legally required certificate while knowing or believing it contains a materially false statement.
simple_explanation: If a person signs or issues an official certificate — like a medical certificate, birth certificate, or caste certificate — knowing it contains false information, they will be punished the same as someone who gives false evidence in court.
objective: To ensure the integrity and authenticity of official certificates that have legal evidentiary value.
legal_significance: Targets professionals and officials such as doctors and government officers who misuse their authority to issue false certificates that can affect legal proceedings.
essential_elements: Issuing or signing a certificate.
The certificate is required by law to be given or signed, or is admissible in evidence by law.
The certificate is false in a material point.
The person knows or believes the certificate to be false.

mens_rea.description: Knowledge or belief that the certificate is false in a material point at the time of issuing or signing.

normal_case.imprisonment: Same as giving false evidence — up to 7 years in judicial proceedings, up to 3 years otherwise

examples.scenario: A doctor issues a false medical certificate stating a patient is unfit for trial, knowing the patient is perfectly healthy.
examples.result: The doctor is liable under Section 197 IPC.

max_punishment: Rigorous Imprisonment up to 7 years
ai_summary: Section 197 IPC punishes officials and professionals who knowingly issue or sign false certificates having legal evidentiary value, treating such conduct on par with giving false evidence in court.

title: Using as True a Certificate Known to Be False
chapter: Chapter XI - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
bare_act_text: Whoever corruptly uses or attempts to use any such certificate as a true certificate, knowing the same to be false in any material point, shall be punished in the same manner as if he gave false evidence.
meaning: Section 198 IPC punishes a person who knowingly and corruptly uses a false certificate as if it were genuine.
simple_explanation: If someone knows an official certificate is false in some important way but still presents or uses it as true, they will be punished the same as someone who gives false evidence in court.
objective: To prevent the corrupt exploitation of false certificates in legal or official proceedings.
legal_significance: Mirrors Section 196 but applies specifically to certificates. Together with Section 197, it covers both the issuer and the user of false certificates.
essential_elements: Using or attempting to use a certificate.
The certificate is false in a material point.
The person knows the certificate to be false.
The use is corrupt — with dishonest or fraudulent intent.

mens_rea.description: Knowledge that the certificate is false in a material point, combined with corrupt intent to use it as genuine.

normal_case.imprisonment: Same as giving false evidence — up to 7 years in judicial proceedings, up to 3 years otherwise

examples.scenario: B obtains a forged caste certificate and submits it to a government authority knowing it is false.
examples.result: B is liable under Section 198 IPC.

max_punishment: Rigorous Imprisonment up to 7 years
ai_summary: Section 198 IPC punishes the corrupt use of a known false certificate as genuine. It complements Section 197 by targeting the user rather than the issuer of a false certificate.

title: False Statement Made in Declaration Which is by Law Receivable as Evidence
chapter: Chapter XI - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
bare_act_text: Whoever, in any declaration made or subscribed by him, which declaration any Court of Justice, or any public servant or other person, is bound or authorised by law to receive as evidence of any fact, makes any statement which is false, and which he either knows or believes to be false or does not believe to be true, touching any point material to the object for which the declaration is made or used, shall be punished in the same manner as if he gave false evidence.
meaning: Section 199 IPC punishes a person who makes a false statement in any declaration that is legally receivable as evidence before a court, public servant, or authorised authority.
simple_explanation: If a person makes a sworn declaration like an affidavit before a court or public officer and includes a false statement on an important point knowing it is false, they will be punished the same as someone who lies under oath in court.
objective: To ensure truthfulness in legal declarations and affidavits that are treated as evidence by courts and public authorities.
legal_significance: Extends the reach of perjury laws to out-of-court declarations and affidavits that have evidentiary value, covering sworn statements beyond live testimony.
essential_elements: Making or subscribing a declaration.
The declaration is one that a court, public servant, or authorised person is bound or authorised by law to receive as evidence.
The statement made in the declaration is false.
The person knows or believes the statement to be false, or does not believe it to be true.
The false statement touches a point material to the purpose of the declaration.

mens_rea.description: Knowledge or belief that the statement is false, or absence of belief in its truth, at the time of making the declaration.

normal_case.imprisonment: Same as giving false evidence — up to 7 years in judicial proceedings, up to 3 years otherwise

examples.scenario: A files an affidavit before the High Court falsely stating he has no prior criminal convictions, knowing this to be untrue.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 199 IPC.

max_punishment: Rigorous Imprisonment up to 7 years
ai_summary: Section 199 IPC punishes false statements made in legally receivable declarations such as affidavits. Any authorised declaration admissible as evidence falls within this section's scope, even if made outside a courtroom.

title: Using as True Such Declaration Knowing It to Be False
chapter: Chapter XI - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
bare_act_text: Whoever corruptly uses or attempts to use as true any such declaration, knowing the same to be false in any material point, shall be punished in the same manner as if he gave false evidence. Explanation: A declaration which is inadmissible merely upon the ground of some informality, is a declaration within the meaning of sections 199 and 200.
meaning: Section 200 IPC punishes a person who knowingly and corruptly uses a false declaration as if it were true before a court or public authority.
simple_explanation: If someone knows a sworn declaration like an affidavit is false but still presents it to a court or authority as genuine, they will be punished the same as someone who lies in court. Even a technically defective declaration still counts under this section.
objective: To prevent the fraudulent use of false declarations in legal proceedings and before public authorities.
legal_significance: Completes the pair with Section 199. The Explanation ensures technical loopholes around informality cannot be exploited to escape liability.
essential_elements: Using or attempting to use a declaration before a court, public servant, or authorised authority.
The declaration is false in a material point.
The person knows the declaration to be false.
The use is corrupt — with dishonest intent.
A declaration inadmissible only due to informality still falls within this section.

mens_rea.description: Knowledge that the declaration is false in a material point, combined with corrupt intent to use it as genuine.

normal_case.imprisonment: Same as giving false evidence — up to 7 years in judicial proceedings, up to 3 years otherwise

examples.scenario: B obtains a false affidavit from C and files it in civil court knowing it contains false statements about property ownership.
examples.result: B is liable under Section 200 IPC.

max_punishment: Rigorous Imprisonment up to 7 years
ai_summary: Section 200 IPC punishes the corrupt use of a known false declaration as genuine. The Explanation clarifies that even technically inadmissible declarations due to informality alone are covered, preventing procedural escapes.

title: Causing Disappearance of Evidence of Offence, or Giving False Information to Screen Offender
chapter: Chapter XI - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
bare_act_text: Whoever, knowing or having reason to believe that an offence has been committed, causes any evidence of the commission of that offence to disappear, with the intention of screening the offender from legal punishment, or with that intention gives any information respecting the offence which he knows or believes to be false, shall be punished as follows: if the offence is punishable with death — with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine; if the offence is punishable with imprisonment for life or imprisonment up to ten years — with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, and shall also be liable to fine; if the offence is punishable with imprisonment for any term not extending to ten years — with imprisonment of the description provided for the offence, for a term which may extend to one-fourth part of the longest term of imprisonment provided for the offence, or with fine, or with both.
meaning: Section 201 IPC punishes a person who destroys evidence or gives false information to protect an offender from legal punishment, with the severity of punishment depending on the gravity of the underlying offence.
simple_explanation: If someone knows a crime has been committed and helps hide evidence or lies about it to protect the criminal, they will be punished. The more serious the original crime, the more severe their punishment.
objective: To prevent obstruction of justice by ensuring that persons who destroy evidence or mislead investigators are held criminally liable.
legal_significance: This section covers both physical destruction of evidence and giving false information — two common methods of shielding offenders. Punishment is graduated based on the severity of the screened offence.
essential_elements: Knowledge or reason to believe that an offence has been committed.
Causing evidence of that offence to disappear, or giving false information about the offence.
Intention to screen the offender from legal punishment.
The severity of punishment depends on the punishment for the underlying offence.

mens_rea.description: Intention to screen the offender from legal punishment.

illustration: A, knowing that B has murdered Z, assists B to hide the body with the intention of screening B from punishment. A is liable to imprisonment of either description for seven years, and also to fine.

tiered.condition: Underlying offence punishable with imprisonment for life or up to 10 years
Underlying offence punishable with imprisonment less than 10 years
tiered.imprisonment: Up to one-fourth of the longest term provided for that offence

examples.scenario: A helps B dispose of a murder weapon knowing B committed the murder.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 201 IPC with up to 7 years imprisonment.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years
ai_summary: Section 201 IPC punishes destruction of evidence or giving false information to shield an offender. Punishment is tiered — the graver the underlying offence, the harsher the sentence for the person obstructing justice.

title: Intentional Omission to Give Information of Offence by Person Bound to Inform
chapter: Chapter XI - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
bare_act_text: Whoever, knowing or having reason to believe that an offence has been committed, intentionally omits to give any information respecting that offence which he is legally bound to give, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine, or with both.
meaning: Section 202 IPC punishes a person who is legally obligated to report an offence but intentionally fails to do so despite knowing or having reason to believe it was committed.
simple_explanation: If a person is legally required to report a crime — like a public servant or police officer — but deliberately stays silent knowing a crime has occurred, they can be jailed for up to 6 months or fined.
objective: To ensure that persons legally bound to report offences do not remain silent and thereby enable offenders to escape justice.
legal_significance: This section imposes a positive legal duty to report crimes on those who are bound by law to do so. It punishes deliberate inaction rather than active obstruction.
essential_elements: Knowledge or reason to believe that an offence has been committed.
A legal obligation on the person to give information about that offence.
Intentional omission to give such information.

mens_rea.description: Intentional omission — the failure to inform must be deliberate, not accidental.

examples.scenario: A police officer knows a robbery has been committed but intentionally fails to file a report or inform superiors.
examples.result: The officer is liable under Section 202 IPC.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 6 months
ai_summary: Section 202 IPC punishes deliberate silence by persons legally bound to report crimes. It targets willful omission to inform, ensuring those with a legal duty to report cannot shield offenders through inaction.

title: Giving False Information Respecting an Offence Committed
chapter: Chapter XI - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
bare_act_text: Whoever, knowing or having reason to believe that an offence has been committed, gives any information respecting that offence which he knows or believes to be false, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both. Explanation: In sections 201 and 202 and in this section the word 'offence' includes any act committed at any place out of India, which, if committed in India, would be punishable under any of the following sections, namely, 302, 304, 382, 392, 393, 394, 395, 396, 397, 398, 399, 402, 435, 436, 449, 450, 457, 458, 459 and 460.
meaning: Section 203 IPC punishes a person who knowingly gives false information about a committed offence to mislead investigators or authorities.
