simple_explanation: If someone knows a crime has happened and deliberately gives wrong or false information about it to the police or any authority, they can be jailed for up to 2 years or fined.
objective: To prevent deliberate misdirection of law enforcement and protect the integrity of criminal investigations.
legal_significance: Unlike Section 202 which targets silence, Section 203 targets active deception. The Explanation extends the scope to cover serious offences committed outside India if they would be punishable under the listed sections if committed in India.
essential_elements: Knowledge or reason to believe that an offence has been committed.
Giving information respecting that offence.
The information given is known or believed by the person to be false.

mens_rea.description: Knowledge or belief that the information being given is false.

examples.scenario: A, knowing that B committed a theft, tells the police that C committed it.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 203 IPC for giving false information.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 2 years
ai_summary: Section 203 IPC punishes active deception of authorities by giving false information about a known offence. The Explanation broadens the section's reach to serious transnational offences that would be crimes if committed in India.

title: Destruction of Document to Prevent Its Production as Evidence
chapter: Chapter XI - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
bare_act_text: Whoever secretes or destroys any document and electronic record which he may be lawfully compelled to produce as evidence in a Court of Justice, or in any proceeding lawfully held before a public servant, as such, or obliterates or renders illegible the whole or any part of such document or electronic record with the intention of preventing the same from being produced or used as evidence before such Court or public servant as aforesaid, or after he shall have been lawfully summoned or required to produce the same for that purpose, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.
meaning: Section 204 IPC punishes a person who hides, destroys, or tampers with a document or electronic record to prevent it from being used as evidence in court or before a public servant.
simple_explanation: If someone is required by law to produce a document or electronic record in court or before an authority and instead hides it, destroys it, or makes it unreadable to prevent it being used as evidence, they can be jailed for up to 2 years or fined.
objective: To protect the integrity of evidence in judicial and quasi-judicial proceedings by penalising deliberate destruction or tampering of documents and electronic records.
legal_significance: Notably updated to include electronic records, reflecting modern evidentiary needs. Covers both pre-summons destruction (with intent) and post-summons destruction.
essential_elements: The person is in possession of a document or electronic record.
They are lawfully compellable to produce it as evidence.
They secrete, destroy, obliterate, or render it illegible.
The act is done with the intention of preventing its production or use as evidence.
Liability also attaches after being lawfully summoned to produce it.

mens_rea.description: Intention to prevent the document or electronic record from being produced or used as evidence.

examples.scenario: A, after being summoned to produce financial records in court, burns the documents to prevent their use as evidence.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 204 IPC.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 2 years
ai_summary: Section 204 IPC punishes destruction or concealment of documents and electronic records to obstruct their use as evidence. It covers both physical documents and digital records, making it relevant to modern cybercrime and financial fraud cases.

title: False Personation for Purpose of Act or Proceeding in Suit or Prosecution
chapter: Chapter XI - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
bare_act_text: Whoever falsely personates another, and in such assumed character makes any admission or statement, or confesses judgment, or causes any process to be issued or becomes bail or security, or does any other act in any suit or criminal prosecution, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both.
meaning: Section 205 IPC punishes a person who impersonates another and performs any legal act — such as making a statement, becoming a surety, or causing a legal process to be issued — in that false identity during a suit or criminal proceeding.
simple_explanation: If someone pretends to be another person in a court case or legal proceeding and makes statements, confesses, stands as bail, or does anything else in that false identity, they can be jailed for up to 3 years or fined.
objective: To prevent fraud and misrepresentation in judicial proceedings by criminalising false impersonation that interferes with the course of justice.
legal_significance: Protects the integrity of judicial proceedings. Covers a wide range of acts — from making admissions to becoming a surety — performed under a false identity, making it a comprehensive anti-impersonation provision.
essential_elements: Falsely personating another person.
Acting in such assumed character.
Performing a legal act — making admissions, statements, confessing judgment, causing process to be issued, becoming bail or security, or any other act.
The act must occur in a suit or criminal prosecution.

mens_rea.description: Knowledge that the person is assuming a false identity to perform legal acts in a proceeding.

examples.scenario: A impersonates B in a civil court and makes admissions on B's behalf without B's knowledge or consent.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 205 IPC.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 years
ai_summary: Section 205 IPC punishes false impersonation in legal proceedings — anyone who assumes another's identity to make statements, stand as bail, or perform any legal act in a suit or prosecution faces up to 3 years imprisonment.

title: Fraudulent Removal or Concealment of Property to Prevent Its Seizure as Forfeited or in Execution
chapter: Chapter XI - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
bare_act_text: Whoever fraudulently removes, conceals, transfers or delivers to any person any property or any interest therein, intending thereby to prevent that property or interest therein from being taken as a forfeiture or in satisfaction of a fine, under a sentence which has been pronounced, or which he knows to be likely to be pronounced, by a Court of Justice or other competent authority, or from being taken in execution of a decree or order which has been made, or which he knows to be likely to be made by a Court of Justice in a civil suit, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years or with fine, or with both.
meaning: Section 206 IPC punishes a person who fraudulently hides, transfers, or moves property to prevent it from being seized by a court as forfeiture, fine, or in execution of a civil decree.
simple_explanation: If someone knows a court is likely to seize their property — as a fine or under a civil decree — and they fraudulently hide, transfer, or hand it over to someone else to avoid that seizure, they can be jailed for up to 2 years or fined.
objective: To protect the enforceability of court orders and decrees by preventing fraudulent disposal of property subject to forfeiture or execution.
legal_significance: Covers both pronounced sentences and anticipated ones, and both criminal forfeitures and civil execution — making it a broad anti-fraud provision protecting judicial processes.
essential_elements: Fraudulent removal, concealment, transfer, or delivery of property or interest therein.
Intention to prevent the property from being taken as forfeiture, fine, or in execution of a decree.
The sentence or decree must have been pronounced or the person must know it is likely to be pronounced.
Applies to both criminal and civil court orders.

mens_rea.description: Fraudulent intent to prevent lawful seizure of property by a court or competent authority.

examples.scenario: A, knowing a court is about to attach his property in execution of a civil decree, secretly transfers it to his brother to avoid seizure.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 206 IPC.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 2 years
ai_summary: Section 206 IPC punishes fraudulent disposal of property to evade court-ordered forfeiture or civil execution. It covers anticipated as well as pronounced orders, preventing pre-emptive evasion of judicial process.

title: Fraudulent Claim to Property to Prevent Its Seizure as Forfeited or in Execution
chapter: Chapter XI - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
bare_act_text: Whoever fraudulently accepts, receives or claims any property or any interest therein, knowing that he has no right or rightful claim to such property or interest, or practices any deception touching any right to any property or any interest therein, intending thereby to prevent that property or interest therein from being taken as a forfeiture or in satisfaction of a fine, under a sentence which has been pronounced, or which he knows to be likely to be pronounced by a Court of Justice or other competent authority, or from being taken in execution of a decree or order which has been made, or which he knows to be likely to be made by a Court of Justice in a civil suit, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.
meaning: Section 207 IPC punishes a person who fraudulently claims or accepts property they have no right to, or deceives others about property rights, in order to prevent that property from being seized by a court.
simple_explanation: If someone falsely claims ownership of another person's property — knowing they have no real right to it — just to stop a court from seizing it as forfeiture or in execution of a decree, they can be jailed for up to 2 years or fined.
objective: To prevent third parties from acting as shields for persons whose property is subject to court seizure by making fraudulent claims over it.
legal_significance: Complements Section 206 — while Section 206 targets the property owner who hides it, Section 207 targets the third party who fraudulently claims it to obstruct court execution.
essential_elements: Fraudulently accepting, receiving, or claiming property or interest therein.
Knowledge that the person has no right or rightful claim to the property.
Or practising deception about rights over the property.
Intention to prevent the property from being seized as forfeiture, fine, or in execution of a decree.

mens_rea.description: Knowledge of having no rightful claim combined with fraudulent intent to obstruct court seizure.

examples.scenario: B, knowing A's property is about to be attached by court, falsely claims the property belongs to him to prevent its seizure.
examples.result: B is liable under Section 207 IPC.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 2 years
ai_summary: Section 207 IPC targets third parties who fraudulently claim property to shield it from court seizure. It works alongside Section 206 to close both ends of property-concealment fraud — the owner who hides it and the accomplice who falsely claims it.

title: Fraudulently Suffering Decree for Sum Not Due
chapter: Chapter XI - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
bare_act_text: Whoever fraudulently causes or suffers a decree or order to be passed against him at the suit of any person for a sum not due or for a larger sum than is due to such person or for any property or interest in property to which such person is not entitled, or fraudulently causes or suffers a decree or order to be executed against him after it has been satisfied, or for anything in respect of which it has been satisfied, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.
meaning: Section 208 IPC punishes a person who deliberately allows a false or inflated decree to be passed against themselves — or allows an already-satisfied decree to be re-executed — as a fraudulent device to defeat other creditors or claimants.
simple_explanation: If someone intentionally lets a court pass a decree against them for money they don't actually owe — or for more than they owe — to defraud their real creditors, they can be jailed for up to 2 years or fined. The same applies if they let an already-paid decree be executed again.
objective: To prevent collusive decrees used as a tool to defraud legitimate creditors by artificially burdening property with fake liabilities.
legal_significance: Targets collusion between a debtor and a friendly creditor to manufacture false debt, thereby reducing assets available to genuine decree-holders or creditors.
essential_elements: Fraudulently causing or suffering a decree or order to be passed.
The decree is for a sum not due, a larger sum than due, or for property to which the claimant is not entitled.
Or fraudulently allowing an already-satisfied decree to be executed again.
The act must be done with fraudulent intent.

mens_rea.description: Fraudulent intent to allow a false, inflated, or already-satisfied decree to be passed or executed.

illustration: A institutes a suit against Z. Z, knowing that A is likely to obtain a decree against him, fraudulently suffers a judgment to pass against him for a larger amount at the suit of B, who has no just claim against him, so that B may share in the proceeds of any sale of Z's property made under A's decree. Z has committed an offence under this section.

examples.scenario: Z, facing a legitimate decree from A, collusively allows B to obtain a larger fake decree against him so B can take priority in the distribution of Z's assets.
examples.result: Z is liable under Section 208 IPC.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 2 years
ai_summary: Section 208 IPC punishes collusive decrees — where a debtor intentionally allows inflated or false court orders against themselves to defeat genuine creditors. It also covers re-execution of already-satisfied decrees.

title: Dishonestly Making False Claim in Court
chapter: Chapter XI - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
bare_act_text: Whoever fraudulently or dishonestly, or with intent to injure or annoy any person, makes in a Court of Justice any claim which he knows to be false, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, and shall also be liable to fine.
meaning: Section 209 IPC punishes a person who knowingly makes a false claim in a court of law with fraudulent, dishonest, or malicious intent.
simple_explanation: If someone files a false claim in court — knowing it is untrue — to cheat someone, cause them loss, or simply to harass them, they can be jailed for up to 2 years and fined.
objective: To prevent abuse of judicial process through fraudulent, dishonest, or vexatious claims that waste court resources and harm innocent parties.
legal_significance: Covers three distinct motives — fraud, dishonesty, and intent to injure or annoy — making it a broad provision against misuse of court proceedings. Importantly, fine is mandatory in addition to imprisonment.
essential_elements: Making a claim in a Court of Justice.
The claim is known by the person to be false.
The claim is made fraudulently, dishonestly, or with intent to injure or annoy any person.

mens_rea.description: Fraudulent or dishonest intent, or intent to injure or annoy, combined with knowledge that the claim is false.

examples.scenario: A files a civil suit against B claiming B owes him money, knowing fully that no such debt exists, intending to harass B.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 209 IPC.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 2 years
ai_summary: Section 209 IPC punishes the filing of knowingly false claims in court — whether motivated by fraud, dishonesty, or malice. Both imprisonment and fine are prescribed, reflecting the seriousness of abusing judicial process.

title: Fraudulently Obtaining Decree for Sum Not Due
chapter: Chapter XI - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
bare_act_text: Whoever fraudulently obtains a decree or order against any person for a sum not due, or for a larger sum than is due or for any property or interest in property to which he is not entitled, or fraudulently causes a decree or order to be executed against any person after it has been satisfied or for anything in respect of which it has been satisfied, or fraudulently suffers or permits any such act to be done in his name, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.
meaning: Section 210 IPC punishes a person who fraudulently obtains a court decree for money or property they are not entitled to, or who re-executes an already-satisfied decree against someone.
simple_explanation: If someone tricks a court into passing a decree in their favour for money or property they have no right to — or gets an already-paid decree executed again against someone — they can be jailed for up to 2 years or fined.
objective: To prevent fraudulent use of court decrees to extract money or property beyond what is legitimately owed.
legal_significance: Mirrors Section 208 from the claimant's side — Section 208 targets the defendant who colludes to allow a false decree, while Section 210 targets the claimant who fraudulently obtains one. Together they cover both sides of collusive decree fraud.
essential_elements: Fraudulently obtaining a decree or order.
The decree is for a sum not due, a larger sum than due, or for property to which the person is not entitled.
Or fraudulently causing a satisfied decree to be re-executed.
Also covers permitting such acts to be done in one's name.

mens_rea.description: Fraudulent intent to obtain or execute a decree beyond what is legitimately due.

examples.scenario: A obtains a court decree against B for Rs. 1,00,000 knowing B only owed Rs. 20,000, by submitting false accounts.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 210 IPC.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 2 years
ai_summary: Section 210 IPC punishes fraudulent procurement of inflated or undeserved court decrees, and re-execution of satisfied ones. It is the claimant-side counterpart to Section 208, together covering both parties in collusive decree fraud.

title: False Charge of Offence Made with Intent to Injure
chapter: Chapter XI - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
bare_act_text: Whoever, with intent to cause injury to any person, institutes or causes to be instituted any criminal proceeding against that person, or falsely charges any person with having committed an offence, knowing that there is no just or lawful ground for such proceeding or charge against that person, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both; and if such criminal proceeding be instituted on a false charge of an offence punishable with death, imprisonment for life, or imprisonment for seven years or upwards, shall be punishable with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.
meaning: Section 211 IPC punishes a person who maliciously institutes criminal proceedings or makes false charges against another person knowing there is no lawful ground for it, with enhanced punishment if the false charge relates to a serious offence.
simple_explanation: If someone files a false criminal case against another person just to harm them — knowing fully there is no valid ground — they can be jailed for up to 2 years. If the false charge is for a serious offence like murder or one punishable with 7+ years, the punishment goes up to 7 years.
objective: To protect innocent persons from malicious prosecution and abuse of criminal justice machinery as a tool of harassment.
legal_significance: This section is the primary remedy against malicious prosecution in Indian criminal law. The graded punishment reflects the gravity of the false charge — a false murder charge carries far more severe consequences than a minor false accusation.
essential_elements: Instituting or causing to be instituted a criminal proceeding, or falsely charging a person with an offence.
Knowledge that there is no just or lawful ground for such proceeding or charge.
Intent to cause injury to that person.
Enhanced punishment applies if the false charge is for an offence punishable with death, life imprisonment, or 7+ years.

mens_rea.description: Intent to cause injury to the person falsely charged, combined with knowledge of no lawful ground.

aggravated_case.condition: False charge is for an offence punishable with death, life imprisonment, or imprisonment of 7 years or more
aggravated_case.punishment: Up to 7 years (either description) and fine

state_amendment.provision: If the false charge is for offences under sections 354, 354A, 354B, 354C, 354D, 354E, 376B, 376C, 376F, 509, 509A or 509B — minimum 3 years, maximum 5 years imprisonment and fine.

examples.scenario: A, with intent to harass B, files a false FIR accusing B of murder knowing B had nothing to do with it.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 211 IPC with up to 7 years imprisonment.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years
ai_summary: Section 211 IPC punishes malicious prosecution — filing false criminal charges with intent to harm. Punishment is graded: up to 2 years for minor false charges, up to 7 years if the false charge involves a capital or serious offence.

title: Harbouring Offender
chapter: Chapter XI - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
bare_act_text: Whenever an offence has been committed, whoever harbours or conceals a person whom he knows or has reason to believe to be the offender, with the intention of screening him from legal punishment, shall be punished as follows: if the offence is punishable with death — with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to five years, and shall also be liable to fine; if the offence is punishable with imprisonment for life or imprisonment up to ten years — with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, and shall also be liable to fine; if the offence is punishable with imprisonment up to one year but not ten years — with imprisonment of the description provided for the offence for a term which may extend to one-fourth part of the longest term of imprisonment provided for the offence, or with fine, or with both. Exception: This provision shall not extend to any case in which the harbour or concealment is by the husband or wife of the offender.
meaning: Section 212 IPC punishes a person who shelters or hides a known offender with the intention of protecting them from legal punishment, with tiered punishment based on the gravity of the underlying offence.
simple_explanation: If someone hides or shelters a criminal — knowing or having reason to believe they committed an offence — to protect them from arrest or punishment, they will be punished. The more serious the crime committed by the person being hidden, the harsher the punishment. A spouse cannot be punished under this section for harbouring their partner.
objective: To prevent persons from shielding criminals from the law by making harbouring itself a punishable offence.
legal_significance: Punishment is graduated based on the severity of the offence committed by the harboured person. The spousal exception reflects the special nature of the marital relationship in law.
essential_elements: An offence has been committed.
The person harbours or conceals the offender.
Knowledge or reason to believe that the concealed person is the offender.
Intention to screen the offender from legal punishment.
Punishment is tiered based on the severity of the underlying offence.

mens_rea.description: Knowledge or reason to believe the person is an offender, combined with intention to screen them from legal punishment.

illustration: A, knowing that B has committed dacoity, knowingly conceals B to screen him from legal punishment. As B is liable to imprisonment for life, A is liable to imprisonment of either description for a term not exceeding three years, and is also liable to fine.

tiered.condition: Underlying offence punishable with life imprisonment or up to 10 years
Underlying offence punishable with imprisonment up to 1 year but not 10 years
tiered.imprisonment: Up to one-fourth of the longest term for that offence

exception: Does not apply when the harbouring or concealment is by the husband or wife of the offender.

examples.scenario: A shelters B in his house knowing B has just committed murder and is being sought by police.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 212 IPC with up to 5 years imprisonment.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 5 years
ai_summary: Section 212 IPC punishes harbouring of known offenders with tiered punishment tied to the severity of the offence committed. The spousal exception is a notable carve-out recognising the unique nature of marital bonds.

title: Taking Gift to Screen an Offender from Punishment
chapter: Chapter XI - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
bare_act_text: Whoever accepts or attempts to obtain, or agrees to accept, any gratification for himself or any other person, or any restitution of property to himself or any other person, in consideration of his concealing an offence or of his screening any person from legal punishment for any offence, or of his not proceeding against any person for the purpose of bringing him to legal punishment, shall be punished as follows: if the offence is punishable with death — with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine; if the offence is punishable with imprisonment for life or up to ten years — with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, and shall also be liable to fine; if the offence is punishable with imprisonment not extending to ten years — with imprisonment of the description provided for the offence for a term which may extend to one-fourth part of the longest term provided for the offence, or with fine, or with both. Exception: Sections 213 and 214 do not extend to any case in which the offence may lawfully be compounded.
meaning: Section 213 IPC punishes a person who accepts or agrees to accept any benefit — money, property, or restitution — in exchange for concealing an offence or shielding an offender from legal punishment.
simple_explanation: If someone accepts a bribe or any benefit in return for hiding a crime or protecting a criminal from punishment, they will be punished. The more serious the underlying crime, the harsher the punishment. This does not apply to offences that can legally be compounded.
objective: To prevent corrupt arrangements where persons accept benefits to suppress crimes or shield offenders, thereby undermining the criminal justice system.
legal_significance: Covers both accepting and agreeing to accept gratification — even an unsuccessful attempt to obtain a bribe is punishable. The exception for compoundable offences reflects the legal recognition that some offences can be legitimately settled.
essential_elements: Accepting, attempting to obtain, or agreeing to accept any gratification or property restitution.
The consideration is concealing an offence, screening an offender, or not proceeding against them.
Punishment is tiered based on the severity of the underlying offence.
Does not apply to lawfully compoundable offences.

mens_rea.description: Knowledge of the offence and intent to conceal it or shield the offender in exchange for gratification.

tiered.condition: Underlying offence punishable with life imprisonment or up to 10 years
Underlying offence punishable with less than 10 years
tiered.imprisonment: Up to one-fourth of the longest term for that offence

exception: Sections 213 and 214 do not apply where the offence may lawfully be compounded.

examples.scenario: A accepts Rs. 5,00,000 from B in exchange for not reporting to police that B committed a robbery.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 213 IPC.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years
ai_summary: Section 213 IPC punishes acceptance of bribes or benefits to conceal crimes or protect offenders. Punishment is tiered by the severity of the shielded offence, and the section does not apply to lawfully compoundable offences.

title: Offering Gift or Restoration of Property in Consideration of Screening Offender
chapter: Chapter XI - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
bare_act_text: Whoever gives or causes, or offers or agrees to give or cause, any gratification to any person, or restores or causes the restoration of any property to any person, in consideration of that person's concealing an offence, or of his screening any person from legal punishment for any offence, or of his not proceeding against any person for the purpose of bringing him to legal punishment, shall be punished as follows: if the offence is punishable with death — with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine; if the offence is punishable with imprisonment for life or up to ten years — with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, and shall also be liable to fine; if the offence is punishable with imprisonment not extending to ten years — with imprisonment of the description provided for the offence for a term which may extend to one-fourth part of the longest term provided for the offence, or with fine, or with both. Exception: Sections 213 and 214 do not extend to any case in which the offence may lawfully be compounded.
meaning: Section 214 IPC punishes a person who offers or gives any benefit — money, property, or restitution — to another person in exchange for that person concealing an offence or shielding an offender from legal punishment.
simple_explanation: If someone pays a bribe or offers any benefit to another person to get them to hide a crime or protect a criminal from punishment, they will be punished. Section 214 targets the person giving the bribe, while Section 213 targets the person receiving it.
objective: To penalise both sides of corrupt arrangements aimed at suppressing crimes — the bribe-giver (Section 214) and the bribe-taker (Section 213).
legal_significance: Works in tandem with Section 213 to cover both parties in bribery-for-concealment arrangements. The exception for compoundable offences applies equally to both sections.
essential_elements: Giving, causing, offering, or agreeing to give gratification or restore property.
The consideration is the recipient concealing an offence, screening an offender, or not proceeding against them.
Punishment is tiered based on the severity of the underlying offence.
Does not apply to lawfully compoundable offences.

mens_rea.description: Intent to induce another person to conceal an offence or shield an offender through gratification.

tiered.condition: Underlying offence punishable with life imprisonment or up to 10 years
Underlying offence punishable with less than 10 years
tiered.imprisonment: Up to one-fourth of the longest term for that offence

exception: Sections 213 and 214 do not apply where the offence may lawfully be compounded.

examples.scenario: B offers Rs. 5,00,000 to A to convince A not to report B's robbery to the police.
examples.result: B is liable under Section 214 IPC.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years
ai_summary: Section 214 IPC punishes the offer or giving of bribes to induce concealment of offences or shielding of offenders. It is the giver-side counterpart to Section 213 and together they cover both parties in crime-concealment bribery.

title: Taking Gift to Help to Recover Stolen Property
chapter: Chapter XI - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
bare_act_text: Whoever takes or agrees or consents to take any gratification under pretence or on account of helping any person to recover any movable property of which he shall have been deprived by any offence punishable under this Code, shall, unless he uses all means in his power to cause the offender to be apprehended and convicted of the offence, be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.
meaning: Section 215 IPC punishes a person who accepts payment under the pretence of helping someone recover stolen or lost property, unless they genuinely use all means available to get the offender arrested and convicted.
simple_explanation: If someone takes money from a theft or robbery victim claiming they will help recover the stolen property, but does not actually try to get the criminal caught and convicted, they can be jailed for up to 2 years or fined. To escape liability, they must use every means available to ensure the offender is apprehended.
objective: To prevent persons from exploiting crime victims by taking payment for property recovery while shielding the offender from prosecution.
legal_significance: This section targets a specific form of extortion common in property crime — where middlemen extract money from victims under the guise of recovery assistance while actually protecting the criminal. The escape clause incentivises genuine cooperation with law enforcement.
essential_elements: Taking or agreeing to take any gratification.
Under pretence or on account of helping recover movable property.
The property must have been lost due to an offence punishable under the IPC.
The person fails to use all means in their power to cause the offender to be apprehended and convicted.

mens_rea.description: Taking gratification under pretence of recovery assistance while not genuinely pursuing the offender's apprehension.

examples.scenario: A takes Rs. 10,000 from B — whose motorcycle was stolen — promising to help recover it, but makes no effort to identify or report the thief to police.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 215 IPC.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 2 years
ai_summary: Section 215 IPC punishes middlemen who take payment from crime victims under pretence of recovering stolen property but fail to assist in catching the offender. Genuine effort to apprehend the criminal is the only defence.

title: Harbouring Offender Who Has Escaped from Custody or Whose Apprehension Has Been Ordered
chapter: Chapter XI - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
bare_act_text: Whenever any person convicted of or charged with an offence, being in lawful custody for that offence, escapes from such custody, or whenever a public servant orders a certain person to be apprehended for an offence, whoever, knowing of such escape or order for apprehension, harbours or conceals that person with the intention of preventing him from being apprehended, shall be punished as follows: if the offence is punishable with death — with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine; if the offence is punishable with imprisonment for life or ten years — with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, with or without fine; if the offence is punishable with imprisonment up to one year but not ten years — with imprisonment of the description provided for the offence for a term which may extend to one-fourth part of the longest term provided for such offence, or with fine, or with both. Exception: This provision does not extend to the case in which the harbour or concealment is by the husband or wife of the person to be apprehended.
meaning: Section 216 IPC punishes a person who knowingly shelters or hides someone who has escaped from lawful custody or against whom an order of apprehension has been issued, with the intention of preventing their recapture.
simple_explanation: If someone knows that a person has escaped from police custody or that a warrant has been issued for their arrest, and still hides or shelters that person to prevent their recapture, they will be punished. The punishment depends on how serious the original offence was. A spouse cannot be punished for harbouring their partner.
objective: To prevent persons from frustrating lawful custody and apprehension orders by sheltering escaped or wanted offenders.
legal_significance: Distinct from Section 212 which covers harbouring after commission of an offence — Section 216 specifically targets harbouring of persons who have escaped custody or are subject to apprehension orders. Includes acts committed outside India if extraditable.
essential_elements: A person has escaped from lawful custody or a public servant has ordered their apprehension.
The accused knows of the escape or apprehension order.
The accused harbours or conceals that person.
The intention is to prevent the person from being apprehended.
Punishment is tiered based on the severity of the underlying offence.

mens_rea.description: Knowledge of the escape or apprehension order, combined with intention to prevent recapture.

tiered.condition: Underlying offence punishable with life imprisonment or 10 years
Underlying offence punishable with up to 1 year but not 10 years
tiered.imprisonment: Up to one-fourth of the longest term for that offence

exception: Does not apply when the harbouring or concealment is by the husband or wife of the person to be apprehended.

examples.scenario: A hides B in his house knowing B has escaped from judicial custody while facing a murder charge.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 216 IPC with up to 7 years imprisonment.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years
ai_summary: Section 216 IPC punishes harbouring of escaped prisoners or persons against whom apprehension orders exist. Punishment is tiered by offence severity, and the spousal exception applies. It extends to extraditable offences committed outside India.

title: Penalty for Harbouring Robbers or Dacoits
chapter: Chapter XI - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
bare_act_text: Whoever, knowing or having reason to believe that any persons are about to commit or have recently committed robbery or dacoity, harbours them or any of them, with the intention of facilitating the commission of such robbery or dacoity, or of screening them or any of them from punishment, shall be punished with rigorous imprisonment for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine. Explanation: For the purposes of this section it is immaterial whether the robbery or dacoity is intended to be committed, or has been committed, within or without India. Exception: This provision does not extend to the case in which the harbour is by the husband or wife of the offender.
meaning: Section 216A IPC specifically punishes harbouring of robbers or dacoits — either before they commit the crime or after — with rigorous imprisonment of up to 7 years.
simple_explanation: If someone knowingly shelters robbers or dacoits — whether to help them carry out the robbery/dacoity or to protect them from punishment after the act — they face rigorous imprisonment of up to 7 years. It does not matter whether the crime was committed inside or outside India. A spouse is exempt.
objective: To specifically deter the harbouring of robbers and dacoits, which are particularly serious violent offences, by prescribing rigorous (not simple) imprisonment.
legal_significance: Unlike Section 212 which covers harbouring generally, Section 216A specifically targets robbery and dacoity and mandates rigorous imprisonment — reflecting the serious organised crime nature of these offences. Applies to transnational robberies and dacoities as well.
essential_elements: Knowledge or reason to believe that persons are about to commit or have recently committed robbery or dacoity.
Harbouring those persons or any of them.
Intention to facilitate the robbery/dacoity or screen the offenders from punishment.
Immaterial whether the offence was committed inside or outside India.

mens_rea.description: Knowledge or reason to believe the harboured persons are robbers or dacoits, with intent to facilitate their crime or shield them.

exception: Does not apply when the harbouring is by the husband or wife of the offender.

examples.scenario: A provides shelter to a gang of dacoits knowing they recently committed a dacoity in a nearby village.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 216A IPC with rigorous imprisonment up to 7 years.

max_punishment: Rigorous Imprisonment up to 7 years
ai_summary: Section 216A IPC prescribes rigorous imprisonment for harbouring robbers or dacoits, whether before or after the offence. It applies to transnational offences and is stricter than general harbouring provisions — reflecting the gravity of organised violent crime.

title: Public Servant Disobeying Direction of Law with Intent to Save Person from Punishment or Property from Forfeiture
chapter: Chapter XI - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
bare_act_text: Whoever, being a public servant, knowingly disobeys any direction of the law as to the way in which he is to conduct himself as such public servant, intending thereby to save, or knowing it to be likely that he will thereby save, any person from legal punishment, or subject him to a less punishment than that to which he is liable, or with intent to save, or knowing that he is likely thereby to save, any property from forfeiture or any charge to which it is liable by law, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.
meaning: Section 217 IPC punishes a public servant who knowingly violates legal procedures or directions in their official capacity with the intent to save a person from punishment or property from forfeiture.
simple_explanation: If a public servant — like a police officer or government official — deliberately breaks the rules of their job to help someone escape punishment or prevent their property from being seized by the law, they can be jailed for up to 2 years or fined.
objective: To ensure public servants faithfully discharge their legal duties without manipulating procedures to benefit offenders or protect property subject to forfeiture.
legal_significance: Specifically targets abuse of official position to obstruct justice — covering both deliberate procedural violations and acts done with knowledge that they will shield offenders or property.
essential_elements: The accused is a public servant.
They knowingly disobey a direction of law regarding their conduct as a public servant.
The intent is to save a person from legal punishment, reduce their punishment, or save property from forfeiture.
Or knowledge that such disobedience is likely to produce these results.

mens_rea.description: Knowledge of disobeying the law combined with intent or knowledge of likely consequences of saving a person from punishment or property from forfeiture.

examples.scenario: A police officer deliberately delays filing a charge sheet against a murder accused to allow the limitation period to lapse, intending to help the accused escape conviction.
examples.result: The officer is liable under Section 217 IPC.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 2 years
ai_summary: Section 217 IPC punishes public servants who knowingly violate legal procedures to shield offenders from punishment or protect property from forfeiture. It targets deliberate procedural abuse by officials entrusted with enforcing the law.

title: Public Servant Framing Incorrect Record or Writing with Intent to Save Person from Punishment or Property from Forfeiture
chapter: Chapter XI - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
bare_act_text: Whoever, being a public servant, and being as such public servant, charged with the preparation of any record or other writing, frames that record or writing in a manner which he knows to be incorrect, with intent to cause, or knowing it to be likely that he will thereby cause, loss or injury to the public or to any person, or with intent thereby to save, or knowing it to be likely that he will thereby save, any person from legal punishment, or with intent to save, or knowing that he is likely thereby to save, any property from forfeiture or other charge to which it is liable by law, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both.
meaning: Section 218 IPC punishes a public servant who knowingly prepares a false or incorrect official record or document with the intent to harm the public, save a person from punishment, or protect property from forfeiture.
simple_explanation: If a public servant deliberately prepares a false official record — like a police report, court document, or government file — knowing it is wrong, in order to harm someone, protect a criminal, or save property from being seized, they can be jailed for up to 3 years or fined.
objective: To ensure integrity of official records prepared by public servants and prevent manipulation of documentation to obstruct justice.
legal_significance: Higher maximum punishment than Section 217 (3 years vs 2 years) reflecting that falsification of official records is more grave than mere procedural disobedience. Covers three distinct harmful intents — public injury, shielding offenders, and protecting property.
essential_elements: The accused is a public servant charged with preparing a record or writing.
They frame the record in a manner they know to be incorrect.
Intent to cause loss or injury to the public or any person, or to save a person from legal punishment, or to save property from forfeiture.

mens_rea.description: Knowledge that the record is incorrect combined with one of three intents: causing loss/injury, saving a person from punishment, or saving property from forfeiture.

examples.scenario: A police officer prepares a false investigation report deliberately omitting key evidence against an accused to help him escape conviction.
examples.result: The officer is liable under Section 218 IPC.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 years
ai_summary: Section 218 IPC punishes public servants who knowingly falsify official records to harm the public, shield offenders, or protect property from forfeiture. Higher punishment than Section 217 reflects the added gravity of documentary fraud by officials.

title: Public Servant in Judicial Proceeding Corruptly Making Report Contrary to Law
chapter: Chapter XI - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
bare_act_text: Whoever, being a public servant, corruptly or maliciously makes or pronounces in any stage of a judicial proceeding, any report, order, verdict, or decision which he knows to be contrary to law, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, or with fine, or with both.
meaning: Section 219 IPC punishes a public servant who corruptly or maliciously makes any report, order, verdict, or decision in a judicial proceeding knowing it to be contrary to law.
simple_explanation: If a public servant — such as a magistrate, judge, or officer involved in judicial proceedings — deliberately and corruptly makes a wrong order or decision knowing it violates the law, they can be jailed for up to 7 years or fined.
objective: To protect the integrity of judicial proceedings by ensuring public servants involved in them act lawfully and without corruption or malice.
legal_significance: The high maximum punishment of 7 years reflects the gravity of judicial corruption — a corrupt order or verdict by a public servant in a judicial proceeding can cause irreparable harm to individuals and public trust in the justice system.
essential_elements: The accused is a public servant.
They make or pronounce a report, order, verdict, or decision in any stage of a judicial proceeding.
The report, order, verdict, or decision is known by them to be contrary to law.
The act is done corruptly or maliciously.

mens_rea.description: Corrupt or malicious intent combined with knowledge that the report, order, verdict, or decision is contrary to law.

examples.scenario: A magistrate, having received a bribe, passes an acquittal order in a case knowing the evidence clearly establishes guilt.
examples.result: The magistrate is liable under Section 219 IPC.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years
ai_summary: Section 219 IPC punishes judicial corruption by public servants — making unlawful orders, verdicts, or decisions corruptly or maliciously. The 7-year maximum reflects the severe damage such conduct causes to the justice system.

title: Commitment for Trial or Confinement by Person Having Authority Who Knows That He is Acting Contrary to Law
chapter: Chapter XI - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
bare_act_text: Whoever, being in any office which gives him legal authority to commit persons for trial or to confinement, or to keep persons in confinement, corruptly or maliciously commits any person for trial or to confinement, or keeps any person in confinement, in the exercise of that authority knowing that in so doing he is acting contrary to law, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, or with fine, or with both.
meaning: Section 220 IPC punishes a person holding authority to commit persons for trial or confinement who corruptly or maliciously exercises that authority contrary to law — such as illegally detaining someone.
simple_explanation: If an official who has the power to send someone to trial or keep them in custody — like a magistrate or senior police officer — deliberately and wrongfully uses that power to illegally detain someone knowing it is against the law, they can be jailed for up to 7 years or fined.
objective: To protect personal liberty by ensuring that persons with authority to detain or commit individuals for trial do not misuse that power corruptly or maliciously.
legal_significance: Directly safeguards the fundamental right to liberty. The 7-year maximum punishment reflects the gravity of illegal detention or commitment for trial by an authorised official — a particularly serious abuse of state power.
essential_elements: The accused holds an office giving them legal authority to commit persons for trial or confinement, or to keep persons in confinement.
They commit a person for trial or confinement, or keep a person in confinement.
They know that in doing so they are acting contrary to law.
The act is done corruptly or maliciously.

mens_rea.description: Corrupt or malicious intent combined with knowledge that the commitment or confinement is contrary to law.

examples.scenario: A magistrate, out of personal enmity, sends A to judicial custody knowing there is no legal basis for doing so.
examples.result: The magistrate is liable under Section 220 IPC.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years
ai_summary: Section 220 IPC punishes corrupt or malicious misuse of authority to detain or commit persons for trial. It directly protects personal liberty against abuse of state power by officials entrusted with custodial authority.

title: Intentional Omission to Apprehend on the Part of Public Servant Bound to Apprehend
chapter: Chapter XI - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
bare_act_text: Whoever, being a public servant, legally bound as such public servant to apprehend or to keep in confinement any person charged with or liable to be apprehended for an offence, intentionally omits to apprehend such person, or intentionally suffers such person to escape, or intentionally aids such person in escaping or attempting to escape from such confinement, shall be punished as follows: with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, with or without fine, if the person ought to have been apprehended for an offence punishable with death; with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, with or without fine, if the offence is punishable with imprisonment for life or imprisonment for a term which may extend to ten years; with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, with or without fine, if the offence is punishable with imprisonment for a term less than ten years.
meaning: Section 221 IPC punishes a public servant who is legally bound to apprehend or detain a person but intentionally omits to do so, allows escape, or aids escape. The punishment varies based on the severity of the offence the person was charged with.
simple_explanation: If a police officer or other public servant who is legally required to arrest or keep someone in custody deliberately lets that person go free or helps them escape, they can be punished. The punishment is higher if the escaped person was accused of a more serious crime.
objective: To ensure accountability of public servants entrusted with powers of arrest and detention, and to prevent deliberate dereliction of duty that undermines the justice system.
legal_significance: This section creates a tiered punishment structure directly linked to the gravity of the offence of the person who escaped or was not apprehended, recognising that the harm caused to public justice is proportional to the seriousness of that underlying offence.
essential_elements: The accused is a public servant.
The public servant is legally bound to apprehend or keep in confinement the person concerned.
The public servant intentionally omits to apprehend, or intentionally suffers the person to escape, or intentionally aids in escape.
The act is intentional, not merely negligent.
The person concerned is charged with or liable to be apprehended for an offence.

mens_rea.description: Intentional omission or deliberate act of allowing or aiding escape — negligence does not attract liability under this section.

normal_case.imprisonment: Up to 2 years (either description) — where underlying offence punishable with less than 10 years

punishment.aggravated_case: Up to 3 years if underlying offence punishable with life imprisonment or up to 10 years; up to 7 years if underlying offence punishable with death

examples.scenario: A police constable, bribed by the family of an accused charged with murder, intentionally unlocks the cell and allows the accused to flee.
examples.result: The constable is liable under Section 221 IPC with imprisonment up to 7 years, as murder is punishable with death.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years
ai_summary: Section 221 IPC holds public servants criminally liable for intentionally failing to arrest or detain a person they are legally bound to apprehend. The punishment escalates in proportion to the gravity of the offence for which the escaped person was charged, reaching up to 7 years where the underlying offence carries the death penalty.

title: Intentional Omission to Apprehend on the Part of Public Servant Bound to Apprehend Person Under Sentence or Lawfully Committed
chapter: Chapter XI - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
bare_act_text: Whoever, being a public servant, legally bound as such public servant to apprehend or to keep in confinement any person under sentence of a Court of Justice for any offence or lawfully committed to custody, intentionally omits to apprehend such person, or intentionally suffers such person to escape or intentionally aids such person in escaping or attempting to escape from such confinement, shall be punished as follows: with imprisonment for life or with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to fourteen years, with or without fine, if the person is under sentence of death; with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, with or without fine, if the person is subject to imprisonment for life or imprisonment for a term of ten years or upwards; with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both, if the person is subject to imprisonment for a term not extending to ten years or was lawfully committed to custody.
meaning: Section 222 IPC punishes a public servant who intentionally omits to apprehend or allows to escape a person who is already under a court sentence or lawfully committed to custody. It is distinct from Section 221 which deals with undertrial persons.
simple_explanation: If a public servant responsible for keeping a convicted prisoner in custody deliberately lets them escape or helps them flee, they face serious punishment — up to life imprisonment if the prisoner was on death row. The punishment depends on the sentence the prisoner was serving.
objective: To protect the finality and enforcement of court sentences by ensuring public servants do not sabotage the execution of judicial orders through deliberate omission or active assistance to convicted persons.
legal_significance: Section 222 is more stringent than Section 221 because it deals with persons already convicted or lawfully committed — making any intentional omission a more direct affront to the authority of courts and the justice system. The maximum punishment of life imprisonment reflects this gravity.
essential_elements: The accused is a public servant.
The public servant is legally bound to apprehend or keep in confinement the person concerned.
The person concerned is under a court sentence or lawfully committed to custody.
The public servant intentionally omits to apprehend, intentionally suffers escape, or intentionally aids escape.
The act is intentional.

mens_rea.description: The act must be intentional — deliberate omission or active assistance in escape. Mere negligence is covered under Section 223.

normal_case.imprisonment: Up to 3 years (either description) — where person is serving less than 10 years or lawfully committed to custody

punishment.aggravated_case: Up to 7 years if sentence is life or 10+ years; up to 14 years or life imprisonment if the person is under sentence of death

examples.scenario: A jail warden, out of sympathy, intentionally leaves the cell door unlocked allowing a convict sentenced to life imprisonment to escape.
examples.result: The warden is liable under Section 222 IPC with imprisonment up to 7 years.

max_punishment: Imprisonment for life or up to 14 years
ai_summary: Section 222 IPC punishes public servants who intentionally allow convicted prisoners or persons lawfully in custody to escape. It carries harsher penalties than Section 221 because it concerns persons already sentenced by a court, with punishment escalating up to life imprisonment when the escapee was under a death sentence.

title: Escape from Confinement or Custody Negligently Suffered by Public Servant
chapter: Chapter XI - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
bare_act_text: Whoever, being a public servant legally bound as such public servant to keep in confinement any person charged with or convicted of any offence or lawfully committed to custody, negligently suffers such person to escape from confinement, shall be punished with simple imprisonment for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.
meaning: Section 223 IPC punishes a public servant whose negligence — as opposed to intentional act — results in a person charged with or convicted of an offence escaping from custody.
simple_explanation: If a police officer or prison official carelessly allows a person in their custody to escape — not deliberately but due to negligence — they can be imprisoned for up to 2 years or fined. This covers situations like failing to properly secure a prisoner.
objective: To impose a duty of care on public servants responsible for custodial functions, ensuring that even negligent conduct that compromises the integrity of custody is punishable.
legal_significance: Section 223 is the negligence counterpart to the intentional offences in Sections 221 and 222. The punishment is limited to simple imprisonment (not rigorous), reflecting the lesser culpability of negligent versus intentional conduct. It applies regardless of the nature of the offence the confined person was charged with.
essential_elements: The accused is a public servant.
The public servant is legally bound to keep the person in confinement.
The person confined is charged with or convicted of an offence, or lawfully committed to custody.
The public servant negligently suffers such person to escape.
The escape is due to negligence, not intentional conduct.

mens_rea.description: Negligence suffices — no intent to allow escape is needed. This distinguishes Section 223 from Sections 221 and 222 which require intentional conduct.

examples.scenario: A police constable escorting an undertrial prisoner falls asleep during transit, allowing the prisoner to run away.
examples.result: The constable is liable under Section 223 IPC for negligently suffering escape, punishable with simple imprisonment up to 2 years.

max_punishment: Simple imprisonment up to 2 years
ai_summary: Section 223 IPC addresses negligent (not intentional) escape of a person from lawful custody due to dereliction of duty by a public servant. The punishment is limited to simple imprisonment up to 2 years, reflecting the lower culpability compared to the intentional offences under Sections 221 and 222.

title: Resistance or Obstruction by a Person to His Lawful Apprehension
chapter: Chapter XI - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
bare_act_text: Whoever intentionally offers any resistance or illegal obstruction to the lawful apprehension of himself for any offence with which he is charged or of which he has been convicted, or escapes or attempts to escape from any custody in which he is lawfully detained for any such offence, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both. Explanation: The punishment in this section is in addition to the punishment for which the person to be apprehended or detained in custody was liable for the offence with which he was charged, or of which he was convicted.
meaning: Section 224 IPC punishes a person who resists, obstructs, or escapes from their own lawful arrest or detention. The punishment under this section is over and above any punishment for the underlying offence.
simple_explanation: If someone tries to resist being legally arrested or escapes from lawful custody for an offence they are charged with or convicted of, they can be punished with up to 2 years of imprisonment or a fine — in addition to whatever punishment they face for their original crime.
objective: To protect the lawful exercise of police powers of arrest and to ensure that persons do not obstruct or undermine the process of justice by resisting or evading lawful apprehension.
legal_significance: The Explanation to this section makes clear that the punishment is cumulative — it does not merge with the punishment for the underlying offence. This additive nature reinforces the seriousness with which the law treats obstruction of lawful arrest.
essential_elements: The accused intentionally offers resistance or illegal obstruction.
The resistance is to their own lawful apprehension.
The apprehension is for an offence with which they are charged or convicted.
Alternatively, they escape or attempt to escape from lawful custody.
The act is intentional.

mens_rea.description: The resistance, obstruction, or escape must be intentional. Accidental or involuntary acts would not attract liability under this section.

examples.scenario: A person charged with theft physically pushes away arresting officers and flees, but is later caught.
examples.result: The person is liable under Section 224 IPC in addition to liability for theft, punishable with up to 2 years imprisonment.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 2 years
ai_summary: Section 224 IPC penalises a person who intentionally resists their own lawful arrest or escapes from lawful custody. The punishment of up to 2 years is explicitly cumulative — added on top of the sentence for the underlying offence — reinforcing that obstruction of the justice process carries independent criminal consequences.

title: Resistance or Obstruction to Lawful Apprehension of Another Person
chapter: Chapter XI - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
bare_act_text: Whoever intentionally offers any resistance or illegal obstruction to the lawful apprehension of any other person for an offence, or rescues or attempts to rescue any other person from any custody in which that person is lawfully detained for an offence, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both; or, if the person to be apprehended or rescued is charged with or liable to be apprehended for an offence punishable with imprisonment for life or imprisonment for a term which may extend to ten years, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, and shall also be liable to fine; or, if the person to be apprehended or rescued is charged with or liable to be apprehended for an offence punishable with death, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine; or, if the person to be apprehended or rescued is under sentence of imprisonment for life or imprisonment for a term of ten years or upwards, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine; or, if the person to be apprehended or rescued is under sentence of death, shall be punished with imprisonment for life or imprisonment of either description for a term not exceeding ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.
meaning: Section 225 IPC punishes a person who obstructs the lawful arrest of another person or rescues or attempts to rescue another person from lawful custody. The punishment escalates based on the gravity of the offence for which the person being rescued was charged or convicted.
simple_explanation: If someone helps another person escape arrest or rescue them from police custody, they will be punished. The punishment increases depending on how serious a crime the rescued person was accused of — ranging from 2 years for minor offences up to life imprisonment if the rescued person was on death row.
objective: To protect the integrity of the arrest and detention process by penalising third parties who interfere with lawful apprehension or custody of offenders.
legal_significance: Section 225 creates a tiered punishment structure mirroring the gravity of the underlying offence of the person rescued, recognising that aiding the escape of a dangerous criminal poses a proportionally greater threat to public safety and justice.
essential_elements: The accused intentionally offers resistance or illegal obstruction.
The obstruction is to the lawful apprehension of another person, or the accused rescues or attempts to rescue another from lawful custody.
The other person is charged with or convicted of an offence.
The act is intentional.
The punishment varies based on the nature of the offence of the person rescued or obstructed.

mens_rea.description: Intentional resistance, obstruction, rescue, or attempted rescue is required. The act must be deliberate.

normal_case.imprisonment: Up to 2 years (either description) — for general cases

punishment.aggravated_case: Up to 3 years if underlying offence punishable with life or 10 years; up to 7 years if punishable with death or sentence of life/10+ years; life imprisonment or up to 10 years if the rescued person is under sentence of death

examples.scenario: B helps A, who is accused of murder and being arrested by police, escape from police custody by providing a vehicle.
examples.result: B is liable under Section 225 IPC with imprisonment up to 7 years and fine, as murder is punishable with death.

max_punishment: Imprisonment for life or up to 10 years (in most aggravated cases up to 7 years)
ai_summary: Section 225 IPC penalises any person who obstructs the lawful arrest of another or rescues them from lawful custody. The punishment is graded in proportion to the seriousness of the offence for which the rescued person was charged or sentenced, reaching up to life imprisonment where the rescued person was on death row.

title: Omission to Apprehend, or Sufferance of Escape, on Part of Public Servant, in Cases Not Otherwise Provided For
chapter: Chapter XI - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
bare_act_text: Whoever, being a public servant legally bound as such public servant to apprehend, or to keep in confinement, any person in any case not provided for in section 221, section 222 or section 223, or in any other law for the time being in force, omits to apprehend that person or suffers him to escape from confinement, shall be punished— (a) if he does so intentionally, with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine or with both; and (b) if he does so negligently, with simple imprisonment for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.
meaning: Section 225A IPC is a residual provision that covers cases of omission to apprehend or sufferance of escape by a public servant which are not specifically covered under Sections 221, 222, or 223 or any other law. It provides for both intentional and negligent variants.
simple_explanation: This is a catch-all provision. If a public servant fails to arrest someone or lets them escape, and the situation is not covered by any other specific law, they can still be punished — up to 3 years if done deliberately, or up to 2 years (simple imprisonment) if done negligently.
objective: To plug gaps in Sections 221–223 by ensuring that no public servant escapes liability for omission to apprehend or sufferance of escape simply because the specific circumstances do not fit neatly within those sections.
legal_significance: Section 225A serves as a residual safety net in the statutory framework, ensuring comprehensive accountability of public servants with custodial duties. Its dual-limb structure — intentional and negligent — mirrors the approach of earlier sections but consolidates residual cases into one provision.
essential_elements: The accused is a public servant.
The public servant is legally bound to apprehend or keep in confinement the person.
The case is not covered by Sections 221, 222, 223 or any other law.
The public servant omits to apprehend or suffers the person to escape.
The act is either intentional (clause a) or negligent (clause b).

mens_rea.description: Both intentional omission and negligent omission attract liability, but the punishment differs — intentional conduct is punished more severely than negligent conduct.

normal_case.imprisonment: Up to 2 years simple imprisonment (if negligent)

punishment.aggravated_case: Up to 3 years (either description) with or without fine, if done intentionally

examples.scenario: A public servant bound to apprehend a person detained under a special preventive detention law intentionally omits to do so, in a situation not covered by Sections 221–223.
examples.result: The public servant is liable under Section 225A(a) with imprisonment up to 3 years or fine or both.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 years (intentional); simple imprisonment up to 2 years (negligent)
ai_summary: Section 225A IPC is a residual provision penalising public servants who fail to apprehend or allow persons to escape in cases not covered by Sections 221–223 or any other law. It separately addresses intentional omission (up to 3 years) and negligent omission (up to 2 years simple imprisonment), ensuring no gap in accountability for custodial dereliction.

title: Resistance or Obstruction to Lawful Apprehension, or Escape or Rescue in Cases Not Otherwise Provided For
chapter: Chapter XI - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
bare_act_text: Whoever, in any case not provided for in section 224 or section 225 or in any other law for the time being in force, intentionally offers any resistance or illegal obstruction to the lawful apprehension of himself or of any other person, or escapes or attempts to escape from any custody in which he is lawfully detained, or rescues or attempts to rescue any other person from any custody in which that person is lawfully detained, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine, or with both.
meaning: Section 225B IPC is a residual provision penalising resistance to lawful apprehension, escape from lawful custody, or rescue of another from lawful custody in cases not specifically covered by Sections 224 or 225 or any other law.
simple_explanation: This is a catch-all provision. If someone resists their own arrest, escapes from lawful custody, or helps another person escape — and the specific situation is not covered by any other law — they can be punished with up to 6 months imprisonment or a fine.
objective: To ensure that no gap exists in the law by covering residual cases of obstruction to lawful apprehension or escape from custody that fall outside the scope of Sections 224 and 225.
legal_significance: Section 225B serves as a safety net complementing Sections 224 and 225, ensuring comprehensive coverage of obstruction and escape offences. The relatively lighter punishment of 6 months reflects the residual and less-defined nature of the cases it covers.
essential_elements: The act is not covered by Section 224, Section 225, or any other law.

The accused intentionally resists or obstructs their own or another's lawful apprehension, or escapes or attempts to escape from lawful custody, or rescues or attempts to rescue another from lawful custody.
The apprehension or custody is lawful.
The act is intentional.

mens_rea.description: The resistance, obstruction, escape, or rescue must be intentional. Negligent or accidental acts do not attract liability under this section.

examples.scenario: A person lawfully detained under a preventive detention order not covered by any specific penal provision escapes from custody.
examples.result: The person is liable under Section 225B IPC with imprisonment up to 6 months or fine or both.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 6 months
ai_summary: Section 225B IPC is a residual catch-all provision penalising intentional resistance to lawful apprehension, escape from lawful custody, or rescue of another from custody in cases not covered by Sections 224, 225, or any other law. The maximum punishment of 6 months reflects its subsidiary role in the statutory framework.

title: Unlawful Return from Transportation
chapter: Chapter XI - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
bare_act_text: Repealed by the Code of Criminal Procedure (Amendment) Act, 1955 (26 of 1955), s. 117 and the Schedule, with effect from 1-1-1956.
meaning: Section 226 IPC, which dealt with unlawful return from transportation (penal transportation being a form of punishment), was repealed in 1956 following the abolition of transportation as a form of punishment in India.
simple_explanation: This section no longer exists in law. It used to punish persons who unlawfully returned from transportation (exile to a penal colony), but since transportation as a punishment was abolished, this section was repealed in 1956.
objective: Originally intended to enforce sentences of transportation by penalising those who returned unlawfully before serving their full term.
legal_significance: The repeal of this section reflects the evolution of Indian penal law with the abolition of transportation as a form of punishment. It has no operative legal effect today.

mens_rea.description: Not applicable — section has been repealed.

max_punishment: Repealed — not applicable
ai_summary: Section 226 IPC dealing with unlawful return from transportation was repealed by the Code of Criminal Procedure (Amendment) Act, 1955 with effect from 1 January 1956, following the abolition of transportation as a form of criminal punishment in India.

title: Violation of Condition of Remission of Punishment
chapter: Chapter XI - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
bare_act_text: Whoever, having accepted any conditional remission of punishment, knowingly violates any condition on which such remission was granted, shall be punished with the punishment to which he was originally sentenced, if he has already suffered no part of that punishment, and if he has suffered any part of that punishment, then with so much of that punishment as he has not already suffered.
meaning: Section 227 IPC punishes a person who knowingly breaches any condition attached to a conditional remission of their sentence. On violation, they must serve the remaining portion of their original sentence.
simple_explanation: If a prisoner is released early with conditions (like not leaving a certain area or not committing further offences) and they knowingly break those conditions, they will be sent back to serve the remaining part of their original sentence.
objective: To ensure that conditional remissions of sentence — a discretionary executive act — are respected and not abused, maintaining the deterrent effect of the original sentence.
legal_significance: Section 227 enforces compliance with conditional remissions granted under the executive power to remit sentences. It ensures that the benefit of early release does not become a loophole by making the violation of conditions a cognisable basis for reimposition of the original sentence.
essential_elements: The accused was serving a sentence of punishment.
A conditional remission of that punishment was granted and accepted by the accused.
The accused knowingly violates a condition of the remission.
The violation is knowing — not accidental or unknowing.

mens_rea.description: The violation must be knowing — the accused must be aware of the condition and deliberately breach it.

normal_case.imprisonment: Remainder of the original sentence not yet served (or full original sentence if none served)

examples.scenario: A convict is conditionally released on the condition that he does not leave the district. He knowingly travels to another state.
examples.result: He is liable under Section 227 IPC and must serve the remaining portion of his original sentence.

max_punishment: Remainder of original sentence
ai_summary: Section 227 IPC penalises knowing violation of conditions attached to a conditional remission of sentence by reimposing the unserved portion of the original sentence. It reinforces the integrity of the conditional release system by ensuring that beneficiaries cannot breach conditions without consequence.

title: Intentional Insult or Interruption to Public Servant Sitting in Judicial Proceeding
chapter: Chapter XI - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
