bare_act_text: Whoever intentionally offers any insult, or causes any interruption to any public servant, while such public servant is sitting in any stage of a judicial proceeding, shall be punished with simple imprisonment for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine which may extend to one thousand rupees, or with both.
meaning: Section 228 IPC punishes intentional insult or interruption to a public servant — such as a judge or magistrate — while they are conducting any stage of a judicial proceeding.
simple_explanation: If someone deliberately insults a judge or magistrate or disrupts court proceedings while they are in session, they can be imprisoned for up to 6 months or fined up to ₹1,000. This provision protects the dignity and smooth functioning of courts.
objective: To protect the dignity of judicial proceedings and ensure that public servants conducting judicial functions can discharge their duties without disruption, insult, or intimidation.
legal_significance: Section 228 is a codified provision protecting judicial dignity, supplementing the inherent power of courts to punish for contempt. It is specifically limited to intentional acts during an ongoing judicial proceeding, making the context and intent essential to the offence.
essential_elements: The accused intentionally offers insult or causes interruption.
The insult or interruption is directed at a public servant.
The public servant is sitting in a judicial proceeding at the time.
The act occurs at any stage of the judicial proceeding.
The act is intentional.

mens_rea.description: The insult or interruption must be intentional. Unintentional disturbances do not attract liability under this section.

examples.scenario: During a hearing, an accused person deliberately shouts abuses at the presiding magistrate and refuses to sit down, disrupting the proceedings.
examples.result: The accused is liable under Section 228 IPC with simple imprisonment up to 6 months or fine up to ₹1,000 or both.

max_punishment: Simple imprisonment up to 6 months or fine up to ₹1,000
ai_summary: Section 228 IPC penalises intentional insult or disruption directed at a public servant during any stage of a judicial proceeding. It protects the dignity and orderly conduct of courts, with punishment limited to simple imprisonment up to 6 months or a fine of up to ₹1,000.

title: Disclosure of Identity of the Victim of Certain Offences
chapter: Chapter XI - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
section_type: Offence Against Public Justice / Offence Protecting Victim Privacy
bare_act_text: Whoever prints or publishes the name or any matter which may make known the identity of any person against whom an offence under section 376, section 376A, section 376AB, section 376B, section 376C, section 376D, section 376DA, section 376DB or section 376E is alleged or found to have been committed shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years and shall also be liable to fine. Exceptions apply where printing or publication is authorised by the investigating officer in good faith, by the victim, or by the next of kin of a deceased, minor or unsound-mind victim (through a recognised welfare institution). Whoever prints or publishes any matter in relation to proceedings before a court for such offences without prior court permission shall also be punished with imprisonment up to two years and fine. The printing or publication of judgments of High Courts or the Supreme Court does not constitute an offence under this section.
meaning: Section 228A IPC prohibits the printing or publication of the name or identity of victims of sexual offences under Sections 376 to 376E, protecting their privacy and dignity. It also prohibits publication of court proceedings related to such offences without court permission.
simple_explanation: No one can publish the name or any details that could identify a rape victim or victim of related sexual offences. Doing so can lead to imprisonment up to 2 years and a fine. Publishing details of court proceedings in such cases without the court's permission is also an offence. Exceptions exist for police investigations, victim's own consent, or authorisation by next of kin.
objective: To protect the privacy, dignity, and safety of victims of sexual offences by preventing their identification in media or public discourse, and to encourage reporting of such offences without fear of social stigma.
legal_significance: Section 228A reflects a victim-centric approach in Indian criminal law, recognising that the harm caused by public identification of sexual assault victims can be severe and lasting. It balances freedom of the press against the fundamental right to dignity and privacy of victims.
essential_elements: The accused prints or publishes the name or identity-revealing information of the victim.
The victim is a person against whom an offence under Sections 376, 376A, 376AB, 376B, 376C, 376D, 376DA, 376DB, or 376E is alleged or found to have been committed.
No valid exception applies (investigating officer's authorisation, victim's consent, or next-of-kin authorisation).
Alternatively, the accused publishes matter related to court proceedings in such cases without prior court permission.

mens_rea.description: The section does not expressly require intent — the act of publishing itself attracts liability unless a statutory exception applies. However, good faith by the investigating officer is a recognised exception.

examples.scenario: A news channel broadcasts the full name and face of a rape victim in its prime-time news report without her consent.
examples.result: The channel and responsible persons are liable under Section 228A IPC with imprisonment up to 2 years and fine.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 2 years and fine
ai_summary: Section 228A IPC protects the identity and privacy of victims of sexual offences under Sections 376–376E by criminalising their identification in print or media and prohibiting publication of related court proceedings without permission. The section balances press freedom with victim dignity, carrying a maximum punishment of 2 years imprisonment and fine.

title: Personation of a Juror or Assessor
chapter: Chapter XI - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
bare_act_text: Whoever, by personation or otherwise, shall intentionally cause, or knowingly suffer himself to be returned, empanelled or sworn as a juryman or assessor in any case in which he knows that he is not entitled by law to be so returned, empanelled or sworn, or knowing himself to have been so returned, empanelled or sworn contrary to law, shall voluntarily serve on such jury or as such assessor, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.
meaning: Section 229 IPC punishes a person who fraudulently causes themselves to be included as a juror or assessor in a case when they know they are not legally entitled to serve in that capacity.
simple_explanation: If someone impersonates another person or uses any other means to get themselves illegally included as a juror or court assessor — knowing they are not eligible — they can be imprisoned for up to 2 years or fined. The same applies if they knowingly continue to serve as a juror after being illegally empanelled.
objective: To protect the integrity of the jury and assessor system in judicial proceedings by preventing fraudulent infiltration of persons not legally entitled to serve in such capacities.
legal_significance: Though the jury system is largely obsolete in India (except in certain cases), Section 229 retains significance in protecting the integrity of assessor-based proceedings. It addresses both the act of fraudulently getting empanelled and the knowing continuation of unlawful service.
essential_elements: The accused causes themselves to be returned, empanelled, or sworn as a juror or assessor.
This is done by personation or any other means.
The accused knows they are not legally entitled to serve as a juror or assessor in that case.
Alternatively, the accused knowingly continues to serve as a juror or assessor after being unlawfully empanelled.
The act is intentional or knowing.

mens_rea.description: Knowledge that one is not entitled to serve as a juror or assessor is essential. The act must be intentional or knowing.

examples.scenario: A person impersonates an eligible juror and gets himself empanelled in a criminal trial, knowing he is disqualified from serving.
examples.result: The person is liable under Section 229 IPC with imprisonment up to 2 years or fine or both.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 2 years
ai_summary: Section 229 IPC penalises fraudulent empanelment as a juror or assessor by a person who knows they are not legally entitled to serve in that capacity. It protects the integrity of judicial processes dependent on jurors and assessors, carrying a maximum punishment of 2 years imprisonment or fine.

title: Failure by Person Released on Bail or Bond to Appear in Court
chapter: Chapter XI - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
bare_act_text: Whoever, having been charged with an offence and released on bail or on bond without sureties, fails without sufficient cause (the burden of proving which shall lie upon him), to appear in court in accordance with the terms of the bail or bond, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to one year, or with fine, or with both. Explanation: The punishment under this section is (a) in addition to the punishment to which the offender would be liable on a conviction for the offence with which he has been charged; and (b) without prejudice to the power of the court to order forfeiture of the bond.
meaning: Section 229A IPC punishes a person released on bail or personal bond who, without sufficient cause, fails to appear in court as required by the terms of their bail or bond. The burden of proving sufficient cause lies on the accused.
simple_explanation: If someone gets bail or is released on a personal bond and then deliberately fails to appear in court on the required date without a good reason, they can be imprisoned for up to 1 year or fined — in addition to any punishment for their original crime. They must prove they had a valid reason for not appearing.
objective: To enforce compliance with bail conditions and ensure that persons released on bail or personal bond honour their obligation to appear before court, thereby upholding the integrity of the bail system.
legal_significance: Section 229A places the burden of proof on the accused to establish sufficient cause for non-appearance — a reversal of the usual presumption of innocence in this limited context. The punishment is explicitly cumulative, reinforcing that bail is a legal obligation and not merely a procedural courtesy.
essential_elements: The accused has been charged with an offence.
The accused was released on bail or on bond without sureties.
The accused fails to appear in court in accordance with the terms of bail or bond.
The failure is without sufficient cause.
The burden of proving sufficient cause lies on the accused.

mens_rea.description: The section does not require proof of intent to abscond. Mere failure to appear without sufficient cause is sufficient. The accused bears the burden of proving justification.

examples.scenario: A accused person released on personal bond in a cheating case without surety does not appear on the next hearing date without any explanation.
examples.result: The accused is liable under Section 229A IPC with imprisonment up to 1 year or fine or both, in addition to liability for the cheating offence.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 1 year
ai_summary: Section 229A IPC penalises persons released on bail or personal bond who fail to appear in court without sufficient cause. It reverses the burden of proof onto the accused and prescribes cumulative punishment of up to 1 year imprisonment, reinforcing that bail is a legal obligation whose breach carries independent criminal consequences.

title: Coin Defined
chapter: Chapter XII - Of Offences Relating to Coin and Government Stamps
bare_act_text: Coin is metal used for the time being as money, and stamped and issued by the authority of some State or Sovereign Power in order to be so used. Indian coin is metal stamped and issued by the authority of the Government of India in order to be used as money; and metal which has been so stamped and issued shall continue to be Indian coin for the purposes of this Chapter, notwithstanding that it may have ceased to be used as money. Illustrations: (a) Cowries are not coin. (b) Lumps of unstamped copper, though used as money, are not coin. (c) Medals are not coin, inasmuch as they are not intended to be used as money. (d) The coin denominated as the Company's rupee is Indian coin. (e) The Farukhabad rupee, which was formerly used as money under the authority of the Government of India, is Indian coin although it is no longer so used.
meaning: Section 230 IPC defines 'coin' as metal stamped and issued by a State or Sovereign Power for use as money, and 'Indian coin' as metal stamped and issued by the Government of India for monetary use. A coin retains its character as Indian coin even after it ceases to be in active circulation.
simple_explanation: This section defines what counts as a 'coin' and 'Indian coin' under the IPC for the purpose of offences in Chapter XII. Only metal stamped and officially issued by a government qualifies. Cowries, medals, and unstamped metal — even if used as money — are not coins under this definition.
objective: To provide a precise legal definition of 'coin' and 'Indian coin' as the foundation for all offences relating to counterfeiting and coin-related fraud in Chapter XII.
legal_significance: The definition is foundational — all counterfeiting and coin-related offences in Chapter XII depend on whether the object in question qualifies as a 'coin' or 'Indian coin' under this section. The inclusion of formerly-circulated Indian coins ensures that older currency remains protected even after demonetisation.
essential_elements: The object must be metal.
It must be stamped and issued by the authority of a State or Sovereign Power (for coin) or the Government of India (for Indian coin).
It must be intended to be used as money.
Indian coin retains its character even after it ceases to be used as money.

mens_rea.description: This is a definitional section — no mens rea is applicable.

examples.scenario: A person is found with counterfeit medals resembling government coins.
examples.result: Medals are not 'coin' under Section 230 as they are not intended to be used as money — offences under Chapter XII would not apply.

max_punishment: Not applicable — definitional section
ai_summary: Section 230 IPC defines 'coin' and 'Indian coin' for the purposes of Chapter XII offences. A coin must be metal stamped and issued by state authority for monetary use. Indian coin retains its legal character even after it is no longer in circulation, ensuring continued protection of demonetised currency from counterfeiting offences.

title: Counterfeiting Coin
chapter: Chapter XII - Of Offences Relating to Coin and Government Stamps
bare_act_text: Whoever counterfeits or knowingly performs any part of the process of counterfeiting coin, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine. Explanation: A person commits this offence who intending to practise deception, or knowing it to be likely that deception will thereby be practised, causes a genuine coin to appear like a different coin.
meaning: Section 231 IPC punishes the counterfeiting of any coin (not necessarily Indian coin) or knowingly participating in any part of the counterfeiting process. Even causing a genuine coin to resemble a different coin with deceptive intent is covered.
simple_explanation: Anyone who makes fake coins or participates in any step of making fake coins — even just one part of the process — can be imprisoned for up to 7 years and fined. This also covers altering a real coin to make it look like a more valuable or different coin with intent to deceive.
objective: To protect the monetary system and public trust in coinage by criminalising the manufacture of counterfeit coins of any country or sovereign authority.
legal_significance: Section 231 covers foreign coins as well as Indian coins, though the latter attracts more severe punishment under Section 232. The Explanation expands the scope by including manipulation of genuine coins to resemble different coins — addressing a specific form of monetary fraud.
essential_elements: The accused counterfeits a coin or knowingly performs any part of the counterfeiting process.
The coin counterfeited may be any coin — not restricted to Indian coin.
Alternatively, the accused causes a genuine coin to appear like a different coin with intent to deceive or knowing deception is likely.
Knowledge of participation in the counterfeiting process is required.

mens_rea.description: Knowledge that one is performing part of the counterfeiting process is required. For the Explanation, intent to deceive or knowledge that deception is likely must be established.

examples.scenario: A person operates a machine that stamps fake foreign currency coins, knowingly participating in the counterfeiting operation.
examples.result: The person is liable under Section 231 IPC with imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine
ai_summary: Section 231 IPC criminalises the counterfeiting of any coin or knowing participation in any part of the counterfeiting process. It also covers manipulation of genuine coins to resemble different ones with deceptive intent. The maximum punishment is 7 years imprisonment, with higher penalties applying specifically to Indian coin counterfeiting under Section 232.

title: Counterfeiting Indian Coin
chapter: Chapter XII - Of Offences Relating to Coin and Government Stamps
bare_act_text: Whoever counterfeits, or knowingly performs any part of the process of counterfeiting Indian coin, shall be punished with imprisonment for life, or with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.
meaning: Section 232 IPC is the aggravated form of Section 231, specifically penalising the counterfeiting of Indian coin. It carries a significantly higher punishment — up to life imprisonment — reflecting the greater threat to national monetary security.
simple_explanation: Making fake Indian government coins or participating in any part of the process of making them is a very serious offence. The punishment can go up to life imprisonment along with a fine, because it directly attacks the integrity of India's official currency.
objective: To protect the integrity of India's national coinage and monetary system by imposing the severest penalties on those who counterfeit Indian coin, treating it as an offence against the state.
legal_significance: Section 232 is one of the most serious offences in Chapter XII. The possibility of life imprisonment places counterfeiting Indian coin in the same gravity bracket as other offences against the state, recognising that currency counterfeiting is a threat to national economic security.
essential_elements: The accused counterfeits Indian coin or knowingly performs any part of the process of counterfeiting Indian coin.
The coin counterfeited must qualify as 'Indian coin' as defined under Section 230.
Knowledge of participation in the counterfeiting process is required.

mens_rea.description: Knowledge that one is performing part of the process of counterfeiting Indian coin is essential.

examples.scenario: A person operates an illegal mint producing fake Indian rupee coins and distributes them in the market.
examples.result: The person is liable under Section 232 IPC with punishment up to life imprisonment and fine.

max_punishment: Imprisonment for life and fine
ai_summary: Section 232 IPC is the aggravated counterpart to Section 231, specifically targeting counterfeiting of Indian coin. It carries the harshest punishment in Chapter XII — up to life imprisonment and fine — reflecting the gravity of attacks on India's national monetary system.

title: Making or Selling Instrument for Counterfeiting Coin
chapter: Chapter XII - Of Offences Relating to Coin and Government Stamps
bare_act_text: Whoever makes or mends, or performs any part of the process of making or mending, or buys, sells or disposes of, any die or instrument, for the purpose of being used, or knowing or having reason to believe that it is intended to be used, for the purpose of counterfeiting coin, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, and shall also be liable to fine.
meaning: Section 233 IPC punishes the making, mending, buying, selling, or disposing of any die or instrument intended to be used for counterfeiting coin. It targets the preparatory stage — the manufacture or trade of tools used in counterfeiting.
simple_explanation: Anyone who makes, repairs, buys, sells, or hands over any tool or die that is meant to be used for making fake coins — even if no coins have been made yet — can be imprisoned for up to 3 years and fined. It covers those who supply the tools for counterfeiting.
objective: To target the supply chain of counterfeiting by criminalising the creation and trade of instruments used in the counterfeiting process, before actual counterfeiting occurs.
legal_significance: Section 233 addresses the preparatory stage of counterfeiting, recognising that punishing only the final act is insufficient. By criminalising the tools trade, it disrupts counterfeiting networks at an earlier stage. Section 234 provides enhanced punishment when the instruments are intended for Indian coin.
essential_elements: The accused makes, mends, buys, sells, or disposes of a die or instrument.
The die or instrument is for the purpose of counterfeiting coin, or the accused knows or has reason to believe it is intended for such purpose.
The coin need not be Indian coin — any coin suffices for this section.

mens_rea.description: The accused must act for the purpose of counterfeiting coin, or must know or have reason to believe the instrument is intended for counterfeiting. Constructive knowledge ('reason to believe') suffices.

examples.scenario: A metalworker sells a specially crafted die to a known counterfeiter, having reason to believe it will be used to stamp fake coins.
examples.result: The metalworker is liable under Section 233 IPC with imprisonment up to 3 years and fine.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 years and fine
ai_summary: Section 233 IPC criminalises the manufacture, trade, and disposal of dies or instruments intended for counterfeiting any coin. It targets the preparatory supply chain of counterfeiting operations, carrying a maximum punishment of 3 years imprisonment and fine, with a higher penalty applicable under Section 234 when the instruments are for Indian coin.

title: Making or Selling Instrument for Counterfeiting Indian Coin
chapter: Chapter XII - Of Offences Relating to Coin and Government Stamps
bare_act_text: Whoever makes or mends, or performs any part of the process of making or mending or buys, sells or disposes of, any die or instrument, for the purpose of being used, or knowing or having reason to believe that it is intended to be used, for the purpose of counterfeiting Indian coin, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.
meaning: Section 234 IPC is the aggravated form of Section 233, applying when the die or instrument is intended specifically for counterfeiting Indian coin. The maximum punishment is doubled to 7 years.
simple_explanation: If someone makes, repairs, buys, sells, or hands over a tool or die specifically meant to produce fake Indian government coins, they can be imprisoned for up to 7 years and fined. This is more serious than Section 233 because it targets Indian currency specifically.
objective: To impose enhanced penalties on those who facilitate the counterfeiting of Indian coin by manufacturing or trading the instruments used, recognising the special threat such activity poses to national monetary integrity.
legal_significance: Section 234 mirrors the escalation from Section 231 to Section 232 — the same conduct attracts heavier punishment when Indian coin is the target. The 7-year maximum reflects the seriousness of attacks on India's official currency infrastructure.
essential_elements: The accused makes, mends, buys, sells, or disposes of a die or instrument.
The die or instrument is intended to be used for counterfeiting Indian coin.
The accused acts for that purpose, or knows or has reason to believe the instrument is so intended.
The coin targeted must be Indian coin as defined under Section 230.

mens_rea.description: The accused must act for the purpose of counterfeiting Indian coin, or must know or have reason to believe the instrument is so intended. Constructive knowledge suffices.

examples.scenario: A toolmaker fabricates a custom die bearing the design of a 10-rupee coin and sells it knowing it will be used for counterfeiting.
examples.result: The toolmaker is liable under Section 234 IPC with imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine
ai_summary: Section 234 IPC is the aggravated form of Section 233, targeting the manufacture or trade of dies and instruments specifically intended for counterfeiting Indian coin. The maximum punishment of 7 years — more than double that of Section 233 — reflects the heightened threat to India's national monetary system.

title: Possession of Instrument or Material for the Purpose of Using the Same for Counterfeiting Coin
chapter: Chapter XII - Of Offences Relating to Coin and Government Stamps
bare_act_text: Whoever is in possession of any instrument or material, for the purpose of using the same for counterfeiting coin, or knowing or having reason to believe that the same is intended to be used for that purpose, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, and shall also be liable to fine; and if the coin to be counterfeited is Indian coin, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.
meaning: Section 235 IPC punishes mere possession of any instrument or material intended for use in counterfeiting coin. If the intended coin is Indian coin, the punishment is significantly enhanced to up to 10 years.
simple_explanation: Simply possessing tools or materials meant for making fake coins is a crime. If the fake coins intended to be made are Indian rupees or other Indian government coins, the punishment goes up to 10 years imprisonment. Even if you just have the tools — without having made any fake coins yet — you can be prosecuted.
objective: To criminalise possession of counterfeiting instruments as a preventive measure, enabling law enforcement to act before actual counterfeiting occurs.
legal_significance: Section 235 extends the reach of the law to the earliest preparatory stage — mere possession. Combined with Sections 233 and 234 (which cover making and trading of instruments), it creates a comprehensive net covering the entire counterfeiting supply chain from possession to production.
essential_elements: The accused is in possession of an instrument or material.
The possession is for the purpose of counterfeiting coin, or the accused knows or has reason to believe it is so intended.
If the coin to be counterfeited is Indian coin, the enhanced punishment applies.

mens_rea.description: The possession must be for the purpose of counterfeiting, or the accused must know or have reason to believe the instrument is intended for counterfeiting. Constructive knowledge suffices.

normal_case.imprisonment: Up to 3 years (either description) — for non-Indian coin

punishment.aggravated_case: Up to 10 years (either description) and fine — if the coin to be counterfeited is Indian coin

examples.scenario: Police raid a suspect's home and recover dies, moulds, and metal blanks intended for producing counterfeit 2-rupee coins.
examples.result: The suspect is liable under Section 235 IPC (Indian coin limb) with imprisonment up to 10 years and fine.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 10 years and fine (Indian coin); up to 3 years (other coin)
ai_summary: Section 235 IPC criminalises possession of instruments or materials intended for counterfeiting coin at the earliest preparatory stage. The punishment doubles from 3 years to 10 years when Indian coin is the target, reflecting the special importance of protecting national currency.

title: Abetting in India the Counterfeiting Out of India of Coin
chapter: Chapter XII - Of Offences Relating to Coin and Government Stamps
bare_act_text: Whoever, being within India abets the counterfeiting of coin out of India shall be punished in the same manner as if he abetted the counterfeiting of such coin within India.
meaning: Section 236 IPC ensures that a person in India who abets counterfeiting of coin in a foreign country is punished as if the counterfeiting had taken place within India, preventing India from being used as a base for planning or organising foreign coin counterfeiting.
simple_explanation: If someone sitting in India helps plan or organise the counterfeiting of coins in another country, they will be punished just as if the fake coins were being made in India. The law does not allow India to be used as a safe base for international coin counterfeiting operations.
objective: To prevent India from being used as a planning or coordination base for international counterfeiting operations, and to assert extraterritorial jurisdiction over abetment of coin counterfeiting.
legal_significance: Section 236 is an extraterritoriality provision that closes a potential jurisdictional loophole — without it, a person could abet overseas counterfeiting from India with impunity. It reinforces India's international obligations to suppress currency counterfeiting.
essential_elements: The accused is present within India.
The accused abets the counterfeiting of coin.
The actual counterfeiting takes place outside India.
The punishment is equivalent to abetting counterfeiting within India.

mens_rea.description: The accused must intentionally abet the counterfeiting — the standard mens rea for abetment under Sections 107–109 IPC applies.

normal_case.imprisonment: Same as abetting counterfeiting within India — up to 7 years (Section 231) or life imprisonment (Section 232) depending on whether the coin is Indian coin

examples.scenario: A person in Mumbai provides financial support and technical instructions to a counterfeiting ring operating in Nepal, which produces fake Indian rupee coins.
examples.result: The person is liable under Section 236 read with Section 232 IPC with punishment up to life imprisonment and fine.

max_punishment: Same as the counterfeiting offence abetted — up to life imprisonment if Indian coin
ai_summary: Section 236 IPC extends India's jurisdiction over abetment of coin counterfeiting committed abroad by persons present in India. It prevents India from being used as a safe haven for planning international counterfeiting operations by treating such abetment on par with abetting domestic counterfeiting.

title: Import or Export of Counterfeit Coin
chapter: Chapter XII - Of Offences Relating to Coin and Government Stamps
bare_act_text: Whoever imports into India, or exports therefrom, any counterfeit coin, knowing or having reason to believe that the same is counterfeit, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, and shall also be liable to fine.
meaning: Section 237 IPC punishes the import or export of counterfeit coin — of any denomination or country — into or from India, when the accused knows or has reason to believe the coin is counterfeit.
simple_explanation: Bringing fake coins into India or taking them out of India, knowing they are fake, is a crime punishable by up to 3 years imprisonment and a fine. This covers fake coins of any country, not just Indian currency.
objective: To prevent India from becoming a conduit for international counterfeit coin trafficking by criminalising both import and export of counterfeit coins.
legal_significance: Section 237 targets the cross-border movement of counterfeit coins, complementing the domestic counterfeiting offences in Sections 231–235. Import of counterfeit Indian coin attracts enhanced punishment under Section 238.
essential_elements: The accused imports into India or exports from India any counterfeit coin.
The accused knows or has reason to believe the coin is counterfeit.
The coin may be of any country — not restricted to Indian coin.

mens_rea.description: Knowledge or reason to believe that the coin is counterfeit is required. Constructive knowledge suffices.

examples.scenario: A traveller is caught at a customs checkpoint attempting to bring in a consignment of counterfeit foreign currency coins, knowing they are fake.
examples.result: The traveller is liable under Section 237 IPC with imprisonment up to 3 years and fine.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 years and fine
ai_summary: Section 237 IPC criminalises the import or export of counterfeit coins into or from India with knowledge or reason to believe they are counterfeit. It targets cross-border counterfeit coin trafficking for coins of any country, with enhanced punishment under Section 238 applying specifically to counterfeit Indian coin.

title: Import or Export of Counterfeits of the Indian Coin
chapter: Chapter XII - Of Offences Relating to Coin and Government Stamps
bare_act_text: Whoever imports into India, or exports therefrom, any counterfeit coin, which he knows or has reason to believe to be a counterfeit of Indian coin, shall be punished with imprisonment for life, or with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.
meaning: Section 238 IPC is the aggravated form of Section 237, applying when the counterfeit coin imported or exported is specifically a counterfeit of Indian coin. The punishment escalates dramatically to up to life imprisonment.
simple_explanation: Smuggling fake Indian rupee coins into or out of India can lead to life imprisonment and a fine. This is far more serious than importing fake foreign coins because it directly attacks India's national currency.
objective: To severely deter the cross-border trafficking of counterfeit Indian currency coins, protecting the integrity of India's monetary system from international threats.
legal_significance: The dramatic escalation from 3 years (Section 237) to life imprisonment (Section 238) for the same act when Indian coin is involved reflects the state's heightened concern for national monetary security. It places counterfeit Indian coin trafficking among the most severely punished offences in Chapter XII.
essential_elements: The accused imports into India or exports from India a counterfeit coin.
The coin is a counterfeit of Indian coin as defined under Section 230.
The accused knows or has reason to believe the coin is a counterfeit of Indian coin.

mens_rea.description: Knowledge or reason to believe that the coin is a counterfeit of Indian coin is required. Constructive knowledge suffices.

examples.scenario: A person is arrested at an airport trying to smuggle a bag of counterfeit Indian 10-rupee coins from a neighbouring country into India.
examples.result: The person is liable under Section 238 IPC with punishment up to life imprisonment and fine.

max_punishment: Imprisonment for life and fine
ai_summary: Section 238 IPC is the aggravated counterpart to Section 237, targeting cross-border trafficking of counterfeit Indian coin specifically. The maximum punishment of life imprisonment reflects the severity with which Indian law treats threats to national currency integrity from international counterfeiting networks.

title: Delivery of Coin Possessed with Knowledge That It Is Counterfeit
chapter: Chapter XII - Of Offences Relating to Coin and Government Stamps
bare_act_text: Whoever, having any counterfeit coin, which at the time when he became possessed of it, he knew to be counterfeit, fraudulently or with intent that fraud may be committed, delivers the same to any person or attempts to induce any person to receive it, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to five years, and shall also be liable to fine.
meaning: Section 239 IPC punishes a person who, knowing at the time of acquiring a counterfeit coin that it is fake, later delivers it to someone or tries to get someone to accept it, with fraudulent intent.
simple_explanation: If someone obtains a fake coin knowing it is fake, and then tries to pass it off to someone else or persuade someone to accept it as genuine, they can be imprisoned for up to 5 years and fined. The key is that they knew it was fake when they first got it.
objective: To penalise the circulation of counterfeit coins by those who knowingly acquire and then fraudulently distribute them, targeting the 'uttering' stage of counterfeiting operations.
legal_significance: Section 239 targets a distinct link in the counterfeiting chain — the knowing acquirer who later utters the counterfeit coin. It is distinguished from Section 241 which applies when the person did not know of the counterfeit nature at the time of acquisition but discovers it later.
essential_elements: The accused possesses a counterfeit coin.
At the time of acquiring possession, the accused knew the coin was counterfeit.
The accused delivers the coin to another person or attempts to induce another to receive it.
The delivery or inducement is done fraudulently or with intent that fraud may be committed.

mens_rea.description: Knowledge of the counterfeit nature at the time of acquisition and fraudulent intent at the time of delivery are both required.

examples.scenario: B buys counterfeit rupees from a coiner knowing they are fake and then passes them on to C, another utterer.
examples.result: B is liable under Section 239 IPC (or Section 240 if Indian coin) with imprisonment up to 5 years and fine.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 5 years and fine
ai_summary: Section 239 IPC penalises persons who knowingly acquire counterfeit coins and then fraudulently deliver or attempt to circulate them. It targets the 'uttering' link in counterfeiting operations, carrying up to 5 years imprisonment, with enhanced punishment under Section 240 when Indian coin is involved.

title: Delivery of Indian Coin Possessed with Knowledge That It Is Counterfeit
chapter: Chapter XII - Of Offences Relating to Coin and Government Stamps
bare_act_text: Whoever, having any counterfeit coin which is a counterfeit of Indian coin, and which, at the time when he became possessed of it, he knew to be a counterfeit of Indian coin, fraudulently or with intent that fraud may be committed, delivers the same to any person, or attempts to induce any person to receive it, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.
meaning: Section 240 IPC is the aggravated form of Section 239, applying specifically when the counterfeit coin knowingly acquired and fraudulently delivered is a counterfeit of Indian coin. The punishment is doubled to 10 years.
simple_explanation: If someone acquires fake Indian rupee coins knowing they are fake and then tries to pass them off to others, they face up to 10 years imprisonment and a fine — twice the punishment for doing the same with foreign counterfeit coins under Section 239.
objective: To impose enhanced penalties for the circulation of counterfeit Indian coin, recognising the special harm caused to India's monetary system and public trust by the knowing distribution of fake Indian currency.
legal_significance: Section 240 mirrors the escalation pattern throughout Chapter XII — the same conduct directed at Indian coin always attracts a higher penalty. The 10-year maximum for knowing uttering of counterfeit Indian coin reflects the gravity of actively circulating fake national currency.
essential_elements: The accused possesses a counterfeit coin that is a counterfeit of Indian coin.
At the time of acquiring possession, the accused knew it was a counterfeit of Indian coin.
The accused delivers the coin to another or attempts to induce another to receive it.
The act is done fraudulently or with intent that fraud may be committed.

mens_rea.description: Knowledge of the counterfeit Indian coin nature at the time of acquisition and fraudulent intent at delivery are both essential.

examples.scenario: A person knowingly purchases counterfeit 500-rupee coins from a counterfeiter and then attempts to use them at a market.
examples.result: The person is liable under Section 240 IPC with imprisonment up to 10 years and fine.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 10 years and fine
ai_summary: Section 240 IPC is the aggravated counterpart to Section 239, penalising the knowing acquisition and fraudulent delivery or circulation of counterfeit Indian coin. The 10-year maximum punishment — double that of Section 239 — reflects the heightened harm of circulating fake Indian national currency.

title: Delivery of Coin as Genuine Which, When First Possessed, the Deliverer Did Not Know to Be Counterfeit
chapter: Chapter XII - Of Offences Relating to Coin and Government Stamps
bare_act_text: Whoever delivers to any other person as genuine, or attempts to induce any other person to receive as genuine, any counterfeit coin which he knows to be counterfeit, but which he did not know to be counterfeit at the time when he took it into his possession, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine to an amount which may extend to ten times the value of the coin counterfeited, or with both. Illustration: A, a coiner, delivers counterfeit Company's rupees to his accomplice B, for the purpose of uttering them. B sells the rupees to C, another utterer, who buys them knowing them to be counterfeit. C pays away the rupees for goods to D, who receives them, not knowing them to be counterfeit. D, after receiving the rupees, discovers that they are counterfeit and pays them away as if they were good. Here D is punishable only under this section, but B and C are punishable under section 239 or 240, as the case may be.
meaning: Section 241 IPC applies to a person who received a counterfeit coin innocently (not knowing it was fake at the time of acquisition) but later, upon discovering it is counterfeit, still attempts to pass it off as genuine. The reduced punishment reflects the lesser moral culpability compared to Sections 239 and 240.
simple_explanation: If someone receives a fake coin without knowing it is fake, but later discovers it is counterfeit and still tries to spend it as if it were real, they can be imprisoned for up to 2 years or fined up to ten times the value of the coin. The key distinction from Sections 239/240 is that they did not know it was fake when they first got it.
objective: To deter even innocent recipients of counterfeit coin from attempting to pass them off as genuine once they discover the fraud, while recognising their lesser culpability through a lighter punishment.
legal_significance: Section 241 fills an important gap — without it, a person who innocently received a counterfeit coin and later tried to circulate it knowingly could not be punished under Sections 239 or 240 (which require knowledge at the time of acquisition). The fine ceiling of ten times the coin's value is a unique proportional penalty in Chapter XII.
essential_elements: The accused possesses a counterfeit coin.
At the time of acquisition, the accused did not know it was counterfeit.
The accused subsequently discovers it is counterfeit.
Despite this knowledge, the accused delivers it as genuine or attempts to induce another to receive it as genuine.

mens_rea.description: Knowledge of the counterfeit nature must exist at the time of delivery — not at the time of acquisition. The act of passing it off as genuine after discovering the fraud is the culpable mental element.

examples.scenario: D receives counterfeit rupees in a transaction without knowing they are fake. After discovering they are counterfeit, D pays them away at a shop as if they were genuine.
examples.result: D is liable under Section 241 IPC with imprisonment up to 2 years or fine up to ten times the value of the coins.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 2 years or fine up to ten times the value of the coin
ai_summary: Section 241 IPC addresses the scenario where an innocent recipient of a counterfeit coin later discovers it is fake but still attempts to pass it off as genuine. The reduced punishment — up to 2 years or a fine proportional to the coin's value — reflects the lesser culpability compared to Sections 239 and 240 where knowledge existed from the time of acquisition.

title: Possession of Counterfeit Coin by Person Who Knew It to Be Counterfeit When He Became Possessed Thereof
chapter: Chapter XII - Of Offences Relating to Coin and Government Stamps
bare_act_text: Whoever, fraudulently or with intent that fraud may be committed, is in possession of counterfeit coin, having known at the time when he became possessed thereof that such coin was counterfeit, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, and shall also be liable to fine.
meaning: Section 242 IPC punishes mere possession of counterfeit coin (other than Indian coin) when the accused knew it was counterfeit at the time of acquiring possession, and holds the coin fraudulently or with intent to commit fraud.
simple_explanation: Simply having fake coins in your possession — knowing they are fake when you first got them and intending to use them fraudulently — is a crime punishable by up to 3 years imprisonment and a fine. You don't have to actually use them; possession with fraudulent intent is enough.
objective: To criminalise the fraudulent possession of counterfeit coins as a preventive measure, targeting those who hold counterfeit coins in preparation for circulating them.
legal_significance: Section 242 targets the possession stage — before delivery or circulation — complementing Sections 239 and 241 which deal with actual delivery. The requirement of fraudulent intent or intent to commit fraud distinguishes innocent possession from criminal possession.
essential_elements: The accused is in possession of counterfeit coin.
At the time of acquiring possession, the accused knew the coin was counterfeit.
The possession is fraudulent or with intent that fraud may be committed.
The coin need not be Indian coin.

mens_rea.description: Knowledge of the counterfeit nature at the time of acquisition and fraudulent intent in possession are both required.

examples.scenario: A person is found with a bag of counterfeit foreign coins, which they knowingly purchased from a counterfeiter with the intent to pass them off as genuine.
examples.result: The person is liable under Section 242 IPC with imprisonment up to 3 years and fine.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 years and fine
ai_summary: Section 242 IPC criminalises fraudulent possession of counterfeit coin (other than Indian coin) when the accused knew it was counterfeit at the time of acquisition. It targets the possession stage of the counterfeiting chain, carrying a maximum of 3 years imprisonment, with enhanced punishment under Section 243 for Indian coin.

title: Possession of Indian Coin by Person Who Knew It to Be Counterfeit When He Became Possessed Thereof
chapter: Chapter XII - Of Offences Relating to Coin and Government Stamps
bare_act_text: Whoever, fraudulently or with intent that fraud may be committed, is in possession of counterfeit coin, which is a counterfeit of Indian coin, having known at the time when he became possessed of it that it was counterfeit, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.
meaning: Section 243 IPC is the aggravated form of Section 242, applying when the counterfeit coin fraudulently possessed is specifically a counterfeit of Indian coin. The punishment is more than doubled to 7 years.
simple_explanation: Knowingly holding fake Indian government coins with the intent to use them fraudulently can result in up to 7 years imprisonment and a fine. This is nearly double the punishment under Section 242 because it directly threatens India's national currency.
objective: To impose significantly enhanced penalties for the fraudulent possession of counterfeit Indian coin, recognising the special threat to national monetary security.
legal_significance: Section 243 follows the consistent escalation pattern of Chapter XII — identical conduct directed at Indian coin always attracts a higher penalty. The 7-year maximum for mere possession of counterfeit Indian coin underscores how seriously the law treats threats to national currency.
essential_elements: The accused is in possession of counterfeit coin that is a counterfeit of Indian coin.
At the time of acquiring possession, the accused knew it was a counterfeit of Indian coin.
The possession is fraudulent or with intent that fraud may be committed.

mens_rea.description: Knowledge of the counterfeit Indian coin nature at time of acquisition and fraudulent intent in possession are both essential.

examples.scenario: Police recover a stash of counterfeit Indian 10-rupee coins from a suspect who had knowingly purchased them from a counterfeiter.
examples.result: The suspect is liable under Section 243 IPC with imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine
ai_summary: Section 243 IPC is the aggravated counterpart to Section 242, penalising fraudulent possession of counterfeit Indian coin with knowledge of its nature at the time of acquisition. The 7-year maximum punishment reflects the heightened threat to India's national monetary system.

title: Person Employed in Mint Causing Coin to Be of Different Weight or Composition from That Fixed by Law
chapter: Chapter XII - Of Offences Relating to Coin and Government Stamps
bare_act_text: Whoever, being employed in any mint lawfully established in India, does any act, or omits what he is legally bound to do, with the intention of causing any coin issued from that mint to be of a different weight or composition from the weight or composition fixed by law, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.
meaning: Section 244 IPC punishes a mint employee who intentionally acts or omits to act with the purpose of causing official coins to be issued with a weight or composition different from what is legally prescribed — essentially committing fraud from within the official minting process.
simple_explanation: If a person working in an official government mint deliberately tampers with the weight or metal composition of coins being produced — either by doing something they shouldn't or failing to do something they are required to — they can be imprisoned for up to 7 years and fined.
objective: To protect the integrity of official coinage from corruption within the minting process itself, holding mint employees to a special standard of accountability given their privileged position.
legal_significance: Section 244 addresses an insider threat to monetary integrity — corruption within the official mint. It covers both acts of commission and omission, recognising that a mint employee can compromise coin quality either way. This is distinct from external counterfeiting addressed in earlier sections.
essential_elements: The accused is employed in a mint lawfully established in India.
The accused does any act or omits what they are legally bound to do.
The act or omission is done with the intention of causing coins to be issued with a different weight or composition from that fixed by law.

mens_rea.description: Intention to cause coins to deviate from the legally prescribed weight or composition is required. Accidental deviations would not attract liability.

examples.scenario: A mint technician deliberately reduces the silver content in coins being produced, intending to allow the surplus metal to be misappropriated.
examples.result: The technician is liable under Section 244 IPC with imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine
ai_summary: Section 244 IPC addresses insider corruption within official mints, punishing employees who intentionally cause coins to deviate from their legally prescribed weight or composition through acts or omissions. It carries up to 7 years imprisonment, recognising the unique threat posed by corruption within the official minting process.

title: Unlawfully Taking Coining Instrument from Mint
chapter: Chapter XII - Of Offences Relating to Coin and Government Stamps
bare_act_text: Whoever, without lawful authority, takes out of any mint, lawfully established in India, any coining tool or instrument, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.
meaning: Section 245 IPC punishes the unauthorised removal of any coining tool or instrument from a lawfully established Indian mint, regardless of the purpose for which it is removed.
simple_explanation: Taking any coining tool or equipment out of an official government mint without authorisation is a crime punishable by up to 7 years imprisonment and a fine. This applies even if the person had access to the mint — they cannot remove coining instruments without lawful authority.
objective: To prevent official minting equipment from falling into unauthorised hands, where it could be used for counterfeiting or other fraudulent purposes.
legal_significance: Section 245 does not require proof of intent to use the instrument for counterfeiting — the mere unauthorised removal is sufficient. This strict approach prevents the diversion of official minting infrastructure to criminal purposes at the earliest stage.
essential_elements: The accused takes out a coining tool or instrument.
The mint from which it is taken is lawfully established in India.
The removal is without lawful authority.

mens_rea.description: No specific fraudulent intent is required — the unauthorised act of removal itself constitutes the offence. Knowledge that one lacks authority suffices.

examples.scenario: A mint employee secretly removes a coin-stamping die from the mint without authorisation.
examples.result: The employee is liable under Section 245 IPC with imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine
ai_summary: Section 245 IPC criminalises the unauthorised removal of coining tools or instruments from any lawfully established Indian mint. No fraudulent intent needs to be proved — the act of unauthorised removal alone is sufficient, carrying up to 7 years imprisonment to protect official minting infrastructure from diversion.

title: Fraudulently or Dishonestly Diminishing Weight or Altering Composition of Coin
chapter: Chapter XII - Of Offences Relating to Coin and Government Stamps
bare_act_text: Whoever, fraudulently or dishonestly performs on any coin any operation which diminishes the weight or alters the composition of that coin, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, and shall also be liable to fine. Explanation: A person who scoops out part of the coin and puts anything else into the cavity alters the composition of the coin.
meaning: Section 246 IPC punishes fraudulent or dishonest tampering with any coin (other than Indian coin specifically) by reducing its weight or altering its metal composition. The Explanation clarifies that scooping out metal and filling the cavity with another substance constitutes altering composition.
simple_explanation: Filing down a coin to reduce its weight, or scooping it out and filling it with cheaper metal, with the intent to defraud is a crime punishable by up to 3 years imprisonment and a fine. This covers any coin, not just Indian coins — for which Section 247 provides higher punishment.
objective: To protect the integrity of coins in circulation by penalising those who physically tamper with their weight or composition with fraudulent or dishonest intent.
legal_significance: Section 246 targets a specific form of monetary fraud — physical debasement of existing coins. The Explanation ensures that sophisticated methods of altering coin composition are also covered, not just simple weight reduction.
essential_elements: The accused performs an operation on a coin.
The operation diminishes the weight or alters the composition of the coin.
The act is done fraudulently or dishonestly.

mens_rea.description: Fraudulent or dishonest intent is required. Accidental changes to weight or composition through legitimate use do not attract liability.

examples.scenario: A person systematically files the edges off foreign silver coins to collect the shavings, then passes the lightened coins in transactions.
examples.result: The person is liable under Section 246 IPC with imprisonment up to 3 years and fine.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 years and fine
ai_summary: Section 246 IPC penalises fraudulent or dishonest physical tampering with any coin by reducing its weight or altering its metal composition. The Explanation extends coverage to cavity-filling techniques. Maximum punishment is 3 years, with enhanced penalties under Section 247 when Indian coin is the target.

title: Fraudulently or Dishonestly Diminishing Weight or Altering Composition of Indian Coin
chapter: Chapter XII - Of Offences Relating to Coin and Government Stamps
bare_act_text: Whoever fraudulently or dishonestly performs on any Indian coin any operation which diminishes the weight or alters the composition of that coin, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.
meaning: Section 247 IPC is the aggravated form of Section 246, applying when the coin whose weight is reduced or composition is altered is an Indian coin. The punishment is more than doubled to 7 years.
simple_explanation: Tampering with Indian government coins — such as filing them down or scooping out the metal and replacing it with a cheaper substance — with fraudulent or dishonest intent is punishable by up to 7 years imprisonment and a fine.
objective: To impose enhanced penalties for physical debasement of Indian coin, protecting the integrity of India's national currency from fraudulent tampering.
legal_significance: Section 247 follows the consistent escalation pattern of Chapter XII. The 7-year maximum for tampering with Indian coin versus 3 years for other coins reflects the heightened concern for protecting national monetary integrity.
essential_elements: The accused performs an operation on Indian coin.
The operation diminishes the weight or alters the composition of the coin.
The act is done fraudulently or dishonestly.
The coin must qualify as Indian coin under Section 230.

mens_rea.description: Fraudulent or dishonest intent is essential, identical to Section 246 but applied to Indian coin.

examples.scenario: A person dishonestly files the edges of Indian rupee coins to collect metal shavings, then circulates the debased coins.
examples.result: The person is liable under Section 247 IPC with imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine
ai_summary: Section 247 IPC is the aggravated counterpart to Section 246, penalising fraudulent or dishonest physical tampering with Indian coin by reducing weight or altering composition. The 7-year maximum punishment — more than double that for other coins — reflects the special importance of protecting India's national currency from debasement.

title: Altering Appearance of Coin with Intent That It Shall Pass as Coin of Different Description
chapter: Chapter XII - Of Offences Relating to Coin and Government Stamps
bare_act_text: Whoever performs on any coin any operation which alters the appearance of that coin, with the intention that the said coin shall pass as a coin of a different description, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, and shall also be liable to fine.
meaning: Section 248 IPC punishes any alteration to the appearance of a coin — such as its colour, markings, or design — done with the intent to make it pass as a coin of a higher denomination or different type. Unlike Section 246, it focuses on appearance rather than weight or composition.
simple_explanation: If someone alters a coin's appearance — for example, coating a lower-denomination coin to make it look like a higher-denomination one — with the intent to deceive, they can be imprisoned for up to 3 years and fined. This covers appearance-based fraud as opposed to the weight/composition fraud covered by Section 246.
objective: To penalise appearance-based manipulation of coins intended to pass them off as coins of a higher or different denomination, a distinct form of monetary fraud.
legal_significance: Section 248 complements Section 246 by covering appearance fraud rather than weight/composition fraud. Together they comprehensively address physical manipulation of coins. Section 249 provides the enhanced counterpart for Indian coin.
essential_elements: The accused performs an operation on a coin.
The operation alters the appearance of the coin.
The accused intends the coin to pass as a coin of a different description.
The coin need not be Indian coin.

mens_rea.description: Specific intention that the coin shall pass as a coin of a different description is required.

examples.scenario: A person electroplates low-value copper coins with silver coating to make them resemble higher-denomination silver coins, intending to use them in transactions.
examples.result: The person is liable under Section 248 IPC with imprisonment up to 3 years and fine.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 years and fine
ai_summary: Section 248 IPC penalises appearance-based manipulation of coins — altering their look to make them pass as a different denomination — with intent to deceive. Carrying a maximum of 3 years imprisonment, it complements Section 246 (weight/composition fraud) and has an aggravated counterpart in Section 249 for Indian coin.

title: Altering Appearance of Indian Coin with Intent That It Shall Pass as Coin of Different Description
chapter: Chapter XII - Of Offences Relating to Coin and Government Stamps
bare_act_text: Whoever performs on any Indian coin any operation which alters the appearance of that coin, with the intention that the said coin shall pass as a coin of a different description, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.
meaning: Section 249 IPC is the aggravated form of Section 248, applying when the coin whose appearance is altered to pass as a different denomination is an Indian coin. The punishment is more than doubled to 7 years.
simple_explanation: Altering the appearance of an Indian government coin — for example, making a 1-rupee coin look like a 5-rupee coin — with intent to deceive is punishable by up to 7 years imprisonment and a fine.
objective: To impose enhanced penalties for appearance-based fraud targeting Indian coin, protecting the public from being deceived through manipulated national currency.
legal_significance: Section 249 mirrors the escalation from Section 248 to a higher penalty when Indian coin is involved, consistent with the framework throughout Chapter XII that treats offences against Indian currency more severely.
essential_elements: The accused performs an operation on Indian coin.
The operation alters the appearance of the coin.
The accused intends the coin to pass as a coin of a different description.
The coin must qualify as Indian coin under Section 230.

mens_rea.description: Specific intention that the Indian coin shall pass as a coin of a different description is required.

examples.scenario: A person plates 1-rupee Indian coins with gold-coloured coating to make them pass as 5-rupee coins in vending machines.
examples.result: The person is liable under Section 249 IPC with imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine
ai_summary: Section 249 IPC is the aggravated counterpart to Section 248, targeting appearance-based manipulation of Indian coin to pass as a different denomination. The 7-year maximum punishment reflects the heightened concern for protecting India's national currency from appearance-based fraud.

title: Delivery of Coin Possessed with Knowledge That It Is Altered
chapter: Chapter XII - Of Offences Relating to Coin and Government Stamps
bare_act_text: Whoever, having coin in his possession with respect to which the offence defined in section 246 or 248 has been committed, and having known at the time when he became possessed of such coin that such offence had been committed with respect to it, fraudulently or with intent that fraud may be committed, delivers such coin to any other person, or attempts to induce any other person to receive the same, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to five years, and shall also be liable to fine.
meaning: Section 250 IPC punishes a person who, knowing at the time of acquisition that a coin had been weight-reduced or appearance-altered (under Sections 246 or 248), delivers it to another or induces another to receive it with fraudulent intent.
simple_explanation: If someone obtains a coin that has been tampered with — either having its weight reduced or appearance altered — knowing about the tampering when they first got it, and then tries to pass it on to someone else fraudulently, they can be imprisoned for up to 5 years and fined.
objective: To penalise the circulation of physically tampered coins by those who knowingly acquire and fraudulently distribute them, targeting the delivery stage in the chain of coin-tampering fraud.
legal_significance: Section 250 is the delivery/circulation counterpart to Section 252 (possession). It mirrors the structure of Sections 239/240 (delivery of counterfeit coin) but applies to altered — rather than counterfeit — coins. Section 251 provides enhanced punishment for Indian coin.
essential_elements: The accused possesses a coin on which an offence under Section 246 or 248 has been committed.
At the time of acquiring possession, the accused knew the offence had been committed on the coin.
The accused delivers the coin to another person or attempts to induce another to receive it.
The act is done fraudulently or with intent that fraud may be committed.

mens_rea.description: Knowledge of the tampering at the time of acquisition and fraudulent intent at the time of delivery are both required.

examples.scenario: A person buys edge-filed foreign coins from a tamperer knowing they are debased, then passes them to traders as genuine full-weight coins.
examples.result: The person is liable under Section 250 IPC with imprisonment up to 5 years and fine.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 5 years and fine
ai_summary: Section 250 IPC penalises the knowing delivery or attempted circulation of coins that have been weight-reduced or appearance-altered under Sections 246 or 248, where the accused was aware of the tampering at the time of acquisition. Maximum punishment is 5 years, with enhanced penalties under Section 251 for Indian coin.

title: Delivery of Indian Coin Possessed with Knowledge That It Is Altered
chapter: Chapter XII - Of Offences Relating to Coin and Government Stamps
bare_act_text: Whoever, having coin in his possession with respect to which the offence defined in section 247 or 249 has been committed, and having known at the time when he became possessed of such coin that such offence had been committed with respect to it, fraudulently or with intent that fraud may be committed, delivers such coin to any other person, or attempts to induce any other person to receive the same, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.
meaning: Section 251 IPC is the aggravated form of Section 250, applying when the tampered coin knowingly acquired and fraudulently delivered is an Indian coin on which an offence under Section 247 or 249 has been committed.
simple_explanation: Knowingly receiving a tampered Indian coin — one that has had its weight reduced or appearance altered — and then fraudulently passing it on to someone else can result in up to 10 years imprisonment and a fine.
objective: To impose the highest delivery-stage penalties for circulation of tampered Indian coin, consistent with the enhanced protection afforded to national currency throughout Chapter XII.
legal_significance: Section 251 doubles the punishment from Section 250 (5 years to 10 years) when Indian coin is involved, consistent with the escalation pattern throughout Chapter XII. It represents one of the most severe punishments in the altered coin provisions.
essential_elements: The accused possesses a coin on which an offence under Section 247 or 249 has been committed.
The coin is Indian coin.
At the time of acquiring possession, the accused knew the offence had been committed on it.
The accused delivers it or attempts to induce another to receive it fraudulently.

mens_rea.description: Knowledge of the tampering with Indian coin at time of acquisition and fraudulent intent at delivery are both required.

examples.scenario: A person knowingly acquires appearance-altered Indian 5-rupee coins (plated to look like 10-rupee coins) and uses them in commercial transactions.
examples.result: The person is liable under Section 251 IPC with imprisonment up to 10 years and fine.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 10 years and fine
ai_summary: Section 251 IPC is the aggravated counterpart to Section 250, penalising the knowing delivery of tampered Indian coin (weight-reduced or appearance-altered under Sections 247/249). The 10-year maximum punishment reflects the heightened protection afforded to Indian national currency.

title: Possession of Coin by Person Who Knew It to Be Altered When He Became Possessed Thereof
chapter: Chapter XII - Of Offences Relating to Coin and Government Stamps
bare_act_text: Whoever fraudulently or with intent that fraud may be committed, is in possession of coin with respect to which the offence defined in either of the section 246 or 248 has been committed, having known at the time of becoming possessed thereof that such offence had been committed with respect to such coin, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, and shall also be liable to fine.
meaning: Section 252 IPC punishes mere fraudulent possession of a tampered coin (weight-reduced or appearance-altered under Sections 246 or 248) when the accused knew about the tampering at the time of acquiring possession.
simple_explanation: Simply holding a tampered coin — one that has been filed down or made to look like a different denomination — knowing it was tampered when you got it and intending to commit fraud, is punishable by up to 3 years imprisonment and a fine. You don't have to have passed it on yet.
objective: To criminalise the possession stage of altered coin fraud as a preventive measure, enabling prosecution before actual delivery or circulation occurs.
legal_significance: Section 252 is the possession counterpart to Section 250 (delivery of altered coin), mirroring the structure of Section 242 (possession of counterfeit coin) in the altered coin context. Section 253 provides the enhanced counterpart for Indian coin.
essential_elements: The accused is in possession of a coin on which an offence under Section 246 or 248 has been committed.
At the time of acquiring possession, the accused knew the offence had been committed.
The possession is fraudulent or with intent that fraud may be committed.

mens_rea.description: Knowledge of the tampering at the time of acquisition and fraudulent intent in possession are both required.

examples.scenario: A person is found in possession of a collection of edge-clipped foreign coins which they knowingly purchased from a coin tamperer.
examples.result: The person is liable under Section 252 IPC with imprisonment up to 3 years and fine.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 years and fine
ai_summary: Section 252 IPC criminalises fraudulent possession of tampered coins (weight-reduced or appearance-altered under Sections 246/248) with knowledge of the tampering at the time of acquisition. It targets the possession stage before circulation, carrying up to 3 years imprisonment with enhanced penalties under Section 253 for Indian coin.

title: Possession of Indian Coin by Person Who Knew It to Be Altered When He Became Possessed Thereof
chapter: Chapter XII - Of Offences Relating to Coin and Government Stamps
bare_act_text: Whoever, fraudulently or with intent that fraud may be committed, is in possession of coin with respect to which the offence defined in either of the section 247 or 249 has been committed having known at the time of becoming possessed thereof, that such offence had been committed with respect to such coin, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to five years, and shall also be liable to fine.
meaning: Section 253 IPC is the aggravated form of Section 252, applying when the tampered coin fraudulently possessed is an Indian coin on which an offence under Section 247 or 249 has been committed.
simple_explanation: Knowingly holding a tampered Indian government coin — knowing it has been weight-reduced or made to look like a different denomination — with fraudulent intent is punishable by up to 5 years imprisonment and a fine.
objective: To impose enhanced penalties for the fraudulent possession of tampered Indian coin, maintaining the consistent escalation framework for offences against national currency.
legal_significance: Section 253 increases the possession-stage penalty from 3 years (Section 252) to 5 years when Indian coin is involved, consistent with the chapter-wide escalation for Indian coin offences.
essential_elements: The accused is in possession of Indian coin on which an offence under Section 247 or 249 has been committed.
At the time of acquiring possession, the accused knew the offence had been committed.
The possession is fraudulent or with intent that fraud may be committed.

mens_rea.description: Knowledge of the tampering with Indian coin at the time of acquisition and fraudulent intent in possession are both required.

examples.scenario: A person knowingly acquires Indian rupee coins that have been appearance-altered to look like higher-denomination coins and holds them with intent to use them fraudulently.
examples.result: The person is liable under Section 253 IPC with imprisonment up to 5 years and fine.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 5 years and fine
ai_summary: Section 253 IPC is the aggravated counterpart to Section 252, penalising fraudulent possession of tampered Indian coin (weight-reduced or appearance-altered under Sections 247/249) with knowledge of the tampering at acquisition. The 5-year maximum reflects enhanced protection for Indian national currency.

title: Delivery of Coin as Genuine Which, When First Possessed, the Deliverer Did Not Know to Be Altered
chapter: Chapter XII - Of Offences Relating to Coin and Government Stamps
bare_act_text: Whoever delivers to any other person as genuine or as a coin of a different description from what it is, or attempts to induce any person to receive as genuine, or as a different coin from what it is, any coin in respect of which he knows that any such operation as that mentioned in sections 246, 247, 248 or 249 has been performed, but in respect of which he did not, at the time when he took it into his possession, know that such operation had been performed, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine to an amount which may extend to ten times the value of the coin for which the altered coin is passed, or attempted to be passed.
meaning: Section 254 IPC applies to a person who innocently received an altered coin without knowing it was tampered, but later discovered the tampering and still attempted to pass it off as genuine or as a different denomination. It is the altered coin equivalent of Section 241 for counterfeit coin.
simple_explanation: If someone receives a coin that has been tampered with (weight reduced or appearance altered) without knowing it was tampered at the time, but later discovers the tampering and still tries to pass it off as genuine, they can be imprisoned up to 2 years or fined up to ten times the value of the coin.
objective: To deter even innocent recipients of tampered coins from attempting to circulate them once they discover the tampering, while recognising their lesser culpability through a lighter punishment.
legal_significance: Section 254 mirrors Section 241 (innocent receipt of counterfeit coin later passed off as genuine) but applies to altered coins under Sections 246–249. The fine ceiling of ten times the coin's value provides a proportional deterrent tied to the potential gain from the fraud.
essential_elements: The accused possesses a coin on which an operation under Sections 246, 247, 248, or 249 has been performed.
At the time of acquisition, the accused did not know the operation had been performed.
The accused subsequently discovers the tampering.
Despite this knowledge, the accused delivers it as genuine or as a different denomination, or attempts to induce another to receive it as such.

mens_rea.description: Knowledge of the tampering must exist at the time of delivery — not at the time of acquisition. The deliberate act of passing it off after discovery is the culpable mental element.

examples.scenario: D innocently receives an appearance-altered Indian coin in change. After discovering it is tampered, D deliberately uses it in a shop transaction as if it were genuine.
examples.result: D is liable under Section 254 IPC with imprisonment up to 2 years or fine up to ten times the value of the coin.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 2 years or fine up to ten times the value of the coin
ai_summary: Section 254 IPC addresses innocent recipients of tampered coins who later discover the tampering but still attempt to circulate them as genuine.
