Mirroring Section 241 for counterfeit coin, it carries a reduced punishment of up to 2 years or a proportional fine, reflecting lesser culpability compared to those who knew of the tampering from the outset.

title: Counterfeiting Government Stamp
chapter: Chapter XII - Of Offences Relating to Coin and Government Stamps
bare_act_text: Whoever counterfeits, or knowingly performs any part of the process of counterfeiting, any stamp issued by Government for the purpose of revenue shall be punished with imprisonment for life or with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine. Explanation: A person commits this offence who counterfeits by causing a genuine stamp of one denomination to appear like a genuine stamp of a different denomination.
meaning: Section 255 IPC punishes the counterfeiting of any Government revenue stamp, or knowing participation in any part of the counterfeiting process. It also covers causing a genuine stamp of one denomination to appear as a stamp of a different denomination.
simple_explanation: Making fake government revenue stamps — like court fee stamps or postal stamps — or participating in any part of the process of making them can result in life imprisonment and a fine. Even altering a genuine stamp to make it look like a higher-denomination stamp is covered.
objective: To protect government revenue and the integrity of official stamp systems by imposing severe penalties for stamp counterfeiting, which directly defrauds the state exchequer.
legal_significance: The maximum punishment of life imprisonment for stamp counterfeiting places it on par with serious offences against the state, reflecting the significant revenue implications of large-scale stamp fraud. The Explanation ensures denomination-alteration fraud is also covered.
essential_elements: The accused counterfeits a Government stamp or knowingly performs any part of the counterfeiting process.
The stamp is issued by the Government for revenue purposes.
Alternatively, the accused causes a genuine stamp of one denomination to appear like a stamp of a different denomination.

mens_rea.description: Knowledge that one is performing part of the counterfeiting process is required. For the Explanation, intent to make one denomination appear as another must be established.

examples.scenario: A person operates a printing press producing fake court fee stamps identical to genuine government stamps.
examples.result: The person is liable under Section 255 IPC with punishment up to life imprisonment and fine.

max_punishment: Imprisonment for life and fine
ai_summary: Section 255 IPC criminalises the counterfeiting of Government revenue stamps or knowing participation in the counterfeiting process. The maximum punishment of life imprisonment reflects the serious threat stamp counterfeiting poses to government revenue and the integrity of official documentation systems.

title: Having Possession of Instrument or Material for Counterfeiting Government Stamp
chapter: Chapter XII - Of Offences Relating to Coin and Government Stamps
bare_act_text: Whoever has in his possession any instrument or material for the purpose of being used, or knowing or having reason to believe that it is intended to be used, for the purpose of counterfeiting any stamp issued by Government for the purpose of revenue, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.
meaning: Section 256 IPC punishes possession of any instrument or material intended to be used for counterfeiting Government revenue stamps, targeting the preparatory stage before actual counterfeiting occurs.
simple_explanation: Simply having tools, plates, or materials meant for making fake government revenue stamps — knowing they are intended for that purpose — is a crime punishable by up to 7 years imprisonment and a fine, even if no fake stamps have been made yet.
objective: To enable early intervention by criminalising the possession of counterfeiting instruments before actual stamp counterfeiting occurs, disrupting stamp fraud operations at the preparatory stage.
legal_significance: Section 256 is the preparatory-stage counterpart to Section 255, mirroring the structure of Section 235 for coin counterfeiting instruments. It enables prosecution before the actual counterfeiting is completed, providing a powerful preventive tool for law enforcement.
essential_elements: The accused is in possession of any instrument or material.
The possession is for the purpose of counterfeiting a Government revenue stamp, or the accused knows or has reason to believe it is so intended.
The stamp targeted must be a Government revenue stamp.

mens_rea.description: The possession must be for the purpose of stamp counterfeiting, or the accused must know or have reason to believe the instrument is so intended. Constructive knowledge suffices.

examples.scenario: Police recover printing plates and ink matching government stamp designs from a suspect's premises, with the suspect knowing they are intended for stamp counterfeiting.
examples.result: The suspect is liable under Section 256 IPC with imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine
ai_summary: Section 256 IPC criminalises possession of instruments or materials intended for counterfeiting Government revenue stamps at the preparatory stage. Carrying up to 7 years imprisonment, it enables law enforcement to act before actual counterfeiting occurs, complementing the substantive offence under Section 255.

title: Making or Selling Instrument for Counterfeiting Government Stamp
chapter: Chapter XII - Of Offences Relating to Coin and Government Stamps
bare_act_text: Whoever makes or performs any part of the process of making, or buys, or sells, or disposes of, any instrument for the purpose of being used, or knowing or having reason to believe that it is intended to be used, for the purpose of counterfeiting any stamp issued by Government for the purpose of revenue, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.
meaning: Section 257 IPC criminalizes the manufacture, purchase, sale, disposal, or handling of instruments intended for counterfeiting Government revenue stamps.
simple_explanation: Making, buying, selling, or possessing tools intended to create fake Government revenue stamps is a crime punishable with up to 7 years imprisonment and a fine.
objective: To prevent stamp counterfeiting by targeting the production and circulation of instruments used for creating counterfeit Government revenue stamps.
legal_significance: This section expands criminal liability beyond actual counterfeiting and covers all persons involved in preparing or supplying instruments used for stamp forgery.
essential_elements: The accused makes, buys, sells, disposes of, or participates in making an instrument.
The instrument is intended for counterfeiting Government revenue stamps.
The accused knows or has reason to believe such intended use.

mens_rea.description: Knowledge, intention, or reason to believe that the instrument is intended for stamp counterfeiting is necessary.

examples.scenario: A person sells specialized printing plates knowing they will be used to create fake revenue stamps.
examples.result: The person is liable under Section 257 IPC.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine
ai_summary: Section 257 IPC punishes the making, sale, purchase, or disposal of instruments intended for counterfeiting Government revenue stamps, carrying imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.

title: Sale of Counterfeit Government Stamp
chapter: Chapter XII - Of Offences Relating to Coin and Government Stamps
bare_act_text: Whoever sells, or offers for sale, any stamp which he knows or has reason to believe to be a counterfeit of any stamp issued by Government for the purpose of revenue, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.
meaning: Section 258 IPC criminalizes selling or offering for sale counterfeit Government revenue stamps.
simple_explanation: Selling or attempting to sell a fake Government revenue stamp while knowing it is fake is a serious offence punishable with up to 7 years imprisonment and a fine.
objective: To prevent circulation and commercial distribution of counterfeit Government revenue stamps.
legal_significance: The section targets the market for counterfeit stamps and penalizes those who distribute them even if they did not manufacture them.
essential_elements: The accused sells or offers a stamp for sale.
The stamp is counterfeit.
The accused knows or has reason to believe the stamp is counterfeit.

mens_rea.description: Knowledge or reason to believe that the stamp is counterfeit is essential.

examples.scenario: A trader knowingly offers fake revenue stamps to customers.
examples.result: The trader is punishable under Section 258 IPC.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine
ai_summary: Section 258 IPC punishes the sale or offer for sale of counterfeit Government revenue stamps when the seller knows or suspects they are counterfeit.

title: Having Possession of Counterfeit Government Stamp
chapter: Chapter XII - Of Offences Relating to Coin and Government Stamps
bare_act_text: Whoever has in his possession any stamp which he knows to be a counterfeit of any stamp issued by Government for the purpose of revenue, intending to use, or dispose of the same as a genuine stamp, or in order that it may be used as a genuine stamp, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.
meaning: Section 259 IPC punishes possession of counterfeit Government revenue stamps with the intention of using or circulating them as genuine.
simple_explanation: Keeping fake Government revenue stamps with the intention of using or passing them off as genuine is a crime punishable with up to 7 years imprisonment and a fine.
objective: To prevent counterfeit stamps from entering circulation by criminalizing possession with fraudulent intent.
legal_significance: The section penalizes preparatory conduct before actual use of counterfeit stamps and helps law enforcement intervene early.
essential_elements: The accused possesses a counterfeit Government revenue stamp.
The accused knows the stamp is counterfeit.
The possession is intended for use or disposal as a genuine stamp.

mens_rea.description: Knowledge of the counterfeit nature and intention to use or circulate it as genuine are required.

examples.scenario: A person stores fake revenue stamps intending to sell them as genuine documents.
examples.result: The person is liable under Section 259 IPC.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine
ai_summary: Section 259 IPC punishes possession of counterfeit Government revenue stamps when the holder intends to use or circulate them as genuine.

title: Using as Genuine a Government Stamp Known to be Counterfeit
chapter: Chapter XII - Of Offences Relating to Coin and Government Stamps
bare_act_text: Whoever uses as genuine any stamp, knowing it to be a counterfeit of any stamp issued by Government for the purpose of revenue, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, or with fine, or with both.
meaning: Section 260 IPC criminalizes the actual use of a counterfeit Government revenue stamp as though it were genuine.
simple_explanation: Using a fake Government revenue stamp while knowing it is fake is a criminal offence punishable with imprisonment up to 7 years, fine, or both.
objective: To protect Government revenue and public confidence in official stamps by punishing fraudulent use of counterfeit stamps.
legal_significance: This section targets the final stage of stamp fraud where counterfeit stamps are actually used in transactions or documents.
essential_elements: The accused uses a Government revenue stamp as genuine.
The stamp is counterfeit.
The accused knows the stamp is counterfeit.

mens_rea.description: Knowledge that the stamp is counterfeit is mandatory.

examples.scenario: A person attaches a fake revenue stamp to a legal document knowing it is counterfeit.
examples.result: The person is punishable under Section 260 IPC.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years, or fine, or both
ai_summary: Section 260 IPC punishes anyone who knowingly uses a counterfeit Government revenue stamp as genuine, with imprisonment up to 7 years, fine, or both.

title: Effacing Writing from Substance Bearing Government Stamp, or Removing from Document a Stamp Used for It, with Intent to Cause Loss to Government
chapter: Chapter XII - Of Offences Relating to Coin and Government Stamps
bare_act_text: Whoever, fraudulently or with intent to cause loss to the Government, removes or effaces from any substance, bearing any stamp issued by Government for the purpose of revenue, any writing or document for which such stamp has been used, or removes from any writing or document a stamp which has been used for such writing or document, in order that such stamp may be used for a different writing or document, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both.
meaning: Section 261 IPC punishes the fraudulent removal of used Government revenue stamps or the erasure of writings connected to them so that the stamps can be reused.
simple_explanation: Removing a used Government stamp from a document or erasing the writing connected with it to reuse the stamp and avoid paying revenue again is a criminal offence.
objective: To protect Government revenue by preventing the reuse of revenue stamps that have already served their purpose.
legal_significance: The section criminalizes acts intended to recycle or reuse Government revenue stamps, thereby preventing revenue loss and fraud against the Government.
essential_elements: A Government revenue stamp has already been used.
The accused removes the stamp or erases the writing connected with it.
The act is done fraudulently or with intent to cause loss to the Government.
The purpose is to enable reuse of the stamp.

mens_rea.description: The act must be done fraudulently or with the intention of causing loss to Government revenue.

examples.scenario: A person carefully removes a revenue stamp from an old agreement and pastes it on a new document to avoid purchasing a fresh stamp.
examples.result: The person is liable under Section 261 IPC.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 years, or fine, or both
ai_summary: Section 261 IPC punishes fraudulent removal or alteration of used Government revenue stamps for the purpose of reusing them and causing loss to Government revenue.

title: Using Government Stamp Known to Have Been Before Used
chapter: Chapter XII - Of Offences Relating to Coin and Government Stamps
bare_act_text: Whoever, fraudulently or with intent to cause loss to the Government, uses for any purpose a stamp issued by Government for the purpose of revenue, which he knows to have been before used, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.
meaning: Section 262 IPC punishes the use of a Government revenue stamp that has already been used earlier.
simple_explanation: Using an old revenue stamp again while knowing it has already been used is a criminal offence.
objective: To ensure that Government revenue stamps are used only once and that proper revenue is collected for each transaction.
legal_significance: The section discourages fraudulent reuse of revenue stamps and protects the integrity of the revenue collection system.
essential_elements: The stamp is a Government revenue stamp.
The stamp has already been used before.
The accused knows the stamp was previously used.
The use is fraudulent or intended to cause loss to Government.

mens_rea.description: Knowledge of previous use and fraudulent intention or intent to cause loss to Government are necessary.

examples.scenario: A person knowingly attaches a previously used revenue stamp to a new agreement to avoid paying stamp duty.
examples.result: The person is punishable under Section 262 IPC.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both
ai_summary: Section 262 IPC punishes the fraudulent use of a Government revenue stamp known to have been used previously.

title: Erasure of Mark Denoting that Stamp Has Been Used
chapter: Chapter XII - Of Offences Relating to Coin and Government Stamps
bare_act_text: Whoever, fraudulently or with intent to cause loss to Government, erases or removes from a stamp issued by Government for the purpose of revenue, any mark put or impressed upon such stamp for denoting that the same has been used, or knowingly has in his possession or sells or disposes of any such stamp from which such mark has been erased or removed, or sells or disposes of any such stamp which he knows to have been used, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both.
meaning: Section 263 IPC punishes removal of cancellation marks from used Government revenue stamps and dealing in such altered stamps.
simple_explanation: Removing marks showing that a Government stamp has already been used, or possessing or selling such altered stamps, is a criminal offence.
objective: To prevent the fraudulent reuse and circulation of previously used Government revenue stamps.
legal_significance: The section protects the effectiveness of cancellation markings used to indicate that a stamp has already served its purpose.
essential_elements: A cancellation mark exists on a Government revenue stamp.
The accused removes or erases the mark, or possesses, sells, or disposes of such a stamp.
The act is done fraudulently or with intent to cause loss to Government.

mens_rea.description: Fraudulent intention or intent to cause loss to Government is required, along with knowledge where possession or sale is involved.

examples.scenario: A person removes cancellation marks from used revenue stamps and sells them as unused stamps.
examples.result: The person is liable under Section 263 IPC.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 years, or fine, or both
ai_summary: Section 263 IPC punishes erasing cancellation marks from used Government revenue stamps and dealing in such altered stamps.

title: Prohibition of Fictitious Stamps
chapter: Chapter XII - Of Offences Relating to Coin and Government Stamps
bare_act_text: Whoever makes, knowingly utters, deals in, sells, uses for postal purposes, possesses without lawful excuse any fictitious stamp, or makes or possesses materials for making such fictitious stamps, shall be punished with fine which may extend to two hundred rupees.
meaning: Section 263A IPC prohibits the creation, possession, sale, use, or circulation of fictitious postal stamps and the tools used to make them.
simple_explanation: Making, selling, using, or keeping fake postal stamps, or the materials needed to create them, is a punishable offence.
objective: To protect the postal system and prevent misuse through fake or imitation postage stamps.
legal_significance: The section specifically addresses fictitious postal stamps and provides authority for seizure and forfeiture of such stamps and related materials.
essential_elements: A fictitious postal stamp exists or is involved.
The accused makes, sells, uses, possesses, or deals in such stamps.
Or the accused possesses materials or instruments used to make fictitious stamps.
No lawful excuse exists for such possession.

mens_rea.description: Knowledge is required for dealing, selling, using, or possessing fictitious stamps. Possession without lawful excuse may itself attract liability.

examples.scenario: A person prints imitation postage stamps resembling official postal stamps and sells them online.
examples.result: The person is punishable under Section 263A IPC.

max_punishment: Fine up to ₹200
ai_summary: Section 263A IPC prohibits making, possessing, using, or dealing in fictitious postal stamps and the materials used to create them, with punishment extending to a fine of ₹200.

title: Fraudulent Use of False Instrument for Weighing
chapter: Chapter XIII - Of Offences Relating to Weights and Measures
section_type: Offence Relating to Weights and Measures
bare_act_text: Whoever, fraudulently uses any instrument for weighing which he knows to be false, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to one year, or with fine, or with both.
meaning: Section 264 IPC punishes the fraudulent use of a weighing instrument that the user knows to be inaccurate or false.
simple_explanation: Using a weighing machine, balance, or scale that you know gives wrong measurements in order to cheat others is a crime.
objective: To ensure fairness and honesty in commercial transactions involving weighing of goods.
legal_significance: The section protects consumers and traders from fraud arising from the deliberate use of false weighing instruments.
essential_elements: The accused uses an instrument for weighing.
The instrument is false or inaccurate.
The accused knows the instrument is false.
The use is fraudulent.

mens_rea.description: The accused must know that the weighing instrument is false and use it fraudulently.

examples.scenario: A shopkeeper knowingly uses a tampered weighing scale to provide customers with less quantity than paid for.
examples.result: The shopkeeper is liable under Section 264 IPC.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 1 year, or fine, or both
ai_summary: Section 264 IPC punishes the fraudulent use of a false weighing instrument when the user knows that it is inaccurate.

title: Fraudulent Use of False Weight or Measure
chapter: Chapter XIII - Of Offences Relating to Weights and Measures
section_type: Offence Relating to Weights and Measures
bare_act_text: Whoever, fraudulently uses any false weight or false measure of length or capacity, or fraudulently uses any weight or any measure of length or capacity as a different weight or measure from what it is, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to one year, or with fine, or with both.
meaning: Section 265 IPC punishes the fraudulent use of false weights or measures or the misuse of genuine weights and measures as something different.
simple_explanation: Using fake weights, fake measuring tools, or misrepresenting a weight or measure to cheat someone is a criminal offence.
objective: To maintain accuracy and honesty in trade and commercial dealings.
legal_significance: The section safeguards public confidence in systems of measurement and prevents economic fraud.
essential_elements: The accused uses a false weight or measure.
Or uses a weight or measure as a different one from its actual value.
The use is fraudulent.

mens_rea.description: Fraudulent intention is essential for the commission of the offence.

examples.scenario: A vendor uses a 900-gram weight while claiming it is a 1-kilogram weight.
examples.result: The vendor commits an offence under Section 265 IPC.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 1 year, or fine, or both
ai_summary: Section 265 IPC punishes the fraudulent use of false weights or measures and the misrepresentation of genuine weights or measures.

title: Being in Possession of False Weight or Measure
chapter: Chapter XIII - Of Offences Relating to Weights and Measures
section_type: Offence Relating to Weights and Measures
bare_act_text: Whoever is in possession of any instrument for weighing, or of any weight, or of any measure of length or capacity, which he knows to be false, intending that the same may be fraudulently used, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to one year, or with fine, or with both.
meaning: Section 266 IPC criminalizes possession of false weights, measures, or weighing instruments intended for fraudulent use.
simple_explanation: Keeping fake weights or measuring devices with the intention of using them to cheat people is a crime.
objective: To prevent fraud before it occurs by penalizing possession of false measuring instruments intended for dishonest use.
legal_significance: The section targets preparatory conduct and enables early intervention before actual fraud is committed.
essential_elements: The accused possesses a weight, measure, or weighing instrument.
The item is false or inaccurate.
The accused knows it is false.
There is an intention that it be used fraudulently.

mens_rea.description: Knowledge of falsity and intention of fraudulent use are required.

examples.scenario: A trader stores several altered weighing scales intending to use them in business transactions.
examples.result: The trader is liable under Section 266 IPC.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 1 year, or fine, or both
ai_summary: Section 266 IPC punishes possession of false weights, measures, or weighing instruments when intended for fraudulent use.

title: Making or Selling False Weight or Measure
chapter: Chapter XIII - Of Offences Relating to Weights and Measures
section_type: Offence Relating to Weights and Measures
bare_act_text: Whoever makes, sells or disposes of any instrument for weighing, or any weight, or any measure of length or capacity which he knows to be false, in order that the same may be used as true, or knowing that the same is likely to be used as true, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to one year, or with fine, or with both.
meaning: Section 267 IPC punishes the manufacture, sale, or disposal of false weights and measures intended to be used as genuine.
simple_explanation: Making or selling fake weights or measuring instruments while knowing they may be used as genuine is a criminal offence.
objective: To eliminate the supply of false weights and measures used to cheat consumers and businesses.
legal_significance: The section targets the source of measurement fraud by punishing those who manufacture or distribute false measuring devices.
essential_elements: The accused makes, sells, or disposes of a weight, measure, or weighing instrument.
The item is false.
The accused knows it is false.
The item is intended or likely to be used as genuine.

mens_rea.description: Knowledge that the weight or measure is false and intention or awareness that it may be used as genuine.

examples.scenario: A manufacturer sells inaccurate weighing scales to shopkeepers while claiming they are properly calibrated.
examples.result: The manufacturer commits an offence under Section 267 IPC.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 1 year, or fine, or both
ai_summary: Section 267 IPC punishes making or selling false weights, measures, or weighing instruments intended to be used as genuine.

title: Public Nuisance
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of Offences Affecting the Public Health, Safety, Convenience, Decency and Morals
section_type: Offence Affecting Public Health and Safety
bare_act_text: A person is guilty of a public nuisance who does any act or is guilty of an illegal omission which causes any common injury, danger or annoyance to the public or to the people in general who dwell or occupy property in the vicinity, or which must necessarily cause injury, obstruction, danger or annoyance to persons who may have occasion to use any public right. A common nuisance is not excused on the ground that it causes some convenience or advantage.
meaning: Section 268 IPC defines public nuisance as any act or illegal omission that causes common injury, danger, obstruction, or annoyance to the public.
simple_explanation: If a person's act or failure to act causes harm, danger, or inconvenience to the public at large, it may amount to public nuisance.
objective: To protect public rights, safety, convenience, and comfort from harmful acts affecting the community.
legal_significance: This section provides the legal definition of public nuisance and forms the basis for several related offences under the IPC.
essential_elements: An act or illegal omission occurs.
The conduct affects the public or a section of the public.
The conduct causes injury, danger, annoyance, or obstruction.
The nuisance affects public rights or public convenience.

mens_rea.description: The section primarily defines the offence; specific intent may not always be necessary depending on the circumstances.

examples.scenario: A factory illegally releases toxic fumes affecting an entire neighborhood.
examples.result: The conduct may amount to public nuisance under Section 268 IPC.

max_punishment: Definition section – punishment provided elsewhere
ai_summary: Section 268 IPC defines public nuisance as conduct that causes common injury, danger, obstruction, or annoyance to the public.

title: Negligent Act Likely to Spread Infection of Disease Dangerous to Life
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of Offences Affecting the Public Health, Safety, Convenience, Decency and Morals
bare_act_text: Whoever unlawfully or negligently does any act which is, and which he knows or has reason to believe to be, likely to spread the infection of any disease dangerous to life, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine, or with both.
meaning: Section 269 IPC punishes negligent acts that are likely to spread life-threatening infectious diseases.
simple_explanation: Carelessly doing something that could spread a dangerous disease and knowing the risk can lead to criminal liability.
objective: To protect public health by discouraging negligent behavior that may spread dangerous infections.
legal_significance: The section became particularly relevant during epidemics and pandemics where negligent conduct threatens public health.
essential_elements: An act is committed unlawfully or negligently.
The act is likely to spread a disease dangerous to life.
The accused knows or has reason to believe the risk exists.

mens_rea.description: Knowledge or reason to believe that the act may spread a dangerous disease is necessary.

examples.scenario: A person infected with a highly contagious disease knowingly ignores quarantine rules and interacts with the public.
examples.result: The person may be liable under Section 269 IPC.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 6 months, or fine, or both
ai_summary: Section 269 IPC punishes negligent conduct likely to spread infectious diseases dangerous to life.

title: Malignant Act Likely to Spread Infection of Disease Dangerous to Life
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of Offences Affecting the Public Health, Safety, Convenience, Decency and Morals
bare_act_text: Whoever malignantly does any act which is, and which he knows or has reason to believe to be, likely to spread the infection of any disease dangerous to life, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.
meaning: Section 270 IPC punishes intentional or malicious acts likely to spread dangerous infectious diseases.
simple_explanation: Deliberately doing something that may spread a life-threatening disease is a serious criminal offence.
objective: To deter malicious conduct that endangers public health through the spread of infectious diseases.
legal_significance: The section imposes stricter punishment than Section 269 because the act is done maliciously rather than negligently.
essential_elements: The accused commits an act maliciously.
The act is likely to spread a disease dangerous to life.
The accused knows or has reason to believe the risk.

mens_rea.description: Malicious intention along with knowledge of the likelihood of spreading infection is required.

examples.scenario: A person intentionally exposes others to a highly contagious dangerous disease despite knowing the consequences.
examples.result: The person may be prosecuted under Section 270 IPC.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both
ai_summary: Section 270 IPC punishes malicious acts likely to spread infectious diseases dangerous to life and carries a higher punishment than negligent conduct under Section 269.

title: Disobedience to Quarantine Rule
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of Offences Affecting the Public Health, Safety, Convenience, Decency and Morals
bare_act_text: Whoever knowingly disobeys any rule made and promulgated by the Government for putting any vessel into a state of quarantine, or for regulating the intercourse of vessels in a state of quarantine with the shore or with other vessels, or for regulating the intercourse between places where an infectious disease prevails and other places, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine, or with both.
meaning: Section 271 IPC punishes the knowing violation of quarantine regulations made to prevent the spread of infectious diseases.
simple_explanation: If a person knowingly breaks quarantine rules intended to control the spread of dangerous diseases, he commits an offence.
objective: To protect public health by ensuring compliance with quarantine and disease-control regulations.
legal_significance: The section strengthens public health measures by making deliberate violations of quarantine rules punishable.
essential_elements: A valid quarantine rule exists.
The accused knowingly disobeys the rule.
The rule relates to quarantine or disease-control measures.

mens_rea.description: Knowledge of the quarantine rule and intentional disobedience are required.

examples.scenario: A person knowingly leaves a quarantine facility despite government restrictions during an epidemic.
examples.result: The person is liable under Section 271 IPC.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 6 months, or fine, or both
ai_summary: Section 271 IPC punishes deliberate violation of quarantine rules intended to prevent the spread of infectious diseases.

title: Adulteration of Food or Drink Intended for Sale
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of Offences Affecting the Public Health, Safety, Convenience, Decency and Morals
bare_act_text: Whoever adulterates any article of food or drink, so as to make such article noxious as food or drink, intending to sell such article as food or drink, or knowing it to be likely that the same will be sold as food or drink, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine which may extend to one thousand rupees, or with both.
meaning: Section 272 IPC punishes adulteration of food or drink that makes it harmful for consumption.
simple_explanation: Mixing harmful substances into food or drinks intended for sale is a criminal offence.
objective: To protect public health and consumer safety from harmful food adulteration.
legal_significance: The section targets manufacturers and sellers who intentionally compromise the safety of food products.
essential_elements: Food or drink is adulterated.
The adulteration makes it noxious or harmful.
There is intent or likelihood of sale for consumption.

mens_rea.description: The accused must intend sale or know that the adulterated food is likely to be sold.

examples.scenario: A vendor mixes harmful chemicals into milk to increase profits.
examples.result: The vendor commits an offence under Section 272 IPC.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 6 months, or fine up to ₹1,000, or both
ai_summary: Section 272 IPC punishes adulteration of food or drink intended for sale when it becomes harmful for consumption.

title: Sale of Noxious Food or Drink
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of Offences Affecting the Public Health, Safety, Convenience, Decency and Morals
bare_act_text: Whoever sells, or offers or exposes for sale, as food or drink, any article which has been rendered or has become noxious, or is in a state unfit for food or drink, knowing or having reason to believe that the same is noxious as food or drink, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine which may extend to one thousand rupees, or with both.
meaning: Section 273 IPC punishes the sale or offering for sale of harmful or unfit food and drink.
simple_explanation: Selling spoiled, contaminated, or harmful food while knowing it is unsafe is a crime.
objective: To prevent public health risks arising from unsafe food and beverages.
legal_significance: The section focuses on the sale and distribution of harmful food products regardless of who caused the contamination.
essential_elements: Food or drink is noxious or unfit.
The accused sells or offers it for sale.
The accused knows or has reason to believe it is harmful.

mens_rea.description: Knowledge or reason to believe that the food or drink is harmful is necessary.

examples.scenario: A restaurant knowingly serves spoiled food to customers.
examples.result: The restaurant owner may be prosecuted under Section 273 IPC.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 6 months, or fine up to ₹1,000, or both
ai_summary: Section 273 IPC punishes the sale or offering for sale of food or drink known to be harmful or unfit for consumption.

title: Adulteration of Drugs
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of Offences Affecting the Public Health, Safety, Convenience, Decency and Morals
bare_act_text: Whoever adulterates any drug or medical preparation in such a manner as to lessen the efficacy or change the operation of such drug or medical preparation, or to make it noxious, intending that it shall be sold or used for, or knowing it to be likely that it will be sold or used for, any medicinal purpose, as if it had not undergone such adulteration, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine which may extend to one thousand rupees, or with both.
meaning: Section 274 IPC punishes the adulteration of drugs or medical preparations.
simple_explanation: Changing a medicine so that it becomes weaker, harmful, or works differently is a criminal offence.
objective: To protect patients from unsafe and ineffective medicines.
legal_significance: The section safeguards public health by ensuring the integrity and effectiveness of medicinal products.
essential_elements: A drug or medical preparation is adulterated.
The adulteration reduces effectiveness, changes operation, or makes it harmful.
The accused intends or knows it may be used as medicine.

mens_rea.description: The accused must intend or know that the adulterated drug will be sold or used medicinally.

examples.scenario: A manufacturer dilutes a medicine to reduce costs while selling it as genuine.
examples.result: The manufacturer is liable under Section 274 IPC.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 6 months, or fine up to ₹1,000, or both
ai_summary: Section 274 IPC punishes adulteration of drugs that reduces effectiveness or makes them harmful.

title: Adulteration of Drugs
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of Offences Affecting the Public Health, Safety, Convenience, Decency and Morals
bare_act_text: Whoever adulterates any drug or medical preparation in such a manner as to lessen the efficacy or change the operation of such drug or medical preparation, or to make it noxious, intending that it shall be sold or used for, or knowing it to be likely that it will be sold or used for, any medicinal purpose, as if it had not undergone such adulteration, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine which may extend to one thousand rupees, or with both.
meaning: Section 274 IPC punishes the adulteration of drugs or medical preparations.
simple_explanation: Changing a medicine so that it becomes weaker, harmful, or works differently is a criminal offence.
objective: To protect patients from unsafe and ineffective medicines.
legal_significance: The section safeguards public health by ensuring the integrity and effectiveness of medicinal products.
essential_elements: A drug or medical preparation is adulterated.
The adulteration reduces effectiveness, changes operation, or makes it harmful.
The accused intends or knows it may be used as medicine.

mens_rea.description: The accused must intend or know that the adulterated drug will be sold or used medicinally.

examples.scenario: A manufacturer dilutes a medicine to reduce costs while selling it as genuine.
examples.result: The manufacturer is liable under Section 274 IPC.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 6 months, or fine up to ₹1,000, or both
ai_summary: Section 274 IPC punishes adulteration of drugs that reduces effectiveness or makes them harmful.

title: Sale of Drug as a Different Drug or Preparation
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of Offences Affecting the Public Health, Safety, Convenience, Decency and Morals
bare_act_text: Whoever knowingly sells, or offers or exposes for sale, or issues from a dispensary for medicinal purposes, any drug or medical preparation, as a different drug or medical preparation, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine which may extend to one thousand rupees, or with both.
meaning: Section 276 IPC punishes knowingly selling or supplying one drug while representing it as another drug.
simple_explanation: Giving or selling the wrong medicine while falsely claiming it is a different medicine is a criminal offence.
objective: To ensure accuracy and honesty in the sale and distribution of medicines.
legal_significance: The section protects patients from deception that could lead to ineffective treatment or health risks.
essential_elements: A drug or medical preparation is sold, offered, or supplied.
It is represented as a different drug or preparation.
The accused knowingly misrepresents the drug.

mens_rea.description: Knowledge of the misrepresentation is required.

examples.scenario: A pharmacist knowingly supplies a cheaper medicine while claiming it is a prescribed branded drug.
examples.result: The pharmacist commits an offence under Section 276 IPC.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 6 months, or fine up to ₹1,000, or both
ai_summary: Section 276 IPC punishes the knowing sale or supply of a drug falsely represented as another drug or preparation.

title: Fouling Water of Public Spring or Reservoir
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of Offences Affecting the Public Health, Safety, Convenience, Decency and Morals
bare_act_text: Whoever voluntarily corrupts or fouls the water of any public spring or reservoir, so as to render it less fit for the purpose for which it is ordinarily used, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three months, or with fine which may extend to five hundred rupees, or with both.
meaning: Section 277 IPC punishes the intentional contamination of water in public springs or reservoirs.
simple_explanation: Purposely polluting water sources used by the public is a criminal offence.
objective: To protect public water sources and safeguard public health.
legal_significance: The section helps prevent environmental harm and health risks arising from contaminated public water supplies.
essential_elements: The water source is a public spring or reservoir.
The accused voluntarily corrupts or fouls the water.
The contamination reduces its suitability for normal use.

mens_rea.description: The act must be done voluntarily.

examples.scenario: A person intentionally dumps waste into a public reservoir used for drinking water.
examples.result: The person is liable under Section 277 IPC.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 months, or fine up to ₹500, or both
ai_summary: Section 277 IPC punishes voluntary contamination of public water sources that makes them less fit for public use.

title: Making Atmosphere Noxious to Health
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of Offences Affecting the Public Health, Safety, Convenience, Decency and Morals
bare_act_text: Whoever voluntarily vitiates the atmosphere in any place so as to make it noxious to the health of persons in general dwelling or carrying on business in the neighbourhood or passing along a public way, shall be punished with fine which may extend to five hundred rupees.
meaning: Section 278 IPC punishes acts that intentionally pollute the air and make it harmful to public health.
simple_explanation: Deliberately releasing harmful substances into the air that affect people's health is a criminal offence.
objective: To protect public health from harmful air pollution.
legal_significance: The section addresses environmental and public health concerns arising from intentional atmospheric pollution.
essential_elements: The accused voluntarily vitiates the atmosphere.
The atmosphere becomes harmful to health.
The pollution affects persons in the area or on public ways.

mens_rea.description: The act must be voluntary and intentional.

examples.scenario: A factory owner intentionally releases toxic fumes into a residential area without safeguards.
examples.result: The owner may be prosecuted under Section 278 IPC.

max_punishment: Fine up to ₹500
ai_summary: Section 278 IPC punishes voluntary acts that make the atmosphere harmful to public health.

title: Rash Driving or Riding on a Public Way
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of Offences Affecting the Public Health, Safety, Convenience, Decency and Morals
bare_act_text: Whoever drives any vehicle, or rides, on any public way in a manner so rash or negligent as to endanger human life, or to be likely to cause hurt or injury to any other person, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine which may extend to one thousand rupees, or with both.
meaning: Section 279 IPC punishes rash or negligent driving or riding that endangers human life or safety.
simple_explanation: Driving or riding recklessly on a public road in a way that can injure people is a criminal offence.
objective: To ensure road safety and prevent accidents caused by reckless conduct.
legal_significance: This is one of the most commonly invoked IPC provisions in road accident and traffic-related criminal cases.
essential_elements: The accused drives a vehicle or rides on a public way.
The driving or riding is rash or negligent.
Human life is endangered or injury is likely to occur.

mens_rea.description: Criminal rashness or negligence is sufficient; intention to cause harm is not required.

examples.scenario: A driver speeds through a crowded market area and narrowly avoids hitting pedestrians.
examples.result: The driver may be prosecuted under Section 279 IPC.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 6 months, or fine up to ₹1,000, or both
ai_summary: Section 279 IPC punishes rash or negligent driving on public roads that endangers human life or safety.

title: Rash Navigation of Vessel
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of Offences Affecting the Public Health, Safety, Convenience, Decency and Morals
bare_act_text: Whoever navigates any vessel in a manner so rash or negligent as to endanger human life, or to be likely to cause hurt or injury to any other person, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine which may extend to one thousand rupees, or with both.
meaning: Section 280 IPC punishes rash or negligent navigation of vessels that endangers human life or safety.
simple_explanation: Operating a boat, ship, or other vessel recklessly in a way that could injure people is a criminal offence.
objective: To ensure safety in waterways and prevent accidents caused by careless navigation.
legal_significance: The section applies the principle of criminal rashness and negligence to water transport and navigation.
essential_elements: The accused navigates a vessel.
The navigation is rash or negligent.
Human life is endangered or injury is likely.

mens_rea.description: Criminal rashness or negligence is sufficient; intention to cause harm is unnecessary.

examples.scenario: A boat operator recklessly speeds through a crowded waterway, risking collision with other vessels.
examples.result: The operator may be liable under Section 280 IPC.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 6 months, or fine up to ₹1,000, or both
ai_summary: Section 280 IPC punishes rash or negligent navigation of vessels that endangers human life or is likely to cause injury.

title: Exhibition of False Light, Mark or Buoy
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of Offences Affecting the Public Health, Safety, Convenience, Decency and Morals
section_type: Offence Affecting Public Safety and Navigation
bare_act_text: Whoever exhibits any false light, mark or buoy, intending or knowing it to be likely that such exhibition will mislead any navigator, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, or with fine, or with both.
meaning: Section 281 IPC punishes the display of false navigational signals that may mislead navigators.
simple_explanation: Showing a fake lighthouse signal, marker, or buoy that could mislead ships or boats is a serious criminal offence.
objective: To ensure the safety of navigation and prevent maritime accidents caused by deceptive signals.
legal_significance: The section protects vessels and passengers from dangers arising from false navigational guidance.
essential_elements: A false light, mark, or buoy is exhibited.
The accused intends or knows it is likely to mislead a navigator.
The act creates a risk to navigation.

mens_rea.description: Intention or knowledge that the signal is likely to mislead navigators is necessary.

examples.scenario: A person places a fake navigation light near a shoreline causing vessels to alter course dangerously.
examples.result: The person is liable under Section 281 IPC.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years, or fine, or both
ai_summary: Section 281 IPC punishes the exhibition of false navigational signals intended or likely to mislead navigators.

title: Conveying Person by Water for Hire in Unsafe or Overloaded Vessel
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of Offences Affecting the Public Health, Safety, Convenience, Decency and Morals
bare_act_text: Whoever knowingly or negligently conveys, or causes to be conveyed for hire, any person by water in any vessel, when that vessel is in such a state or so loaded as to endanger the life of that person, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine which may extend to one thousand rupees, or with both.
meaning: Section 282 IPC punishes transporting passengers for hire in an unsafe or overloaded vessel.
simple_explanation: Taking passengers in a boat that is unsafe or overloaded and thereby endangering their lives is a crime.
objective: To protect passengers from risks arising from unsafe water transport.
legal_significance: The section imposes criminal liability on operators who ignore vessel safety standards.
essential_elements: A person is conveyed by water for hire.
The vessel is unsafe or overloaded.
Life is endangered.
The act is done knowingly or negligently.

mens_rea.description: Knowledge or negligence regarding the unsafe condition of the vessel is sufficient.

examples.scenario: A ferry operator allows twice the permitted number of passengers onboard.
examples.result: The operator may be prosecuted under Section 282 IPC.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 6 months, or fine up to ₹1,000, or both
ai_summary: Section 282 IPC punishes carrying passengers for hire in unsafe or overloaded vessels.

title: Danger or Obstruction in Public Way or Line of Navigation
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of Offences Affecting the Public Health, Safety, Convenience, Decency and Morals
bare_act_text: Whoever, by doing any act, or by omitting to take order with any property in his possession or under his charge, causes danger, obstruction or injury to any person in any public way or public line of navigation, shall be punished with fine which may extend to two hundred rupees.
meaning: Section 283 IPC punishes acts or omissions causing danger or obstruction in public ways or navigational routes.
simple_explanation: Blocking a road, pathway, or navigation route and causing danger to others is an offence.
objective: To ensure safe and unobstructed use of public roads and navigation routes.
legal_significance: The section protects public convenience and safety in common pathways.
essential_elements: An act or omission occurs.
Danger, obstruction, or injury is caused.
The location is a public way or line of navigation.

mens_rea.description: Liability may arise from negligent omission even without criminal intent.

examples.scenario: A contractor leaves construction materials blocking a public road.
examples.result: The contractor may be liable under Section 283 IPC.

max_punishment: Fine up to ₹200
ai_summary: Section 283 IPC punishes acts or omissions causing danger or obstruction in public ways or navigation routes.

title: Negligent Conduct with Respect to Poisonous Substance
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of Offences Affecting the Public Health, Safety, Convenience, Decency and Morals
bare_act_text: Whoever does, with any poisonous substance, any act in a manner so rash or negligent as to endanger human life, or to be likely to cause hurt or injury to any person, or knowingly or negligently omits to take such order with any poisonous substance in his possession as is sufficient to guard against any probable danger to human life from such poisonous substance, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine which may extend to one thousand rupees, or with both.
meaning: Section 284 IPC punishes rash or negligent handling of poisonous substances.
simple_explanation: Carelessly using or storing poisonous substances in a way that endangers people is a crime.
objective: To protect human life from risks associated with poisonous substances.
legal_significance: The section imposes a duty of care on persons handling toxic materials.
essential_elements: A poisonous substance is involved.
The accused acts rashly or negligently, or fails to take proper precautions.
Human life is endangered or injury is likely.

mens_rea.description: Criminal negligence or rashness is sufficient.

examples.scenario: A chemical dealer leaves toxic substances unsecured where children can access them.
examples.result: The dealer may be prosecuted under Section 284 IPC.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 6 months, or fine up to ₹1,000, or both
ai_summary: Section 284 IPC punishes negligent handling or storage of poisonous substances that endanger human life.

title: Negligent Conduct with Respect to Fire or Combustible Matter
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of Offences Affecting the Public Health, Safety, Convenience, Decency and Morals
bare_act_text: Whoever does, with fire or any combustible matter, any act so rashly or negligently as to endanger human life, or to be likely to cause hurt or injury to any other person, or knowingly or negligently omits to take such order with any fire or combustible matter in his possession as is sufficient to guard against probable danger to human life, shall be punished with imprisonment up to six months, or fine up to one thousand rupees, or both.
meaning: Section 285 IPC punishes negligent handling of fire or combustible substances.
simple_explanation: Carelessly dealing with fire or flammable materials and thereby endangering people is a criminal offence.
objective: To prevent fires and accidents that threaten human life.
legal_significance: The section imposes a legal duty to handle fire and combustible materials safely.
essential_elements: Fire or combustible matter is involved.
The conduct is rash or negligent.
Human life is endangered or injury is likely.

mens_rea.description: Criminal rashness or negligence is sufficient.

examples.scenario: A person stores fuel near an open flame without safety precautions.
examples.result: The person may be liable under Section 285 IPC.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 6 months, or fine up to ₹1,000, or both
ai_summary: Section 285 IPC punishes negligent handling of fire or combustible materials endangering human life.

title: Negligent Conduct with Respect to Explosive Substance
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of Offences Affecting the Public Health, Safety, Convenience, Decency and Morals
bare_act_text: Whoever does, with any explosive substance, any act so rashly or negligently as to endanger human life, or to be likely to cause hurt or injury to any other person, or knowingly or negligently omits to take such order with any explosive substance in his possession as is sufficient to guard against any probable danger to human life from that substance, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine which may extend to one thousand rupees, or with both.
meaning: Section 286 IPC punishes rash or negligent handling of explosive substances that may endanger human life.
simple_explanation: Carelessly storing, using, or handling explosives in a way that could harm people is a criminal offence.
objective: To protect human life and property from dangers arising from improper handling of explosive substances.
legal_significance: The section imposes a duty of care on persons possessing or dealing with explosives and criminalizes negligent conduct that creates risks.
essential_elements: An explosive substance is involved.
The accused acts rashly or negligently, or fails to take proper precautions.
Human life is endangered or injury is likely.

mens_rea.description: Criminal rashness or negligence is sufficient; intention to cause harm is not necessary.

examples.scenario: A fireworks dealer stores large quantities of explosives without safety measures, creating a risk of explosion.
examples.result: The dealer may be prosecuted under Section 286 IPC.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 6 months, or fine up to ₹1,000, or both
ai_summary: Section 286 IPC punishes negligent handling or storage of explosive substances that endanger human life.

title: Negligent Conduct with Respect to Machinery
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of Offences Affecting the Public Health, Safety, Convenience, Decency and Morals
bare_act_text: Whoever does, with any machinery, any act so rashly or negligently as to endanger human life or to be likely to cause hurt or injury to any other person, or knowingly or negligently omits to take such order with any machinery in his possession or under his care as is sufficient to guard against any probable danger to human life from such machinery, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine which may extend to one thousand rupees, or with both.
meaning: Section 287 IPC punishes rash or negligent handling, operation, or maintenance of machinery that endangers human life.
simple_explanation: Using or maintaining machines carelessly in a way that can injure people is a criminal offence.
objective: To ensure workplace and public safety by requiring proper care in the use of machinery.
legal_significance: The section places legal responsibility on owners, operators, and supervisors to maintain machinery safely.
essential_elements: Machinery is involved.
The accused acts rashly or negligently or fails to take adequate precautions.
Human life is endangered or injury is likely.

mens_rea.description: Criminal rashness or negligence is sufficient.

examples.scenario: A factory owner allows workers to operate dangerous machinery without protective guards.
examples.result: The owner may be liable under Section 287 IPC.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 6 months, or fine up to ₹1,000, or both
ai_summary: Section 287 IPC punishes negligent conduct involving machinery that creates danger to human life or safety.

title: Negligent Conduct with Respect to Pulling Down or Repairing Buildings
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of Offences Affecting the Public Health, Safety, Convenience, Decency and Morals
bare_act_text: Whoever, in pulling down or repairing any building, knowingly or negligently omits to take such order with that building as is sufficient to guard against any probable danger to human life from the fall of that building, or of any part thereof, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine which may extend to one thousand rupees, or with both.
meaning: Section 288 IPC punishes failure to take proper safety precautions while demolishing or repairing buildings.
simple_explanation: Neglecting safety measures during construction, demolition, or repair work that may endanger people is a criminal offence.
objective: To prevent accidents and injuries caused by unsafe building repair or demolition activities.
legal_significance: The section imposes a duty of care on builders, contractors, and property owners engaged in such work.
essential_elements: A building is being repaired or demolished.
Necessary safety measures are omitted.
The omission is knowing or negligent.
Human life is endangered.

mens_rea.description: Knowledge or negligence regarding safety precautions is sufficient.

examples.scenario: A contractor demolishes a building without installing safety barriers, causing debris to fall near pedestrians.
examples.result: The contractor may be prosecuted under Section 288 IPC.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 6 months, or fine up to ₹1,000, or both
ai_summary: Section 288 IPC punishes negligent failure to ensure safety while repairing or demolishing buildings.

title: Negligent Conduct with Respect to Animal
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of Offences Affecting the Public Health, Safety, Convenience, Decency and Morals
bare_act_text: Whoever knowingly or negligently omits to take such order with any animal in his possession as is sufficient to guard against any probable danger to human life, or any probable danger of grievous hurt from such animal, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine which may extend to one thousand rupees, or with both.
meaning: Section 289 IPC punishes failure to control or restrain animals that may pose a danger to people.
simple_explanation: If a person carelessly fails to control a dangerous animal and people are put at risk, it is a criminal offence.
objective: To protect the public from injuries caused by dangerous or uncontrolled animals.
legal_significance: The section imposes responsibility on animal owners and custodians to prevent foreseeable harm.
essential_elements: The accused has possession or control of an animal.
The animal poses a probable danger to human life or may cause grievous hurt.
The accused knowingly or negligently fails to take proper precautions.

mens_rea.description: Knowledge or negligence in controlling the animal is sufficient.

examples.scenario: An owner knowingly allows a violent dog to roam freely in a crowded public area without restraint.
examples.result: The owner may be liable under Section 289 IPC.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 6 months, or fine up to ₹1,000, or both
ai_summary: Section 289 IPC punishes negligent failure to control animals that may endanger human life or cause grievous injury.

title: Punishment for Public Nuisance in Cases Not Otherwise Provided For
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of Offences Affecting the Public Health, Safety, Convenience, Decency and Morals
bare_act_text: Whoever commits a public nuisance in any case not otherwise punishable by this Code, shall be punished with fine which may extend to two hundred rupees.
meaning: Section 290 IPC provides punishment for acts constituting public nuisance when no specific punishment is provided elsewhere in the Code.
simple_explanation: If a person creates a public nuisance and no other IPC section specifically punishes that act, this section applies.
objective: To ensure that all forms of public nuisance remain punishable even when not covered by a specific offence.
legal_significance: This is a residual penal provision that supplements the definition of public nuisance contained in Section 268 IPC.
essential_elements: A public nuisance exists as defined under Section 268 IPC.
No other IPC provision specifically punishes the conduct.
The accused is responsible for the nuisance.

mens_rea.description: Liability generally arises from the commission of the nuisance itself.

examples.scenario: A person repeatedly creates an obstruction causing inconvenience to the public, but no specific IPC offence applies.
examples.result: The person may be punished under Section 290 IPC.

max_punishment: Fine up to ₹200
ai_summary: Section 290 IPC provides a general punishment for public nuisances not specifically punishable under any other IPC provision.

title: Continuance of Nuisance After Injunction to Discontinue
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of Offences Affecting the Public Health, Safety, Convenience, Decency and Morals
bare_act_text: Whoever repeats or continues a public nuisance, having been enjoined by any public servant who has lawful authority to issue such injunction not to repeat or continue such nuisance, shall be punished with simple imprisonment for a term which may extend to six months, or with fine, or with both.
meaning: Section 291 IPC punishes a person who continues or repeats a public nuisance despite being legally ordered to stop it.
simple_explanation: If a government authority orders someone to stop creating a public nuisance and the person continues it, he commits an offence.
objective: To ensure compliance with lawful orders aimed at removing public nuisances.
legal_significance: The section strengthens the enforcement of public nuisance laws by penalizing disobedience of official injunctions.
essential_elements: A public nuisance exists.
A lawful injunction has been issued by a competent public servant.
The accused repeats or continues the nuisance despite the injunction.

mens_rea.description: The accused must knowingly continue or repeat the nuisance after receiving the injunction.

examples.scenario: A factory continues releasing excessive smoke after being ordered by authorities to stop the nuisance.
examples.result: The owner may be prosecuted under Section 291 IPC.

max_punishment: Simple imprisonment up to 6 months, or fine, or both
ai_summary: Section 291 IPC punishes continuation of a public nuisance after a lawful order has been issued to discontinue it.

title: Sale, etc., of Obscene Books, etc.
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of Offences Affecting the Public Health, Safety, Convenience, Decency and Morals
bare_act_text: Whoever sells, lets to hire, distributes, publicly exhibits, circulates, imports, exports, advertises, produces, possesses for circulation, or otherwise deals with obscene books, pamphlets, papers, drawings, paintings, representations, figures, or other obscene objects shall be punished as provided under this section.
meaning: Section 292 IPC criminalizes the sale, distribution, circulation, advertisement, import, export, and possession for circulation of obscene materials.
simple_explanation: Selling, distributing, displaying, advertising, or circulating obscene content is a criminal offence.
objective: To protect public morality and prevent the spread of obscene materials.
legal_significance: The section provides the legal definition of obscenity and penalizes commercial and public dealings in obscene materials.
essential_elements: The material is legally obscene.
The accused sells, distributes, circulates, exhibits, imports, exports, advertises, or possesses it for such purposes.
The act falls outside statutory exceptions.

mens_rea.description: Knowledge or reason to believe regarding the nature of the obscene material is generally required.

aggravated_case.imprisonment: Subsequent conviction: Up to 5 years

examples.scenario: A person sells obscene magazines to the public through a shop.
examples.result: The person may be prosecuted under Section 292 IPC.

max_punishment: Up to 5 years imprisonment and fine up to ₹5,000
ai_summary: Section 292 IPC punishes the sale, circulation, exhibition, import, export, and advertisement of obscene materials, subject to certain exceptions.

title: Sale, etc., of Obscene Objects to Young Person
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of Offences Affecting the Public Health, Safety, Convenience, Decency and Morals
bare_act_text: Whoever sells, lets to hire, distributes, exhibits or circulates to any person under the age of twenty years any obscene object referred to in Section 292, or offers or attempts to do so, shall be punished as provided under this section.
meaning: Section 293 IPC punishes supplying obscene materials to persons below twenty years of age.
simple_explanation: Giving, selling, showing, or distributing obscene materials to a person under 20 years old is a criminal offence.
objective: To protect young persons from exposure to obscene materials.
legal_significance: The section imposes stricter punishment than Section 292 due to the involvement of young persons.
essential_elements: The object is obscene within the meaning of Section 292.
The recipient is below 20 years of age.
The accused sells, distributes, exhibits, circulates, or attempts to do so.

mens_rea.description: Knowledge regarding the nature of the obscene material is generally required.

aggravated_case.imprisonment: Subsequent conviction: Up to 7 years

examples.scenario: A shopkeeper knowingly sells obscene magazines to a 17-year-old customer.
examples.result: The shopkeeper is liable under Section 293 IPC.

max_punishment: Up to 7 years imprisonment and fine up to ₹5,000
ai_summary: Section 293 IPC punishes the sale or distribution of obscene materials to persons below twenty years of age.

title: Obscene Acts and Songs
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of Offences Affecting the Public Health, Safety, Convenience, Decency and Morals
bare_act_text: Whoever, to the annoyance of others, does any obscene act in any public place, or sings, recites, or utters any obscene song, ballad or words in or near any public place, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three months, or with fine, or with both.
meaning: Section 294 IPC punishes obscene acts, songs, or words performed in or near public places causing annoyance to others.
simple_explanation: Performing obscene acts or using obscene words or songs in public that annoy people is a criminal offence.
objective: To maintain public decency and prevent annoyance caused by obscene conduct.
legal_significance: The section addresses public obscenity and protects public morality and order.
essential_elements: An obscene act, song, ballad, or words are involved.
The conduct occurs in or near a public place.
The conduct causes annoyance to others.

mens_rea.description: The act must be voluntary; annoyance to others is a necessary consequence.

examples.scenario: A person publicly performs obscene acts in a crowded marketplace causing annoyance to bystanders.
examples.result: The person may be prosecuted under Section 294 IPC.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 months, or fine, or both
ai_summary: Section 294 IPC punishes obscene acts and obscene songs or words performed in public places causing annoyance.

title: Keeping Lottery Office
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of Offences Affecting the Public Health, Safety, Convenience, Decency and Morals
bare_act_text: Whoever keeps any office or place for the purpose of drawing any lottery not being a State lottery or a lottery authorized by the State Government, or publishes any proposal relating to such lottery, shall be punished as provided under this section.
meaning: Section 294A IPC punishes keeping unauthorized lottery offices and promoting unauthorized lotteries.
simple_explanation: Running an illegal lottery office or advertising an unauthorized lottery is a criminal offence.
objective: To regulate lotteries and prevent illegal gambling activities.
legal_significance: The section distinguishes lawful State-authorized lotteries from illegal lotteries.
essential_elements: A lottery office or place is maintained.
The lottery is not authorized by law.
Or the accused publishes proposals relating to such lottery.

mens_rea.description: Knowledge and intentional participation in the unauthorized lottery activity are generally present.

examples.scenario: A person operates an illegal lottery center and sells unauthorized lottery tickets.
examples.result: The person may be prosecuted under Section 294A IPC.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 6 months, or fine, or both
ai_summary: Section 294A IPC punishes keeping unauthorized lottery offices and promoting unlawful lotteries.

title: Injuring or Defiling Place of Worship, with Intent to Insult the Religion of Any Class
chapter: Chapter XV - Of Offences Relating to Religion
bare_act_text: Whoever destroys, damages or defiles any place of worship, or any object held sacred by any class of persons, with the intention of thereby insulting the religion of any class of persons or with the knowledge that any class of persons is likely to consider such destruction, damage or defilement as an insult to their religion, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.
meaning: Section 295 IPC punishes acts that damage or defile places of worship or sacred objects with intent to insult religion.
simple_explanation: Damaging a temple, mosque, church, shrine, or sacred object to insult a religion is a criminal offence.
objective: To protect religious harmony and safeguard places of worship and sacred objects.
legal_significance: The section protects religious sentiments and prevents communal tensions arising from attacks on religious institutions.
essential_elements: A place of worship or sacred object is damaged, destroyed, or defiled.
The act is intentional.
The intention is to insult religion or knowledge exists that such insult is likely.

mens_rea.description: Intent to insult religion or knowledge that the act is likely to be perceived as an insult is essential.

examples.scenario: A person intentionally vandalizes a religious shrine to insult followers of that religion.
examples.result: The person may be prosecuted under Section 295 IPC.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both
ai_summary: Section 295 IPC punishes destruction, damage, or defilement of places of worship or sacred objects intended to insult religion.

title: Deliberate and Malicious Acts Intended to Outrage Religious Feelings
chapter: Chapter XV - Of Offences Relating to Religion
bare_act_text: Whoever, with deliberate and malicious intention of outraging the religious feelings of any class of citizens of India, by words, either spoken or written, or by signs or by visible representations or otherwise, insults or attempts to insult the religion or religious beliefs of that class, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both.
meaning: Section 295A IPC punishes deliberate and malicious acts intended to outrage the religious feelings of a class of citizens.
simple_explanation: Intentionally insulting a religion or religious beliefs with the purpose of offending followers is a criminal offence.
objective: To maintain communal harmony and prevent deliberate attacks on religious sentiments.
legal_significance: The section applies only to deliberate and malicious acts, not to genuine academic, historical, or good-faith criticism.
essential_elements: Words, signs, representations, or other acts are used.
Religion or religious beliefs are insulted or attempted to be insulted.
The act is deliberate and malicious.
The intention is to outrage religious feelings.

mens_rea.description: Deliberate and malicious intention is the core requirement of the offence.

examples.scenario: A person intentionally publishes material solely to provoke and outrage followers of a religion.
examples.result: The person may be prosecuted under Section 295A IPC.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 years, or fine, or both
ai_summary: Section 295A IPC punishes deliberate and malicious acts intended to outrage the religious feelings of any class of citizens.

title: Disturbing Religious Assembly
chapter: Chapter XV - Of Offences Relating to Religion
bare_act_text: Whoever voluntarily causes disturbance to any assembly lawfully engaged in the performance of religious worship, or religious ceremonies, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to one year, or with fine, or with both.
meaning: Section 296 IPC punishes intentional disturbance of lawful religious worship or ceremonies.
simple_explanation: Disrupting a religious gathering or ceremony intentionally is a criminal offence.
objective: To ensure peaceful conduct of religious worship and ceremonies.
legal_significance: The section protects the right of people to perform religious worship without unlawful interference.
essential_elements: A lawful religious assembly exists.
The assembly is engaged in worship or religious ceremonies.
The accused voluntarily causes disturbance.

mens_rea.description: The disturbance must be voluntary and intentional.

examples.scenario: A person intentionally interrupts a religious ceremony by creating disorder and noise.
examples.result: The person may be prosecuted under Section 296 IPC.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 1 year, or fine, or both
ai_summary: Section 296 IPC punishes voluntary disturbance of assemblies lawfully engaged in religious worship or ceremonies.

title: Trespassing on Burial Places, etc.
chapter: Chapter XV - Of Offences Relating to Religion
bare_act_text: Whoever, with the intention of wounding the feelings of any person, or of insulting the religion of any person or with the knowledge that the feelings of any person are likely to be wounded, or that the religion of any person is likely to be insulted thereby, commits any trespass in any place of worship or on any place of sepulture, or any place set apart for the performance of funeral rites or as a depository for the remains of the dead, or offers any indignity to any human corpse, or causes disturbance to any persons assembled for the performance of funeral ceremonies, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to one year, or with fine, or with both.
meaning: Section 297 IPC punishes trespass and disrespectful acts in burial places, places of worship, funeral sites, and towards human corpses when done with intent to wound religious or personal feelings.
simple_explanation: Entering burial grounds or disturbing funeral ceremonies to insult religion or hurt people's feelings is a criminal offence.
objective: To protect the dignity of the dead, funeral ceremonies, and religious sentiments associated with burial places.
legal_significance: The section safeguards religious and emotional sentiments connected with death and funeral rites.
essential_elements: Trespass or indignity to a corpse or funeral ceremony.
The act occurs at a place of worship, burial, or funeral rites.
Intent to wound feelings or insult religion, or knowledge of likely insult.

mens_rea.description: Intention to wound feelings or insult religion, or knowledge of likely consequences, is required.
