examples.scenario: A person intentionally disrupts a funeral ceremony to insult the deceased's community.
examples.result: The person is liable under Section 297 IPC.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 1 year, or fine, or both
ai_summary: Section 297 IPC punishes trespass and disrespectful acts at burial places, funeral sites, and towards human remains intended to insult religion or wound feelings.

title: Uttering Words, etc., with Deliberate Intent to Wound Religious Feelings
chapter: Chapter XV - Of Offences Relating to Religion
bare_act_text: Whoever, with the deliberate intention of wounding the religious feelings of any person, utters any word or makes any sound in the hearing of that person or makes any gesture in the sight of that person or places any object in the sight of that person, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to one year, or with fine, or with both.
meaning: Section 298 IPC punishes deliberate acts, words, sounds, gestures, or objects intended to hurt the religious feelings of another person.
simple_explanation: Intentionally insulting someone's religious feelings through words, gestures, sounds, or objects is a criminal offence.
objective: To prevent deliberate personal attacks on religious sentiments.
legal_significance: Unlike Section 295A, this section focuses on individuals rather than a class of persons.
essential_elements: Words, sounds, gestures, or objects are used.
The act is directed at a person.
There is deliberate intention to wound religious feelings.

mens_rea.description: Deliberate intention to wound religious feelings is essential.

examples.scenario: A person intentionally uses insulting religious gestures towards another individual to offend their faith.
examples.result: The person may be prosecuted under Section 298 IPC.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 1 year, or fine, or both
ai_summary: Section 298 IPC punishes deliberate words, gestures, sounds, or objects intended to wound another person's religious feelings.

title: Culpable Homicide
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
bare_act_text: Whoever causes death by doing an act with the intention of causing death, or with the intention of causing such bodily injury as is likely to cause death, or with the knowledge that he is likely by such act to cause death, commits the offence of culpable homicide.
meaning: Section 299 IPC defines culpable homicide as causing the death of a person through an act done with intention or knowledge that death is likely to occur.
simple_explanation: A person commits culpable homicide when he causes someone's death intending to kill, intending to cause a dangerous injury likely to cause death, or knowing that his act is likely to cause death.
objective: To define unlawful killings involving intention or knowledge regarding the likelihood of death.
legal_significance: Section 299 forms the foundation of homicide law in India and distinguishes unlawful killings involving intention or knowledge from accidental deaths. It serves as the basis for Sections 300, 302 and 304 IPC.
essential_elements: Death of a person must occur.
The death must be caused by the act of the accused.
The act must be accompanied by intention to cause death, intention to cause bodily injury likely to cause death, or knowledge that death is likely.

mens_rea.description: Intention to cause death, intention to cause bodily injury likely to cause death, or knowledge that death is likely.

normal_case.imprisonment: Punishable under Section 304 IPC depending on circumstances

examples.scenario: A intentionally stabs B in a vital organ knowing that the injury is likely to cause death and B dies.
examples.result: A commits culpable homicide under Section 299 IPC.

max_punishment: Life imprisonment under Section 304 Part I
bailable: Depends on the applicable punishment provision
ai_summary: Section 299 IPC defines culpable homicide as causing death with intention or knowledge that death is likely to result and forms the basis of homicide offences under IPC.

title: Murder
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
bare_act_text: Except in the cases hereinafter excepted, culpable homicide is murder if the act by which death is caused is done with the intention of causing death, or with the intention or knowledge specified in this section.
meaning: Section 300 IPC defines when culpable homicide becomes murder due to a higher degree of intention or knowledge.
simple_explanation: A killing becomes murder when it is committed with a strong intention to cause death or such serious injury that death is almost certain, unless one of the statutory exceptions applies.
objective: To distinguish the most serious forms of unlawful killing from other forms of culpable homicide.
legal_significance: Section 300 is the principal provision defining murder and contains four clauses and five exceptions that determine whether a homicide is punishable as murder under Section 302 or as culpable homicide under Section 304.
essential_elements: Death of a person must occur.
The act must satisfy one of the clauses defining murder.
None of the statutory exceptions should apply.

mens_rea.description: A high degree of intention or knowledge regarding death or fatal bodily injury is required.

aggravated_case.punishment: Death penalty or life imprisonment under Section 302 IPC

examples.scenario: A intentionally shoots B with the aim of killing him and B dies.
examples.result: A commits murder under Section 300 IPC.

max_punishment: Death penalty under Section 302 IPC
ai_summary: Section 300 IPC defines murder as the gravest form of culpable homicide involving a higher degree of intention or knowledge, subject to five statutory exceptions.

title: Culpable Homicide by Causing Death of Person Other Than Person Whose Death Was Intended
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
bare_act_text: If a person, by doing anything which he intends or knows to be likely to cause death, commits culpable homicide by causing the death of any person whose death he neither intends nor knows himself to be likely to cause, the culpable homicide committed is of the description which it would have been if he had caused the death of the person whose death he intended or knew himself likely to cause.
meaning: Section 301 IPC applies the doctrine of transferred malice, making a person liable when an intended harmful act kills someone other than the intended victim.
simple_explanation: If a person intends to kill one person but accidentally kills another, the law treats it as if the intended victim had been killed.
objective: To prevent offenders from escaping liability merely because a different person died.
legal_significance: This section embodies the principle of transferred intention or transferred malice.
essential_elements: An act is done with intention or knowledge likely to cause death.
A different person dies than the intended target.
Death results from the accused's act.

mens_rea.description: The original intention or knowledge transfers to the actual victim.

normal_case.imprisonment: Depends on whether the act amounts to murder or culpable homicide.

examples.scenario: A shoots at B intending to kill him but misses and kills C.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 301 IPC.

max_punishment: Depends on the resulting offence
ai_summary: Section 301 IPC applies transferred malice, making an offender liable when a person other than the intended victim dies.

title: Punishment for Murder
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
bare_act_text: Whoever commits murder shall be punished with death or imprisonment for life, and shall also be liable to fine.
meaning: Section 302 IPC prescribes punishment for the offence of murder.
simple_explanation: A person convicted of murder may be punished with death or life imprisonment and fine.
objective: To punish the gravest offences against human life.
legal_significance: It is the principal punishment provision for murder under IPC.
essential_elements: Murder as defined under Section 300 IPC.
Commission of the offence by the accused.

mens_rea.description: The mental elements are derived from Section 300 IPC.

examples.scenario: A intentionally shoots and kills B with premeditation.
examples.result: A may be punished under Section 302 IPC.

max_punishment: Death penalty
ai_summary: Section 302 IPC prescribes death or life imprisonment and fine for the offence of murder.

title: Punishment for Murder by Life-Convict
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
bare_act_text: Whoever, being under sentence of imprisonment for life, commits murder, shall be punished with death.
meaning: Section 303 IPC provided mandatory death penalty for murder committed by a life convict.
simple_explanation: A life convict committing murder was earlier punishable only with death.
objective: To impose stricter punishment on life convicts committing murder.
legal_significance: This section was declared unconstitutional by the Supreme Court in Mithu v. State of Punjab (1983) and is no longer enforceable.
essential_elements: The accused is serving life imprisonment.
The accused commits murder.

mens_rea.description: Mens rea for murder under Section 300 IPC applies.

examples.scenario: A life convict commits murder while serving his sentence.
examples.result: Section 303 historically applied but is now unconstitutional.

max_punishment: Death (historically)
ai_summary: Section 303 IPC prescribed mandatory death penalty for murder by a life convict but has been declared unconstitutional.

title: Punishment for Culpable Homicide Not Amounting to Murder
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
bare_act_text: Whoever commits culpable homicide not amounting to murder shall be punished with imprisonment for life, or imprisonment up to ten years and fine if committed with intention; or imprisonment up to ten years, or fine, or both if committed with knowledge but without intention.
meaning: Section 304 IPC prescribes punishment for culpable homicide that does not amount to murder.
simple_explanation: If a person's act causes death but falls short of murder, punishment is given under Section 304.
objective: To distinguish less serious forms of unlawful killing from murder.
legal_significance: The section is divided into Part I (intention) and Part II (knowledge without intention).
essential_elements: Culpable homicide has occurred.
The act does not amount to murder under Section 300.
The accused acted with intention or knowledge.

mens_rea.description: Part I requires intention; Part II requires knowledge without intention.

part_i.imprisonment: Life imprisonment or up to 10 years

examples.scenario: A causes death during a sudden fight without premeditation, attracting an exception to murder.
examples.result: A may be punished under Section 304 IPC.

max_punishment: Life imprisonment and fine
ai_summary: Section 304 IPC provides punishment for culpable homicide not amounting to murder and distinguishes between intention and knowledge-based liability.

title: Causing Death by Negligence
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
bare_act_text: Whoever causes the death of any person by doing any rash or negligent act not amounting to culpable homicide, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.
meaning: Section 304A IPC punishes causing death through a rash or negligent act that does not amount to culpable homicide.
simple_explanation: If a person causes someone's death due to carelessness or negligence, without intention or knowledge required for culpable homicide, he commits an offence under this section.
objective: To punish deaths caused by negligence and promote reasonable care in activities affecting human life.
legal_significance: Section 304A covers accidental deaths caused by criminal negligence and is commonly applied in road accident and medical negligence cases.
essential_elements: Death of a person must occur.
The death must be caused by a rash or negligent act.
The act must not amount to culpable homicide.

mens_rea.description: Criminal rashness or negligence is sufficient; intention to cause death is absent.

examples.scenario: A driver negligently drives at excessive speed and causes a fatal accident.
examples.result: The driver may be prosecuted under Section 304A IPC.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both
ai_summary: Section 304A IPC punishes causing death through rash or negligent acts that do not amount to culpable homicide.

title: Dowry Death
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
bare_act_text: Where the death of a woman is caused by burns, bodily injury or occurs otherwise than under normal circumstances within seven years of her marriage and it is shown that soon before her death she was subjected to cruelty or harassment by her husband or his relatives in connection with a demand for dowry, such death shall be called dowry death.
meaning: Section 304B IPC defines and punishes dowry death.
simple_explanation: If a married woman dies under abnormal circumstances within seven years of marriage and was harassed for dowry shortly before her death, it is treated as a dowry death.
objective: To prevent dowry-related harassment and deaths of married women.
legal_significance: The section creates a legal presumption against the husband and relatives when the required conditions are established.
essential_elements: Death of a woman by burns, bodily injury, or abnormal circumstances.
Death occurs within seven years of marriage.
Cruelty or harassment for dowry occurred soon before death.
The harassment was by husband or his relatives.

mens_rea.description: Cruelty or harassment connected with dowry demand must be established.

aggravated_case.imprisonment: Life imprisonment

examples.scenario: A woman dies from burns within three years of marriage after repeated harassment for additional dowry.
examples.result: The husband and responsible relatives may be prosecuted under Section 304B IPC.

max_punishment: Life imprisonment
ai_summary: Section 304B IPC punishes dowry deaths occurring within seven years of marriage following dowry-related cruelty or harassment.

title: Abetment of Suicide of Child or Insane Person
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
bare_act_text: If any person under eighteen years of age, any insane person, any delirious person, any idiot, or any person in a state of intoxication, commits suicide, whoever abets the commission of such suicide shall be punished with death or imprisonment for life, or imprisonment up to ten years, and fine.
meaning: Section 305 IPC punishes abetment of suicide by vulnerable persons such as minors, insane persons, or intoxicated persons.
simple_explanation: Helping, encouraging, or instigating a child or mentally incapable person to commit suicide is a very serious offence.
objective: To provide enhanced protection to vulnerable individuals.
legal_significance: The section prescribes a harsher punishment than ordinary abetment of suicide under Section 306 IPC.
essential_elements: Suicide is committed.
The deceased belongs to a protected category.
The accused abetted the suicide.

mens_rea.description: Intentional abetment of the suicide is required.

examples.scenario: A person repeatedly instigates a minor to commit suicide and the minor does so.
examples.result: The offender may be prosecuted under Section 305 IPC.

max_punishment: Death penalty
ai_summary: Section 305 IPC punishes abetment of suicide of minors and other vulnerable persons with severe penalties including death or life imprisonment.

title: Abetment of Suicide
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
bare_act_text: If any person commits suicide, whoever abets the commission of such suicide shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.
meaning: Section 306 IPC punishes a person who abets or instigates another to commit suicide.
simple_explanation: Encouraging, aiding, or provoking someone to commit suicide is a criminal offence.
objective: To punish those who intentionally contribute to another person's suicide.
legal_significance: Proof of abetment under Sections 107 and 108 IPC is necessary for conviction.
essential_elements: A person commits suicide.
The accused abetted the commission of suicide.
There is a direct connection between the abetment and the suicide.

mens_rea.description: Intentional instigation, conspiracy, or aid is required.

examples.scenario: A person continuously instigates another individual to end his life and the individual commits suicide.
examples.result: The offender may be liable under Section 306 IPC.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 10 years and fine
ai_summary: Section 306 IPC punishes abetment of suicide with imprisonment up to ten years and fine.

title: Abetment of Suicide
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
bare_act_text: If any person commits suicide, whoever abets the commission of such suicide shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.
meaning: Section 306 IPC punishes a person who abets or instigates another to commit suicide.
simple_explanation: Encouraging, aiding, or provoking someone to commit suicide is a criminal offence.
objective: To punish those who intentionally contribute to another person's suicide.
legal_significance: Proof of abetment under Sections 107 and 108 IPC is necessary for conviction.
essential_elements: A person commits suicide.
The accused abetted the commission of suicide.
There is a direct connection between the abetment and the suicide.

mens_rea.description: Intentional instigation, conspiracy, or aid is required.

examples.scenario: A person continuously instigates another individual to end his life and the individual commits suicide.
examples.result: The offender may be liable under Section 306 IPC.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 10 years and fine
ai_summary: Section 306 IPC punishes abetment of suicide with imprisonment up to ten years and fine.

title: Attempt to Commit Culpable Homicide
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
bare_act_text: Whoever does any act with such intention or knowledge and under such circumstances that, if he by that act caused death, he would be guilty of culpable homicide not amounting to murder, shall be punished with imprisonment up to three years, or fine, or both; and if hurt is caused, with imprisonment up to seven years, or fine, or both.
meaning: Section 308 IPC punishes attempts to commit culpable homicide not amounting to murder.
simple_explanation: If a person attempts an act that could have resulted in culpable homicide, but death does not occur, he may be punished under this section.
objective: To punish dangerous acts likely to cause death that fall short of murder.
legal_significance: The section is the counterpart of Section 307 IPC for culpable homicide not amounting to murder.
essential_elements: An act is committed.
The act is done with intention or knowledge constituting culpable homicide.
Death does not occur.

mens_rea.description: Intention or knowledge sufficient for culpable homicide.

aggravated_case.imprisonment: Up to 7 years if hurt is caused

examples.scenario: A fires at Z during a sudden fight, but Z survives.
examples.result: A may be liable under Section 308 IPC.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine
ai_summary: Section 308 IPC punishes attempts to commit culpable homicide not amounting to murder.

title: Attempt to Commit Suicide
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
bare_act_text: Whoever attempts to commit suicide and does any act towards the commission of such offence shall be punished with simple imprisonment for a term which may extend to one year, or with fine, or with both.
meaning: Section 309 IPC punishes an attempt to commit suicide.
simple_explanation: A person who tries to end his own life and performs an act towards that purpose may be punished under this section.
objective: To address attempts at self-destruction and provide legal intervention.
legal_significance: The operation of this section has been substantially affected by the Mental Healthcare Act, 2017, which presumes severe stress in suicide attempts.
essential_elements: An attempt to commit suicide.
An act is done towards carrying out the attempt.

mens_rea.description: Intention to commit suicide is essential.

examples.scenario: A person consumes poison intending to end his life but survives.
examples.result: The act falls within Section 309 IPC.

max_punishment: Simple imprisonment up to 1 year, or fine, or both
ai_summary: Section 309 IPC punishes attempts to commit suicide.

title: Thug
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
bare_act_text: Whoever, at any time after the passing of this Act, shall have been habitually associated with any other or others for the purpose of committing robbery or child-stealing by means of or accompanied with murder, is a thug.
meaning: Section 310 IPC defines the term 'thug'.
simple_explanation: A person habitually associated with others for committing robbery or child-stealing accompanied by murder is called a thug under IPC.
objective: To identify and classify organized criminal offenders involved in violent crimes.
legal_significance: The section provides the definition used for punishment under Section 311 IPC.
essential_elements: Habitual association with others.
Purpose of robbery or child-stealing.
The crime involves or is accompanied by murder.

mens_rea.description: Intentional participation in such criminal association is necessary.

examples.scenario: A person repeatedly joins a gang that commits robberies accompanied by murder.
examples.result: The person may be classified as a thug under Section 310 IPC.

max_punishment: Punishable under Section 311 IPC
ai_summary: Section 310 IPC defines a thug as a person habitually associated with others for robbery or child-stealing accompanied by murder.

title: Punishment for Being a Thug
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
bare_act_text: Whoever is a thug shall be punished with imprisonment for life and shall also be liable to fine.
meaning: Section 311 IPC prescribes punishment for a person classified as a thug.
simple_explanation: A person legally identified as a thug may be sentenced to life imprisonment and fine.
objective: To deter organized violent criminal groups.
legal_significance: The section imposes severe punishment on habitual offenders falling within the definition of Section 310 IPC.
essential_elements: The accused must be a thug within the meaning of Section 310 IPC.

mens_rea.description: Mens rea derives from participation in the criminal activities described in Section 310.

examples.scenario: A gang member is proven to be habitually involved in robberies accompanied by murder.
examples.result: He may be punished under Section 311 IPC.

max_punishment: Life imprisonment and fine
ai_summary: Section 311 IPC punishes a thug with life imprisonment and fine.

title: Causing Miscarriage
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of the Causing of Miscarriage, of Injuries to Unborn Children, of the Exposure of Infants, and of the Concealment of Births
bare_act_text: Whoever voluntarily causes a woman with child to miscarry, if such miscarriage is not caused in good faith for the purpose of saving the life of the woman, shall be punished as provided under this section.
meaning: Section 312 IPC punishes voluntarily causing miscarriage except when done in good faith to save the woman's life.
simple_explanation: Causing a pregnant woman to miscarry without legal justification is a criminal offence.
objective: To protect unborn children and pregnant women from unlawful termination of pregnancy.
legal_significance: The section criminalizes unlawful miscarriage while recognizing a limited exception for saving the woman's life.
essential_elements: The woman is pregnant.
Miscarriage is voluntarily caused.
The act is not done in good faith to save the woman's life.

mens_rea.description: The miscarriage must be voluntarily caused.

aggravated_case.imprisonment: Up to 7 years if the woman is quick with child

examples.scenario: A person unlawfully administers substances to terminate a pregnancy.
examples.result: The person may be prosecuted under Section 312 IPC.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine
ai_summary: Section 312 IPC punishes voluntarily causing miscarriage except when done in good faith to save the woman's life.

title: Causing Miscarriage Without Woman's Consent
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of the Causing of Miscarriage, of Injuries to Unborn Children, of the Exposure of Infants, and of the Concealment of Births
bare_act_text: Whoever commits the offence of causing miscarriage without the consent of the woman, whether the woman is quick with child or not, shall be punished with imprisonment for life, or imprisonment up to ten years, and fine.
meaning: Section 313 IPC punishes causing miscarriage without the consent of the pregnant woman.
simple_explanation: Forcing or causing a woman to miscarry without her consent is a serious criminal offence.
objective: To protect a woman's bodily autonomy and reproductive rights.
legal_significance: The section imposes a much harsher punishment than Section 312 because consent is absent.
essential_elements: Miscarriage is caused.
The woman did not consent.
The act is voluntary.

mens_rea.description: Intentional causing of miscarriage without consent is required.

normal_case.imprisonment: Life imprisonment or up to 10 years

examples.scenario: A person secretly administers medication causing a pregnant woman to miscarry without her knowledge.
examples.result: The offender may be prosecuted under Section 313 IPC.

max_punishment: Life imprisonment and fine
ai_summary: Section 313 IPC punishes causing miscarriage without the woman's consent with life imprisonment or imprisonment up to ten years and fine.

title: Death Caused by Act Done with Intent to Cause Miscarriage
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of the Causing of Miscarriage, of Injuries to Unborn Children, of the Exposure of Infants, and of the Concealment of Births
bare_act_text: Whoever, with intent to cause the miscarriage of a woman with child, does any act which causes the death of such woman, shall be punished with imprisonment up to ten years and fine; and if the act is done without the consent of the woman, with imprisonment for life or such punishment.
meaning: Section 314 IPC punishes causing the death of a woman while attempting to cause her miscarriage.
simple_explanation: If a person tries to cause a miscarriage and the woman dies because of that act, he commits an offence under this section.
objective: To protect pregnant women from dangerous acts intended to cause miscarriage.
legal_significance: The offence is complete even if the offender did not know that death was likely to occur.
essential_elements: The accused intended to cause miscarriage.
An act is committed towards that purpose.
The act causes the death of the woman.

mens_rea.description: Intent to cause miscarriage is required; knowledge of likely death is not essential.

aggravated_case.imprisonment: Life imprisonment if done without woman's consent

examples.scenario: A unlawfully administers a substance to cause miscarriage and the woman dies.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 314 IPC.

max_punishment: Life imprisonment
ai_summary: Section 314 IPC punishes causing the death of a woman through acts intended to cause miscarriage.

title: Act Done with Intent to Prevent Child Being Born Alive or to Cause it to Die After Birth
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of the Causing of Miscarriage, of Injuries to Unborn Children, of the Exposure of Infants, and of the Concealment of Births
bare_act_text: Whoever before the birth of any child does any act with the intention of thereby preventing that child from being born alive or causing it to die after its birth, and does by such act prevent that child from being born alive or causes it to die after birth, shall be punished with imprisonment up to ten years, or fine, or both, unless done in good faith for saving the life of the mother.
meaning: Section 315 IPC punishes acts intended to prevent a child from being born alive or to cause its death after birth.
simple_explanation: Intentionally preventing a child from being born alive, or causing it to die after birth, is a crime unless done to save the mother's life.
objective: To protect unborn and newborn children from intentional harm.
legal_significance: The section applies to acts committed before birth that result in the child not surviving.
essential_elements: The act is committed before birth.
There is intent to prevent live birth or cause death after birth.
The act achieves that result.

mens_rea.description: Specific intention to prevent live birth or cause death after birth.

examples.scenario: A person intentionally administers a harmful substance to prevent a child from being born alive.
examples.result: The person may be prosecuted under Section 315 IPC.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 10 years, or fine, or both
ai_summary: Section 315 IPC punishes acts intended to prevent a child from being born alive or to cause its death after birth.

title: Causing Death of Quick Unborn Child by Act Amounting to Culpable Homicide
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of the Causing of Miscarriage, of Injuries to Unborn Children, of the Exposure of Infants, and of the Concealment of Births
bare_act_text: Whoever does any act under such circumstances that, if he thereby caused death he would be guilty of culpable homicide, and does by such act cause the death of a quick unborn child, shall be punished with imprisonment up to ten years and fine.
meaning: Section 316 IPC punishes causing the death of a quick unborn child through an act amounting to culpable homicide.
simple_explanation: If a person's act would amount to culpable homicide and it causes the death of a quick unborn child, he commits an offence.
objective: To protect viable unborn children from serious criminal acts.
legal_significance: The section extends criminal liability to acts causing the death of a quick unborn child.
essential_elements: The act would amount to culpable homicide if death were caused.
A quick unborn child dies.
The death results from the accused's act.

mens_rea.description: Intention or knowledge sufficient for culpable homicide.

examples.scenario: A assaults a pregnant woman, causing the death of her quick unborn child.
examples.result: A may be prosecuted under Section 316 IPC.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 10 years and fine
ai_summary: Section 316 IPC punishes causing the death of a quick unborn child through an act amounting to culpable homicide.

title: Exposure and Abandonment of Child Under Twelve Years
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of the Causing of Miscarriage, of Injuries to Unborn Children, of the Exposure of Infants, and of the Concealment of Births
bare_act_text: Whoever, being the father or mother of a child under twelve years of age, or having the care of such child, exposes or leaves such child in any place with the intention of wholly abandoning such child, shall be punished with imprisonment up to seven years, or fine, or both.
meaning: Section 317 IPC punishes abandonment of a child below twelve years by a parent or guardian.
simple_explanation: Leaving a child under twelve years with the intention of permanently abandoning him or her is a criminal offence.
objective: To protect children from neglect and abandonment.
legal_significance: The section applies even if the child survives; more serious offences may apply if the child dies.
essential_elements: The child is under twelve years of age.
The accused is a parent or caretaker.
The child is exposed or abandoned.
There is intention to wholly abandon the child.

mens_rea.description: Intent to wholly abandon the child is essential.

examples.scenario: A parent leaves a young child in a deserted area intending never to return.
examples.result: The parent is liable under Section 317 IPC.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years, or fine, or both
ai_summary: Section 317 IPC punishes the exposure or abandonment of a child under twelve years by a parent or guardian.

title: Concealment of Birth by Secret Disposal of Dead Body
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of the Causing of Miscarriage, of Injuries to Unborn Children, of the Exposure of Infants, and of the Concealment of Births
section_type: Offence Relating to Concealment of Birth
bare_act_text: Whoever, by secretly burying or otherwise disposing of the dead body of a child, whether such child died before, during, or after birth, intentionally conceals or endeavours to conceal the birth of such child, shall be punished with imprisonment up to two years, or fine, or both.
meaning: Section 318 IPC punishes concealment of a child's birth by secretly disposing of its dead body.
simple_explanation: Secretly burying or disposing of the dead body of a child to hide the fact of birth is a criminal offence.
objective: To prevent concealment of births and ensure proper investigation of child deaths.
legal_significance: The section applies whether the child died before, during, or after birth.
essential_elements: A child's dead body is secretly disposed of.
The disposal is intended to conceal the birth.
There is intentional concealment or attempt to conceal.

mens_rea.description: Intentional concealment of birth is necessary.

examples.scenario: A person secretly buries the dead body of a newborn to hide the fact that the child was born.
examples.result: The person may be prosecuted under Section 318 IPC.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both
ai_summary: Section 318 IPC punishes concealment of a child's birth through secret disposal of the child's dead body.

title: Hurt
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Hurt
bare_act_text: Whoever causes bodily pain, disease or infirmity to any person is said to cause hurt.
meaning: Section 319 IPC defines the term 'hurt'.
simple_explanation: Any act that causes bodily pain, disease, or physical weakness to a person amounts to hurt.
objective: To provide the basic definition for offences relating to hurt.
legal_significance: This is the foundational definition for offences under Sections 321 to 338 IPC.
essential_elements: Bodily pain, disease, or infirmity is caused.
The effect is suffered by a person.

mens_rea.description: This section is definitional and does not itself prescribe a mental element.

examples.scenario: A punches B causing physical pain.
examples.result: B has suffered hurt under Section 319 IPC.

max_punishment: Not applicable
ai_summary: Section 319 IPC defines hurt as causing bodily pain, disease, or infirmity to a person.

title: Grievous Hurt
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Hurt
bare_act_text: The following kinds of hurt only are designated as grievous hurt: emasculation, permanent loss of sight, permanent loss of hearing, loss of any member or joint, destruction or permanent impairment of powers of any member or joint, permanent disfiguration of head or face, fracture or dislocation of bone or tooth, and hurt endangering life or causing severe pain or inability to follow ordinary pursuits for twenty days.
meaning: Section 320 IPC defines the specific categories of injuries that constitute grievous hurt.
simple_explanation: Only the eight serious types of injuries listed in this section are treated as grievous hurt.
objective: To distinguish serious bodily injuries from ordinary hurt.
legal_significance: The definition is crucial for offences relating to grievous hurt under Sections 322, 325, 326 and related provisions.
essential_elements: The injury falls within one of the eight categories listed in the section.

mens_rea.description: This section is purely definitional.

examples.scenario: A breaks B's arm during an assault.
examples.result: The injury amounts to grievous hurt because it involves fracture of a bone.

max_punishment: Not applicable
ai_summary: Section 320 IPC defines eight specific categories of grievous hurt recognized by law.

title: Voluntarily Causing Hurt
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Hurt
bare_act_text: Whoever does any act with the intention of thereby causing hurt to any person, or with the knowledge that he is likely thereby to cause hurt to any person, and does thereby cause hurt to any person, is said voluntarily to cause hurt.
meaning: Section 321 IPC defines voluntarily causing hurt.
simple_explanation: A person voluntarily causes hurt when he intentionally or knowingly does an act that results in bodily pain, disease, or infirmity to another person.
objective: To define when causing hurt becomes a voluntary act attracting criminal liability.
legal_significance: This section forms the basis for punishment under Section 323 IPC and related offences involving hurt.
essential_elements: An act is committed by the accused.
The act is done with intention or knowledge of causing hurt.
Hurt is actually caused.

mens_rea.description: Intention to cause hurt or knowledge that hurt is likely to be caused.

examples.scenario: A intentionally punches B causing bodily pain.
examples.result: A voluntarily causes hurt under Section 321 IPC.

max_punishment: Not applicable
ai_summary: Section 321 IPC defines voluntarily causing hurt as causing hurt with intention or knowledge.

title: Voluntarily Causing Grievous Hurt
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Hurt
bare_act_text: Whoever voluntarily causes hurt, if the hurt which he intends to cause or knows himself to be likely to cause is grievous hurt, and if the hurt which he causes is grievous hurt, is said voluntarily to cause grievous hurt.
meaning: Section 322 IPC defines voluntarily causing grievous hurt.
simple_explanation: A person voluntarily causes grievous hurt when he intentionally or knowingly causes one of the injuries classified as grievous hurt.
objective: To define criminal liability for intentionally causing serious bodily injuries.
legal_significance: The section serves as the basis for punishment under Sections 325 and 326 IPC.
essential_elements: The accused voluntarily causes hurt.
The intended or likely hurt is grievous hurt.
The hurt actually caused is grievous hurt.

mens_rea.description: Intention or knowledge relating to grievous hurt is essential.

examples.scenario: A intentionally strikes B causing a fracture of the arm.
examples.result: A voluntarily causes grievous hurt under Section 322 IPC.

max_punishment: Not applicable
ai_summary: Section 322 IPC defines voluntarily causing grievous hurt where grievous injury is intentionally or knowingly caused.

title: Punishment for Voluntarily Causing Hurt
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Hurt
bare_act_text: Whoever, except in the case provided for by Section 334, voluntarily causes hurt, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to one year, or with fine which may extend to one thousand rupees, or with both.
meaning: Section 323 IPC prescribes punishment for voluntarily causing hurt.
simple_explanation: A person who intentionally causes bodily pain, disease, or infirmity to another can be punished under this section.
objective: To punish unlawful acts causing physical harm to individuals.
legal_significance: It is the general punishment provision for voluntarily causing hurt.
essential_elements: Hurt is caused.
The hurt is caused voluntarily.
The case does not fall under Section 334 IPC.

mens_rea.description: Intention or knowledge required under Section 321 IPC.

examples.scenario: A slaps B during an argument causing bodily pain.
examples.result: A may be punished under Section 323 IPC.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 1 year, or fine up to ₹1,000, or both
ai_summary: Section 323 IPC punishes voluntarily causing hurt with imprisonment up to one year, fine, or both.

title: Voluntarily Causing Hurt by Dangerous Weapons or Means
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Hurt
bare_act_text: Whoever, except in the case provided for by Section 334, voluntarily causes hurt by means of any instrument for shooting, stabbing or cutting, or any dangerous weapon or means specified in this section, shall be punished with imprisonment up to three years, or with fine, or with both.
meaning: Section 324 IPC punishes voluntarily causing hurt using dangerous weapons or means.
simple_explanation: Causing hurt with weapons like knives, guns, fire, poison, explosives, or dangerous animals is a more serious offence.
objective: To deter the use of dangerous weapons and harmful means in causing injury.
legal_significance: The section imposes enhanced punishment due to the dangerous nature of the weapon or means used.
essential_elements: Hurt is voluntarily caused.
A dangerous weapon or specified dangerous means is used.
The case does not fall under Section 334 IPC.

mens_rea.description: Intention or knowledge required for voluntarily causing hurt.

examples.scenario: A attacks B with a knife causing injuries.
examples.result: A may be prosecuted under Section 324 IPC.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 years, or fine, or both
ai_summary: Section 324 IPC punishes voluntarily causing hurt by dangerous weapons or means.

title: Punishment for Voluntarily Causing Grievous Hurt
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Hurt
bare_act_text: Whoever, except in the case provided for by Section 335, voluntarily causes grievous hurt, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.
meaning: Section 325 IPC prescribes punishment for voluntarily causing grievous hurt.
simple_explanation: A person who intentionally causes a serious injury classified as grievous hurt can be punished under this section.
objective: To punish serious bodily injuries that are intentionally inflicted.
legal_significance: The section provides the general punishment for grievous hurt where no dangerous weapon is involved.
essential_elements: Grievous hurt is caused.
The grievous hurt is caused voluntarily.
The case does not fall under Section 335 IPC.

mens_rea.description: Intention or knowledge relating to grievous hurt.

examples.scenario: A intentionally breaks B's leg during an assault.
examples.result: A may be punished under Section 325 IPC.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine
ai_summary: Section 325 IPC punishes voluntarily causing grievous hurt with imprisonment up to seven years and fine.

title: Voluntarily Causing Grievous Hurt by Dangerous Weapons or Means
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Hurt
bare_act_text: Whoever voluntarily causes grievous hurt by means of dangerous weapons or means such as shooting, stabbing, cutting instruments, fire, poison, corrosive substances, explosives or animals shall be punished with imprisonment for life, or imprisonment up to ten years, and fine.
meaning: Section 326 IPC punishes voluntarily causing grievous hurt using dangerous weapons or means.
simple_explanation: Causing serious injuries with weapons like guns, knives, acid, poison, fire, explosives or dangerous animals is a serious offence.
objective: To provide enhanced punishment for grievous hurt caused through dangerous means.
legal_significance: The section imposes stricter punishment than Section 325 because of the dangerous nature of the weapon or means used.
essential_elements: Grievous hurt is caused.
The hurt is caused voluntarily.
Dangerous weapons or means are used.

mens_rea.description: Intention or knowledge required for voluntarily causing grievous hurt.

aggravated_case.imprisonment: Life imprisonment

examples.scenario: A attacks B with a sword causing permanent disability.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 326 IPC.

max_punishment: Life imprisonment and fine
ai_summary: Section 326 IPC punishes voluntarily causing grievous hurt through dangerous weapons or means.

title: Voluntarily Causing Grievous Hurt by Use of Acid
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Hurt
bare_act_text: Whoever causes permanent or partial damage, deformity, burns, maiming, disfigurement, disability or grievous hurt by throwing or administering acid shall be punished with imprisonment not less than ten years, which may extend to life imprisonment, and fine.
meaning: Section 326A IPC punishes acid attacks causing serious bodily injury or disfigurement.
simple_explanation: Throwing acid on a person and causing burns, disfigurement or disability is a serious criminal offence.
objective: To protect individuals from acid attacks and provide severe punishment for offenders.
legal_significance: The section was introduced specifically to address acid attack crimes and ensure victim compensation.
essential_elements: Acid or similar corrosive substance is used.
The victim suffers damage, burns, deformity or grievous hurt.
The act is intentional or done with knowledge.

mens_rea.description: Intention or knowledge of causing such injury.

aggravated_case.imprisonment: Life imprisonment

examples.scenario: A throws acid on B causing severe facial burns and disfigurement.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 326A IPC.

max_punishment: Life imprisonment and fine
ai_summary: Section 326A IPC punishes acid attacks causing grievous hurt, disfigurement or disability.

title: Voluntarily Throwing or Attempting to Throw Acid
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Hurt
bare_act_text: Whoever throws or attempts to throw acid on any person, or attempts to administer acid with the intention of causing damage, burns, disfigurement, disability or grievous hurt, shall be punished with imprisonment not less than five years but which may extend to seven years, and fine.
meaning: Section 326B IPC punishes attempted acid attacks.
simple_explanation: Even attempting to throw acid on a person is a criminal offence, whether injury occurs or not.
objective: To prevent acid attacks at the attempt stage.
legal_significance: The section criminalizes acid attack attempts separately from completed attacks.
essential_elements: Acid is thrown or attempted to be thrown.
The act is done intentionally.
The purpose is to cause burns, disfigurement or grievous hurt.

mens_rea.description: Specific intention to cause acid-related injury.

normal_case.imprisonment: Not less than 5 years and up to 7 years

examples.scenario: A attempts to throw acid on B but misses.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 326B IPC.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine
ai_summary: Section 326B IPC punishes throwing or attempting to throw acid with intent to cause injury.

title: Voluntarily Causing Hurt to Extort Property or Constrain to an Illegal Act
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Hurt
bare_act_text: Whoever voluntarily causes hurt for the purpose of extorting property, valuable security, or constraining any person to do an illegal act shall be punished with imprisonment up to ten years and fine.
meaning: Section 327 IPC punishes causing hurt for extortion or coercion.
simple_explanation: Hurting someone to force them to give property or perform an illegal act is a criminal offence.
objective: To prevent the use of violence for extortion and unlawful coercion.
legal_significance: The offence combines hurt with the intention of extortion or illegal compulsion.
essential_elements: Hurt is voluntarily caused.
The purpose is extortion or illegal coercion.

mens_rea.description: Specific intention to extort property or compel an illegal act.

examples.scenario: A beats B to force him to transfer property documents.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 327 IPC.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 10 years and fine
ai_summary: Section 327 IPC punishes causing hurt to extort property or compel illegal acts.

title: Causing Hurt by Means of Poison, etc., with Intent to Commit an Offence
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Hurt
bare_act_text: Whoever administers poison, intoxicating, stupefying or unwholesome substances with intent to cause hurt, commit an offence, or facilitate an offence shall be punished with imprisonment up to ten years and fine.
meaning: Section 328 IPC punishes causing hurt through poison or intoxicating substances.
simple_explanation: Giving poison, drugs or intoxicating substances to harm someone or facilitate a crime is an offence.
objective: To prevent the misuse of harmful substances for criminal purposes.
legal_significance: The section is frequently applied in poisoning and drugging cases.
essential_elements: Poison or harmful substance is administered.
The act causes hurt or facilitates an offence.
The act is intentional.

mens_rea.description: Intent to cause hurt or facilitate an offence.

examples.scenario: A mixes a stupefying drug into B's drink to rob him.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 328 IPC.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 10 years and fine
ai_summary: Section 328 IPC punishes causing hurt through poison or intoxicating substances to commit or facilitate offences.

title: Voluntarily Causing Grievous Hurt to Extort Property or Constrain to an Illegal Act
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Hurt
bare_act_text: Whoever voluntarily causes grievous hurt for the purpose of extorting property, valuable security, or compelling any person to do an illegal act shall be punished with life imprisonment or imprisonment up to ten years and fine.
meaning: Section 329 IPC punishes causing grievous hurt for extortion or illegal coercion.
simple_explanation: Causing serious injuries to force someone to hand over property or do something illegal is a grave offence.
objective: To deter extortion through severe violence.
legal_significance: The offence combines grievous hurt with extortionary motives and carries severe punishment.
essential_elements: Grievous hurt is caused.
The act is voluntary.
The purpose is extortion or illegal compulsion.

mens_rea.description: Specific intention to extort property or compel an illegal act.

aggravated_case.imprisonment: Life imprisonment

examples.scenario: A breaks B's leg to force him to sign property papers.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 329 IPC.

max_punishment: Life imprisonment and fine
ai_summary: Section 329 IPC punishes causing grievous hurt for extortion or illegal coercion.

title: Voluntarily Causing Hurt to Extort Confession or Compel Restoration of Property
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Hurt
section_type: Offence Relating to Extortion of Confession
bare_act_text: Whoever voluntarily causes hurt for the purpose of extorting a confession, obtaining information relating to an offence, or compelling restoration of property, valuable security, claim or demand, shall be punished with imprisonment up to seven years and fine.
meaning: Section 330 IPC punishes causing hurt to obtain confessions, information, or recovery of property.
simple_explanation: Hurting a person to force a confession, obtain information, or recover property is a criminal offence.
objective: To prevent torture and coercive methods for obtaining confessions or information.
legal_significance: The section protects individuals against physical coercion and custodial violence.
essential_elements: Hurt is voluntarily caused.
The purpose is obtaining a confession, information, or restoration of property.
The act is intentional.

mens_rea.description: Specific intention to extort confession, information, or restoration of property.

examples.scenario: A police officer beats a suspect to force a confession.
examples.result: The officer is liable under Section 330 IPC.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine
ai_summary: Section 330 IPC punishes causing hurt to extort confessions, information, or restoration of property.

title: Voluntarily Causing Grievous Hurt to Extort Confession or Compel Restoration of Property
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Hurt
section_type: Offence Relating to Extortion of Confession
bare_act_text: Whoever voluntarily causes grievous hurt for the purpose of extorting a confession, obtaining information relating to an offence, or compelling restoration of property, valuable security, claim or demand, shall be punished with imprisonment up to ten years and fine.
meaning: Section 331 IPC punishes causing grievous hurt to obtain confessions, information, or recovery of property.
simple_explanation: Causing serious injuries to force a confession or obtain information is a serious criminal offence.
objective: To prevent torture resulting in grievous injuries.
legal_significance: The section provides enhanced punishment where grievous hurt is used as a means of coercion.
essential_elements: Grievous hurt is voluntarily caused.
The purpose is obtaining a confession, information, or restoration of property.
The act is intentional.

mens_rea.description: Specific intention to extort confession, information, or property.

examples.scenario: A person fractures another's arm to force disclosure of stolen property.
examples.result: The offender is liable under Section 331 IPC.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 10 years and fine
ai_summary: Section 331 IPC punishes causing grievous hurt to extort confessions, information, or restoration of property.

title: Voluntarily Causing Hurt to Deter Public Servant from His Duty
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Hurt
bare_act_text: Whoever voluntarily causes hurt to a public servant while discharging his duty, or with intent to prevent or deter him from discharging his duty, shall be punished with imprisonment up to three years, or fine, or both.
meaning: Section 332 IPC punishes causing hurt to a public servant in connection with the discharge of official duties.
simple_explanation: Hurting a public servant to stop or punish him for performing official duties is a criminal offence.
objective: To protect public servants from violence while performing lawful duties.
legal_significance: The section ensures effective functioning of public administration by safeguarding officials.
essential_elements: The victim is a public servant.
Hurt is voluntarily caused.
The act is connected to the discharge of official duty.

mens_rea.description: Intent to deter or retaliate against a public servant.

examples.scenario: A attacks a police officer to prevent an arrest.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 332 IPC.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 years, or fine, or both
ai_summary: Section 332 IPC punishes voluntarily causing hurt to deter a public servant from performing official duties.

title: Voluntarily Causing Grievous Hurt to Deter Public Servant from His Duty
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Hurt
bare_act_text: Whoever voluntarily causes grievous hurt to a public servant while discharging his duty, or with intent to prevent or deter him from discharging his duty, shall be punished with imprisonment up to ten years and fine.
meaning: Section 333 IPC punishes causing grievous hurt to a public servant in connection with official duties.
simple_explanation: Causing serious injuries to a public servant to stop or punish him for doing his duty is a serious offence.
objective: To provide stronger protection to public servants against serious violence.
legal_significance: The section enhances punishment where grievous hurt is caused to public servants.
essential_elements: The victim is a public servant.
Grievous hurt is voluntarily caused.
The act is connected with official duty.

mens_rea.description: Intent to deter or retaliate against a public servant.

examples.scenario: A breaks a government officer's arm to stop an official inspection.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 333 IPC.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 10 years and fine
ai_summary: Section 333 IPC punishes voluntarily causing grievous hurt to deter a public servant from performing official duties.

title: Voluntarily Causing Hurt on Provocation
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Hurt
bare_act_text: Whoever voluntarily causes hurt on grave and sudden provocation, if he neither intends nor knows himself likely to cause hurt to any person other than the person who gave the provocation, shall be punished with imprisonment up to one month, or fine up to five hundred rupees, or both.
meaning: Section 334 IPC provides a reduced punishment where hurt is caused due to grave and sudden provocation.
simple_explanation: If a person causes hurt immediately after serious provocation, the law treats the offence less severely.
objective: To recognize the reduced culpability of actions committed under grave and sudden provocation.
legal_significance: The section acts as a mitigating provision compared to ordinary hurt offences.
essential_elements: Hurt is voluntarily caused.
There is grave and sudden provocation.
The hurt is caused to the person giving the provocation.

mens_rea.description: The act is committed under the influence of grave and sudden provocation.

examples.scenario: A is suddenly and seriously provoked by B and immediately strikes B causing hurt.
examples.result: A may be liable under Section 334 IPC.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 1 month, or fine up to ₹500, or both
ai_summary: Section 334 IPC provides reduced punishment for voluntarily causing hurt on grave and sudden provocation.

title: Voluntarily Causing Grievous Hurt on Provocation
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Hurt
bare_act_text: Whoever voluntarily causes grievous hurt on grave and sudden provocation, if he neither intends nor knows himself likely to cause grievous hurt to any person other than the person who gave the provocation, shall be punished with imprisonment up to four years, or fine up to two thousand rupees, or both.
meaning: Section 335 IPC provides reduced punishment where grievous hurt is caused due to grave and sudden provocation.
simple_explanation: If a person causes grievous hurt immediately after serious provocation, the punishment is less severe than under ordinary grievous hurt provisions.
objective: To recognize reduced criminal responsibility in cases involving grave and sudden provocation.
legal_significance: The section mitigates liability that would otherwise arise under Sections 325 or 326 IPC.
essential_elements: Grievous hurt is voluntarily caused.
There is grave and sudden provocation.
The injury is caused to the person who gave the provocation.

mens_rea.description: The act occurs under grave and sudden provocation.

examples.scenario: A is gravely provoked by B and immediately causes a fracture to B.
examples.result: A may be liable under Section 335 IPC.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 4 years, or fine up to ₹2,000, or both
ai_summary: Section 335 IPC provides reduced punishment for voluntarily causing grievous hurt on grave and sudden provocation.

title: Act Endangering Life or Personal Safety of Others
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Hurt
bare_act_text: Whoever does any act so rashly or negligently as to endanger human life or the personal safety of others shall be punished with imprisonment up to three months, or fine up to two hundred and fifty rupees, or both.
meaning: Section 336 IPC punishes rash or negligent acts that endanger human life or personal safety.
simple_explanation: Doing a careless or reckless act that puts other people's lives or safety at risk is a criminal offence.
objective: To prevent dangerous conduct that may cause injury or death.
legal_significance: The section addresses risky behaviour even when no actual injury occurs.
essential_elements: An act is committed.
The act is rash or negligent.
Human life or personal safety is endangered.

mens_rea.description: Criminal rashness or negligence is sufficient.

examples.scenario: A throws stones from a rooftop onto a crowded street, endangering pedestrians.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 336 IPC.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 months, or fine up to ₹250, or both
ai_summary: Section 336 IPC punishes rash or negligent acts that endanger human life or personal safety.

title: Causing Hurt by Act Endangering Life or Personal Safety of Others
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Hurt
bare_act_text: Whoever causes hurt to any person by doing any act so rashly or negligently as to endanger human life or the personal safety of others shall be punished with imprisonment up to six months, or fine up to five hundred rupees, or both.
meaning: Section 337 IPC punishes causing hurt through rash or negligent acts that endanger human life or personal safety.
simple_explanation: If a person's careless or reckless act causes injury to someone, he may be punished under this section.
objective: To discourage negligent conduct that results in bodily harm.
legal_significance: The section applies when hurt is actually caused due to criminal rashness or negligence.
essential_elements: Hurt is caused to a person.
The act is rash or negligent.
The act endangers human life or personal safety.

mens_rea.description: Criminal rashness or negligence is sufficient.

examples.scenario: A drives recklessly and causes minor injuries to a pedestrian.
examples.result: A may be prosecuted under Section 337 IPC.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 6 months, or fine up to ₹500, or both
ai_summary: Section 337 IPC punishes causing hurt through rash or negligent acts endangering human life or personal safety.

title: Causing Grievous Hurt by Act Endangering Life or Personal Safety of Others
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Hurt
bare_act_text: Whoever causes grievous hurt to any person by doing any act so rashly or negligently as to endanger human life or the personal safety of others shall be punished with imprisonment up to two years, or fine up to one thousand rupees, or both.
meaning: Section 338 IPC punishes causing grievous hurt through rash or negligent acts.
simple_explanation: If a person's careless or reckless act causes serious injuries to someone, he commits an offence under this section.
objective: To punish serious injuries resulting from criminal negligence.
legal_significance: The section is the aggravated form of Section 337 IPC where grievous hurt is caused.
essential_elements: Grievous hurt is caused.
The act is rash or negligent.
The act endangers human life or personal safety.

mens_rea.description: Criminal rashness or negligence is sufficient.

examples.scenario: A drives negligently and causes a fracture to another person.
examples.result: A may be prosecuted under Section 338 IPC.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine up to ₹1,000, or both
ai_summary: Section 338 IPC punishes causing grievous hurt through rash or negligent acts endangering human life or personal safety.

title: Wrongful Restraint
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Wrongful Restraint and Wrongful Confinement
bare_act_text: Whoever voluntarily obstructs any person so as to prevent that person from proceeding in any direction in which that person has a right to proceed is said wrongfully to restrain that person.
meaning: Section 339 IPC defines wrongful restraint.
simple_explanation: Stopping a person from moving in a direction where he has a legal right to go amounts to wrongful restraint.
objective: To protect the freedom of movement of individuals.
legal_significance: This section forms the basis for punishment under Section 341 IPC.
essential_elements: Voluntary obstruction of a person.
The person has a legal right to proceed.
The obstruction prevents such movement.

mens_rea.description: The obstruction must be voluntary.

examples.scenario: A blocks a public pathway preventing B from passing.
examples.result: A wrongfully restrains B under Section 339 IPC.

max_punishment: Not applicable
ai_summary: Section 339 IPC defines wrongful restraint as voluntarily preventing a person from proceeding where he has a right to go.

title: Wrongful Confinement
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Wrongful Restraint and Wrongful Confinement
bare_act_text: Whoever wrongfully restrains any person in such a manner as to prevent that person from proceeding beyond certain circumscribing limits is said wrongfully to confine that person.
meaning: Section 340 IPC defines wrongful confinement.
simple_explanation: Keeping a person restricted within a particular area and preventing him from leaving amounts to wrongful confinement.
objective: To protect personal liberty and freedom of movement.
legal_significance: Wrongful confinement is an aggravated form of wrongful restraint.
essential_elements: Wrongful restraint exists.
The victim is confined within specific limits.
The victim cannot move beyond those limits.

mens_rea.description: The confinement must be intentional.

examples.scenario: A locks B inside a room and prevents him from leaving.
examples.result: A wrongfully confines B under Section 340 IPC.

max_punishment: Not applicable
ai_summary: Section 340 IPC defines wrongful confinement as preventing a person from moving beyond certain limits.

title: Punishment for Wrongful Restraint
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Wrongful Restraint and Wrongful Confinement
bare_act_text: Whoever wrongfully restrains any person shall be punished with simple imprisonment up to one month, or fine up to five hundred rupees, or both.
meaning: Section 341 IPC prescribes punishment for wrongful restraint.
simple_explanation: A person who illegally prevents another from moving freely may be punished under this section.
objective: To protect freedom of movement.
legal_significance: This is the punishment provision corresponding to Section 339 IPC.
essential_elements: Wrongful restraint as defined under Section 339 IPC.

mens_rea.description: Voluntary obstruction is required.

examples.scenario: A blocks B's way on a public road and prevents him from passing.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 341 IPC.

max_punishment: Simple imprisonment up to 1 month, or fine up to ₹500, or both
ai_summary: Section 341 IPC punishes wrongful restraint.

title: Punishment for Wrongful Confinement
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Wrongful Restraint and Wrongful Confinement
bare_act_text: Whoever wrongfully confines any person shall be punished with imprisonment up to one year, or fine up to one thousand rupees, or both.
meaning: Section 342 IPC prescribes punishment for wrongful confinement.
simple_explanation: Keeping a person confined within a place against his will is punishable under this section.
objective: To protect individual liberty against unlawful detention.
legal_significance: This is the basic punishment provision for wrongful confinement.
essential_elements: Wrongful confinement as defined under Section 340 IPC.

mens_rea.description: Intentional confinement is required.

examples.scenario: A locks B in a room for several hours without legal authority.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 342 IPC.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 1 year, or fine up to ₹1,000, or both
ai_summary: Section 342 IPC punishes wrongful confinement.

title: Wrongful Confinement for Three or More Days
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Wrongful Restraint and Wrongful Confinement
bare_act_text: Whoever wrongfully confines any person for three days or more shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.
meaning: Section 343 IPC punishes wrongful confinement lasting three days or more.
simple_explanation: Keeping a person unlawfully confined for at least three days is a more serious offence.
objective: To impose greater punishment for prolonged unlawful confinement.
legal_significance: This is an aggravated form of wrongful confinement under Section 342 IPC.
essential_elements: Wrongful confinement exists.
The confinement continues for three days or more.

mens_rea.description: Intentional wrongful confinement.

examples.scenario: A unlawfully locks B in a room for four days.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 343 IPC.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both
ai_summary: Section 343 IPC punishes wrongful confinement that continues for three days or more.

title: Wrongful Confinement for Ten or More Days
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Wrongful Restraint and Wrongful Confinement
bare_act_text: Whoever wrongfully confines any person for ten days or more shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years and shall also be liable to fine.
meaning: Section 344 IPC punishes wrongful confinement lasting ten days or more.
simple_explanation: Keeping a person unlawfully confined for ten days or longer is a serious offence.
objective: To provide stricter punishment for extended unlawful confinement.
legal_significance: The punishment is enhanced due to the prolonged duration of confinement.
essential_elements: Wrongful confinement exists.
The confinement continues for ten days or more.

mens_rea.description: Intentional wrongful confinement.

examples.scenario: A unlawfully detains B in a warehouse for fifteen days.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 344 IPC.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 years and fine
ai_summary: Section 344 IPC punishes wrongful confinement for ten days or more.

title: Wrongful Confinement of Person for Whose Liberation Writ Has Been Issued
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Wrongful Restraint and Wrongful Confinement
bare_act_text: Whoever keeps any person in wrongful confinement knowing that a writ for the liberation of that person has been duly issued shall be punished with imprisonment up to two years in addition to any other punishment to which he may be liable.
meaning: Section 345 IPC punishes continued wrongful confinement despite a court order for release.
simple_explanation: Keeping a person confined even after knowing that a court has ordered his release is a criminal offence.
objective: To ensure compliance with judicial orders relating to personal liberty.
legal_significance: The punishment is additional to any punishment for wrongful confinement.
essential_elements: Wrongful confinement exists.
A writ for release has been issued.
The accused knows about the writ.

mens_rea.description: Knowledge of the writ is essential.

examples.scenario: A continues to confine B despite knowing a court has ordered B's release.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 345 IPC.

max_punishment: Additional imprisonment up to 2 years
ai_summary: Section 345 IPC punishes wrongful confinement despite knowledge of a release writ.

title: Wrongful Confinement in Secret
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Wrongful Restraint and Wrongful Confinement
bare_act_text: Whoever wrongfully confines any person in such a manner as to indicate an intention that the confinement may not be known to interested persons or public servants shall be punished with imprisonment up to two years in addition to any punishment for wrongful confinement.
meaning: Section 346 IPC punishes secret wrongful confinement.
simple_explanation: Secretly keeping a person confined so that others do not know about it is a serious offence.
objective: To prevent secret detention and concealment of confined persons.
legal_significance: The punishment is additional to the punishment for wrongful confinement.
essential_elements: Wrongful confinement exists.
The confinement is kept secret.
There is intent to conceal the confinement.

mens_rea.description: Intentional secrecy regarding the confinement.

examples.scenario: A secretly confines B in an unknown location and prevents anyone from discovering it.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 346 IPC.

max_punishment: Additional imprisonment up to 2 years
ai_summary: Section 346 IPC punishes secret wrongful confinement.

title: Wrongful Confinement to Extort Property or Constrain to Illegal Act
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Wrongful Restraint and Wrongful Confinement
bare_act_text: Whoever wrongfully confines any person for the purpose of extorting property or valuable security, or compelling any person to do anything illegal or give information facilitating an offence, shall be punished with imprisonment up to three years and fine.
meaning: Section 347 IPC punishes wrongful confinement for extortion or illegal coercion.
simple_explanation: Confining someone to force them to hand over property or do something illegal is a criminal offence.
objective: To prevent the use of confinement as a tool of extortion and coercion.
legal_significance: The motive behind the confinement makes the offence more serious.
essential_elements: Wrongful confinement exists.
The purpose is extortion or illegal coercion.

mens_rea.description: Specific intent to extort or compel an illegal act.

examples.scenario: A locks B in a room until B signs over property documents.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 347 IPC.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 years and fine
ai_summary: Section 347 IPC punishes wrongful confinement for extortion or illegal coercion.

title: Wrongful Confinement to Extort Confession or Compel Restoration of Property
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Wrongful Restraint and Wrongful Confinement
section_type: Wrongful Confinement for Extortion of Confession
bare_act_text: Whoever wrongfully confines any person for the purpose of extorting a confession, obtaining information relating to an offence, or compelling restoration of property or valuable security, shall be punished with imprisonment up to three years and fine.
meaning: Section 348 IPC punishes wrongful confinement used to obtain confessions, information, or property.
simple_explanation: Keeping someone confined to force a confession or recover property is a criminal offence.
objective: To prevent coercive confinement and custodial abuse.
legal_significance: The section protects individuals against confinement used as a means of extracting information.
essential_elements: Wrongful confinement exists.
The purpose is obtaining a confession, information, or restoration of property.

mens_rea.description: Specific intent to extort confession, information, or property.

examples.scenario: A unlawfully detains B to force him to reveal the location of stolen property.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 348 IPC.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 years and fine
ai_summary: Section 348 IPC punishes wrongful confinement used to extort confessions, information, or property.

title: Force
chapter: Chapter XVIII - Of Criminal Force and Assault
bare_act_text: A person is said to use force to another if he causes motion, change of motion, or cessation of motion to that person or to any substance which comes into contact with that person's body, clothing, or things carried by him.
meaning: Section 349 IPC defines the term 'force'.
simple_explanation: Force means causing movement, stopping movement, or changing movement that affects another person directly or indirectly.
objective: To provide the foundation for offences involving criminal force and assault.
legal_significance: This definition forms the basis for Section 350 IPC.
essential_elements: Motion, change of motion, or cessation of motion is caused.
The effect reaches another person directly or indirectly.

mens_rea.description: This is a definitional provision.

examples.scenario: A pushes B causing him to move.
examples.result: A uses force within the meaning of Section 349 IPC.

max_punishment: Not applicable
ai_summary: Section 349 IPC defines force as causing motion, change of motion, or cessation of motion affecting another person.

title: Criminal Force
chapter: Chapter XVIII - Of Criminal Force and Assault
bare_act_text: Whoever intentionally uses force to any person without that person's consent, in order to commit an offence or intending or knowing that it is likely to cause injury, fear or annoyance, is said to use criminal force.
meaning: Section 350 IPC defines criminal force.
simple_explanation: Using force against a person without consent to commit an offence or cause injury, fear, or annoyance amounts to criminal force.
objective: To distinguish unlawful force from ordinary physical contact.
legal_significance: Criminal force is a key element in several offences including assault and outraging modesty.
essential_elements: Force is used.
The force is intentional.
The force is without consent.
There is intent to commit an offence or cause injury, fear, or annoyance.

mens_rea.description: Intent or knowledge regarding injury, fear, annoyance, or commission of an offence.

examples.scenario: A intentionally pushes B to frighten him.
examples.result: A uses criminal force under Section 350 IPC.

max_punishment: Not applicable
ai_summary: Section 350 IPC defines criminal force as intentional use of force without consent to cause injury, fear, or annoyance.

title: Assault
chapter: Chapter XVIII - Of Criminal Force and Assault
bare_act_text: Whoever makes any gesture or preparation intending or knowing it to be likely that such gesture or preparation will cause any person present to apprehend that criminal force is about to be used against him is said to commit an assault.
meaning: Section 351 IPC defines assault.
simple_explanation: Threatening gestures or actions that make a person fear immediate use of criminal force amount to assault.
objective: To protect individuals from threats of immediate physical violence.
legal_significance: Actual physical contact is not necessary; reasonable apprehension is sufficient.
essential_elements: A gesture or preparation is made.
The victim apprehends immediate use of criminal force.
The accused intends or knows such apprehension is likely.

mens_rea.description: Intent or knowledge that the victim will apprehend criminal force.

examples.scenario: A raises his fist at B in a threatening manner causing B to fear being struck.
examples.result: A commits assault under Section 351 IPC.

max_punishment: Not applicable
ai_summary: Section 351 IPC defines assault as conduct causing apprehension of immediate criminal force.

title: Punishment for Assault or Criminal Force Otherwise Than on Grave Provocation
chapter: Chapter XVIII - Of Criminal Force and Assault
bare_act_text: Whoever assaults or uses criminal force to any person otherwise than on grave and sudden provocation given by that person shall be punished with imprisonment up to three months, or fine up to five hundred rupees, or both.
meaning: Section 352 IPC punishes assault or criminal force not committed under grave and sudden provocation.
simple_explanation: A person who assaults or uses criminal force against another without lawful justification commits an offence under this section.
objective: To punish unlawful assault and use of criminal force.
legal_significance: This is the general punishment provision for assault and criminal force.
essential_elements: Assault or criminal force is used.
The act is not covered by grave and sudden provocation.

mens_rea.description: Intentional assault or use of criminal force.

examples.scenario: A intentionally pushes B during an argument without any grave provocation.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 352 IPC.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 months, or fine up to ₹500, or both
ai_summary: Section 352 IPC punishes assault or criminal force used without grave and sudden provocation.

title: Assault or Criminal Force to Deter Public Servant from Discharge of Duty
chapter: Chapter XVIII - Of Criminal Force and Assault
bare_act_text: Whoever assaults or uses criminal force to a public servant in the execution of his duty, or to prevent or deter him from discharging his duty, shall be punished with imprisonment up to two years, or fine, or both.
meaning: Section 353 IPC punishes assault or criminal force against public servants performing official duties.
