(4) Whoever, after committing or attempting to commit snatching, causes hurt or wrongful restraint of fear of hurt, in order to effect his escape shall be punished with rigorous imprisonment for a term which may extend to three years, in addition to the punishment provided for the offence of snatching by the preceding sub-sections.
meaning: Section 379A (Gujarat Amendment) defines and punishes 'snatching' as the sudden, quick, or forcible seizure of movable property from a person's possession with intent to commit theft and escape with the property.
simple_explanation: If a person suddenly or forcibly grabs and runs away with someone's property (such as a phone, bag, or chain) with the intention to steal it, this is 'snatching' under Gujarat's amendment, punishable with rigorous imprisonment of 7 to 10 years (or 5 to 10 years for an attempt) and fine up to ₹25,000; additional punishment applies if hurt or fear of hurt is caused while escaping.
objective: To specifically address and deter the offence of snatching, a distinct and prevalent form of theft involving sudden force, by prescribing stringent minimum sentences within Gujarat.
legal_significance: Creates a state-specific aggravated category of theft (snatching) with mandatory minimum sentences higher than ordinary theft under Section 379, applicable only in Gujarat.
essential_elements: Intention to commit theft.
Sudden, quick, or forcible seizure, securing, grabbing, or taking away of movable property from a person or their physical possession.
The offender makes or attempts to make an escape with such property.

mens_rea.description: Intention to commit theft through sudden, quick, or forcible seizure of property and to escape with it.

normal_case.imprisonment: Rigorous imprisonment not less than 7 years, extendable to 10 years (for completed snatching); not less than 5 years, extendable to 10 years (for attempt)

aggravated_case.imprisonment: Additional imprisonment up to 3 years if hurt or wrongful restraint/fear of hurt is caused while escaping

examples.scenario: A suddenly grabs Z's mobile phone from Z's hand and runs away with it in Gujarat.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 379A(3) with rigorous imprisonment of 7 to 10 years and fine up to ₹25,000.

max_punishment: Rigorous imprisonment up to 10 years (plus up to 3 additional years if hurt caused), and fine up to ₹25,000
ai_summary: Section 379A, a Gujarat state amendment, defines and punishes 'snatching' (sudden or forcible theft of movable property) with rigorous imprisonment of 7 to 10 years (5 to 10 years for attempts) and fine up to ₹25,000, with additional punishment if hurt is caused during escape.

title: Snatching After Preparation Made for Causing Death, Hurt or Restraint (Gujarat State Amendment)
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
bare_act_text: Whoever commits or attempts to commit snatching, having made preparation for causing death, or hurt, or restraint, or fear of death, or of hurt, or of restraint, to any person, in order to the committing of such snatching, or in order to the retaining of property taken by such snatching, shall be punished with rigorous imprisonment for a term which shall not be less than seven years but which may extend to ten years, and with fine which may extend to twenty-five thousand rupees.
meaning: Section 379B (Gujarat Amendment) punishes snatching, or an attempt to commit snatching, where the offender has made preparation for causing death, hurt, or restraint (or fear thereof) in order to commit or retain the property obtained by snatching.
simple_explanation: If a person commits or attempts snatching after having prepared to cause death, injury, or confinement (or fear of these) to carry out the snatching or to keep the snatched property, they face rigorous imprisonment of 7 to 10 years and fine up to ₹25,000 in Gujarat.
objective: To impose enhanced punishment on snatching offences that involve advance preparation for violence, reflecting their greater danger to victims.
legal_significance: Parallels Section 382 (theft with preparation for violence) but specifically for the offence of snatching as defined under Section 379A, applicable only in Gujarat.
essential_elements: The accused commits or attempts to commit snatching.
The accused has made preparation for causing death, hurt, or restraint, or fear of death, hurt, or restraint to any person.
Such preparation is made in order to commit the snatching or to retain the property taken by the snatching.

mens_rea.description: Intention to commit or attempt snatching, coupled with advance preparation to cause or threaten death, hurt, or restraint in order to commit the offence or retain the snatched property.

normal_case.imprisonment: Rigorous imprisonment not less than 7 years, extendable to 10 years

examples.scenario: A, carrying a weapon prepared to injure anyone who resists, snatches Z's bag in Gujarat.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 379B with rigorous imprisonment of 7 to 10 years and fine up to ₹25,000.

max_punishment: Rigorous imprisonment up to 10 years and fine up to ₹25,000
ai_summary: Section 379B, a Gujarat state amendment, punishes snatching (or attempted snatching) committed with advance preparation for causing death, hurt, or restraint, with rigorous imprisonment of 7 to 10 years and fine up to ₹25,000.

title: Theft in Dwelling House, etc.
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
bare_act_text: Whoever commits theft in any building, tent or vessel, which building, tent or vessel is used as a human dwelling, or used for the custody of property, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.
meaning: Section 380 IPC punishes theft committed in a building, tent, or vessel used as a human dwelling or for the custody of property, with enhanced punishment compared to simple theft.
simple_explanation: If theft is committed inside a house, tent, or vessel that is used for living in or for storing property, the punishment is higher than for ordinary theft—imprisonment up to 7 years along with a fine.
objective: To provide enhanced punishment for theft committed in places where people live or store property, recognizing the greater violation of security and privacy involved.
legal_significance: Creates an aggravated form of theft based on the location of the offence (a dwelling, tent, or vessel used for habitation or custody of property), carrying a higher maximum punishment than Section 379.
essential_elements: The accused has committed theft as defined under Section 378.
The theft is committed in a building, tent, or vessel.
Such building, tent, or vessel is used as a human dwelling or for the custody of property.

mens_rea.description: Dishonest intention to take movable property out of another's possession without consent, as required for theft under Section 378, in respect of property within a dwelling or place of custody.

examples.scenario: A enters Z's house and dishonestly takes Z's jewellery without consent.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 380 with imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine
ai_summary: Section 380 IPC punishes theft committed in a building, tent, or vessel used as a dwelling or for custody of property with imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.

title: Theft by Clerk or Servant of Property in Possession of Master
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
bare_act_text: Whoever, being a clerk or servant, or being employed in the capacity of a clerk or servant, commits theft in respect of any property in the possession of his master or employer, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.
meaning: Section 381 IPC punishes theft committed by a clerk or servant in respect of property belonging to or in the possession of their master or employer.
simple_explanation: If a person employed as a clerk or servant steals property that belongs to or is in the possession of their employer, they can be punished with imprisonment up to 7 years along with a fine—a higher punishment than ordinary theft due to the breach of trust involved.
objective: To provide enhanced punishment for theft involving a breach of the trust relationship between an employer and employee.
legal_significance: Creates an aggravated form of theft based on the relationship between the offender and the victim (employee stealing from employer), reflecting the additional element of betrayal of trust.
essential_elements: The accused is, or is employed in the capacity of, a clerk or servant.
The accused commits theft as defined under Section 378.
The property stolen is in the possession of the accused's master or employer.

mens_rea.description: Dishonest intention to take the master's or employer's property out of possession without consent, as required for theft under Section 378.

examples.scenario: A, employed as a clerk by Z, dishonestly takes cash from Z's office without Z's consent.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 381 with imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine
ai_summary: Section 381 IPC punishes theft committed by a clerk or servant in respect of property in the possession of their master or employer with imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.

title: Theft After Preparation Made for Causing Death, Hurt or Restraint in Order to the Committing of the Theft
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
bare_act_text: Whoever commits theft, having made preparation for causing death, or hurt, or restraint, or fear of death, or of hurt, or of restraint, to any person, in order to the committing of such theft, or in order to the effecting of his escape after the committing of such theft, or in order to the retaining of property taken by such theft, shall be punished with rigorous imprisonment for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.
meaning: Section 382 IPC punishes theft committed after the offender has made preparation for causing death, hurt, or restraint (or fear thereof) in order to commit the theft, escape after it, or retain the stolen property.
simple_explanation: If a person, before or while committing theft, has prepared to cause death, injury, or confinement (or to create fear of these) in order to carry out the theft, escape afterward, or keep the stolen goods, the punishment is more severe—rigorous imprisonment of up to 10 years along with a fine.
objective: To impose enhanced punishment on theft involving advance preparation for violence, recognizing the greater threat posed to the victim's safety.
legal_significance: Creates an aggravated form of theft based on the offender's preparedness to use or threaten violence in connection with the theft, even if such violence is not ultimately used.
essential_elements: The accused commits theft as defined under Section 378.
The accused has made preparation for causing death, hurt, or restraint, or fear of death, hurt, or restraint to any person.
Such preparation is made in order to commit the theft, to effect escape after the theft, or to retain the stolen property.

mens_rea.description: Dishonest intention to commit theft, coupled with advance preparation to cause or threaten death, hurt, or restraint for the purpose of committing the theft, escaping, or retaining the property.

examples.scenario: A commits theft on Z's property while carrying a loaded pistol, having provided it for the purpose of hurting Z if Z resists.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 382 with rigorous imprisonment up to 10 years and fine.

examples.scenario: A picks Z's pocket after positioning several companions nearby to restrain Z if Z notices and attempts to apprehend A.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 382 with rigorous imprisonment up to 10 years and fine.

max_punishment: Rigorous imprisonment up to 10 years and fine
ai_summary: Section 382 IPC punishes theft committed with advance preparation for causing death, hurt, or restraint (in order to commit the theft, escape, or retain stolen property) with rigorous imprisonment up to 10 years and fine.

title: Extortion
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
bare_act_text: Whoever intentionally puts any person in fear of any injury to that person, or to any other, and thereby dishonestly induces the person so put in fear to deliver to any person any property, or valuable security or anything signed or sealed which may be converted into a valuable security, commits "extortion".
meaning: Section 383 IPC defines extortion as intentionally putting a person in fear of injury and thereby dishonestly inducing them to deliver property, valuable security, or anything convertible into valuable security.
simple_explanation: Extortion happens when someone intentionally threatens another person with harm (to themselves or someone else), and because of that fear, dishonestly gets them to hand over property, money, or a document that could become a valuable security.
objective: To define the offence of extortion, distinguishing it from theft by the element of inducement through fear rather than taking without consent.
legal_significance: Establishes the foundational definition of extortion, which forms the basis for the punishment provisions in Sections 384-389 and is also a key element in defining robbery under Section 390.
essential_elements: The accused intentionally puts a person in fear of injury to that person or to any other person.
Such fear dishonestly induces the person to deliver property, valuable security, or anything signed/sealed convertible into valuable security.
The delivery is made to any person.

mens_rea.description: Intentional creation of fear of injury, coupled with dishonest intent to induce delivery of property or valuable security.

examples.scenario: A threatens to publish a defamatory libel concerning Z unless Z gives him money, and Z gives the money.
examples.result: A has committed extortion as defined under Section 383.

examples.scenario: A, by putting Z in fear of grievous hurt, dishonestly induces Z to sign a blank paper and deliver it to A.
examples.result: A has committed extortion, as the signed paper may be converted into a valuable security.

max_punishment: Not applicable (definition section; punishment prescribed under Section 384)
ai_summary: Section 383 IPC defines extortion as intentionally inducing fear of injury to dishonestly compel a person to deliver property, valuable security, or any document convertible into a valuable security.

title: Punishment for Extortion
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
bare_act_text: Whoever commits extortion shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both.
meaning: Section 384 IPC prescribes the punishment for the offence of extortion as defined under Section 383.
simple_explanation: Anyone who commits extortion can be punished with imprisonment of up to 3 years, or a fine, or both.
objective: To prescribe the general punishment for the basic offence of extortion.
legal_significance: Provides the standard punishment for simple extortion, which is enhanced under subsequent sections (386, 388, 389) where the threat involves death, grievous hurt, or accusation of serious offences.
essential_elements: The accused has committed extortion as defined under Section 383.

mens_rea.description: Intentional creation of fear of injury coupled with dishonest intent to induce delivery of property, as required under Section 383.

examples.scenario: A threatens to defame Z unless Z pays him money, and Z pays.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 384 with imprisonment up to 3 years, or fine, or both.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 years, or fine, or both
ai_summary: Section 384 IPC prescribes punishment for extortion as imprisonment up to 3 years, or fine, or both.

title: Putting Person in Fear of Injury in Order to Commit Extortion
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
bare_act_text: Whoever, in order to the committing of extortion, puts any person in fear, or attempts to put any person in fear, of any injury, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.
meaning: Section 385 IPC punishes the act of putting or attempting to put a person in fear of injury for the purpose of committing extortion, even if the extortion itself is not completed.
simple_explanation: If someone threatens or tries to threaten another person with harm in order to extort property from them, even if the extortion does not succeed, they can still be punished with imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both.
objective: To penalize preparatory acts of creating fear with the purpose of committing extortion, even where the extortion is not completed.
legal_significance: Criminalizes the attempt or preparatory step of inducing fear for extortion as a standalone offence with a lesser punishment than completed extortion.
essential_elements: The accused puts, or attempts to put, a person in fear of any injury.
Such act is done in order to commit extortion.

mens_rea.description: Intention to commit extortion through creating or attempting to create fear of injury in the victim.

examples.scenario: A threatens Z with harm intending to later demand money from Z, but Z does not part with any property.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 385 with imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both
ai_summary: Section 385 IPC punishes putting or attempting to put a person in fear of injury in order to commit extortion, with imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both.

title: Extortion by Putting a Person in Fear of Death or Grievous Hurt
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
bare_act_text: Whoever commits extortion by putting any person in fear of death or of grievous hurt to that person or to any other, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.
meaning: Section 386 IPC punishes extortion committed by putting a person in fear of death or grievous hurt to themselves or another person, with enhanced punishment compared to ordinary extortion.
simple_explanation: If a person commits extortion by threatening someone with death or serious injury (to that person or someone else), the punishment is more severe—imprisonment up to 10 years along with a fine.
objective: To impose a higher punishment for extortion involving the most serious threats, namely death or grievous hurt.
legal_significance: Creates an aggravated category of extortion based on the severity of the threat used, carrying a significantly higher maximum punishment than Section 384.
essential_elements: The accused commits extortion as defined under Section 383.
The extortion is committed by putting a person in fear of death or grievous hurt.
Such fear may relate to the person himself or to any other person.

mens_rea.description: Intentional creation of fear of death or grievous hurt, coupled with dishonest intent to induce delivery of property, as required for extortion.

examples.scenario: A threatens to kill Z unless Z hands over his property, and Z complies out of fear.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 386 with imprisonment up to 10 years and fine.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 10 years and fine
ai_summary: Section 386 IPC punishes extortion committed by putting a person in fear of death or grievous hurt with imprisonment up to 10 years and fine.

title: Putting Person in Fear of Death or of Grievous Hurt, in Order to Commit Extortion
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
bare_act_text: Whoever, in order to the committing of extortion, puts or attempts to put any person in fear of death or of grievous hurt to that person or to any other, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.
meaning: Section 387 IPC punishes putting or attempting to put a person in fear of death or grievous hurt for the purpose of committing extortion, even if the extortion is not completed.
simple_explanation: If someone threatens or attempts to threaten another person with death or serious injury in order to extort property, even if they don't succeed in getting the property, they can be punished with imprisonment up to 7 years along with a fine.
objective: To penalize the serious preparatory act of threatening death or grievous hurt for the purpose of extortion, regardless of whether the extortion is completed.
legal_significance: Creates an aggravated counterpart to Section 385, applicable where the threat used (or attempted) is one of death or grievous hurt, carrying a higher punishment.
essential_elements: The accused puts, or attempts to put, a person in fear of death or grievous hurt.
Such fear may relate to that person or to any other person.
The act is done in order to commit extortion.

mens_rea.description: Intention to commit extortion through creating or attempting to create fear of death or grievous hurt in the victim or another person.

examples.scenario: A attempts to threaten Z with death intending to extort money, but Z refuses and no property is delivered.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 387 with imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine
ai_summary: Section 387 IPC punishes putting or attempting to put a person in fear of death or grievous hurt in order to commit extortion, with imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.

title: Extortion by Threat of Accusation of an Offence Punishable with Death or Imprisonment for Life, etc.
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
bare_act_text: Whoever commits extortion by putting any person in fear of an accusation against that person or any other, of having committed or attempted to commit any offence punishable with death, or with imprisonment for life, or with imprisonment for a term which may extend to ten years, or of having attempted to induce any other person to commit such offence, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine; and, if the offence be one punishable under section 377 of this Code, may be punished with imprisonment for life.
meaning: Section 388 IPC punishes extortion committed by threatening a person with an accusation of having committed (or attempted to commit) a serious offence punishable with death, life imprisonment, or imprisonment up to ten years.
simple_explanation: If a person commits extortion by threatening to accuse someone (or another person) of having committed a serious crime—one punishable by death, life imprisonment, or up to 10 years' imprisonment—the punishment can extend to 10 years' imprisonment and fine; if the threatened accusation relates to an offence under Section 377, the punishment may extend to life imprisonment.
objective: To impose enhanced punishment for extortion carried out through the particularly coercive threat of accusing someone of a grave criminal offence.
legal_significance: Creates an aggravated category of extortion based on the nature of the threat (false or coercive accusation of a serious crime), with a special enhanced punishment of life imprisonment where the underlying accusation relates to Section 377.
essential_elements: The accused commits extortion as defined under Section 383.
The extortion is committed by putting a person in fear of an accusation against that person or another.
The accusation relates to having committed, attempted to commit, or attempted to induce another to commit an offence punishable with death, life imprisonment, or imprisonment up to ten years.

mens_rea.description: Intentional creation of fear of a serious criminal accusation, coupled with dishonest intent to induce delivery of property, as required for extortion.

aggravated_case.imprisonment: Imprisonment for life (where the threatened accusation relates to an offence under Section 377)

examples.scenario: A extorts money from Z by threatening to falsely accuse Z of an offence punishable with life imprisonment.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 388 with imprisonment up to 10 years and fine.

max_punishment: Imprisonment for life (where related to Section 377 accusation), otherwise up to 10 years, and fine
ai_summary: Section 388 IPC punishes extortion committed by threatening a person with accusation of a serious offence (punishable with death, life imprisonment, or up to 10 years) with imprisonment up to 10 years and fine, extendable to life imprisonment where the accusation relates to Section 377.

title: Putting Person in Fear of Accusation of Offence, in Order to Commit Extortion
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
bare_act_text: Whoever, in order to the committing of extortion, puts or attempts to put any person in fear of an accusation, against that person or any other, of having committed, or attempted to commit, an offence punishable with death or with imprisonment for life, or with imprisonment for a term which may extend to ten years, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine; and, if the offence be punishable under section 377 of this Code, may be punished with imprisonment for life.
meaning: Section 389 IPC punishes putting or attempting to put a person in fear of accusation of a serious offence in order to commit extortion, even if the extortion is not completed.
simple_explanation: If a person threatens or attempts to threaten someone with accusation of a serious crime (punishable by death, life imprisonment, or up to 10 years) in order to extort property, even without success, they can be punished with imprisonment up to 10 years and fine, extendable to life imprisonment if the accusation relates to Section 377.
objective: To penalize the serious preparatory act of threatening accusation of a grave offence for the purpose of extortion, regardless of whether the extortion is completed.
legal_significance: Creates an aggravated counterpart to Section 385/387, applicable where the threat used (or attempted) is an accusation of a serious offence, with the same enhanced punishment structure as Section 388.
essential_elements: The accused puts, or attempts to put, a person in fear of an accusation against that person or another.
The accusation relates to having committed, or attempted to commit, an offence punishable with death, life imprisonment, or imprisonment up to ten years.
The act is done in order to commit extortion.

mens_rea.description: Intention to commit extortion through creating or attempting to create fear of a serious criminal accusation against the victim or another person.

aggravated_case.imprisonment: Imprisonment for life (where the threatened accusation relates to an offence under Section 377)

examples.scenario: A attempts to threaten Z with a false accusation of a crime punishable by life imprisonment, in order to extort money, but fails to obtain anything.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 389 with imprisonment up to 10 years and fine.

max_punishment: Imprisonment for life (where related to Section 377 accusation), otherwise up to 10 years, and fine
ai_summary: Section 389 IPC punishes putting or attempting to put a person in fear of accusation of a serious offence in order to commit extortion, with imprisonment up to 10 years and fine, extendable to life imprisonment where the accusation relates to Section 377.

title: Robbery
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
bare_act_text: In all robbery there is either theft or extortion. When theft is robbery.—Theft is "robbery" if, in order to the committing of the theft, or in committing the theft, or in carrying away or attempting to carry away property obtained by the theft, the offender, for that end voluntarily causes or attempts to cause to any person death or hurt or wrongful restraint, or fear of instant death or of instant hurt, or of instant wrongful restraint. When extortion is robbery.—Extortion is "robbery" if the offender, at the time of committing the extortion, is in the presence of the person put in fear, and commits the extortion by putting that person in fear of instant death, of instant hurt, or of instant wrongful restraint to that person or to some other person, and, by so putting in fear, induces the person so put in fear then and there to deliver up the thing extorted. Explanation.—The offender is said to be present if he is sufficiently near to put the other person in fear of instant death, of instant hurt, or of instant wrongful restraint.
meaning: Section 390 IPC defines robbery as an aggravated form of either theft or extortion, distinguished by the use or threat of immediate force, violence, or restraint.
simple_explanation: Robbery is either theft accompanied by the offender voluntarily causing or threatening instant death, hurt, or wrongful restraint to carry out the theft or escape with the stolen property, or it is extortion where the offender is physically present and uses the threat of instant death, hurt, or restraint to make the victim hand over property immediately.
objective: To define robbery as a distinct, more severe offence than simple theft or extortion, based on the element of immediate violence or threat of violence.
legal_significance: Forms the foundational definition for robbery and dacoity (Sections 391-402), establishing the dividing line between simple theft/extortion and their aggravated, violence-linked counterparts.
essential_elements: Either theft or extortion is committed.

For theft to be robbery: in committing the theft, attempting it, or carrying away/attempting to carry away the stolen property, the offender voluntarily causes or attempts to cause death, hurt, or wrongful restraint, or fear of instant death, hurt, or wrongful restraint.
For extortion to be robbery: the offender is present before the person put in fear, and induces immediate delivery of the property by creating fear of instant death, hurt, or wrongful restraint.

mens_rea.description: Dishonest intention as required for theft or extortion, combined with the voluntary causing of, or attempt to cause, death, hurt, wrongful restraint, or fear of the same, occurring instantly and in connection with the offence.

examples.scenario: A holds Z down and fraudulently takes Z's money and jewels from Z's clothes without consent, causing wrongful restraint to Z in order to commit the theft.
examples.result: A has committed robbery under Section 390.

examples.scenario: A meets Z on the highway, shows a pistol, and demands Z's purse; Z surrenders the purse out of fear of instant hurt.
examples.result: A has committed robbery under Section 390, as the extortion was committed in A's presence with fear of instant hurt.

max_punishment: Not applicable (definition section; punishment prescribed under Section 392)
ai_summary: Section 390 IPC defines robbery as theft or extortion aggravated by the offender voluntarily causing or threatening instant death, hurt, or wrongful restraint in connection with the offence.

title: Dacoity
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
bare_act_text: When five or more persons conjointly commit or attempt to commit a robbery, or where the whole number of persons conjointly committing or attempting to commit a robbery, and persons present and aiding such commission or attempt, amount to five or more, every person so committing, attempting or aiding, is said to commit "dacoity".
meaning: Section 391 IPC defines dacoity as robbery committed (or attempted) conjointly by five or more persons, including those present and aiding the commission or attempt.
simple_explanation: Dacoity is robbery committed by a group of five or more people acting together, or where the total number of people involved—either committing, attempting, or aiding/present during the robbery—is five or more.
objective: To define dacoity as a distinct, more serious form of group robbery, reflecting the heightened danger posed by organized group crime.
legal_significance: Establishes the numerical threshold (five or more persons) that elevates robbery to the more severe offence of dacoity, attracting harsher punishment under subsequent sections.
essential_elements: A robbery is committed or attempted.
The robbery is committed or attempted conjointly by five or more persons, or the total number of persons committing, attempting, or present and aiding amounts to five or more.
Every such person is said to commit dacoity.

mens_rea.description: Mens rea as required for robbery (theft or extortion accompanied by voluntary causing or threat of death, hurt, or wrongful restraint), combined with conjoint participation by five or more persons.

examples.scenario: A group of six men together rob a traveller on the highway, using force and threats.
examples.result: Each of the six men has committed dacoity as defined under Section 391.

max_punishment: Not applicable (definition section; punishment prescribed under Section 395)
ai_summary: Section 391 IPC defines dacoity as robbery committed or attempted conjointly by five or more persons, including those present and aiding.

title: Punishment for Robbery
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
bare_act_text: Whoever commits robbery shall be punished with rigorous imprisonment for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine; and, if the robbery be committed on the highway between sunset and sunrise, the imprisonment may be extended to fourteen years.
meaning: Section 392 IPC prescribes the punishment for robbery, with an enhanced maximum sentence if the robbery is committed on the highway between sunset and sunrise.
simple_explanation: Anyone who commits robbery can be punished with rigorous imprisonment up to 10 years and a fine; if the robbery happens on a highway at night (between sunset and sunrise), the imprisonment can go up to 14 years.
objective: To prescribe punishment for robbery, with a higher maximum sentence for robberies committed on highways at night due to the increased risk and vulnerability of victims.
legal_significance: Provides the standard punishment for robbery as defined under Section 390, with a specific time-and-place-based enhancement (nighttime highway robbery) reflecting greater culpability.
essential_elements: The accused has committed robbery as defined under Section 390.
For the enhanced punishment: the robbery is committed on the highway between sunset and sunrise.

mens_rea.description: Mens rea as required for robbery under Section 390 (dishonest intention for theft or extortion, combined with voluntary causing or threat of death, hurt, or wrongful restraint).

aggravated_case.imprisonment: Rigorous imprisonment up to 14 years (if committed on the highway between sunset and sunrise)

examples.scenario: A robs Z of his wallet by threatening him with instant hurt during the daytime.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 392 with rigorous imprisonment up to 10 years and fine.

examples.scenario: A robs Z on a highway at night, between sunset and sunrise.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 392 with rigorous imprisonment up to 14 years and fine.

max_punishment: Rigorous imprisonment up to 14 years (highway robbery at night) or up to 10 years (otherwise), and fine
ai_summary: Section 392 IPC punishes robbery with rigorous imprisonment up to 10 years and fine, extendable to 14 years if the robbery is committed on the highway between sunset and sunrise.

title: Attempt to Commit Robbery
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
bare_act_text: Whoever attempts to commit robbery shall be punished with rigorous imprisonment for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.
meaning: Section 393 IPC punishes the attempt to commit robbery, even if the robbery itself is not completed.
simple_explanation: If someone tries to commit a robbery but does not succeed, they can still be punished with rigorous imprisonment up to 7 years along with a fine.
objective: To criminalize and penalize attempts at robbery, ensuring that the offence does not need to be completed for punishment to apply.
legal_significance: Provides a lesser punishment than completed robbery (Section 392) while still treating the attempt as a serious offence.
essential_elements: The accused attempts to commit robbery as defined under Section 390.
The robbery is not completed.

mens_rea.description: Intention to commit robbery, with steps taken towards its commission though not completed.

examples.scenario: A attempts to snatch Z's bag using force but is stopped before succeeding.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 393 with rigorous imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.

max_punishment: Rigorous imprisonment up to 7 years and fine
ai_summary: Section 393 IPC punishes attempted robbery with rigorous imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.

title: Voluntarily Causing Hurt in Committing Robbery
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
bare_act_text: If any person, in committing or in attempting to commit robbery, voluntarily causes hurt, such person, and any other person jointly concerned in committing or attempting to commit such robbery, shall be punished with imprisonment for life, or with rigorous imprisonment for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.
meaning: Section 394 IPC punishes any person who voluntarily causes hurt while committing or attempting to commit robbery, along with every other person jointly involved in that robbery or attempt.
simple_explanation: If, during a robbery (or attempted robbery), any person voluntarily causes hurt to someone, then not only that person but everyone else jointly involved in the robbery can be punished with life imprisonment or rigorous imprisonment up to 10 years, along with a fine.
objective: To impose enhanced and joint liability on all participants in a robbery where hurt is voluntarily caused by any one of them, deterring violence during robberies.
legal_significance: Creates joint liability for all persons concerned in a robbery if any one of them voluntarily causes hurt, regardless of who actually inflicted the hurt, and prescribes a significantly enhanced punishment compared to simple robbery.
essential_elements: A robbery is committed or attempted.
In the course of committing or attempting it, any person voluntarily causes hurt.
The accused is either the person who caused the hurt, or any other person jointly concerned in committing or attempting the robbery.

mens_rea.description: Intention to commit robbery, combined with the voluntary causing of hurt by any participant during the commission or attempt of the robbery.

normal_case.imprisonment: Imprisonment for life, or rigorous imprisonment up to 10 years

examples.scenario: During a robbery committed jointly by A and B, A voluntarily causes hurt to the victim while B stands guard.
examples.result: Both A and B are liable under Section 394 with imprisonment for life or rigorous imprisonment up to 10 years, and fine.

max_punishment: Imprisonment for life, or rigorous imprisonment up to 10 years, and fine
ai_summary: Section 394 IPC punishes any person who voluntarily causes hurt during the commission or attempt of robbery, and every person jointly concerned in that robbery, with imprisonment for life or rigorous imprisonment up to 10 years, and fine.

title: Punishment for Dacoity
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
bare_act_text: Whoever commits dacoity shall be punished with imprisonment for life, or with rigorous imprisonment for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.
meaning: Section 395 IPC prescribes the punishment for the offence of dacoity as defined under Section 391.
simple_explanation: Anyone who commits dacoity (robbery by five or more persons acting together) can be punished with life imprisonment or rigorous imprisonment up to 10 years, along with a fine.
objective: To prescribe a stringent punishment for dacoity, reflecting the heightened danger posed by organized group robbery.
legal_significance: Provides the standard punishment for dacoity as defined under Section 391, which is further enhanced under subsequent sections (396) where murder is committed during dacoity.
essential_elements: The accused has committed dacoity as defined under Section 391 (robbery by five or more persons conjointly, including those present and aiding).

mens_rea.description: Mens rea as required for robbery under Section 390, combined with conjoint participation by five or more persons as required for dacoity under Section 391.

normal_case.imprisonment: Imprisonment for life, or rigorous imprisonment up to 10 years

examples.scenario: A group of six persons conjointly commit robbery on a traveller using force and threats.
examples.result: Each of the six persons is liable under Section 395 with imprisonment for life or rigorous imprisonment up to 10 years, and fine.

max_punishment: Imprisonment for life, or rigorous imprisonment up to 10 years, and fine
ai_summary: Section 395 IPC punishes dacoity with imprisonment for life, or rigorous imprisonment up to 10 years, and fine.

title: Dacoity with Murder
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
bare_act_text: If any one of five or more persons, who are conjointly committing dacoity, commits murder in so committing dacoity, every one of those persons shall be punished with death, or imprisonment for life, or rigorous imprisonment for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.
meaning: Section 396 IPC punishes every member of a group of five or more persons conjointly committing dacoity, with death, life imprisonment, or rigorous imprisonment up to 10 years, if any one of them commits murder during the dacoity.
simple_explanation: If a group of five or more people commit dacoity together and any one of them murders someone during the act, then every member of the group can be punished with death, life imprisonment, or rigorous imprisonment up to 10 years, along with a fine—regardless of who actually committed the murder.
objective: To impose the harshest possible collective liability on all members of a dacoity gang when murder occurs during the commission of the dacoity, deterring such extreme violence.
legal_significance: Establishes constructive/vicarious liability for murder on all members of a dacoity gang, even those who did not personally commit the killing, with punishment extending up to death.
essential_elements: Five or more persons conjointly commit dacoity.
In the course of committing such dacoity, any one of those persons commits murder.
Every one of those persons (the whole group) is liable, regardless of who committed the murder.

mens_rea.description: Participation with intent in the conjoint commission of dacoity; liability for murder extends to all members constructively once murder occurs during the dacoity, irrespective of individual intent to kill.

normal_case.imprisonment: Imprisonment for life, or rigorous imprisonment up to 10 years

aggravated_case.imprisonment: Death

examples.scenario: A gang of six persons commits dacoity at a house, and during the act, one member kills the homeowner.
examples.result: All six persons are liable under Section 396 with punishment of death, imprisonment for life, or rigorous imprisonment up to 10 years, and fine.

max_punishment: Death
ai_summary: Section 396 IPC punishes every member of a group of five or more persons conjointly committing dacoity with death, life imprisonment, or rigorous imprisonment up to 10 years, and fine, if any one of them commits murder during the dacoity.

title: Robbery, or Dacoity, with Attempt to Cause Death or Grievous Hurt
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
bare_act_text: If, at the time of committing robbery or dacoity, the offender uses any deadly weapon, or causes grievous hurt to any person, or attempts to cause death or grievous hurt to any person, the imprisonment with which such offender shall be punished shall not be less than seven years.
meaning: Section 397 IPC prescribes a mandatory minimum sentence of 7 years' imprisonment where, during the commission of robbery or dacoity, the offender uses a deadly weapon, causes grievous hurt, or attempts to cause death or grievous hurt to any person.
simple_explanation: If, while committing robbery or dacoity, the offender uses a deadly weapon, causes serious injury, or tries to kill or seriously injure someone, the minimum punishment is 7 years' imprisonment—there is no discretion to give less.
objective: To impose a mandatory minimum sentence for robbery or dacoity involving the use of deadly weapons or attempts/infliction of serious harm, ensuring deterrence against violent robberies.
legal_significance: Establishes a mandatory minimum sentence (a floor, not a ceiling) that applies to the specific offender who used the deadly weapon or caused/attempted grievous hurt, distinguishing it from the joint liability provision of Section 394.
essential_elements: Robbery or dacoity is committed.
At the time of committing it, the offender uses any deadly weapon, OR causes grievous hurt to any person, OR attempts to cause death or grievous hurt to any person.

mens_rea.description: Intention to commit robbery or dacoity, combined with the use of a deadly weapon or the intent/attempt to cause death or grievous hurt.

normal_case.imprisonment: Not less than 7 years (no specified upper limit beyond general robbery/dacoity punishment provisions)

examples.scenario: While committing robbery, A brandishes a knife (a deadly weapon) and causes grievous hurt to the victim.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 397 with a minimum imprisonment of 7 years.

max_punishment: Not specified (minimum 7 years' imprisonment mandatory)
ai_summary: Section 397 IPC mandates a minimum sentence of 7 years' imprisonment where the offender, while committing robbery or dacoity, uses a deadly weapon, causes grievous hurt, or attempts to cause death or grievous hurt.

title: Attempt to Commit Robbery or Dacoity When Armed with Deadly Weapon
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
bare_act_text: If, at the time of attempting to commit robbery or dacoity, the offender is armed with any deadly weapon, the imprisonment with which such offender shall be punished shall not be less than seven years.
meaning: Section 398 IPC prescribes a mandatory minimum sentence of 7 years' imprisonment where the offender is armed with a deadly weapon while attempting to commit robbery or dacoity.
simple_explanation: If a person attempts to commit robbery or dacoity while carrying a deadly weapon, the minimum punishment is 7 years' imprisonment, even if the weapon was not actually used.
objective: To impose a mandatory minimum sentence for attempted robbery or dacoity where the offender is armed with a deadly weapon, deterring armed criminal attempts.
legal_significance: Establishes a mandatory minimum sentence based merely on being armed with a deadly weapon during an attempted robbery or dacoity, regardless of whether the weapon was actually used or any hurt was caused.
essential_elements: The accused attempts to commit robbery or dacoity.
At the time of such attempt, the offender is armed with any deadly weapon.

mens_rea.description: Intention to commit robbery or dacoity, combined with being armed with a deadly weapon at the time of the attempt.

normal_case.imprisonment: Not less than 7 years (no specified upper limit beyond general attempted robbery/dacoity punishment provisions)

examples.scenario: A, carrying a loaded firearm, attempts to commit dacoity but is apprehended before completing the offence.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 398 with a minimum imprisonment of 7 years.

max_punishment: Not specified (minimum 7 years' imprisonment mandatory)
ai_summary: Section 398 IPC mandates a minimum sentence of 7 years' imprisonment where the offender is armed with a deadly weapon while attempting to commit robbery or dacoity.

title: Making Preparation to Commit Dacoity
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
bare_act_text: Whoever makes any preparation for committing dacoity, shall be punished with rigorous imprisonment for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.
meaning: Section 399 IPC punishes the act of making preparations to commit dacoity, even before the offence itself is attempted.
simple_explanation: If a person makes preparations to commit dacoity—such as gathering weapons, planning, or arranging resources—they can be punished with rigorous imprisonment up to 10 years and a fine, even if the dacoity is never attempted.
objective: To criminalize preparatory acts towards dacoity at an early stage, allowing intervention before the offence is carried out.
legal_significance: Extends criminal liability to the preparatory stage of dacoity, which is generally not punishable for most offences under the IPC, reflecting the seriousness with which organized group robbery is treated.
essential_elements: The accused makes preparation for committing dacoity.
The preparation need not result in an attempt or commission of dacoity.

mens_rea.description: Intention to commit dacoity, evidenced by preparatory acts undertaken towards that end.

examples.scenario: A group of persons gather weapons and plan routes in preparation for committing dacoity at a house, but are arrested before any attempt.
examples.result: Each person is liable under Section 399 with rigorous imprisonment up to 10 years and fine.

max_punishment: Rigorous imprisonment up to 10 years and fine
ai_summary: Section 399 IPC punishes the making of preparations to commit dacoity with rigorous imprisonment up to 10 years and fine.

title: Punishment for Belonging to Gang of Dacoits
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
bare_act_text: Whoever, at any time after the passing of this Act, shall belong to a gang of persons associated for the purpose of habitually committing dacoity, shall be punished with imprisonment for life, or with rigorous imprisonment for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.
meaning: Section 400 IPC punishes mere membership in a gang of persons associated for the purpose of habitually committing dacoity.
simple_explanation: If a person belongs to a gang that is organized for the purpose of habitually committing dacoity, that mere membership is punishable with life imprisonment or rigorous imprisonment up to 10 years, along with a fine—even without proof of participation in a specific dacoity.
objective: To criminalize and dismantle organized criminal gangs associated for habitual dacoity by punishing membership itself, irrespective of participation in any particular offence.
legal_significance: Creates a status-based offence (mere membership of a habitual dacoit gang) rather than an act-based offence, allowing prosecution without proof of a specific dacoity committed by the accused.
essential_elements: The accused belongs to a gang of persons.
Such gang is associated for the purpose of habitually committing dacoity.
The accused's membership occurs at any time after the passing of the Act.

mens_rea.description: Knowledge of and voluntary association with a gang organized for the purpose of habitually committing dacoity.

normal_case.imprisonment: Imprisonment for life, or rigorous imprisonment up to 10 years

examples.scenario: A is found to be a member of a gang that has been habitually committing dacoity in a region, even though A's specific role in any single dacoity is not separately proven.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 400 with imprisonment for life or rigorous imprisonment up to 10 years, and fine.

max_punishment: Imprisonment for life, or rigorous imprisonment up to 10 years, and fine
ai_summary: Section 400 IPC punishes mere membership of a gang of persons associated for the purpose of habitually committing dacoity, with imprisonment for life or rigorous imprisonment up to 10 years, and fine.

title: Punishment for Belonging to Gang of Thieves
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
bare_act_text: Whoever, at any time after the passing of this Act, shall belong to any wandering or other gang of persons associated for the purpose of habitually committing theft or robbery, and not being a gang of thugs or dacoits, shall be punished with rigorous imprisonment for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.
meaning: Section 401 IPC punishes mere membership in a wandering or other gang of persons associated for the purpose of habitually committing theft or robbery (not being a gang of thugs or dacoits).
simple_explanation: If a person belongs to a gang—whether wandering or settled—that is organized for habitually committing theft or robbery (but not a gang of thugs or dacoits, which are covered elsewhere), that membership itself is punishable with rigorous imprisonment up to 7 years and a fine.
objective: To criminalize membership in organized gangs habitually engaged in theft or robbery, distinct from the more serious offences of thuggee or dacoity.
legal_significance: Creates a status-based offence similar to Section 400 but applicable to gangs habitually committing theft or robbery (lesser offences than dacoity), with a correspondingly lower maximum punishment.
essential_elements: The accused belongs to a wandering or other gang of persons.
Such gang is associated for the purpose of habitually committing theft or robbery.
The gang is not a gang of thugs or dacoits.
The accused's membership occurs at any time after the passing of the Act.

mens_rea.description: Knowledge of and voluntary association with a gang organized for the purpose of habitually committing theft or robbery.

examples.scenario: A is found to be a member of a wandering gang that habitually commits thefts in various towns, though not proven to be a gang of dacoits.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 401 with rigorous imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.

max_punishment: Rigorous imprisonment up to 7 years and fine
ai_summary: Section 401 IPC punishes mere membership of a wandering or other gang associated for habitually committing theft or robbery (not being a gang of thugs or dacoits) with rigorous imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.

title: Assembling for Purpose of Committing Dacoity
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
bare_act_text: Whoever, at any time after the passing of this Act, shall be one of five or more persons assembled for the purpose of committing dacoity, shall be punished with rigorous imprisonment for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.
meaning: Section 402 IPC punishes any person who is one of five or more persons assembled for the purpose of committing dacoity, even if the dacoity is not attempted or committed.
simple_explanation: If five or more people gather together with the purpose of committing dacoity, every one of them can be punished with rigorous imprisonment up to 7 years and a fine, even if they don't go on to attempt or commit the dacoity.
objective: To allow early intervention against organized group crime by criminalizing the mere assembly of five or more persons for the purpose of committing dacoity.
legal_significance: Criminalizes an even earlier preparatory stage than Section 399 (preparation), focusing on the act of assembling with the requisite common purpose, regardless of whether any further steps are taken.
essential_elements: Five or more persons are assembled together.
The purpose of such assembly is to commit dacoity.
The accused is one of those five or more persons.

mens_rea.description: Common purpose or intention to commit dacoity, shared by the assembled persons.

examples.scenario: A group of seven persons assemble at a location with the shared purpose of committing dacoity at a nearby house, but are arrested before taking further action.
examples.result: Each of the seven persons is liable under Section 402 with rigorous imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.

max_punishment: Rigorous imprisonment up to 7 years and fine
ai_summary: Section 402 IPC punishes any person who is one of five or more persons assembled for the purpose of committing dacoity, with rigorous imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.

title: Dishonest Misappropriation of Property
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
bare_act_text: Whoever dishonestly misappropriates or converts to his own use any movable property, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both. Explanation 1.—A dishonest misappropriation for a time only is a misappropriation within the meaning of this section. Explanation 2.—A person who finds property not in the possession of any other person, and takes such property for the purpose of protecting it for, or of restoring it to, the owner, does not take or misappropriate it dishonestly, and is not guilty of an offence; but he is guilty of the offence above defined, if he appropriates it to his own use, when he knows or has the means of discovering the owner, or before he has used reasonable means to discover and give notice to the owner and has kept the property a reasonable time to enable the owner to claim it. What are reasonable means or what is a reasonable time in such a case, is a question of fact. It is not necessary that the finder should know who is the owner of the property, or that any particular person is the owner of it; it is sufficient if, at the time of appropriating it, he does not believe it to be his own property, or in good faith believe that the real owner cannot be found.
meaning: Section 403 IPC punishes the dishonest misappropriation or conversion of movable property to one's own use, including property found and later dishonestly appropriated.
simple_explanation: If a person dishonestly keeps or uses movable property belonging to someone else as their own—even if they initially obtained it innocently, such as by finding it—they can be punished with imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both. Even a temporary dishonest use counts as misappropriation. A genuine finder who tries to return property to its owner is not guilty unless they later dishonestly keep it despite knowing or being able to discover the owner.
objective: To punish dishonest retention or conversion of another's movable property where the initial taking may not amount to theft, particularly covering situations involving found property.
legal_significance: Covers situations distinct from theft (Section 378), where the property may have come into the accused's possession lawfully or innocently, but is subsequently dishonestly retained or converted, including detailed guidance on the duties of a finder of lost property.
essential_elements: The property in question is movable property.
The accused dishonestly misappropriates or converts such property to his own use.
Dishonest misappropriation for even a temporary period suffices.
Where the property was found, the finder becomes liable only if he appropriates it knowing or having means to discover the owner, or without using reasonable means/time to locate the owner.

mens_rea.description: Dishonest intention to misappropriate or convert movable property to one's own use, which may arise even after an initially innocent acquisition (such as finding the property).

examples.scenario: A finds a letter on the road containing a bank note, learns from the letter to whom it belongs, and appropriates the note for himself.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 403 with imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both.

examples.scenario: A takes property believing in good faith it belongs to him, but after discovering it belongs to Z, dishonestly appropriates it to his own use.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 403, as the dishonest appropriation occurred after discovering the mistake.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both
compoundable: Yes (compoundable by the owner of the property with permission of the court)
ai_summary: Section 403 IPC punishes dishonest misappropriation or conversion of movable property to one's own use, including temporary misappropriation and dishonest retention of found property, with imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both.

title: Dishonest Misappropriation of Property Possessed by Deceased Person at the Time of His Death
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
bare_act_text: Whoever dishonestly misappropriates or converts to his own use property, knowing that such property was in the possession of a deceased person at the time of that person's decease, and has not since been in the possession of any person legally entitled to such possession, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, and shall also be liable to fine, and if the offender at the time of such person's decease was employed by him as a clerk or servant, the imprisonment may extend to seven years.
meaning: Section 404 IPC punishes the dishonest misappropriation of property that was in the possession of a deceased person at the time of death and has not since passed to a person legally entitled to it, with an enhanced punishment if the offender was the deceased's clerk or servant.
simple_explanation: If someone dishonestly takes for themselves the property of a person who has just died—property that hasn't yet reached the rightful heir or legal owner—they can be punished with imprisonment up to 3 years and a fine. If the person doing this was the deceased's clerk or servant, the punishment can go up to 7 years.
objective: To protect the property of deceased persons from misappropriation during the vulnerable period before it passes to legally entitled persons, with enhanced punishment for breach of trust by employees.
legal_significance: Creates a specific offence addressing misappropriation of a deceased person's property in the gap period before succession, with an employment-based aggravation similar to the rationale in Section 381.
essential_elements: The property was in the possession of a deceased person at the time of their death.
Such property has not since been in the possession of any person legally entitled to it.
The accused dishonestly misappropriates or converts such property to his own use, knowing the above facts.
For enhanced punishment: the accused was employed by the deceased as a clerk or servant at the time of the deceased's death.

mens_rea.description: Knowledge that the property was in the possession of a deceased person at death and has not passed to a legally entitled person, combined with dishonest intention to misappropriate or convert it.

aggravated_case.imprisonment: Up to 7 years (if the offender was the deceased's clerk or servant at the time of death)

examples.scenario: Z dies in possession of furniture and money. Z's servant A, before the money comes into the possession of any person entitled to it, dishonestly misappropriates it.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 404 with imprisonment up to 7 years (due to being Z's servant) and fine.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years (if offender was clerk/servant of the deceased), otherwise up to 3 years, and fine
ai_summary: Section 404 IPC punishes dishonest misappropriation of a deceased person's property before it passes to a legally entitled person, with imprisonment up to 3 years and fine, extendable to 7 years if the offender was the deceased's clerk or servant.

title: Criminal Breach of Trust
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
bare_act_text: Whoever, being in any manner entrusted with property, or with any dominion over property, dishonestly misappropriates or converts to his own use that property, or dishonestly uses or disposes of that property in violation of any direction of law prescribing the mode in which such trust is to be discharged, or of any legal contract, express or implied, which he has made touching the discharge of such trust, or wilfully suffers any other person so to do, commits "criminal breach of trust". Explanation 1.—A person, being an employer of an establishment whether exempted under section 17 of the Employees' Provident Funds and Miscellaneous Provisions Act, 1952 or not, who deducts the employee's contribution from the wages payable to the employee for credit to a Provident Fund or Family Pension Fund established by any law for the time being in force, shall be deemed to have been entrusted with the amount of the contribution so deducted by him and if he makes default in the payment of such contribution to the said Fund in violation of the said law, shall be deemed to have dishonestly used the amount of the said contribution in violation of a direction of law as aforesaid. Explanation 2.—A person, being an employer, who deducts the employees' contribution from the wages payable to the employee for credit to the Employees' State Insurance Fund held and administered by the Employees' State Insurance Corporation established under the Employees' State Insurance Act, 1948, shall be deemed to have been entrusted with the amount of the contribution so deducted by him and if he makes default in the payment of such contribution to the said Fund in violation of the said Act, shall be deemed to have dishonestly used the amount of the said contribution in violation of a direction of law as aforesaid.
meaning: Section 405 IPC defines criminal breach of trust as the dishonest misappropriation, conversion, use, or disposal of entrusted property in violation of a legal direction or contract, including by wilful default, and extends this to employers who default on depositing deducted employee contributions to Provident Fund or ESI Fund.
simple_explanation: Criminal breach of trust occurs when a person who has been entrusted with property (or given control over it) dishonestly keeps it for themselves, uses it improperly, or disposes of it against the terms of the trust, law, or contract under which it was entrusted—or knowingly allows someone else to do so. Employers who deduct PF or ESI contributions from employees' wages but fail to deposit them as required by law are deemed to have committed this offence with respect to those amounts.
objective: To define the offence of criminal breach of trust, covering dishonest dealings with property entrusted under a fiduciary relationship, contract, or legal obligation, including statutory employer obligations regarding employee welfare fund contributions.
legal_significance: Forms the foundational definition for criminal breach of trust, which is punished under Section 406 and its aggravated variants (Sections 407-409), and extends the concept of 'entrustment' through deeming provisions to cover statutory deductions like PF and ESI contributions.
essential_elements: The accused is entrusted with property or with dominion over property in any manner.

The accused dishonestly misappropriates or converts that property to his own use, OR dishonestly uses or disposes of it in violation of a legal direction or contract governing the trust, OR wilfully allows another person to do so.
For the deeming provisions: the accused is an employer who deducted employee contributions for PF or ESI and defaulted on depositing them as required by law.

mens_rea.description: Dishonest intention in misappropriating, converting, using, or disposing of entrusted property in violation of the terms of the trust, law, or contract; or wilfully permitting another to do so.

examples.scenario: A, being executor to the will of a deceased person, dishonestly disobeys the law directing him to divide the effects according to the will, and appropriates them to his own use.
examples.result: A has committed criminal breach of trust under Section 405.

examples.scenario: A is a warehouse-keeper; Z entrusts furniture to A under a contract for its return upon payment of warehouse charges, but A dishonestly sells the goods.
examples.result: A has committed criminal breach of trust under Section 405.

examples.scenario: A, an agent for Z, is directed to invest remitted funds in Company's paper but instead dishonestly uses the money in his own business.
examples.result: A has committed criminal breach of trust under Section 405.

max_punishment: Not applicable (definition section; punishment prescribed under Section 406)
ai_summary: Section 405 IPC defines criminal breach of trust as the dishonest misappropriation, conversion, improper use, or disposal of entrusted property in violation of law or contract, including deemed entrustment for employers defaulting on PF or ESI contribution deposits.

title: Punishment for Criminal Breach of Trust
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
bare_act_text: Whoever commits criminal breach of trust shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both.
meaning: Section 406 IPC prescribes the punishment for the offence of criminal breach of trust as defined under Section 405 IPC.
simple_explanation: Any person who commits criminal breach of trust (as defined in Section 405) can be punished with imprisonment up to 3 years, or a fine, or both.
objective: To prescribe punishment for the basic offence of criminal breach of trust.
legal_significance: This is the primary punishment section for criminal breach of trust. Aggravated forms of the same offence attract higher punishment under Sections 407, 408, and 409.
essential_elements: Commission of criminal breach of trust as defined under Section 405 IPC.

mens_rea.description: Dishonest intention as required under Section 405 IPC.

punishment.normal_case: Imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to 3 years, or fine, or both.

examples.scenario: A is entrusted with money by B to purchase goods, but A spends the money on himself.
examples.result: A is guilty of criminal breach of trust and liable to punishment under Section 406.

max_punishment: 3 years imprisonment, or fine, or both
ai_summary: Section 406 IPC punishes the offence of criminal breach of trust with up to 3 years imprisonment, or fine, or both, serving as the base punishment provision for the offence defined under Section 405.

title: Criminal Breach of Trust by Carrier, etc.
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
bare_act_text: Whoever, being entrusted with property as a carrier, wharfinger or warehouse-keeper, commits criminal breach of trust in respect of such property, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.
meaning: Section 407 IPC provides enhanced punishment for criminal breach of trust committed by a carrier, wharfinger, or warehouse-keeper with respect to property entrusted to them in that capacity.
simple_explanation: If a person who is a carrier (transporter), wharfinger (one who manages a wharf), or warehouse-keeper commits criminal breach of trust regarding property entrusted to them in that role, they face a higher punishment of up to 7 years imprisonment along with a fine.
objective: To impose stricter liability on persons in specific custodial or logistical roles — carriers, wharfingers, and warehouse-keepers — who are entrusted with others' property in the course of trade or commerce.
legal_significance: An aggravated form of Section 406, recognising the higher degree of trust placed in commercial custodians of property. The enhanced punishment reflects the greater public and commercial reliance on such persons.
essential_elements: The accused is entrusted with property in the capacity of a carrier, wharfinger, or warehouse-keeper.
The accused commits criminal breach of trust in respect of that property as defined under Section 405 IPC.

mens_rea.description: Dishonest intention in misappropriating or improperly using or disposing of the entrusted property.

punishment.normal_case: Imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to 7 years, and also liable to fine.

examples.scenario: A, a warehouse-keeper, is entrusted with goods by Z for storage. A dishonestly sells those goods without Z's consent.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 407 for criminal breach of trust by a warehouse-keeper.

examples.scenario: A, a carrier, is entrusted with goods to transport to a destination but diverts and sells them on the way.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 407 for criminal breach of trust by a carrier.

max_punishment: 7 years imprisonment and fine
ai_summary: Section 407 IPC imposes enhanced punishment of up to 7 years imprisonment and fine for criminal breach of trust committed by carriers, wharfingers, or warehouse-keepers with respect to property entrusted to them in those capacities.

title: Criminal Breach of Trust by Clerk or Servant
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
bare_act_text: Whoever, being a clerk or servant or employed as a clerk or servant, and being in any manner entrusted in such capacity with property, or with any dominion over property, commits criminal breach of trust in respect of that property, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.
meaning: Section 408 IPC prescribes enhanced punishment for criminal breach of trust committed by a clerk or servant who is entrusted with property or dominion over property in that capacity.
simple_explanation: If a clerk or servant — who is given property or control over property as part of their job — dishonestly misappropriates or misuses it, they can be punished with up to 7 years imprisonment and a fine. The punishment is higher than the basic breach of trust offence because of the special trust placed in employees.
objective: To deter employees (clerks and servants) from abusing the trust placed in them by their employers by prescribing a higher punishment than the basic offence under Section 406.
legal_significance: An aggravated form of criminal breach of trust recognising the fiduciary relationship between employer and employee. The entrustment arises specifically from the employment relationship.
essential_elements: The accused is a clerk or servant, or employed as a clerk or servant.
The accused is entrusted with property or dominion over property in that capacity.
The accused commits criminal breach of trust in respect of that property as defined under Section 405 IPC.

mens_rea.description: Dishonest intention in misappropriating or improperly using property entrusted in the capacity of a clerk or servant.

punishment.normal_case: Imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to 7 years, and also liable to fine.

examples.scenario: A, a cashier (clerk) in a shop, is entrusted with the day's cash collections. A secretly takes a portion of the money for personal use.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 408 for criminal breach of trust by a clerk or servant.

examples.scenario: A, a domestic servant entrusted with household goods, sells some of them without the owner's knowledge.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 408 for criminal breach of trust by a servant.

max_punishment: 7 years imprisonment and fine
ai_summary: Section 408 IPC punishes criminal breach of trust by a clerk or servant — who is entrusted with property in that capacity — with up to 7 years imprisonment and fine, reflecting the higher degree of trust inherent in employment relationships.

title: Criminal Breach of Trust by Public Servant, or by Banker, Merchant or Agent
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
bare_act_text: Whoever, being in any manner entrusted with property, or with any dominion over property in his capacity of a public servant or in the way of his business as a banker, merchant, factor, broker, attorney or agent, commits criminal breach of trust in respect of that property, shall be punished with imprisonment for life, or with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.
meaning: Section 409 IPC prescribes the most severe punishment for criminal breach of trust when committed by a public servant or by certain professionals — bankers, merchants, factors, brokers, attorneys, or agents — who are entrusted with property in those capacities.
simple_explanation: When a public servant or high-trust commercial professionals like bankers, merchants, brokers, or agents commit criminal breach of trust with respect to property entrusted to them in that role, they face the harshest punishment: life imprisonment, or up to 10 years imprisonment, along with a fine. The law treats such breach most seriously because of the immense public and commercial trust placed in these persons.
objective: To impose the most stringent punishment for breach of trust by persons holding positions of the highest public or commercial trust — public servants and key financial/commercial intermediaries.
legal_significance: The most aggravated form of criminal breach of trust under IPC. Frequently invoked in cases involving government officials misusing public funds, bank frauds, and misappropriation by agents or attorneys. The provision for life imprisonment underscores the gravity attached to such offences.
essential_elements: The accused is entrusted with property or has dominion over property.
Such entrustment is in the capacity of a public servant OR in the way of business as a banker, merchant, factor, broker, attorney, or agent.
The accused commits criminal breach of trust in respect of that property as defined under Section 405 IPC.

mens_rea.description: Dishonest intention in misappropriating or improperly using property entrusted in the above-mentioned capacities.

punishment.normal_case: Imprisonment for life, or imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to 10 years, and also liable to fine.

examples.scenario: A, a government officer entrusted with public funds for distribution as relief, misappropriates a portion for personal use.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 409 for criminal breach of trust by a public servant.

examples.scenario: A, a broker entrusted by clients with funds to invest in securities, diverts those funds to his own account.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 409 for criminal breach of trust by an agent/broker.

examples.scenario: A, a bank manager, dishonestly uses depositors' funds for his personal business.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 409 for criminal breach of trust by a banker.

max_punishment: Imprisonment for life, or 10 years imprisonment and fine
ai_summary: Section 409 IPC imposes the severest punishment — life imprisonment or up to 10 years and fine — for criminal breach of trust committed by public servants or professionals such as bankers, merchants, brokers, attorneys, or agents, in respect of property entrusted to them in those capacities.

title: Stolen Property
