chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
bare_act_text: Property, the possession whereof has been transferred by theft, or by extortion, or by robbery, and property which has been criminally misappropriated or in respect of which criminal breach of trust has been committed, is designated as 'stolen property', whether the transfer has been made, or the misappropriation or breach of trust has been committed, within or without India. But, if such property subsequently comes into the possession of a person legally entitled to the possession thereof, it then ceases to be stolen property.
meaning: Section 410 IPC defines 'stolen property' as any property whose possession was transferred through theft, extortion, robbery, criminal misappropriation, or criminal breach of trust — whether committed in India or abroad — and ceases to be stolen property once it comes into the lawful possession of a person entitled to it.
simple_explanation: Property is called 'stolen property' if it was taken by theft, extortion, robbery, criminal misappropriation, or criminal breach of trust, regardless of where in the world this happened. However, once the property reaches the hands of the rightful owner or a person legally entitled to possess it, it is no longer considered stolen property.
objective: To define 'stolen property' for the purposes of offences relating to receiving, retaining, or dealing in stolen property under Sections 411–414 IPC.
legal_significance: Provides the foundational definition for a chain of offences involving stolen property. The broad definition covers property obtained through multiple modes of criminal acquisition, and the extra-territorial scope ensures that property stolen abroad is also covered.
essential_elements: The property's possession must have been transferred by theft, extortion, or robbery; OR the property must have been criminally misappropriated or subject to criminal breach of trust.
The transfer, misappropriation, or breach of trust may have occurred within or outside India.
The property ceases to be 'stolen property' once it comes into the possession of a person legally entitled to it.

mens_rea.description: Not applicable — this is a definition section, not a standalone offence.

examples.scenario: A's watch is stolen by B. The watch is 'stolen property'. B sells it to C. C later returns it to A.
examples.result: The watch was stolen property until it returned to A, who is legally entitled to it. It then ceases to be stolen property.

examples.scenario: A property is obtained by extortion in another country and brought to India.
examples.result: It is still 'stolen property' under Section 410 as the definition extends to acts committed outside India.

max_punishment: Not applicable (definition section)
ai_summary: Section 410 IPC defines 'stolen property' as property obtained through theft, extortion, robbery, criminal misappropriation, or criminal breach of trust — whether in India or abroad — and provides that property ceases to be stolen once it comes into the lawful possession of an entitled person.

title: Dishonestly Receiving Stolen Property
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
bare_act_text: Whoever dishonestly receives or retains any stolen property, knowing or having reason to believe the same to be stolen property, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both.
meaning: Section 411 IPC punishes any person who dishonestly receives or retains stolen property while knowing, or having reason to believe, that it is stolen property.
simple_explanation: If a person receives or keeps stolen goods knowing (or having good reason to believe) that they are stolen, they are guilty under this section and can be punished with up to 3 years imprisonment, a fine, or both. The offence can be committed either by receiving the stolen property or by continuing to retain it after learning it is stolen.
objective: To punish those who knowingly receive or retain stolen property, thereby discouraging the creation of a market for stolen goods and aiding thieves.
legal_significance: This section punishes not just the thief but also those who benefit from theft by receiving stolen goods. The element of knowledge or reason to believe is crucial — mere possession without knowledge is not sufficient for conviction.
essential_elements: The property in question is stolen property as defined under Section 410 IPC.
The accused dishonestly receives or retains such property.
The accused knows or has reason to believe that the property is stolen.

mens_rea.description: Knowledge or reason to believe that the property is stolen, combined with dishonest receipt or retention.

punishment.normal_case: Imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to 3 years, or fine, or both.

examples.scenario: A buys a motorcycle from B at a suspiciously low price knowing that B is a known thief. The motorcycle turns out to be stolen.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 411 for dishonestly receiving stolen property.

examples.scenario: A receives goods from B, later discovers they are stolen, but continues to retain them instead of reporting to the police.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 411 for dishonestly retaining stolen property.

max_punishment: 3 years imprisonment, or fine, or both
ai_summary: Section 411 IPC punishes dishonest receipt or retention of stolen property by a person who knows or has reason to believe it to be stolen, with up to 3 years imprisonment or fine or both.

title: Dishonestly Receiving Property Stolen in the Commission of a Dacoity
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
bare_act_text: Whoever dishonestly receives or retains any stolen property, the possession whereof he knows or has reason to believe to have been transferred by the commission of dacoity, or dishonestly receives from a person, whom he knows or has reason to believe to belong or to have belonged to a gang of dacoits, property which he knows or has reason to believe to have been stolen, shall be punished with imprisonment for life, or with rigorous imprisonment for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.
meaning: Section 412 IPC prescribes severe punishment for receiving or retaining stolen property that was obtained in the commission of dacoity, or receiving any stolen property from a known or suspected dacoit.
simple_explanation: If a person receives or keeps property that they know or have reason to believe was stolen during a dacoity — or receives stolen property from someone they know or believe to be a dacoit — they face very harsh punishment: life imprisonment, or up to 10 years rigorous imprisonment, along with a fine. The law treats this very seriously because it directly fuels organised criminal gangs.
objective: To impose the most stringent deterrence against persons who aid or abet dacoits by receiving the proceeds of dacoity, thereby discouraging organised gang crimes.
legal_significance: An aggravated form of Section 411, specifically targeting those who deal in proceeds of dacoity. The provision for life imprisonment reflects the gravity of enabling and sustaining organised criminal activity.
essential_elements: The accused dishonestly receives or retains stolen property.

The accused knows or has reason to believe that the property was obtained by dacoity; OR the accused receives stolen property from a person known or believed to be a member or former member of a gang of dacoits.

mens_rea.description: Knowledge or reason to believe that the property was obtained by dacoity, or that the person from whom it is received is a dacoit.

punishment.normal_case: Imprisonment for life, or rigorous imprisonment for a term which may extend to 10 years, and also liable to fine.

examples.scenario: A receives gold ornaments from B, whom A knows to be a member of a gang that recently committed dacoity in a village.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 412 for dishonestly receiving property stolen in the commission of a dacoity.

max_punishment: Imprisonment for life, or 10 years rigorous imprisonment and fine
ai_summary: Section 412 IPC punishes dishonest receipt or retention of property stolen in a dacoity, or received from a known dacoit, with life imprisonment or up to 10 years rigorous imprisonment and fine.

title: Habitually Dealing in Stolen Property
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
bare_act_text: Whoever habitually receives or deals in property which he knows or has reason to believe to be stolen property, shall be punished with imprisonment for life, or with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.
meaning: Section 413 IPC punishes persons who make it a habit to receive or deal in stolen property — knowing or having reason to believe it to be stolen — with life imprisonment or up to 10 years imprisonment and fine.
simple_explanation: If someone repeatedly and habitually buys, sells, or deals in stolen goods knowing them to be stolen, they face very serious punishment — life imprisonment or up to 10 years jail, plus a fine. This section targets professional 'fences' or dealers in stolen property rather than a one-time receiver.
objective: To target and punish habitual receivers and dealers in stolen property who form a crucial link in the chain sustaining theft and organised property crime.
legal_significance: While Section 411 covers single instances of receiving stolen property, Section 413 targets repeat offenders and professional dealers in stolen goods. The provision for life imprisonment reflects the systemic harm caused by habitual dealing in stolen property.
essential_elements: The accused habitually receives or deals in property.
The accused knows or has reason to believe the property to be stolen.
The conduct is habitual — i.e., repeated and not merely a one-time act.

mens_rea.description: Knowledge or reason to believe that the property is stolen, combined with a habitual pattern of receiving or dealing in such property.

punishment.normal_case: Imprisonment for life, or imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to 10 years, and also liable to fine.

examples.scenario: A runs an informal shop where he routinely purchases stolen electronics and resells them, having done this repeatedly over the years.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 413 for habitually dealing in stolen property.

max_punishment: Imprisonment for life, or 10 years imprisonment and fine
ai_summary: Section 413 IPC punishes habitual receivers or dealers in stolen property — who know or have reason to believe it is stolen — with life imprisonment or up to 10 years imprisonment and fine, targeting professional fences and repeat offenders.

title: Assisting in Concealment of Stolen Property
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
bare_act_text: Whoever voluntarily assists in concealing or disposing of or making away with property which he knows or has reason to believe to be stolen property, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both.
meaning: Section 414 IPC punishes any person who voluntarily helps in hiding, disposing of, or moving stolen property with the knowledge or reason to believe that it is stolen.
simple_explanation: If a person knowingly helps to hide, get rid of, or move stolen goods — even if they did not steal them or receive them for themselves — they are guilty under this section and can be punished with up to 3 years imprisonment, a fine, or both. The key is that the assistance must be voluntary and with knowledge that the property is stolen.
objective: To punish those who, without directly receiving stolen property themselves, aid in its concealment or disposal, thereby assisting criminals in evading detection.
legal_significance: Extends criminal liability beyond the receiver of stolen property to any person who facilitates the concealment or disposal of stolen goods. This section plugs a gap to ensure that accessories who help in covering up theft are also punished.
essential_elements: The property in question is stolen property as defined under Section 410 IPC.
The accused voluntarily assists in concealing, disposing of, or making away with such property.
The accused knows or has reason to believe that the property is stolen.

mens_rea.description: Knowledge or reason to believe that the property is stolen, combined with voluntary assistance in concealing, disposing of, or removing it.

punishment.normal_case: Imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to 3 years, or fine, or both.

examples.scenario: A helps B hide stolen jewellery in his house knowing that the jewellery was stolen in a burglary.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 414 for voluntarily assisting in the concealment of stolen property.

examples.scenario: A helps B transport and sell stolen goods at a distant market knowing them to be stolen.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 414 for assisting in disposing of stolen property.

max_punishment: 3 years imprisonment, or fine, or both
ai_summary: Section 414 IPC punishes voluntary assistance in concealing, disposing of, or moving stolen property — with knowledge that it is stolen — with up to 3 years imprisonment or fine or both.

title: Cheating
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
bare_act_text: Whoever, by deceiving any person, fraudulently or dishonestly induces the person so deceived to deliver any property to any person, or to consent that any person shall retain any property, or intentionally induces the person so deceived to do or omit to do anything which he would not do or omit if he were not so deceived, and which act or omission causes or is likely to cause damage or harm to that person in body, mind, reputation or property, is said to 'cheat'. Explanation.—A dishonest concealment of facts is a deception within the meaning of this section.
meaning: Section 415 IPC defines 'cheating' as deceiving a person so as to fraudulently or dishonestly induce them to deliver property or consent to its retention, or to do or omit anything they would not otherwise do, resulting in harm or likely harm to them in body, mind, reputation, or property.
simple_explanation: Cheating happens when someone deceives another person and that deception causes the victim to (i) hand over property, or (ii) allow someone to keep property, or (iii) do or not do something that causes or is likely to cause them harm in body, mind, reputation, or property. Even deliberately hiding facts counts as deception. The victim must have been influenced by the deception.
objective: To define the offence of cheating in a comprehensive manner, covering all forms of fraudulent deception that lead to wrongful gain or harm to the victim.
legal_significance: Foundational definition for cheating offences. Punishment is prescribed under Section 417, and aggravated forms are covered under Sections 418–420. Courts have consistently held that both deception AND resulting harm (or likelihood thereof) are essential — mere deception without harm is insufficient.
essential_elements: The accused deceives a person — including by dishonest concealment of facts.

As a result of the deception, the person is fraudulently or dishonestly induced to deliver property, OR to consent to retention of property; OR is intentionally induced to do or omit something they would not do or omit but for the deception.
The act or omission causes or is likely to cause damage or harm to the deceived person in body, mind, reputation, or property.

mens_rea.description: Fraudulent or dishonest intention to deceive and thereby induce delivery of property or cause harm; mere broken promises without fraudulent intent at the time do not constitute cheating.

examples.scenario: A falsely pretends to be in the Civil Service and dishonestly induces Z to let him have goods on credit which he does not intend to pay for.
examples.result: A cheats under Section 415.

examples.scenario: A puts a counterfeit mark on an article and deceives Z into believing it was made by a famous manufacturer, inducing Z to buy and pay for it.
examples.result: A cheats under Section 415.

examples.scenario: A pledges articles as diamonds knowing them not to be diamonds, and thereby induces Z to lend money.
examples.result: A cheats under Section 415.

examples.scenario: A intentionally deceives Z into believing A will repay a loan, with no intention to repay, inducing Z to lend money.
examples.result: A cheats under Section 415.

examples.scenario: A sells an estate to B, and then, without disclosing the prior sale, sells or mortgages the same property to Z and takes money from Z.
examples.result: A cheats Z under Section 415.

max_punishment: Not applicable (definition section; punishment under Section 417)
ai_summary: Section 415 IPC defines cheating as deception-induced delivery of property or harmful acts/omissions, with dishonest concealment of facts also constituting deception, forming the basis for punishment under Sections 417–420.

title: Cheating by Personation
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
bare_act_text: A person is said to 'cheat by personation' if he cheats by pretending to be some other person, or by knowingly substituting one person for another, or representing that he or any other person is a person other than he or such other person really is. Explanation.—The offence is committed whether the individual personated is a real or imaginary person.
meaning: Section 416 IPC defines cheating by personation as a form of cheating where the accused pretends to be someone else, knowingly substitutes one person for another, or misrepresents the identity of himself or another person — whether the personated individual is real or fictitious.
simple_explanation: Cheating by personation means deceiving someone by pretending to be a different person — whether that person is real or made up. This could be impersonating a real person or even a fictional identity. For example, posing as a bank official, a government officer, or even a deceased person to gain someone's trust and cheat them.
objective: To specifically define and address the form of cheating that involves identity fraud or impersonation, which is a distinct and serious mode of deception.
legal_significance: Cheating by personation is punished under Section 419 with a higher punishment than ordinary cheating under Section 417, reflecting the additional element of identity fraud. The Explanation makes clear that personating even an imaginary person constitutes the offence.
essential_elements: The accused cheats as defined under Section 415 IPC.
The cheating is effected by pretending to be another person, OR by substituting one person for another, OR by misrepresenting the identity of any person.
The personated person may be real or imaginary.

mens_rea.description: Intentional and knowing misrepresentation of identity with fraudulent or dishonest intent to cheat.

examples.scenario: A cheats by pretending to be a certain rich banker of the same name.
examples.result: A cheats by personation under Section 416.

examples.scenario: A cheats by pretending to be B, a person who is deceased.
examples.result: A cheats by personation under Section 416, as the offence applies even when the personated individual is deceased or imaginary.

max_punishment: Not applicable (definition section; punishment under Section 419)
ai_summary: Section 416 IPC defines cheating by personation as cheating through impersonation of another real or imaginary person, forming the basis for the enhanced punishment prescribed under Section 419 IPC.

title: Punishment for Cheating
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
bare_act_text: Whoever cheats shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to one year, or with fine, or with both.
meaning: Section 417 IPC prescribes punishment for the basic offence of cheating as defined under Section 415 IPC.
simple_explanation: Any person who cheats another (as defined under Section 415) can be punished with up to 1 year imprisonment, or a fine, or both. This is the base punishment for ordinary cheating; higher punishments apply for aggravated forms of cheating under Sections 418, 419, and 420.
objective: To prescribe punishment for the general offence of cheating defined under Section 415 IPC.
legal_significance: Base punishment provision for cheating. Aggravated forms — cheating with knowledge of wrongful loss to a protected person (Section 418), cheating by personation (Section 419), and cheating inducing delivery of property (Section 420) — attract higher punishments.
essential_elements: Commission of cheating as defined under Section 415 IPC.

mens_rea.description: Fraudulent or dishonest intention to deceive as required under Section 415 IPC.

punishment.normal_case: Imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to 1 year, or fine, or both.

examples.scenario: A deceives Z into believing A will provide services and collects payment, but never intends to provide them, causing Z harm.
examples.result: A is liable for cheating under Section 415 and punishment under Section 417.

max_punishment: 1 year imprisonment, or fine, or both
ai_summary: Section 417 IPC prescribes punishment of up to 1 year imprisonment or fine or both for the basic offence of cheating as defined under Section 415 IPC.

title: Cheating with Knowledge that Wrongful Loss May Ensue to Person Whose Interest Offender is Bound to Protect
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
bare_act_text: Whoever cheats with the knowledge that he is likely thereby to cause wrongful loss to a person whose interest in the transaction to which the cheating relates, he was bound, either by law, or by a legal contract, to protect, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both.
meaning: Section 418 IPC prescribes enhanced punishment for cheating where the offender knowingly causes wrongful loss to a person whose interests he was legally or contractually obligated to protect.
simple_explanation: If a person cheats someone whose interests they were duty-bound to protect — either by law or by a legal contract — and they knew the cheating was likely to cause wrongful loss to that person, they face a higher punishment of up to 3 years imprisonment, or a fine, or both. This section targets persons in positions of trust and fiduciary responsibility.
objective: To impose stricter punishment on persons who abuse their legal or contractual duty to protect another's interests by cheating that very person.
legal_significance: An aggravated form of cheating under Section 417, targeting breach of fiduciary or legal duty. It is commonly applied to trustees, agents, guardians, attorneys, and others who owe a legal duty of protection to the victim.
essential_elements: The accused cheats as defined under Section 415 IPC.
The accused had knowledge that the cheating was likely to cause wrongful loss to the victim.
The accused was bound by law or a legal contract to protect the interests of the victim in the relevant transaction.

mens_rea.description: Knowledge that the cheating is likely to cause wrongful loss to a person whose interests the accused was legally or contractually obligated to protect.

punishment.normal_case: Imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to 3 years, or fine, or both.

examples.scenario: A, an agent legally bound to invest Z's funds in a safe scheme, knowingly invests them in a fraudulent scheme causing Z wrongful loss.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 418 for cheating a person whose interests he was contractually bound to protect.

examples.scenario: A, a guardian of a minor, fraudulently disposes of the minor's property for personal gain.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 418 as he was legally bound to protect the minor's interests.

max_punishment: 3 years imprisonment, or fine, or both
ai_summary: Section 418 IPC imposes enhanced punishment of up to 3 years imprisonment or fine or both for cheating a person whose interests the offender was legally or contractually bound to protect, with knowledge that wrongful loss was likely.

title: Punishment for Cheating by Personation
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
bare_act_text: Whoever cheats by personation shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both.
meaning: Section 419 IPC prescribes punishment for the offence of cheating by personation as defined under Section 416 IPC.
simple_explanation: If a person cheats by impersonating someone else — whether a real or imaginary person — they can be punished with up to 3 years imprisonment, or a fine, or both. The punishment is higher than ordinary cheating (Section 417) because of the additional element of identity fraud.
objective: To prescribe an enhanced punishment for cheating by personation, reflecting the additional dimension of identity fraud that makes this form of cheating more serious.
legal_significance: Punishment provision for the specific offence defined under Section 416. Frequently invoked in cases of identity theft, impersonation of government officials, online fraud, and impersonation for financial gain.
essential_elements: The accused cheats as defined under Section 415 IPC.
The cheating is committed by personation as defined under Section 416 IPC.

mens_rea.description: Intentional misrepresentation of identity with fraudulent or dishonest intent to cheat.

punishment.normal_case: Imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to 3 years, or fine, or both.

examples.scenario: A pretends to be a government officer and collects money from villagers under false pretences.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 419 for cheating by personation.

examples.scenario: A creates a fake online profile of a real person and uses it to deceive others into transferring money.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 419 for cheating by personation.

max_punishment: 3 years imprisonment, or fine, or both
ai_summary: Section 419 IPC punishes cheating by personation — as defined under Section 416 — with up to 3 years imprisonment or fine or both, recognising the additional gravity of identity fraud in cheating offences.

title: Cheating and Dishonestly Inducing Delivery of Property
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
bare_act_text: Whoever cheats and thereby dishonestly induces the person deceived to deliver any property to any person, or to make, alter or destroy the whole or any part of a valuable security, or anything which is signed or sealed, and which is capable of being converted into a valuable security, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.
meaning: Section 420 IPC prescribes the highest punishment for cheating when it results in the victim being dishonestly induced to deliver property or to make, alter, or destroy a valuable security.
simple_explanation: When someone cheats another person and that cheating actually causes the victim to hand over property, or to sign, alter, or destroy a valuable document (like a cheque, deed, or contract), the punishment is much higher — up to 7 years imprisonment along with a fine. This is the most commonly invoked cheating provision in India.
objective: To impose severe punishment for the most harmful form of cheating — where the deception results in actual transfer or destruction of property or valuable securities.
legal_significance: The most widely used and serious cheating provision in IPC. The section covers cheating that results in actual delivery of property or manipulation of valuable securities. Commonly invoked in fraud, financial crime, property scams, and cheque/document forgery cases.
essential_elements: The accused cheats as defined under Section 415 IPC.

As a result of the cheating, the deceived person is dishonestly induced to: (a) deliver any property to any person, OR (b) make, alter, or destroy the whole or any part of a valuable security, or anything signed or sealed capable of being converted into a valuable security.

mens_rea.description: Dishonest or fraudulent intention to deceive and thereby induce the delivery of property or manipulation of a valuable security.

punishment.normal_case: Imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to 7 years, and also liable to fine.

examples.scenario: A, by false representation that he is a bank officer, deceives Z into handing over cash and jewellery.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 420 for cheating and inducing delivery of property.

examples.scenario: A deceives Z into signing a blank cheque by falsely representing it is a receipt, and later fills in a large amount.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 420 as a valuable security was made/altered as a result of cheating.

max_punishment: 7 years imprisonment and fine
ai_summary: Section 420 IPC punishes the most serious form of cheating — where the victim is dishonestly induced to deliver property or make/alter/destroy a valuable security — with up to 7 years imprisonment and fine.

title: Dishonest or Fraudulent Removal or Concealment of Property to Prevent Distribution Among Creditors
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
section_type: Offence Against Property / Fraudulent Deeds and Dispositions
bare_act_text: Whoever dishonestly or fraudulently removes, conceals or delivers to any person, or transfers or causes to be transferred to any person, without adequate consideration, any property, intending thereby to prevent, or knowing it to be likely that he will thereby prevent, the distribution of that property according to law among his creditors or the creditors of any other person, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.
meaning: Section 421 IPC punishes a debtor who dishonestly or fraudulently removes, conceals, or transfers property — without adequate consideration — with the intention or knowledge of thereby preventing its lawful distribution among creditors.
simple_explanation: If a person who owes money to creditors dishonestly hides, transfers, or gives away their property (without proper payment in return) in order to stop creditors from getting what is legally owed to them, they can be punished with up to 2 years imprisonment, or a fine, or both.
objective: To protect creditors from fraudulent dissipation of a debtor's assets and to uphold the integrity of lawful debt recovery and insolvency proceedings.
legal_significance: Forms part of the group of sections (421–424) dealing with fraudulent deeds and dispositions of property. Particularly relevant in insolvency and bankruptcy contexts where debtors attempt to defeat creditors' claims.
essential_elements: The accused dishonestly or fraudulently removes, conceals, delivers, transfers, or causes to be transferred any property.
Such transfer is without adequate consideration.
The accused intends thereby to prevent, or knows it is likely to prevent, the lawful distribution of the property among creditors (of themselves or another person).

mens_rea.description: Dishonest or fraudulent intention to prevent, or knowledge of the likelihood of preventing, the lawful distribution of property among creditors.

punishment.normal_case: Imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to 2 years, or fine, or both.

examples.scenario: A, facing recovery proceedings by his creditors, secretly transfers his property to his relative without any payment to defraud his creditors.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 421 for fraudulent removal of property to prevent distribution among creditors.

max_punishment: 2 years imprisonment, or fine, or both
ai_summary: Section 421 IPC punishes dishonest or fraudulent removal, concealment, or transfer of property without adequate consideration to defeat the lawful claims of creditors, with up to 2 years imprisonment or fine or both.

title: Dishonestly or Fraudulently Preventing Debt Being Available for Creditors
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
section_type: Offence Against Property / Fraudulent Deeds and Dispositions
bare_act_text: Whoever dishonestly or fraudulently prevents any debt or demand due to himself or to any other person from being made available according to law for payment of his debts or the debts of such other person, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.
meaning: Section 422 IPC punishes a person who dishonestly or fraudulently prevents any debt or demand owed to them (or another person) from being applied lawfully toward payment of debts.
simple_explanation: If a person who owes money to creditors dishonestly prevents a debt that is owed to them from being used to pay their own creditors — for example, by secretly waiving it or hiding it — they can be punished with up to 2 years imprisonment, or a fine, or both.
objective: To prevent debtors from dishonestly shielding assets in the form of receivable debts or demands from being applied toward satisfaction of their creditors' claims.
legal_significance: Complements Section 421 by covering scenarios where the fraud involves manipulation of receivable debts rather than physical property. Relevant in insolvency, debt recovery, and commercial fraud contexts.
essential_elements: There is a debt or demand due to the accused or to any other person.
The accused dishonestly or fraudulently prevents that debt or demand from being made available according to law.
The prevention is for the purpose of defeating payment of the accused's debts or the debts of another person.

mens_rea.description: Dishonest or fraudulent intention to prevent a debt or demand from being legally applied toward payment of debts owed to creditors.

punishment.normal_case: Imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to 2 years, or fine, or both.

examples.scenario: A, who owes money to B, secretly waives or releases a debt that C owes to A, in order to prevent that amount from being available to pay B.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 422 for fraudulently preventing a debt from being available to creditors.

max_punishment: 2 years imprisonment, or fine, or both
ai_summary: Section 422 IPC punishes dishonest or fraudulent prevention of a debt or demand from being applied toward lawful payment of the debtor's creditors, with up to 2 years imprisonment or fine or both.

title: Dishonest or Fraudulent Execution of Deed of Transfer Containing False Statement of Consideration
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
section_type: Offence Against Property / Fraudulent Deeds and Dispositions
bare_act_text: Whoever dishonestly or fraudulently signs, executes or becomes a party to any deed or instrument which purports to transfer or subject to any charge any property, or any interest therein, and which contains any false statement relating to the consideration for such transfer or charge, or relating to the person or persons for whose use or benefit it is really intended to operate, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.
meaning: Section 423 IPC punishes the dishonest or fraudulent execution of a deed or instrument of transfer that contains a false statement about the consideration or the true beneficiary, thereby misrepresenting the real nature of the transaction.
simple_explanation: If a person dishonestly signs or executes a property transfer document or charge that contains a false statement about the price paid for the transfer, or about who the real beneficiary is, they can be punished with up to 2 years imprisonment, or a fine, or both. This targets fraudulent property documents designed to mislead creditors, courts, or authorities.
objective: To prevent and punish the execution of fraudulent property documents that misrepresent consideration or beneficial ownership, commonly used to defeat creditors or evade legal obligations.
legal_significance: Specifically addresses documentary fraud in property transactions. Commonly relevant in cases of benami transactions, sham transfers to defeat creditors, and fraudulent mortgages with false consideration.
essential_elements: The accused signs, executes, or becomes a party to a deed or instrument.
The deed purports to transfer property or any interest therein, or subject it to a charge.
The deed contains a false statement relating to the consideration for the transfer or charge, OR relating to the person(s) for whose use or benefit the transaction is really intended.
The accused acts dishonestly or fraudulently.

mens_rea.description: Dishonest or fraudulent intention in executing a deed containing false statements about consideration or true beneficiary.

punishment.normal_case: Imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to 2 years, or fine, or both.

examples.scenario: A executes a sale deed showing the consideration as Rs. 10 lakhs when no money was actually paid, intending to fraudulently transfer property to defeat creditors.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 423 for executing a deed containing a false statement of consideration.

examples.scenario: A, a debtor, executes a transfer deed in favour of B but the deed falsely states it is for B's benefit when it is actually meant to benefit A's family to hide assets.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 423 for fraudulent execution of a deed with false statement of beneficiary.

max_punishment: 2 years imprisonment, or fine, or both
ai_summary: Section 423 IPC punishes the dishonest or fraudulent execution of a property transfer deed that contains a false statement about consideration or true beneficiary, with up to 2 years imprisonment or fine or both.

title: Dishonest or Fraudulent Removal or Concealment of Property
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
section_type: Offence Against Property / Fraudulent Deeds and Dispositions
bare_act_text: Whoever dishonestly or fraudulently conceals or removes any property of himself or any other person, or dishonestly or fraudulently assists in the concealment or removal thereof, or dishonestly releases any demand or claim to which he is entitled, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.
meaning: Section 424 IPC is a general provision punishing dishonest or fraudulent concealment or removal of any property — including one's own — or dishonest release of a claim or demand, covering cases not specifically addressed by Sections 421–423.
simple_explanation: A person who dishonestly hides or moves away any property (their own or someone else's), or helps in doing so, or dishonestly gives up a claim or demand they are entitled to, can be punished with up to 2 years imprisonment, or a fine, or both. This is a broad 'catch-all' provision for various types of fraudulent property dealings.
objective: To serve as a general provision covering dishonest or fraudulent concealment and removal of property and the dishonest release of claims, supplementing the more specific provisions of Sections 421–423.
legal_significance: Acts as a residual provision within the group of fraudulent property offences. Unlike Section 421, it does not require the act to be directed against creditors specifically, making it applicable in a broader range of fraud and property concealment scenarios.
essential_elements: The accused dishonestly or fraudulently conceals or removes property of themselves or another person; OR assists in such concealment or removal; OR dishonestly releases any demand or claim to which they are entitled.
The act is done with dishonest or fraudulent intent.

mens_rea.description: Dishonest or fraudulent intention in concealing, removing property, or releasing a claim or demand.

punishment.normal_case: Imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to 2 years, or fine, or both.

examples.scenario: A, knowing that his assets are about to be attached by a court order, secretly removes goods from his warehouse to another location.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 424 for dishonest removal of property.

examples.scenario: A dishonestly waives a substantial debt owed to him by B in order to prevent those funds from being available to satisfy a decree against A.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 424 for dishonestly releasing a claim.

max_punishment: 2 years imprisonment, or fine, or both
ai_summary: Section 424 IPC is a general provision punishing dishonest or fraudulent concealment, removal of property, or release of a claim, with up to 2 years imprisonment or fine or both, covering fraudulent property dealings not specifically addressed by Sections 421–423.

title: Mischief
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
bare_act_text: Whoever with intent to cause, or knowing that he is likely to cause, wrongful loss or damage to the public or to any person, causes the destruction of any property, or any such change in any property or in the situation thereof as destroys or diminishes its value or utility, or affects it injuriously, commits 'mischief'. Explanation 1.—It is not essential to the offence of mischief that the offender should intend to cause loss or damage to the owner of the property injured or destroyed. It is sufficient if he intends to cause, or knows that he is likely to cause, wrongful loss or damage to any person by injuring any property, whether it belongs to that person or not. Explanation 2.—Mischief may be committed by an act affecting property belonging to the person who commits the act, or to that person and others jointly.
meaning: Section 425 IPC defines mischief as intentionally or knowingly causing destruction of, or injurious change to, any property so as to cause wrongful loss or damage to the public or any person — even if the property belongs to the offender themselves.
simple_explanation: Mischief means damaging, destroying, or altering property in a way that causes or is likely to cause wrongful loss or harm to anyone — whether or not they own the property. Importantly, a person can commit mischief even by destroying their own property if it causes wrongful loss to another. Even burning a document, melting ice, or poisoning an animal can be mischief if done with wrongful intent.
objective: To define the offence of mischief broadly, covering all forms of intentional or knowing damage to property that results in wrongful loss to any person, including the public.
legal_significance: Foundational definition for a range of mischief offences punished under Sections 426–440. The broad definition captures destruction, damage, and any change that diminishes value or utility. The explanations ensure that lack of direct ownership of the damaged property is not a defence.
essential_elements: The accused causes destruction of any property, OR any change in property or its situation that destroys or diminishes its value or utility, or affects it injuriously.
The accused acts with intent to cause wrongful loss or damage to the public or any person, OR with knowledge that such loss or damage is likely.
It is immaterial whether the property belongs to the victim, a third party, or even the offender themselves (Explanation 2).

mens_rea.description: Intent to cause wrongful loss or damage, or knowledge that wrongful loss or damage is likely to be caused, to the public or any person.

examples.scenario: A voluntarily burns a valuable security belonging to Z intending to cause wrongful loss to Z.
examples.result: A has committed mischief under Section 425.

examples.scenario: A introduces water into an ice-house belonging to Z, causing the ice to melt, intending wrongful loss to Z.
examples.result: A has committed mischief under Section 425.

examples.scenario: A, knowing his assets are about to be seized, destroys them to prevent Z from recovering a debt.
examples.result: A has committed mischief under Section 425.

examples.scenario: A having joint property with Z in a horse, shoots the horse intending wrongful loss to Z.
examples.result: A has committed mischief under Section 425 — mischief can be committed on jointly owned property.

max_punishment: Not applicable (definition section; punishment under Section 426 and subsequent sections)
ai_summary: Section 425 IPC defines mischief as intentional or knowing destruction or injurious alteration of property causing wrongful loss to any person — including by damaging one's own or jointly-held property — forming the basis for a range of mischief offences punished under Sections 426–440.

title: Punishment for Mischief
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
bare_act_text: Whoever commits mischief shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three months, or with fine, or with both.
meaning: Section 426 IPC prescribes the base punishment for the offence of mischief as defined under Section 425 IPC.
simple_explanation: Anyone who commits mischief (as defined in Section 425) can be punished with up to 3 months imprisonment, or a fine, or both. This is the minimum punishment for ordinary mischief; more serious forms attract much higher punishments under Sections 427–440.
objective: To prescribe the basic punishment for the general offence of mischief, with enhanced punishments for aggravated forms specified in subsequent sections.
legal_significance: Base punishment provision for mischief. More serious forms — such as mischief causing damage of Rs. 50 or more, killing animals, damaging public infrastructure, or arson — attract enhanced punishments under Sections 427–440.
essential_elements: Commission of mischief as defined under Section 425 IPC.

mens_rea.description: Intent or knowledge of likely wrongful loss as required under Section 425 IPC.

punishment.normal_case: Imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to 3 months, or fine, or both.

examples.scenario: A intentionally damages Z's garden fence causing minor damage worth less than Rs. 50.
examples.result: A is liable for basic mischief under Section 425, punishable under Section 426.

max_punishment: 3 months imprisonment, or fine, or both
ai_summary: Section 426 IPC prescribes punishment of up to 3 months imprisonment or fine or both for the basic offence of mischief as defined under Section 425, with aggravated forms punished more severely under Sections 427–440.

title: Mischief Causing Damage to the Amount of Fifty Rupees
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
bare_act_text: Whoever commits mischief and thereby causes loss or damage to the amount of fifty rupees or upwards, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.
meaning: Section 427 IPC provides enhanced punishment for mischief where the loss or damage caused amounts to Rs. 50 or more.
simple_explanation: If a person commits mischief and the resulting damage or loss is Rs. 50 or more, they face a higher punishment of up to 2 years imprisonment, or a fine, or both — significantly more than the 3-month maximum under Section 426.
objective: To prescribe enhanced punishment for mischief that causes damage of a meaningful monetary threshold, recognising the greater harm caused.
legal_significance: Though the Rs. 50 threshold is archaic by modern standards, this section is still regularly applied by courts for cases of property damage, with the threshold being interpreted in context. It is a frequently charged section in cases of vandalism and property destruction.
essential_elements: The accused commits mischief as defined under Section 425 IPC.
The mischief causes loss or damage to the amount of Rs. 50 or more.

mens_rea.description: Intent or knowledge of likely wrongful loss as required under Section 425 IPC.

punishment.normal_case: Imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to 2 years, or fine, or both.

examples.scenario: A smashes the windowpanes of Z's shop, causing damage worth several thousand rupees.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 427 for mischief causing damage above Rs. 50.

max_punishment: 2 years imprisonment, or fine, or both
ai_summary: Section 427 IPC prescribes enhanced punishment of up to 2 years imprisonment or fine or both for mischief that causes damage or loss of Rs. 50 or more.

title: Mischief by Killing or Maiming Animal of the Value of Ten Rupees
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
bare_act_text: Whoever commits mischief by killing, poisoning, maiming or rendering useless any animal or animals of the value of ten rupees or upwards, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.
meaning: Section 428 IPC provides punishment for mischief committed by killing, poisoning, maiming, or rendering useless any animal valued at Rs. 10 or more.
simple_explanation: If a person, with mischievous intent, kills, poisons, maims, or makes useless any animal worth Rs. 10 or more, they can be punished with up to 2 years imprisonment, or a fine, or both. Animals are treated as property under this provision, and this section specifically addresses harm to them.
objective: To specifically punish mischief committed against animals, recognising their value as property and the particular cruelty involved in harming them.
legal_significance: Specifically addresses harm to animals as a form of mischief. Animals are treated as property for the purpose of mischief offences. The section covers not only killing but also poisoning, maiming, and rendering the animal useless.
essential_elements: The accused commits mischief as defined under Section 425 IPC.
The mischief is committed by killing, poisoning, maiming, or rendering useless any animal.
The animal or animals are valued at Rs. 10 or more.

mens_rea.description: Intent or knowledge of likely wrongful loss as required under Section 425 IPC.

punishment.normal_case: Imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to 2 years, or fine, or both.

examples.scenario: A intentionally poisons Z's dog worth Rs. 500 to cause wrongful loss to Z.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 428 for mischief by killing or poisoning an animal.

max_punishment: 2 years imprisonment, or fine, or both
ai_summary: Section 428 IPC punishes mischief by killing, poisoning, maiming, or rendering useless any animal valued at Rs. 10 or more, with up to 2 years imprisonment or fine or both.

title: Mischief by Killing or Maiming Cattle, etc., of Any Value or Any Animal of the Value of Fifty Rupees
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
bare_act_text: Whoever commits mischief by killing, poisoning, maiming or rendering useless, any elephant, camel, horse, mule, buffalo, bull, cow or ox, whatever may be the value thereof, or any other animal of the value of fifty rupees or upwards, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to five years, or with fine, or with both.
meaning: Section 429 IPC prescribes enhanced punishment for mischief by killing, poisoning, maiming, or rendering useless specified cattle and working animals (regardless of value) or any other animal valued at Rs. 50 or more.
simple_explanation: If someone kills, poisons, maims, or renders useless an elephant, camel, horse, mule, buffalo, bull, cow, or ox — regardless of its value — or any other animal worth Rs. 50 or more, they can be punished with up to 5 years imprisonment, or a fine, or both. Cows and cattle are given special protection with no minimum value requirement.
objective: To provide enhanced protection to valuable draught and agricultural animals — including cows, bulls, buffaloes, horses, camels, and elephants — which are of critical importance to agriculture and livelihoods.
legal_significance: An aggravated form of Section 428, this section specifically protects economically and culturally significant animals — particularly cattle — without any minimum value threshold. The higher punishment reflects the greater harm to agriculture and livelihoods.
essential_elements: The accused commits mischief as defined under Section 425 IPC.

The mischief is committed by killing, poisoning, maiming, or rendering useless an elephant, camel, horse, mule, buffalo, bull, cow, or ox (regardless of value); OR any other animal valued at Rs. 50 or more.

mens_rea.description: Intent or knowledge of likely wrongful loss as required under Section 425 IPC.

punishment.normal_case: Imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to 5 years, or fine, or both.

examples.scenario: A intentionally kills Z's cow to cause wrongful loss, regardless of the cow's monetary value.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 429, as cows are specifically protected irrespective of value.

examples.scenario: A poisons a horse belonging to Z worth Rs. 5,000.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 429 for mischief involving a horse.

max_punishment: 5 years imprisonment, or fine, or both
ai_summary: Section 429 IPC punishes mischief against specified cattle and working animals (elephant, camel, horse, mule, buffalo, bull, cow, ox — regardless of value) or any animal worth Rs. 50 or more, with up to 5 years imprisonment or fine or both.

title: Mischief by Injury to Works of Irrigation or by Wrongfully Diverting Water
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
bare_act_text: Whoever commits mischief by doing any act which causes, or which he knows to be likely to cause, a diminution of the supply of water for agricultural purposes, or for food or drink for human beings or for animals which are property, or for cleanliness or for carrying on any manufacture, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to five years, or with fine, or with both.
meaning: Section 430 IPC punishes mischief committed by any act that diminishes or is likely to diminish the supply of water for agriculture, human/animal consumption, cleanliness, or manufacturing purposes.
simple_explanation: If a person damages or tampers with irrigation works, canals, or water sources — causing or likely to cause a reduction in water supply for farming, drinking, cleanliness, or industry — they can be punished with up to 5 years imprisonment, or a fine, or both. This section protects public and agricultural water infrastructure.
objective: To protect water resources and irrigation infrastructure essential for agriculture, public health, and industry from deliberate damage or interference.
legal_significance: Particularly significant in an agrarian society like India. The section covers all acts that reduce water supply — whether by damaging infrastructure or diverting water — for any of the protected purposes listed.
essential_elements: The accused commits mischief as defined under Section 425 IPC.

The act causes or is likely to cause a diminution of water supply for: (a) agricultural purposes, (b) food or drink for human beings or animals that are property, (c) cleanliness, or (d) carrying on any manufacture.

mens_rea.description: Intent or knowledge that the act is likely to cause diminution of water supply for the protected purposes.

punishment.normal_case: Imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to 5 years, or fine, or both.

examples.scenario: A deliberately breaks a canal embankment, diverting water away from Z's agricultural fields.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 430 for mischief by injury to irrigation works.

examples.scenario: A pollutes and blocks a stream that supplies drinking water to a village.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 430 for causing diminution of water supply for human consumption.

max_punishment: 5 years imprisonment, or fine, or both
ai_summary: Section 430 IPC punishes mischief that causes or is likely to cause a reduction in water supply for agriculture, drinking, cleanliness, or manufacturing, with up to 5 years imprisonment or fine or both.

title: Mischief by Injury to Public Road, Bridge, River or Channel
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
bare_act_text: Whoever commits mischief by doing any act which renders or which he knows to be likely to render any public road, bridge, navigable river or navigable channel, natural or artificial, impassable or less safe for travelling or conveying property, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to five years, or with fine, or with both.
meaning: Section 431 IPC punishes mischief that makes or is likely to make any public road, bridge, or navigable waterway impassable or less safe for travel or transportation of property.
simple_explanation: If a person damages a public road, bridge, river, or canal — making it impassable or unsafe for travel or transporting goods — they can be punished with up to 5 years imprisonment, or a fine, or both. This section protects public transportation infrastructure from deliberate sabotage.
objective: To protect public roads, bridges, and navigable waterways from deliberate damage that endangers travel and the movement of goods.
legal_significance: Safeguards critical public infrastructure. The section covers both natural and artificial navigable channels, and applies to any act that makes such infrastructure impassable or merely less safe.
essential_elements: The accused commits mischief as defined under Section 425 IPC.
The act renders or is likely to render a public road, bridge, navigable river, or navigable channel (natural or artificial) impassable or less safe for travelling or conveying property.

mens_rea.description: Intent or knowledge that the act is likely to render public roads, bridges, or waterways impassable or less safe.

punishment.normal_case: Imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to 5 years, or fine, or both.

examples.scenario: A deliberately digs up a public road, making it impassable for vehicles.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 431 for mischief by injury to a public road.

examples.scenario: A sabotages a bridge by removing bolts, making it unsafe for crossing.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 431 for mischief by injury to a public bridge.

max_punishment: 5 years imprisonment, or fine, or both
ai_summary: Section 431 IPC punishes mischief that renders a public road, bridge, or navigable waterway impassable or less safe for travel or conveyance of property, with up to 5 years imprisonment or fine or both.

title: Mischief by Causing Inundation or Obstruction to Public Drainage Attended with Damage
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
bare_act_text: Whoever commits mischief by doing any act which causes or which he knows to be likely to cause an inundation or an obstruction to any public drainage attended with injury or damage, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to five years, or with fine, or with both.
meaning: Section 432 IPC punishes mischief committed by any act that causes or is likely to cause flooding (inundation) or obstruction to public drainage, resulting in injury or damage.
simple_explanation: If a person deliberately causes flooding or blocks public drainage systems — resulting in injury or damage — they can be punished with up to 5 years imprisonment, or a fine, or both. This section protects public drainage infrastructure and prevents deliberate acts of flooding.
objective: To punish deliberate interference with public drainage systems that causes flooding or damage to public or private property.
legal_significance: Protects public drainage infrastructure from deliberate sabotage. The section requires the inundation or obstruction to be 'attended with injury or damage', meaning there must be actual harm or a likelihood thereof.
essential_elements: The accused commits mischief as defined under Section 425 IPC.
The act causes or is likely to cause an inundation (flooding) or obstruction to any public drainage.
The inundation or obstruction is attended with injury or damage.

mens_rea.description: Intent or knowledge that the act is likely to cause inundation or obstruction to public drainage with injury or damage.

punishment.normal_case: Imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to 5 years, or fine, or both.

examples.scenario: A deliberately blocks a public drainage channel causing flooding of nearby houses and crop fields.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 432 for mischief by causing inundation and obstruction to public drainage.

max_punishment: 5 years imprisonment, or fine, or both
ai_summary: Section 432 IPC punishes mischief that causes or is likely to cause flooding or obstruction to public drainage resulting in injury or damage, with up to 5 years imprisonment or fine or both.

title: Mischief by Destroying, Moving or Rendering Less Useful a Light-House or Sea-Mark
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
bare_act_text: Whoever commits mischief by destroying or moving any light-house or other light used as a sea-mark, or any sea-mark or buoy or other thing placed as a guide for navigators, or by any act which renders any such light-house, sea-mark, buoy or other such thing as aforesaid less useful as a guide for navigators, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, or with fine, or with both.
meaning: Section 433 IPC punishes mischief committed by destroying, moving, or impairing the usefulness of a lighthouse, sea-mark, buoy, or any navigational guide for mariners.
simple_explanation: If a person destroys, moves, or reduces the usefulness of a lighthouse, sea-mark, buoy, or any device that guides ships and navigators at sea, they can be punished with up to 7 years imprisonment, or a fine, or both. The law treats interference with navigational safety very seriously because it can endanger ships and lives at sea.
objective: To protect navigational aids — lighthouses, sea-marks, buoys — which are critical to maritime safety, from deliberate interference that could endanger vessels and lives at sea.
legal_significance: Carries one of the highest punishments among the mischief provisions, reflecting the potentially catastrophic consequences of interfering with maritime navigation aids. The section covers not just destruction but also moving or reducing the effectiveness of such aids.
essential_elements: The accused commits mischief as defined under Section 425 IPC.
The mischief involves destroying or moving a lighthouse, sea-mark, buoy, or navigational guide; OR any act that renders such a navigational aid less useful.

mens_rea.description: Intent or knowledge of likely wrongful loss or damage as required under Section 425 IPC.

punishment.normal_case: Imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to 7 years, or fine, or both.

examples.scenario: A deliberately destroys a lighthouse light, causing ships navigating at night to lose their bearings.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 433 for mischief by destroying a lighthouse.

examples.scenario: A moves a buoy marking a dangerous reef to a different location, rendering it useless as a navigational warning.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 433 for mischief by moving a sea-mark.

max_punishment: 7 years imprisonment, or fine, or both
ai_summary: Section 433 IPC punishes mischief involving destruction, removal, or impairment of lighthouses, sea-marks, buoys, or other navigational aids with up to 7 years imprisonment or fine or both, reflecting the grave danger such acts pose to maritime safety.

title: Mischief by Destroying or Moving, etc., a Land-Mark Fixed by Public Authority
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
bare_act_text: Whoever commits mischief by destroying or moving any land-mark fixed by the authority of a public servant, or by any act which renders such land-mark less useful as such, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to one year, or with fine, or with both.
meaning: Section 434 IPC punishes mischief committed by destroying, moving, or impairing the usefulness of a land-mark fixed by a public authority.
simple_explanation: If a person destroys, moves, or reduces the usefulness of a boundary marker or land-mark officially placed by a public authority, they can be punished with up to 1 year imprisonment, or a fine, or both. Such land-marks are used for survey, land revenue, and boundary demarcation purposes, and tampering with them causes serious legal and administrative harm.
objective: To protect official land-marks and boundary markers fixed by public authorities from deliberate tampering, which can cause disputes over land ownership, boundaries, and revenue administration.
legal_significance: Protects the integrity of officially fixed land-marks that form the basis of land records, survey maps, and boundary demarcation. Tampering with such marks can give rise to serious property disputes and is therefore specifically criminalised.
essential_elements: The accused commits mischief as defined under Section 425 IPC.
The mischief involves destroying or moving a land-mark fixed by the authority of a public servant; OR any act that renders such a land-mark less useful.

mens_rea.description: Intent or knowledge of likely wrongful loss or damage as required under Section 425 IPC.

punishment.normal_case: Imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to 1 year, or fine, or both.

examples.scenario: A removes a boundary pillar placed by a government surveyor to alter the apparent boundary of his land in his favour.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 434 for mischief by moving a land-mark fixed by public authority.

max_punishment: 1 year imprisonment, or fine, or both
ai_summary: Section 434 IPC punishes mischief involving destruction, removal, or impairment of officially fixed land-marks with up to 1 year imprisonment or fine or both, protecting the integrity of public survey and boundary demarcation.

title: Mischief by Fire or Explosive Substance with Intent to Cause Damage to Amount of One Hundred or (in Case of Agricultural Produce) Ten Rupees
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
bare_act_text: Whoever commits mischief by fire or any explosive substance intending to cause, or knowing it to be likely that he will thereby cause, damage to any property to the amount of one hundred rupees or upwards, or (where the property is agricultural produce) ten rupees or upwards, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years and shall also be liable to fine.
meaning: Section 435 IPC punishes mischief committed using fire or explosive substances where the intended or likely damage amounts to Rs. 100 or more for general property, or Rs. 10 or more for agricultural produce.
simple_explanation: If a person uses fire or explosives to cause damage worth Rs. 100 or more (or Rs. 10 or more for agricultural produce like crops), they can be punished with up to 7 years imprisonment and a fine. The lower threshold for agricultural produce reflects the special protection given to farmers' crops and produce.
objective: To impose severe punishment for the particularly dangerous and destructive method of causing mischief using fire or explosives, with special protection for agricultural produce.
legal_significance: An aggravated form of mischief given the inherent danger of fire and explosives. The lower threshold for agricultural produce recognises the vulnerability of farmers and the disproportionate harm that even small-scale crop damage can cause to livelihoods.
essential_elements: The accused commits mischief as defined under Section 425 IPC.
The mischief is committed by fire or any explosive substance.
The accused intends to cause, or knows it is likely to cause, damage of Rs. 100 or more to any property; OR damage of Rs. 10 or more to agricultural produce.

mens_rea.description: Intent to cause, or knowledge of the likelihood of causing, damage of the specified amount by use of fire or explosive substance.

punishment.normal_case: Imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to 7 years, and also liable to fine.

examples.scenario: A sets fire to Z's warehouse, intending to cause damage worth several lakhs.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 435 for mischief by fire causing damage exceeding Rs. 100.

examples.scenario: A sets fire to Z's standing crops worth Rs. 50 to cause wrongful loss.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 435 as the damage to agricultural produce exceeds Rs. 10.

max_punishment: 7 years imprisonment and fine
ai_summary: Section 435 IPC punishes mischief by fire or explosive substance causing damage of Rs. 100 or more to property (or Rs. 10 or more to agricultural produce) with up to 7 years imprisonment and fine.

title: Mischief by Fire or Explosive Substance with Intent to Destroy House, etc.
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
bare_act_text: Whoever commits mischief by fire or any explosive substance, intending to cause, or knowing it to be likely that he will thereby cause, the destruction of any building which is ordinarily used as a place of worship or as a human dwelling or as a place for the custody of property, shall be punished with imprisonment for life, or with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.
meaning: Section 436 IPC prescribes the most severe mischief punishment for arson or use of explosives intended to destroy a building used as a place of worship, a human dwelling, or a place for custody of property.
simple_explanation: If a person uses fire or explosives to destroy — or intending to destroy — a home, place of worship, or a building used for storing property, they face the harshest punishment: life imprisonment, or up to 10 years imprisonment, along with a fine. Setting a house or temple on fire is treated as one of the gravest property offences under IPC.
objective: To impose the most severe punishment for arson targeting buildings used as homes, places of worship, or property storage, given the extreme danger to human life and the sanctity of such places.
legal_significance: The most serious mischief provision in IPC. The provision for life imprisonment reflects the grave risk to human life and the particularly heinous nature of arson targeting dwellings and places of worship. Commonly invoked in communal riot cases involving arson.
essential_elements: The accused commits mischief as defined under Section 425 IPC.
The mischief is committed by fire or any explosive substance.
The accused intends to cause, or knows it likely to cause, the destruction of a building ordinarily used as: (a) a place of worship, (b) a human dwelling, or (c) a place for the custody of property.

mens_rea.description: Intent to cause or knowledge of the likely destruction of a building used as a place of worship, human dwelling, or custody of property by fire or explosives.

punishment.normal_case: Imprisonment for life, or imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to 10 years, and also liable to fine.

examples.scenario: A sets fire to Z's house intending to destroy it, causing the building to burn down.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 436 for mischief by fire with intent to destroy a human dwelling.

examples.scenario: A uses explosives to destroy a temple during communal unrest.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 436 for mischief by explosive substance targeting a place of worship.

max_punishment: Imprisonment for life, or 10 years imprisonment and fine
ai_summary: Section 436 IPC prescribes life imprisonment or up to 10 years imprisonment and fine for mischief by fire or explosives intended to destroy a building used as a place of worship, human dwelling, or property storage — the most severe mischief provision under IPC.

title: Mischief with Intent to Destroy or Make Unsafe a Decked Vessel or One of Twenty Tons Burden
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
bare_act_text: Whoever commits mischief to any decked vessel or any vessel of a burden of twenty tons or upwards, intending to destroy or render unsafe, or knowing it to be likely that he will thereby destroy or render unsafe, that vessel, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.
meaning: Section 437 IPC punishes mischief committed to a decked vessel or a vessel of 20 tons or more with intent to destroy it or render it unsafe.
simple_explanation: If a person damages a decked ship or a vessel weighing 20 tons or more, intending to destroy it or make it unsafe for navigation, they can be punished with up to 10 years imprisonment and a fine. This section protects larger commercial and passenger vessels from deliberate sabotage.
objective: To protect substantial vessels from deliberate sabotage that could endanger lives, cargo, and navigation at sea.
legal_significance: Specifically targets mischief to larger vessels that could cause significant loss of life and property. Applies to decked vessels of any size and any other vessel of 20 tons burden or more. Section 438 provides for a higher punishment when fire or explosives are used.
essential_elements: The accused commits mischief as defined under Section 425 IPC.
The mischief is committed to a decked vessel OR any vessel of a burden of 20 tons or upwards.
The accused intends to destroy or render the vessel unsafe, or knows it is likely to do so.

mens_rea.description: Intent to destroy or render unsafe the vessel, or knowledge that the act is likely to do so.

punishment.normal_case: Imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to 10 years, and also liable to fine.

examples.scenario: A deliberately damages the hull of a 30-ton cargo vessel, knowing it will render the vessel unsafe for sailing.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 437 for mischief with intent to render a vessel unsafe.

max_punishment: 10 years imprisonment and fine
ai_summary: Section 437 IPC punishes mischief to a decked vessel or vessel of 20 tons or more with intent to destroy or render it unsafe, with up to 10 years imprisonment and fine.

title: Punishment for the Mischief Described in Section 437 Committed by Fire or Explosive Substance
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
bare_act_text: Whoever commits, or attempts to commit, by fire or any explosive substance, such mischief as is described in the last preceding section, shall be punished with imprisonment for life, or with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.
meaning: Section 438 IPC prescribes enhanced punishment — including life imprisonment — for committing or attempting to commit mischief to a vessel (as described in Section 437) by means of fire or an explosive substance.
simple_explanation: If a person commits or even attempts to commit the vessel-related mischief described in Section 437 but does so using fire or explosives, the punishment is much higher — life imprisonment, or up to 10 years imprisonment, along with a fine. Even an attempt using fire or explosives against a vessel is punishable under this section.
objective: To impose the highest punishment for the most dangerous form of vessel mischief — using fire or explosives — and to also punish mere attempts given the catastrophic potential of such acts.
legal_significance: An aggravated form of Section 437, specifically for fire and explosive-based attacks on vessels. Notably, the section also punishes attempts, unlike most other mischief provisions. The provision for life imprisonment reflects the grave risk to life and property.
essential_elements: The accused commits or attempts to commit mischief as described in Section 437 IPC.
The mischief or attempted mischief is committed by fire or any explosive substance.

mens_rea.description: Intent to destroy or render unsafe the vessel (as under Section 437), with the use of fire or explosives.

punishment.normal_case: Imprisonment for life, or imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to 10 years, and also liable to fine.

examples.scenario: A sets fire to a 25-ton cargo vessel intending to destroy it while it is docked at port.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 438 for mischief by fire on a vessel as described in Section 437.

examples.scenario: A attempts to plant explosives on a decked vessel to destroy it but is caught before the explosion.
examples.result: A is still liable under Section 438 as mere attempt using explosives is punishable.

max_punishment: Imprisonment for life, or 10 years imprisonment and fine
ai_summary: Section 438 IPC punishes commission or attempt of vessel mischief (under Section 437) by fire or explosives with life imprisonment or up to 10 years imprisonment and fine — one of the few mischief provisions that explicitly punishes attempts.

title: Punishment for Intentionally Running Vessel Aground or Ashore with Intent to Commit Theft, etc.
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
bare_act_text: Whoever intentionally runs any vessel aground or ashore, intending to commit theft of any property contained therein or to dishonestly misappropriate any such property, or with intent that such theft or misappropriation of property may be committed, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.
meaning: Section 439 IPC punishes any person who intentionally runs a vessel aground or ashore with the intent to steal or misappropriate property on board, or to enable others to do so.
simple_explanation: If a person deliberately causes a ship to run aground or onto a shore — not by accident but with the intention of stealing or misappropriating its cargo, or enabling someone else to do so — they face up to 10 years imprisonment and a fine. This section targets deliberate wrecking of vessels for criminal gain.
objective: To punish deliberate wrecking of vessels carried out with criminal intent to steal or misappropriate property on board, commonly known as wrecking for plunder.
legal_significance: Addresses a historically recognised form of maritime crime — deliberately wrecking a vessel to plunder its cargo. The section is notable in that the criminal intent (theft/misappropriation) must exist at the time of running the vessel aground.
essential_elements: The accused intentionally runs any vessel aground or ashore.
The accused acts with intent to commit theft or dishonest misappropriation of property on board; OR with intent that such theft or misappropriation may be committed by any person.
