mens_rea.description: Specific intent to commit theft or misappropriation of property on the vessel, or to enable others to do so.

punishment.normal_case: Imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to 10 years, and also liable to fine.

examples.scenario: A, the captain of a cargo ship, intentionally steers the ship aground in a secluded location so that his accomplices can plunder the cargo.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 439 for intentionally running a vessel aground with intent to enable theft.

max_punishment: 10 years imprisonment and fine
ai_summary: Section 439 IPC punishes intentionally running a vessel aground or ashore with intent to commit or enable theft or misappropriation of property on board, with up to 10 years imprisonment and fine.

title: Mischief Committed After Preparation Made for Causing Death or Hurt
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
bare_act_text: Whoever commits mischief, having made preparation for causing to any person death, or hurt, or wrongful restraint, or fear of death, or of hurt, or of wrongful restraint, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to five years, and shall also be liable to fine.
meaning: Section 440 IPC prescribes enhanced punishment for mischief committed by a person who has already made preparations to cause death, hurt, wrongful restraint, or fear thereof to any person.
simple_explanation: If a person commits mischief after having made preparations to hurt, kill, or restrain someone — or to put them in fear of such harm — they face up to 5 years imprisonment and a fine. The preparation to harm a person makes the mischief more dangerous and attracts higher punishment.
objective: To punish mischief that is accompanied by preparation for violence or restraint against a person, recognising that such mischief carries a far greater risk of harm.
legal_significance: The last of the aggravated mischief provisions, closing the chapter on mischief offences. The section addresses situations where mischief is accompanied by preparation for physical harm — making it analogous to armed trespass in the trespass provisions.
essential_elements: The accused commits mischief as defined under Section 425 IPC.
Prior to or during the mischief, the accused has made preparation to cause any person: death, hurt, wrongful restraint, or fear of death, hurt, or wrongful restraint.

mens_rea.description: Intent to cause wrongful loss (as under Section 425) combined with preparation for causing death, hurt, wrongful restraint, or fear thereof.

punishment.normal_case: Imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to 5 years, and also liable to fine.

examples.scenario: A, armed with a knife to threaten anyone who intervenes, breaks into Z's factory and destroys machinery.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 440 for mischief after preparation for causing hurt or fear of hurt.

max_punishment: 5 years imprisonment and fine
ai_summary: Section 440 IPC punishes mischief committed after the offender has made preparation for causing death, hurt, wrongful restraint, or fear thereof, with up to 5 years imprisonment and fine.

title: Criminal Trespass
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
bare_act_text: Whoever enters into or upon property in the possession of another with intent to commit an offence or to intimidate, insult or annoy any person in possession of such property, or having lawfully entered into or upon such property, unlawfully remains there with intent thereby to intimidate, insult or annoy any such person, or with intent to commit an offence, is said to commit 'criminal trespass'.
meaning: Section 441 IPC defines criminal trespass as entering another's property with intent to commit an offence or to intimidate, insult, or annoy the possessor — or lawfully entering but remaining unlawfully with such intent.
simple_explanation: Criminal trespass has two forms: (1) entering someone else's property with a criminal intent — to commit an offence, or to intimidate, insult, or annoy the person in possession; and (2) entering lawfully but then refusing to leave and remaining there with the same criminal intent. The key element is the criminal intention — simple trespass without such intent is not covered.
objective: To protect a person's peaceful possession of property from intentional intrusion with criminal or harmful intent, going beyond civil trespass to address criminally motivated entry or stay.
legal_significance: Foundational definition for a series of trespass offences (Sections 441–462). The distinction between criminal and civil trespass lies in the intent. Note that the Orissa State Amendment added a third form covering unauthorised occupation after being asked to leave by notice.
essential_elements: The accused enters into or upon property in the possession of another; OR having lawfully entered, unlawfully remains.
The entry or remaining is with intent to: (a) commit an offence, OR (b) intimidate, insult, or annoy the person in possession.

mens_rea.description: Intent to commit an offence, or to intimidate, insult, or annoy the person in possession of the property, at the time of entry or remaining.

examples.scenario: A enters Z's field intending to steal crops.
examples.result: A commits criminal trespass under Section 441.

examples.scenario: A lawfully enters Z's office as a visitor but refuses to leave intending to annoy and embarrass Z in front of clients.
examples.result: A commits criminal trespass by unlawfully remaining with intent to annoy.

max_punishment: Not applicable (definition section; punishment under Section 447)
ai_summary: Section 441 IPC defines criminal trespass as entry or unlawful remaining in another's property with intent to commit an offence or to intimidate, insult, or annoy the possessor, forming the basis for a range of trespass offences punished under Sections 447 onwards.

title: House-Trespass
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
bare_act_text: Whoever commits criminal trespass by entering into or remaining in any building, tent or vessel used as a human dwelling or any building used as a place for worship, or as a place for the custody of property, is said to commit 'house-trespass'. Explanation.—The introduction of any part of the criminal trespasser's body is entering sufficient to constitute house-trespass.
meaning: Section 442 IPC defines house-trespass as criminal trespass committed specifically in a building, tent, or vessel used as a human dwelling, or a building used as a place of worship or for property storage.
simple_explanation: House-trespass is a more serious form of criminal trespass — it is committed when a person trespasses into or remains in a home, tent, ship used as a dwelling, temple/mosque/church, or a building used to store property. Even putting just a hand or part of the body inside qualifies as entry under this section.
objective: To provide enhanced protection to spaces closely associated with human habitation, religious sanctity, and secure storage of property — treating trespass in such spaces as more serious than ordinary criminal trespass.
legal_significance: An aggravated form of criminal trespass, attracting higher punishment under Section 448. The Explanation makes clear that partial bodily entry is sufficient — the entire body need not enter — making the definition deliberately broad.
essential_elements: The accused commits criminal trespass as defined under Section 441 IPC.
The trespass is committed by entering or remaining in a building, tent, or vessel used as a human dwelling; OR a building used as a place of worship or for custody of property.
Even the introduction of any part of the body into such premises constitutes entry.

mens_rea.description: Criminal intent as required under Section 441 IPC.

examples.scenario: A pushes open the door of Z's house and reaches his hand inside to take something, intending theft.
examples.result: A commits house-trespass as even partial bodily entry is sufficient under the Explanation.

examples.scenario: A enters a temple intending to steal the deity's ornaments.
examples.result: A commits house-trespass in a building used as a place of worship.

max_punishment: Not applicable (definition section; punishment under Section 448)
ai_summary: Section 442 IPC defines house-trespass as criminal trespass in a dwelling, place of worship, or property storage building — with even partial bodily entry being sufficient — forming the basis for enhanced punishment under Section 448 and subsequent sections.

title: Lurking House-Trespass
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
bare_act_text: Whoever commits house-trespass having taken precautions to conceal such house-trespass from some person who has a right to exclude or eject the trespasser from the building, tent or vessel which is the subject of the trespass, is said to commit 'lurking house-trespass'.
meaning: Section 443 IPC defines lurking house-trespass as house-trespass in which the offender takes precautions to conceal the trespass from a person who has the right to exclude or eject them.
simple_explanation: Lurking house-trespass is a sneaky form of house-trespass — where the trespasser takes deliberate steps to hide their presence from someone who has the right to throw them out. The element of concealment makes this more serious than ordinary house-trespass.
objective: To recognise and punish the more dangerous form of house-trespass where the offender deliberately hides their presence, making it harder for the rightful possessor to detect and eject them.
legal_significance: An aggravated form of house-trespass (Section 442), attracting higher punishment under Section 453. The defining feature is the act of concealment from a person with the right to eject the trespasser.
essential_elements: The accused commits house-trespass as defined under Section 442 IPC.
The accused takes precautions to conceal the trespass from some person who has a right to exclude or eject the trespasser.

mens_rea.description: Criminal intent as required under Section 441 IPC, combined with deliberate concealment of the trespass.

examples.scenario: A sneaks into Z's house through a back door, hiding behind furniture to avoid detection by Z, intending to steal.
examples.result: A commits lurking house-trespass by taking precautions to conceal the trespass from Z.

max_punishment: Not applicable (definition section; punishment under Section 453)
ai_summary: Section 443 IPC defines lurking house-trespass as house-trespass with deliberate concealment from a person with the right to eject the trespasser, forming the basis for enhanced punishment under Section 453 onwards.

title: Lurking House-Trespass by Night
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
bare_act_text: Whoever commits lurking house-trespass after sunset and before sunrise, is said to commit 'lurking house-trespass by night'.
meaning: Section 444 IPC defines lurking house-trespass by night as lurking house-trespass committed after sunset and before sunrise.
simple_explanation: Lurking house-trespass by night is simply lurking house-trespass (as defined in Section 443) that takes place at night — i.e., after sunset and before sunrise. Committing such trespass at night is treated as even more serious because of the greater danger and vulnerability it creates.
objective: To define and distinguish the nighttime variant of lurking house-trespass, which is treated as more dangerous due to the cover of darkness and the heightened vulnerability of occupants.
legal_significance: Nighttime trespass combined with concealment is treated as the most dangerous form of non-violent trespass. Punished under Section 456, and further aggravated forms involving preparedness for violence or actual offences are covered under Sections 457 and 458.
essential_elements: The accused commits lurking house-trespass as defined under Section 443 IPC.
The trespass is committed after sunset and before sunrise.

mens_rea.description: Criminal intent as required under Section 441 IPC, combined with concealment (Section 443) and nighttime commission.

examples.scenario: A sneaks into Z's house at 2 AM, hiding from Z and his family, intending to steal valuables.
examples.result: A commits lurking house-trespass by night under Section 444.

max_punishment: Not applicable (definition section; punishment under Section 456)
ai_summary: Section 444 IPC defines lurking house-trespass by night as lurking house-trespass committed after sunset and before sunrise, forming the basis for enhanced punishment under Section 456 and its aggravated variants.

title: House-Breaking
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
bare_act_text: A person is said to commit 'house-breaking' who commits house-trespass if he effects his entrance into the house or any part of it in any of the six ways hereinafter described; or if, being in the house or any part of it for the purpose of committing an offence, or having committed an offence therein, he quits the house or any part of it in any of such six ways, that is to say: First.—If he enters or quits through a passage made by himself, or by any abettor of the house-trespass, in order to the committing of the house-trespass. Secondly.—If he enters or quits through any passage not intended by any person, other than himself or an abettor of the offence, for human entrance; or through any passage to which he has obtained access by scaling or climbing over any wall or building. Thirdly.—If he enters or quits through any passage which he or any abettor of the house-trespass has opened, in order to the committing of the house-trespass by any means by which that passage was not intended by the occupier of the house to be opened. Fourthly.—If he enters or quits by opening any lock in order to the committing of the house-trespass, or in order to the quitting of the house after a house-trespass. Fifthly.—If he effects his entrance or departure by using criminal force or committing an assault, or by threatening any person with assault. Sixthly.—If he enters or quits by any passage which he knows to have been fastened against such entrance or departure, and to have been unfastened by himself or by an abettor of the house-trespass. Explanation.—Any out-house or building occupied with a house, and between which and such house there is an immediate internal communication, is part of the house within the meaning of this section.
meaning: Section 445 IPC defines house-breaking as house-trespass effected by entry or exit through any of six specified methods — including breaking in, climbing, opening locks, using force, or passing through a fastened passage — or by any such means on exit after committing an offence inside.
simple_explanation: House-breaking is a serious form of house-trespass where the offender forces or tricks their way in (or out) through one of six specific methods: (1) making a passage themselves, (2) using an unintended passage or climbing over walls, (3) opening a passage in an unintended way, (4) picking or opening a lock, (5) using force or threats, or (6) passing through a fastened passage that they or their accomplice unfastened. Importantly, it also applies when the person entered lawfully but exits by any of these methods after committing an offence inside.
objective: To define house-breaking with precision, covering all forms of forcible or unauthorized entry or exit from a dwelling, going beyond ordinary house-trespass to capture the breaking-in element.
legal_significance: A more serious form of house-trespass, attracting higher punishment under Section 453 onwards. Notably, the section applies to both entry and exit — a person who entered lawfully but breaks out after committing an offence is equally guilty. The Explanation extends the definition of 'house' to include connected outhouses.
essential_elements: The accused commits house-trespass as defined under Section 442 IPC.

The entry into or exit from the house is effected by one of six specified methods: making a passage, using an unintended passage or scaling, opening a passage in an unintended way, opening a lock, using criminal force or threats, or passing through a fastened passage unfastened by the accused or an abettor.

mens_rea.description: Criminal intent as required under Section 441 IPC, combined with the specific act of breaking in or out by one of the six defined methods.

examples.scenario: A enters Z's house through a window.
examples.result: A commits house-breaking (entry through an unintended passage — Second method).

examples.scenario: A enters Z's house through the door after opening a lock.
examples.result: A commits house-breaking (Fourth method — opening a lock).

examples.scenario: A forces Z's doorkeeper aside by threatening to beat him and enters the house.
examples.result: A commits house-breaking (Fifth method — using threats of assault).

examples.scenario: A makes a hole through the wall of Z's house and puts his hand through.
examples.result: A commits house-breaking (First method — making a passage).

max_punishment: Not applicable (definition section; punishment under Section 453 and subsequent sections)
ai_summary: Section 445 IPC defines house-breaking as house-trespass effected by one of six specified methods of forcible or unauthorised entry or exit — including making passages, climbing, opening locks, using force, or passing through fastened passages — forming the basis for enhanced punishment under Section 453 onwards.

title: House-Breaking by Night
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
bare_act_text: Whoever commits house-breaking after sunset and before sunrise, is said to commit 'house-breaking by night'.
meaning: Section 446 IPC defines house-breaking by night as house-breaking (as defined in Section 445) committed after sunset and before sunrise.
simple_explanation: House-breaking by night is simply house-breaking that takes place at night — after sunset and before sunrise. Nighttime house-breaking is treated as more dangerous because of the cover of darkness and the heightened risk to occupants.
objective: To distinguish and define the nighttime variant of house-breaking, which is treated as a more serious offence given the greater danger it poses to the occupants of the house.
legal_significance: Attracts enhanced punishment under Section 456. Further aggravated forms — involving intent to commit offences, preparation for violence — are punished under Sections 457 and 458, and causing grievous hurt under Section 459.
essential_elements: The accused commits house-breaking as defined under Section 445 IPC.
The house-breaking is committed after sunset and before sunrise.

mens_rea.description: Criminal intent as required under Sections 441 and 445 IPC.

examples.scenario: A breaks open the lock of Z's house at midnight and enters to steal.
examples.result: A commits house-breaking by night under Section 446.

max_punishment: Not applicable (definition section; punishment under Section 456)
ai_summary: Section 446 IPC defines house-breaking by night as house-breaking committed after sunset and before sunrise, forming the basis for enhanced punishment under Section 456 and its aggravated variants.

title: Punishment for Criminal Trespass
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
bare_act_text: Whoever commits criminal trespass shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three months, or with fine which may extend to five hundred rupees, or with both.
meaning: Section 447 IPC prescribes punishment for the basic offence of criminal trespass as defined under Section 441 IPC.
simple_explanation: Any person who commits criminal trespass (as defined in Section 441) can be punished with up to 3 months imprisonment, or a fine up to Rs. 500, or both. This is the minimum punishment for the simplest form of criminal trespass.
objective: To prescribe base punishment for criminal trespass, with aggravated forms attracting higher punishment under Sections 448–460.
legal_significance: Base punishment provision for criminal trespass. More serious forms — house-trespass, lurking house-trespass, house-breaking, and nighttime variants — attract significantly higher punishments under subsequent sections.
essential_elements: Commission of criminal trespass as defined under Section 441 IPC.

mens_rea.description: Criminal intent as required under Section 441 IPC.

punishment.normal_case: Imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to 3 months, or fine up to Rs. 500, or both.

examples.scenario: A enters Z's garden with intent to annoy Z but causes no harm.
examples.result: A is liable for criminal trespass punishable under Section 447.

max_punishment: 3 months imprisonment, or fine up to Rs. 500, or both
ai_summary: Section 447 IPC prescribes punishment of up to 3 months imprisonment or fine up to Rs. 500 or both for basic criminal trespass as defined under Section 441.

title: Punishment for House-Trespass
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
bare_act_text: Whoever commits house-trespass shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to one year, or with fine which may extend to one thousand rupees, or with both.
meaning: Section 448 IPC prescribes punishment for house-trespass as defined under Section 442 IPC.
simple_explanation: Anyone who commits house-trespass — trespassing into a home, place of worship, or property storage building — can be punished with up to 1 year imprisonment, or a fine up to Rs. 1,000, or both. This is higher than ordinary criminal trespass given the violation of the sanctity of a dwelling.
objective: To prescribe enhanced punishment for the more serious form of criminal trespass that involves entry into dwellings, religious places, and property storage buildings.
legal_significance: Base punishment for house-trespass. Aggravated forms — house-trespass to commit a more serious offence, house-trespass after preparation for violence — attract much higher punishments under Sections 449–452.
essential_elements: Commission of house-trespass as defined under Section 442 IPC.

mens_rea.description: Criminal intent as required under Sections 441 and 442 IPC.

punishment.normal_case: Imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to 1 year, or fine up to Rs. 1,000, or both.

examples.scenario: A enters Z's house without permission intending to insult Z in front of his family.
examples.result: A is liable for house-trespass punishable under Section 448.

max_punishment: 1 year imprisonment, or fine up to Rs. 1,000, or both
ai_summary: Section 448 IPC prescribes punishment of up to 1 year imprisonment or fine up to Rs. 1,000 or both for house-trespass as defined under Section 442.

title: House-Trespass in Order to Commit Offence Punishable with Death
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
bare_act_text: Whoever commits house-trespass in order to the committing of any offence punishable with death, shall be punished with imprisonment for life, or with rigorous imprisonment for a term not exceeding ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.
meaning: Section 449 IPC prescribes severe punishment for house-trespass committed with the purpose of carrying out an offence that is itself punishable with death.
simple_explanation: If a person trespasses into a house with the intention of committing an offence that carries the death penalty — such as murder or gang rape — they can be punished with life imprisonment, or up to 10 years rigorous imprisonment, along with a fine.
objective: To impose the most severe punishment for house-trespass that is a precursor to the gravest offences under law — those punishable by death.
legal_significance: The most serious house-trespass provision, reflecting the gravity of entering a dwelling with intent to commit a capital offence. The punishment for the trespass itself approaches that of the intended offence.
essential_elements: The accused commits house-trespass as defined under Section 442 IPC.
The house-trespass is committed in order to commit an offence punishable with death.

mens_rea.description: Specific intent to commit an offence punishable with death.

punishment.normal_case: Imprisonment for life, or rigorous imprisonment for a term not exceeding 10 years, and also liable to fine.

examples.scenario: A enters Z's house with the intent to commit murder.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 449 for house-trespass in order to commit an offence punishable with death.

max_punishment: Imprisonment for life, or 10 years rigorous imprisonment and fine
ai_summary: Section 449 IPC punishes house-trespass committed with intent to carry out an offence punishable with death, with life imprisonment or up to 10 years rigorous imprisonment and fine.

title: House-Trespass in Order to Commit Offence Punishable with Imprisonment for Life
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
bare_act_text: Whoever commits house-trespass in order to the committing of any offence punishable with imprisonment for life, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term not exceeding ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.
meaning: Section 450 IPC punishes house-trespass committed with the intent to carry out an offence that is punishable with life imprisonment.
simple_explanation: If a person enters a house with the intention of committing an offence that carries life imprisonment as its maximum punishment — such as dacoity or certain serious assaults — they can be punished with up to 10 years imprisonment and a fine.
objective: To impose enhanced punishment for house-trespass committed as a precursor to very serious offences carrying life imprisonment.
legal_significance: Part of the escalating punishment structure for house-trespass based on the gravity of the intended offence. Sits between Section 449 (death-penalty offences) and Section 451 (imprisonment offences).
essential_elements: The accused commits house-trespass as defined under Section 442 IPC.
The house-trespass is committed in order to commit an offence punishable with imprisonment for life.

mens_rea.description: Specific intent to commit an offence punishable with imprisonment for life.

punishment.normal_case: Imprisonment of either description for a term not exceeding 10 years, and also liable to fine.

examples.scenario: A enters Z's house with the intent to commit dacoity (punishable with life imprisonment).
examples.result: A is liable under Section 450 for house-trespass in order to commit an offence punishable with life imprisonment.

max_punishment: 10 years imprisonment and fine
ai_summary: Section 450 IPC punishes house-trespass committed with intent to carry out an offence punishable with life imprisonment, with up to 10 years imprisonment and fine.

title: House-Trespass in Order to Commit Offence Punishable with Imprisonment
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
bare_act_text: Whoever commits house-trespass in order to the committing of any offence punishable with imprisonment, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, and shall also be liable to fine; and if the offence intended to be committed is theft, the term of the imprisonment may be extended to seven years.
meaning: Section 451 IPC punishes house-trespass committed with intent to carry out any offence punishable with imprisonment, with an enhanced term if the intended offence is theft.
simple_explanation: If a person trespasses into a house intending to commit any offence that is punishable with imprisonment, they face up to 2 years imprisonment and a fine. But if the intended offence is theft, the punishment can go up to 7 years imprisonment — since house-breaking with intent to steal is one of the most common and serious forms of this offence.
objective: To punish house-trespass committed as a precursor to any imprisonable offence, with a special enhanced punishment for theft — the most frequent motive for house-trespass.
legal_significance: The most commonly applied provision in this series, as theft is the predominant motive for house-trespass. The 7-year punishment for theft-related house-trespass reflects the gravity of residential burglary.
essential_elements: The accused commits house-trespass as defined under Section 442 IPC.
The house-trespass is committed in order to commit an offence punishable with imprisonment.
If the intended offence is theft, the maximum punishment is enhanced to 7 years.

mens_rea.description: Specific intent to commit an offence punishable with imprisonment, particularly theft.

punishment.normal_case: Imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to 2 years, and also liable to fine.
punishment.aggravated_case: If the intended offence is theft: imprisonment may extend to 7 years and fine.

examples.scenario: A enters Z's house intending to assault Z (punishable with imprisonment).
examples.result: A is liable under Section 451, punishable with up to 2 years imprisonment and fine.

examples.scenario: A enters Z's house intending to steal valuables.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 451 with the enhanced punishment of up to 7 years imprisonment and fine.

max_punishment: 2 years imprisonment and fine (7 years if intended offence is theft)
ai_summary: Section 451 IPC punishes house-trespass with intent to commit any imprisonable offence with up to 2 years imprisonment and fine, enhanced to 7 years if the intended offence is theft.

title: House-Trespass After Preparation for Hurt, Assault or Wrongful Restraint
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
bare_act_text: Whoever commits house-trespass, having made preparation for causing hurt to any person or for assaulting any person, or for wrongfully restraining any person, or for putting any person in fear of hurt, or of assault, or of wrongful restraint, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.
meaning: Section 452 IPC prescribes enhanced punishment for house-trespass committed after the offender has made preparations to hurt, assault, wrongfully restrain, or put someone in fear of such harm.
simple_explanation: If a person trespasses into a house having already prepared to hurt someone, assault them, restrain them, or make them fear any of these — for example, by carrying a weapon — they can be punished with up to 7 years imprisonment and a fine. The preparation for violence makes the trespass much more serious.
objective: To impose strict punishment for armed or violence-prepared house-trespass, recognising the grave threat such trespass poses to the safety of occupants.
legal_significance: Addresses the dangerous combination of house-trespass with preparedness for violence. Parallels Section 440 (mischief after preparation for hurt) and Section 455 (lurking house-trespass after preparation for hurt).
essential_elements: The accused commits house-trespass as defined under Section 442 IPC.
Prior to or during the trespass, the accused has made preparation to cause: hurt, assault, wrongful restraint, or fear of any of these, to any person.

mens_rea.description: Criminal intent under Section 441 combined with preparation for causing hurt, assault, or wrongful restraint.

punishment.normal_case: Imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to 7 years, and also liable to fine.

examples.scenario: A enters Z's house armed with a rod, having prepared to beat Z if he resists.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 452 for house-trespass after preparation for causing hurt.

max_punishment: 7 years imprisonment and fine
ai_summary: Section 452 IPC punishes house-trespass committed after preparation for causing hurt, assault, wrongful restraint, or fear thereof, with up to 7 years imprisonment and fine.

title: Punishment for Lurking House-Trespass or House-Breaking
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
bare_act_text: Whoever commits lurking house-trespass or house-breaking, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, and shall also be liable to fine.
meaning: Section 453 IPC prescribes the base punishment for lurking house-trespass (Section 443) and house-breaking (Section 445).
simple_explanation: Anyone who commits lurking house-trespass or house-breaking — either sneaking in while hiding their presence, or breaking into a building — can be punished with up to 2 years imprisonment and a fine. This is the base punishment, with aggravated forms attracting higher sentences.
objective: To prescribe base punishment for lurking house-trespass and house-breaking, recognising their greater seriousness compared to ordinary house-trespass or criminal trespass.
legal_significance: Base punishment for two distinct but equally serious forms of aggravated trespass — lurking house-trespass and house-breaking. Further aggravated forms based on intent, preparation for violence, or nighttime commission attract higher punishments under Sections 454–460.
essential_elements: Commission of lurking house-trespass as defined under Section 443 IPC; OR commission of house-breaking as defined under Section 445 IPC.

mens_rea.description: Criminal intent as required under the applicable definition sections.

punishment.normal_case: Imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to 2 years, and also liable to fine.

examples.scenario: A sneaks into Z's house by hiding from Z's family, but is caught before committing any further offence.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 453 for lurking house-trespass.

examples.scenario: A breaks open a window to enter Z's house.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 453 for house-breaking.

max_punishment: 2 years imprisonment and fine
ai_summary: Section 453 IPC prescribes base punishment of up to 2 years imprisonment and fine for lurking house-trespass or house-breaking.

title: Lurking House-Trespass or House-Breaking in Order to Commit Offence Punishable with Imprisonment
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
bare_act_text: Whoever commits lurking house-trespass or house-breaking, in order to the committing of any offence punishable with imprisonment, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, and shall also be liable to fine; and if the offence intended to be committed is theft, the term of the imprisonment may be extended to ten years.
meaning: Section 454 IPC prescribes enhanced punishment for lurking house-trespass or house-breaking committed with intent to carry out an imprisonable offence, with an even higher punishment if the intended offence is theft.
simple_explanation: If someone commits lurking house-trespass or house-breaking with the intention of committing any offence that is punishable with imprisonment, they face up to 3 years imprisonment and a fine. If the intended offence is theft, the punishment goes up to 10 years — as residential burglary with concealment or forced entry is treated very seriously.
objective: To punish the more dangerous combination of concealed or forcible entry with criminal intent to commit an imprisonable offence, with special deterrence for theft.
legal_significance: An aggravated form of Section 453, layering in the criminal intent to commit a further offence. The 10-year punishment for theft reflects the seriousness of planned residential burglary involving forced entry or concealment.
essential_elements: The accused commits lurking house-trespass (Section 443) or house-breaking (Section 445).
The trespass or breaking is committed in order to commit an offence punishable with imprisonment.
If the intended offence is theft, the maximum punishment is enhanced to 10 years.

mens_rea.description: Specific intent to commit an offence punishable with imprisonment, particularly theft.

punishment.normal_case: Imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to 3 years, and also liable to fine.
punishment.aggravated_case: If the intended offence is theft: imprisonment may extend to 10 years and fine.

examples.scenario: A breaks into Z's house by picking the lock, intending to steal jewellery.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 454 with the enhanced punishment of up to 10 years imprisonment and fine.

max_punishment: 3 years imprisonment and fine (10 years if intended offence is theft)
ai_summary: Section 454 IPC punishes lurking house-trespass or house-breaking with intent to commit any imprisonable offence with up to 3 years imprisonment and fine, enhanced to 10 years if the intended offence is theft.

title: Lurking House-Trespass or House-Breaking After Preparation for Hurt, Assault or Wrongful Restraint
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
bare_act_text: Whoever commits lurking house-trespass, or house-breaking, having made preparation for causing hurt to any person, or for assaulting any person, or for wrongfully restraining any person, or for putting any person in fear of hurt or of assault or of wrongful restraint, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.
meaning: Section 455 IPC prescribes severe punishment for lurking house-trespass or house-breaking committed after the offender has made preparations for violence — causing hurt, assault, wrongful restraint, or fear of these.
simple_explanation: If a person sneaks into a house or breaks into one after having prepared to hurt, assault, or restrain someone — or to make them fear such harm — they face up to 10 years imprisonment and a fine. The combination of concealed or forcible entry with readiness for violence is treated as especially dangerous.
objective: To impose severe punishment for lurking house-trespass or house-breaking that is accompanied by preparation for violence, recognising the extreme danger to occupants.
legal_significance: Combines the aggravating elements of lurking/house-breaking (Sections 443/445) with preparation for violence (as in Section 452 for ordinary house-trespass). Parallels Section 458, which deals with the nighttime variant.
essential_elements: The accused commits lurking house-trespass (Section 443) or house-breaking (Section 445).
Prior to or during the trespass, the accused has made preparation to cause: hurt, assault, wrongful restraint, or fear of any of these.

mens_rea.description: Criminal intent for trespass combined with preparation for causing hurt, assault, wrongful restraint, or fear thereof.

punishment.normal_case: Imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to 10 years, and also liable to fine.

examples.scenario: A breaks into Z's house at dusk, carrying a knife to threaten Z's family into compliance.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 455 for house-breaking after preparation for causing hurt or fear of hurt.

max_punishment: 10 years imprisonment and fine
ai_summary: Section 455 IPC punishes lurking house-trespass or house-breaking committed after preparation for hurt, assault, wrongful restraint, or fear thereof, with up to 10 years imprisonment and fine.

title: Punishment for Lurking House-Trespass or House-Breaking by Night
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
bare_act_text: Whoever commits lurking house-trespass by night, or house-breaking by night, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, and shall also be liable to fine.
meaning: Section 456 IPC prescribes the base punishment for lurking house-trespass by night (Section 444) and house-breaking by night (Section 446).
simple_explanation: Anyone who commits lurking house-trespass or house-breaking at night — after sunset and before sunrise — can be punished with up to 3 years imprisonment and a fine. Nighttime trespass and breaking-in are treated more seriously than their daytime equivalents.
objective: To prescribe enhanced punishment for nighttime variants of lurking house-trespass and house-breaking, given the greater danger they pose to occupants.
legal_significance: Base punishment for nighttime lurking house-trespass and house-breaking. Further aggravated forms — involving criminal intent, preparation for violence, or causing grievous hurt — attract much higher punishments under Sections 457–460.
essential_elements: Commission of lurking house-trespass by night (Section 444) or house-breaking by night (Section 446).

mens_rea.description: Criminal intent as required under the applicable definition sections.

punishment.normal_case: Imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to 3 years, and also liable to fine.

examples.scenario: A breaks into Z's house at night by climbing through a window, but is caught without committing any further offence.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 456 for house-breaking by night.

max_punishment: 3 years imprisonment and fine
ai_summary: Section 456 IPC prescribes base punishment of up to 3 years imprisonment and fine for lurking house-trespass by night or house-breaking by night.

title: Lurking House-Trespass or House-Breaking by Night in Order to Commit Offence Punishable with Imprisonment
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
bare_act_text: Whoever commits lurking house-trespass by night, or house-breaking by night, in order to the committing of any offence punishable with imprisonment, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to five years, and shall also be liable to fine; and, if the offence intended to be committed is theft, the term of the imprisonment may be extended to fourteen years.
meaning: Section 457 IPC prescribes severe punishment for nighttime lurking house-trespass or house-breaking committed with intent to carry out an imprisonable offence, with the highest non-life-imprisonment punishment for theft.
simple_explanation: If someone commits lurking house-trespass or house-breaking at night intending to commit any imprisonable offence, they face up to 5 years imprisonment and a fine. If the intended offence is theft, the punishment shoots up to 14 years — making this one of the most heavily punished property offences in IPC.
objective: To impose the most stringent punishment short of life imprisonment for the most dangerous and premeditated form of residential burglary — nighttime, concealed/forcible entry with criminal intent.
legal_significance: The 14-year punishment for theft under this section is among the highest in the property offences chapter, reflecting how seriously IPC treats planned nighttime residential burglary. Commonly invoked in cases of professional burglars.
essential_elements: The accused commits lurking house-trespass by night (Section 444) or house-breaking by night (Section 446).
The trespass or breaking is committed in order to commit an offence punishable with imprisonment.
If the intended offence is theft, the maximum punishment is enhanced to 14 years.

mens_rea.description: Specific intent to commit an offence punishable with imprisonment, particularly theft, combined with nighttime lurking trespass or house-breaking.

punishment.normal_case: Imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to 5 years, and also liable to fine.
punishment.aggravated_case: If the intended offence is theft: imprisonment may extend to 14 years and fine.

examples.scenario: A breaks into Z's house at midnight by picking the lock, intending to steal cash and jewellery.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 457 with the enhanced punishment of up to 14 years imprisonment and fine.

max_punishment: 5 years imprisonment and fine (14 years if intended offence is theft)
ai_summary: Section 457 IPC punishes nighttime lurking house-trespass or house-breaking with intent to commit any imprisonable offence with up to 5 years and fine — enhanced to 14 years if the intended offence is theft, making it one of the most severe property offence punishments.

title: Lurking House-Trespass or House-Breaking by Night After Preparation for Hurt, Assault, or Wrongful Restraint
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
bare_act_text: Whoever commits lurking house-trespass by night, or house-breaking by night, having made preparation for causing hurt to any person or for assaulting any person, or for wrongfully restraining any person, or for putting any person in fear of hurt, or of assault, or of wrongful restraint, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to fourteen years, and shall also be liable to fine.
meaning: Section 458 IPC prescribes severe punishment for nighttime lurking house-trespass or house-breaking carried out after the offender has made preparations for violence — causing hurt, assault, wrongful restraint, or fear thereof.
simple_explanation: If a person breaks into a house or sneaks in at night having prepared to hurt, assault, restrain, or threaten someone inside, they face up to 14 years imprisonment and a fine. This is one of the most heavily punished trespass offences — the combination of nighttime, concealment/force, and readiness for violence is treated as extremely dangerous.
objective: To impose the harshest non-life punishment for the most dangerous form of trespass — nighttime, concealed/forcible entry combined with preparation for violence against occupants.
legal_significance: The nighttime and violence-prepared equivalent of Section 455. The 14-year maximum mirrors the punishment for nighttime theft-related house-breaking under Section 457, reflecting the comparable severity of both scenarios.
essential_elements: The accused commits lurking house-trespass by night (Section 444) or house-breaking by night (Section 446).
Prior to or during the trespass, the accused has made preparation to cause: hurt, assault, wrongful restraint, or fear of any of these.

mens_rea.description: Criminal intent for nighttime lurking trespass or house-breaking combined with preparation for violence.

punishment.normal_case: Imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to 14 years, and also liable to fine.

examples.scenario: A breaks into Z's house at midnight carrying a weapon, prepared to assault anyone who resists.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 458 for house-breaking by night after preparation for causing hurt.

max_punishment: 14 years imprisonment and fine
ai_summary: Section 458 IPC punishes nighttime lurking house-trespass or house-breaking committed after preparation for hurt, assault, wrongful restraint, or fear thereof, with up to 14 years imprisonment and fine.

title: Grievous Hurt Caused Whilst Committing Lurking House-Trespass or House-Breaking
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
bare_act_text: Whoever, whilst committing lurking house-trespass or house-breaking, causes grievous hurt to any person or attempts to cause death or grievous hurt to any person, shall be punished with imprisonment for life, or imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.
meaning: Section 459 IPC prescribes life imprisonment or up to 10 years imprisonment for causing grievous hurt — or attempting to cause death or grievous hurt — to any person while committing lurking house-trespass or house-breaking.
simple_explanation: If a person commits lurking house-trespass or house-breaking and during the commission of this offence causes grievous hurt to someone — or even attempts to cause death or grievous hurt — they can be punished with life imprisonment, or up to 10 years imprisonment, along with a fine.
objective: To impose life imprisonment for the most violent form of house-trespass or house-breaking — where occupants are seriously harmed or attempts are made to kill or grievously hurt them.
legal_significance: Represents the apex of the lurking house-trespass / house-breaking punishment scale (excluding Section 460). The provision applies even to attempts to cause grievous hurt or death, not just completed acts of grievous hurt.
essential_elements: The accused commits lurking house-trespass (Section 443) or house-breaking (Section 445).
Whilst committing such trespass or breaking, the accused causes grievous hurt to any person; OR attempts to cause death or grievous hurt to any person.

mens_rea.description: Criminal intent for trespass/breaking combined with intent to cause grievous hurt or death, or the actual causation of grievous hurt.

punishment.normal_case: Imprisonment for life, or imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to 10 years, and also liable to fine.

examples.scenario: A breaks into Z's house and, when confronted by Z, stabs Z causing grievous injuries.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 459 for causing grievous hurt while committing house-breaking.

examples.scenario: A sneaks into Z's house and attempts to strangle Z but is overpowered before succeeding.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 459 for attempting to cause death while committing lurking house-trespass.

max_punishment: Imprisonment for life, or 10 years imprisonment and fine
ai_summary: Section 459 IPC punishes causing grievous hurt or attempting to cause death or grievous hurt while committing lurking house-trespass or house-breaking, with life imprisonment or up to 10 years imprisonment and fine.

title: All Persons Jointly Concerned in Lurking House-Trespass or House-Breaking by Night Punishable Where Death or Grievous Hurt Caused by One of Them
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
bare_act_text: If, at the time of the committing of lurking house-trespass by night or house-breaking by night, any person guilty of such offence shall voluntarily cause or attempt to cause death or grievous hurt to any person, every person jointly concerned in committing such lurking house-trespass by night or house-breaking by night, shall be punished with imprisonment for life, or with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.
meaning: Section 460 IPC imposes collective liability — life imprisonment or up to 10 years — on all persons jointly involved in nighttime lurking house-trespass or house-breaking, if any one of them voluntarily causes or attempts to cause death or grievous hurt.
simple_explanation: If a group commits lurking house-trespass or house-breaking at night, and even just one member of the group kills or seriously injures someone (or attempts to), then every single member of the group faces life imprisonment or up to 10 years imprisonment and a fine — even if the others did not personally commit the violence. This is a collective punishment provision.
objective: To hold all participants in a nighttime house-breaking gang equally responsible for violence committed by any one of them, deterring group crimes involving trespass and breaking-in.
legal_significance: A significant collective liability provision, similar in principle to Section 149 (unlawful assembly) and Section 34 (common intention). All participants are punished equally for the violence of one, regardless of their individual role in causing the hurt or death.
essential_elements: Multiple persons are jointly concerned in committing lurking house-trespass by night (Section 444) or house-breaking by night (Section 446).
At the time of the offence, any one of them voluntarily causes or attempts to cause death or grievous hurt to any person.
All persons jointly concerned are liable, regardless of whether they personally caused the hurt or death.

mens_rea.description: Joint participation in nighttime lurking house-trespass or house-breaking; individual knowledge of the violent acts of co-participants is not required for collective liability.

punishment.normal_case: Imprisonment for life, or imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to 10 years, and also liable to fine.

examples.scenario: A, B, and C jointly break into Z's house at night. During the robbery, A kills Z while B and C were in another room. All three are arrested.
examples.result: All three — A, B, and C — are liable under Section 460, even though only A caused the death.

max_punishment: Imprisonment for life, or 10 years imprisonment and fine
ai_summary: Section 460 IPC imposes collective liability for life imprisonment or up to 10 years imprisonment and fine on all persons jointly involved in nighttime lurking house-trespass or house-breaking, if any one of them causes or attempts to cause death or grievous hurt.

title: Dishonestly Breaking Open Receptacle Containing Property
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
bare_act_text: Whoever dishonestly or with intent to commit mischief, breaks open or unfastens any closed receptacle which contains or which he believes to contain property, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.
meaning: Section 461 IPC punishes any person who dishonestly or with mischievous intent breaks open or unfastens a closed container — such as a box, safe, or locker — that contains or is believed to contain property.
simple_explanation: If a person dishonestly breaks open or unlocks any closed container — like a safe, box, trunk, or locker — that contains or is believed to contain property, they can be punished with up to 2 years imprisonment, or a fine, or both. The offence applies even if the container turns out to be empty, as long as the person believed it contained property.
objective: To punish dishonest or mischievous interference with closed receptacles containing property, extending the principle of property protection to containers and secure storage.
legal_significance: Extends the protection of property to secured containers, short of full house-breaking. The section covers both actual property and believed property — the offence is complete if the person believed the receptacle contained property, regardless of its actual contents.
essential_elements: The accused breaks open or unfastens any closed receptacle.
The receptacle contains or the accused believes it to contain property.
The accused acts dishonestly or with intent to commit mischief.

mens_rea.description: Dishonest intention or intent to commit mischief in breaking open or unfastening the receptacle.

punishment.normal_case: Imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to 2 years, or fine, or both.

examples.scenario: A dishonestly breaks open Z's locked box believing it contains cash.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 461 even if the box turns out to be empty, as A believed it contained property.

max_punishment: 2 years imprisonment, or fine, or both
ai_summary: Section 461 IPC punishes dishonestly or with mischievous intent breaking open or unfastening any closed receptacle containing or believed to contain property, with up to 2 years imprisonment or fine or both.

title: Punishment for Same Offence When Committed by Person Entrusted with Custody
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
bare_act_text: Whoever, being entrusted with any closed receptacle which contains or which he believes to contain property, without having authority to open the same, dishonestly, or with intent to commit mischief, breaks open or unfastens that receptacle, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both.
meaning: Section 462 IPC prescribes enhanced punishment for the offence under Section 461 when committed by a person who was specifically entrusted with the closed receptacle — making it a breach of trust.
simple_explanation: If a person who has been entrusted with a closed box, safe, or container — without authority to open it — dishonestly breaks it open or unfastens it, they face a higher punishment of up to 3 years imprisonment, or a fine, or both. The breach of trust element makes this more serious than Section 461.
objective: To impose higher punishment when the offender was in a position of trust over the receptacle, recognising the additional element of breach of trust in such cases.
legal_significance: An aggravated form of Section 461, paralleling the relationship between Section 405 (criminal breach of trust) and its punishment under Section 406. The entrustment creates a higher duty of honesty that is violated by the offence.
essential_elements: The accused is entrusted with a closed receptacle containing or believed to contain property.
The accused does not have authority to open the receptacle.
The accused dishonestly or with intent to commit mischief breaks open or unfastens the receptacle.

mens_rea.description: Dishonest intention or intent to commit mischief in breaking open a receptacle entrusted to the accused without authority to open it.

punishment.normal_case: Imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to 3 years, or fine, or both.

examples.scenario: A, a bank employee, is handed a sealed cash box for safekeeping. Without authority to open it, A dishonestly breaks open the box and takes cash.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 462 for the aggravated offence of breaking open a receptacle entrusted to them.

max_punishment: 3 years imprisonment, or fine, or both
ai_summary: Section 462 IPC prescribes enhanced punishment of up to 3 years imprisonment or fine or both for dishonestly breaking open a closed receptacle entrusted to the accused without authority to open it — an aggravated form of Section 461 involving breach of trust.

title: Forgery
chapter: Chapter XVIII - Of Offences Relating to Documents and to Property Marks
section_type: Definition Section / Offence Relating to Documents
bare_act_text: Whoever makes any false document or false electronic record or part of a document or electronic record, with intent to cause damage or injury, to the public or to any person, or to support any claim or title, or to cause any person to part with property, or to enter into any express or implied contract, or with intent to commit fraud or that fraud may be committed, commits forgery.
meaning: Section 463 IPC defines forgery as the making of a false document or false electronic record — or part thereof — with fraudulent intent: to cause damage or injury, to support a false claim or title, to induce delivery of property, to induce entry into a contract, or to commit or facilitate fraud.
simple_explanation: Forgery means creating a false document or electronic record (or part of one) with any of the following criminal intentions: to harm someone, to back up a false claim, to make someone hand over property, to trick someone into a contract, or to commit fraud. The false document or record as defined under Section 464 must be made with one of these specific intents.
objective: To define forgery comprehensively, covering all forms of creation of false documents or electronic records with fraudulent intent, including electronic records in the digital age.
legal_significance: Foundational definition for a cluster of forgery-related offences punished under Sections 465–477A. The 2000 amendment extended the definition to include electronic records, making it applicable to digital fraud and cyber crimes. What constitutes a 'false document' is defined in Section 464.
essential_elements: The accused makes a false document or false electronic record, or part thereof, as defined under Section 464 IPC.

The making is with one of the following intents: (a) to cause damage or injury to the public or any person, (b) to support any claim or title, (c) to cause any person to part with property, (d) to cause any person to enter into any express or implied contract, or (e) to commit fraud or that fraud may be committed.

mens_rea.description: Specific fraudulent intent — one of the five intents specified in the section — at the time of making the false document or electronic record.

examples.scenario: A adds a zero to a cheque of Rs. 10,000 making it Rs. 1,00,000, intending to deceive the bank.
examples.result: A commits forgery under Section 463.

examples.scenario: A affixes Z's seal on a property document without Z's authority, intending to sell the property to B.
examples.result: A commits forgery under Section 463.

examples.scenario: A fills in a blank cheque signed by B with an unauthorised amount, intending to defraud B.
examples.result: A commits forgery under Section 463.

max_punishment: Not applicable (definition section; punishment under Section 465)
ai_summary: Section 463 IPC defines forgery as the making of a false document or electronic record with fraudulent intent — to cause damage, support a false claim, induce delivery of property, induce a contract, or commit fraud — forming the basis for punishment under Sections 465 onwards.

title: Making a False Document
chapter: Chapter XVIII - Of Offences Relating to Documents and to Property Marks
bare_act_text: A person is said to make a false document or false electronic record— First.—Who dishonestly or fraudulently makes, signs, seals or executes a document or part of a document, or makes or transmits any electronic record or part of any electronic record, or affixes any electronic signature on any electronic record, or makes any mark denoting the execution of a document or the authenticity of the electronic signature, with the intention of causing it to be believed that such document or part of document, electronic record or electronic signature was made, signed, sealed, executed, transmitted or affixed by or by the authority of a person by whom or by whose authority he knows that it was not made, signed, sealed, executed, or affixed; or Secondly.—Who without lawful authority, dishonestly or fraudulently, by cancellation or otherwise, alters a document or an electronic record in any material part thereof, after it has been made, executed or affixed with electronic signature either by himself or by any other person, whether such person be living or dead at the time of such alteration; or Thirdly.—Who dishonestly or fraudulently causes any person to sign, seal, execute or alter a document or an electronic record or to affix his electronic signature on any electronic record knowing that such person by reason of unsoundness of mind or intoxication cannot, or that by reason of deception practised upon him, he does not know the contents of the document or electronic record or the nature of the alteration.
meaning: Section 464 IPC defines what constitutes 'making a false document or false electronic record' for the purpose of the offence of forgery under Section 463, covering three modes: (1) creating a document falsely attributed to another, (2) materially altering an existing genuine document without authority, and (3) causing an incapacitated or deceived person to sign or alter a document.
simple_explanation: A false document is made in three ways under IPC: (1) making or signing a document pretending it was made by someone else who didn't authorise it; (2) altering an existing genuine document in a material way without authority — even after the original maker has died; and (3) tricking or taking advantage of a mentally incapacitated, intoxicated, or deceived person to get them to sign or alter a document without knowing what they are signing. Signing your own name can also be forgery if the intent is to make others believe it was signed by a different person of the same name.
objective: To define with precision what constitutes a 'false document' for the purposes of forgery, covering all three principal modes of documentary fraud in both physical and electronic form.
legal_significance: The definitional backbone of the forgery chapter. Extends to electronic records and electronic signatures in light of the Information Technology Act, 2000. The three modes collectively cover virtually all forms of documentary fraud. Explanations make clear that signing one's own name or creating documents in a fictitious or deceased person's name can also constitute forgery.
essential_elements: First mode: Dishonestly or fraudulently making, signing, sealing, executing, or transmitting a document/electronic record as if made by or with the authority of a person who did not make or authorise it.
Second mode: Without lawful authority, dishonestly or fraudulently altering a document or electronic record in any material part after its execution, whether the original maker is living or dead.

Third mode: Dishonestly or fraudulently causing a person — who due to unsoundness of mind, intoxication, or deception does not know the contents or nature of the alteration — to sign, seal, execute, or alter a document.

mens_rea.description: Dishonest or fraudulent intention in each of the three modes of making a false document.

examples.scenario: A signs a bill of exchange in B's name without B's authority, intending to use it as a genuine instrument.
examples.result: A makes a false document under the First mode of Section 464.

examples.scenario: A erases a beneficiary's name from Z's will after Z's death, intending to misappropriate their share.
examples.result: A makes a false document under the Second mode of Section 464.

examples.scenario: A deceives Z — by representing a blank paper as a rent receipt — into signing it, then writes a property transfer on it.
examples.result: A makes a false document under the Third mode of Section 464.

examples.scenario: A signs his own name on a bill of exchange intending others to believe it was drawn by a different person of the same name.
examples.result: A makes a false document — a person's signature of their own name can amount to forgery per Explanation 1.

max_punishment: Not applicable (definition section; punishment under Section 465)
ai_summary: Section 464 IPC defines 'making a false document' for the purposes of forgery through three modes — false attribution, material alteration without authority, and procuring signature by deception or incapacity — covering both physical documents and electronic records.

title: Punishment for Forgery
chapter: Chapter XVIII - Of Offences Relating to Documents and to Property Marks
bare_act_text: Whoever commits forgery shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.
meaning: Section 465 IPC prescribes the base punishment for the offence of forgery as defined under Sections 463 and 464 IPC.
simple_explanation: Any person who commits forgery (as defined in Sections 463–464) can be punished with up to 2 years imprisonment, or a fine, or both. This is the base punishment; more serious forms of forgery — such as forging court records, valuable securities, or using forged documents — attract much higher punishments under Sections 466–477A.
objective: To prescribe the base punishment for the general offence of forgery, with aggravated forms of forgery attracting higher punishments under subsequent sections.
legal_significance: Base punishment for forgery. Aggravated forms — forging court records (Section 466), forging wills or valuable securities (Section 467), forging for fraud (Section 468), forging for reputation damage (Section 469), and using forged documents (Section 471) — attract significantly higher punishments.
essential_elements: Commission of forgery as defined under Sections 463 and 464 IPC.

mens_rea.description: Fraudulent intent as required under Section 463 IPC.

punishment.normal_case: Imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to 2 years, or fine, or both.

examples.scenario: A forges B's signature on a letter to gain employment with Z.
examples.result: A is liable for forgery under Section 463/464, punishable under Section 465.

max_punishment: 2 years imprisonment, or fine, or both
ai_summary: Section 465 IPC prescribes base punishment of up to 2 years imprisonment or fine or both for forgery as defined under Sections 463 and 464 IPC.

title: Forgery of Record of Court or of Public Register, etc.
chapter: Chapter XVIII - Of Offences Relating to Documents and to Property Marks
bare_act_text: Whoever forges a document or an electronic record, purporting to be a record or proceeding of or in a Court of Justice, or a register of birth, baptism, marriage or burial, or a register kept by a public servant as such, or a certificate or document purporting to be made by a public servant in his official capacity, or an authority to institute or defend a suit, or to take any proceedings therein, or to confess judgment, or a power of attorney, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine. Explanation.—For the purposes of this section, 'register' includes any list, data or record of any entries maintained in the electronic form as defined in clause (r) of sub-section (1) of section 2 of the Information Technology Act, 2000.
meaning: Section 466 IPC prescribes enhanced punishment for forging specific high-importance documents — court records, public registers (birth, death, marriage), official certificates, powers of attorney, and electronic records of such documents.
simple_explanation: Forging court records, birth/death/marriage certificates, government registers, official certificates by public servants, or powers of attorney is treated much more seriously than ordinary forgery — punishable with up to 7 years imprisonment and a fine. These documents carry high public trust and their forgery can have serious legal, social, and administrative consequences.
objective: To impose enhanced punishment for forgery of documents that carry special public importance and trust — including court records, vital registers, and official certificates — whose forgery undermines the administration of justice and public records.
legal_significance: An aggravated form of Section 465, recognising the heightened harm caused by forging official and public documents. The Explanation extends 'register' to include electronic records, keeping the provision relevant in the digital era.
essential_elements: The accused commits forgery as defined under Sections 463 and 464 IPC.

The forged document purports to be: (a) a court record or proceeding, (b) a register of births, baptisms, marriages, or burials, (c) a register kept by a public servant, (d) a certificate or document made by a public servant in official capacity, (e) an authority to institute/defend a suit or take proceedings, or confess judgment, or (f) a power of attorney.

mens_rea.description: Fraudulent intent as required under Section 463 IPC.

punishment.normal_case: Imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to 7 years, and also liable to fine.

examples.scenario: A forges a court order purporting to be from a High Court to obtain release of a person.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 466 for forging a court record.

examples.scenario: A creates a false birth certificate purporting to be an official register entry.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 466 for forging a public register.

examples.scenario: A forges a power of attorney in B's name to sell B's property.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 466 for forging a power of attorney.

max_punishment: 7 years imprisonment and fine
ai_summary: Section 466 IPC punishes forgery of court records, public registers, official certificates, and powers of attorney with up to 7 years imprisonment and fine, reflecting the heightened harm from forging documents of public importance.

title: Forgery of Valuable Security, Will, etc.
chapter: Chapter XVIII - Of Offences Relating to Documents and Property Marks
bare_act_text: Whoever forges a document which purports to be a valuable security or a will, or an authority to adopt a son, or which purports to give authority to any person to make or transfer any valuable security, or to receive the principal, interest or dividends thereon, or to receive or deliver any money, movable property, or valuable security, or any document purporting to be an acquittance or receipt acknowledging the payment of money, or an acquittance or receipt for the delivery of any movable property or valuable security, shall be punished with imprisonment for life, or with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.
meaning: Section 467 IPC punishes forgery of the most serious class of documents — valuable securities, wills, adoption authorities, and receipts or acquittances for money or property.
simple_explanation: If someone forges a will, a fixed deposit certificate, a property transfer authority, or a receipt for money or goods, they can be sentenced to life imprisonment or up to 10 years in jail and fined. This section covers the most financially and legally significant documents.
objective: To protect the integrity of high-value legal and financial documents that directly affect property rights, inheritance, and financial transactions.
legal_significance: One of the most serious forgery provisions in the IPC. Life imprisonment is prescribed reflecting the grave financial and legal harm caused by forging wills, securities, and financial instruments. Forms the basis for enhanced punishment under Sections 472, 474, and 475.
essential_elements: Committing forgery of a document.
The document purports to be a valuable security, will, adoption authority, or authority to transfer/receive money or property.
Or the document purports to be an acquittance or receipt for money or movable property or valuable security.

mens_rea.description: Intent inherent in the act of forgery — dishonest or fraudulent making of a false document.

normal_case.imprisonment: Life imprisonment or imprisonment of either description up to 10 years

examples.scenario: A forges B's signature on a will to inherit B's property.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 467 IPC with up to life imprisonment.

examples.scenario: A creates a fake fixed deposit receipt to fraudulently encash it at a bank.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 467 IPC.

max_punishment: Life Imprisonment
ai_summary: Section 467 IPC prescribes life imprisonment for forgery of the most serious documents — wills, valuable securities, adoption authorities, and financial receipts. It is the highest-tier forgery provision and anchors several related sections.

title: Forgery for Purpose of Cheating
chapter: Chapter XVIII - Of Offences Relating to Documents and Property Marks
bare_act_text: Whoever commits forgery, intending that the document or electronic record forged shall be used for the purpose of cheating, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.
meaning: Section 468 IPC punishes forgery committed with the specific intention that the forged document or electronic record will be used to cheat someone.
simple_explanation: If someone forges a document or electronic record — like a fake degree certificate, a forged cheque, or a fabricated contract — intending it to be used to deceive and cheat another person, they can be jailed for up to 7 years and fined.
objective: To punish forgery that is specifically aimed at facilitating cheating, recognising the combined harm of both forgery and fraud.
legal_significance: Covers electronic records in addition to physical documents, making it applicable to digital fraud. Frequently charged alongside Section 420 (cheating) in cases involving forged documents used to defraud victims.
essential_elements: Committing forgery of a document or electronic record.
Intention that the forged document or electronic record shall be used for the purpose of cheating.

mens_rea.description: Specific intention that the forged document or electronic record will be used for cheating.

examples.scenario: A forges a fake degree certificate intending to use it to fraudulently obtain a job.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 468 IPC.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years
ai_summary: Section 468 IPC punishes forgery committed with cheating as its intended purpose. It applies to both physical documents and electronic records and is commonly charged with Section 420 in fraud cases.

title: Forgery for Purpose of Harming Reputation
chapter: Chapter XVIII - Of Offences Relating to Documents and Property Marks
bare_act_text: Whoever commits forgery, intending that the document or electronic record forged shall harm the reputation of any party, or knowing that it is likely to be used for that purpose, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, and shall also be liable to fine.
meaning: Section 469 IPC punishes forgery committed with the intent to harm the reputation of any person, or with knowledge that the forged document or electronic record is likely to be used for that purpose.
simple_explanation: If someone forges a document or electronic record — like a fake letter or fabricated social media post — to damage another person's reputation, they can be jailed for up to 3 years and fined.
objective: To protect individuals from reputational harm caused by forged documents or electronic records falsely attributed to them or about them.
legal_significance: Extends forgery law to cover reputational harm — not just financial fraud. Covers electronic records, making it applicable to fabricated emails, messages, and social media content used to defame someone.
essential_elements: Committing forgery of a document or electronic record.
Intention that it shall harm the reputation of any party, or knowledge that it is likely to be used for that purpose.

mens_rea.description: Intent to harm reputation or knowledge that the forged document is likely to be used for that purpose.

examples.scenario: A forges a letter purportedly written by B confessing to a crime, and circulates it to harm B's reputation.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 469 IPC.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 years
ai_summary: Section 469 IPC punishes forgery aimed at damaging someone's reputation. It covers both physical documents and electronic records — making it relevant to fabricated digital content used for defamation.

title: Forged Document
chapter: Chapter XVIII - Of Offences Relating to Documents and Property Marks
bare_act_text: A false document or electronic record made wholly or in part by forgery is designated a forged document or electronic record.
meaning: Section 470 IPC defines what constitutes a forged document or electronic record — any false document or electronic record made wholly or partially by forgery.
simple_explanation: A forged document is any false document or electronic record that has been created — entirely or even partially — through forgery. This definition applies to all forgery provisions in the IPC.
objective: To provide a clear statutory definition of a forged document or electronic record for use across all forgery-related provisions.
legal_significance: A definitional provision rather than an offence-creating one. Clarifies that even partial forgery makes the entire document a forged document. Includes electronic records, keeping the definition current with digital realities.
essential_elements: A document or electronic record that is false.
