Made wholly or in part by forgery.
Designated as a forged document or electronic record.

mens_rea.description: This is a definitional section — no independent mens rea requirement. Mens rea is addressed in the operative forgery sections.

punishment.note: This is a definitional section. No punishment prescribed here — see Sections 465, 467, 468, 469 for punishments.

examples.scenario: A document with genuine parts but a forged signature.
examples.result: The entire document is a forged document within the meaning of Section 470 IPC.

max_punishment: N/A (definitional section)
ai_summary: Section 470 IPC defines a forged document or electronic record as any false document made wholly or partly by forgery. Even partial forgery taints the entire document. This definition underpins all IPC forgery offences.

title: Using as Genuine a Forged Document or Electronic Record
chapter: Chapter XVIII - Of Offences Relating to Documents and Property Marks
bare_act_text: Whoever fraudulently or dishonestly uses as genuine any document or electronic record which he knows or has reason to believe to be a forged document or electronic record, shall be punished in the same manner as if he had forged such document or electronic record.
meaning: Section 471 IPC punishes a person who knowingly uses a forged document or electronic record as if it were genuine, with the same punishment as if they had forged it themselves.
simple_explanation: If someone knows or has reason to believe a document or electronic record is forged but still uses it as genuine — like submitting a fake certificate to a court or employer — they will be punished the same as the person who forged it.
objective: To deter the use of forged documents by making users equally liable as forgers, closing the loophole where a person who did not forge but knowingly used a forged document could escape liability.
legal_significance: Frequently charged alongside the forgery sections. Applies to electronic records — covering use of forged digital documents, emails, and electronic certificates. The equal-punishment rule is a strong deterrent.
essential_elements: Using a document or electronic record as genuine.
The document or electronic record is forged.
The person knows or has reason to believe it to be forged.
The use is fraudulent or dishonest.

mens_rea.description: Knowledge or reason to believe the document is forged, combined with fraudulent or dishonest intent in using it.

normal_case.imprisonment: Same as the punishment for forging that document — up to life imprisonment for Section 467 documents, up to 7 years for Section 468 documents, etc.

examples.scenario: A uses a forged degree certificate knowing it is fake to obtain employment.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 471 IPC with the same punishment as forging the certificate under Section 468.

max_punishment: Life Imprisonment (if document falls under Section 467)
ai_summary: Section 471 IPC makes using a known forged document as genuine equally punishable as forging it. Covers electronic records and is routinely charged with forgery sections in cases involving fraudulent use of fake documents.

title: Making or Possessing Counterfeit Seal with Intent to Commit Forgery Punishable under Section 467
chapter: Chapter XVIII - Of Offences Relating to Documents and Property Marks
bare_act_text: Whoever makes or counterfeits any seal, plate or other instrument for making an impression, intending that the same shall be used for the purpose of committing any forgery which would be punishable under section 467 of this Code, or, with such intent, has in his possession any such seal, plate or other instrument, knowing the same to be counterfeit, shall be punished with imprisonment for life, or with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.
meaning: Section 472 IPC punishes a person who makes or possesses counterfeit seals, plates, or instruments intended for committing forgery of documents falling under Section 467 — such as wills, valuable securities, and financial instruments.
simple_explanation: If someone makes or keeps a fake stamp, seal, or printing plate — intending to use it to forge important documents like wills or bank securities — they can be sentenced to life imprisonment or up to 7 years and fined, even if they have not yet committed the forgery.
objective: To criminalise preparatory acts for the most serious category of forgery, preventing such crimes before they occur.
legal_significance: Targets preparatory possession — making even the possession of counterfeit seals punishable if intended for Section 467 forgery. Life imprisonment reflects the gravity of Section 467 documents. Punishes the act of preparation, not just the completed forgery.
essential_elements: Making or counterfeiting a seal, plate, or other impression-making instrument.
Or possessing such a counterfeit instrument knowing it to be counterfeit.
Intent that it shall be used to commit forgery punishable under Section 467.

mens_rea.description: Intent to use the counterfeit instrument for Section 467 forgery, or knowledge that the possessed instrument is counterfeit combined with that intent.

normal_case.imprisonment: Life imprisonment or imprisonment of either description up to 7 years

examples.scenario: A makes a counterfeit bank seal intending to use it to forge fixed deposit receipts.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 472 IPC even before any forgery is committed.

max_punishment: Life Imprisonment
ai_summary: Section 472 IPC punishes making or possessing counterfeit seals intended for Section 467 forgery. Life imprisonment is prescribed, reflecting the severity of the targeted documents. It criminalises preparation before the forgery is even committed.

title: Making or Possessing Counterfeit Seal with Intent to Commit Forgery Punishable Otherwise
chapter: Chapter XVIII - Of Offences Relating to Documents and Property Marks
bare_act_text: Whoever makes or counterfeits any seal, plate or other instrument for making an impression, intending that the same shall be used for the purpose of committing any forgery which would be punishable under any section of this Chapter other than section 467, or, with such intent, has in his possession any such seal, plate or other instrument, knowing the same to be counterfeit, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.
meaning: Section 473 IPC punishes making or possessing counterfeit seals or instruments intended for forgery of documents other than those covered by Section 467 — such as documents for cheating or reputational harm.
simple_explanation: If someone makes or possesses a fake stamp or seal intending to forge documents — but not the most serious ones like wills or bank securities — they can be jailed for up to 7 years and fined. This section covers all other forgery not falling under Section 467.
objective: To criminalise preparatory possession of counterfeit seals for all categories of forgery other than the most serious Section 467 category.
legal_significance: The counterpart to Section 472 for less serious forgeries. Maximum 7 years (no life imprisonment) reflects the lower gravity of non-Section 467 documents. Together Sections 472 and 473 cover all counterfeit seal offences comprehensively.
essential_elements: Making or counterfeiting a seal, plate, or other impression-making instrument.
Or possessing such a counterfeit instrument knowing it to be counterfeit.
Intent that it shall be used to commit forgery under any Chapter XVIII section other than Section 467.

mens_rea.description: Intent to use the counterfeit instrument for forgery under any chapter section except Section 467, or knowledge of counterfeiting combined with that intent.

examples.scenario: A makes a fake university seal intending to forge degree certificates for cheating purposes.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 473 IPC.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years
ai_summary: Section 473 IPC is the companion to Section 472 for non-Section 467 forgeries. It punishes making or possessing counterfeit seals intended for any other category of forgery with up to 7 years imprisonment.

title: Having Possession of Document Described in Section 466 or 467, Knowing It to Be Forged and Intending to Use It as Genuine
chapter: Chapter XVIII - Of Offences Relating to Documents and Property Marks
bare_act_text: Whoever has in his possession any document or electronic record, knowing the same to be forged and intending that the same shall fraudulently or dishonestly be used as genuine, shall, if the document or electronic record is one of the description mentioned in section 466 of this Code, be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine; and if the document is one of the description mentioned in section 467, shall be punished with imprisonment for life, or with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.
meaning: Section 474 IPC punishes possession of a known forged document or electronic record with the intention of using it as genuine — with higher punishment for Section 467 documents.
simple_explanation: If someone has a forged document in their possession knowing it is forged and planning to use it as genuine, they will be punished. If the forged document is an ordinary official document (Section 466), they face up to 7 years. If it is a will, valuable security, or financial instrument (Section 467), they face life imprisonment.
objective: To punish possession of forged documents intended for fraudulent use — targeting persons who possess but have not yet used forged documents.
legal_significance: Criminalises mere possession with intent — the fraud need not have been carried out. Tiered punishment based on document type mirrors the forgery punishment scale, maintaining proportionality.
essential_elements: Possession of a document or electronic record.
Knowledge that it is forged.
Intention that it shall be fraudulently or dishonestly used as genuine.
Punishment tier depends on whether the document falls under Section 466 or Section 467.

mens_rea.description: Knowledge that the document is forged combined with intent to use it fraudulently or dishonestly as genuine.

tiered.condition: Document falls under Section 467 (valuable security, will, etc.)
tiered.imprisonment: Life imprisonment or up to 7 years (either description)

examples.scenario: A is found in possession of a forged will knowing it is fake, intending to submit it in court to claim inheritance.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 474 IPC with up to life imprisonment.

max_punishment: Life Imprisonment (for Section 467 documents)
ai_summary: Section 474 IPC punishes possession of known forged documents intended for fraudulent use. Life imprisonment applies for Section 467 documents (wills, securities), up to 7 years for Section 466 documents.

title: Counterfeiting Device or Mark Used for Authenticating Documents Described in Section 467, or Possessing Counterfeit Marked Material
chapter: Chapter XVIII - Of Offences Relating to Documents and Property Marks
bare_act_text: Whoever counterfeits upon, or in the substance of, any material, any device or mark used for the purpose of authenticating any document described in section 467 of this Code, intending that such device or mark shall be used for the purpose of giving the appearance of authenticity to any document then forged or thereafter to be forged on such material, or who, with such intent, has in his possession any material upon or in the substance of which any such device or mark has been counterfeited, shall be punished with imprisonment for life, or with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.
meaning: Section 475 IPC punishes counterfeiting authentication marks or devices used on Section 467 documents — such as official stamps or watermarks on wills, securities, or financial instruments — or possessing such counterfeited material.
simple_explanation: If someone fakes the authentication marks, watermarks, or official stamps that are used to verify important documents like wills or bank securities — or even just possesses material with such fake marks — they can be sentenced to life imprisonment or up to 7 years and fined.
objective: To protect authentication mechanisms of the most critical legal and financial documents from counterfeiting.
legal_significance: Targets counterfeiting of authentication devices rather than the documents themselves — a preparatory step before forging Section 467 documents. Life imprisonment reflects the severity of tampering with authentication systems of high-value documents.
essential_elements: Counterfeiting a device or mark on or in any material.
The device or mark is used for authenticating Section 467 documents.
Intent that the counterfeit mark shall give appearance of authenticity to a forged or to-be-forged document.
Or possessing material bearing such counterfeited device or mark with the same intent.

mens_rea.description: Intent that the counterfeited device or mark shall be used to give appearance of authenticity to forged Section 467 documents.

normal_case.imprisonment: Life imprisonment or imprisonment of either description up to 7 years

examples.scenario: A counterfeits the Reserve Bank of India watermark on paper intending to use it to forge government securities.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 475 IPC with up to life imprisonment.

max_punishment: Life Imprisonment
ai_summary: Section 475 IPC punishes counterfeiting of authentication marks for Section 467 documents and possession of such counterfeited material. Life imprisonment is prescribed — matching Section 467's severity — as authentication counterfeiting directly enables the most serious forgeries.

title: Counterfeiting Device or Mark Used for Authenticating Documents Other Than Those Described in Section 467, or Possessing Counterfeit Marked Material
chapter: Chapter XVIII - Of Offences Relating to Documents and Property Marks
bare_act_text: Whoever counterfeits upon, or in the substance of, any material, any device or mark used for the purpose of authenticating any document or electronic record other than the documents described in section 467 of this Code, intending that such device or mark shall be used for the purpose of giving the appearance of authenticity to any document then forged or thereafter to be forged on such material, or who with such intent, has in his possession any material upon or in the substance of which any such device or mark has been counterfeited, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.
meaning: Section 476 IPC punishes counterfeiting of authentication marks used on documents or electronic records other than Section 467 documents, or possessing material bearing such counterfeit marks.
simple_explanation: If someone fakes the authentication stamps or marks used on ordinary official documents — not the most serious ones like wills or securities — they can be jailed for up to 7 years and fined. This section is the counterpart of Section 475 for less serious documents.
objective: To protect authentication mechanisms of all legal documents and electronic records outside the Section 467 category from counterfeiting.
legal_significance: Companion to Section 475 covering non-Section 467 documents. Expressly includes electronic records — covering digital authentication marks, e-signatures, and digital certificates. Maximum 7 years with no life imprisonment distinguishes it from Section 475.
essential_elements: Counterfeiting a device or mark on or in any material.
The device or mark authenticates documents or electronic records other than Section 467 documents.
Intent that the counterfeit mark shall give appearance of authenticity to forged documents.
Or possessing material bearing such counterfeited mark with the same intent.

mens_rea.description: Intent that the counterfeited device or mark shall be used to give appearance of authenticity to forged non-Section 467 documents or electronic records.

examples.scenario: A counterfeits a university's official seal on paper intending to forge degree certificates.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 476 IPC.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years
ai_summary: Section 476 IPC is the companion to Section 475 for non-Section 467 documents and electronic records. It punishes counterfeiting of authentication marks for ordinary official documents with up to 7 years imprisonment.

title: Fraudulent Cancellation, Destruction, etc., of Will, Authority to Adopt, or Valuable Security
chapter: Chapter XVIII - Of Offences Relating to Documents and Property Marks
bare_act_text: Whoever fraudulently or dishonestly, or with intent to cause damage or injury to the public or to any person, cancels, destroys or defaces, or attempts to cancel, destroy or deface, or secretes or attempts to secrete any document which is or purports to be a will, or an authority to adopt a son, or any valuable security, or commits mischief in respect of such document, shall be punished with imprisonment for life, or with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.
meaning: Section 477 IPC punishes fraudulent or dishonest destruction, cancellation, defacement, or concealment of wills, adoption authorities, or valuable securities.
simple_explanation: If someone fraudulently destroys, cancels, hides, or damages a will, an adoption authority document, or a valuable security — like a fixed deposit or property deed — they can be sentenced to life imprisonment or up to 7 years in jail and fined.
objective: To protect the integrity and preservation of the most critical legal and financial documents from fraudulent destruction or concealment.
legal_significance: Life imprisonment is prescribed — matching Section 467 — reflecting that destruction of such documents can cause the same harm as forging them. Covers attempts as well as completed acts, and extends to mischief in respect of such documents.
essential_elements: Cancelling, destroying, defacing, secreting, or committing mischief in respect of a document.
The document is or purports to be a will, authority to adopt a son, or a valuable security.
The act is done fraudulently, dishonestly, or with intent to cause damage or injury to the public or any person.
Attempts to do any of the above are equally punishable.

mens_rea.description: Fraudulent or dishonest intent, or intent to cause damage or injury to the public or any person.

normal_case.imprisonment: Life imprisonment or imprisonment of either description up to 7 years

examples.scenario: A destroys B's original will after B's death to prevent B's intended beneficiaries from inheriting property.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 477 IPC with up to life imprisonment.

max_punishment: Life Imprisonment
ai_summary: Section 477 IPC punishes fraudulent destruction, cancellation, or concealment of wills, adoption authorities, and valuable securities with life imprisonment. Attempts are equally punishable, reflecting the irreversible harm such destruction causes.

title: Falsification of Accounts
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
section_type: Offence Against Property / White Collar Crime
bare_act_text: Whoever, being a clerk, officer or servant, or employed or acting in the capacity of a clerk, officer or servant, wilfully, and with intent to defraud, destroys, alters, mutilates or falsifies any book, electronic record, paper, writing, valuable security or account which belongs to or is in the possession of his employer, or has been received by him for or on behalf of his employer, or wilfully, and with intent to defraud, makes or abets the making of any false entry in, or omits or alters or abets the omission or alteration of any material particular from or in any such book, electronic record, paper, writing, valuable security or account, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, or with fine, or with both. Explanation: It shall be sufficient in any charge under this section to allege a general intent to defraud without naming any particular person intended to be defrauded or specifying any particular sum of money intended to be the subject of the fraud, or any particular day on which the offence was committed.
meaning: Section 477A IPC punishes clerks, officers, and servants who wilfully and fraudulently falsify, destroy, alter, or make false entries in their employer's books, electronic records, accounts, or other documents. It also covers abetting such acts.
simple_explanation: If an employee — such as an accountant, clerk, or officer — deliberately tampers with their employer's accounts, records, or documents to commit fraud — whether by changing entries, deleting information, or making false records — they can be imprisoned for up to 7 years or fined. Even helping someone else do this counts as an offence.
objective: To protect employers and the public from fraudulent manipulation of financial and other records by persons in positions of trust, who have access to official books and accounts.
legal_significance: Section 477A is a critical provision in white-collar crime law. The Explanation removes the prosecution's burden of identifying specific victims or amounts defrauded, making it easier to prosecute account falsification cases. It covers electronic records, making it relevant to modern digital accounting fraud.
essential_elements: The accused is a clerk, officer, servant, or acts in such capacity.
The accused wilfully destroys, alters, mutilates, falsifies, makes false entries in, or omits material particulars from any book, electronic record, paper, writing, valuable security or account.
The document belongs to or is in the possession of the employer, or was received by the accused for the employer.
The act is done with intent to defraud.
Abetting such acts is equally punishable.

mens_rea.description: Wilful conduct combined with intent to defraud is required. Accidental errors or negligent record-keeping do not attract liability under this section.

examples.scenario: An accountant at a company wilfully alters sales figures in the company's electronic accounts to conceal embezzlement of funds.
examples.result: The accountant is liable under Section 477A IPC with imprisonment up to 7 years or fine or both.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years or fine
ai_summary: Section 477A IPC penalises employees — clerks, officers, and servants — who wilfully falsify, destroy, or tamper with their employer's books, electronic records, or accounts with intent to defraud. The Explanation eases the prosecution's burden by not requiring identification of specific victims or amounts, making it a powerful tool against white-collar accounting fraud.

title: Trade Mark (Repealed)
chapter: Repealed Provisions
bare_act_text: Repealed by the Trade and Merchandise Marks Act, 1958 (Act 43 of 1958).
meaning: Section 478 IPC no longer exists in force.
simple_explanation: This section was removed from the Indian Penal Code and is not applicable today.
objective: Repealed.
legal_significance: The provision was omitted and replaced by separate legislation governing trade marks.

mens_rea.description: Not applicable.

max_punishment: Not applicable
ai_summary: Section 478 IPC was repealed by the Trade and Merchandise Marks Act, 1958 and is no longer in force.

title: Property Mark
chapter: Chapter XVIII - Of Offences Relating to Documents and to Property and Other Marks
bare_act_text: A mark used for denoting that movable property belongs to a particular person is called a property mark.
meaning: Section 479 IPC defines a 'property mark' as any mark used to indicate that a particular movable property belongs to a specific person. This definition provides the foundation for offences relating to false property marks under subsequent sections.
simple_explanation: A property mark is simply any mark put on movable property to show who it belongs to — like a brand on livestock, an engraved name on tools, or a stamp on goods. This section defines the term used in the offences that follow.
objective: To provide a precise legal definition of 'property mark' as the foundation for all offences relating to false, counterfeit, or tampered property marks under Sections 481–489.
legal_significance: This is a definitional section — all property mark offences in Chapter XVIII depend on whether the mark in question qualifies as a 'property mark' under this definition. It is limited to movable property, distinguishing it from marks on immovable property.
essential_elements: The mark must be used on movable property.
The purpose of the mark must be to denote ownership — that the property belongs to a particular person.

mens_rea.description: Not applicable — this is a definitional section.

examples.scenario: A farmer brands his cattle with a unique symbol to identify them as his property.
examples.result: That brand is a 'property mark' within the meaning of Section 479 IPC.

max_punishment: Not applicable — definitional section
ai_summary: Section 479 IPC defines 'property mark' as any mark denoting ownership of movable property. It is a foundational definitional provision for the property mark offences in Sections 481–489 of the IPC.

title: Using a False Trade Mark (Repealed)
chapter: Repealed Provisions
bare_act_text: Repealed by the Trade and Merchandise Marks Act, 1958 (Act 43 of 1958).
meaning: Section 480 IPC dealing with the use of a false trade mark is no longer in force.
simple_explanation: This section was removed from the Indian Penal Code and matters relating to trade marks are now governed by separate trade mark legislation.
objective: Repealed.
legal_significance: The provision was omitted by the Trade and Merchandise Marks Act, 1958 and replaced by specialized trade mark laws.

mens_rea.description: Not applicable.

max_punishment: Not applicable
ai_summary: Section 480 IPC relating to the use of a false trade mark was repealed by the Trade and Merchandise Marks Act, 1958 and is no longer in force.

title: Using a False Property Mark
chapter: Chapter XVIII - Of Offences Relating to Documents and to Property and Other Marks
section_type: Definition / Offence Relating to Property Marks
bare_act_text: Whoever marks any movable property or goods or any case, package or other receptacle containing movable property or goods, or uses any case, package or other receptacle having any mark thereon, in a manner reasonably calculated to cause it to be believed that the property or goods so marked, or any property or goods contained in any such receptacle so marked, belong to a person to whom they do not belong, is said to use a false property mark.
meaning: Section 481 IPC defines what constitutes 'using a false property mark' — marking goods or their containers in a way reasonably likely to mislead others into believing they belong to a person they do not actually belong to.
simple_explanation: Using a false property mark means marking goods or their packaging in a way that makes people reasonably believe those goods belong to someone they don't belong to. This section defines the act — Section 482 provides the punishment for it.
objective: To define the specific conduct of using a false property mark, providing the basis for punishment under Section 482 and related provisions.
legal_significance: Section 481 is a definitional provision that sets out what amounts to using a false property mark. The test is objective — whether the marking is 'reasonably calculated' to cause a false belief — not whether it actually deceived anyone.
essential_elements: The accused marks movable property, goods, or their receptacle.
The marking is reasonably calculated to cause it to be believed that the property belongs to a person to whom it does not belong.
The false marking may be on the goods directly or on their case, package, or receptacle.

mens_rea.description: This is a definitional section — mens rea is addressed under Section 482 which provides punishment and allows a defence of acting without intent to defraud.

normal_case.imprisonment: Not applicable — definitional section (punishment under Section 482)

examples.scenario: A trader affixes a well-known manufacturer's ownership mark on goods produced by a different person to make buyers believe they are the original manufacturer's goods.
examples.result: This constitutes using a false property mark under Section 481, punishable under Section 482.

max_punishment: Not applicable — definitional section
ai_summary: Section 481 IPC defines 'using a false property mark' as marking goods or their containers in a manner reasonably calculated to make others believe they belong to a person they do not. The objective test — reasonable likelihood of deception — is key, with punishment provided under Section 482.

title: Punishment for Using a False Property Mark
chapter: Chapter XVIII - Of Offences Relating to Documents and to Property and Other Marks
bare_act_text: Whoever uses any false property mark shall, unless he proves that he acted without intent to defraud, be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to one year, or with fine, or with both.
meaning: Section 482 IPC provides the punishment for using a false property mark as defined under Section 481. Notably, the burden of proving absence of fraudulent intent is placed on the accused.
simple_explanation: Using a false property mark on goods or packaging is punishable by up to 1 year imprisonment or a fine. Importantly, the law presumes fraudulent intent — the accused must prove they acted innocently to escape punishment.
objective: To deter the fraudulent use of false property marks by imposing criminal liability and reversing the burden of proof on the accused to establish innocent intent.
legal_significance: The reverse burden of proof in Section 482 is a significant feature — unlike most criminal offences where the prosecution must prove intent, here the accused must prove they acted without intent to defraud. This reflects the inherently suspicious nature of using another's property mark.
essential_elements: The accused uses a false property mark as defined under Section 481.
The accused fails to prove that they acted without intent to defraud.

mens_rea.description: Intent to defraud is presumed — the burden lies on the accused to prove absence of such intent. This is a reverse burden provision.

examples.scenario: A seller marks counterfeit goods with another manufacturer's ownership mark and sells them. He cannot prove he had no intent to defraud.
examples.result: The seller is liable under Section 482 IPC with imprisonment up to 1 year or fine or both.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 1 year or fine
ai_summary: Section 482 IPC punishes the use of a false property mark with up to 1 year imprisonment or fine, with the notable feature that the burden of proving absence of fraudulent intent lies on the accused — a reverse burden provision that reflects the inherently deceptive nature of the conduct.

title: Counterfeiting a Property Mark Used by Another
chapter: Chapter XVIII - Of Offences Relating to Documents and to Property and Other Marks
bare_act_text: Whoever counterfeits any property mark used by any other person shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.
meaning: Section 483 IPC punishes the counterfeiting of a property mark that is used by another person — that is, creating a fake version of someone else's ownership mark for goods.
simple_explanation: Making a fake copy of someone else's property mark — the mark they use to identify their goods or property as theirs — is a crime punishable by up to 2 years imprisonment or a fine.
objective: To protect the integrity of property marks used by individuals and businesses to identify their goods, preventing fraudulent imitation of ownership identifiers.
legal_significance: Section 483 targets the act of counterfeiting the mark itself, as opposed to Section 482 which punishes the use of a false mark. Together they address both the creation and use stages of property mark fraud.
essential_elements: The accused counterfeits a property mark.
The property mark is one used by another person.
The act of counterfeiting — creating a false version of the genuine mark — must be established.

mens_rea.description: The act of counterfeiting inherently implies intent — knowingly creating a false version of another's mark. No specific additional mens rea is expressly required beyond the act itself.

examples.scenario: A counterfeiter replicates a well-known trader's brand mark used on packaged goods and stamps it on inferior products.
examples.result: The counterfeiter is liable under Section 483 IPC with imprisonment up to 2 years or fine or both.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 2 years or fine
ai_summary: Section 483 IPC criminalises the act of counterfeiting another person's property mark — creating a fake version of their ownership identifier for goods. Carrying up to 2 years imprisonment, it targets the creation stage of property mark fraud, complementing Section 482 which addresses the use stage.

title: Counterfeiting a Mark Used by a Public Servant
chapter: Chapter XVIII - Of Offences Relating to Documents and to Property and Other Marks
bare_act_text: Whoever counterfeits any property mark used by a public servant, or any mark used by a public servant to denote that any property has been manufactured by a particular person or at a particular time or place, or that the property is of a particular quality or has passed through a particular office, or that it is entitled to any exemption, or uses as genuine any such mark knowing the same to be counterfeit, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, and shall also be liable to fine.
meaning: Section 484 IPC punishes the counterfeiting of marks used by public servants — such as hallmarks, quality certification marks, or exemption marks — and also punishes the use of such counterfeit marks knowing them to be fake.
simple_explanation: Forging any official mark used by a government official — like a hallmark, quality certification, or customs exemption mark — or knowingly using such a forged mark is punishable by up to 3 years imprisonment and a fine. This is more serious than counterfeiting private property marks because it involves official government marks.
objective: To protect the integrity of official government and public servant marks used to certify quality, origin, manufacture, or exemption status of goods, preventing fraud against both the state and the public.
legal_significance: Section 484 carries a higher penalty than Section 483 (private marks) because counterfeiting public servant marks undermines official certification systems, which the public relies on for assurance of quality, authenticity, and legality of goods.
essential_elements: The accused counterfeits a property mark or certification mark used by a public servant.
The mark denotes manufacture, time, place, quality, passage through an office, or entitlement to exemption.
Alternatively, the accused uses as genuine any such mark knowing it to be counterfeit.

mens_rea.description: For the use limb, knowledge that the mark is counterfeit is expressly required. For the counterfeiting limb, the act inherently implies fraudulent intent.

examples.scenario: A trader counterfeits the BIS hallmark used by government officials to certify gold purity and stamps it on substandard jewellery.
examples.result: The trader is liable under Section 484 IPC with imprisonment up to 3 years and fine.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 years and fine
ai_summary: Section 484 IPC penalises counterfeiting of marks used by public servants to certify quality, origin, manufacture, or exemption status of goods, and also punishes knowing use of such counterfeit marks. The 3-year maximum — higher than for private marks — reflects the special harm caused by undermining official certification systems.

title: Making or Possession of Any Instrument for Counterfeiting a Property Mark
chapter: Chapter XVIII - Of Offences Relating to Documents and to Property and Other Marks
bare_act_text: Whoever makes or has in his possession any die, plate or other instrument for the purpose of counterfeiting a property mark, or has in his possession a property mark for the purpose of denoting that any goods belong to a person to whom they do not belong, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both.
meaning: Section 485 IPC punishes the making or possession of any instrument — such as a die, plate, or other tool — intended to be used for counterfeiting property marks, targeting the preparatory stage before actual counterfeiting occurs.
simple_explanation: Making or keeping any tool or instrument meant for creating fake property marks — or possessing a property mark to falsely attribute ownership of goods — is punishable by up to 3 years imprisonment or a fine. You don't have to have actually made any fake marks yet.
objective: To enable early intervention by criminalising the possession or making of instruments used for property mark counterfeiting, disrupting such fraud at the preparatory stage.
legal_significance: Section 485 is the preparatory-stage provision for property mark counterfeiting, enabling prosecution before actual counterfeiting is completed — mirroring the structure of Section 256 for government stamps and Section 235 for coins.
essential_elements: The accused makes or possesses a die, plate, or other instrument.
The purpose is counterfeiting a property mark, or the accused possesses a property mark for falsely attributing ownership of goods.

mens_rea.description: The making or possession must be for the purpose of counterfeiting a property mark or falsely denoting ownership. Purpose is the essential mental element.

examples.scenario: A person is found with engraved plates designed to replicate another trader's ownership stamp on goods.
examples.result: The person is liable under Section 485 IPC with imprisonment up to 3 years or fine or both.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 years or fine
ai_summary: Section 485 IPC criminalises the making or possession of instruments intended for counterfeiting property marks at the preparatory stage, enabling prosecution before actual counterfeiting occurs. Maximum punishment is 3 years imprisonment or fine.

title: Selling Goods Marked with a Counterfeit Property Mark
chapter: Chapter XVIII - Of Offences Relating to Documents and to Property and Other Marks
bare_act_text: Whoever sells, or exposes, or has in possession for sale, any goods or things with a counterfeit property mark affixed to or impressed upon the same or to or upon any case, package or other receptacle in which such goods are contained, shall, unless he proves (a) that, having taken all reasonable precautions against committing an offence against this section, he had at the time of the commission of the alleged offence no reason to suspect the genuineness of the mark, and (b) that, on demand made by or on behalf of the prosecutor, he gave all the information in his power with respect to the persons from whom he obtained such goods or things, or (c) that otherwise he had acted innocently, be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to one year, or with fine, or with both.
meaning: Section 486 IPC punishes selling, exposing for sale, or possessing for sale goods bearing a counterfeit property mark. The accused bears the burden of proving they took reasonable precautions, had no reason to suspect the mark was fake, and cooperated with investigators.
simple_explanation: Selling or stocking for sale any goods with a fake ownership mark on them is a crime punishable by up to 1 year imprisonment or a fine — unless the seller can prove they had no reason to know the mark was fake, took precautions, and cooperated with authorities when asked.
objective: To protect consumers and genuine mark owners from the circulation of goods bearing counterfeit property marks by holding sellers accountable even for unknowing distribution, subject to a due diligence defence.
legal_significance: The reverse burden and three-limbed defence in Section 486 places responsibility on sellers to verify the authenticity of property marks on their goods, incentivising due diligence in supply chains. All three conditions of the defence must be satisfied.
essential_elements: The accused sells, exposes for sale, or possesses for sale goods or their receptacles.
The goods bear a counterfeit property mark.
The accused fails to establish the statutory defence of innocent conduct with precautions taken.

mens_rea.description: No positive proof of fraudulent intent is required from the prosecution. The burden is on the accused to prove innocent conduct through the three-limbed statutory defence.

examples.scenario: A shopkeeper sells goods bearing a counterfeit ownership mark, cannot prove he took precautions or had no reason to suspect the mark was fake.
examples.result: The shopkeeper is liable under Section 486 IPC with imprisonment up to 1 year or fine or both.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 1 year or fine
ai_summary: Section 486 IPC penalises selling or possessing for sale goods with counterfeit property marks, with a reverse burden allowing acquittal if the accused proves due diligence, no reason to suspect, and cooperation with investigators. Maximum punishment is 1 year imprisonment or fine.

title: Making a False Mark upon Any Receptacle Containing Goods
chapter: Chapter XVIII - Of Offences Relating to Documents and to Property and Other Marks
bare_act_text: Whoever makes any false mark upon any case, package or other receptacle containing goods, in a manner reasonably calculated to cause any public servant or any other person to believe that such receptacle contains goods which it does not contain or that it does not contain goods which it does contain, or that the goods contained in such receptacle are of a nature or quality different from the real nature or quality thereof, shall, unless he proves that he acted without intent to defraud, be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both.
meaning: Section 487 IPC punishes the making of any false mark on packaging or receptacles to deceive public servants or others about the nature, quality, or contents of the goods inside — such as mislabelling packages to evade customs or misrepresent quality.
simple_explanation: Putting a false mark on any box, package, or container of goods — to make someone believe it contains different goods than it actually does, or that the goods are of a different quality — is punishable by up to 3 years imprisonment or a fine, unless the accused proves innocent intent.
objective: To protect public servants (such as customs officers) and consumers from being deceived about the contents or quality of packaged goods through false markings on containers.
legal_significance: Section 487 is broader than property mark offences — it covers any false marking on receptacles intended to mislead about contents or quality, not just ownership. It has particular relevance in customs fraud, quality misrepresentation, and smuggling contexts.
essential_elements: The accused makes a false mark on a case, package, or other receptacle containing goods.

The false mark is reasonably calculated to cause a public servant or any person to believe the receptacle contains different goods, lacks goods it actually contains, or that goods are of a different nature or quality.
The accused fails to prove they acted without intent to defraud.

mens_rea.description: Intent to defraud is presumed — the reverse burden requires the accused to prove they acted without such intent.

examples.scenario: An importer marks boxes containing luxury goods as containing agricultural produce to evade customs duty, intending to deceive customs officers.
examples.result: The importer is liable under Section 487 IPC with imprisonment up to 3 years or fine or both.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 years or fine
ai_summary: Section 487 IPC penalises false marking on packaging to deceive public servants or others about the contents or quality of goods. With a reverse burden of proof, it applies widely including customs fraud and quality misrepresentation, carrying up to 3 years imprisonment or fine.

title: Punishment for Making Use of Any Such False Mark
chapter: Chapter XVIII - Of Offences Relating to Documents and to Property and Other Marks
bare_act_text: Whoever makes use of any such false mark in any manner prohibited by the last foregoing section shall, unless he proves that he acted without intent to defraud, be punished as if he had committed an offence against that section.
meaning: Section 488 IPC extends the offence under Section 487 to the use of a false mark on receptacles — not just the making of it. Anyone who uses such a false mark faces the same punishment as the person who created it.
simple_explanation: Using a false mark on a package or container — even if someone else made the false mark — is punishable the same as making it, up to 3 years imprisonment or a fine, unless the accused proves they had no fraudulent intent.
objective: To ensure that the entire chain of persons involved in deploying false marks on receptacles — both makers and users — are equally liable, preventing any loophole based on the distinction between making and using the mark.
legal_significance: Section 488 closes the gap that would exist if only the maker of the false mark were liable. By equating the punishment for use with that for making, it targets all participants in the false marking scheme.
essential_elements: The accused uses a false mark of the type described in Section 487.
The use is in a manner prohibited by Section 487.
The accused fails to prove they acted without intent to defraud.

mens_rea.description: Intent to defraud is presumed — the reverse burden requires the accused to prove they acted without such intent, as under Section 487.

normal_case.imprisonment: Up to 3 years (either description) — same as Section 487

examples.scenario: A distributor knowingly uses packages with false quality markings created by the manufacturer to sell inferior goods to retailers.
examples.result: The distributor is liable under Section 488 IPC with the same punishment as Section 487 — up to 3 years imprisonment or fine or both.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 years or fine (same as Section 487)
ai_summary: Section 488 IPC extends the liability under Section 487 to persons who use false marks on receptacles — not just those who make them — ensuring equal accountability across the entire chain of false marking fraud. Punishment is identical to Section 487.

title: Tampering with Property Mark with Intent to Cause Injury
chapter: Chapter XVIII - Of Offences Relating to Documents and to Property and Other Marks
bare_act_text: Whoever removes, destroys, defaces or adds to any property mark, intending or knowing it to be likely that he may thereby cause injury to any person, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to one year, or with fine, or with both.
meaning: Section 489 IPC punishes tampering with a genuine property mark — by removing, destroying, defacing, or adding to it — when done with the intent or knowledge that it may cause injury to any person.
simple_explanation: Deliberately damaging, removing, or altering a legitimate property mark on goods — knowing it could harm someone — is punishable by up to 1 year imprisonment or a fine. This covers situations like removing an owner's mark from stolen goods, or defacing quality marks to harm a competitor.
objective: To protect property marks from deliberate tampering that could cause harm — whether to the owner of the mark, consumers, or others — ensuring that ownership and quality identifiers remain intact and trustworthy.
legal_significance: Section 489 addresses the destruction or alteration of genuine marks (as opposed to other sections which address creation of false marks). The injury contemplated may be to any person — including the legitimate owner, buyers, or competitors — making this a broad protective provision.
essential_elements: The accused removes, destroys, defaces, or adds to any property mark.
The accused intends to cause injury to any person, or knows it is likely that injury may be caused.

mens_rea.description: Intent to cause injury or knowledge that injury is likely from the tampering is required.

examples.scenario: A person removes the original owner's mark from stolen cattle, knowing the owner will be unable to identify and reclaim them.
examples.result: The person is liable under Section 489 IPC with imprisonment up to 1 year or fine or both.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 1 year or fine
ai_summary: Section 489 IPC penalises deliberate tampering with genuine property marks — removing, destroying, defacing, or adding to them — when done with intent or knowledge of likely injury to any person. Maximum punishment is 1 year imprisonment or fine.

title: Counterfeiting Currency-Notes or Bank-Notes
chapter: Chapter XVIII - Of Currency-Notes and Bank-Notes
section_type: Offence Relating to Currency Notes and Bank Notes
bare_act_text: Whoever counterfeits, or knowingly performs any part of the process of counterfeiting, any currency-note or bank-note, shall be punished with imprisonment for life, or with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine. Explanation: For the purposes of this section and of sections 489B, 489C, 489D and 489E, the expression 'bank-note' means a promissory note or engagement for the payment of money to bearer on demand issued by any person carrying on the business of banking in any part of the world, or issued by or under the authority of any State or Sovereign Power, and intended to be used as equivalent to, or as a substitute for money.
meaning: Section 489A IPC punishes the counterfeiting of currency-notes or bank-notes, or knowing participation in any part of the counterfeiting process, with a maximum punishment of life imprisonment. It is one of the most serious offences in the IPC.
simple_explanation: Making fake currency notes or bank notes — or participating in any part of the process of making them — is punishable by life imprisonment and a fine. This covers Indian rupee notes as well as notes of any bank or government in the world that are meant to be used as money.
objective: To protect the integrity of monetary systems — both national and international — by imposing the severest penalties for currency note counterfeiting, recognising it as a direct attack on economic stability.
legal_significance: Section 489A carries life imprisonment as its maximum punishment, placing currency counterfeiting among the most serious offences in Indian law. The Explanation's broad definition of 'bank-note' extends protection to notes of any banking institution or sovereign power worldwide.
essential_elements: The accused counterfeits a currency-note or bank-note, or knowingly performs any part of the counterfeiting process.
A 'bank-note' includes any promissory note or payment engagement to bearer on demand issued by any bank or state/sovereign power anywhere in the world, intended as a substitute for money.

mens_rea.description: Knowledge that one is performing part of the counterfeiting process is required. The act of counterfeiting itself implies intent.

examples.scenario: A person operates a high-quality printing setup producing counterfeit Indian Rs. 500 notes.
examples.result: The person is liable under Section 489A IPC with punishment up to life imprisonment and fine.

max_punishment: Imprisonment for life and fine
ai_summary: Section 489A IPC criminalises the counterfeiting of currency-notes or bank-notes with a maximum punishment of life imprisonment — one of the harshest in the IPC. The broad definition of 'bank-note' extends protection to notes of any banking institution or sovereign power globally, reflecting the international dimension of currency counterfeiting threats.

title: Using as Genuine, Forged or Counterfeit Currency-Notes or Bank-Notes
chapter: Chapter XVIII - Of Currency-Notes and Bank-Notes
section_type: Offence Relating to Currency Notes and Bank Notes
bare_act_text: Whoever sells to, or buys or receives from, any other person, or otherwise traffics in or uses as genuine, any forged or counterfeit currency-note or bank-note, knowing or having reason to believe the same to be forged or counterfeit, shall be punished with imprisonment for life, or with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.
meaning: Section 489B IPC punishes any person who sells, buys, receives, traffics in, or uses as genuine any forged or counterfeit currency-note or bank-note, knowing or having reason to believe it is counterfeit.
simple_explanation: Buying, selling, using, or dealing in fake currency notes — knowing or having reason to believe they are fake — can result in life imprisonment and a fine. This covers the entire chain of distribution of counterfeit currency, from traffickers to those who use fake notes in transactions.
objective: To prevent the circulation of counterfeit currency by criminalising all forms of trafficking and use of fake notes, targeting not just counterfeiters but the entire distribution network.
legal_significance: Section 489B casts a very wide net — it covers selling, buying, receiving, trafficking, and using as genuine, ensuring that all participants in the distribution chain of counterfeit currency face the same severe maximum punishment of life imprisonment. Constructive knowledge ('reason to believe') suffices.
essential_elements: The accused sells to, buys from, receives from, traffics in, or uses as genuine any forged or counterfeit currency-note or bank-note.
The accused knows or has reason to believe it is forged or counterfeit.

mens_rea.description: Knowledge or reason to believe that the currency-note or bank-note is counterfeit is required. Constructive knowledge suffices.

examples.scenario: A person purchases counterfeit Rs. 2000 notes at a discounted rate from a distributor, knowing they are fake, and uses them at shops.
examples.result: The person is liable under Section 489B IPC with punishment up to life imprisonment and fine.

max_punishment: Imprisonment for life and fine
ai_summary: Section 489B IPC criminalises all forms of dealing in counterfeit currency — selling, buying, trafficking, and using as genuine — by anyone who knows or has reason to believe it is fake. The life imprisonment maximum ensures that the entire distribution chain of counterfeit currency faces severe consequences.

title: Possession of Forged or Counterfeit Currency-Notes or Bank-Notes
chapter: Chapter XVIII - Of Currency-Notes and Bank-Notes
section_type: Offence Relating to Currency Notes and Bank Notes
bare_act_text: Whoever has in his possession any forged or counterfeit currency-note or bank-note, knowing or having reason to believe the same to be forged or counterfeit and intending to use the same as genuine or that it may be used as genuine, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, or with fine, or with both.
meaning: Section 489C IPC punishes the possession of forged or counterfeit currency-notes or bank-notes when the possessor knows or has reason to believe they are fake and intends them to be used as genuine.
simple_explanation: Simply having fake currency notes in your possession — knowing they are fake and intending to use them or let them be used as real money — is punishable by up to 7 years imprisonment or a fine. Possession alone, without actual use, is sufficient to attract liability.
objective: To enable early intervention against currency counterfeit distribution by criminalising possession before actual use or circulation occurs.
legal_significance: Section 489C targets the possession stage, enabling prosecution before fake notes are actually circulated. The lower maximum punishment (7 years versus life) compared to Sections 489A and 489B reflects the lesser harm at the possession stage compared to active counterfeiting or use.
essential_elements: The accused possesses forged or counterfeit currency-notes or bank-notes.
The accused knows or has reason to believe they are forged or counterfeit.
The accused intends to use them as genuine or that they may be used as genuine.

mens_rea.description: Both knowledge/reason to believe of the counterfeit nature AND intent for them to be used as genuine are required.

examples.scenario: Police recover a stash of counterfeit Rs. 500 notes from a suspect's home. The suspect knew they were fake and planned to distribute them.
examples.result: The suspect is liable under Section 489C IPC with imprisonment up to 7 years or fine or both.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years or fine
ai_summary: Section 489C IPC criminalises possession of counterfeit currency-notes or bank-notes with knowledge of their fake nature and intent for use as genuine. Maximum punishment is 7 years, targeting the possession stage before actual circulation to enable early prosecution of counterfeit currency networks.

title: Making or Possessing Instruments or Materials for Forging or Counterfeiting Currency-Notes or Bank-Notes
chapter: Chapter XVIII - Of Currency-Notes and Bank-Notes
section_type: Offence Relating to Currency Notes and Bank Notes
bare_act_text: Whoever makes, or performs any part of the process of making, or buys or sells or disposes of, or has in his possession, any machinery, instrument or material for the purpose of being used, or knowing or having reason to believe that it is intended to be used, for forging or counterfeiting any currency-note or bank-note, shall be punished with imprisonment for life, or with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.
meaning: Section 489D IPC punishes the making, buying, selling, disposing of, or possessing any machinery, instrument, or material intended for forging or counterfeiting currency-notes or bank-notes, with a maximum punishment of life imprisonment.
simple_explanation: Making, buying, selling, or possessing any machine, tool, or material meant for producing fake currency notes — even before any fake notes have been made — can result in life imprisonment and a fine. This is as serious as actually making fake notes because the tools themselves are the infrastructure of counterfeiting.
objective: To eliminate the infrastructure of currency counterfeiting by imposing the same severe penalties for possession of counterfeiting machinery as for the actual counterfeiting, targeting the supply chain at its earliest stage.
legal_significance: Unlike Section 256 (7 years for stamp counterfeiting instruments) and Section 235 (10 years for coin counterfeiting instruments), Section 489D carries life imprisonment for currency-note counterfeiting instruments — reflecting the supreme importance of protecting the currency note system.
essential_elements: The accused makes, performs any part of making, buys, sells, disposes of, or possesses any machinery, instrument, or material.
The purpose is forging or counterfeiting currency-notes or bank-notes, or the accused knows or has reason to believe it is so intended.

mens_rea.description: The act must be for the purpose of currency note counterfeiting, or the accused must know or have reason to believe the instruments are so intended. Constructive knowledge suffices.

examples.scenario: Police seize a high-quality printing press, special paper, and security ink from a suspect's premises, all intended for printing counterfeit currency notes.
examples.result: The suspect is liable under Section 489D IPC with punishment up to life imprisonment and fine.

max_punishment: Imprisonment for life and fine
ai_summary: Section 489D IPC criminalises the making, trading, or possession of machinery, instruments, or materials intended for counterfeiting currency-notes or bank-notes. The life imprisonment maximum — matching Sections 489A and 489B — treats the infrastructure of counterfeiting with the same severity as the counterfeiting itself.

title: Making or Using Documents Resembling Currency-Notes or Bank-Notes
chapter: Chapter XVIII - Of Currency-Notes and Bank-Notes
section_type: Offence Relating to Currency Notes and Bank Notes
bare_act_text: Whoever makes, or causes to be made, or uses for any purpose whatsoever, or delivers to any person, any document purporting to be, or in any way resembling, or so nearly resembling as to be calculated to deceive, any currency-note or bank-note shall be punished with fine which may extend to one hundred rupees. If any person, whose name appears on a document the making of which is an offence under sub-section (1), refuses, without lawful excuse, to disclose to a police-officer the name and address of the person by whom it was printed or otherwise made, he shall be punished with fine which may extend to two hundred rupees. Where the name of any person appears on any such document it may, until the contrary is proved, be presumed that that person caused the document to be made.
meaning: Section 489E IPC punishes the making, using, or delivering of any document that resembles or is calculated to deceive as a currency-note or bank-note — even if not intended as a direct counterfeit. It also creates a presumption against persons whose names appear on such documents.
simple_explanation: Making, using, or distributing any document that looks like a currency note — even for novelty, advertising, or other purposes — can attract a fine. If someone whose name is on such a document refuses to tell police who printed it, they face a higher fine. This prevents even 'novelty money' from causing confusion with genuine currency.
objective: To prevent public confusion between genuine currency and documents resembling currency, even where there is no direct intent to counterfeit, by imposing a nominal fine as a deterrent.
legal_significance: Section 489E addresses a lower level of risk than Sections 489A–489D — mere resemblance to currency rather than actual counterfeiting. The relatively low fine reflects this reduced culpability. The evidentiary presumption in sub-section (3) facilitates investigation by placing the burden on named persons to prove non-involvement.
essential_elements: The accused makes, causes to be made, uses for any purpose, or delivers any document.
The document purports to be, resembles, or so nearly resembles a currency-note or bank-note as to be calculated to deceive.

mens_rea.description: No specific fraudulent intent is required — the mere act of making, using, or distributing a currency-resembling document is sufficient for liability under sub-section (1).

punishment.aggravated_case: Fine up to ₹200 for refusal to disclose printer's identity (sub-section 2)

examples.scenario: An advertiser prints promotional vouchers designed to closely resemble currency notes and distributes them to the public.
examples.result: The advertiser is liable under Section 489E(1) IPC with fine up to ₹100.

max_punishment: Fine up to ₹100 (sub-section 1); fine up to ₹200 for refusal to disclose (sub-section 2)
ai_summary: Section 489E IPC addresses the lowest level of currency-note offences — making or distributing documents that merely resemble currency notes, without necessarily intending counterfeiting. The nominal fine reflects reduced culpability, while a presumption against named persons on such documents assists investigation.

title: Breach of Contract of Service During Voyage or Journey (Repealed)
chapter: Chapter XIX - Of the Criminal Breach of Contracts of Service
bare_act_text: Repealed by the Workmen's Breach of Contract (Repealing) Act, 1925 (Act 3 of 1925).
meaning: Section 490 IPC is no longer in force.
simple_explanation: This section dealing with breach of contract of service during a voyage or journey was removed from the Indian Penal Code.
objective: Repealed.
legal_significance: The provision was repealed by the Workmen's Breach of Contract (Repealing) Act, 1925 and is no longer applicable.

mens_rea.description: Not applicable.

max_punishment: Not applicable
ai_summary: Section 490 IPC relating to breach of contract of service during voyage or journey was repealed by the Workmen's Breach of Contract (Repealing) Act, 1925 and is no longer in force.

title: Breach of Contract to Attend on and Supply Wants of Helpless Person
chapter: Chapter XIX - Of the Criminal Breach of Contracts of Service
section_type: Criminal Breach of Contract of Service
bare_act_text: Whoever, being bound by a lawful contract to attend on or to supply the wants of any person who, by reason of youth, or of unsoundness of mind, or of a disease or bodily weakness, is helpless or incapable of providing for his own safety or of supplying his own wants, voluntarily omits so to do, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three months, or with fine which may extend to two hundred rupees, or with both.
meaning: Section 491 IPC punishes a person who is contractually bound to care for or supply the needs of a helpless person — such as a child, mentally ill person, or physically incapacitated individual — and voluntarily fails to do so.
simple_explanation: If someone is contractually obligated to care for a helpless person — like a nurse, caregiver, or attendant hired to look after a sick, mentally ill, or young person — and deliberately fails to do so, they can be imprisoned for up to 3 months or fined up to ₹200.
objective: To protect vulnerable persons — those incapacitated by age, mental illness, or physical weakness — from being abandoned or neglected by those contractually entrusted with their care.
legal_significance: Section 491 criminalises contractual neglect of vulnerable persons, filling a gap between civil breach of contract and more serious criminal offences. It places special emphasis on the protection of those unable to fend for themselves, reflecting a duty of care that rises to criminal law when voluntarily breached.
essential_elements: The accused is bound by a lawful contract to attend on or supply the wants of a person.
The person is helpless — due to youth, unsoundness of mind, disease, or bodily weakness.
The accused voluntarily omits to perform their contractual duty.
The omission is voluntary, not accidental or unavoidable.

mens_rea.description: The omission must be voluntary — a deliberate decision not to perform the contractual duty of care.

examples.scenario: A nurse contractually engaged to care for a bedridden elderly patient voluntarily abandons the patient without arranging alternative care, leaving the patient without food or medication.
examples.result: The nurse is liable under Section 491 IPC with imprisonment up to 3 months or fine up to ₹200 or both.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 months or fine up to ₹200
ai_summary: Section 491 IPC criminalises voluntary neglect of contractual duties to care for helpless persons — those incapacitated by youth, mental illness, or physical weakness. The relatively modest punishment of 3 months or ₹200 fine reflects the specific contractual and civil dimensions of the offence, while ensuring criminal accountability for those who abandon vulnerable persons in their care.

title: Cohabitation Caused by a Man Deceitfully Inducing a Belief of Lawful Marriage
chapter: Chapter XX - Of Offences Relating to Marriage
bare_act_text: Every man who by deceit causes any woman who is not lawfully married to him to believe that she is lawfully married to him and to cohabit or have sexual intercourse with him in that belief, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.
meaning: Section 493 IPC punishes a man who deceives a woman into believing she is lawfully married to him and thereby induces her to cohabit or have sexual intercourse with him. The section protects women from fraudulent pseudo-marriages.
simple_explanation: If a man tricks a woman into believing they are legally married — when they are not — and she cohabits with or has sexual intercourse with him because of that false belief, the man can be imprisoned for up to 10 years and fined. The woman is the victim of fraud here.
objective: To protect women from being deceived into cohabitation or sexual intercourse through fraudulent misrepresentation of a valid marriage, preserving their right to make informed decisions about intimate relationships.
legal_significance: Section 493 is a specific form of fraud targeting the marriage institution. The high maximum punishment of 10 years reflects the gravity of sexual deception and the serious harm to the woman's dignity, autonomy, and social standing. It applies only where the woman genuinely believes in the marriage.
essential_elements: The accused is a man.
He, by deceit, causes a woman to believe she is lawfully married to him.
The woman is not in fact lawfully married to him.
The woman, in that belief, cohabits with or has sexual intercourse with him.

mens_rea.description: Deceit — intentional misrepresentation to induce the false belief of a lawful marriage — is the essential mental element.

examples.scenario: A man performs a fake marriage ceremony with a woman, intentionally concealing that the marriage has no legal validity, and cohabits with her on the basis of her resulting belief.
examples.result: The man is liable under Section 493 IPC with imprisonment up to 10 years and fine.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 10 years and fine
ai_summary: Section 493 IPC penalises men who deceive women into believing they are lawfully married and thereby induce cohabitation or sexual intercourse. The 10-year maximum punishment reflects the serious violation of personal dignity and autonomy involved in marriage fraud of this nature.

title: Marrying Again During Lifetime of Husband or Wife
chapter: Chapter XX - Of Offences Relating to Marriage
bare_act_text: Whoever, having a husband or wife living, marries in any case in which such marriage is void by reason of its taking place during the life of such husband or wife, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine. Exception: This section does not extend to any person whose marriage with such husband or wife has been declared void by a Court of competent jurisdiction, nor to any person who contracts a marriage during the life of a former husband or wife, if such husband or wife, at the time of the subsequent marriage, shall have been continually absent from such person for the space of seven years, and shall not have been heard of by such person as being alive within that time, provided the person contracting such subsequent marriage shall, before such marriage takes place, inform the person with whom such marriage is contracted of the real state of facts so far as the same are within his or her knowledge.
meaning: Section 494 IPC is the provision on bigamy — it punishes a person who contracts a second marriage while their first spouse is still alive and the first marriage has not been legally dissolved or declared void.
simple_explanation: Getting married again while you are still legally married to someone else is bigamy and is punishable by up to 7 years imprisonment and a fine. Exceptions apply if the first marriage was declared void by a court, or if the first spouse has been missing for 7 years and is presumed dead — but the second partner must be informed of the facts.
objective: To protect the sanctity of marriage and the rights of spouses by criminalising bigamy — the fraudulent contraction of a second marriage without dissolving the first.
legal_significance: Section 494 is the foundational bigamy provision in Indian law. The two exceptions — court declaration of voidness and seven-year unexplained absence — reflect practical considerations while maintaining the duty of disclosure to the second spouse. Section 495 provides an aggravated version where the second spouse is not informed of the first marriage.
essential_elements: The accused has a husband or wife living.
The accused marries again.
The second marriage is void by reason of the first spouse being alive.
The exceptions of court-declared voidness or seven-year unexplained absence do not apply.

mens_rea.description: Knowledge of the subsisting first marriage is implied in the offence. The accused must know that their first spouse is alive and the first marriage has not been legally dissolved.

examples.scenario: A man with a living wife whom he has not divorced marries another woman in a registered ceremony.
examples.result: The man is liable under Section 494 IPC with imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine
compoundable: Yes (by husband or wife of the person so marrying)
ai_summary: Section 494 IPC is the principal bigamy provision, punishing any person who contracts a second marriage while their first spouse is alive and the first marriage subsists. Maximum punishment is 7 years, with exceptions for court-voided marriages and seven-year unexplained absence, subject to disclosure obligations to the second spouse.

title: Same Offence with Concealment of Former Marriage from Person with Whom Subsequent Marriage Is Contracted
chapter: Chapter XX - Of Offences Relating to Marriage
bare_act_text: Whoever commits the offence defined in the last preceding section having concealed from the person with whom the subsequent marriage is contracted, the fact of the former marriage, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years, and shall also be liable to fine.
meaning: Section 495 IPC is the aggravated form of Section 494 (bigamy), applying when the bigamist additionally conceals the existence of the first marriage from the person they are marrying in the second ceremony.
simple_explanation: If someone not only commits bigamy but also hides the fact of their first marriage from their new spouse, the punishment increases to up to 10 years imprisonment and a fine. Concealment of the prior marriage makes the offence more serious because the second spouse is deprived of the knowledge needed to make an informed decision.
objective: To protect persons entering into marriage from being deceived about their partner's existing marital status, imposing a higher penalty where concealment compounds the fraud of bigamy.
legal_significance: Section 495 recognises that concealment of a prior marriage is a distinct and more serious wrong — it deprives the innocent second spouse of the ability to make an informed choice. The 10-year maximum (versus 7 years under Section 494) reflects this additional fraudulent element.
essential_elements: All elements of the offence under Section 494 are satisfied.
The accused additionally conceals the fact of the former marriage from the person with whom the subsequent marriage is contracted.

mens_rea.description: Knowledge of the prior marriage and deliberate concealment of it from the second spouse are both required.

examples.scenario: A man with a living wife deliberately conceals his marriage when entering a second marriage, and his second wife is unaware of the first marriage.
examples.result: The man is liable under Section 495 IPC with imprisonment up to 10 years and fine.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 10 years and fine
compoundable: Yes (by husband or wife of the person so marrying)
ai_summary: Section 495 IPC is the aggravated bigamy provision, adding the element of concealment of the prior marriage from the second spouse. The 10-year maximum — higher than Section 494 — reflects the compounded fraud of not only contracting a void marriage but actively deceiving the innocent second spouse about one's marital status.

title: Marriage Ceremony Fraudulently Gone Through Without Lawful Marriage
chapter: Chapter XX - Of Offences Relating to Marriage
bare_act_text: Whoever, dishonestly or with a fraudulent intention, goes through the ceremony of being married, knowing that he is not thereby lawfully married, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, and shall also be liable to fine.
meaning: Section 496 IPC punishes a person who goes through the outward ceremony of marriage dishonestly or with fraudulent intent, knowing that no valid legal marriage is being contracted — for instance, performing a fake marriage rite to deceive the other party.
simple_explanation: If someone deliberately goes through a marriage ceremony knowing it will not result in a legally valid marriage — and does so to deceive the other person — they can be imprisoned for up to 7 years and fined. Unlike Section 494 (bigamy), this doesn't require an existing spouse; the fraud lies in pretending to contract a valid marriage when knowing it is not valid.
objective: To protect persons from being deceived into believing they have entered a valid marriage when the ceremony was fraudulently performed with no intention of creating a lawful union.
legal_significance: Section 496 covers a distinct scenario from bigamy — where one party knows the ceremony creates no valid marriage and proceeds fraudulently. It complements Section 493 (deceitful inducement of belief of marriage) by addressing the ceremony itself rather than the resulting cohabitation.
essential_elements: The accused goes through the ceremony of being married.
The accused knows that the ceremony does not result in a lawful marriage.
The accused acts dishonestly or with fraudulent intention.

mens_rea.description: Dishonesty or fraudulent intention combined with knowledge that no lawful marriage is being contracted are both required.

examples.scenario: A person arranges and performs a marriage ceremony with a woman, knowing that the marriage is void due to a legal defect, without informing her, intending to have her believe she is legally married.
examples.result: The person is liable under Section 496 IPC with imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine
ai_summary: Section 496 IPC penalises persons who go through a marriage ceremony dishonestly or fraudulently, knowing it will not result in a legally valid marriage. It protects the innocent party from being deceived into believing they have contracted a valid marriage, carrying a maximum punishment of 7 years imprisonment and fine.

title: Adultery
chapter: Chapter XX - Of Offences Relating to Marriage
