bare_act_text: Whoever has sexual intercourse with a person who is and whom he knows or has reason to believe to be the wife of another man, without the consent or connivance of that man, such sexual intercourse not amounting to the offence of rape, is guilty of the offence of adultery, and shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to five years, or with fine, or with both. In such case the wife shall not be punishable as an abettor.
meaning: Section 497 IPC defined adultery as sexual intercourse with the wife of another man, without that man's consent or connivance, where such intercourse does not amount to rape, with the wife not liable as an abettor.
simple_explanation: This section made it a crime for a man to have sexual intercourse with another man's wife without that husband's consent, but only the man could be punished, not the woman.
objective: Originally intended to protect the marital rights of a husband over his wife's sexual fidelity by penalizing a third party who engaged in intercourse with her without his consent.
legal_significance: This section was struck down as unconstitutional by the Supreme Court of India in Joseph Shine v. Union of India (2018), on grounds of violating Articles 14, 15, and 21, and is no longer enforceable as a criminal offence.
essential_elements: The accused has sexual intercourse with a woman.
The woman is, and the accused knows or has reason to believe she is, the wife of another man.
Such intercourse is without the consent or connivance of the husband.
The intercourse does not amount to rape.

mens_rea.description: Knowledge or reason to believe that the woman is the wife of another man, and absence of that man's consent or connivance to the intercourse.

examples.scenario: A has consensual sexual intercourse with Z's wife, knowing her to be married to Z, without Z's consent.
examples.result: Under the now-struck-down Section 497, A would have been guilty of adultery; the wife would not be punishable.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 5 years, or fine, or both (provision struck down and no longer in force)
ai_summary: Section 497 IPC, which criminalized adultery by a man with another man's wife without the husband's consent, has been declared unconstitutional and struck down by the Supreme Court in 2018, and is no longer operative.

title: Enticing or Taking Away or Detaining with Criminal Intent a Married Woman
chapter: Chapter XX - Of Offences Relating to Marriage
bare_act_text: Whoever takes or entices away any woman who is and whom he knows or has reason to believe to be the wife of any other man, from that man, or from any person having the care of her on behalf of that man, with intent that she may have illicit intercourse with any person, or conceals or detains with that intent any such woman, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.
meaning: Section 498 IPC punishes taking, enticing away, concealing, or detaining a married woman from her husband (or his caretaker) with the intent that she have illicit intercourse with any person.
simple_explanation: If a person takes away, lures away, hides, or keeps a married woman away from her husband (or someone the husband has entrusted her care to) intending that she engage in illicit sexual relations with someone, that person can be punished with imprisonment up to 2 years, fine, or both.
objective: To protect the marital relationship by penalizing third parties who facilitate the removal or concealment of a married woman for purposes of illicit intercourse.
legal_significance: Operates alongside Section 497 (now struck down) as part of the historical scheme protecting marital rights; its continued validity and interpretation may be affected by the constitutional reasoning that invalidated Section 497, though the section itself has not been formally struck down.
essential_elements: The woman is, and the accused knows or has reason to believe she is, the wife of another man.
The accused takes or entices away such woman from her husband or from a person having her care on the husband's behalf, OR conceals or detains her.
The act is done with intent that the woman may have illicit intercourse with any person.

mens_rea.description: Knowledge or reason to believe that the woman is married to another man, combined with intent that she have illicit intercourse with any person.

examples.scenario: A entices away Z's wife from Z's house, intending that she have illicit intercourse with himself or another person.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 498 with imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both
ai_summary: Section 498 IPC punishes taking, enticing away, concealing, or detaining a married woman with intent that she have illicit intercourse with any person, with imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both.

title: Husband or Relative of Husband of a Woman Subjecting Her to Cruelty
chapter: Chapter XXA - Of Cruelty by Husband or Relatives of Husband
bare_act_text: Whoever, being the husband or the relative of the husband of a woman, subjects such woman to cruelty shall be punished with imprisonment for a term which may extend to three years and shall also be liable to fine. Explanation.—For the purposes of this section, "cruelty" means—(a) any wilful conduct which is of such a nature as is likely to drive the woman to commit suicide or to cause grave injury or danger to life, limb or health (whether mental or physical) of the woman; or (b) harassment of the woman where such harassment is with a view to coercing her or any person related to her to meet any unlawful demand for any property or valuable security or is on account of failure by her or any person related to her to meet such demand.
meaning: Section 498A IPC punishes a husband or his relative who subjects a woman to cruelty, whether through conduct likely to drive her to suicide or cause grave injury, or through harassment to coerce unlawful demands for property or valuable security (such as dowry).
simple_explanation: If a husband or his relatives mistreat a married woman in a way that could drive her to suicide or cause serious physical or mental harm, or harass her (or her family) to extract money, property, or valuables (such as dowry demands), they can be punished with imprisonment up to 3 years and a fine.
objective: To address and curb domestic cruelty and dowry-related harassment of married women by their husbands and in-laws.
legal_significance: Introduced a dedicated chapter and offence specifically targeting matrimonial cruelty, particularly dowry harassment, providing women with legal recourse against abusive marital households.
essential_elements: The accused is the husband or a relative of the husband of the woman.
The accused subjects the woman to cruelty.

Cruelty includes either (a) wilful conduct likely to drive the woman to suicide or cause grave injury/danger to life, limb, or health (mental or physical), or (b) harassment to coerce her or her relatives to meet an unlawful demand for property/valuable security, or harassment due to failure to meet such a demand.

mens_rea.description: Wilful conduct constituting cruelty as defined, or intentional harassment aimed at coercing unlawful demands or arising from failure to satisfy such demands.

examples.scenario: A husband and his mother repeatedly harass his wife, demanding additional dowry from her family, causing her mental agony.
examples.result: Both the husband and his mother are liable under Section 498A with imprisonment up to 3 years and fine.

examples.scenario: A husband's wilful conduct towards his wife is of such a nature that it drives her to attempt suicide.
examples.result: The husband is liable under Section 498A with imprisonment up to 3 years and fine.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 years and fine
compoundable: No (non-compoundable, though some states permit compounding with court permission)
ai_summary: Section 498A IPC punishes a husband or his relatives for subjecting a married woman to cruelty—whether conduct likely to drive her to suicide or cause grave injury, or harassment to coerce unlawful property/dowry demands—with imprisonment up to 3 years and fine.

title: Defamation
chapter: Chapter XXI - Of Defamation
bare_act_text: Whoever, by words either spoken or intended to be read, or by signs or by visible representations, makes or publishes any imputation concerning any person intending to harm, or knowing or having reason to believe that such imputation will harm, the reputation of such person, is said, except in the cases hereinafter excepted, to defame that person. Explanation 1.—It may amount to defamation to impute anything to a deceased person, if the imputation would harm the reputation of that person if living, and is intended to be hurtful to the feelings of his family or other near relatives. Explanation 2.—It may amount to defamation to make an imputation concerning a company or an association or collection of persons as such. Explanation 3.—An imputation in the form of an alternative or expressed ironically, may amount to defamation. Explanation 4.—No imputation is said to harm a person's reputation, unless that imputation directly or indirectly, in the estimation of others, lowers the moral or intellectual character of that person, or lowers the character of that person in respect of his caste or of his calling, or lowers the credit of that person, or causes it to be believed that the body of that person is in a loathsome state, or in a state generally considered as disgraceful. The section provides ten exceptions covering: imputation of truth for public good; public conduct of public servants; conduct of any person touching a public question; publication of court proceedings; merits of a case decided by court or conduct of witnesses; merits of public performance; censure passed in good faith by a person having lawful authority; accusation in good faith to an authorised person; imputation made in good faith for protection of interests; and caution intended for the good of the person to whom conveyed or for public good.
meaning: Section 499 IPC defines defamation as making or publishing any imputation concerning a person, by words, signs, or visible representations, with intent to harm or knowledge/reason to believe it will harm that person's reputation, subject to ten specified exceptions.
simple_explanation: Defamation occurs when someone says, writes, or otherwise communicates something about a person that is intended to, or is likely to, damage that person's reputation. However, the law lists ten exceptions where such statements are not considered defamation, such as true statements made for public good, fair comment on public servants' conduct, fair reporting of court proceedings, and good-faith opinions on matters of public interest.
objective: To define the offence of defamation precisely, balancing protection of individual reputation with freedom of expression through a detailed set of exceptions for legitimate speech.
legal_significance: Forms the foundational definition for criminal defamation, punishable under Section 500, and its ten exceptions are critical in distinguishing protected speech (fair comment, truth for public good, court reporting, etc.) from defamatory statements.
essential_elements: The accused makes or publishes an imputation concerning a person (or deceased person, company, or association).
Such imputation is made by words (spoken or intended to be read), signs, or visible representations.
The accused intends to harm, or knows or has reason to believe, that the imputation will harm the reputation of that person.
The imputation does not fall within any of the ten specified exceptions.

mens_rea.description: Intention to harm a person's reputation, or knowledge or reason to believe that the imputation made will cause such harm.

examples.scenario: A says "Z is an honest man; he never stole B's watch," intending to cause it to be believed that Z did steal B's watch.
examples.result: This amounts to defamation under Section 499, unless covered by an exception.

examples.scenario: A, in good faith, expresses an opinion on the merits of a case decided by a court, commenting on a witness's conduct as it appeared in that case.
examples.result: A is protected under the Fifth Exception and does not commit defamation.

max_punishment: Not applicable (definition section; punishment prescribed under Section 500)
ai_summary: Section 499 IPC defines defamation as making or publishing harmful imputations concerning a person's reputation through words, signs, or visible representations, subject to ten exceptions covering truth for public good, fair comment on public matters, court reporting, and good-faith opinions.

title: Punishment for Defamation
chapter: Chapter XXI - Of Defamation
bare_act_text: Whoever defames another shall be punished with simple imprisonment for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.
meaning: Section 500 IPC prescribes the punishment for defamation as defined under Section 499.
simple_explanation: Anyone who defames another person (as defined under Section 499) can be punished with simple imprisonment up to 2 years, or a fine, or both.
objective: To prescribe the punishment for the offence of defamation.
legal_significance: Provides the standard punishment for criminal defamation as defined under Section 499, with related sections (501, 502) extending liability to printing, engraving, and sale of defamatory matter.
essential_elements: The accused has defamed another person as defined under Section 499.

mens_rea.description: Intention to harm, or knowledge or reason to believe that the imputation made will harm, the reputation of the person defamed, as required under Section 499.

examples.scenario: A publishes a false statement about Z intending to harm Z's reputation, not falling within any exception to Section 499.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 500 with simple imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both.

max_punishment: Simple imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both
ai_summary: Section 500 IPC prescribes punishment for defamation as simple imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both.

title: Printing or Engraving Matter Known to Be Defamatory
chapter: Chapter XXI - Of Defamation
bare_act_text: Whoever prints or engraves any matter, knowing or having good reason to believe that such matter is defamatory of any person, shall be punished with simple imprisonment for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.
meaning: Section 501 IPC punishes printing or engraving any matter while knowing or having good reason to believe it is defamatory of a person.
simple_explanation: If a person prints or engraves something, knowing or having good reason to believe that it is defamatory of someone, they can be punished with simple imprisonment up to 2 years, fine, or both—even if they are not the original author of the defamatory content.
objective: To extend liability for defamation to those involved in the printing or engraving process, ensuring that publishers and printers cannot escape liability by claiming they merely reproduced someone else's words.
legal_significance: Creates a separate offence targeting the printing/engraving stage of disseminating defamatory material, distinct from the original making/publishing under Section 499.
essential_elements: The accused prints or engraves any matter.
The accused knows, or has good reason to believe, that such matter is defamatory of a person.

mens_rea.description: Knowledge or good reason to believe that the matter being printed or engraved is defamatory of a person.

examples.scenario: A printer prints a pamphlet containing statements he knows to be defamatory of Z.
examples.result: The printer is liable under Section 501 with simple imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both.

max_punishment: Simple imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both
ai_summary: Section 501 IPC punishes printing or engraving matter known or believed to be defamatory of a person, with simple imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both.

title: Sale of Printed or Engraved Substance Containing Defamatory Matter
chapter: Chapter XXI - Of Defamation
bare_act_text: Whoever sells or offers for sale any printed or engraved substance containing defamatory matter, knowing that it contains such matter, shall be punished with simple imprisonment for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.
meaning: Section 502 IPC punishes selling or offering for sale any printed or engraved material containing defamatory matter, knowing that it contains such matter.
simple_explanation: If a person sells or offers to sell printed or engraved material (such as a book, pamphlet, or image) that contains defamatory content, and they know it contains such content, they can be punished with simple imprisonment up to 2 years, fine, or both.
objective: To extend liability for defamation to those involved in distributing or selling defamatory printed material, completing the chain of liability from creation to dissemination.
legal_significance: Creates a separate offence targeting the sale/distribution stage of defamatory material, complementing Sections 499 (making/publishing) and 501 (printing/engraving).
essential_elements: The accused sells or offers for sale any printed or engraved substance.
Such substance contains defamatory matter.
The accused knows that it contains such defamatory matter.

mens_rea.description: Knowledge that the printed or engraved substance being sold or offered for sale contains defamatory matter.

examples.scenario: A bookseller sells copies of a pamphlet, knowing it contains defamatory statements about Z.
examples.result: The bookseller is liable under Section 502 with simple imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both.

max_punishment: Simple imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both
ai_summary: Section 502 IPC punishes selling or offering for sale printed or engraved material containing defamatory matter, knowing it contains such matter, with simple imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both.

title: Criminal Intimidation
chapter: Chapter XXII - Of Criminal Intimidation, Insult and Annoyance
bare_act_text: Whoever threatens another with any injury to his person, reputation or property, or to the person or reputation of any one in whom that person is interested, with intent to cause alarm to that person, or to cause that person to do any act which he is not legally bound to do, or to omit to do any act which that person is legally entitled to do, as the means of avoiding the execution of such threat, commits criminal intimidation. Explanation.—A threat to injure the reputation of any deceased person in whom the person threatened is interested, is within this section.
meaning: Section 503 IPC defines criminal intimidation as threatening a person with injury to person, reputation, or property (or that of someone they are interested in), with intent to cause alarm or to compel them to act or refrain from acting against their legal rights/duties.
simple_explanation: Criminal intimidation occurs when someone threatens another person with harm to themselves, their reputation, their property, or that of someone they care about, intending to scare them or force them to do something they are not legally required to do, or stop them from doing something they have a legal right to do.
objective: To define and criminalize threats made with the intent to coerce a person's behaviour through fear, protecting individual liberty of action and peace of mind.
legal_significance: Forms the foundational definition for criminal intimidation, punishable under Section 506, with aggravated punishment where the threat involves death, grievous hurt, destruction by fire, or imputation of unchastity to a woman.
essential_elements: The accused threatens another person with injury to person, reputation, or property, or to that of someone the threatened person is interested in.

The threat is made with intent to cause alarm to that person, OR to cause that person to do an act they are not legally bound to do, OR to omit an act they are legally entitled to do, as a means of avoiding the threat's execution.

mens_rea.description: Intent to cause alarm to the threatened person, or to compel them to act/refrain from acting against their legal rights or obligations through fear of the threatened injury.

examples.scenario: A, for the purpose of inducing B to desist from prosecuting a civil suit, threatens to burn B's house.
examples.result: A is guilty of criminal intimidation under Section 503.

max_punishment: Not applicable (definition section; punishment prescribed under Section 506)
ai_summary: Section 503 IPC defines criminal intimidation as threatening injury to a person's body, reputation, or property (or that of someone they care about) with intent to cause alarm or compel an act/omission contrary to their legal rights, including threats concerning deceased persons' reputations.

title: Intentional Insult with Intent to Provoke Breach of the Peace
chapter: Chapter XXII - Of Criminal Intimidation, Insult and Annoyance
bare_act_text: Whoever intentionally insults, and thereby gives provocation to any person, intending or knowing it to be likely that such provocation will cause him to break the public peace, or to commit any other offence, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both.
meaning: Section 504 IPC punishes intentional insult that provokes a person, where the accused intends or knows it likely that such provocation will cause the person to breach public peace or commit another offence.
simple_explanation: If someone intentionally insults another person in a way that is likely to provoke them into breaking the peace (such as fighting or causing a disturbance) or committing some other offence, and the insulter intended or knew this was likely, they can be punished with imprisonment up to 2 years, fine, or both.
objective: To penalize intentional insults that are likely to provoke a breach of public peace or other offences, thereby preserving public order.
legal_significance: Distinct from defamation (Section 499) and criminal intimidation (Section 503), this section focuses on insults intended to provoke a reaction that disturbs public peace or leads to further offences.
essential_elements: The accused intentionally insults a person.
Such insult gives provocation to that person.
The accused intends, or knows it likely, that such provocation will cause the person to break the public peace or commit any other offence.

mens_rea.description: Intention to insult, coupled with intent or knowledge that the provocation caused is likely to result in a breach of public peace or another offence.

examples.scenario: A intentionally hurls insulting remarks at Z in public, knowing it is likely to provoke Z into a physical altercation.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 504 with imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both
ai_summary: Section 504 IPC punishes intentional insult that provokes a person, where the accused intends or knows it likely to cause a breach of public peace or another offence, with imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both.

title: Statements Conducing to Public Mischief
chapter: Chapter XXII - Of Criminal Intimidation, Insult and Annoyance
bare_act_text: (1) Whoever makes, publishes or circulates any statement, rumour or report,—(a) with intent to cause, or which is likely to cause, any officer, soldier, sailor or airman in the Army, Navy or Air Force of India to mutiny or otherwise disregard or fail in his duty as such; or (b) with intent to cause, or which is likely to cause, fear or alarm to the public, or to any section of the public whereby any person may be induced to commit an offence against the State or against the public tranquility; or (c) with intent to incite, or which is likely to incite, any class or community of persons to commit any offence against any other class or community, shall be punished with imprisonment which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both. (2) Statements creating or promoting enmity, hatred or ill-will between classes.—Whoever makes, publishes or circulates any statement or report containing rumour or alarming news with intent to create or promote, or which is likely to create or promote, on grounds of religion, race, place of birth, residence, language, caste or community or any other ground whatsoever, feelings of enmity, hatred or ill-will between different religious, racial, language or regional groups or castes or communities, shall be punished with imprisonment which may extend to three years, or with fine, or with both. (3) Offence under sub-section (2) committed in place of worship, etc.—Whoever commits an offence specified in sub-section (2) in any place of worship or in any assembly engaged in the performance of religious worship or religious ceremonies, shall be punished with imprisonment which may extend to five years and shall also be liable to fine. Exception.—It does not amount to an offence, within the meaning of this section, when the person making, publishing or circulating any such statement, rumour or report, has reasonable grounds for believing that such statement, rumour or report is true and makes, publishes or circulates it in good faith and without any such intent as aforesaid.
meaning: Section 505 IPC punishes the making, publishing, or circulating of statements, rumours, or reports that incite mutiny in the armed forces, cause public fear/alarm leading to offences against the State or public tranquility, incite inter-community offences, or promote enmity/hatred between groups on grounds such as religion, race, caste, or community, with enhanced punishment if committed in a place of worship.
simple_explanation: This section criminalizes spreading false statements, rumours, or reports that could incite soldiers to mutiny, cause public panic leading to crimes against the State or public order, provoke one community against another, or create hatred between religious, racial, caste, or regional groups. If such statements are made in a place of worship or religious gathering, the punishment is even higher. However, it is not an offence if the person genuinely and reasonably believed the statement to be true and made it in good faith without any harmful intent.
objective: To prevent the spread of statements that could incite mutiny, public disorder, inter-community violence, or communal hatred, while protecting good-faith statements believed to be true.
legal_significance: Addresses public mischief through misinformation and rumour-mongering, particularly statements that threaten armed forces discipline, public tranquility, or communal harmony, with an exception protecting good-faith, reasonably-believed-true statements.
essential_elements: The accused makes, publishes, or circulates a statement, rumour, or report.

For sub-section (1): such statement is intended to cause, or likely to cause, mutiny among armed forces personnel, OR fear/alarm to the public inducing offences against the State/public tranquility, OR incitement of one class/community against another.

For sub-section (2): the statement/report contains rumour or alarming news intended to, or likely to, create/promote enmity, hatred, or ill-will between groups based on religion, race, place of birth, residence, language, caste, community, or any other ground.
For sub-section (3): the offence under sub-section (2) is committed in a place of worship or religious assembly.
Exception applies if the accused had reasonable grounds to believe the statement was true and made it in good faith without the prohibited intent.

mens_rea.description: Intent to cause, or knowledge that the statement is likely to cause, the specified harmful outcomes (mutiny, public fear leading to offences, inter-community incitement, or communal enmity/hatred); absent where good faith and reasonable belief in truth are established.

aggravated_case.imprisonment: Up to 5 years (sub-section 3, if committed in a place of worship or religious assembly)

examples.scenario: A circulates a false rumour intended to incite one religious community to commit offences against another community.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 505(1)(c) with imprisonment up to 3 years, or fine, or both.

examples.scenario: A makes a statement promoting hatred between two caste groups while addressing a religious assembly in a temple.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 505(3) with imprisonment up to 5 years and fine.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 5 years and fine (if committed in a place of worship under sub-section 3), otherwise up to 3 years, or fine, or both
bailable: Bailable (non-bailable for sub-section 2 in some interpretations)
ai_summary: Section 505 IPC punishes making, publishing, or circulating statements/rumours that incite mutiny, cause public fear leading to offences, incite inter-community offences, or promote enmity/hatred between groups based on religion, race, caste, etc., with imprisonment up to 3 years (or up to 5 years if committed in a place of worship), subject to a good-faith exception for reasonably believed true statements.

title: Punishment for Criminal Intimidation
chapter: Chapter XXII - Of Criminal Intimidation, Insult and Annoyance
bare_act_text: Whoever commits the offence of criminal intimidation shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both; If threat be to cause death or grievous hurt, etc.—and if the threat be to cause death or grievous hurt, or to cause the destruction of any property by fire, or to cause an offence punishable with death or imprisonment for life, or with imprisonment for a term which may extend to seven years, or to impute unchastity to a woman, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, or with fine, or with both.
meaning: Section 506 IPC prescribes the punishment for criminal intimidation as defined under Section 503, with an enhanced punishment where the threat is of death, grievous hurt, destruction of property by fire, an offence punishable with death/life imprisonment/imprisonment up to 7 years, or imputation of unchastity to a woman.
simple_explanation: Anyone who commits criminal intimidation can be punished with imprisonment up to 2 years, fine, or both. But if the threat made is more serious—such as a threat to kill, cause grievous injury, burn down property, commit a very serious crime, or falsely accuse a woman of being unchaste—the punishment increases to imprisonment up to 7 years, fine, or both.
objective: To prescribe the punishment for criminal intimidation, with a significantly enhanced punishment for the most serious categories of threats.
legal_significance: Provides the standard punishment for criminal intimidation as defined under Section 503, with a graded enhancement for threats involving death, grievous hurt, arson, serious offences, or imputation of unchastity to a woman.
essential_elements: The accused has committed criminal intimidation as defined under Section 503.

For enhanced punishment: the threat is to cause death or grievous hurt, OR to destroy property by fire, OR to cause an offence punishable with death/life imprisonment/imprisonment up to 7 years, OR to impute unchastity to a woman.

mens_rea.description: Mens rea as required for criminal intimidation under Section 503 (intent to cause alarm or to compel an act/omission through fear of the threatened injury).

aggravated_case.imprisonment: Up to 7 years (if the threat is of death, grievous hurt, destruction of property by fire, a serious offence, or imputation of unchastity to a woman)

examples.scenario: A threatens Z with minor harm to compel Z to perform an act Z is not legally bound to do.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 506 with imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both.

examples.scenario: A threatens to kill Z's family member if Z does not comply with A's demand.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 506 with imprisonment up to 7 years, or fine, or both, due to the threat of death.

examples.scenario: A threatens to falsely impute unchastity to Z's wife unless Z pays him money.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 506 with imprisonment up to 7 years, or fine, or both.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years, or fine, or both (for aggravated threats)
cognizable: Non-cognizable (cognizable for aggravated category in some interpretations)
compoundable: Yes (compoundable with permission of the court)
ai_summary: Section 506 IPC punishes criminal intimidation with imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both, enhanced to imprisonment up to 7 years, or fine, or both, where the threat is of death, grievous hurt, destruction by fire, a serious offence, or imputation of unchastity to a woman.

title: Criminal Intimidation by an Anonymous Communication
chapter: Chapter XXII - Of Criminal Intimidation, Insult and Annoyance
bare_act_text: Whoever commits the offence of criminal intimidation by an anonymous communication, or having taken precaution to conceal the name or abode of the person from whom the threat comes, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, in addition to the punishment provided for the offence by the last preceding section.
meaning: Section 507 IPC punishes criminal intimidation committed through an anonymous communication, or where the offender has concealed their identity or whereabouts, with an additional punishment over and above that prescribed under Section 506.
simple_explanation: If a person commits criminal intimidation anonymously—or takes steps to hide who they are or where they live—they face an additional punishment of up to 2 years' imprisonment, on top of the punishment they would already receive under Section 506 for the intimidation itself.
objective: To impose additional punishment for the aggravating factor of anonymity in criminal intimidation, recognizing that anonymous threats can be more insidious and harder for victims to address.
legal_significance: Operates as an add-on offence to Section 506, with the punishment under Section 507 being cumulative/additional to that already prescribed for criminal intimidation under Section 506.
essential_elements: The accused commits the offence of criminal intimidation as defined under Section 503.
Such intimidation is committed by anonymous communication, OR the accused has taken precautions to conceal his name or abode.

mens_rea.description: Mens rea as required for criminal intimidation under Section 503, combined with the deliberate act of concealing one's identity or whereabouts while committing the intimidation.

normal_case.imprisonment: Up to 2 years (in addition to punishment under Section 506)

examples.scenario: A sends an anonymous letter to Z threatening to cause grievous hurt unless Z pays a sum of money.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 506 for the intimidation (up to 7 years due to the nature of the threat) and additionally under Section 507 (up to 2 years), for the anonymous nature of the communication.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 2 years in addition to punishment under Section 506
compoundable: Yes (compoundable with permission of the court)
ai_summary: Section 507 IPC punishes criminal intimidation committed through anonymous communication or with concealment of identity/whereabouts, with imprisonment up to 2 years in addition to the punishment prescribed under Section 506.

title: Act Caused by Inducing Person to Believe That He Will Be Rendered an Object of the Divine Displeasure
chapter: Chapter XXII - Of Criminal Intimidation, Insult and Annoyance
bare_act_text: Whoever voluntarily causes or attempts to cause any person to do anything which that person is not legally bound to do, or to omit to do anything which he is legally entitled to do, by inducing or attempting to induce that person to believe that he or any person in whom he is interested will become or will be rendered by some act of the offender an object of Divine displeasure if he does not do the thing which it is the object of the offender to cause him to do, or if he does the thing which it is the object of the offender to cause him to omit, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to one year, or with fine, or with both.
meaning: Section 508 IPC punishes voluntarily causing or attempting to cause a person to act or omit to act, against their legal rights or obligations, by inducing them to believe that failure to comply (or compliance, in the case of an omission sought) will render them or someone they care about an object of Divine displeasure.
simple_explanation: If someone makes another person believe that they (or someone they care about) will incur the wrath or displeasure of God or a deity unless they do (or refrain from doing) something, and thereby gets that person to act or not act in a way they are not legally required to, the person making such inducement can be punished with imprisonment up to 1 year, fine, or both.
objective: To penalize the use of religious or superstitious threats (such as invoking divine displeasure) as a means of coercing a person's conduct, recognizing this as a form of intimidation.
legal_significance: Addresses a culturally specific form of coercion through religious/superstitious belief (such as the practice of 'sitting dharna'), distinct from physical or material threats covered under Section 503.
essential_elements: The accused voluntarily causes or attempts to cause a person to do something they are not legally bound to do, or to omit something they are legally entitled to do.

This is achieved by inducing or attempting to induce the person to believe that they (or someone they are interested in) will become, or be rendered, an object of Divine displeasure by some act of the offender if they do not comply (or if they do the act sought to be omitted).

mens_rea.description: Intention to induce belief in Divine displeasure as a means to cause the person to act or refrain from acting in a manner not legally required.

examples.scenario: A sits dhurna at Z's door with the intention of causing it to be believed that, by so sitting, he renders Z an object of Divine displeasure.
examples.result: A has committed the offence under Section 508, punishable with imprisonment up to 1 year, or fine, or both.

examples.scenario: A threatens Z that, unless Z performs a certain act, A will kill one of A's own children under circumstances believed to render Z an object of Divine displeasure.
examples.result: A has committed the offence under Section 508.

max_punishment: Imprisonment up to 1 year, or fine, or both
compoundable: Yes (compoundable with permission of the court)
ai_summary: Section 508 IPC punishes voluntarily causing or attempting to cause a person to act or omit an act against their legal rights/obligations by inducing belief that doing otherwise will render them or someone they care about an object of Divine displeasure, with imprisonment up to 1 year, or fine, or both.

title: Word, Gesture or Act Intended to Insult the Modesty of a Woman
chapter: Chapter XXII - Of Criminal Intimidation, Insult and Annoyance
bare_act_text: Whoever, intending to insult the modesty of any woman, utters any words, makes any sound or gesture, or exhibits any object, intending that such word or sound shall be heard, or that such gesture or object shall be seen, by such woman, or intrudes upon the privacy of such woman, shall be punished with simple imprisonment for a term which may extend to three years, and also with fine.
meaning: Section 509 IPC punishes uttering words, making sounds or gestures, exhibiting objects, or intruding upon a woman's privacy, with the intention of insulting her modesty.
simple_explanation: If a person, intending to insult a woman's modesty, says something, makes a sound or gesture, shows an object meant to be seen or heard by her, or intrudes on her privacy, they can be punished with simple imprisonment up to 3 years and a fine.
objective: To protect women from verbal, gestural, or visual acts intended to insult their modesty, as well as from intrusions upon their privacy.
legal_significance: Covers a broad range of acts—words, sounds, gestures, exhibition of objects, and privacy intrusion (such as voyeurism)—aimed at insulting a woman's modesty; the punishment was significantly enhanced (from 1 year to 3 years, with mandatory fine) following the 2013 amendment in the wake of increased focus on women's safety.
essential_elements: The accused intends to insult the modesty of a woman.
The accused utters words, makes a sound or gesture, or exhibits an object, intending it to be heard or seen by the woman, OR intrudes upon the privacy of such woman.

mens_rea.description: Intention to insult the modesty of the woman through the words, sound, gesture, object, or intrusion upon her privacy.

examples.scenario: A makes lewd gestures towards Z, a woman, intending to insult her modesty, ensuring she sees the gestures.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 509 with simple imprisonment up to 3 years and fine.

examples.scenario: A secretly watches or records a woman in a private space without her knowledge, intruding upon her privacy with intent to insult her modesty.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 509 with simple imprisonment up to 3 years and fine.

max_punishment: Simple imprisonment up to 3 years and fine
ai_summary: Section 509 IPC punishes words, sounds, gestures, exhibition of objects intended to be heard or seen by a woman, or intrusion upon her privacy, done with intent to insult her modesty, with simple imprisonment up to 3 years and fine.

title: Sexual Harassment by Relative (Chhattisgarh State Amendment)
chapter: Chapter XXII - Of Criminal Intimidation, Insult and Annoyance
bare_act_text: Whoever, being related to a woman through blood, adoption or marriage, and not being her husband, takes the advantage of his proximity and induces, seduces or threatens such woman with intent to insult her modesty by word, gesture or act shall be punished with rigorous imprisonment which shall not be less than one year but which may extend to five years and shall also liable to fine.
meaning: Section 509A (Chhattisgarh Amendment) punishes a relative of a woman (by blood, adoption, or marriage, but not her husband) who, taking advantage of his proximity to her, induces, seduces, or threatens her with intent to insult her modesty by word, gesture, or act.
simple_explanation: If a person who is related to a woman (through blood, adoption, or marriage, but is not her husband) uses his closeness to her to induce, seduce, or threaten her in a way meant to insult her modesty—through words, gestures, or actions—he can be punished with rigorous imprisonment of 1 to 5 years and a fine, under Chhattisgarh's amendment.
objective: To specifically address and penalize sexual harassment committed by relatives who exploit their familial proximity and trust to insult a woman's modesty.
legal_significance: Creates a state-specific aggravated category of offence against modesty, recognizing the breach of familial trust involved when a relative (other than the husband) commits such acts, with a mandatory minimum sentence.
essential_elements: The accused is related to the woman through blood, adoption, or marriage.
The accused is not the woman's husband.
The accused takes advantage of his proximity to the woman.
The accused induces, seduces, or threatens the woman with intent to insult her modesty by word, gesture, or act.

mens_rea.description: Intent to insult the woman's modesty through inducement, seduction, or threats, exploiting the proximity arising from the familial relationship.

normal_case.imprisonment: Rigorous imprisonment not less than 1 year, extendable to 5 years

examples.scenario: A, the brother-in-law of Z, takes advantage of his access to Z's home to make repeated suggestive remarks intended to insult Z's modesty.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 509A with rigorous imprisonment of 1 to 5 years and fine, under the Chhattisgarh amendment.

max_punishment: Rigorous imprisonment up to 5 years and fine
ai_summary: Section 509A, a Chhattisgarh state amendment, punishes a relative (other than the husband) who, exploiting his proximity, induces, seduces, or threatens a woman with intent to insult her modesty, with rigorous imprisonment of 1 to 5 years and fine.

title: Sexual Harassment by Electronic Mode (Chhattisgarh State Amendment)
chapter: Chapter XXII - Of Criminal Intimidation, Insult and Annoyance
bare_act_text: Whoever, by means of telecommunication device or by any other electronic mode including internet, makes creates, solicits or initiates the transmission of any comment, request, suggestion, proposal, image or other communication, which is obscene, lewd, lascivious, filthy or indecent with intent to harass or cause or having knowledge that it would harass or cause annoyance or mental agony to a woman shall be punished with rigorous imprisonment for a term which shall not be less than six months but may extend to two years and shall also be liable to fine.
meaning: Section 509B (Chhattisgarh Amendment) punishes the creation, solicitation, or transmission of obscene, lewd, lascivious, filthy, or indecent communications (via telecommunication devices or electronic/internet means) intended to or known to harass or cause annoyance/mental agony to a woman.
simple_explanation: If a person uses a phone, the internet, or any other electronic means to send, create, or solicit obscene, lewd, or indecent comments, images, suggestions, or proposals to a woman, intending to harass her or knowing it will cause her annoyance or mental distress, they can be punished with rigorous imprisonment of 6 months to 2 years and a fine, under Chhattisgarh's amendment.
objective: To address the growing problem of online and electronic sexual harassment of women, including obscene messages, images, and proposals sent via digital means.
legal_significance: Creates a state-specific offence specifically targeting cyber-harassment of women through obscene or indecent electronic communications, recognizing the distinct nature of harassment facilitated by technology.
essential_elements: The accused, by means of a telecommunication device or other electronic mode (including internet), makes, creates, solicits, or initiates the transmission of a comment, request, suggestion, proposal, image, or other communication.
Such communication is obscene, lewd, lascivious, filthy, or indecent.
The accused intends to harass, or causes, or knows that it would cause, harassment, annoyance, or mental agony to a woman.

mens_rea.description: Intent to harass a woman through obscene or indecent electronic communication, or knowledge that such communication would cause her harassment, annoyance, or mental agony.

normal_case.imprisonment: Rigorous imprisonment not less than 6 months, extendable to 2 years

examples.scenario: A repeatedly sends Z, a woman, obscene images via messaging apps, knowing it causes her mental distress.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 509B with rigorous imprisonment of 6 months to 2 years and fine, under the Chhattisgarh amendment.

max_punishment: Rigorous imprisonment up to 2 years and fine
ai_summary: Section 509B, a Chhattisgarh state amendment, punishes creating, soliciting, or transmitting obscene, lewd, or indecent electronic communications with intent to harass, or knowledge that they would cause harassment or mental agony to a woman, with rigorous imprisonment of 6 months to 2 years and fine.

title: Misconduct in Public by a Drunken Person
chapter: Chapter XXII - Of Criminal Intimidation, Insult and Annoyance
bare_act_text: Whoever, in a state of intoxication, appears in any public place, or in any place which it is a trespass in him to enter, and there conducts himself in such a manner as to cause annoyance to any person, shall be punished with simple imprisonment for a term which may extend to twenty-four hours, or with fine which may extend to ten rupees, or with both.
meaning: Section 510 IPC punishes a person who, while intoxicated, appears in a public place or a place they are trespassing into, and behaves in a manner causing annoyance to any person.
simple_explanation: If someone gets drunk and then appears in a public place (or somewhere they have no right to be), and behaves in a way that annoys other people, they can be punished with simple imprisonment of up to 24 hours, or a small fine of up to ten rupees, or both.
objective: To maintain public order and decorum by penalizing minor disorderly conduct caused by intoxication in public or trespassed places.
legal_significance: Represents one of the most minor offences under the IPC, with a token punishment reflecting its nature as a public nuisance rather than a serious crime.
essential_elements: The accused is in a state of intoxication.
The accused appears in a public place, or a place where it would be trespass for him to enter.
The accused conducts himself in a manner causing annoyance to any person.

mens_rea.description: No specific intent is required beyond voluntary intoxication and conduct causing annoyance in a public or trespassed place.

examples.scenario: A, in a state of intoxication, enters a public park and behaves boisterously, causing annoyance to other visitors.
examples.result: A is liable under Section 510 with simple imprisonment up to 24 hours, or fine up to ten rupees, or both.

max_punishment: Simple imprisonment up to 24 hours, or fine up to ten rupees, or both
ai_summary: Section 510 IPC punishes a person who, while intoxicated, appears in a public place or trespassed location and behaves in a manner causing annoyance to others, with simple imprisonment up to 24 hours, or a fine up to ten rupees, or both.

title: Punishment for Attempting to Commit Offences Punishable with Imprisonment for Life or Other Imprisonment
chapter: Chapter XXIII - Of Attempts to Commit Offences
bare_act_text: Whoever attempts to commit an offence punishable by this Code with imprisonment for life or imprisonment, or to cause such an offence to be committed, and in such attempt does any act towards the commission of the offence, shall, where no express provision is made by this Code for the punishment of such attempt, be punished with imprisonment of any description provided for the offence, for a term which may extend to one-half of the imprisonment for life or, as the case may be, one-half of the longest term of imprisonment provided for that offence, or with such fine as is provided for the offence, or with both.
meaning: Section 511 IPC provides the general punishment for attempting to commit any offence punishable with imprisonment for life or imprisonment, where no specific provision for such attempt exists elsewhere in the Code.
simple_explanation: If a person attempts to commit a crime that is punishable with life imprisonment or any term of imprisonment, and takes some action towards committing it, but the Code doesn't have a specific punishment for that particular attempt, then the punishment for the attempt is up to half of the maximum punishment (whether life imprisonment or a fixed term) that would apply for the completed offence, or the fine for that offence, or both.
objective: To provide a general residual punishment framework for attempts to commit offences, ensuring that attempts are punishable even where the Code does not specifically provide for them, while keeping the punishment proportionate (generally half of that for the completed offence).
legal_significance: Serves as a catch-all/residual provision for criminal attempts under the IPC, applicable only where no express provision (such as Section 393 for attempted robbery) exists for the attempt of that specific offence.
essential_elements: The accused attempts to commit an offence punishable under the IPC with imprisonment for life or imprisonment, or causes such an offence to be attempted.
In such attempt, the accused does some act towards the commission of the offence.
No express provision is made elsewhere in the Code for the punishment of such an attempt.

mens_rea.description: Intention to commit the underlying offence, evidenced by an act done towards its commission, even though the offence itself is not completed.

normal_case.imprisonment: Up to one-half of the imprisonment for life, or one-half of the longest term of imprisonment provided for the completed offence

examples.scenario: A attempts to steal jewels by breaking open a box, but finds after opening it that there are no jewels inside.
examples.result: A is guilty under Section 511, having done an act towards the commission of theft, even though the theft was not completed.

examples.scenario: A attempts to pick Z's pocket by thrusting his hand into Z's pocket, but fails because Z's pocket is empty.
examples.result: A is guilty under Section 511 for the attempt.

max_punishment: Up to one-half of the imprisonment for life, or one-half of the longest term of imprisonment provided for the completed offence, and/or fine as provided for the offence
cognizable: Depends on the nature of the underlying offence
bailable: Depends on the nature of the underlying offence
compoundable: Depends on the nature of the underlying offence
ai_summary: Section 511 IPC provides the general punishment for attempting to commit any offence punishable with life imprisonment or imprisonment, where no specific provision exists, fixing the punishment at up to one-half of that prescribed for the completed offence, or the corresponding fine, or both.
